    <document rank="2" url="https://medlineplus.gov/diabetes.html">
      <content name="title"><span class="qt0">Diabetes</span></content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle"><span class="qt0">Diabetes Mellitus</span></content>
      <content name="altTitle">Sugar <span class="qt0">Diabetes</span></content>
      <content name="altTitle">DM</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>?<p><span class="qt0">Diabetes</span>, also known as <span class="qt0">diabetes mellitus</span>, is a disease in which your blood glucose, or blood sugar, levels are too high. Glucose is your body's main source of energy. Your body can make glucose, but it also comes from the food you eat. Insulin is a hormone made by your pancreas. Insulin helps move glucose from your bloodstream into your cells, where it can be used for energy.</p><p>If you have <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>, your body can't make insulin, can't use insulin as well as it should, or both. Too much glucose stays in your blood and doesn't reach your cells. This can cause glucose levels to get too high. Over time, high blood glucose levels can lead to serious health conditions. But you can take steps to manage your <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> and try to prevent these health problems.</p>What are the types of <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>?<p>There are different types of <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>:</p><ul><li>Type 1 <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>. If you have type 1 <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>, your body makes little or no insulin. It happens when your immune system attacks and destroys the cells that produce insulin. </li><li>Type 2 <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>. This is the most common form of <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>. If you have type 2 <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>, your body may still be able to make insulin, but your cells don't respond well to insulin. They can't easily take up enough glucose from your blood.</li><li>Gestational <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>. This is a form of <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> that develops during pregnancy. It happens when your body can't make the extra insulin it needs during pregnancy.</li></ul>What causes <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>?<p>The different types of <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> have different causes:</p><ul><li>Researchers think type 1 <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> is caused by genes and factors in the environment that might trigger the disease.</li><li>Type 2 <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> is caused by several factors, including lifestyle factors and genes. The lifestyle factors include not being physically active and being overweight or having obesity.</li><li>Researchers think gestational <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> is caused by the hormonal changes of pregnancy along with genetic and lifestyle factors.</li></ul>Who is more likely to develop <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>?<p>The different types of <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> have different risk factors:</p><ul><li>You can develop type 1 <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> at any age, but it most often starts in childhood. Having a parent or sibling with type 1 <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> may increase your chance of developing it.</li><li>You are at higher risk of developing type 2 <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> if you:</li><ul><li>Are overweight or have obesity.</li><li>Are over age 35. Children, teenagers, and younger adults can get <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>, but it is more common in middle-aged and older adults.</li><li>Have a family history of <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>.</li><li>Have prediabetes. This means that your blood glucose is higher than normal, but it's not high enough to be called <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>.</li><li>Had gestational <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>.</li><li>Have given birth to a baby weighing 9 pounds or more.</li><li>Are African American, American Indian, Asian American, Hispanic/Latino, or Pacific Islander.</li><li>Are not physically active.</li><li>Have certain other health conditions, such as high blood pressure or polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS).</li></ul><li>You are at higher risk of developing gestational <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> if you:</li><ul><li>Are overweight or have obesity.</li><li>Have a family history of <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>.</li><li>Had gestational <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> in a previous pregnancy.</li><li>Have given birth to a baby weighing 9 pounds or more.</li><li>Have polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS).</li><li>Are African American, Hispanic/Latino, American Indian, Alaska Native, Native Hawaiian, or Pacific Islander.</li></ul></ul>What are the symptoms of <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>?<p>The symptoms of <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> may include:</p><ul><li>Feeling very thirsty</li><li>Feeling very hungry</li><li>Urinating (peeing) more often, including at night</li><li>Fatigue</li><li>Blurry vision</li><li>Numbness or tingling in the feet or hands</li><li>Sores that do not heal</li><li>Losing weight without trying</li></ul><p>But it's important to know that your symptoms may vary, depending on which type you have:</p><ul><li>The symptoms of type 1 <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> usually come on quickly and can be severe.</li><li>With type 2 <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>, the symptoms often develop slowly, over several years. The symptoms can be so mild that you might not even notice them.</li><li>Gestational <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> often has no symptoms. If you do have symptoms, they may be mild. If you are pregnant, you will usually be screened for this condition between 24 and 28 weeks of pregnancy. </li></ul>How is <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> diagnosed?<p>To find out if you have <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>, your health care provider will use one or more glucose blood tests. There are several types, including the A1C test.</p>What are the treatments for <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>?<p>Treatment for <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> involves managing your blood glucose levels:</p><ul><li>If you have type 1 <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>, you will need to take daily doses of insulin, either by injection or through a special pump. Some people also need to take another type of <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> medicine that works with insulin.</li><li>If you have type 2 <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>, you may be able to manage or even reverse it by making lifestyle changes. These include eating a healthy diet, staying at healthy weight, and getting regular physical activity. Some people also need to take <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> medicines to manage their <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>.</li><li>If you have gestational <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>, you may be able to lower your glucose levels by eating a healthy diet and getting regular exercise. But be sure to talk to your provider about your treatment options. Gestational <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> usually goes away after you give birth. But you will have a higher risk of developing type 2 <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> later.</li></ul><p>Checking your blood glucose levels is also an important part of managing your <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>. Ask your provider about the best way to check your blood glucose level and how often you should check it.</p>Can <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> be prevented?<p>Type 1 <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> can't be prevented.</p><p>You may be able to delay or prevent type 2 <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> through the same lifestyle changes that are used to manage <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> (eating a healthy diet, staying at a healthy weight, and getting regular physical activity). These lifestyle changes may also help prevent gestational <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>.</p><p>NIH: National Institute of <span class="qt0">Diabetes</span> and Digestive and Kidney Diseases</p></content>

      <content name="title"><span class="qt0">Diabetes</span> Type 1</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Insulin-Dependent <span class="qt0">Diabetes Mellitus</span></content>
      <content name="altTitle">Juvenile <span class="qt0">Diabetes</span></content>
      <content name="altTitle">Type 1 <span class="qt0">Diabetes</span></content>
      <content name="altTitle">Insulin-dependent <span class="qt0">diabetes</span></content>
      <content name="altTitle">Type I <span class="qt0">diabetes</span></content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p><span class="qt0">Diabetes</span> means your blood glucose, or blood sugar, levels are too 
high.  With type 1 <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>, your pancreas does not make insulin. Insulin is a hormone that 
helps glucose get into your cells to give them energy. Without insulin, too much glucose 
stays in your blood.  Over time, high blood glucose can lead to serious problems with your 
heart, eyes, kidneys, nerves, and gums and teeth. </p><p>Type 1 <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> happens most often in children and young adults but can appear at any 
age. Symptoms may include:</p><ul><li>	Being very thirsty </li><li>	Urinating often </li><li>	Feeling very hungry or tired </li><li>	Losing weight without trying </li><li>	Having sores that heal slowly </li><li>	Having dry, itchy skin </li><li>	Losing the feeling in your feet or having tingling in your feet </li><li>	Having blurry eyesight </li></ul><p>A blood test can show if you have <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>. If you do, you will need to take insulin for 
the rest of your life. A blood test called the A1C can check to see how well you are managing your <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>.</p><p>NIH: National Institute of <span class="qt0">Diabetes</span> and Digestive and Kidney Diseases</p></content>

    <document rank="1" url="https://medlineplus.gov/diabetestype2.html">
      <content name="title"><span class="qt0">Diabetes</span> Type 2</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Non-Insulin Dependent <span class="qt0">Diabetes Mellitus</span></content>
      <content name="altTitle">Sugar <span class="qt0">Diabetes</span></content>
      <content name="altTitle">Type II <span class="qt0">Diabetes</span></content>
      <content name="altTitle">Type 2 <span class="qt0">Diabetes</span></content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is type 2 <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>?<p>Type 2 <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> is a disease in which your blood glucose, or blood sugar, levels are too high. Glucose is your main source of energy. It comes from the foods you eat. A hormone called insulin helps the glucose get into your cells to give them energy. If you have <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>, your body doesn't make enough insulin or doesn't use insulin well. The glucose then stays in your blood and not enough goes into your cells.</p><p>Over time, having too much glucose in your blood can cause health problems. But you can take steps to manage your <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> and try to prevent these health problems.</p>What causes type 2 <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>?<p>Type 2 <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> may be caused by a combination of factors:</p><ul><li>Being overweight or having obesity</li><li>Not being physically active</li><li>Genetics and family history</li></ul><p>Type 2 <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> usually starts with insulin resistance. This is a condition in which your cells don't respond normally to insulin. As a result, your body needs more insulin to help the glucose enter your cells. At first, your body makes more insulin to try to get cells to respond. But over time, your body can't make enough insulin, and your blood glucose levels rise.</p>Who is at risk for type 2 <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>?<p>You are at higher risk of developing type 2 <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> if you:</p><ul><li>Are over age 45. Children, teenagers, and younger adults can get type 2 <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>, but it is more common in middle-aged and older people.</li><li>Have prediabetes, which means that your blood glucose is high for you but not high enough to be called <span class="qt0">diabetes</span></li><li>Had <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> in pregnancy or gave birth to a baby weighing 9 pounds or more.</li><li>Have a family history of <span class="qt0">diabetes</span></li><li>Are overweight or have obesity</li><li>Are Black or African American, Hispanic/Latino, American Indian, Asian American, or Pacific Islander</li><li>Are not physically active</li><li>Have other conditions such as high blood pressure, heart disease, stroke, polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS), or depression</li><li>Have low HDL (good) cholesterol and high triglycerides</li><li>Have acanthosis nigricans - dark, thick, and velvety skin around your neck or armpits</li></ul>What are the symptoms of type 2 <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>?<p>Many people with type 2 <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> have no symptoms at all. If you do have them, the symptoms develop slowly over several years. They might be so mild that you do not notice them. The symptoms can include:</p><ul><li>Increased thirst and urination</li><li>Increased hunger</li><li>Feeling tired</li><li>Blurred vision</li><li>Numbness or tingling in the feet or hands</li><li>Sores that do not heal</li><li>Unexplained weight loss</li></ul>How is type 2 <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> diagnosed?<p>Your provider will use blood tests to diagnose type 2 <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>. The blood tests include:</p><ul><li>A1C test, which measures your average blood glucose level over the past 3 months.</li><li>Fasting plasma glucose (FPG) test, which measures your current blood glucose level. You need to fast (not eat or drink anything except water) for at least 8 hours before the test.</li><li>Random plasma glucose (RPG) test, which measures your current blood glucose level. This test is used when you have <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> symptoms and the provider does not want to wait for you to fast before having the test.</li></ul>What are the treatments for type 2 <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>?<p>Treatment for type 2 <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> involves managing your blood glucose levels. Many people are able to do this by living a healthy lifestyle. Some people may also need to take medicine:</p><ul><li>A healthy lifestyle includes following a healthy eating plan and getting regular physical activity. You need to learn how to balance what you eat and drink with physical activity and <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> medicine, if you take any.</li><li>Medicines for <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> include oral medicines, insulin, and other injectable medicines. Over time, some people will need to take more than one type of medicine to control their <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>.</li><li>You will need to check your blood glucose regularly. Your provider will tell you how often you need to do it.</li><li>It's also important to keep your blood pressure and cholesterol levels close to the targets your provider sets for you. Make sure to get your screening tests regularly.</li></ul>Can type 2 <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> be prevented?<p>You can take steps to help prevent or delay type 2 <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> by losing weight if you are overweight, eating fewer calories, and being more physically active. If you have a condition which raises your risk for type 2 <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>, managing that condition may lower your risk of getting type 2 <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>.</p><p>NIH: National Institute of <span class="qt0">Diabetes</span> and Digestive and Kidney Diseases</p></content>

    <document rank="3" url="https://medlineplus.gov/diabetesandpregnancy.html">
      <content name="title"><span class="qt0">Diabetes</span> and Pregnancy</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Gestational <span class="qt0">Diabetes</span></content>
      <content name="altTitle">Pregnancy and <span class="qt0">Diabetes</span></content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>? <p>If you have <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>, your blood glucose, or blood sugar, levels are too high. Glucose comes from the foods you eat. A hormone called insulin helps the glucose get into your cells to give them energy. With type 1 <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>, your body does not make insulin. With type 2 <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>, your body does not make or use insulin well. Without enough insulin, the glucose stays in your blood.</p>What is gestational <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>?<p>Some people already have <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> before they get pregnant. But others may develop <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> during pregnancy. This type of <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> is called gestational <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>. It usually develops around the 24th week of pregnancy. It happens when your body can't make the extra insulin it needs during pregnancy. Researchers think gestational <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> is caused by the hormonal changes of pregnancy, along with genetic and lifestyle factors.</p>Who is more likely to develop gestational <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>?<p>Anyone who is pregnant could develop gestational <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>. But you are more likely to develop it if you:</p><ul><li>Are overweight or have obesity</li><li>Have a family history of <span class="qt0">diabetes</span></li><li>Had gestational <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> in a previous pregnancy</li><li>Have given birth to a baby weighing 9 pounds or more</li><li>Have polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS)</li><li>Are African American, Hispanic/Latino, American Indian, Alaska Native, Native Hawaiian, or Pacific Islander person</li></ul>How do I know if I have gestational <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>?<p>Gestational <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> often has no symptoms. If you do have symptoms, they may be mild, such as being thirstier than normal or having to urinate (pee) more often.</p><p>If you are pregnant, you will most likely be screened for gestational <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> between 24 and 28 weeks of pregnancy. But if you have an increased chance of developing gestational <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>, you may be tested during your first prenatal visit. Your health care provider will use one or more blood glucose tests to check for gestational <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>. You may have the glucose challenge test, the oral glucose tolerance test (OGTT), or both.</p><p>For these two tests, you will drink a sugary liquid and wait for an hour before your blood sample is taken. If you have an oral glucose tolerance test, you will also get your blood drawn after 2 and 3 hours.

</p>How can <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> affect my pregnancy?<p>Having <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> during pregnancy can affect your health. For example:</p><ul><li>You are more likely to develop preeclampsia, a serious medical condition that causes a sudden increase in your blood pressure.</li><li>You are more likely to need a cesarean delivery, because your baby is more likely to be bigger than average.</li><li>Changes to your hormones and your body during pregnancy can affect your blood glucose levels. If you had <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> before pregnancy, you may now need to adjust your meal plan, physical activity routine, and/or medicines. If you have any <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> health problems, they may get worse during pregnancy.</li><li>Gestational <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> usually goes away after you have your baby. But you will be at higher risk of developing type 2 <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> later.</li></ul><p>Having <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> during pregnancy can also affect the health of your developing baby:</p><ul><li>If you have high blood glucose levels at the beginning of your pregnancy, there is a higher risk of birth defects.</li><li>Your baby will be at risk for obesity and type 2 <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> later in life.</li><li>Your baby is more likely to be born early.</li><li>Your baby may have breathing problems or hypoglycemia (low blood glucose levels) right after birth.</li><li>There is a higher risk of miscarriage and stillbirth.</li></ul>How can I manage <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> during pregnancy?<p>There are steps you can take to manage your <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> before, during, and after pregnancy.</p><p>If you already have <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>, the best time to control your blood glucose is before you get pregnant. High blood glucose levels can be harmful to your developing baby during the first weeks of pregnancy, even before you know you are pregnant. See your provider to help you plan for pregnancy. You can talk about how to lower the risk of health problems for you and your developing baby. You can also discuss your diet, physical activity, and which <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> medicines are safe during pregnancy.</p><p>During your pregnancy, you will work with your provider to manage your blood glucose levels. You may be able to manage them with a healthy diet and regular physical activity. If that's not enough, then you will need to take <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> medicines. It's also important that you:
</p><ul><li>Get regular prenatal checkups</li><li>Take your prenatal vitamins</li><li>Don't use harmful substances such as alcohol, tobacco, and illegal drugs</li></ul><p>After pregnancy, there are steps you need to take to stay healthy:</p><ul><li>If you had gestational <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>, you are at risk of developing type 2 <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>. You will be tested for it within 4 to 12 weeks after giving birth. Even if your blood glucose levels have returned to normal, you will need to get them tested every 1 to 3 years.</li><li>If you already had <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> before pregnancy, you and your provider will monitor changes to your blood glucose levels. They will tell you if you need to adjust your <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> management plan.</li></ul><p>NIH: National Institute of <span class="qt0">Diabetes</span> and Digestive and Kidney Diseases</p></content>
      <content name="mesh">Pregnancy in Diabetics</content>

    <document rank="4" url="https://medlineplus.gov/diabetesinchildrenandteens.html">
      <content name="title"><span class="qt0">Diabetes</span> in Children and Teens</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Children and <span class="qt0">Diabetes</span></content>
      <content name="altTitle">Teens and <span class="qt0">Diabetes</span></content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>Until recently, the common type of <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> in children and teens was type 1. It was called juvenile <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>. With Type 1 <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>, the pancreas does not make insulin. Insulin is a hormone that helps glucose,or sugar, get into your cells to give them energy. Without insulin, too much sugar stays in the blood.</p><p>Now younger people are also getting type 2 <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>.  Type 2 <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> used to be called adult-onset <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>. But now it is becoming more common in children and teens, due to more obesity. With Type 2 <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>, the body does not make or use insulin well.</p><p>Children have a higher risk of type 2 <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> if they  are overweight or have obesity, have a family history of <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>, or are not active. Children who are African American, Hispanic, Native American/Alaska Native, Asian American, or Pacific Islander also have a higher risk. To lower the risk of type 2 <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> in children:</p><ul><li>Have them maintain a healthy weight</li><li>Be sure they are physically active</li><li>Have them eat smaller portions of healthy foods</li><li>Limit time with the TV, computer, and video</li></ul><p>Children and teens with type 1 <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> may need to take insulin. Type 2 <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> may be  controlled with diet and exercise. If not, patients will need to take oral <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> medicines or insulin. A blood test called the A1C can check on how you are managing your <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>.</p></content>

    <document rank="5" url="https://medlineplus.gov/howtopreventdiabetes.html">
      <content name="title">How to Prevent <span class="qt0">Diabetes</span></content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle"><span class="qt0">Diabetes</span> Prevention</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>?<p><span class="qt0">Diabetes</span>, also called <span class="qt0">diabetes mellitus</span>, is a disease that causes your blood glucose, or blood sugar, levels to be too high. There are three types of <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>: type 1 <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>, gestational <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>, and type 2 <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>. Type 2 <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> is the most common type and may be prevented, delayed, or managed by making lifestyle changes.</p>What is type 2 <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>?<p>If you have type 2 <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>, your body doesn't make enough insulin or doesn't use it well. This is called insulin resistance. Insulin is a hormone that helps glucose get into your cells. Glucose is your cells' main source of energy and comes from the foods you eat. If your body is resistant to insulin, then insulin can't get enough glucose into your cells. This then leads to glucose staying in your blood, leading to high blood glucose levels.</p><p>Over time, having too much glucose in your blood can cause health problems. If you are at risk for type 2 <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>, you might be able to prevent or delay developing it.</p>Who is at risk for type 2 <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>?<p>Many people are at risk for type 2 <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>. Your chances of getting it depend on a combination of risk factors such as your genes and lifestyle. The risk factors include:</p><ul><li>Having prediabetes, which means you have blood glucose levels that are high for you, but not high enough to be called <span class="qt0">diabetes</span></li><li>Being overweight or having obesity</li><li>Being age 35 or older</li><li>A family history of <span class="qt0">diabetes</span></li><li>Being African American, Alaska Native, American Indian, Asian American, Hispanic/Latino, Native Hawaiian, or Pacific Islander</li><li>Having high blood pressure</li><li>Having a low level of HDL (good) cholesterol or a high level of triglycerides</li><li>A history of <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> in pregnancy</li><li>Having given birth to a baby weighing 9 pounds or more</li><li>An inactive lifestyle</li><li>A history of heart disease or stroke</li><li>Having depression</li><li>Having polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS)</li><li>Having acanthosis nigricans, a skin condition in which your skin becomes dark and thick, especially around your neck or armpits</li><li>Smoking</li></ul>How can I prevent or delay getting type 2 <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>?<p>Most of the things that you need to do to prevent or delay getting type 2 <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> involve having a healthier lifestyle. Lifestyle changes include:</p><ul><li>Lose weight.Weight control is an important part of <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> prevention. You may be able to prevent or delay <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> by losing 5 to 7% of your current weight. For example, if you weigh 200 pounds, your goal would be to lose between 10 to 14 pounds. And once you lose the weight, it is important that you don't gain it back.</li><li>Follow a healthy eating plan. It is important to reduce the amount of calories you eat and drink each day, so you can lose weight and keep it off. To do that, your diet should include smaller portions and less fat and sugar. You should also eat a variety of foods from each food group, including plenty of whole grains, fruits, and vegetables. It's also a good idea to limit red meat and avoid processed meats.</li><li>Get regular exercise. Being physically active has many health benefits, including helping you to lose weight and lower your blood glucose levels. These both lower your risk of type 2 <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>. Try to get at least 30 minutes of physical activity on most days of the week. The amount of exercise you need depends on your age and health. Even small amounts of exercise can be helpful. If you have not been active, talk with your health care provider to figure out which types of exercise are best for you. You can start slowly and work up to your goal.</li><li>Don't smoke. Smoking can contribute to insulin resistance, which can lead to type 2 <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>. If you already smoke, try to quit.</li><li>Talk to your provider to see whether there is anything else you can do to delay or to prevent type 2 <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>. If you are at high risk, your provider may suggest that you take one of a few types of <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> medicines.</li></ul><p>If you make these changes, you will get other health benefits as well. You may lower your risk of other diseases, and you will probably feel better and have more energy.</p><p>NIH: National Institute of <span class="qt0">Diabetes</span> and Digestive and Kidney Diseases</p></content>

    <document rank="6" url="https://medlineplus.gov/diabetescomplications.html">
      <content name="title"><span class="qt0">Diabetes</span> Complications</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Diabetic complications</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>?<p>If you have <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>, your blood glucose, or blood sugar, levels are too high. Glucose comes from the foods you eat. A hormone called insulin helps the glucose get into your cells to give them energy.</p><p> With type 1 <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>, your body does not make insulin. With type 2 <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>, your body does not make or use insulin well. Without enough insulin, the glucose stays in your blood.</p>What health problems can <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> cause?<p>Over time, having too much glucose in your blood can cause complications, including:</p><ul><li>Eye disease, due to changes in fluid levels, swelling in the tissues, and damage to the blood vessels in the eyes.</li><li>Foot problems, caused by damage to the nerves and reduced blood flow to your feet.</li><li>Gum disease and other dental problems, because a high amount of glucose in your saliva helps harmful bacteria grow in your mouth. The bacteria combine with food to form a soft, sticky film called plaque. Plaque also comes from eating foods that contain sugars or starches. Some types of plaque cause gum disease and bad breath. Other types cause tooth decay and cavities.</li><li>Heart disease and stroke, caused by damage to your blood vessels and the nerves that control your heart and blood vessels.</li><li>Kidney disease, due to damage to the blood vessels in your kidneys. Many people with <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> develop high blood pressure. That can also damage your kidneys.</li><li>Nerve problems (diabetic neuropathy), caused by damage to the nerves and the small blood vessels that nourish your nerves with oxygen and nutrients.</li><li>Sexual and bladder problems, caused by damage to the nerves and reduced blood flow in the genitals and bladder.</li><li>Skin conditions, some of which are caused by changes in the small blood vessels and reduced circulation. People with <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> are also more likely to have infections, including skin infections.</li></ul>What other problems can people with <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> have?<p>If you have <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>, you need to watch out for blood glucose levels that are too high (hyperglycemia) or too low for you (hypoglycemia). These can happen quickly and can become dangerous. Some of the causes include having another illness or infection and certain medicines. They can also happen if you don't get the right amount of <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> medicines. To try to prevent these problems, make sure to take your <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> medicines correctly, follow your diabetic diet, and check your blood glucose regularly.</p><p>NIH: National Institute of <span class="qt0">Diabetes</span> and Digestive and Kidney Diseases</p></content>

    <document rank="7" url="https://medlineplus.gov/diabetesinsipidus.html">
      <content name="title"><span class="qt0">Diabetes</span> Insipidus</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">DI</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p><span class="qt0">Diabetes</span> insipidus (DI) causes frequent urination. You become extremely thirsty, so you drink. Then you urinate. This cycle can keep you from sleeping or even make you wet the bed. Your body produces lots of urine that is almost all water.</p><p>DI is different from <span class="qt0">diabetes mellitus</span> (DM), which involves insulin problems and high blood sugar. The symptoms can be similar. However, DI is related to how your kidneys handle fluids. It's much less common than DM. Urine and blood tests can show which one you have.</p><p>Usually, DI is caused by a problem with your pituitary gland or your kidneys. Treatment depends on the cause of the problem.  Medicines can often help.</p><p>NIH: National Institute of <span class="qt0">Diabetes</span> and Digestive and Kidney Diseases</p></content>

    <document rank="8" url="https://medlineplus.gov/diabetesmedicines.html">
      <content name="title"><span class="qt0">Diabetes</span> Medicines</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Hypoglycemic Medicines</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Insulin</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>?<p><span class="qt0">Diabetes</span> is a disease in which your blood glucose, or blood sugar, levels are too high. Glucose comes from the foods you eat. The cells of your body need glucose for energy. A hormone called insulin helps the glucose get into your cells.</p>type 1 diabetestype 2 <span class="qt0">diabetes</span><p></p>What are the treatments for <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>?<p>Treatments for <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> can depend on the type. Common treatments include a diabetic meal plan, regular physical activity, and medicines. Some less common treatments are weight loss surgery for either type and an artificial pancreas or pancreatic islet transplantation for some people with type 1 <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>.</p>Who needs <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> medicines?<p>People with type 1 <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> need to take a <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> medicine called insulin to control their blood sugar.</p><p>Some people with type 2 <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> can control their blood sugar with healthy food choices and physical activity. But for others, a diabetic meal plan and physical activity are not enough. They need to take <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> medicines.</p><p>The kind of medicine you take depends on your type of <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>, daily schedule, medicine costs, and any other health conditions that you have. Over time, you may need to take more than one <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> medicine.</p>What are the types of medicines for type 1 <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>?<p>If you have type 1 <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>, you must take insulin because your body no longer makes it. There are different types of insulin that start to work at different speeds, and the effects of each last a different length of time. Your health care provider will measure your blood glucose to decide on the type of insulin. You may need to use more than one type.</p><p>You will also need to check your blood sugar at home.  Your provider will tell you how often. The results of your blood sugar testing can help you make decisions about food, physical activity, and medicines.</p><p>You can take insulin several different ways. The most common are with a needle and syringe, an insulin pen, or an insulin pump. If you use a needle and syringe or a pen, you have to take insulin several times during the day, including with meals. An insulin pump gives you small, steady doses throughout the day. Less common ways to take insulin include inhalers, injection ports, and jet injectors.</p><p>In rare cases, taking insulin alone might not be enough to manage your blood sugar. Then you would need to take another <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> medicine.</p>What are the types of medicines for type 2 <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>?<p>There are several different medicines for type 2 <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>. Each works in a different way. Many of them are pills. There are also medicines that you inject under your skin, such as insulin.</p><p>Over time, you may need more than one <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> medicine to manage your blood sugar. You might add another <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> medicine or switch to a combination medicine.  A combination medicine contains more than one type of <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> medicine in the same pill. Some people with type 2 <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> take both pills and injections.</p><p>Even if you don't usually take insulin, you may need it at special times, such as during pregnancy or if you are in the hospital.</p>What else should I know about taking medicines for <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>?<p>Even if you take medicines for <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>, you still need to eat a healthy diet, stop smoking, take your other medicines, and get regular physical activity. These will help you manage your <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>.</p><p>It is important to make sure that you understand your <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> treatment plan. Talk to your provider about:</p><ul><li>What your target blood sugar level is</li><li>What to do if your blood sugar gets too low or too high</li><li>Whether your <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> medicines will affect other medicines you take</li><li>If you will have any side effects from the <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> medicines</li></ul><p>You should not change or stop your <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> medicines on your own. Talk to your provider first.</p><p>NIH: National Institute of <span class="qt0">Diabetes</span> and Digestive and Kidney Diseases</p></content>

    <document rank="9" url="https://medlineplus.gov/a1c.html">
      <content name="title">A1C</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Hemoglobin A1c</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Glycohemoglobin</content>
      <content name="altTitle">HbA1C</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Hemoglobin A1C test</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>A1C is a blood test for type 2 <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> and prediabetes. It measures your average blood glucose, or blood sugar, level over the past 3 months. Doctors may use the A1C alone or in combination with other <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> tests to make a diagnosis. They also use the A1C to see how well you are managing your <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>. This test is different from the blood sugar checks that people with <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> do every day.</p><p>Your A1C test result is given in percentages. The higher the percentage, the higher your blood sugar levels have been:</p><ul><li>A normal A1C level is below 5.7%</li><li>Prediabetes is between 5.7 to 6.4%. Having prediabetes is a risk factor for getting type 2 <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>. People with prediabetes may need retests every year.</li><li>Type 2 <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> is above 6.5%</li><li>If you have <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>, you should have the A1C test at least twice a year. The A1C goal for many people with <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> is below 7. It may be different for you. Ask what your goal should be. If your A1C result is too high, you may need to change your <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> care plan.</li></ul><p>NIH: National Institute of <span class="qt0">Diabetes</span> and Digestive and Kidney Diseases</p></content>
      <content name="mesh">Glycated Hemoglobin</content>
      <content name="groupName">Diagnostic Tests</content>
      <content name="groupName"><span class="qt0">Diabetes Mellitus</span></content>

    <document rank="10" url="https://medlineplus.gov/bloodglucose.html">
      <content name="title">Blood Glucose</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Blood Sugar</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Continuous Glucose Monitoring</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Glucose</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is blood glucose?<p>Blood glucose, or blood sugar, is the main sugar found in your blood. It is your body's primary source of energy. It comes from the food you eat. Your body breaks down most of that food into glucose and releases it into your bloodstream. When your blood glucose goes up, it signals your pancreas to release insulin. Insulin is a hormone that helps the glucose get into your cells to be used for energy.</p>What is <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>?<p><span class="qt0">Diabetes</span> is a disease in which your blood glucose levels are too high. When you have <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>, your body doesn't make enough insulin, can't use it as well as it should, or both. Too much glucose stays in your blood and doesn't reach your cells. Over time, having too much glucose in your blood can cause serious health problems (<span class="qt0">diabetes</span> complications). So if you have <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>, it's important to keep your blood glucose levels within your target range.

</p>What are blood glucose targets?<p>If you have <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>, your blood glucose target is the range you try to reach as much as possible. The typical targets are:</p><ul><li>Before a meal: 80 to 130 mg/dL</li><li>Two hours after the start of a meal: Less than 180 mg/dL</li></ul><p>Your blood glucose targets may be different, depending on your age, any additional health problems you have, and other factors. Talk with your health care team  about the best target range for you.</p>When and how should I check my blood glucose?<p>If you have <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>, you'll likely need to check your blood glucose every day to make sure that your blood glucose numbers are in your target range. Some people may need to check their blood glucose several times a day. Ask your health care team how often you need to check it.</p><p>The most common way to check your blood glucose level at home is with a blood glucose meter. A blood glucose meter measures the amount of glucose in a small sample of blood, usually from your fingertip.</p><p>Continuous glucose monitoring (CGM) is another way to check your glucose levels. Most CGM systems use a tiny sensor that is inserted under your skin. The sensor measures your glucose level every few minutes. It can show changes in your glucose level throughout the day and night. A CGM system is especially useful for people who take insulin and have problems with low blood glucose.</p><p>Your provider will also check your blood glucose with a blood test called an A1C.  It checks your average blood glucose level over the past three months. People with <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> usually have an A1C test at least twice a year. But you may need the test more often if you aren't meeting your <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> treatment goals.</p>What happens if my blood glucose level becomes too high?<p>High blood glucose is called hyperglycemia. Symptoms that your blood glucose levels may be too high include:</p><ul><li>Feeling thirsty</li><li>Feeling tired or weak</li><li>Headaches</li><li>Urinating (peeing) often</li><li>Blurred vision</li></ul><p>If you often have high blood glucose levels or symptoms of high blood glucose, talk with your health care team. You may need a change in your <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> meal plan, physical activity plan, or <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> medicines.</p><p>High blood glucose may also be caused by other conditions that can affect insulin or glucose levels in your blood. These conditions include problems with your pancreas or adrenal glands.</p>What happens if my blood glucose level becomes low for me?<p>Hypoglycemia, also called low blood glucose, happens when your blood glucose level drops below what is healthy for you. For many people with <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>, this means a blood glucose reading lower than 70 mg/dL. Your number might be different, so check with your health care team to find out what blood glucose level is low for you.</p><p>Symptoms of low blood glucose tend to come on quickly. The symptoms can be different for everyone, but they may include:</p><ul><li>Shaking</li><li>Sweating</li><li>Nervousness or anxiety</li><li>Irritability or confusion</li><li>Dizziness</li><li>Hunger</li></ul><p>Low blood glucose levels can be common in people with type 1 <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> and people with type 2 <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> who take certain <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> medicines. If you think you may have low blood glucose, check your level, even if you don't have symptoms. Low blood glucose can be dangerous and should be treated as soon as possible.</p><p>Although it's rare, you can still get low blood glucose without having <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>. The causes can include conditions such as liver disease, kidney disease, and hormone deficiencies (lack of certain hormones). Some medicines, such as certain heart medicines and antibiotics, can also cause it. See your provider to find out the cause of your low blood glucose and how to treat it.</p><p>NIH: National Institute of <span class="qt0">Diabetes</span> and Digestive and Kidney Diseases</p></content>

    <document rank="11" url="https://medlineplus.gov/diabeticdiet.html">
      <content name="title">Diabetic Diet</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Diabetic meal plan</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>?<p>If you have <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>, your blood glucose, or blood sugar, levels are too high. Glucose comes from foods you eat. The cells of your body need glucose for energy. A hormone called insulin helps glucose get into your cells.</p><p>With type 1 <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>, your body doesn't make insulin. With type 2 <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>, your body doesn't make or use insulin well. Without enough insulin, glucose builds up in your blood and causes high blood glucose levels.</p><p>Prediabetes means that your blood glucose levels are higher than normal but not high enough to be called <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>. If you have prediabetes, you are more likely to develop type 2 <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>.</p>How do the foods I eat affect my blood glucose levels?<p>The glucose in your blood comes from certain foods called carbohydrates, or "carbs." Foods that are high in carbs include candy and sweets, sodas, breads, tortillas, and white rice. The more carbs you eat, the higher your blood glucose level will be. Some, but not all people with <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>, may need to count the amount of carbs eaten in drinks and meals.</p><p>Whether you have type 1 or type 2 <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>, making the right food choices is an important way to keep your blood glucose at a level that is healthy for you. When you control your blood glucose, you lower your chance of having serious health problems from <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>, such as vision loss and heart problems.</p><p>And if you have prediabetes or are at risk for <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>, eating foods that keep your blood glucose levels healthy may help prevent type 2 <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> later on.</p>What's the best diet for <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>?<p>There isn't a specific diet or meal plan that works for everybody. Your health care provider may have you see a registered dietician (RD) or a <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> educator who can help design the best eating plan for you. The plan will consider:</p><ul><li>Any medicines that you take</li><li>Your weight</li><li>Any other health conditions you have</li><li>Your lifestyle and tastes</li><li>Your goals</li></ul><p>All eating plans for <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> have a few things in common, including eating the right foods in the right amounts at the right times.</p>What foods should I eat if I have <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>?<p>Eating the right foods for <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> means eating a variety of healthy foods from all the food groups:</p><ul><li>Fruits and vegetables</li><li>Whole grains, such as whole wheat, brown rice, barley, quinoa, and oats</li><li>Proteins, such as lean meats, chicken, turkey, fish, eggs, nuts, beans, lentils, and tofu</li><li>Nonfat or low-fat dairy, such as milk, yogurt, and cheese</li></ul>What foods should I limit to control my blood glucose?<p>To keep your blood glucose under control, you may need to cut back on certain foods and drinks. This doesn't mean that you can never enjoy them. But you should have them less often or in smaller amounts.</p><p>The foods and drinks you should limit include:</p><ul><li>High-carb foods and drinks, such as:
	<ul><li>Sugary foods, such as candy, cookies, cake, ice cream, sweetened cereals, and canned fruits with added sugar</li><li>Drinks with added sugars, such as juice, regular soda, and regular sports or energy drinks</li><li>White rice, tortillas, breads, and pasta - especially those made with white flour</li><li>Starchy vegetables, such as white potatoes, corn, and peas</li></ul></li><li>Fried foods and other foods high in saturated and trans fats.</li><li>Foods high in sodium (salt).</li><li>Alcohol. If you do drink, you should drink moderately. This means no more than one standard drink a day if you're a woman or two standard drinks a day if you're a man.</li></ul>What else do I need to know about diabetic diets?<p>If you have <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>, it's important to eat the right amount of food every day. Your eating plan will include how much to eat, and help you choose the types of food for each meal or snack. If you take insulin, you may need to learn how to count carbs to help you know how much insulin to take.</p><p>Eating at the right times is also important. You will want to plan for regular, balanced meals to avoid blood glucose levels that are too high or too low for you. Eating about the same amount of carbs at each meal can be helpful.</p><p>Your eating plan will also teach you how to stick with your plan at home and when you eat out.</p><p>Eating healthy to control your blood glucose does take some effort. But the reward is a chance to live your healthiest life with <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>.</p><p>NIH: National Institute of <span class="qt0">Diabetes</span> and Digestive and Kidney Diseases</p></content>

    <document rank="12" url="https://medlineplus.gov/diabeticeyeproblems.html">
      <content name="title">Diabetic Eye Problems</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Diabetic Retinopathy</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>?<p><span class="qt0">Diabetes</span> is a disease in which your blood glucose, or blood sugar, levels are too high. Glucose comes from foods you eat. The cells of your body need glucose for energy. A hormone called insulin helps the glucose get into your cells.</p><p>With type 1 <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>, your body doesn't make insulin. With type 2 <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>, your body doesn't make or use insulin well. Without enough insulin, glucose builds up in your blood and causes high blood glucose levels.</p>What eye problems can <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> cause?<p>Over time, high blood glucose may damage the blood vessels and lenses in your eyes. This can lead to serious diabetic eye problems which can harm your vision and sometimes cause blindness. Some common diabetic eye problems include:</p><ul><li>Diabetic retinopathy, which is the leading cause of blindness in American adults. It affects blood vessels in the retina (the light-sensitive layer of tissue in the back of your eye). The blood vessels may swell and leak fluid into your eye. If it's not treated, it can cause serious problems such as vision loss and retinal detachment, where the retina is pulled away from its normal position at the back of your eye.</li><li>Diabetic macular edema (DME), which happens when blood vessels in the retina leak fluid into the macula (the part of the retina needed for sharp, central vision). This usually develops in people who already have other signs of diabetic retinopathy.</li><li>Glaucoma, a group of eye diseases that can damage the optic nerve (the bundle of nerves that connects the eye to the brain). Glaucoma from <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> happens when the blood vessels in the front of your eye are damaged, and new blood vessels grow near the iris (the colored part of your eye). The blood vessels block the space where fluid drains from your eye. This causes fluid to build up and pressure to increase inside your eye.</li><li>Cataract, which is the leading cause of blindness worldwide. It happens when the clear lens in the front of your eye becomes cloudy. Cataracts are common as people age. But people with <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> are more likely to develop cataracts younger and faster than people without <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>. Researchers think that high glucose levels cause deposits to build up in the lenses of your eyes.</li></ul>Who is more likely to develop diabetic eye problems?<p>Anyone with <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> can develop diabetic eye disease. But your risk of developing it is higher if you have <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> and:</p><ul><li>Have had <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> for a long time</li><li>Don't have good control over your high blood glucose or high blood pressure</li><li>Are pregnant</li><li>Have high blood cholesterol</li><li>Smoke tobacco</li></ul>What are the symptoms of diabetic eye problems?<p>In the early stages, diabetic eye problems usually don't have any symptoms. That's why regular dilated eye exams are so important, even if you think your eyes are healthy.</p><p>You should also watch for sudden changes in your vision that could be signs of an emergency. Call your eye care professional right away if you notice any of these symptoms:</p><ul><li>Many new spots or dark wavy strings floating in your vision (floaters)</li><li>Flashes of light</li><li>A dark shadow over part of your vision, like a curtain</li><li>Vision loss</li><li>Eye pain or redness</li></ul><p>Talk with your eye care professional if you have these symptoms, even if they come and go:</p><ul><li>Spots or dark wavy strings floating in your vision</li><li>Blurry or wavy vision</li><li>Vision that changes a lot</li><li>Trouble seeing colors</li></ul>How are diabetic eye problems diagnosed?<p>Eye care professionals do dilated eye exams to diagnose eye problems. A dilated eye exam uses eye drops to open your pupils wide so your eye care professional can look for signs of eye problems and treat them before they harm your vision. They will also test your vision and measure the pressure in your eyes.</p><p>

</p>What are the treatments for diabetic eye problems?<p>Treatment for diabetic eye problems depends on the problem and how serious it is. Some of the treatments include:</p><ul><li>Lasers to stop blood vessels from leaking</li><li>Injections (shots) in the eye to stop new, leaky blood vessels from growing</li><li>Surgery to remove blood and scar tissue or replace a cloudy lens</li><li>Eye drops to lower fluid pressure in the eye</li></ul><p>But these treatments aren't cures. Eye problems can come back. That's why your best defense against serious vision loss is to take control of your <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> and get regular eye exams. It's also important to keep your blood pressure and cholesterol in a healthy range.</p><p>NIH: National Institute of <span class="qt0">Diabetes</span> and Digestive and Kidney Diseases</p></content>

    <document rank="13" url="https://medlineplus.gov/diabeticfoot.html">
      <content name="title">Diabetic Foot</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>?<p><span class="qt0">Diabetes</span> is a disease in which your blood glucose, or blood sugar, levels are too high. Glucose comes from foods you eat. The cells of your body need glucose for energy. A hormone called insulin helps the glucose get into your cells.</p><p>With type 1 <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>, your body doesn't make insulin. With type 2 <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>, your body doesn't make or use insulin well. Without enough insulin, glucose can't get into your cells as quickly as usual. The glucose builds up in your blood and causes high blood sugar levels.</p>How does <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> cause foot problems?<p>Foot problems are common in people with <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>. They can happen over time when high blood sugar damages the nerves and blood vessels in the feet. The nerve damage, called diabetic neuropathy, can cause numbness, tingling, pain, or a loss of feeling in your feet.</p><p>If you can't feel pain, you may not know when you have a cut, blister, or ulcer (open sore) on your foot. A wound like that could get infected. The infection may not heal well because the damaged blood vessels can cause poor blood flow in your feet.</p><p>Having an infection and poor blood flow can lead to gangrene. That means the muscle, skin, and other tissues start to die. If you have gangrene or a foot ulcer that does not get better with treatment, you may need an amputation. This is a surgery to cut off your damaged toe, foot, or part of your leg. It may prevent a bad infection from spreading and could save your life.</p><p>But there's a lot you can do to prevent a foot wound from becoming a major health problem.</p>How can I protect my feet if I have <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>?<p>The best way to protect your feet is by controlling your blood sugar levels every day. This will help keep nerve and blood vessel damage from getting worse. The next step is to keep the skin of your feet healthy.</p><p>Good foot care for people with <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> includes:</p><ul><li>Checking your feet every day. Look for cuts, redness, and other changes in the skin and toenails, including warts or other spots that your shoes could rub. Make sure to check the bottoms of your feet too.</li><li>Washing your feet every day. Use warm water and soap. Don't soak your feet because that can dry out your skin. After you dry your feet, you can use talcum powder or cornstarch between your toes. They soak up moisture that can cause infection. If you use lotion, don't apply it between your toes.</li><li>Asking your doctor how to remove corns and calluses safely. Thick skin on your feet can rub and lead to sores. But removing it the wrong way could damage your skin. So you don't want to cut the skin or use medicated pads or liquid removers.</li><li>Trimming your toenails straight across with a clipper. If it's hard for you to trim your own toenails, or if they're thick or curve into the skin, have a podiatrist (foot doctor) do it for you.</li><li>Always wearing well-fitting shoes and socks or slippers to protect your feet when walking. You don't want to walk barefoot, even indoors. And be sure your shoes are smooth inside. A seam or pebble could rub your skin raw.</li><li>Protecting your feet from heat and cold. Use sunscreen on exposed skin and don't walk barefoot at the beach. In cold weather, wear warm socks instead of warming your feet near a heater or fireplace.</li><li>Keeping the blood flowing in your feet. Put your feet up when you're sitting. Wiggle your toes and circle your feet throughout the day. Don't wear tight socks. And get plenty of activity that's not too hard on the feet, such as walking.</li><li>Getting your feet checked at your health care visits. Even if you haven't noticed a problem, it's good to have your health care provider look at your feet.</li></ul>When should I see my health care provider about diabetic foot problems?<p>Serious foot problems can develop quickly. See your health care provider right away if you notice:</p><ul><li>A cut, blister, or bruise on your feet that doesn't start to heal in a few days</li><li>Red, warm, or painful skin on your feet</li><li>A callus with dried blood inside</li><li>A foot infection that becomes black and smells bad that could be gangrene</li></ul><p>Remember, controlling your blood sugar and caring for your feet every day are the best steps you can take to prevent serious diabetic foot problems.</p><p>NIH: National Institute of <span class="qt0">Diabetes</span> and Digestive and Kidney Diseases</p></content>

    <document rank="14" url="https://medlineplus.gov/diabeticheartdisease.html">
      <content name="title">Diabetic Heart Disease</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>?<p><span class="qt0">Diabetes</span> is a disease in which your blood glucose, or blood sugar, levels are too high. Glucose comes from foods you eat. The cells of your body need glucose for energy. A hormone called insulin helps the glucose get into your cells.</p><p>With type 1 <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>, your body doesn't make insulin. With type 2 <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>, your body doesn't make or use insulin well. Without enough insulin, glucose builds up in your blood and causes high blood sugar levels.</p>What is diabetic heart disease?<p>Diabetic heart disease is a term for heart disease in people who have <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>. If you have <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>, you are much more likely to develop heart disease or have a stroke than people who don't have <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>. And you may start having these problems at a younger age.</p><p>Over time, the effects of high blood sugar from <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> can include damage to the blood vessels and nerves in your heart. This damage increases your chance of developing heart diseases, including:</p><ul><li>Coronary artery disease (CAD). It happens slowly as a sticky material called plaque builds up in the arteries that supply your heart muscle with blood.</li><li>Heart failure. With this condition, your heart can't pump enough oxygen-rich blood to meet the needs of your body.</li><li>Cardiomyopathy. This is a group of diseases in which the heart muscle may become thick or stiff.</li></ul>Who is more likely to develop diabetic heart disease?<p><span class="qt0">Diabetes</span> puts you at serious risk for heart disease. It also makes you more likely to develop other conditions that raise your risk even more, including:</p><ul><li>High blood pressure</li><li>High blood levels of LDL "bad" cholesterol</li><li>High triglycerides (a type of fat in your blood) with low blood levels of HDL "good" cholesterol</li></ul><p>Your risk for heart disease is also higher if you</p><ul><li>Are male</li><li>Smoke</li><li>Have obesity</li><li>Have too much belly fat around your waist, even though you're at a healthy weight:
	<ul><li>For men, that's a waist more than 40 inches</li><li>For women, that's a waist more than 35 inches</li></ul>
</li><li>Have a family history of heart disease</li><li>Have chronic kidney disease</li></ul>What are the symptoms of diabetic heart disease?<p>In the early stages, heart disease usually doesn't have any symptoms. But if your heart disease worsens, you can have symptoms. Your symptoms will depend on the type of heart disease you have. They might include:</p><ul><li>Shortness of breath</li><li>Fatigue</li><li>Dizziness or fainting</li><li>Arrhythmia (problem with the rate or rhythm of your heartbeat)</li><li>Swollen feet and ankles</li><li>Chest pain</li></ul><p>It's important to know that people with <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> may not feel chest pain. That's because <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> can damage the nerves in your heart. If you have any symptoms that could be heart disease, talk with your health care provider.</p>How is diabetic heart disease diagnosed?<p>To find out if you have diabetic heart disease, your provider will:</p><ul><li>Ask about your medical history, including your symptoms and other health conditions you may have</li><li>Ask about your family history, to find out if you have relatives who have or had heart disease</li><li>Do a physical exam</li><li>Likely run some tests to help understand your personal risk for heart disease, including:
	<ul><li>Blood tests to check your cholesterol, triglycerides, and blood sugar levels</li><li>A blood-pressure check</li><li>Heart tests, if needed</li></ul>
</li></ul><p>Depending on your risk level, your provider may send you to a cardiologist (a doctor who specializes in heart diseases) for care. If you do have heart disease, treatment will depend on the type of heart disease you have.</p>How can I prevent diabetic heart disease?<p>You may be able to prevent heart disease or keep it from getting worse by working with your provider to:</p><ul><li>Control your blood sugar levels</li><li>Manage other conditions you may have that can raise your risk for heart disease </li><li>Take any medicines your provider prescribed</li><li>Make heart-healthy habits part of your daily life</li><li>Follow a healthy eating plan for <span class="qt0">diabetes</span></li></ul><p>NIH: National Institute of <span class="qt0">Diabetes</span> and Digestive and Kidney Diseases</p></content>

    <document rank="15" url="https://medlineplus.gov/diabetickidneyproblems.html">
      <content name="title">Diabetic Kidney Problems</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Diabetic Nephropathy</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>If you have <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>, your blood glucose, or blood sugar, levels are too high.  Over time, this can damage your kidneys. Your kidneys clean your blood. If they are damaged, waste and fluids build up in your blood instead of leaving your body.</p><p>Kidney damage from <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> is called diabetic nephropathy. It begins long before you have symptoms. People with <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> should get regular screenings for kidney disease. Tests include a urine test to detect protein in your urine and a blood test to show how well your kidneys are working.</p><p>If the damage continues, your kidneys could fail. In fact, <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> is the most common cause of kidney failure in the United States. People with kidney failure need either dialysis or a kidney transplant.</p><p>You can slow down kidney damage or keep it from getting worse. Controlling your blood sugar and blood pressure, taking your medicines and not eating too much protein can help.</p><p>NIH: National Institute of <span class="qt0">Diabetes</span> and Digestive and Kidney Diseases</p></content>
      <content name="mesh">Diabetic Nephropathies</content>

    <document rank="16" url="https://medlineplus.gov/diabeticnerveproblems.html">
      <content name="title">Diabetic Nerve Problems</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Neuropathy</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Diabetic neuropathy</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>If you have <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>, your blood glucose, or blood sugar, levels are too high.  Over time, this can damage the covering on your nerves or the blood vessels that bring oxygen to your nerves. Damaged nerves may stop sending messages, or may send messages slowly or at the wrong times.  </p><p>This damage is called diabetic neuropathy.  Over half of people with <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> get it.  Symptoms may include:</p><ul><li>Numbness in your hands, legs, or feet</li><li>Shooting pains, burning, or tingling</li><li>Nausea, vomiting, constipation, or diarrhea</li><li>Problems with sexual function</li><li>Urinary problems</li><li>Dizziness when you change positions quickly</li></ul><p>Your doctor will diagnose diabetic neuropathy with a physical exam and nerve tests. Controlling your blood sugar can help prevent nerve problems, or keep them from getting worse. Treatment may include pain relief and other medicines.</p><p>NIH: National Institute of <span class="qt0">Diabetes</span> and Digestive and Kidney Diseases</p></content>

      <content name="snippet"> If you have <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>, your blood glucose, or blood sugar, levels are too high.  Over time, this can damage the covering on your nerves or the blood vessels  ... </content>

    <document rank="17" url="https://medlineplus.gov/hyperglycemia.html">
      <content name="title">Hyperglycemia</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">High Blood Glucose</content>
      <content name="altTitle">High Blood Sugar</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is blood glucose?<p>Blood glucose, or blood sugar, is the main sugar found in your blood. It is your body's primary source of energy. It comes from the food you eat. Your body breaks down most of that food into glucose and releases it into your bloodstream. When your blood glucose goes up, it signals your pancreas to release insulin. Insulin is a hormone that helps the glucose get into your cells to be used for energy.</p><p>For people with <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>, your body doesn't make enough insulin, can't use it as well as it should, or both. Too much glucose stays in your blood and doesn't reach your cells.</p>What is hyperglycemia?<p>Hyperglycemia means high blood glucose. It most often affects people who have <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>. When you have <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>, your body doesn't make enough insulin or can't use it the right way. Too much glucose stays in your blood and doesn't reach your cells.  </p>What causes hyperglycemia?<p>A key part of managing <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> is controlling your blood glucose levels. To do this, you need to follow a <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> meal plan and get regular physical activity. You might also need to take <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> medicines. You have to balance all of these to keep your blood glucose at the right levels. But if you eat too much food or the wrong foods, don't take your medicines correctly, or don't get physical activity, you can get hyperglycemia. It can also happen if you are stressed or sick.</p><p>Less commonly, people who don't have <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> can also get hyperglycemia. It can be caused by conditions that can affect insulin or glucose levels in your blood. They include problems with your pancreas or adrenal glands, certain medicines, and severe illnesses.</p>What are the symptoms of hyperglycemia?<p>The symptoms of hyperglycemia include:</p><ul><li>Feeling thirsty</li><li>Feeling tired or weak</li><li>Headaches</li><li>Urinating (peeing) often</li><li>Blurred vision</li></ul><p>If you are diabetic and you often have high blood glucose levels or the symptoms of hyperglycemia, talk with your health care team. You may need a change in your <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> meal plan, physical activity plan, or <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> medicines.</p><p>If you don't have <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> and you are having these symptoms, see your provider to find out the cause and how to treat it.</p>What other problems can hyperglycemia cause?<p>If hyperglycemia is not treated, it can cause other problems. In people with <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>, long-term hyperglycemia can lead to serious health problems (<span class="qt0">diabetes</span> complications).</p><p>If your blood glucose levels get very high, you can develop <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>-related ketoacidosis (DKA). It happens when your body doesn't have enough insulin to allow blood glucose into your cells for use as energy. Instead, your liver breaks down fat for fuel. This process produces acids called ketones. When too many ketones are produced too fast, they can build up to dangerous levels in your body. This can be life-threatening.</p><p>The symptoms of DKA may include:</p><ul><li>Trouble breathing</li><li>Nausea or vomiting</li><li>Pain in your abdomen (belly)</li><li>Confusion</li><li>Feeling very tired or sleepy</li></ul><p>If you have an an at-home test for ketones, check your ketone level every 4 to 6 hours when your blood glucose is very high or when you are having these symptoms. If the test shows that your ketones are moderate or high, or if you don't have a ketones test, contact your health care provider right away or get emergency medical help.</p>How is hyperglycemia diagnosed?<p>If you have <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>, you'll most likely need to check your blood glucose every day and make sure that it's not too high. You can do this with a blood glucose meter or continuous glucose monitoring (CGM) system.</p><p>There are also blood tests that providers can use to check if your blood glucose is too high.</p><p></p>What are the treatments for hyperglycemia?<p>If you have <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> and often have high blood glucose, your health care team may make changes to your <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> meal plan, physical activity plan, and/or <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> medicines.</p><p>If you have severe hyperglycemia and are having symptoms of DKA, you will need treatment at the hospital. The treatment often includes I.V. (intravenous) fluids and insulin.</p>Can hyperglycemia be prevented?<p>If you have <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>, managing your <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> can help prevent hyperglycemia. To manage your <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>, it's important to:</p><ul><li>Follow your <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> meal plan</li><li>Get regular physical activity</li><li>If you need <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> medicines, take them correctly</li><li>Regularly check your blood glucose level</li><li>Get regular checkups with your health care team</li></ul></content>
