    <document rank="18" url="https://medlineplus.gov/hypoglycemia.html">
      <content name="title">Hypoglycemia</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Low Blood Sugar</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is blood glucose?<p>Blood glucose, or blood sugar, is the main sugar found in your blood. It is your body's primary source of energy. It comes from the food you eat. Your body breaks down most of that food into glucose and releases it into your bloodstream. When your blood glucose goes up, it signals your pancreas to release insulin. Insulin is a hormone that helps the glucose get into your cells to be used for energy.</p><p>For people with <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>, your body doesn't make enough insulin, can't use it as well as it should, or both. Too much glucose stays in your blood and doesn't reach your cells.</p>What is hypoglycemia?<p>Hypoglycemia means low glucose. It happens when the level of glucose in your blood drops below what is healthy for you:</p><ul><li>For many people with <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>, this means a blood glucose level lower than 70 mg/dL. Your number might be different, so check with your health care team to find out what blood glucose level is too low for you.</li><li>For people who don't have <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>, hypoglycemia is typically a blood glucose level lower than 55 mg/dL.</li></ul>What causes hypoglycemia?<p>Hypoglycemia is common in people who have <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> type 1 or who have <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> type 2 and take insulin or other <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> medicines. It can happen:</p><ul><li>As a side effect of insulin or some other medicines that help your pancreas release insulin into your blood. These medicines can lower your blood glucose level.</li><li>If you don't eat or drink enough carbohydrates (carbs). Carbs are the main source of glucose for your body.</li><li>If you get a lot more physical activity than usual.</li><li>If you drink too much alcohol without enough food.</li><li>When you are sick and can't eat enough food or keep food down.</li></ul><p>Although it's rare, you can still get low blood glucose without having <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>. The causes can include conditions such as liver disease, kidney disease, and hormone deficiencies (lack of certain hormones). It can also happen in people who have had certain types of weight loss surgery. Some medicines, such as certain heart medicines and antibiotics, can also cause it. See your health care provider to find out the cause of your low blood glucose and how to treat it.</p>What are the symptoms of hypoglycemia?<p>The symptoms of low blood glucose tend to come on quickly. The symptoms can be different for everyone, but they may include:</p><ul><li>Shaking</li><li>Sweating</li><li>Nervousness or anxiety</li><li>Irritability or confusion</li><li>Dizziness</li><li>Hunger</li></ul>How is hypoglycemia diagnosed?<p>If you have <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>, you'll most likely need to check your blood glucose every day and make sure that it's not too low. You can do this with a blood glucose meter or continuous glucose monitoring (CGM) system.</p><p>There are also blood tests that providers can use to check if your blood glucose is too low.</p><p>If you don't have <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> and you have hypoglycemia, your provider will likely order other tests to try to figure out the cause.</p>What are the treatments for hypoglycemia?<p>If you have mild or moderate hypoglycemia, eating or drinking something with carbohydrates can help. But severe hypoglycemia can cause serious complications, including passing out, coma, or even death. Severe hypoglycemia can be treated with glucagon, a hormone that raises blood glucose levels. It can be given as nasal spray or injection. If you have <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>, your provider can prescribe you a glucagon kit for use in case of an emergency.</p><p>If you have <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> and you often have mild or moderate low blood glucose, your health care team may make changes to your <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> meal plan, physical activity plan, and/or <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> medicines.</p><p>If you don't have <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> and you keep having low blood glucose, the treatment will depend on what is causing it to happen.</p>Can hypoglycemia be prevented?<p>If you have <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> and you take insulin or other medicines that lower blood glucose, you can help prevent hypoglycemia if you:</p><ul><li>Follow your <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> meal plan. Eat and drink enough carbs to keep your blood glucose in your target range. Also carry a source of fast-acting carbohydrate, such as glucose tablets or a juice box, with you in case your blood glucose gets too low.</li><li>Be safe during physical activity. Check your blood glucose before and afterwards. You may need to eat a snack before your physical activity.</li><li>If you take <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> medicines, make sure to take them correctly.</li></ul></content>

    <document rank="19" url="https://medlineplus.gov/prediabetes.html">
      <content name="title">Prediabetes</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Insulin Resistance</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is prediabetes?<p>Prediabetes means that your blood glucose, or blood sugar, levels are higher than normal but not high enough to be called <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>. Glucose comes from the foods you eat. Too much glucose in your blood can damage your body over time.</p><p>If you have prediabetes, you are more likely to develop type 2 <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>, heart disease, and stroke. But if you make some lifestyle changes now, you may be able to delay or prevent type 2 <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>.</p>What causes prediabetes?<p>Prediabetes usually happens when your body has a problem with insulin. Insulin is a hormone that helps the glucose get into your cells to give them energy. A problem with insulin could be:</p><ul><li>Insulin resistance, a condition in which the body can't use its insulin properly. It makes it hard for your cells to get glucose from your blood. This can cause your blood sugar levels to rise.</li><li>Your body can't make enough insulin to keep your blood sugar levels at a healthy level</li></ul><p>Researchers think that being overweight and not getting regular physical activity are major factors in causing prediabetes.</p>Who is at risk for prediabetes?<p>About 1 out of every 3 adults has prediabetes. It is more common in people who:</p><ul><li>Are overweight or have obesity</li><li>Are age 45 or older</li><li>Have a parent, brother, or sister with <span class="qt0">diabetes</span></li><li>Are African American, Alaska Native, American Indian, Asian American, Hispanic/Latino, Native Hawaiian, or Pacific Islander American</li><li>Are not physically active</li><li>Have health conditions such as high blood pressure and high cholesterol</li><li>Have had gestational <span class="qt0">diabetes (diabetes</span> in pregnancy)</li><li>Have a history of heart disease or stroke</li><li>Have metabolic syndrome</li><li>Have polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS)</li></ul>What are the symptoms of prediabetes?<p>Most people don't know they have prediabetes because usually there are no symptoms.</p><p>Some people with prediabetes may have darkened skin in the armpit or on the back and sides of the neck. They may also have many small skin growths in those same areas.</p>How is prediabetes diagnosed?<p>There are a few different blood tests that can diagnose prediabetes. The most common ones are:</p><ul><li>Fasting plasma glucose (FPG) test, which measures your blood sugar at a single point in time. You need to fast (not eat or drink) for at least 8 hours before the test. The results of the test are given in mg/dL (milligrams per deciliter):
		<ul><li>A normal level is 99 or below</li><li>Prediabetes is 100 to 125</li><li>Type 2 <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> is 126 and above</li></ul>
</li><li>A1C test, which measures your average blood sugar over the past 3 months. The results of an A1C test are given as a percentage. The higher the percentage, the higher your blood sugar levels have been. 
		<ul><li>A normal level is below 5.7%</li><li>Prediabetes is between 5.7 to 6.4%</li><li>Type 2 <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> is above 6.5%</li></ul></li></ul>If I have prediabetes, will I get <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>?<p>If you have prediabetes, you may be able to delay or prevent type 2 <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> through lifestyle changes:</p><ul><li>Losing weight, if you are overweight</li><li>Getting regular physical activity</li><li>Following a healthy, reduced-calorie eating plan</li></ul><p>In some cases, your health care provider may also recommend taking <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> medicines.</p>Can prediabetes be prevented?<p>If you are at risk for prediabetes, those same lifestyle changes (losing weight, regular physical activity, and a healthy eating plan) may prevent you from getting it.</p><p>NIH: National Institute of <span class="qt0">Diabetes</span> and Digestive and Kidney Diseases</p></content>
      <content name="mesh">Prediabetic State</content>
      <content name="groupName">Endocrine System</content>
      <content name="groupName"><span class="qt0">Diabetes Mellitus</span></content>
      <content name="snippet">What is prediabetes? Prediabetes means that your blood glucose, or blood sugar, levels are higher than normal but not high enough to be called <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>.  ... </content>

    <document rank="20" url="https://medlineplus.gov/metabolicsyndrome.html">
      <content name="title">Metabolic Syndrome</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Insulin Resistance</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Syndrome X (Metabolic)</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Insulin resistance syndrome</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Metabolic syndrome X</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is metabolic syndrome?<p>Metabolic syndrome is the name for a group of risk factors for heart disease, <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>, and other health problems. You can have just one risk factor, but people often have several of them together. When you have at least three of them, it is called metabolic syndrome. These risk factors include:</p><ul><li>A large waistline, also called abdominal obesity or "having an apple shape." Too much fat around the stomach is a greater risk factor for heart disease than too much fat in other parts of the body.</li><li>Having a high triglyceride level. Triglycerides are a type of fat found in the blood.</li><li>Having a low HDL cholesterol level. HDL is sometimes called the "good" cholesterol because it helps remove cholesterol from your arteries.</li><li>Having high blood pressure. If your blood pressure stays high over time, it can damage your heart and lead to other health problems.</li><li>Having a high fasting blood sugar. Mildly high blood sugar may be an early sign of <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>.</li></ul><p>The more factors you have, the higher your risk for heart disease, <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>, and stroke is.</p>What causes metabolic syndrome?<p>Metabolic syndrome has several causes that act together:</p><ul><li>Overweight and obesity</li><li>An inactive lifestyle</li><li>Insulin resistance, a condition in which the body can't use insulin properly. Insulin is a hormone that helps move blood sugar into your cells to give them energy. Insulin resistance can lead to high blood sugar levels.</li><li>Age - your risk goes up as get older</li><li>Genetics - ethnicity and family history</li></ul><p>People who have metabolic syndrome often also have excessive blood clotting and inflammation throughout the body. Researchers don't know whether these conditions cause metabolic syndrome or worsen it.</p>Who is at risk for metabolic syndrome?<p>The most important risk factors for metabolic syndrome are:</p><ul><li>Abdominal obesity (a large waistline)</li><li>An inactive lifestyle</li><li>Insulin resistance</li></ul><p>There are certain groups of people who have an increased risk of metabolic syndrome:</p><ul><li>Some racial and ethnic groups. Mexican Americans have the highest rate of metabolic syndrome, followed by White and Black people.</li><li>People who have <span class="qt0">diabetes</span></li><li>People who have a sibling or parent who has <span class="qt0">diabetes</span></li><li>Women with polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS)</li><li>People who take medicines that cause weight gain or changes in blood pressure, blood cholesterol, and blood sugar levels</li></ul>What are the symptoms of metabolic syndrome?<p>Most of the metabolic risk factors have no obvious signs or symptoms, except for a large waistline.</p>How is metabolic syndrome diagnosed?<p>Your health care provider will diagnose metabolic syndrome based on the results of a physical exam and blood tests. You must have at least three of the risk factors to be diagnosed with metabolic syndrome:</p><ul><li>A large waistline, which means a waist measurement of 
		<ul><li>35 inches or more for women</li><li>40 inches or more for men</li></ul></li><li>A high triglyceride level, which is 150 mg/dL or higher</li><li>A low HDL cholesterol level, which is
		<ul><li>Less than 50 mg/dL for women</li><li>Less than 40 mg/dL for men</li></ul></li><li>High blood pressure, which is a reading of 130/85 mmHg or higher.</li><li>A high fasting blood sugar, which is 100 mg/dL or higher</li></ul>What are the treatments for metabolic syndrome?<p>The most important treatment for metabolic syndrome is a heart-healthy lifestyle, which includes:</p><ul><li>A heart-healthy eating plan, which limits the amount of saturated and trans fats that you eat. It encourages you to choose a variety of nutritious foods, including fruits, vegetables, whole grains, and lean meats.</li><li>Aiming for a healthy weight</li><li>Managing stress</li><li>Getting regular physical activity</li><li>Quitting smoking (or not starting if you don't already smoke)</li></ul><p>If making lifestyle changes is not enough, you may need to take medicines. For example, you may need medicines to lower cholesterol or blood pressure.</p>Can metabolic syndrome be prevented?<p>The best way to prevent metabolic syndrome is through the heart-healthy lifestyle changes.</p><p>NIH: National Heart, Lung, and Blood Institute</p></content>
      <content name="mesh">Metabolic Syndrome</content>

    <document rank="21" url="https://medlineplus.gov/howtopreventheartdisease.html">
      <content name="title">How to Prevent Heart Disease</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Heart Disease Prevention</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>Heart disease is the leading cause of the death in the United States. It is also a major cause of disability. But you can take steps to improve your health and help prevent heart disease. The first step is understanding your risk of heart disease. Your risk depends on many factors, some of which are changeable and others that are not. Learning about them and working on the things that you can change can lower your risk of heart disease.</p>What are the heart disease risk factors that I cannot change?<p>There are some risk factors for heart disease that you cannot change:</p><ul><li>Your age. Your risk of heart disease increases as you get older. The risk is higher in:
  <ul><li>Men age 45 and older</li><li>Women age 55 and older</li></ul>
</li><li>Your sex. Some risk factors may affect heart disease risk differently in women than in men. For example: 
  <ul><li>The hormone estrogen provides women some protection against heart disease</li><li><span class="qt0">Diabetes</span> raises the risk of heart disease more in women than in men.</li></ul>
</li><li>Your race or ethnicity. Certain groups have higher risks than others. For example:
  <ul><li>African Americans are more likely than Whites to die of heart disease, while Hispanic Americans are less likely to die of it</li><li>Asian Americans as a group have lower rates of heart disease than other groups, but South Asian Americans have higher rates</li></ul>
</li><li>Your family history. You have a greater risk if you have a close family member who had heart disease at an early age, for example if:
  <ul><li>Your father or brother was diagnosed before age 55</li><li>Your mother or sister was diagnosed before age 65</li></ul>
</li></ul>What are the heart disease risk factors I can change and what can I do to lower my risk?<p>There are many heart disease risk factors that you can change.  If you make these changes, you don't just help protect your heart. You can also improve your overall health and well-being.</p><p>You may have a lot of changes to make. If you need to, you can make the changes gradually, one at a time. What's most important is that you make them. Depending on your lifestyle, these changes could include:</p><ul><li>Controlling your blood pressure.High blood pressure is a major risk factor for heart disease. It is important to get your blood pressure checked regularly - at least once a year for most adults, and more often if you have high blood pressure. You can also take steps, including lifestyle changes, to prevent or control high blood pressure.</li><li>Keeping your cholesterol and triglyceride levels under control:<ul><li>High levels of cholesterol can clog your arteries and raise your risk of coronary artery disease and heart attack. Lifestyle changes and medicines (if needed) can lower your cholesterol.</li><li>Triglycerides are another type of fat in the blood. High  levels of triglyceride may also raise the risk of coronary artery disease, especially in women.</li></ul></li><li>Staying at a healthy weight. Being overweight or having obesity can increase your risk of heart disease. This is mostly because they are linked to other heart disease risk factors, including high blood cholesterol and triglyceride levels, high blood pressure, and <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>. Controlling your weight can lower these risks.</li><li>Eating a healthy diet. Try to limit saturated fats, foods high in sodium (salt), and added sugars. Instead, eat plenty of fresh fruit, vegetables, and whole grains. The DASH diet is an example of an eating plan that can help you to lower your blood pressure and cholesterol, two things that can lower your risk of heart disease.</li><li>Getting regular physical activity. Regular physical activity has many benefits, including strengthening your heart and improving your circulation. It can also help you maintain a healthy weight and lower cholesterol and blood pressure. All of these can lower your risk of heart disease.</li><li>Limiting alcohol. Drinking too much alcohol can raise your blood pressure. It also adds extra calories, which may cause weight gain. Both of those raise your risk of heart disease. It's best not to drink, but if you do:
  <ul><li>Have no more than 2 drinks per day if you are a man.</li><li>Have no more than 1 drink per day if you are a woman.</li></ul>
</li><li>Not smoking. Cigarette smoking raises your blood pressure and puts you at higher risk of heart attack and stroke. 
  <ul><li>If you do not smoke, don't start.</li><li> If you do smoke, quitting will lower your risk of heart disease. You can talk with your health care provider for help in finding the best way for you to quit.</li></ul>
</li><li>Managing stress.Stress is linked to heart disease in many ways. It can raise your blood pressure. Extreme stress can be a "trigger" for a heart attack. Also, some common ways of coping with stress, such as overeating, heavy drinking, and smoking, are bad for your heart. Some ways to help manage your stress include exercise, listening to music, focusing on something calm or peaceful, and meditating.</li><li>Managing <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>. Having <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> doubles your risk of diabetic heart disease. That is because over time, high blood glucose (blood sugar) from <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> can damage your blood vessels and the nerves that control your heart and blood vessels. So, it is important to get tested for <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>, and if you have it, to keep it under control.</li><li>Getting enough sleep. If you don't get enough sleep, you raise your risk of high blood pressure, obesity, and <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>. Those three things can raise your risk of heart disease. To improve your sleep:
  <ul><li>If you are an adult, try to get 7 to 9 hours of sleep per night.</li><li>Make sure that you have good sleep habits, such as keeping a regular sleep schedule and creating a good sleeping environment in your bedroom.</li><li> If you have frequent sleep problems, contact your health care provider. One problem, sleep apnea, causes people to briefly stop breathing many times during sleep. This interferes with your ability to get a good rest and can raise your risk of heart disease. If you think you might have sleep apnea, ask your provider about having a sleep study. And if you do have sleep apnea, make sure that you get treatment for it.</li></ul>
</li></ul></content>

    <document rank="22" url="https://medlineplus.gov/chronickidneydisease.html">
      <content name="title">Chronic Kidney Disease</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">CKD</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>You have two kidneys, each about the size of your fist.  Their main job is to filter your blood. They remove wastes and extra water, which become urine. They also keep the body's chemicals balanced, help control blood pressure, and make hormones.</p><p>Chronic kidney disease (CKD) means that your kidneys are damaged and can't filter blood as they should. This damage can cause wastes to build up in your body. It can also cause other problems that can harm your health. <span class="qt0">Diabetes</span> and high blood pressure are the most common causes of CKD.</p><p>The kidney damage occurs slowly over many years. Many people don't have any symptoms until their kidney disease is very advanced. Blood and urine tests are the only way to know if you have kidney disease.</p><p>Treatments cannot cure kidney disease, but they may slow kidney disease. They include medicines to lower blood pressure, control blood sugar, and lower cholesterol. CKD may still get worse over time. Sometimes it can lead to kidney failure. If your kidneys fail, you will need  dialysis or a kidney transplantation.</p><p>You can take steps to keep your kidneys healthier longer:</p><ul><li>Choose foods with less  salt (sodium)</li><li>Control your blood pressure; your health care provider can tell you what your blood pressure should be</li><li>Keep your blood sugar in the target range, if you have <span class="qt0">diabetes</span></li><li>Limit the amount of alcohol you drink</li><li>Choose foods that are healthy for your heart: fruits, vegetables, whole grains, and low-fat dairy foods</li><li>Lose weight if you are overweight</li><li>Be physically active</li><li>Don't smoke</li></ul><p>NIH: National Institute of <span class="qt0">Diabetes</span> and Digestive and Kidney Diseases</p></content>
      <content name="mesh">Renal Insufficiency, Chronic</content>
      <content name="groupName">Kidneys and Urinary System</content>

    <document rank="23" url="https://medlineplus.gov/eyecare.html">
      <content name="title">Eye Care</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Eye health</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Eye safety</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">Why does eye care matter?<p>Eye care is about keeping your eyes healthy through good daily habits, regular checkups, and treatment when needed. Your eyes are an important part of your health. You rely on them every day to see and understand the world around you. Some eye diseases can lead to vision loss without early warning signs, so it's important to catch them as soon as possible. Get your eyes checked as often as your health care provider recommends, or if you notice new vision problems. Just like caring for the rest of your body, it's important to keep your eyes healthy.</p>How can I keep my eyes healthy?<p>There are many things you can do to protect your eyes and see your best:</p><ul><li>Give your eyes a rest. Spending long hours on a computer or other digital screens can make you blink less, causing dryness and tired eyes. To reduce eyestrain, try the 20-20-20 rule: Every 20 minutes, look away about 20 feet in front of you for 20 seconds.</li><li>Wear sunglasses.Sun exposure can damage your eyes and raise your risk of cataracts and age-related macular degeneration. Protect your eyes by using sunglasses that block out 99 to 100% of both UV-A and UV-B radiation.</li><li>Wear protective eyewear. To prevent eye injuries, you need eye protection when playing certain sports, working in jobs such as factory work and construction, and doing repairs or projects in your home.</li><li>Avoid rubbing your eyes. Rubbing your eyes can transfer dirt and bacteria that may cause irritation or lead to an infection.</li><li>Use good lighting. Brighten your space to reduce strain and help you see comfortably.</li><li>If you wear contacts, take steps to prevent eye infections. Wash your hands before you put in or take out your contact lenses. Also follow the instructions on how to properly clean them and replace them when needed.</li></ul>How can my nutrition and lifestyle habits affect my eye health?<p>What you eat and how you take care of your body can have a big impact on your eyes and vision:</p><ul><li>Eat a healthy, balanced diet. Your diet should include plenty of fruits and vegetables, especially dark leafy green vegetables like spinach, kale, or collard greens. Eating fish high in omega-3 fatty acids, such as salmon, tuna, and halibut can also help your eyes.</li><li>Maintain a healthy weight. Being overweight or having obesity increases your risk of developing <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>. Having <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> puts you at higher risk of getting diabetic retinopathy or glaucoma.</li><li>Get regular exercise. Exercise may help to prevent or control <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>, high blood pressure, and high cholesterol. These diseases can lead to some eye or vision problems. Regular exercise helps lower your risk.</li><li>Avoid smoking. Smoking increases the risk of developing age-related eye diseases such as macular degeneration and cataracts and can damage the optic nerve.</li><li>Know your family medical history. Some eye diseases are inherited (passed down through families), so it is important to find out whether anyone in your family has had them. This can help you determine if you are at higher risk of developing an eye disease.</li><li>Know your other risk factors. As you get older, you are at higher risk of developing age-related eye diseases and conditions. It is important to know your risk factors because you may be able to lower your risk by changing some behaviors.</li></ul>What's the difference between an eye test and an eye exam?<p>Everyone needs their eyesight tested to check for vision and eye problems. Children usually have vision screening, also called an eye test, in school or at their provider's office during a checkup. This is a brief test that mainly checks how well you can see things up close and far away. Adults may also get vision screenings during their checkups. But many adults need more than a vision screening. They need a comprehensive dilated eye exam.</p><p>Getting comprehensive dilated eye exams is especially important because some eye diseases may not have warning signs. The exams are the only way to detect these diseases in their early stages, when they are easier to treat.</p><p>The eye exam includes several tests:</p><ul><li>Test your side (peripheral) vision with a visual field test. Losing peripheral vision may be a sign of glaucoma.</li><li>Check how well you see at various distances with a visual acuity test, where you read an eye chart about 20 feet away.</li><li>Follow an object with your eyes during an eye muscle function test to check for problems with the muscles that control your eyes.</li><li>Shine a light into your eyes with a pupil response test to see how your pupils react to light.</li><li>Measure the pressure inside your eyes with tonometry. This test helps to detect glaucoma.</li><li>Dilate (widen) your pupils with special eye drops allows more light to enter the eye. Your eye care provider then uses a special magnifying lens to clearly see important tissues at the back of your eye, including the retina, macula, and optic nerve.</li></ul><p>If you have a refractive error and are going to need glasses or contacts, then you will also have a refraction test. When you have this test, you look through a device that has lenses of different strengths to help your eye care professional figure out which lenses will give you the clearest vision.</p>When should I start getting eye exams?<p>How often you need eye exams depends on your age, race, risk factors, and overall health.  For example, Black/African American adults are at higher risk for glaucoma and may need to start exams earlier. If you have <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>, you should have an exam every year. Check with your provider about when you need these exams.</p><p>See an eye care specialist right away if you have symptoms like sudden loss of vision, double vision, blurred vision with eye pain, flashes of light, black spots or halos around lights. These symptoms may represent a serious medical or eye condition.</p></content>
      <content name="mesh">Eye Diseases</content>

    <document rank="24" url="https://medlineplus.gov/isletcelltransplantation.html">
      <content name="title">Islet Cell Transplantation</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>Islets are cells found in clusters throughout the pancreas. They are made up of several types of cells. One of these is beta cells, which make insulin. Insulin is a hormone that helps the body use glucose for energy. Islet cell transplantation transfers cells from an organ donor into the body of another person. It is an experimental treatment for type 1 <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>.</p><p>In type 1 <span class="qt0">diabetes</span>, the beta cells of the pancreas no longer make insulin. A person who has type 1 <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> must take insulin daily to live. Transplanted islet cells, however, can take over the work of the destroyed cells. The beta cells in these islets will begin to make and release insulin. Researchers hope islet transplantation will help people with type 1 <span class="qt0">diabetes</span> live without daily insulin injections.</p><p>NIH: National Institute of <span class="qt0">Diabetes</span> and Digestive and Kidney Diseases </p></content>

    <document rank="0" url="https://medlineplus.gov/highbloodpressure.html">
      <content name="title"><span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">High Blood Pressure</span></span></content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Blood Pressure</content>
      <content name="altTitle"><span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">Hypertension</span></span></content>
      <content name="altTitle">Benign essential <span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">hypertension</span></span></content>
      <content name="altTitle">Essential <span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">hypertension</span></span></content>
      <content name="altTitle">HBP</content>
      <content name="altTitle">HTN</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is blood pressure?<p>Blood pressure is the force of your blood pushing against the walls of your arteries. Each time your heart beats, it pumps blood into the arteries. Your <span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">blood pressure is highest</span></span> when your heart beats, pumping the blood. This is called systolic pressure. When your heart is at rest, between beats, your blood pressure falls. This is called diastolic pressure.</p><p>Your blood pressure reading uses these two numbers. Usually the systolic number comes before or above the diastolic number. For example, 120/80 means a systolic of 120 and a diastolic of 80.</p>How is <span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">high blood pressure</span></span> diagnosed?<p><span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">High blood pressure</span></span> usually has no symptoms. So the only way to find out if you have it is to get regular blood pressure checks from your health care provider. Your provider will use a gauge, a stethoscope or electronic sensor, and a blood pressure cuff. They will take two or more readings at separate appointments before making a diagnosis.</p>Blood Pressure CategorySystolic Blood PressureDiastolic Blood PressureNormalLess than 120andLess than 80Elevated120 - 129andLess than 80High Blood Pressure Stage 1130 - 139or80 - 89High Blood Pressure Stage 2140 or higheror90 or higherHypertensive Crisis (dangerously <span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">high blood pressure</span></span> - seek medical care right away)Higher than 180andHigher than 120<p>For children and teens, the health care provider compares the blood pressure reading to what is normal for other kids who are the same age, height, and sex.</p>What are the different types of <span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">high blood pressure</span></span>?<p>There are two main types of <span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">high blood pressure</span></span>:</p><ul><li>Primary, or essential, <span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">high blood pressure</span></span>. This is the most common type of <span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">high blood pressure</span></span>. For most people who get this kind of blood pressure, it develops over time as you get older.</li><li>Secondary <span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">high blood pressure</span></span>. This is caused by another medical condition or use of certain medicines. It usually gets better after you treat that condition or stop taking the medicines that are causing it.</li></ul>Why do I need to worry about <span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">high blood pressure</span></span>?<p>When your blood pressure stays high over time, it causes the heart to pump harder and work overtime, possibly leading to serious health problems such as heart attack, stroke, heart failure, and kidney failure.</p>What are the treatments for <span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">high blood pressure</span></span>?<p>Treatments for <span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">high blood pressure</span></span> include heart-healthy lifestyle changes and medicines.</p><p>You will work with your provider to come up with a treatment plan. It may include only the lifestyle changes. These changes, such as heart-healthy eating and exercise, can be very effective. But sometimes the changes do not control or lower your <span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">high blood pressure</span></span>. Then you may need to take medicine. There are different types of blood pressure medicines. Some people need to take more than one type.</p><p>If your <span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">high blood pressure</span></span> is caused by another medical condition or medicine, treating that condition or stopping the medicine may lower your blood pressure.</p><p>NIH: National Heart, Lung, and Blood Institute</p></content>

    <document rank="1" url="https://medlineplus.gov/pulmonaryhypertension.html">
      <content name="title">Pulmonary <span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">Hypertension</span></span></content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Cor Pulmonale</content>
      <content name="altTitle"><span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">Hypertension</span></span>, Pulmonary</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Primary Pulmonary <span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">Hypertension</span></span></content>
      <content name="altTitle">Pulmonary Arterial <span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">Hypertension</span></span></content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is pulmonary <span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">hypertension</span></span>? <p>Pulmonary <span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">hypertension</span></span>, sometimes called PH, is a serious condition that affects the blood vessels in the lungs. It develops when the blood pressure in your lungs is higher than normal.</p><p>Your heart pumps blood to your lungs so they can add oxygen to the blood. The blood goes back to your heart. From there, it travels to the rest of your body so that your tissues can get the oxygen that they need.</p><p>The blood moves from your heart to your lungs through blood vessels called pulmonary arteries. If the pulmonary arteries become damaged, narrowed, or blocked, the blood does not flow through them as well. This can increase the blood pressure in the arteries and cause pulmonary <span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">hypertension</span></span>.</p><p>There are different types of pulmonary <span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">hypertension</span></span>, including pulmonary arterial <span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">hypertension</span></span> (PAH). The different types are based on what caused the disease.</p>What causes pulmonary <span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">hypertension</span></span>?<p>Pulmonary <span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">hypertension</span></span> can develop on its own or be caused by another condition. Sometimes the cause is unknown or is not clear.</p><p>Some of the possible causes include:</p><ul><li>Heart diseases, including left-sided heart failure and congenital heart disease</li><li>Lung diseases such as COPD (chronic obstructive pulmonary disease), interstitial lung disease, emphysema, and sleep apnea</li><li>Other medical conditions such as:
  <ul><li>Liver diseases</li><li>Sickle cell disease</li><li>Pulmonary embolism (blood clots in the lungs)</li><li>Connective tissue disorders like scleroderma</li></ul>
</li></ul>Who is more likely to develop pulmonary <span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">hypertension</span></span>? <p>Certain factors can make you more likely to develop pulmonary <span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">hypertension</span></span>, such as:</p><ul><li>Your age. The risk increases as you get older. The condition is usually diagnosed between ages 30 and 60.</li><li>Your environment. Being exposed to asbestos or having certain infections caused by parasites can raise your risk.</li><li>Your family history and genetics. Certain genetic disorders, such as Down syndrome, congenital heart disease, and Gaucher disease, can raise your risk of pulmonary <span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">hypertension</span></span>. So can a family history of blood clots.</li><li>Your lifestyle habits.Smoking and illegal drug use can raise your risk of developing pulmonary <span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">hypertension</span></span>.</li><li>Certain medicines. For example, some medicines used to treat cancer and depression can make you more likely to develop pulmonary <span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">hypertension</span></span>.</li><li>Your sex. Pulmonary <span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">hypertension</span></span> is more common in women than in men.</li></ul>What are the symptoms of pulmonary <span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">hypertension</span></span>?<p>The symptoms of pulmonary <span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">hypertension</span></span> are sometimes hard to recognize and are similar to the symptoms of other medical conditions. So sometimes it can take years for someone to get diagnosed with pulmonary <span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">hypertension</span></span>.</p><p>The symptoms of pulmonary <span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">hypertension</span></span> may include:</p><ul><li>Shortness of breath</li><li>Chest pain or pressure</li><li>Dizziness that may lead to fainting</li><li>Fatigue</li><li>Swelling of the abdomen, legs, or feet</li><li>Heart palpitations (the feeling that your heart is pounding or beating too fast)</li></ul>What other problems can pulmonary <span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">hypertension</span></span> cause?<p>Pulmonary <span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">hypertension</span></span> can get worse over time and lead to serious problems, including:</p><ul><li>Anemia, which can cause your body to not get enough oxygen-rich blood</li><li>Arrhythmias, problems with the rate or rhythm of your heartbeat</li><li>Blood clots in the pulmonary arteries</li><li>Bleeding in the lungs</li><li>Heart failure</li><li>Liver damage</li><li>Pericardial effusion, a collection of fluid around the heart</li><li>Serious pregnancy complications</li></ul>How is pulmonary <span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">hypertension</span></span> diagnosed?<p>To find out if you have pulmonary <span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">hypertension</span></span>, your health care provider:</p><ul><li>Will ask about your medical history and symptoms</li><li>Will do a physical exam, which may include checking your blood oxygen, listening to your heart and lungs, and checking your blood pressure</li><li>Will likely order some tests, such as:
  <ul><li>Blood tests to look for blood clots, stress on the heart, or anemia</li><li>Heart imaging tests, such as a cardiac MRI</li><li>Lung imaging tests, such as chest x-ray</li><li>An electrocardiogram (ECG or EKG)</li></ul>
</li></ul>What are the treatments for pulmonary <span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">hypertension</span></span>?<p>Often there is no cure for pulmonary <span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">hypertension</span></span>, but treatments can help manage your symptoms. Your provider will work with you to come up with a treatment plan. It will be based on your needs and the cause of the pulmonary <span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">hypertension</span></span>. The plan may include:</p><ul><li>Healthy lifestyle changes, such as:
  <ul><li>Healthy eating, which includes eating less salt</li><li>Regular physical activity, which may be done through pulmonary rehabilitation</li></ul>
</li><li>Medicines, such as:
  <ul><li>Blood thinners</li><li>Medicines to control the rate blood is pumped throughout the body</li><li>Medicines to relax blood vessels and allow the blood to flow better</li><li>Medicine to reduce swelling (diuretics)</li></ul>
</li><li>Oxygen therapy</li><li>Procedures to reduce pressure in the heart or pulmonary artery</li><li>In some severe cases, a lung transplant</li></ul><p>NIH: National Heart, Lung, and Blood Institute</p></content>
      <content name="mesh"><span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">Hypertension</span></span>, Pulmonary</content>

Pulmonary <span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">hypertension</span></span>, sometimes called PH, is a serious condition that affects the blood vessels in the lungs. It develops  ... </content>

    <document rank="2" url="https://medlineplus.gov/howtopreventhighbloodpressure.html">
      <content name="title">How to Prevent <span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">High Blood Pressure</span></span></content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle"><span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">High Blood Pressure</span></span> Prevention</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Lowering <span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">high blood pressure</span></span></content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>Around half of American adults have <span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">high blood pressure (hypertension</span></span>). Many of those people don't know they have it because there are usually no warning signs. This can be dangerous, because <span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">high blood pressure</span></span> can lead to life-threatening conditions like heart attack or stroke. The good news is that you can often prevent or treat <span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">high blood pressure</span></span>. Early diagnosis and heart-healthy lifestyle changes can keep <span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">high blood pressure</span></span> from seriously damaging your health.</p>What is blood pressure?<p>Each time your heart beats, it pumps blood into your arteries (the blood vessels that carry blood from your heart to the tissues and organs in your body). Blood pressure is the force of your blood pushing against the walls of your arteries. Blood pressure is measured as two numbers:</p><ul><li>Systolic pressure (the first and higher number) measures pressure inside your arteries when your heart beats</li><li>Diastolic pressure (the second and lower number) measures the pressure inside your arteries when your heart rests between beats</li></ul><p>As an example, a blood pressure reading of 120/80 means a systolic of 120 and a diastolic of 80.</p>How is <span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">high blood pressure</span></span> diagnosed?<p><span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">High blood pressure</span></span> usually has no symptoms. So the only way to find out if you have it is to get regular blood pressure checks from your health care provider. Your provider will use a gauge, a stethoscope or electronic sensor, and a blood pressure cuff. They will take two or more readings at separate appointments before making a diagnosis:</p>Blood Pressure CategorySystolic Blood PressureDiastolic Blood PressureNormalLess than 120andLess than 80Elevated120 - 129andLess than 80High Blood Pressure Stage 1130 - 139or80 - 89High Blood Pressure Stage 2140 or higheror90 or higherHypertensive Crisis (dangerously <span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">high blood pressure</span></span> - seek medical care right away)Higher than 180andHigher than 120<p>For children and teens, the provider compares the blood pressure reading to what is normal for other kids who are the same age, height, and sex.</p>Who is more likely to develop <span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">high blood pressure</span></span>?<p>Anyone can develop <span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">high blood pressure</span></span>, but there are certain factors that can increase your risk:</p><ul><li>Age. Blood pressure tends to rise with age.</li><li>Race/Ethnicity. <span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">High blood pressure</span></span> is more common in Black adults. They also tend to have higher average blood pressure numbers and get <span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">high blood pressure</span></span> earlier in life.</li><li>Sex. Men are more likely than women to develop <span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">high blood pressure</span></span> throughout middle age. But in older adults, women are more likely than men to develop <span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">high blood pressure</span></span>.</li><li>Lifestyle. Certain lifestyle habits can raise your risk for <span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">high blood pressure</span></span>, such as eating too much sodium (salt) or not enough potassium, lack of exercise, drinking too much alcohol, and smoking.</li><li>Family history and genetics. <span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">High blood pressure</span></span> often runs in families. Many genes are linked to small increases in <span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">high blood pressure</span></span> risk.</li><li>Social and economic factors:<ul><li>Research shows that factors such as income, education level, where you live, and the type of job you have may raise your risk of <span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">high blood pressure</span></span>. For example, working early or late shifts can raise your risk.</li><li>Experiencing discrimination and poverty has been linked to <span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">high blood pressure</span></span>. Also, some research has shown that trauma in childhood may raise the risk of <span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">high blood pressure</span></span>.</li></ul></li><li>Certain medicines. Some medicines can raise your blood pressure, including:
  <ul><li>Certain antidepressants</li><li>Decongestants (medicines to relieve a stuffy nose)</li><li>Hormonal birth control pills</li><li>Non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs), which are a type of pain reliever</li></ul>
</li><li>Having certain other medical conditions, such as:
  <ul><li>Some tumors</li><li>Chronic kidney disease</li><li>Diabetes</li><li>Metabolic syndrome</li><li>Overweight and obesity</li><li>Sleep apnea</li><li>Thyroid problems</li></ul>
</li></ul>How can I prevent <span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">high blood pressure</span></span>?<p>You can help prevent <span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">high blood pressure</span></span> by having a healthy lifestyle. This means:</p><ul><li>Eating a healthy diet. To help manage your blood pressure, you should limit the amount of sodium (salt) that you eat and increase the amount of potassium in your diet. It is also important to eat foods that are lower in fat, as well as plenty of fruits, vegetables, and whole grains. The DASH eating plan is an example of an eating plan that can help you to lower your blood pressure.</li><li>Getting regular physical activity. Regular physical activity can help you maintain a healthy weight and lower your blood pressure.</li><li>Being at a healthy weight. Being overweight or having obesity increases your risk for <span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">high blood pressure</span></span>. Maintaining a healthy weight can help you control <span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">high blood pressure</span></span> and reduce your risk for other health problems.</li><li>Limiting alcohol. Drinking too much alcohol can raise your blood pressure. It also adds extra calories, which may cause weight gain.</li><li>Not smoking. Cigarette smoking raises your blood pressure and puts you at higher risk for heart attack and stroke. If you do not smoke, don't start. If you do smoke, talk to yourprovider for help in finding the best way for you to quit.</li><li>Managing stress. Learning how to relax and manage stress can improve your emotional and physical health and lower <span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">high blood pressure</span></span>. Stress management techniques include exercising, listening to music, focusing on something calm or peaceful, and meditating.</li><li>Getting enough high-quality sleep.</li></ul><p>If you already have <span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">high blood pressure</span></span>, it is important to prevent it from getting worse or causing complications. You need to get regular medical care and follow your treatment plan. Your plan will include healthy lifestyle habit recommendations and possibly medicines.</p><p>NIH: National Heart, Lung, and Blood Institute</p></content>

    <document rank="3" url="https://medlineplus.gov/highbloodpressureinpregnancy.html">
      <content name="title"><span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">High Blood Pressure</span></span> in Pregnancy</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Eclampsia</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Gestational <span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">Hypertension</span></span></content>
      <content name="altTitle">HELLP Syndrome</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Preeclampsia</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Toxemia of Pregnancy</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is <span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">high blood pressure</span></span> in pregnancy?<p>Blood pressure is the force of your blood pushing against the walls of your arteries. Your arteries are blood vessels that carry blood from your heart to other parts of your body. <span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">High blood pressure, or hypertension, is blood pressure that is higher</span></span> than normal. Having <span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">high blood pressure</span></span> can put you at risk for other health problems, such as heart disease, heart attack, and stroke.</p><p>During pregnancy, <span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">high blood pressure</span></span> can cause problems for you and your baby. To keep you and your baby healthy, it's important to get treatment for <span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">high blood pressure</span></span> before, during, and after pregnancy.</p>What are the types of <span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">high blood pressure</span></span> in pregnancy?<p>There are different types of <span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">high blood pressure</span></span> in pregnancy:</p><ul><li>Gestational <span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">hypertension is high blood pressure</span></span> that you develop while you are pregnant. It starts after you are 20 weeks pregnant. You usually don't have any other symptoms. In many cases, it does not harm you or your baby, and it goes away within 12 weeks after childbirth. But it does raise your risk of <span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">high blood pressure</span></span> in the future. If it becomes severe, it can lead to a preterm birth or your baby having a low birth weight. Some women with gestational <span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">hypertension</span></span> do go on to develop preeclampsia, a more serious type of <span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">high blood pressure</span></span> in pregnancy.</li><li>Chronic <span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">hypertension is high blood pressure</span></span> that starts before the 20th week of pregnancy or before you became pregnant. Some people may have had it long before becoming pregnant but didn't know it until they got their blood pressure checked at their prenatal visit. Sometimes chronic <span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">hypertension</span></span> can also lead to preeclampsia.</li><li>Preeclampsia is a sudden increase in blood pressure after the 20th week of pregnancy. It usually happens in the last trimester. Preeclampsia also often includes signs of damage to some of your organs, such as your liver or kidneys. The signs may include protein in the urine (pee) and very <span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">high blood pressure</span></span>. Preeclampsia can be serious or even life-threatening for both you and your baby.
  <ul><li>If preeclampsia becomes severe enough to affect your brain function and causes seizures or a coma, it is called eclampsia.</li><li>In rare cases, preeclampsia symptoms may not start until after delivery. This is called postpartum preeclampsia. If this type of preeclampsia becomes more severe and causes a seizure, it is known as postpartum eclampsia. </li><li>When a person with preeclampsia or eclampsia has damage to the liver and blood cells, it's called HELLP syndrome. It is rare, but very serious.</li></ul>
</li></ul>Who is more likely to develop <span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">high blood pressure</span></span> in pregnancy?<p>You are more likely to develop <span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">high blood pressure</span></span> in pregnancy if you:</p><ul><li>Had chronic <span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">high blood pressure</span></span> or chronic kidney disease before pregnancy</li><li>Had <span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">high blood pressure</span></span> or preeclampsia in a previous pregnancy</li><li>Have obesity
</li><li>Are under age 20 or over age 40</li><li>Are pregnant with more than one baby</li><li>Are African American</li><li>Have a family history of <span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">high blood pressure</span></span> in pregnancy</li><li>Have certain health conditions, such as diabetes or lupus</li></ul>What are the symptoms of <span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">high blood pressure</span></span> in pregnancy?<p><span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">High blood pressure</span></span> usually has no symptoms. People usually find out they have <span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">high blood pressure</span></span> when their health care provider measures their blood pressure.</p><p>Preeclampsia can cause other symptoms, including:</p><ul><li>Too much protein in your urine (called proteinuria).</li><li>Swelling (edema) in your face and hands. Your feet may also swell, but many women have swollen feet during pregnancy. So swollen feet by themselves may not be a sign of a problem.</li><li>A headache that does not go away.</li><li>Vision problems, including blurred vision or seeing spots.</li><li>Pain in your upper right abdomen (belly).</li><li>Trouble breathing.</li></ul><p>Eclampsia can also cause seizures, nausea and/or vomiting, and low urine output.</p><p>If you go on to develop HELLP syndrome, you may also have bleeding or bruising easily, extreme fatigue, and liver failure.</p>What problems can <span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">high blood pressure</span></span> in pregnancy cause?<p><span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">High blood pressure</span></span> in pregnancy can lead to complications such as:</p><ul><li>Placental abruption, where the placenta (the organ that brings oxygen and nutrients to the baby) separates from the uterus (the place where a baby grows during pregnancy)</li><li>Poor fetal growth, caused by a lack of nutrients and oxygen</li><li>Preterm birth</li><li>Your baby having a low birth weight</li><li>Damage to your kidneys, liver, brain, and other organ and blood systems</li><li>A higher risk of heart disease for you</li></ul>How is <span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">high blood pressure</span></span> in pregnancy diagnosed?<p>Your provider will check your blood pressure and urine at each prenatal visit. If your blood pressure reading is high (140/90 or higher), especially after the 20th week of pregnancy, your provider will likely want to order some tests. These may include blood tests and other lab tests, such as a test to look for extra protein in your urine.</p>What are the treatments for <span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">high blood pressure</span></span> in pregnancy?<p>If you have <span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">high blood pressure</span></span>, you and your baby will be closely monitored to lower the chance of complications. You may need to:</p><ul><li>Check your blood pressure at home.</li><li>Keep track of how many times you feel the baby kicking each day.</li><li>Adjust your physical activity. Talk to your provider about what level of physical activity is right for you.</li><li>Take medicine to control your blood pressure. Talk to your provider about which medicines are safe for your baby. </li><li>Take aspirin in the second trimester, if you are at risk of preeclampsia and your provider recommends aspirin.</li><li>Visit your provider more often to monitor your condition and your baby's growth rate and heart rate.</li></ul><p>If you have eclampsia, HELLP syndrome, or a severe case of preeclampsia, you will most likely need to go to the hospital. Treatment often includes medicines. Your provider may also recommend delivering the baby early. They will make the decision based on:</p><ul><li>How severe the condition is</li><li>The possible risks to you and your baby</li><li>How far along the pregnancy is</li></ul><p>The goal is to lower the risks to you while giving your baby as much time as possible to mature before delivery. </p><p>The symptoms of preeclampsia can last after delivery, but they usually go away within 6 weeks.</p></content>
      <content name="mesh"><span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">Hypertension</span></span>, Pregnancy-Induced</content>
      <content name="groupName">Pregnancy and Reproduction</content>
      <content name="groupName">Female Reproductive System</content>

    <document rank="4" url="https://medlineplus.gov/bloodpressuremedicines.html">
      <content name="title">Blood Pressure Medicines</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Antihypertensive Medicines</content>
      <content name="altTitle"><span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">High Blood Pressure</span></span> Medicines</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is <span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">high blood pressure</span></span>?<p><span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">High blood pressure</span></span>, also called <span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">hypertension</span></span>, is when blood puts too much pressure against the walls of your arteries. Almost half of American adults have <span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">high blood pressure</span></span>, usually with no symptoms. But it can cause serious problems such as stroke, heart failure, heart attack, and kidney disease.</p>What lifestyle changes can help lower <span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">high blood pressure</span></span>?<p>Healthy lifestyle changes can help reduce <span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">high blood pressure</span></span>:</p><ul><li>Losing weight</li><li>Being physically active</li><li>Managing stress</li><li>Reducing sodium in your diet</li><li>Avoiding alcohol, tobacco, and illegal drugs</li><li>Getting enough sleep</li></ul>What if lifestyle changes alone cannot lower blood pressure?<p>Sometimes lifestyle changes alone cannot control or lower your <span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">high blood pressure</span></span>. In that case, your health care provider may prescribe blood pressure medicines.</p>How do blood pressure medicines work?<p>The most commonly used blood pressure medicines work in different ways to lower blood pressure:</p><ul><li>Angiotensin-converting enzyme (ACE) inhibitors and angiotensin II receptor blockers (ARBs) keep your blood vessels from narrowing as much and allows blood to move through them with less pressure.</li><li>Beta blockers help your heart beat slower and with less force. This means that your heart pumps less blood through your blood vessels. Beta blockers are typically used only as a backup option or if you also have certain other conditions.</li><li>Calcium channel blockers prevent calcium from entering the muscle cells of your heart and blood vessels. This allows the blood vessels to relax.</li><li>Diuretics remove extra water and sodium (salt) from your body. This lowers the amount of fluid in your blood. Diuretics are often used with other <span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">high blood pressure</span></span> medicines, sometimes in one combined pill.</li></ul><p>Often, two or more medicines work better than one. If these medicines do not lower your blood pressure enough, your provider may suggest that you take another type of blood pressure medicine.</p><p>While taking the medicines, it is still important to keep up with your healthy lifestyle changes. Doing both helps keep blood pressure lower than lifestyle changes or medicines alone.</p><p>NIH: National Heart, Lung, and Blood Institute</p></content>
      <content name="mesh">Antihypertensive Agents</content>
      <content name="groupName">Blood, Heart and Circulation</content>

      <content name="snippet">What is <span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">high blood pressure? High blood pressure</span></span>, also called <span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">hypertension</span></span>, is when blood puts too much pressure against the walls of your arteries. Almost  ... </content>

    <document rank="5" url="https://medlineplus.gov/dasheatingplan.html">
      <content name="title">DASH Eating Plan</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">DASH Diet</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Dietary Approaches to Stop <span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">Hypertension</span></span></content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>DASH stands for Dietary Approaches to Stop <span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">Hypertension</span></span>. It is an eating plan that is based on research studies sponsored by the National Heart, Lung, and Blood Institute (NHLBI). These studies showed that DASH lowers <span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">high blood pressure</span></span> and improves levels of cholesterol. This reduces your risk of getting heart disease.</p><p>The DASH eating plan:</p><ul><li>Emphasizes vegetables, fruits, and whole-grains</li><li>Includes fat-free or low-fat dairy products, fish, poultry, beans, nuts, and vegetable oils</li><li>Limits foods that are high in saturated fat. These foods include fatty meats, full-fat dairy products, and tropical oils such as coconut, palm kernel, and palm oils.</li><li>Limits sugar-sweetened beverages and sweets</li></ul><p>Along with DASH, other lifestyle changes can help lower your blood pressure. They include staying at a healthy weight, exercising, and not smoking.</p><p>NIH: National Heart, Lung, and Blood Institute</p></content>
      <content name="mesh">Dietary Approaches To Stop <span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">Hypertension</span></span></content>
      <content name="groupName">Blood, Heart and Circulation</content>
      <content name="groupName">Food and Nutrition</content>
      <content name="snippet"> DASH stands for Dietary Approaches to Stop <span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">Hypertension</span></span>. It is an eating plan that is based on research studies sponsored by the National Heart, Lung, and Blood Institute (NHLBI). These studies  ... </content>

    <document rank="6" url="https://medlineplus.gov/howtopreventheartdisease.html">
      <content name="title">How to Prevent Heart Disease</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Heart Disease Prevention</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>Heart disease is the leading cause of the death in the United States. It is also a major cause of disability. But you can take steps to improve your health and help prevent heart disease. The first step is understanding your risk of heart disease. Your risk depends on many factors, some of which are changeable and others that are not. Learning about them and working on the things that you can change can lower your risk of heart disease.</p>What are the heart disease risk factors that I cannot change?<p>There are some risk factors for heart disease that you cannot change:</p><ul><li>Your age. Your risk of heart disease increases as you get older. The risk is higher in:
  <ul><li>Men age 45 and older</li><li>Women age 55 and older</li></ul>
</li><li>Your sex. Some risk factors may affect heart disease risk differently in women than in men. For example: 
  <ul><li>The hormone estrogen provides women some protection against heart disease</li><li>Diabetes raises the risk of heart disease more in women than in men.</li></ul>
</li><li>Your race or ethnicity. Certain groups have higher risks than others. For example:
  <ul><li>African Americans are more likely than Whites to die of heart disease, while Hispanic Americans are less likely to die of it</li><li>Asian Americans as a group have lower rates of heart disease than other groups, but South Asian Americans have higher rates</li></ul>
</li><li>Your family history. You have a greater risk if you have a close family member who had heart disease at an early age, for example if:
  <ul><li>Your father or brother was diagnosed before age 55</li><li>Your mother or sister was diagnosed before age 65</li></ul>
</li></ul>What are the heart disease risk factors I can change and what can I do to lower my risk?<p>There are many heart disease risk factors that you can change.  If you make these changes, you don't just help protect your heart. You can also improve your overall health and well-being.</p><p>You may have a lot of changes to make. If you need to, you can make the changes gradually, one at a time. What's most important is that you make them. Depending on your lifestyle, these changes could include:</p><ul><li>Controlling your <span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">blood pressure.High blood pressure</span></span> is a major risk factor for heart disease. It is important to get your blood pressure checked regularly - at least once a year for most adults, and more often if you have <span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">high blood pressure</span></span>. You can also take steps, including lifestyle changes, to prevent or control <span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">high blood pressure</span></span>.</li><li>Keeping your cholesterol and triglyceride levels under control:<ul><li>High levels of cholesterol can clog your arteries and raise your risk of coronary artery disease and heart attack. Lifestyle changes and medicines (if needed) can lower your cholesterol.</li><li>Triglycerides are another type of fat in the blood. High  levels of triglyceride may also raise the risk of coronary artery disease, especially in women.</li></ul></li><li>Staying at a healthy weight. Being overweight or having obesity can increase your risk of heart disease. This is mostly because they are linked to other heart disease risk factors, including high blood cholesterol and triglyceride levels, <span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">high blood pressure</span></span>, and diabetes. Controlling your weight can lower these risks.</li><li>Eating a healthy diet. Try to limit saturated fats, foods high in sodium (salt), and added sugars. Instead, eat plenty of fresh fruit, vegetables, and whole grains. The DASH diet is an example of an eating plan that can help you to lower your blood pressure and cholesterol, two things that can lower your risk of heart disease.</li><li>Getting regular physical activity. Regular physical activity has many benefits, including strengthening your heart and improving your circulation. It can also help you maintain a healthy weight and lower cholesterol and blood pressure. All of these can lower your risk of heart disease.</li><li>Limiting alcohol. Drinking too much alcohol can raise your blood pressure. It also adds extra calories, which may cause weight gain. Both of those raise your risk of heart disease. It's best not to drink, but if you do:
  <ul><li>Have no more than 2 drinks per day if you are a man.</li><li>Have no more than 1 drink per day if you are a woman.</li></ul>
</li><li>Not smoking. Cigarette smoking raises your blood pressure and puts you at higher risk of heart attack and stroke. 
  <ul><li>If you do not smoke, don't start.</li><li> If you do smoke, quitting will lower your risk of heart disease. You can talk with your health care provider for help in finding the best way for you to quit.</li></ul>
</li><li>Managing stress.Stress is linked to heart disease in many ways. It can raise your blood pressure. Extreme stress can be a "trigger" for a heart attack. Also, some common ways of coping with stress, such as overeating, heavy drinking, and smoking, are bad for your heart. Some ways to help manage your stress include exercise, listening to music, focusing on something calm or peaceful, and meditating.</li><li>Managing diabetes. Having diabetes doubles your risk of diabetic heart disease. That is because over time, high blood glucose (blood sugar) from diabetes can damage your blood vessels and the nerves that control your heart and blood vessels. So, it is important to get tested for diabetes, and if you have it, to keep it under control.</li><li>Getting enough sleep. If you don't get enough sleep, you raise your risk of <span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">high blood pressure</span></span>, obesity, and diabetes. Those three things can raise your risk of heart disease. To improve your sleep:
  <ul><li>If you are an adult, try to get 7 to 9 hours of sleep per night.</li><li>Make sure that you have good sleep habits, such as keeping a regular sleep schedule and creating a good sleeping environment in your bedroom.</li><li> If you have frequent sleep problems, contact your health care provider. One problem, sleep apnea, causes people to briefly stop breathing many times during sleep. This interferes with your ability to get a good rest and can raise your risk of heart disease. If you think you might have sleep apnea, ask your provider about having a sleep study. And if you do have sleep apnea, make sure that you get treatment for it.</li></ul>
</li></ul></content>

    <document rank="7" url="https://medlineplus.gov/braindiseases.html">
      <content name="title">Brain Diseases</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>Your brain is the control center of your body. It controls your thoughts, memory, speech, and movement. It regulates the function of many organs. It's part of your nervous system, which also includes your spinal cord and peripheral nerves. The nervous system sends signals between your brain and the rest of the body. Your nerves take in information from your senses and send it to the brain to be processed. Your brain and nerves also communicate to help you move and to control your body's functions.</p><p>When the brain is healthy, it works quickly and automatically. But when you have a brain disease, it may affect how  well you can function and do your daily activities. Some common brain diseases include:</p><ul><li>Brain tumors, which can press on nerves and affect brain function.</li><li>Degenerative nerve diseases, which can affect many of your body's activities, such as balance, movement, talking, breathing, and heart function. Types include Alzheimer's disease and Parkinson's disease.</li><li>Encephalitis (inflammation in the brain), which can lead to problems such as vision loss, weakness, and paralysis.</li><li>Genetic brain disorders, which are caused by changes in genes (also called variants or mutations). These disorders can affect the development and function of the brain.</li><li>Strokes, which can cause a loss of brain cells and can affect your ability to think clearly.</li><li>Traumatic brain injuries (TBIs), which can affect brain function. They may range from mild to severe. The effects of a TBI may be temporary or permanent.</li></ul><p>The symptoms of brain diseases vary widely, depending on the specific problem. In some cases, damage is permanent. In other cases, treatments such as surgery, medicines, or therapies such as physical, occupational, and speech therapies, may cure the disease or improve the symptoms.</p><p> 
</p></content>
      <content name="mesh">Brain Diseases</content>
      <content name="groupName">Brain and Nerves</content>
