    <document rank="8" url="https://medlineplus.gov/atrialfibrillation.html">
      <content name="title">Atrial Fibrillation</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">AF</content>
      <content name="altTitle">AFib</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is atrial fibrillation (AFib)?<p>Atrial fibrillation, also known as AFib or AF, is one of the most common types of arrhythmias.  Arrhythmias are problems with the rate or rhythm of your heartbeat. They can cause your heart to beat too slowly, too fast, or in an irregular way.</p><p>If you have AFib, your heart beats irregularly and sometimes much faster than normal. Also, your heart's upper and lower chambers do not work together as they should. When this happens, the lower chambers do not fill completely or pump enough blood to your lungs and body. This can cause symptoms such as dizziness, fatigue, and a pounding heartbeat.</p><p>AFib may happen in brief episodes, or it may be a permanent condition. It's very important to treat it, since AFib can put you at risk for stroke and other heart conditions.</p>What causes atrial fibrillation (AFib)?<p>AFib is most often caused by changes to the heart's tissue or the electrical signaling that helps the heartbeat. These changes can happen due to different conditions and factors, such as <span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">high blood pressure</span></span>, coronary artery disease, congenital heart defects, infections, and aging. Sometimes the cause is unknown.</p>Who is more likely to develop atrial fibrillation (AFib)?<p>Anyone can develop AFib, but there are certain things that raise your risk for it:</p><ul><li>Aging. The risk of atrial fibrillation increases as you get older, especially when you are over age 65.</li><li>Family history and genetics. AFib can run in families. So can heart disease, which raises your risk of AFib.</li><li>Some lifestyle choices. Your risk is higher if you drink a lot of alcohol, use certain illegal drugs such as cocaine and methamphetamines, or smoke.</li><li>Having certain health conditions, such as:
  <ul><li><span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">High blood pressure</span></span></li><li>Diabetes</li><li>Heart failure</li><li>Heart valve diseases</li><li>Obesity</li><li>Hyperthyroidism</li><li>Chronic kidney disease</li><li>COPD and other lung diseases</li><li>Sleep apnea</li></ul>
</li><li>Race. AFib is more common in people with European ancestry.</li><li>Recent surgery. You may be at risk of atrial fibrillation in the early days and weeks after surgery on your heart, lungs, or esophagus.</li></ul>What are the symptoms of atrial fibrillation (AFib)?<p>Some people who have AFib don't have any symptoms and don't know they have it. If you do have symptoms, you may only notice them once in a while. Or you may have symptoms that are more frequent. And in some cases, the symptoms might be severe. If you have heart disease, you are more likely to notice your symptoms. And those symptoms could get worse if your heart disease gets worse.</p><p>The symptoms of AFib can include:</p><ul><li>Extreme fatigue, which is the most common symptom</li><li>Heart palpitations (the feeling that your heart is skipping a beat, fluttering, pounding, or beating too hard or too fast)</li><li>Trouble breathing, especially when lying down or when exercising</li><li>Chest pain</li><li>Dizziness or fainting</li><li>Low blood pressure</li></ul>What other problems can AFib cause?<p>If AFib is not treated, it can lead to serious health problems (complications) such as:</p><ul><li>Stroke</li><li>Heart failure</li><li>Blood clots</li><li>Sudden cardiac arrest (SCA)</li><li>Cognitive impairment and dementia</li></ul><p>To help prevent these problems, it's important to contact your health care provider if you are having symptoms. If you do have AFib, the sooner you are diagnosed and treated, the better.</p>How is atrial fibrillation (AFib) diagnosed?<p>To find out if you have AFib, your provider:</p><ul><li>Will ask about your medical history, including your symptoms, lifestyle, and any other health conditions you may have</li><li>Will ask about your family history, to find out if you have relatives who have or had AFib</li><li>Will do a physical exam</li><li>May order blood tests</li><li>Will likely order heart tests, such as an electrocardiogram (also called an EKG or ECG) and echocardiogram</li><li>May ask you to wear a heart monitor device that records your heart's electrical activity</li></ul>What are the treatments for atrial fibrillation (AFib)?<p>The treatments for AFib may include:</p><ul><li>Blood thinner medicines that help prevent blood clots from forming.</li><li>Medicines to control your heart's rhythm and rate.</li><li>Following heart-healthy lifestyle changes, such as:
  <ul><li>Following a heart-healthy eating plan that limits saturated fats, salt, and cholesterol. An example is the DASH eating plan.</li><li>Limiting or avoiding alcohol, because it can increase your heart rate.</li><li>Aiming for a healthy weight.</li><li>Getting regular physical activity.</li><li>Managing stress.</li><li>Quitting smoking.</li></ul>
</li><li>Procedures such as:
  <ul><li>Electrical cardioversion, which restores your heart rhythm using low-energy shocks to your heart.</li><li>Catheter ablation, which scars the tissue that is causing the arrhythmia. The scar tissue blocks the abnormal heart signals.</li></ul>
</li><li>Surgeries such as:
  <ul><li>Surgery to put in a pacemaker to help control the arrhythmia.</li><li>A Maze procedure, which creates scar tissue in a maze-like pattern in certain parts of the heart.</li><li>Left atrial appendage closure, a surgery on a small sac in the muscle wall of your left atrium (the upper left chamber of your heart). It helps prevent blood clots and can reduce your risk of stroke. This surgery is for people who are not able to take blood thinners.</li></ul>
</li></ul>Can atrial fibrillation (AFib) be prevented?<p>There are steps you can take to help lower your risk of atrial fibrillation, such as:</p><ul><li>Making heart-healthy lifestyle changes:
  <ul><li>Following a heart-healthy eating plan</li><li>Limiting or avoiding alcohol</li><li>Aiming for a healthy weight</li><li>Getting regular physical activity</li><li>Managing stress</li><li>Not smoking</li></ul>
</li><li>Avoiding illegal drugs, such as cocaine and methamphetamines</li><li>Taking antiarrhythmic medicine (medicine to treat arrhythmia) if you are having heart surgery</li><li>Treating any health conditions that could raise your risk of AFib</li></ul><p>NIH: National Heart, Lung, and Blood Institute</p></content>
      <content name="mesh">Atrial Fibrillation</content>
      <content name="groupName">Blood, Heart and Circulation</content>

   <span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">High blood pressure</span></span>  Diabetes  Heart failure  Heart valve diseases  Obesity  Hyperthyroidism   ... </content>

    <document rank="9" url="https://medlineplus.gov/congenitalheartdefects.html">
      <content name="title">Congenital Heart Defects</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Heart Defects</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Heart Diseases, Congenital</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Heart Murmur</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What are congenital heart defects?<p>Congenital heart defects (CHDs) are problems with the structure of the heart. "Congenital" means that that the problems are present at birth. These defects happen when a fetus's heart doesn't develop normally during pregnancy. Congenital heart defects are the most common type of birth defect.</p><p>Congenital heart defects can change the way the heart pumps blood. They may make blood flow too slowly, go the wrong way, or block it completely.</p><p>There are many types of congenital heart defects. They can happen in one or more parts of the heart. The most common types are:</p><ul><li>Septal defects ("hole in the heart") - openings in the wall between the left and right sides of the heart</li><li>Heart valve defects - problems with the valves that control the flow of blood through the heart</li><li>Defects in the large blood vessels that carry blood in and out of the heart</li></ul><p>Congenital heart defects can range from very mild problems that never need treatment to life-threatening problems at birth. The most serious congenital heart defects are called critical congenital heart disease. Babies with these defects usually need surgery in the first year of life. But the symptoms of milder heart defects may not show up until childhood or adulthood.</p>What causes congenital heart defects?<p>Researchers often don't know what causes congenital heart defects. They do know that changes in a baby's genes sometimes cause a heart defect. The changed genes may come from the parents, or the changes may happen during pregnancy.</p>Who is more likely to have a baby with a congenital heart defect?<p>Several things may increase the chance that your baby has a congenital heart defect, such as:</p><ul><li>Your health before and during pregnancy, including:
	<ul><li>Having diabetes before pregnancy or developing it in the first 3 months of pregnancy (diabetes that develops later in pregnancy isn't a major risk for heart defects). Carefully controlling your blood glucose, also called blood sugar, before and during pregnancy can lower your baby's risk of congenital heart defects.</li><li>Having phenylketonuria (PKU), a rare inherited disorder that affects how the body uses a protein in foods. If you have PKU, eating a low-protein diet before getting pregnant can lower your baby's risk of having a congenital heart defect.</li><li>Having rubella (German measles) during pregnancy.</li></ul></li><li>Your contact with certain substances during pregnancy, including:
	<ul><li>Smoking or secondhand smoke (breathing smoke from another smoker).</li><li>Certain medicines, such as angiotensin-converting (ACE) inhibitors for <span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">high blood pressure</span></span> and retinoic acids for acne. If you're pregnant or plan to get pregnant, talk with your health care provider about all the medicines you take.</li></ul></li><li>Your family history and genetics. In most cases, congenital heart defects don't run in families. But your chance of having a baby with a congenital heart defect does go up if you or the other parent has a congenital heart defect, or if you already have a child with a congenital heart defect.</li></ul>What are the symptoms of congenital heart defects?<p>Congenital heart defects don't cause pain. The signs and symptoms are different, depending on the type and number of defects and how serious they are.</p><p>Common signs and symptoms of congenital heart defects include:</p><ul><li>Cyanosis - a bluish color to the skin, lips, and fingernails. It happens when there isn't enough oxygen in the blood.</li><li>Fatigue - your baby may be unusually sleepy and may become very tired during feedings.</li><li>Poor blood flow.</li><li>Fast or difficult breathing.</li><li>Heart murmur - an unusual sound between heartbeats.</li></ul>What other problems do congenital heart defects cause?<p>Congenital heart defects don't always cause other problems. If they do, which problems you have would depend on the type and number of defects and how serious the defects are.</p><p>Children with congenital heart defects are more likely to:</p><ul><li>Be smaller than other children</li><li>Have problems or delays in mental, and emotional growth, and behavior, such as:
	<ul><li>Speech and language problems</li><li>Attention deficit hyperactivity disorder (ADHD)</li></ul>
</li></ul><p>People with congenital heart defects may develop other health conditions, including:</p><ul><li>Endocarditis - an infection of lining of the heart and valves</li><li>Arrhythmia - a problem with the rate or rhythm of your heartbeat</li><li>Heart failure - when your heart can't pump enough blood to the body</li><li>Pulmonary <span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">hypertension - high blood pressure</span></span> in your lungs</li><li>Kidney and liver disease</li></ul>How are congenital heart defects diagnosed?<ul><li>Before a baby is born, your provider may use ultrasound pictures of the fetus's heart to look for heart defects. This is called a fetal echocardiogram. It's done between weeks 18 and 22 of pregnancy.</li><li>During the first few days after birth, all newborns are checked for congenital heart defects. A pulse oximeter is clipped to your baby's hands or feet to measure blood oxygen. If it shows low levels of blood oxygen, more tests will be needed to find out if your baby has a heart defect.</li><li>To diagnose congenital heart defects in babies, children, and adults, a provider may use many tools, including:
	<ul><li>A physical exam.</li><li>Certain heart tests to see how the heart is working.</li><li>Genetic testing to see if certain gene problems caused the defect.</li></ul></li></ul>What are the treatments for congenital heart defects?<p>Treatment depends on the type of congenital heart defect and how serious it is. Possible treatments include:</p><ul><li>Cardiac catheterization to repair simple defects, such as a small hole in the inside wall of the heart. A catheterization uses a thin tube guided through a vein and into the heart.</li><li>Heart surgery may be needed to:
	<ul><li>Repair defects in the heart and blood vessels.</li><li>Repair or replace a heart valve.</li><li>Place a device in the chest to help the heart pump blood.</li><li>Do a heart transplant</li></ul>
</li><li>Medicine is often used if your baby has a specific type of congenital heart defect called patent ductus arteriosus.</li></ul><p>All children and adults who have congenital heart defects need regular follow-up care from a cardiologist (a doctor who specializes in heart diseases) throughout their life, even if their defect was repaired.</p><p>Some people may need several heart surgeries or catheterizations over the years. They may also need to take medicines to help their hearts work as well as possible.</p><p>NIH: National Heart, Lung, and Blood Institute</p></content>
      <content name="mesh">Heart Defects, Congenital</content>
      <content name="groupName">Blood, Heart and Circulation</content>
      <content name="groupName">Genetics/Birth Defects</content>

    <document rank="10" url="https://medlineplus.gov/heartdiseaseinwomen.html">
      <content name="title">Heart Disease in Women</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is heart disease?<p>Heart disease is a general term that includes many types of heart problems. It's also called cardiovascular disease, which means heart and blood vessel disease.</p><p>Coronary artery disease (also called coronary heart disease) is the most common type of heart disease in both men and women. It happens slowly over time when a sticky substance called plaque builds up in the arteries that supply your heart muscle with blood. The plaque narrows or blocks blood flow to the heart muscle and can lead to:</p><ul><li>Angina - chest pain from lack of blood flow</li><li>Heart attacks - when part of the heart muscle dies from loss of blood flow</li><li>Heart failure - when your heart can't pump enough blood to meet your body's needs</li><li>Arrhythmia - a problem with the rate or rhythm of your heartbeat</li></ul>How does heart disease affect women?<p>In the United States, heart disease is the number one cause of death in women. But women are often not diagnosed with heart disease as quickly as men are. That's because:</p><ul><li>Women are more likely than men to have "silent" heart disease, meaning that they don't have symptoms.</li><li>Health care providers may not recognize heart disease in women because women's symptoms may be different from men's symptoms.</li><li>Women are more likely than men to have certain types of heart disease that can be harder to diagnose.</li></ul><p>A delay in diagnosis may mean a delay in medical care that could help prevent serious problems, such as a heart attack. That's why it's important to learn about your risk for heart disease, the symptoms in women, and how to keep your heart healthy.</p>What types of heart disease do women get?<p>Women can get any type of heart disease. Like men, the most common type of heart disease among women is coronary artery disease. But there are certain types of heart disease which are less common, but affect women more often than men:</p><ul><li>Coronary microvascular disease - Chest pain from spasms (sudden tightening) in the smallest arteries of the heart that pinch off blood flow. It typically happens during rest or routine activities. This serious condition increases your risk of having a heart attack or other heart diseases.  This type may be harder to diagnose since blockages in smaller arteries can be harder to see on imaging tests.</li><li>Broken heart syndrome - Strong chest pain or other signs that look like a heart attack. It's caused by the stress of powerful emotions, such as deep grief, anger, or surprise. It can happen even if you're healthy. It mostly affects women after menopause and usually doesn't cause any lasting damage.</li><li>Variant angina - A rare type of strong chest pain from spasms in the heart arteries. The pain usually happens in a pattern during sleep. Variant angina rarely causes a heart attack.</li></ul>Which women are more likely to develop heart disease?<p>Your risk for developing heart disease increases with:</p><ul><li>Menopause. Women can develop heart disease at any age, but your risk increases after your periods stop, usually by age 55. Before menopause, your body makes more estrogen (a female hormone) which helps protect against heart disease. That's why women generally develop coronary artery disease 10 years later than men. During and after menopause, estrogen levels drop and your risk for heart diseases goes up. If your periods stop before age 40, your risk will be higher than other women your age.</li><li>Family history of heart disease. Your risk for heart disease may be higher if your:
	<ul><li>Mother or sister had heart disease before age 65.</li><li>Father or brother had heart disease before age 55.</li></ul>
</li><li>Problems during pregnancy, such as  <span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">high blood pressure</span></span>, gestational diabetes, anemia, and <span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">high blood pressure</span></span>.</li><li>Use of hormonal birth control (pills, patches, or vaginal rings with estrogen and progesterone).</li><li>Endometriosis.</li><li>Polycystic ovary syndrome.</li><li>Inflammatory and autoimmune diseases.</li><li>Metabolic syndrome.</li><li>Mental health issues, such as stress, anxiety, or depression.</li><li>Health risks from a lack physical activity.</li><li>Obesity or being overweight.</li><li>Diabetes.</li><li>Low HDL cholesterol levels.</li><li>Mild to moderate <span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">high blood pressure</span></span>.</li><li>Smoking.</li></ul><p>If you have one or more risks for heart disease, ask your health care provider for help understanding your risk level. Ask if you need any heart tests to help catch heart disease early.</p>What are the symptoms of heart disease and heart attack in women?<p>When women have symptoms of heart disease, they may include:</p><ul><li>Pain or discomfort in the chest that may be dull and heavy or sharp</li><li>Pain in the neck, jaw, throat, upper belly, or back</li><li>Nausea or vomiting</li><li>Unusual fatigue</li><li>Shortness of breath (feeling like you can't get enough air) during physical activity</li></ul><p>Women who have coronary artery disease are more likely than men to have chest pain when resting or doing daily activities, rather than during exercise. They're also more likely than men to feel chest pain from mental stress.</p><p>Symptoms of a heart attack in women may also be different than in men. Chest pain is the most common symptom for both sexes. It may feel like crushing or squeezing. But women are somewhat less likely than men to have chest pain.</p><p>During a heart attack, women may feel:</p><ul><li>Pressure or tightness in the chest</li><li>Pain in the upper back, arms, neck, jaw or throat</li><li>Dizziness</li><li>Unusual fatigue</li><li>Indigestion, heartburn, nausea, or vomiting </li><li>Stomach pain</li><li>Shortness of breath</li></ul><p>Heart attacks usually don't look like the sudden, dramatic events we see in the movies. The symptoms may be mild or strong. They may start slowly. They can stop and then come back.</p>Can heart disease in women be prevented?<p>You can help lower your risk by:</p><ul><li>Learning how to prevent heart disease and making heart-healthy habits part of your daily life.</li><li>Asking your provider about your personal risk for heart disease and the best way to manage your heart health.</li></ul><p>Remember, women can have heart disease without symptoms. But if you pay attention to your risk for heart disease, you can take action to prevent problems or keep them from getting worse.</p><p>NIH: National Heart, Lung, and Blood Institute</p></content>
      <content name="mesh">Heart Diseases</content>

    <document rank="11" url="https://medlineplus.gov/heartfailure.html">
      <content name="title">Heart Failure</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">CHF</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Cardiac Failure</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Congestive Heart Failure</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Low Cardiac Output</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Left-sided heart failure</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Right-sided heart failure</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is heart failure?<p>Heart failure means that your heart can't pump enough oxygen-rich blood to meet your body's needs. Heart failure doesn't mean that your heart has stopped or is about to stop beating. But without enough blood flow, your organs may not work well, which can cause serious problems.</p><p>Heart failure can affect one or both sides of your heart:</p><ul><li>With right-sided heart failure, your heart is too weak to pump enough blood to your lungs to get oxygen.</li><li>With left-sided heart failure, your heart can't pump enough oxygen-rich blood out to your body. This happens when the left side of your heart becomes either:
	<ul><li>Too weak to pump enough blood.</li><li>Too thick or stiff to relax and fill with enough blood.</li></ul></li></ul><p>Left-sided heart failure is more common than right-sided heart failure.</p>What causes heart failure?<p>Heart failure can start suddenly after a medical condition or injury damages your heart muscle. But in most cases, heart failure develops slowly from long-term medical conditions.</p><p>Conditions that can cause heart failure include:</p><ul><li>Arrhythmia (a problem with the rate or rhythm of your heartbeat)</li><li>Cardiomyopathy</li><li>Congenital heart defects or other types of heart diseases that you are born with</li><li>Coronary artery disease</li><li>Endocarditis</li><li>Heart attack</li><li>Heart valve diseases</li><li><span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">High blood pressure</span></span></li><li>A blood clot in your lung</li><li>Diabetes</li><li>Certain severe lung diseases, such as COPD (chronic obstructive pulmonary disease)</li><li>Obesity</li></ul><p>Over time, left-sided heart failure can lead to right-sided heart failure.</p>Who is more likely to develop heart failure?<p>Heart failure can happen at any age. It happens to both men and women, but men often develop it at a younger age than women.  Your chance of developing heart failure increases if:</p><ul><li>You're 65 years old or older. Aging can weaken and stiffen your heart muscle.</li><li>Your family health history includes relatives who have or have had heart failure.</li><li>You have changes in your genes that affect your heart tissue.</li><li>You have habits that can harm your heart, including:
	<ul><li>Smoking</li><li>Eating foods high in fat, cholesterol, and sodium (salt)</li><li>Having an inactive lifestyle</li><li>Alcohol use disorder (AUD)</li><li>Illegal drug use</li></ul></li><li>You have other medical conditions that can affect your heart, including: 
	<ul><li>Any heart or blood vessel conditions, including <span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">high blood pressure</span></span></li><li>Serious lung diseases</li><li>Infection, such as HIV or COVID-19</li><li>Obesity</li><li>Diabetes</li><li>Sleep apnea</li><li>Chronic kidney disease</li><li>Anemia</li><li>Thyroid disease</li><li>Iron overload disease</li><li>Cancer treatments that can harm your heart, such as radiation and chemotherapy</li></ul></li><li>You are African American. African Americans are more likely to develop heart failure and have more serious cases at younger ages than people of other races. Factors such as stigma, discrimination, income, education, and geographic region can also affect their risk of heart failure.</li></ul>What are the symptoms of heart failure?<p>The symptoms of heart failure depend on which side of your heart is affected and how serious your condition has become.  Most symptoms are caused by reduced blood flow to your organs and fluid buildup in your body.</p><p>Fluid buildup happens because the flow of blood through your heart is too slow. As a result, blood backs up in the vessels that return the blood to your heart. Fluid may leak from the blood vessels and collect in the tissues of your body, causing swelling (edema) and other problems.</p><p>Symptoms of heart failure may include:</p><ul><li>Feeling short of breath (like you can't get enough air) when you do things like climbing stairs. This may be one of the first symptoms you notice.</li><li>Fatigue or weakness even after rest.</li><li>Coughing.</li><li>Swelling and weight gain from fluid in your ankles, lower legs, or abdomen (belly).</li><li>Difficulty sleeping when lying flat.</li><li>Nausea and loss of appetite.</li><li>Swelling in the veins of your neck.</li><li>Needing to urinate (pee) often.</li></ul><p>At first you may have no symptoms or mild symptoms. As the disease gets worse, your symptoms will usually bother you more.</p>What other problems does heart failure cause?<p>Fluid buildup and reduced blood flow to your organs can lead to serious problems, including:</p><ul><li>Breathing problems from fluid in and around your lungs (also called congestive heart failure)</li><li>Kidney or liver damage, including cirrhosis</li><li>Malnutrition if fluid buildup makes eating uncomfortable or if your stomach doesn't get enough blood flow to digest food properly</li><li>Other heart conditions, such as irregular heartbeat and sudden cardiac arrest</li><li>Pulmonary <span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">hypertension</span></span></li></ul>How is heart failure diagnosed?<p>To find out if you have heart failure, your health care provider will</p><p> 
</p><ul><li>Ask about your medical history, including your symptoms</li><li>Ask about your family health history, including relatives who have had heart failure</li><li>Do a physical exam</li><li>Likely run heart tests and blood tests, including a brain natriuretic peptide (BNP) test</li></ul><p>In some cases, your provider may refer you to a cardiologist (a doctor who specializes in heart diseases) for tests, diagnosis, and care.</p>What are the treatments for heart failure?<p>Your treatment will depend on the type of heart failure you have and how serious it is. There's no cure for heart failure. But treatment can help you live longer with fewer symptoms.</p><p>Even with treatment, heart failure usually gets worse over time, so you'll likely need treatment for the rest of your life.</p><p>Most treatment plans include:</p><ul><li>Taking medicine</li><li>Eating less sodium and drinking less liquid to control fluid buildup</li><li>Making other changes, such as quitting smoking, managing stress, and getting as much physical activity as your provider recommends</li><li>Treating any conditions that may make heart failure worse</li></ul><p>You may need heart surgery if:</p><ul><li>You have a congenital heart defect or damage to your heart that can be fixed.</li><li>The left side of your heart is getting weaker and putting a device in your chest could help. Devices include:
	<ul><li>An implantable cardioverter defibrillator.</li><li>A biventricular pacemaker (cardiac resynchronization therapy).</li><li>A mechanical heart pump (a ventricular assist device (VAD) or a total artificial heart).</li></ul>
</li><li>Your heart doctor recommends a heart transplant because your heart failure is life-threatening and nothing else is helping.</li></ul><p>As part of your treatment, you'll need to pay close attention to your symptoms, because heart failure can worsen suddenly. Your provider may suggest a cardiac rehabilitation program to help you learn how to manage your condition.</p>Can heart failure be prevented?<p>You may be able to prevent or delay heart failure if you:</p><ul><li>Work with your provider to manage any health conditions that increase your risk of developing heart failure</li><li>Make healthy changes in your eating, exercise, and other daily habits to help prevent heart disease</li></ul><p>NIH: National Heart, Lung, and Blood Institute</p></content>

    <document rank="12" url="https://medlineplus.gov/peripheralarterialdisease.html">
      <content name="title">Peripheral Arterial Disease</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Arteriosclerosis of Extremities</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Claudication</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Intermittent Claudication</content>
      <content name="altTitle">PAD</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>Peripheral arterial disease (PAD) happens when there is a narrowing of the blood vessels outside of your heart. The cause of PAD is atherosclerosis. This happens when plaque builds up on the walls of the arteries that supply blood to the arms and legs. Plaque is a substance made up of fat and cholesterol. It causes the arteries to narrow or become blocked. This can reduce or stop blood flow, usually to the legs. If severe enough, blocked blood flow can cause tissue death and can sometimes lead to amputation of the foot or leg.</p><p>The main risk factor for PAD is smoking. Other risk factors include older age and diseases like diabetes, high blood cholesterol, <span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">high blood pressure</span></span>, heart disease, and stroke.</p><p>Many people who have PAD don't have any symptoms. If you have symptoms, they may include:</p><ul><li>Pain, numbness, achiness, or heaviness in the leg muscles. This happens when walking or climbing stairs.</li><li>Weak or absent pulses in the legs or feet</li><li>Sores or wounds on the toes, feet, or legs that heal slowly, poorly, or not at all</li><li>A pale or bluish color to the skin</li><li>A lower temperature in one leg than the other leg</li><li>Poor nail growth on the toes and decreased hair growth on the legs</li><li>Erectile dysfunction, especially among men who have diabetes</li></ul><p>PAD can increase your risk of heart attack, stroke, and transient ischemic attack.</p><p>Doctors diagnose PAD with a physical exam and heart and imaging tests. Treatments include lifestyle changes, medicines, and sometimes surgery. Lifestyle changes include dietary changes, exercise, and efforts to lower high cholesterol levels and <span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">high blood pressure</span></span>.</p><p>NIH: National Heart, Lung, and Blood Institute</p></content>
      <content name="mesh">Peripheral Arterial Disease</content>
      <content name="groupName">Blood, Heart and Circulation</content>

Many people who have  ... and efforts to lower high cholesterol levels and <span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">high blood pressure</span></span>. NIH: National Heart, Lung, and Blood Institute</content>

    <document rank="13" url="https://medlineplus.gov/retinaldisorders.html">
      <content name="title">Retinal Disorders</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Floater</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What are retinal disorders?<p>Retinal disorders are conditions that affect the retina, the thin light-sensitive tissue at the back of your eye. The retina senses light and sends signals to your brain so you can see.</p><p>Retinal disorders can cause blurred or distorted vision. Some can lead to vision loss or blindness.</p>What causes retinal disorders?<p>The causes of retinal disorders vary. Aging is a common factor, but other things can raise your risk, such as if you:</p><ul><li>Smoke</li><li>Have obesity</li><li>Have health conditions like diabetes or <span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">high blood pressure</span></span></li><li>Have had an eye injury or trauma</li><li>Have a family history of retinal disorders</li></ul>What are common retinal disorders?<p>Some common retinal disorders include:</p><ul><li>Macular degeneration. Occurs when the macula (the center of the retina that helps you see fine details) breaks down. There are two types: wet and dry macular degeneration.</li><li>Diabetic retinopathy. Occurs when diabetes damages small blood vessels in the retina, causing them to swell or leak.</li><li>Retinal detachment. Occurs when the retina pulls away from the back of the eye. This is a medical emergency.</li><li>Retinal tears. A hole or break in the retina caused when the gel-like substance inside your eye (the vitreous) pulls away.</li><li>Retinoblastoma. A rare cancer of the retina that most often affects young children.</li><li>Macular pucker. Scar tissue that forms on the macula and distorts vision.</li><li>Macular hole. A small opening in the macula, often related to aging or injury. It can blur or distort central vision.</li><li>Eye floaters. Small specks, cobwebs, or threads that drift across your vision. These are more common with aging.</li><li>Retina pigmentosa. A group of inherited disorders (passed down through families) that slowly damage the retina and cause loss of night and side (peripheral) vision.</li></ul>What are the symptoms of retinal disorders?<p>Symptoms can vary, but most retinal disorders affect your vision. Common symptoms include:</p><ul><li>Blurred or distorted vision</li><li>Loss of central or side (peripheral) vision</li><li>Flashes of light</li><li>Seeing floating specks or cobwebs</li><li>Trouble seeing at night</li><li>Sudden vision loss (in severe cases)</li></ul><p>Seek medical care right away if you notice flashes of light, sudden vision changes, or a sudden increase in eye floaters. These can be signs of a serious retinal problem.</p>How are retinal disorders diagnosed?<p>Eye care providers check for retinal disorders with a dilated eye exam. Eye drops are used to widen your pupils so your provider can look at the retina for signs of damage.</p><p>They may also do other tests, such as:</p><ul><li>Optical coherence tomography (OCT) to take detailed pictures of the inside of your eye.</li><li>Fluorescein angiography to check the blood vessels in your retina.</li><li>Visual field tests to measure your side (peripheral) vision.</li></ul>How are retinal disorders treated?<p>Some retinal disorders can be treated to slow or stop vision loss, and sometimes to restore vision. Treatment depends on the cause and severity. It may include:</p><ul><li>Laser therapy</li><li>Medicine or eye injections</li><li>Surgery</li><li>Lifestyle management to control health conditions such as diabetes or <span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">high blood pressure</span></span></li></ul>Can retinal disorders be prevented?<p>Not all retinal disorders can be prevented. Some are related to aging or genetics. But you can protect your eyes by:</p><ul><li>Wearing safety goggles or sunglasses</li><li>Getting regular eye exams</li><li>Making healthy lifestyle changes, such as:
<ul><li>Quitting smoking (or not starting)</li><li>Managing diabetes and blood pressure</li></ul>
</li></ul><p>NIH: National Eye Institute</p></content>

    <document rank="14" url="https://medlineplus.gov/sodium.html">
      <content name="title">Sodium</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Dietary Sodium</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Hyponatremia</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Salt</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>Table salt is a combination of two minerals - sodium and chloride Your body needs some sodium to work properly. It helps with the function of nerves and muscles. It also helps to keep the right balance of fluids in your body. Your kidneys control how much sodium is in your body. If you have too much and your kidneys can't get rid it, sodium builds up in your blood. This can lead to <span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">high blood pressure. High blood pressure</span></span> can lead to other health problems.</p><p>Most people in the U.S. get more sodium in their diets than they need. A key to healthy eating is choosing foods low in sodium. The Dietary Guidelines recommend that most adults eat less than 2.3 grams per day. That equals about 1 teaspoon of table salt a day. Some people are more sensitive to the effects of salt than others and should eat less. This includes people who have <span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">high blood pressure</span></span>, diabetes, or kidney problems, or are African American or over age 50. Reading food labels can help you see how much sodium is in prepared foods.</p><p>NIH: National Heart, Lung, and Blood Institute</p></content>

Most people  ... should eat less.     <document rank="15" url="https://medlineplus.gov/stroke.html">
      <content name="title">Stroke</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Brain Attack</content>
      <content name="altTitle">CVA</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Cerebrovascular Disease</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is a stroke?<p>A stroke happens when there is a loss of blood flow to part of the brain. Your brain cells cannot get the oxygen and nutrients they need from blood, and they start to die within a few minutes. This can cause lasting brain damage, long-term disability, or even death.</p><p>If you think that you or someone else is having a stroke, call 911 right away. Immediate treatment may save someone's life and increase the chances for successful rehabilitation and recovery.</p>What are the types of stroke?<p>There are two types of stroke:</p><ul><li>Ischemic stroke is caused by a blood clot that blocks or plugs a blood vessel in the brain. This is the most common type; about 80% of strokes are ischemic.</li><li>Hemorrhagic stroke is caused by a blood vessel that breaks and bleeds into the brain.</li></ul><p>Another condition that's similar to a stroke is a transient ischemic attack (TIA). It's sometimes called a "mini-stroke." TIAs happen when the blood supply to the brain is blocked for a short time. The damage to the brain cells isn't permanent, but having a TIA puts you at much higher risk of having a stroke.</p>Who is at risk for a stroke?<p>Certain factors can raise your risk of a stroke. The major risk factors include:</p><ul><li><span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">High blood pressure</span></span>. This is the primary risk factor for a stroke.</li><li>Diabetes.</li><li>Heart diseases.Atrial fibrillation and other heart diseases can cause blood clots that lead to stroke.</li><li>Smoking. When you smoke, you damage your blood vessels and raise your blood pressure.</li><li>A personal or family history of stroke or TIA</li><li>Age. Your risk of stroke increases as you get older.</li><li>Race and ethnicity. People who are African Americans or  Hispanic have a higher risk of stroke.</li></ul><p>There are also other factors that are linked to a higher risk of stroke, such as:</p><ul><li>Alcohol and illegal drug use</li><li>Not getting enough physical activity</li><li>High cholesterol</li><li>Unhealthy diet</li><li>Having obesity</li></ul>What are the symptoms of a stroke?<p>The symptoms of a stroke often happen quickly. They include:</p><ul><li>Sudden numbness or weakness of the face, arm, or leg (especially on one side of the body)</li><li>Sudden confusion, trouble speaking, or understanding speech</li><li>Sudden trouble seeing in one or both eyes</li><li>Sudden difficulty walking, dizziness, loss of balance or coordination</li><li>Sudden severe headache with no known cause</li></ul><p>The F.A.S.T. test can help you remember what to look for if you think someone is having a stroke. Think "FAST" and look for:</p><ul><li>Face drooping on one side when smiling.</li><li>Arm weakness occurs when the arms are raised, and one arm drifts downward.</li><li>Speech is slurred or strange.</li><li>Time to call 911.</li></ul><p>If you think that you or someone else is having a stroke, call 911 right away. Every minute counts during a stroke.</p>How are strokes diagnosed?<p>To make a diagnosis, your health care provider will</p><ul><li>Ask about your symptoms and medical history</li><li>Do a physical exam, including a check of
	<ul><li>Your mental alertness</li><li>Your coordination and balance</li><li>Any numbness or weakness in your face, arms, and legs</li><li>Any trouble speaking and seeing clearly</li></ul>
</li><li>Run some tests, which may include
	<ul><li>Diagnostic imaging of the brain, such as a CT scan or MRI.</li><li>Heart tests, which can help detect heart problems or blood clots that may have led to a stroke. Possible tests include an electrocardiogram (EKG) and an echocardiography.</li></ul>
</li></ul>What are the treatments for stroke?<p>Treatments for stroke include medicines, surgery, and rehabilitation. Which treatments you get depend on the type of stroke and the stage of treatment. The different stages are:</p><ul><li>Acute treatment, to try to stop a stroke while it is happening</li><li>Post-stroke rehabilitation, to overcome the disabilities caused by the stroke</li><li>Prevention, to prevent a first stroke or, if you have already had one, prevent another stroke</li></ul><p>Acute treatments for ischemic stroke are usually medicines:</p><ul><li>You may get tPA, (tissue plasminogen activator), a medicine to dissolve the blood clot. You can only get this medicine within 4 hours of when your symptoms started. The sooner you can get it, the better your chance of recovery.</li><li>If you cannot get that medicine, you may get medicine that helps stop platelets from clumping together to form blood clots. Or you may get a blood thinner to keep existing clots from getting bigger.</li><li>If you have carotid artery disease, you may also need a procedure to open your blocked carotid artery.</li></ul><p>Acute treatments for hemorrhagic stroke focus on stopping the bleeding. The first step is to find the cause of bleeding in the brain. The next step is to control it:</p><ul><li>If <span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">high blood pressure</span></span> is the cause of bleeding, you may be given blood pressure medicines.</li><li>If an aneurysm is the cause, you may need aneurysm clipping or coil embolization. These are surgeries to prevent further leaking of blood from the aneurysm. It also can help prevent the aneurysm from bursting again.</li><li>If an arteriovenous malformation (AVM) is the cause of a stroke, you may need an AVM repair. An AVM is a tangle of faulty arteries and veins that can rupture within the brain. An AVM repair may be done through</li><ul><li>Surgery</li><li>Injecting a substance into the blood vessels of the AVM to block blood flow</li><li>Radiation to shrink the blood vessels of the AVM</li></ul></ul><p>Stroke rehabilitation can help you relearn skills you lost because of the damage. The goal is to help you become as independent as possible and to have the best possible quality of life.</p><p>Prevention of another stroke is also important, since having a stroke increases the risk of getting another one. Prevention may include heart-healthy lifestyle changes and medicines.</p>Can strokes be prevented?<p>If you have already had a stroke or are at risk of having a stroke, you can make some heart-healthy lifestyle changes to try to prevent a future stroke:</p><ul><li>Eating a heart-healthy diet</li><li>Aiming for a healthy weight</li><li>Managing stress</li><li>Getting regular physical activity</li><li>Quitting smoking</li><li>Managing your blood pressure and cholesterol levels</li></ul><p>If these changes aren't enough, you may need medicine to control your risk factors.</p><p>NIH: National Institute of Neurological Disorders and Stroke</p></content>

    <document rank="16" url="https://medlineplus.gov/adrenalglanddisorders.html">
      <content name="title">Adrenal Gland Disorders</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Adrenal Insufficiency</content>
      <content name="altTitle">CAH</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Congenital Adrenal Hyperplasia</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What are adrenal glands?<p>Your adrenal glands are two small organs that sit on top of each kidney. The adrenal glands make different types of hormones you need to stay alive and healthy. Hormones are chemicals that travel in your bloodstream and control how different parts of your body work.</p><p>The adrenal glands make the hormones cortisol, aldosterone, adrenaline, and noradrenaline. They also make hormones that your body uses to make sex hormones (estrogen and testosterone). All of these hormones do many important jobs, including:</p><ul><li>Turning food into energy and managing blood sugar levels</li><li>Balancing salt and water</li><li>Keeping blood pressure normal</li><li>Responding to illness and stress (your "fight or flight" response)</li><li>Timing when and how fast a child develops sexually</li><li>Supporting pregnancy</li></ul>What are adrenal gland disorders?<p>When you have an adrenal gland disorder, your body makes too much or too little of one or more hormones. The symptoms depend on the type of problem you have and how much it affects the hormone levels in your body.</p><p>There are many types of adrenal gland disorders, including:</p><ul><li>Addison's Disease - a condition in which the adrenal glands don't make enough cortisol</li><li>Cushing's Syndrome - a condition caused by too much cortisol in the body, often from taking steroid medicines for a long time</li><li>Aldosterone-producing adenoma - a benign tumor (not cancer) that makes too much aldosterone and may cause serious <span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">high blood pressure</span></span></li><li>Hereditary paraganglioma-pheochromocytoma - an inherited condition causing different types of tumors that make adrenaline and other hormones. Some tumors may become cancerous.</li><li>Adrenal gland cancer - cancerous tumors, including adrenocortical carcinoma and neuroblastoma</li><li>Congenital Adrenal Hyperplasia (CAH) - a group of inherited disorders in which the adrenal glands don't make enough cortisol. The most common type is 21-hydroxylase deficiency (also called CAH1). In the United States, newborn babies get a blood test to see if they have CAH. People born with CAH may not have symptoms until childhood or later in life.</li></ul>What causes adrenal gland disorders?<p>The cause of adrenal gland disorders depends on the type of disorder you have. Causes can include:</p><ul><li>Medicines such as steroids</li><li>A problem in another gland, such as the pituitary gland. The pituitary gland releases hormones that affect how the adrenal glands work.</li><li>Changes in genes (mutations). These changes can cause the adrenal glands to make too much or too little of one or more hormones.</li><li>Infections</li></ul><p>In many cases the cause of the problem isn't clear.</p>How are adrenal gland disorders diagnosed?<p>Health care providers use different tests to check for adrenal disorders depending on your symptoms and health history.  For example, you may have tests of your blood, urine (pee), or saliva (spit).  These tests check your hormone levels. Your provider may order x-rays, CT scans, or MRI scans to look for tumors.</p>What are the treatments for adrenal gland disorders?<p>Different types of adrenal gland disorders have different treatments. They include medicines and surgery.  Radiation therapy is sometimes a treatment for tumors. There are treatments to cure certain adrenal gland disorders. For other disorders, treatments can manage your symptoms.</p><p>NIH: National Institute of Child Health and Human Development</p></content>

    <document rank="17" url="https://medlineplus.gov/cardiacrehabilitation.html">
      <content name="title">Cardiac Rehabilitation</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Heart Diseases--Rehabilitation</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>Cardiac rehabilitation (rehab) is a medically supervised program to help people who have:</p><ul><li>A heart attack</li><li>Angioplasty or coronary artery bypass grafting for coronary heart disease</li><li>A heart valve repair or replacement</li><li>A heart transplant or a lung transplant</li><li>Angina</li><li>Heart failure</li></ul><p>The goal is to help you return to an active life, and to reduce the risk of further heart problems.  A team of specialists will create a plan for you that includes exercise training, education on heart healthy living, and counseling to reduce stress.  You will learn how to reduce your risk factors, such as <span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">high blood pressure, high blood</span></span> cholesterol, depression, and diabetes. Being overweight, having obesity, smoking, and not exercising are other risk factors. </p><p>NIH: National Heart, Lung, and Blood Institute</p></content>

    <document rank="18" url="https://medlineplus.gov/chronickidneydisease.html">
      <content name="title">Chronic Kidney Disease</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">CKD</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>You have two kidneys, each about the size of your fist.  Their main job is to filter your blood. They remove wastes and extra water, which become urine. They also keep the body's chemicals balanced, help control blood pressure, and make hormones.</p><p>Chronic kidney disease (CKD) means that your kidneys are damaged and can't filter blood as they should. This damage can cause wastes to build up in your body. It can also cause other problems that can harm your health. Diabetes and <span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">high blood pressure</span></span> are the most common causes of CKD.</p><p>The kidney damage occurs slowly over many years. Many people don't have any symptoms until their kidney disease is very advanced. Blood and urine tests are the only way to know if you have kidney disease.</p><p>Treatments cannot cure kidney disease, but they may slow kidney disease. They include medicines to lower blood pressure, control blood sugar, and lower cholesterol. CKD may still get worse over time. Sometimes it can lead to kidney failure. If your kidneys fail, you will need  dialysis or a kidney transplantation.</p><p>You can take steps to keep your kidneys healthier longer:</p><ul><li>Choose foods with less  salt (sodium)</li><li>Control your blood pressure; your health care provider can tell you what your blood pressure should be</li><li>Keep your blood sugar in the target range, if you have diabetes</li><li>Limit the amount of alcohol you drink</li><li>Choose foods that are healthy for your heart: fruits, vegetables, whole grains, and low-fat dairy foods</li><li>Lose weight if you are overweight</li><li>Be physically active</li><li>Don't smoke</li></ul><p>NIH: National Institute of Diabetes and Digestive and Kidney Diseases</p></content>
      <content name="mesh">Renal Insufficiency, Chronic</content>
      <content name="groupName">Kidneys and Urinary System</content>

    <document rank="19" url="https://medlineplus.gov/cirrhosis.html">
      <content name="title">Cirrhosis</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Liver Cirrhosis</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Hepatic fibrosis</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>Cirrhosis is scarring of the liver. Scar tissue forms because of injury or long-term disease. Scar tissue cannot do what healthy liver tissue does - make protein, help fight infections, clean the blood, help digest food and store energy.   Cirrhosis can lead to  :</p><ul><li>Easy bruising or bleeding, or nosebleeds</li><li>Swelling of the abdomen or legs </li><li>Extra sensitivity to medicines</li><li><span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">High blood pressure</span></span> in the vein entering the liver</li><li>Enlarged veins called varices in the esophagus and stomach. Varices can bleed suddenly.</li><li> Kidney failure</li><li>Jaundice</li><li>Severe itching</li><li>Gallstones</li></ul><p>A small number of people with cirrhosis get liver cancer.</p><p>Your doctor will diagnose cirrhosis with blood tests, imaging tests, or a biopsy.</p><p>Cirrhosis has many causes. In the United States, the most common causes are chronic alcoholism and hepatitis. Nothing will make the scar tissue disappear, but treating the cause can keep it from getting worse. If too much scar tissue forms, you may need to consider a liver transplant.  </p><p>NIH: National Institute of Diabetes and Digestive and Kidney Diseases</p></content>

    <document rank="20" url="https://medlineplus.gov/diabetescomplications.html">
      <content name="title">Diabetes Complications</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Diabetic complications</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is diabetes?<p>If you have diabetes, your blood glucose, or blood sugar, levels are too high. Glucose comes from the foods you eat. A hormone called insulin helps the glucose get into your cells to give them energy.</p><p> With type 1 diabetes, your body does not make insulin. With type 2 diabetes, your body does not make or use insulin well. Without enough insulin, the glucose stays in your blood.</p>What health problems can diabetes cause?<p>Over time, having too much glucose in your blood can cause complications, including:</p><ul><li>Eye disease, due to changes in fluid levels, swelling in the tissues, and damage to the blood vessels in the eyes.</li><li>Foot problems, caused by damage to the nerves and reduced blood flow to your feet.</li><li>Gum disease and other dental problems, because a high amount of glucose in your saliva helps harmful bacteria grow in your mouth. The bacteria combine with food to form a soft, sticky film called plaque. Plaque also comes from eating foods that contain sugars or starches. Some types of plaque cause gum disease and bad breath. Other types cause tooth decay and cavities.</li><li>Heart disease and stroke, caused by damage to your blood vessels and the nerves that control your heart and blood vessels.</li><li>Kidney disease, due to damage to the blood vessels in your kidneys. Many people with diabetes develop <span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">high blood pressure</span></span>. That can also damage your kidneys.</li><li>Nerve problems (diabetic neuropathy), caused by damage to the nerves and the small blood vessels that nourish your nerves with oxygen and nutrients.</li><li>Sexual and bladder problems, caused by damage to the nerves and reduced blood flow in the genitals and bladder.</li><li>Skin conditions, some of which are caused by changes in the small blood vessels and reduced circulation. People with diabetes are also more likely to have infections, including skin infections.</li></ul>What other problems can people with diabetes have?<p>If you have diabetes, you need to watch out for blood glucose levels that are too high (hyperglycemia) or too low for you (hypoglycemia). These can happen quickly and can become dangerous. Some of the causes include having another illness or infection and certain medicines. They can also happen if you don't get the right amount of diabetes medicines. To try to prevent these problems, make sure to take your diabetes medicines correctly, follow your diabetic diet, and check your blood glucose regularly.</p><p>NIH: National Institute of Diabetes and Digestive and Kidney Diseases</p></content>

    <document rank="21" url="https://medlineplus.gov/eyediseases.html">
      <content name="title">Eye Diseases</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Ophthalmology</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Pinguecula</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What diseases can affect the eye?<p>Even if your eyes feel healthy, you could have a problem and not know it. That's why regular eye exams are so important. Refractive errors are the most common type of vision problem that makes it hard to see clearly. But some eye conditions or diseases don't have any symptoms and can lead to a permanent loss of vision.</p><p>Some common eye diseases and conditions include:</p><ul><li>Cataracts, which happens when the clear lens in the front of your eye becomes cloudy. Cataracts are common as you get older and can lead to vision loss.</li><li>Optic nerve disorders, which includes glaucoma. Glaucoma usually happens when the fluid pressure inside your eyes slowly rises.</li><li>Retinal disorders, which are problems with the nerve layer at the back of your eye.</li><li>Macular degeneration, which is a disease that destroys your sharp, central vision.</li><li>Diabetic eye problems, which can cause vision loss and blindness.</li><li>Conjunctivitis, which is an infection also known as pink eye. It can cause pink or red itchy eyes. Some types of pink eye can spread very easily.</li></ul>What are the symptoms of eye diseases?<p>Some eye conditions and diseases do not always have symptoms. Early detection through a comprehensive dilated eye exam could help your eye care provider detect diseases in the early stages. Proper treatment could help slow or prevent vision loss.</p><p>Get regular eye exams and call your eye care professional right away if you notice any of these symptoms:</p><ul><li>A sudden change in vision</li><li>Everything looks dim</li><li>Flashes of light</li><li>Eye pain</li><li>Double vision</li><li>Fluid coming from your eye</li><li>Inflammation (swelling)</li></ul>Who is more likely to develop eye diseases?<p>Getting older increases your risk of some eye diseases. Other things that may put you at a higher risk of some eye diseases can include if you:</p><ul><li>Are overweight or obese</li><li>Have a family history of eye disease</li><li>Are African American, Hispanic, or Native American</li><li>Have certain health conditions such as Diabetic eye problems, or <span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">high blood pressure</span></span></li></ul>What are the treatments for eye diseases?<p>Treatment may depend on the eye condition or disease but can include:</p><ul><li>A new prescription for eyeglasses or contacts</li><li>Use of bright lights or magnifying lenses for reading and other activities</li><li>Medicines</li><li>Prescription eye drops or ointments</li><li>Laser treatment</li><li>Surgery</li></ul><p>Depending on your eye condition or disease, treatment may slow vision loss but may not restore vision. Talk to your provider about what treatment is best for you.</p>Can eye diseases be prevented?<p>Your best defense is to have regular checkups. Be sure to tell your provider if any eye diseases run in your family.</p><p>You can also help take care of your vision by making lifestyle changes to lower your risk of health conditions that can cause vision problems. This could include by:</p><ul><li>Protecting your eyes from the sun</li><li>Wearing protective eyewear while using tools or playing certain sports</li><li>Getting regular physical activity</li><li>Staying at a healthy weight</li><li>Quitting smoking (or not starting)</li><li>Eating a healthy diet</li></ul><p>NIH: National Eye Institute</p></content>

    <document rank="22" url="https://medlineplus.gov/healthysleep.html">
      <content name="title">Healthy Sleep</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Sleep Hygiene</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is sleep?<p>While you are sleeping your brain and body functions are still active. Sleep is a natural process that helps your body restore energy, supports learning and memory, and keeps you healthy.</p><p>During sleep, your brain cycles through different stages of non-rapid eye movement (non-REM) sleep and rapid eye movement (REM) sleep. Non-REM sleep has three stages that range from light to deep sleep, while REM sleep is when most dreaming occurs. During each stage, your brain waves, breathing, heart rate, and body temperature change throughout the night.  Certain phases of sleep help you:</p><ul><li>Feel rested and energetic the next day.</li><li>Learn new information, get insight, and form memories.</li><li>Give your heart and blood vessels a rest.</li><li>Release growth hormone, which helps children grow and assists in muscle repair and tissue healing for everyone.</li><li>Release sex hormones, which contribute to puberty and fertility.</li><li>Maintain a healthy balance of hormones that control hunger and fullness.</li><li>Strengthen your immune system by producing cytokines (hormones that help the body fight infection).</li></ul><p>You need all the stages to get a healthy sleep.</p>How much sleep do I need?<p>The amount of sleep you need depends on your age, lifestyle, health, and sleep patterns. The general recommendations for sleep are:</p><ul><li>Babies (4-12 months): 12-16 hours a day (including naps)</li><li>Children (1-2 years): 11-14 hours a day (including naps)</li><li>Children (3-5 years): 10-13 hours a day (including naps)</li><li>Children (6-12 years): 9-12 hours a day</li><li>Teens (13-18 years): 8-10 hours a day</li><li>Adults (18 years and older): 7-9 hours a day</li></ul><p>During puberty, teenagers' biological clocks shift. They tend to fall asleep later and wake up later, which can conflict with the early school start times. This is one reason many teenagers don't get enough sleep.</p><p>Some people think adults need less sleep as they age, but there is no evidence to support this. However, older adults often get less sleep or spend less time in the deep, restful stages of sleep. They also wake up more easily.</p><p>It's not just the number of hours of sleep you get that matters. The quality of the sleep you get is also important. People whose sleep is frequently interrupted might not get enough of certain stages of sleep. 
If you are wondering whether you are getting enough sleep, including quality sleep, ask yourself:</p><ul><li>Do I have trouble getting up in the morning?</li><li>Do I have trouble focusing during the day?</li><li>Do I doze off during the day?</li></ul><p>If you answered yes to any of these questions, you may need to improve your sleep habits.</p>What are the health effects of not getting enough sleep?<p>Sleep is important for overall health. When you don't get enough sleep (sleep deprivation), it does more than just make you feel tired. It can impair your ability to think clearly, react quickly, and form memories. This increases your risk of making mistakes, taking risks, or getting into accidents.</p><p>Sleep deprivation can also affect your mood, leading to:</p><ul><li>Irritability</li><li>Problems with relationships, especially for children and teenagers</li><li>Depression</li><li>Anxiety</li></ul><p>It can also affect your physical health. Not getting enough sleep, or getting poor-quality sleep, increases your risk of:</p><ul><li><span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">High blood pressure</span></span></li><li>Heart disease</li><li>Stroke</li><li>Kidney disease</li><li>Obesity</li><li>Type 2 diabetes</li></ul><p>Not getting enough sleep can also lower your levels of hormones that help children grow and help the body build muscle, repair tissue, and fight infections.</p><p>Sleep deprivation also magnifies the effect of alcohol. A tired person who drinks too much alcohol will be more impaired than a well-rested person who drinks the same amount.</p>How can I get better sleep?<p>You can take steps to improve your sleep habits. First, make sure that you allow yourself enough time for sleep. Getting enough sleep each night may help you feel happier and more productive during the day.</p><p>To improve your sleep habits, it also may help to:</p><ul><li>Go to bed and wake up at the same time every day.</li><li>Avoid caffeine, especially in the afternoon and evening.</li><li>Avoid nicotine.</li><li>Exercise regularly, but not too close to bedtime.</li><li>Avoid alcoholic drinks before bed.</li><li>Avoid large meals and drinks late at night.</li><li>Don't take a nap after 3 p.m.</li><li>Relax before bed by taking a bath, reading or listening to calm music.</li><li>Keep your bedroom cool, dark, and quiet.</li><li>Get rid of distractions such as TVs, computers, and phones.</li><li>Get enough sunlight exposure during the day.</li><li>If you can't fall asleep for 20 minutes, get up and do something relaxing.</li><li>See a health care provider if you have continued trouble sleeping. You may have a sleep disorder, such as insomnia or sleep apnea. In some cases, your provider may suggest trying a sleep study or other treatments.</li></ul><p>If you are a shift worker, getting good sleep can be more challenging. You may also want to:</p><ul><li>Take naps and extend your total sleep time</li><li>Keep the lights bright at work</li><li>Limit shift changes to help your body adjust</li><li>Limit caffeine to the first part of your shift</li><li>Remove sound and light distractions in your bedroom during daytime sleep (for example, use light-blocking curtains or earplugs)</li></ul></content>
      <content name="mesh">Sleep Hygiene</content>
      <content name="groupName">Brain and Nerves</content>

    </document>
    <document rank="23" url="https://medlineplus.gov/hemorrhagicstroke.html">
      <content name="title">Hemorrhagic Stroke</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Brain Hemorrhage </content>
      <content name="altTitle">Cerebral Hemorrhage </content>
      <content name="altTitle">Subarachnoid Hemorrhage </content>
      <content name="altTitle">Intracerebral Hemorrhage</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Subarachnoid Hemorrhage</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is a hemorrhagic stroke?<p>A stroke happens when there is a loss of blood flow to part of the brain. Your brain cells cannot get the oxygen and nutrients they need from blood, and they start to die within a few minutes. This can cause lasting brain damage, long-term disability, or even death.</p><p>There are two main types of strokes, ischemic and hemorrhagic. A hemorrhagic stroke is less common but often more severe. It occurs when a blood vessel in the brain bursts, causing bleeding (hemorrhage) in or around the brain. This bleeding increases pressure inside the skull and damages brain tissue. Within minutes, brain cells begin to die.</p>What are the types of hemorrhagic stroke?<p>Hemorrhagic strokes are classified by where the bleeding happens:</p><ul><li>Intracerebral hemorrhage. Bleeding occurs directly into the brain tissue. This is the most common type of hemorrhagic stroke.</li><li>Subarachnoid hemorrhage. Bleeding occurs in the space between the brain and the thin layers of tissues covering it. This often happens when a weak spot in an artery (called an aneurysm) bursts on the brain's surface.</li></ul>What causes hemorrhagic stroke?<p>Common causes include:</p><ul><li>Aneurysm. A bulge or "ballooning" in a weakened artery wall that can burst.</li><li>Arteriovenous malformation (AVM). A tangled group of abnormal blood vessels that can rupture inside the brain.</li><li><span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">High blood pressure (hypertension</span></span>). Over time, it can weaken blood vessel walls and increase the risk of them breaking open.</li><li>Head trauma. Brain injury can cause bleeding.</li></ul>What are the symptoms of a hemorrhagic stroke?<p>Stroke symptoms often happen suddenly. They include:</p><ul><li>Sudden numbness or weakness of the face, arm or leg (especially on one side of the body)</li><li>Sudden confusion, trouble speaking, or understanding speech</li><li>Sudden trouble seeing in one or both eyes</li><li>Sudden trouble walking, dizziness, loss of balance or coordination</li><li>Sudden severe headache with no known cause</li></ul><p>The F.A.S.T. test can help you remember what to look for if you think someone is having a 
stroke. Think "FAST" and look for:</p><ul><li>Face drooping on one side when smiling.</li><li>Arm weakness occurs when the arms are raised, and one arm drifts downward.</li><li>Speech is slurred or strange.</li><li>Time to call 911.</li></ul>How is a hemorrhagic stroke diagnosed?<p>To make a diagnosis, your health care provider may do a physical exam, ask about your symptoms and medical history, and use imaging tests to check for bleeding in your brain.</p>How is hemorrhagic stroke treated?<p>It is important to treat strokes as quickly as possible. For a hemorrhagic stroke, the first step is to find the cause of bleeding in the brain and stop it. Treatment may involve medicines or, in some cases, surgery to stop the bleeding or relieve pressure on the brain.</p><p>After the stroke, rehabilitation can help people recover lost abilities and regain independence.</p>Can a hemorrhagic stroke be prevented?<p>The best way to prevent stroke is by making heart-healthy lifestyle changes to lower your risk. If lifestyle changes aren't enough, your provider may prescribe medicine to help manage your risk factors.</p><p>NIH: National Institute of Neurological Disorders and Stroke</p></content>

    <document rank="24" url="https://medlineplus.gov/liverdiseases.html">
      <content name="title">Liver Diseases</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Alagille Syndrome</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Gilbert Disease</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Hepatic Diseases</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>Your liver is the largest organ inside your body. It helps your body digest food, store energy, and remove poisons.</p><p>There are many kinds of liver diseases:</p><ul><li>Diseases caused by  viruses, such as hepatitis A, hepatitis B, and hepatitis C</li><li>Diseases caused by drugs, poisons, or too much alcohol. Examples include fatty liver disease and cirrhosis.</li><li>Liver cancer</li><li>Inherited diseases, such as hemochromatosis and Wilson disease</li></ul><p>Symptoms of liver disease can vary, but they often include swelling of the abdomen and legs, bruising easily, changes in the color of your stool and urine, and jaundice, or yellowing of the skin and eyes. Sometimes there are no symptoms. Tests such as imaging tests and liver function tests can check for liver damage and help to diagnose liver diseases.</p></content>
      <content name="mesh">Liver Diseases</content>
      <content name="groupName">Digestive System</content>
      <content name="snippet"> Your liver is the largest organ inside your body. It helps your body digest food, store energy, and remove poisons. There are many kinds of liver diseases:  ... </content>

    <document rank="0" url="https://medlineplus.gov/headache.html">
      <content name="title"><span class="qt0">Headache</span></content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Cluster <span class="qt0">Headache</span></content>
      <content name="altTitle">Tension <span class="qt0">Headache</span></content>
      <content name="altTitle">Vascular <span class="qt0">Headache</span></content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>Almost everyone has had a <span class="qt0">headache. Headache</span> is the most common form of pain. It's a major reason people miss days at work or school or visit the doctor.</p><p>The most common type of <span class="qt0">headache</span> is a tension <span class="qt0">headache</span>. Tension headaches are due to tight muscles in your shoulders, neck, scalp and jaw. They are often related to stress, depression or anxiety. You are more likely to get tension headaches if you work too much, don't get enough sleep, miss meals, or use alcohol.</p><p>Other common types of headaches include migraines, cluster headaches, and sinus headaches. Most people can feel much better by making lifestyle changes, learning ways to relax and taking pain relievers.</p><p>Not all headaches require a doctor's attention. But sometimes headaches warn of a more serious disorder. Let your health care provider know if you have sudden, severe headaches. Get medical help right away if you have a <span class="qt0">headache</span> after a blow to your head, or if you have a <span class="qt0">headache</span> along with a stiff neck, fever, confusion, loss of consciousness, or pain in the eye or ear.</p><p>NIH: National Institute of Neurological Disorders and Stroke</p></content>
