    <document rank="1" url="https://medlineplus.gov/migraine.html">
      <content name="title">Migraine</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Vascular <span class="qt0">Headache</span></content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What are migraines?<p>Migraines are a recurring type of <span class="qt0">headache</span>. They cause moderate to severe pain that is throbbing or pulsing. The pain is often on one side of your head. You may also have other symptoms, such as nausea and weakness. You may be sensitive to light and sound.</p>What causes migraines?<p>Researchers believe that migraine has a genetic cause. There are also a number of factors that can trigger a migraine. These factors vary from person to person, and they include:</p><ul><li>Stress</li><li>Anxiety</li><li>Hormonal changes in women</li><li>Bright or flashing lights</li><li>Loud noises</li><li>Strong smells</li><li>Medicines</li><li>Too much or not enough sleep</li><li>Sudden changes in weather or environment</li><li>Overexertion (too much physical activity)</li><li>Tobacco</li><li>Caffeine or caffeine withdrawal</li><li>Skipped meals</li><li>Medication overuse (taking medicine for migraines too often)</li></ul><p>Some people have found that certain foods or ingredients can trigger headaches, especially when they are combined with other triggers. These foods and ingredients include:</p><ul><li>Alcohol</li><li>Chocolate</li><li>Aged cheeses</li><li>Monosodium glutamate (MSG)</li><li>Some fruits and nuts</li><li>Fermented or pickled goods</li><li>Yeast</li><li>Cured or processed meats</li></ul>Who is at risk for migraines?<p>About 12% of Americans get migraines. They can affect anyone, but you are more likely to have them if you:</p><ul><li>Are a woman. Women are three times more likely than men to get migraines.</li><li>Have a family history of migraines. Most people with migraines have family members who have migraines.</li><li>Have other medical conditions, such as depression, anxiety, bipolar disorder, sleep disorders, and epilepsy.</li></ul>What are the symptoms of migraines?<p>There are four different phases of migraines. You may not always go through every phase each time you have a migraine.:</p><ul><li>Prodome. This phase starts up to 24 hours before you get the migraine. You have early signs and symptoms, such as food cravings, unexplained mood changes, uncontrollable yawning, fluid retention, and increased urination.</li><li>Aura. If you have this phase, you might see flashing or bright lights or zig-zag lines. You may have muscle weakness or feel like you are being touched or grabbed. An aura can happen just before or during a migraine.</li><li><span class="qt0">Headache</span>. A migraine usually starts gradually and then becomes more severe. It typically causes throbbing or pulsing pain, which is often on one side of your head. But sometimes you can have a migraine without a <span class="qt0">headache</span>. Other migraine symptoms may include</li><ul><li>Increased sensitivity to light, noise, and odors</li><li>Nausea and vomiting</li><li>Worsened pain when you move, cough, or sneeze</li></ul><li>Postdrome (following the <span class="qt0">headache</span>). You may feel exhausted, weak, and confused after a migraine. This can last up to a day.</li></ul><p>Migraines are more common in the morning; people often wake up with them. Some people have migraines at predictable times, such as before menstruation or on weekends following a stressful week of work.</p>How are migraines diagnosed?<p>To make a diagnosis, your health care provider will:</p><ul><li>Take your medical history</li><li>Ask about your symptoms</li><li>Do a physical and neurological exam</li></ul><p>An important part of diagnosing migraines is to rule out other medical conditions which could be causing the symptoms. So you may also have blood tests, an MRI or CT scan, or other tests.</p>How are migraines treated?<p>There is no cure for migraines. Treatment focuses on relieving symptoms and preventing additional attacks.</p><p>There are different types of medicines to relieve symptoms. They include triptan drugs, ergotamine drugs, and pain relievers. The sooner you take the medicine, the more effective it is.</p><p>There are also other things you can do to feel better:</p><ul><li>Resting with your eyes closed in a quiet, darkened room</li><li>Placing a cool cloth or ice pack on your forehead</li><li>Drinking fluids</li></ul><p>There are some lifestyle changes you can make to prevent migraines:</p><ul><li>Stress management strategies, such as exercise, relaxation techniques, and biofeedback, may reduce the number and severity of migraines. Biofeedback uses electronic devices to teach you to control certain body functions, such as your heartbeat, blood pressure, and muscle tension.</li><li>Make a log of what seems to trigger your migraines. You can learn what you need to avoid, such as certain foods and medicines. It also help you figure out what you should do, such as establishing a consistent sleep schedule and eating regular meals.</li><li>Hormone therapy may help some women whose migraines seem to be linked to their menstrual cycle</li><li>If you have obesity, losing weight may also be helpful</li></ul><p>If you have frequent or severe migraines, you may need to take medicines to prevent further attacks. Talk with your health care provider about which drug would be right for you.</p><p>Certain natural treatments, such as riboflavin (vitamin B2) and coenzyme Q10, may help prevent migraines. If your magnesium level is low, you can try taking magnesium. There is also an herb, butterbur, which some people take to prevent migraines. But butterbur may not be safe for long-term use. Always check with your health care provider before taking any supplements.</p><p>NIH: National Institute of Neurological Disorders and Stroke</p></content>

    <document rank="2" url="https://medlineplus.gov/concussion.html">
      <content name="title">Concussion</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Brain Concussion</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Brain Injury</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>A concussion is a type of brain injury.  It involves a short loss of normal brain function. It happens when a hit to the head or body causes your head and brain to move rapidly back and forth. This sudden movement can cause the brain to bounce around or twist in the skull, creating chemical changes in your brain. Sometimes it can also stretch and damage your brain cells.</p><p>Sometimes people call a concussion a "mild" brain injury. It is important to understand that while concussions may not be life-threatening, they can still be serious.</p><p>Concussions are a common type of sports injury. Other causes of concussions include blows to the head, bumping your head when you fall, being violently shaken, and car accidents.</p><p>Symptoms of a concussion may not start right away; they may start days or weeks after the injury. Symptoms may include a <span class="qt0">headache</span> or neck pain. You may also have nausea, ringing in your ears, dizziness, or tiredness. You may feel dazed or not your normal self for several days or weeks after the injury. Consult your health care professional if any of your symptoms get worse, or if you have more serious symptoms such as:</p><ul><li>Convulsions or seizures</li><li>Drowsiness or inability to wake up</li><li>A <span class="qt0">headache</span> that gets worse and does not go away</li><li>Weakness, numbness, or decreased coordination</li><li>Repeated vomiting or nausea</li><li>Confusion</li><li>Slurred speech</li><li>Loss of consciousness</li></ul><p>To diagnose a concussion, your health care provider will do a physical exam and will ask about your injury. You will most likely have a neurological exam, which checks your vision, balance, coordination, and reflexes. Your health care provider may also evaluate your memory and thinking. In some cases, you may also have a scan of the brain, such as a CT scan or an MRI. A scan can check for bleeding or inflammation in the brain, as well as a skull fracture (break in the skull).</p><p>Most people recover fully after a concussion, but it can take some time. Rest is very important after a concussion because it helps the brain to heal. In the very beginning, you may need to limit physical activities or activities that involve a lot of concentration, such as studying, working on the computer, or playing video games. Doing these may cause concussion symptoms (such as <span class="qt0">headache</span> or tiredness) to come back or get worse. Then when your health care provider says that it is ok, you can start to return to your normal activities slowly.</p><p>Centers for Disease Control and Prevention</p></content>

    <document rank="3" url="https://medlineplus.gov/traumaticbraininjury.html">
      <content name="title">Traumatic Brain Injury</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">TBI</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Acquired brain injury</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is traumatic brain injury (TBI)?<p>Traumatic brain injury (TBI) is a sudden injury that causes damage to the brain. It may happen when there is a blow, bump, or jolt to the head. This is a closed head injury. A TBI can also happen when an object penetrates the skull. This is a penetrating injury.</p><p>Symptoms of a TBI can be mild, moderate, or severe. Concussions are a type of mild TBI. The effects of a concussion can sometimes be serious, but most people completely recover in time. More severe TBI can lead to serious physical and psychological symptoms, coma, and even death.</p>What causes traumatic brain injury (TBI)?<p>The main causes of TBI depend on the type of head injury:</p><ul><li>Some of the common causes of a closed head injury include 
	<ul><li>Falls. This is the most common cause in adults age 65 and older.</li><li>Motor vehicle crashes. This is the most common cause in young adults.</li><li>Sports injuries</li><li>Being struck by an object</li><li>Child abuse. This is the most common cause in children under age 4.</li><li>Blast injuries due to explosions</li></ul>
</li><li>Some of the common causes of a penetrating injury include 
	<ul><li>Being hit by a bullet or shrapnel</li><li>Being hit by a weapon such as a hammer, knife, or baseball bat</li><li>A head injury that causes a bone fragment to penetrate the skull</li></ul>
</li></ul><p>Some accidents such as explosions, natural disasters, or other extreme events can cause both closed and penetrating TBI in the same person.</p>Who is at risk for traumatic brain injury (TBI)?<p>Certain groups are at higher risk of TBI:</p><ul><li>Men are more likely to get a TBI than women. They are also more likely to have serious TBI.</li><li>Adults aged 65 and older are at the greatest risk for being hospitalized and dying from a TBI</li></ul>What are the symptoms of traumatic brain injury (TBI)?<p>The symptoms of TBI depend on the type of injury and how serious the brain damage is.</p><p>The symptoms of mild TBI can include:</p><ul><li>A brief loss of consciousness in some cases. However, many people with mild TBI remain conscious after the injury.</li><li><span class="qt0">Headache</span></li><li>Confusion</li><li>Lightheadedness</li><li>Dizziness</li><li>Blurred vision or tired eyes</li><li>Ringing in the ears</li><li>Bad taste in the mouth</li><li>Fatigue or lethargy</li><li>A change in sleep patterns</li><li>Behavioral or mood changes</li><li>Trouble with memory, concentration, attention, or thinking</li></ul><p>If you have a moderate or severe TBI, you may have those same symptoms. You may also have other symptoms such as:</p><ul><li>A <span class="qt0">headache</span> that gets worse or does not go away</li><li>Repeated vomiting or nausea</li><li>Convulsions or seizures</li><li>Not being able to wake up from sleep</li><li>Larger than normal pupil (dark center) of one or both eyes. This is called dilation of the pupil.</li><li>Slurred speech</li><li>Weakness or numbness in the arms and legs</li><li>Loss of coordination</li><li>Increased confusion, restlessness, or agitation</li></ul>How is traumatic brain injury (TBI) diagnosed?<p>If you have a head injury or other trauma that may have caused a TBI, you need to get medical care as soon as possible. To make a diagnosis, your health care provider:</p><ul><li>Will ask about your symptoms and the details of your injury</li><li>Will do a neurologic exam</li><li>May do imaging tests, such as a CT scan or MRI</li><li>May use a tool such as the Glasgow coma scale to determine how severe the TBI is. This scale measures your ability to open your eyes, speak, and move.</li><li>May do neuropsychological tests to check how your brain is functioning</li></ul>What are the treatments for traumatic brain injury (TBI)?<p>The treatments for TBI depend on many factors, including the size, severity, and location of the brain injury.</p><p>For mild TBI, the main treatment is rest. If you have a <span class="qt0">headache</span>, you can try taking over-the-counter pain relievers. It is important to follow your health care provider's instructions for complete rest and a gradual return to your normal activities. If you start doing too much too soon, it may take longer to recover. Contact your provider if your symptoms are not getting better or if you have new symptoms.</p><p>For moderate to severe TBI, the first thing health care providers will do is stabilize you to prevent further injury. They will manage your blood pressure, check the pressure inside your skull, and make sure that there is enough blood and oxygen getting to your brain.</p><p>Once you are stable, the treatments may include:</p><ul><li>Surgery to reduce additional damage to your brain, for example to
	<ul><li>Remove hematomas (clotted blood)</li><li>Get rid of damaged or dead brain tissue</li><li>Repair skull fractures</li><li>Relieve pressure in the skull</li></ul>
</li><li>Medicines to treat the symptoms of TBI and to lower some of the risks associated with it, such as
	<ul><li>Anti-anxiety medication to lessen feelings of nervousness and fear</li><li>Anticoagulants to prevent blood clots</li><li>Anticonvulsants to prevent seizures</li><li>Antidepressants to treat symptoms of depression and mood instability</li><li>Muscle relaxants to reduce muscle spasms</li><li>Stimulants to increase alertness and attention</li></ul>
</li><li>Rehabilitation therapies, which can include therapies for physical, emotional, and cognitive difficulties:
	<ul><li>Physical therapy, to build physical strength, coordination, and flexibility</li><li>Occupational therapy, to help you learn or relearn how to perform daily tasks, such as getting dressed, cooking, and bathing</li><li>Speech therapy, to help you to with speech and other communication skills and treat swallowing disorders</li><li>Psychological counseling, to help you learn coping skills, work on relationships, and improve your emotional well-being</li><li>Vocational counseling, which focuses on your ability to return to work and deal with workplace challenges</li><li>Cognitive therapy, to improve your memory, attention, perception, learning, planning, and judgment</li></ul></li></ul><p>Some people with TBI may have permanent disabilities. A TBI can also put you at risk for other health problems such as anxiety, depression, and post-traumatic stress disorder. Treating these problems can improve your quality of life.</p>Can traumatic brain injury (TBI) be prevented?<p>There are steps you can take to prevent head injuries and TBIs:</p><ul><li>Always wear your seatbelt and use car seats and booster seats for children</li><li>Never drive under the influence of drugs or alcohol</li><li>Wear a properly fitting helmet when riding a bicycle, skateboarding, and playing sports like hockey and football</li><li>Prevent falls by
	<ul><li>Making your house safer. For example, you can install railings on the stairs and grab bars in the tub, get rid of tripping hazards, and use window guards and stair safety gates for young children.</li><li>Improving your balance and strength with regular physical activity</li></ul>
</li></ul></content>

    <document rank="4" url="https://medlineplus.gov/childhoodbraintumors.html">
      <content name="title">Childhood Brain Tumors</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>Brain tumors are abnormal growths inside the skull. They are among the most common types of childhood cancers. Some are benign tumors, which aren't cancer. They can still be serious. Malignant tumors are cancerous.</p><p>Childhood brain and spinal cord tumors can cause headaches and other symptoms. However, other conditions can also cause the same symptoms. Check with a doctor if your child has any of the following problems:</p><ul><li>Morning <span class="qt0">headache or headache</span> that goes away after vomiting</li><li>Frequent nausea and vomiting</li><li>Vision, hearing, and speech problems</li><li>Loss of balance or trouble walking</li><li>Unusual sleepiness</li><li>Personality changes</li><li>Seizures</li><li>Increased head size in infants</li></ul><p>The symptoms are not the same in every child.</p><p>Doctors use physical and neurological exams, lab tests, and imaging to diagnose brain tumors. Most childhood brain tumors are diagnosed and removed in surgery.</p><p>Treatment for children is sometimes different than for an adult. Long-term side effects are an important issue. The options also depend on the type of tumor and where it is. Removal of the tumor is often possible. If not, radiation, chemotherapy, or both may be used.</p><p>NIH: National Cancer Institute</p></content>

    <document rank="5" url="https://medlineplus.gov/encephalitis.html">
      <content name="title">Encephalitis</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is encephalitis? <p>Encephalitis is inflammation (swelling) of the brain. It can happen when an infection or medical condition that affects the brain activates your immune system. The inflammation can cause a wide range of symptoms. In extreme cases, it can lead to brain damage, stroke, or even death.</p>What causes encephalitis?<p>There are different types of encephalitis, based on what the cause is. The two main types are infectious encephalitis and autoimmune encephalitis.</p><p>Infectious encephalitis is usually caused by a virus. In fact, viruses are the most common cause of encephalitis. Some of the different viruses that cause it include:</p><ul><li>Herpes viruses, including herpes simplex (HSV), the Epstein-Barr virus (which causes infectious mononucleosis) and the varicella-zoster virus (which causes chickenpox).</li><li>Viruses you can get if you are bitten by an infected tick, such as tick-borne encephalitis (TBE virus) and Powassan virus.</li><li>Viruses you can get if you are bitten by an infected mosquito, such as eastern equine encephalitis virus, West Nile virus, and La Crosse virus.</li><li>Enteroviruses, which are a common group of viruses that mostly cause mild illness or respiratory infection. These infections usually happen in the summer and fall.</li></ul><p>Bacteria, fungi, and parasites can also cause infectious encephalitis. But this is not common.</p><p>Autoimmune encephalitis happens when your immune system mistakenly attacks healthy brain cells. It can be triggered by conditions such as certain cancers, benign tumors, and infections. Sometimes the cause is not known.</p>Who is more likely to get encephalitis?<p>Anyone can get encephalitis, but you are more likely to get it if you:</p><ul><li>Have a weakened immune system, for example from having HIV or taking certain medicines. These could include medicines taken after an organ transplant, certain chemotherapy medicines, and specialized treatments for certain autoimmune diseases.</li><li>Are a young child or older adult.</li><li>Live in areas where there are ticks and mosquitoes that carry viruses that can cause encephalitis.</li></ul>What are the symptoms of encephalitis?<p>The symptoms of encephalitis can vary a lot, depending on how severe it is. Many people do not have any symptoms. Others may have mild flu-like symptoms such as fever, fatigue, <span class="qt0">headache</span>, or body aches. If encephalitis becomes more serious, it can cause:</p><ul><li>Severe <span class="qt0">headache</span></li><li>Stiff neck</li><li>Vomiting</li><li>Seizures</li><li>Behavior changes</li><li>Drowsiness</li><li>Muscle weakness</li><li>Partial paralysis in your arms and legs</li><li>Coma</li></ul><p>Encephalitis can be dangerous in infants. Their symptoms may include:</p><ul><li>Fever</li><li>Lethargy (weakness or drowsiness)</li><li>Poor feeding</li><li>Vomiting</li><li>Body stiffness</li><li>Unusual irritability or crying</li><li>A full or bulging fontanel (the soft spot on the top of the head)</li></ul><p>If you or your child is having symptoms of encephalitis, it's important to get medical care right away.</p>How is encephalitis diagnosed?<p>To find out if you have encephalitis, your health care provider:</p><ul><li>Will do a physical exam</li><li>Will take your medical history, which includes asking about your symptoms</li><li>May do a neurologic exam</li><li>May order imaging tests, such as a brain CT scan or MRI</li><li>May order an EEG (electroencephalography), which use small electric sensors to measure your brain activity</li><li>May order blood and cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) tests</li></ul>What are the treatments for encephalitis?<p>Most people with encephalitis will need treatment in the hospital. Depending on the cause, treatments may include antiviral medicines, antibiotics, corticosteroids, and other medicines.</p><p>For some types of encephalitis, there is no medicine to treat it. But rest, nutrition, and fluids can help your body fight the infection and relieve symptoms. </p><p>Some people may need physical, speech, and occupational therapy once the illness is under control.</p>Can encephalitis be prevented?<p>There are steps you can take to help prevent encephalitis that is caused by infections:</p><ul><li>Use good hygiene, including washing your hands often with soap and water.</li><li>Don't share food, drinks, utensils, and glasses with other people.</li><li>Get vaccines for viruses that can cause encephalitis.</li><li>Avoid mosquito and tick bites, for example by:
  <ul><li>Wearing insect repellent with DEET or another U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA)-registered insect repellent. Make sure to follow the instructions for using the repellant.</li><li>Wearing clothes that cover your arms, legs and feet.</li><li>Treating your clothing and gear with products containing 0.5% permethrin before you go in grassy or woody areas.</li></ul>
</li></ul><p>NIH: National Institute of Neurological Disorders and Stroke</p></content>

    <document rank="6" url="https://medlineplus.gov/lymedisease.html">
      <content name="title">Lyme Disease</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Erythema Migrans</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Post-Treatment Lyme Disease Syndrome</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Borreliosis</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is Lyme disease?<p>Lyme disease is a bacterial infection you get from the bite of an infected tick. At first, Lyme disease usually causes symptoms such as a rash, fever, <span class="qt0">headache</span>, and fatigue.  But if it is not treated early, the infection can spread to your joints, heart, and nervous system. Prompt treatment can help you recover quickly.</p>What causes Lyme disease?<p>Lyme disease is caused by bacteria. In the United States, this is usually a bacterium called Borrelia burgdorferi. It spreads to humans through the bite of an infected tick. The ticks that spread it are blacklegged ticks (or deer ticks). They are usually found in the:</p><ul><li>Northeast</li><li>Mid-Atlantic</li><li>Upper Midwest</li><li>Pacific coast, especially northern California</li></ul><p>These ticks can attach to any part of your body. But they are often found in hard-to-see areas such as your groin, armpits, and scalp. Usually, the tick must be attached to you for more than 24 hours to spread the bacterium to you.</p>Who is more likely to get Lyme disease?<p>Most tick bites happen in the warmer months (April-September), when ticks are most active and people spend more time outdoors. But you can also get bitten during the rest of the year, especially if the temperatures are warmer than usual.</p><p>Most tick bites happen in the summer months when ticks are most active and people spend more time outdoors. But you can get bitten in the warmer months of early fall, or even late winter if temperatures are unusually high. And if there is a mild winter, ticks may come out earlier than usual.</p>What are the symptoms of Lyme disease?<p>Early symptoms of Lyme disease start between 3 to 30 days after an infected tick bites you. The symptoms can include:</p><ul><li>A red rash called erythema migrans (EM). Most people with Lyme disease get this rash. It gets bigger over several days and may feel warm. It is usually not painful or itchy. As it starts to get better, parts of it may fade. Sometimes this makes the rash look like a "bull's-eye."</li><li>Fever</li><li>Chills</li><li><span class="qt0">Headache</span></li><li>Fatigue</li><li>Muscle and joint aches</li><li>Swollen lymph nodes ("swollen glands")</li></ul><p>If the infection is not treated, it can spread to your joints, heart, and nervous system. The symptoms may include:</p><ul><li>Severe headaches and neck stiffness.</li><li>More EM rashes on other areas of your body.</li><li>Facial palsy, which is a weakness in your facial muscles. It can cause drooping on one or both sides of your face.</li><li>Arthritis with severe joint pain and swelling, especially in your knees and other large joints.</li><li>Pain that comes and goes in your tendons, muscles, joints, and bones.</li><li>Heart palpitations, which are feelings that your heart is skipping a beat, fluttering, pounding, or beating too hard or too fast.</li><li>An irregular heart beat (Lyme carditis).</li><li>Episodes of dizziness or shortness of breath.</li><li>Inflammation of the brain and spinal cord.</li><li>Nerve pain.</li><li>Shooting pains, numbness, or tingling in the hands or feet.</li></ul>How is Lyme disease diagnosed?<p>To make a diagnosis, your health care provider will consider:</p><ul><li>Your symptoms</li><li>How likely it is that you were exposed to infected blacklegged ticks</li><li>How likely it is that you could have other illnesses that cause similar symptoms</li><li>The results of any lab tests</li></ul><p>Most Lyme disease tests check for antibodies made by the body in response to infection. These antibodies can take several weeks to develop. If you are tested right away, it may not show that you have Lyme disease, even if you have it. So you may need to have another test later.</p>What are the treatments for Lyme disease?<p>Lyme disease is treated with antibiotics. The earlier you are treated, the better; it gives you the best chance of fully recovering quickly.</p><p>After treatment, some patients may still have pain, fatigue, or difficulty thinking that lasts more than 6 months. This is called post-treatment Lyme disease syndrome (PTLDS). Researchers don't know why some people have PTLDS. There is no proven treatment for PTLDS; long-term antibiotics have not been shown to help. However, there are ways to help with the symptoms of PTLDS. If you have been treated for Lyme disease and still feel unwell, contact your health care provider about how to manage your symptoms. Most people do get better with time. But it can take several months before you feel better.</p>Can Lyme disease be prevented?<p>To prevent Lyme disease, you should lower your risk of getting a tick bite:</p><ul><li>Avoid areas where ticks live, such as grassy, brushy, or wooded areas. If you are hiking, walk in the center of the trail to avoid brush and grass.</li><li>Use an Environmental Protection Agency (EPA)-registered insect repellent when you go outdoors. They are evaluated to make sure they are safe and effective. Make sure that the repellant has one of these ingredients: DEET, picaridin, IR3535, oil of lemon eucalyptus, or para-menthane-diol. It is important to follow the instructions on the label.</li><li>Treat your clothing and gear with a repellant containing 0.5% permethrin</li><li>Wear light-colored protective clothing, so you can easily see any ticks that get on you</li><li>Wear a long-sleeved shirt and long pants. Also tuck your shirt into your pants and your pant legs into your socks.</li><li>Check yourself, your children, and your pets daily for ticks. Carefully remove any ticks you find.</li><li>Take a shower and wash and dry your clothes at high temperatures after being outdoors</li></ul><p>Centers for Disease Control and Prevention</p></content>

    <document rank="7" url="https://medlineplus.gov/westnilevirus.html">
      <content name="title">West Nile Virus</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">WNV</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is West Nile Virus?<p>West Nile virus (WNV) is an infectious disease. It is most often spread by the bite of an infected mosquito. Most people have no symptoms, while some may develop mild symptoms. But WNV can sometimes cause serious symptoms if the virus enters your brain.</p>How does West Nile Virus spread?<p>WNV usually spreads to people through the bite of an infected mosquito. However, in rare cases, it can spread from person-to-person. This can happen through an organ transplant or blood transfusion. If you are preganant you can spread WNV to your fetus during pregnancy or delivery. And after delivery, it can spread to the baby during breastfeeding.</p>Who is more likely to develop West Nile Virus?<p>Anyone could be bitten by an infected mosquito and get WNV. But certain people are at higher risk for more serious illness they get a WNV infection. They include people who:</p><ul><li>Are over age 60</li><li>Have certain medical conditions, such as cancer, diabetes, high blood pressure, or kidney disease</li><li>Had an organ transplant</li></ul>What are the symptoms of West Nile Virus?<p>Symptoms can be mild to severe. But most people have no symptoms or mild symptoms.</p><p>Mild symptoms of WNV can include:</p><ul><li>Fever</li><li><span class="qt0">Headache</span></li><li>Body aches</li><li>Vomiting</li><li>Diarrhea</li><li>Skin rash</li></ul><p>These symptoms usually go away on their own within a few days to several weeks. But some people will have weakness or fatigue that lasts for weeks or months.</p><p>Although rare, If West Nile virus enters your brain, it can be life-threatening. It may cause inflammation of the brain (encephalitis) or inflammation of the tissue that surrounds the brain and spinal cord (meningitis).</p><p>Serious symptoms of WNV can include:</p><ul><li>High fever</li><li><span class="qt0">Headache</span></li><li>Neck stiffness</li><li>Numbness</li><li>Coma</li><li>Muscle weakness</li><li>Vision loss</li></ul>How is West Nile Virus diagnosed?<p>To find out if you have WNV, your health care provider:</p><ul><li>Will do a physical exam</li><li>Will take your medical history, including asking about your symptoms</li><li>May order blood tests</li></ul>What are the treatments for West Nile Virus?<p>There are no specific vaccines or treatments for human WNV disease. But rest, fluids, and over-the-counter pain medicines may relieve some symptoms. You may need to be hospitalized if you have serious symptoms.</p>Can West Nile Virus be prevented?<p>There are no vaccines or medicines to prevent WNV. The best way to avoid WNV is to help prevent mosquito bites:</p><ul><li>Wear insect repellent with DEET or another U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA)-registered insect repellent. Make sure to follow the instructions for using the repellent.</li><li>Wear long-sleeved shirts and pants if you're outside.</li><li>Get rid of mosquito breeding sites by emptying standing water from flowerpots, buckets, or barrels.</li><li>Stay indoors between dusk and dawn, when mosquitoes are most active.</li><li>Use screens on windows to keep mosquitoes out.</li></ul><p>Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC)</p></content>

    <document rank="8" url="https://medlineplus.gov/anemia.html">
      <content name="title">Anemia</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Iron Deficiency Anemia</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Iron poor blood</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>If you have anemia, your blood does not carry enough oxygen to the rest of your body.  The most common cause of anemia is not having enough iron. Your body needs iron to make hemoglobin. Hemoglobin is an iron-rich protein that gives the red color to blood. It carries oxygen from the lungs to the rest of the body.</p><p>Anemia has three main causes: blood loss, lack of red blood cell production, and high rates of red blood cell destruction.</p><p>Conditions that may lead to anemia include:</p><ul><li>Heavy periods</li><li>Pregnancy</li><li>Ulcers</li><li>Colon polyps or colon cancer</li><li>Inherited disorders</li><li>A diet that does not have enough iron, folic acid or vitamin B12</li><li>Blood disorders such as sickle cell anemia and thalassemia, or cancer</li><li>Aplastic anemia, a condition that can be inherited or acquired</li><li>G6PD deficiency, a metabolic disorder</li></ul><p>Anemia can make you feel tired, cold, dizzy, and irritable. You may be short of breath or have a <span class="qt0">headache</span>.</p><p>Your doctor will diagnose anemia with a physical exam and blood tests. Treatment depends on the kind of anemia you have.</p><p>NIH: National Heart, Lung, and Blood Institute</p></content>
      <content name="mesh">Anemia</content>
      <content name="groupName">Blood, Heart and Circulation</content>

    <document rank="9" url="https://medlineplus.gov/arteriovenousmalformations.html">
      <content name="title">Arteriovenous Malformations</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">AVM</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What are arteriovenous malformations (AVMs)?<p>Arteriovenous malformations (AVMs) are defects in your vascular system.  Your vascular system is your body's network of blood vessels. It includes your:</p><ul><li>Arteries, which carry oxygen-rich blood from your heart to your tissues and organs.</li><li>Veins, which carry the blood and waste products back to your heart.</li><li>Capillaries, which are tiny blood vessels that connect your small arteries to your small veins.</li></ul><p>An AVM is an abnormal tangle of arteries and veins. They are connected to each other, with no capillaries between them. Without the capillaries, the blood flow from the arteries goes directly into the veins at a faster rate than normal. Because of this, the nearby tissue does not get all the oxygen it would normally get. This lack of oxygen can lead to tissue damage and the death of nerve cells and other cells. The fast rate of blood flow can also increase the blood pressure inside the arteries and veins. This can weaken the arteries and veins. A weakened artery or blood vessel could burst or leak blood.</p>What causes arteriovenous malformations (AVMs)?<p>AVMs are rare. The cause of AVMs is unknown. Most of the time, people are born with them. But sometimes they can appear shortly after birth or later in life.</p>What are the symptoms of arteriovenous malformations (AVMs)?<p>The symptoms of AVM will depend on where the AVM is located. They can happen anywhere in the body, but they are more common in the brain or spinal cord. Most people with brain or spinal cord AVMs have few, if any, major symptoms. But if a weakened blood vessel bursts, it can spill blood into the brain (called a hemorrhage). Severe hemorrhages can cause a stroke and brain damage.</p><p>If an AVM is causing symptoms, they can include:</p><ul><li>Seizures</li><li><span class="qt0">Headache</span></li><li>Pain in the area where the AVM is located</li><li>Vision problems</li><li>Muscle weakness</li><li>Problems with movement and speech</li><li>Confusion</li><li>Dizziness</li><li>Loss of consciousness</li></ul>How are arteriovenous malformations (AVMs) diagnosed?<p>To find out if you have an AVM, your health care provider:</p><ul><li>Will ask about your symptoms and medical history.</li><li>Will do a physical exam. This may include listening for a bruit. A bruit is whooshing sound caused by the rapid blood flow through the arteries and veins of an AVM.</li><li>May order imaging tests, such as a CT scan, MRI, ultrasound, or cerebral angiography. For a cerebral angiography, you are injected with a special dye that helps the blood vessels in your brain show up on x-rays.</li></ul>What are the treatments for arteriovenous malformations (AVMs)?<p>Treatments for AVMs will depend on factors such as:</p><ul><li>Where the AVM is located</li><li>Its size</li><li>Your symptoms</li><li>Your overall health</li><li>Your risk of bleeding</li></ul><p>The treatment options may include:</p><ul><li>Monitoring for any signs that may mean you have an increased risk of hemorrhage.</li><li>Medicines to help with the symptoms from AVMs.</li><li>Surgery, which may be done if you have a higher-than-usual risk of bleeding. There are a few different types of surgery for AVMs. In some cases, you may have more than one type. Surgery can be risky, especially when it's done on the brain or spinal cord. So you and your provider will need to discuss the risks and benefits of doing the surgery before you make a decision. </li></ul><p>NIH: National Institute of Neurological Disorders and Stroke</p></content>

    <document rank="10" url="https://medlineplus.gov/bloodclots.html">
      <content name="title">Blood Clots</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Thrombophlebitis</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Venous Thrombosis</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Hypercoagulability</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is a blood clot?<p>A blood clot is a mass of blood that forms when platelets, proteins, and cells in the blood stick together. When you get hurt, your body forms a blood clot to stop the bleeding.  After the bleeding stops and healing takes place, your body usually breaks down and removes the blood clot. But sometimes the blood clots form where they shouldn't, your body makes too many blood clots or abnormal blood clots, or the blood clots don't break down like they should. These blood clots can be dangerous and may cause other health problems.</p><p>Blood clots can form in, or travel to, the blood vessels in the limbs, lungs, brain, heart, and kidneys. The types of problems blood clots can cause will depend on where they are:</p><ul><li>Deep vein thrombosis (DVT) is a blood clot in a deep vein, usually in the lower leg, thigh, or pelvis. It can block a vein and cause damage to your leg.</li><li>A pulmonary embolism (PE) can happen when a DVT breaks off and travels through the bloodstream to the lungs. A PE, especially if it is large, can damage your lungs and prevent your other organs from getting enough oxygen.</li><li>Cerebral venous sinus thrombosis (CVST) is a rare blood clot in the venous sinuses in your brain. Normally the venous sinuses drain blood from your brain. CVST blocks the blood from draining and can cause a hemorrhagic stroke.</li><li>Blood clots in other parts of the body can cause problems such as an ischemic stroke, a heart attack, kidney problems, kidney failure, and pregnancy-related problems.</li></ul>Who is more likely to develop blood clots?<p>Certain factors can raise your risk of developing blood clots:</p><ul><li>Atherosclerosis</li><li>Atrial fibrillation</li><li>Being overweight or having obesity</li><li>Cancer and cancer treatments</li><li>Certain genetic disorders</li><li>Certain surgeries</li><li>COVID-19</li><li>Diabetes</li><li>Family history of blood clots</li><li>Overweight and obesity</li><li>Pregnancy and giving birth</li><li>Serious injuries</li><li>Some medicines, including birth control pills</li><li>Smoking</li><li>Staying in one position for a long time, such as being in the hospital or taking a long car or plane ride</li></ul>What are the symptoms of blood clots?<p>The symptoms for blood clots can be different, depending on where the blood clot is:</p><ul><li>In the abdomen: Abdominal pain, nausea and vomiting</li><li>In an arm or leg: Sudden or gradual pain, swelling, tenderness, and warmth</li><li>In the lungs: Shortness of breath, pain with deep breathing, rapid breathing, and increased heart rate</li><li>In the brain: Trouble speaking, vision problems, seizures, weakness on one side of the body, and sudden severe <span class="qt0">headache</span></li><li>In the heart: Chest pain, sweating, shortness of breath, and pain in the left arm</li></ul>How are blood clots diagnosed?<p>To find out if you have one or more blood clots, your health care provider: </p><ul><li>Will do a physical exam.</li><li>Will take your medical history, which includes asking about your symptoms.</li><li>May order blood tests, including a D-dimer test.</li><li>May order imaging tests, such as
    <ul><li>Ultrasound.</li><li>X-rays of the veins (venography) or blood vessels (angiography) that are taken after you get an injection of special dye. The dye shows up on the x-ray and allows the provider to see how the blood flows.</li><li>CT Scan.</li></ul>
</li></ul>What are the treatments for blood clots?<p>Treatments for blood clots depend on where the blood clot is located and how severe it is. Treatments may include:</p><ul><li>Blood thinners.</li><li>Other medicines, including thrombolytics. Thrombolytics are medicines that dissolve blood clots. They are usually used where the blood clots are severe.</li><li>Surgery and other procedures to remove the blood clots</li></ul>Can blood clots be prevented?<p>You may be able to help prevent blood clots by:</p><ul><li>Moving around as soon as possible after having been confined to your bed, such as after surgery, illness, or injury</li><li>Getting up and moving around every few hours when you have to sit for long periods of time, for example if you are on a long flight or car trip</li><li>Wearing compression stockings after surgery or during a long flight</li><li>Regular physical activity</li><li>Not smoking</li><li>Staying at a healthy weight</li></ul><p>Some people at high risk may need to take blood thinners to prevent blood clots.</p></content>

    <document rank="11" url="https://medlineplus.gov/bloodthinners.html">
      <content name="title">Blood Thinners</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Anti-Platelet Drugs</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Anticoagulants</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What are blood thinners?<p>Blood thinners are medicines that prevent blood clots from forming. They do not break up clots that you already have. But they can stop those clots from getting bigger. It's important to treat blood clots, because clots in your blood vessels and heart can cause heart attacks, strokes, and blockages. </p>Who needs blood thinners?<p>You may need a blood thinner if you have:</p><ul><li>Certain heart or blood vessel diseases</li><li>An abnormal heart rhythm called atrial fibrillation</li><li>A heart valve replacement</li><li>A risk of blood clots after surgery</li><li>Congenital heart defects</li></ul>What are the different types of blood thinners?<p>There are different types of blood thinners:</p><ul><li>Anticoagulants, such as heparin or warfarin (also called Coumadin), slow down your body's process of making clots.</li><li>Antiplatelets, such as aspirin and clopidogrel, prevent blood cells called platelets from clumping together to form a clot. Antiplatelets are mainly taken by people who have had a heart attack or stroke.</li></ul>How can I take blood thinners safely?<p>When you take a blood thinner, follow the directions carefully. Blood thinners may interact with certain foods, medicines, vitamins, and alcohol. Make sure that your health care provider knows all of the medicines and supplements you are using.</p><p>You may need regular blood tests to check how well your blood is clotting. It is important to make sure that you're taking enough medicine to prevent clots, but not so much that it causes bleeding.</p>What are the side effects of blood thinners?<p>Bleeding is the most common side effect of blood thinners. They can also cause an upset stomach, nausea, and diarrhea. 

</p><p>Other possible side effects can depend on which type of blood thinner that you are taking.</p><p>Call your provider if you have any sign of serious bleeding, such as:</p><ul><li>Menstrual bleeding that is much heavier than normal</li><li>Red or brown urine</li><li>Bowel movements that are red or black</li><li>Bleeding from the gums or nose that does not stop quickly</li><li>Vomit that is brown or bright red</li><li>Coughing up something red</li><li>Severe pain, such as a <span class="qt0">headache</span> or stomachache</li><li>Unusual bruising</li><li>A cut that does not stop bleeding</li><li>A serious fall or bump on the head</li><li>Dizziness or weakness</li></ul></content>

    <document rank="12" url="https://medlineplus.gov/botox.html">
      <content name="title">Botox</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Botulinum Toxin</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Botulinum toxin type A</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>Botox is a drug made from a toxin produced by the bacterium Clostridium botulinum.  It's the same toxin that causes a life-threatening type of food poisoning called botulism. Doctors use it in small doses to treat health problems, including:</p><ul><li>Temporary smoothing of facial wrinkles and improving your appearance</li><li>Severe underarm sweating </li><li>Cervical dystonia - a neurological disorder that causes severe neck and shoulder muscle contractions </li><li>Blepharospasm - uncontrollable blinking  </li><li>Strabismus - misaligned eyes </li><li>Chronic migraine</li><li>Overactive bladder</li></ul><p>Botox injections work by weakening or paralyzing certain muscles or by blocking certain nerves. The effects last about three to twelve months, depending on what you are treating. The most common side effects are pain, swelling, or bruising at the injection site.  You could also have flu-like symptoms, <span class="qt0">headache</span>, and upset stomach.  Injections in the face may also cause temporary drooping eyelids. You should not use Botox if you are pregnant or breastfeeding.</p></content>

    <document rank="13" url="https://medlineplus.gov/brainaneurysm.html">
      <content name="title">Brain Aneurysm</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Berry Aneurysm</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Cerebral Aneurysm</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Intracranial Aneurysm</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>A brain aneurysm is an abnormal bulge or "ballooning" in the wall of an artery in the brain. They are sometimes called berry aneurysms because they are often the size of a small berry. Most brain aneurysms produce no symptoms until they become large, begin to leak blood, or burst.</p><p>If a brain aneurysm presses on nerves in your brain, it can cause signs and symptoms. These can include:</p><ul><li>A droopy eyelid </li><li>Double vision or other changes in vision </li><li>Pain above or behind the eye </li><li>A dilated pupil </li><li>Numbness or weakness on one side of the face or body </li></ul><p>Treatment depends on the size and location of the aneurysm, whether it is infected, and whether it has burst. If a brain aneurysm bursts, symptoms can include a sudden, severe <span class="qt0">headache</span>, nausea and vomiting, stiff neck, loss of consciousness, and signs of a stroke. Any of these symptoms requires immediate medical attention.</p><p>NIH: National Heart, Lung, and Blood Institute</p></content>

    <document rank="14" url="https://medlineplus.gov/carbonmonoxidepoisoning.html">
      <content name="title">Carbon Monoxide Poisoning</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">CO Poisoning</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>Carbon monoxide (CO) is a gas that has no odor or color. But it is very dangerous. It can cause sudden illness and death. CO is found in combustion fumes, such as those made by cars and trucks, lanterns, stoves, gas ranges and heating systems. CO from these fumes can build up in places that don't have a good flow of fresh air.  You can be poisoned by breathing them in.  The most common symptoms of CO poisoning are:</p><ul><li>	<span class="qt0">Headache</span></li><li>	Dizziness</li><li>	Weakness</li><li>	Nausea</li><li>	Vomiting</li><li>	Chest pain</li><li>	Confusion</li></ul><p>It is often hard to tell if someone has CO poisoning, because the symptoms may be like those of other illnesses. People who are sleeping or intoxicated can die from CO poisoning before they have symptoms. A CO detector can warn you if you have high levels of CO in your home. </p><p>Centers for Disease Control and Prevention</p></content>

    <document rank="15" url="https://medlineplus.gov/catscratchdisease.html">
      <content name="title">Cat Scratch Disease</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">CSD</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Cat scratch fever</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>Cat scratch disease (CSD) is an illness caused by the bacterium Bartonella henselae. Almost half of all cats carry the infection at some point. The infection does not make cats sick. However, the scratch or bite of an infected cat can cause symptoms in people, including :</p><ul><li>Swollen lymph nodes, especially around the head, neck, and upper limbs</li><li>Fever</li><li> <span class="qt0">Headache</span></li><li>Fatigue</li><li>Poor appetite </li></ul><p>For people with weak immune systems, CSD may cause more serious problems. The best way to avoid CSD is to avoid rough play with cats that could lead to scratches or bites. If you do get a scratch or bite, wash it well with soap and water. If the bite or scratch gets infected or if you have symptoms of CSD, call your doctor. </p><p>Centers for Disease Control and Prevention</p></content>

    <document rank="16" url="https://medlineplus.gov/chagasdisease.html">
      <content name="title">Chagas Disease</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">South American Trypanosomiasis</content>
      <content name="altTitle">American trypanosomiasis</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is Chagas disease?<p>Chagas disease, or American trypanosomiasis, is an illness that can cause serious heart and stomach problems. It is caused by a parasite. Chagas disease is common in Latin America, especially in poor, rural areas. It can also be found in the United States, most often in people who were infected before they moved to the U.S.</p>What causes Chagas disease?<p>Chagas disease is caused by the Trypanosoma cruzi  parasite. It is usually spread by infected blood-sucking bugs called triatomine bugs. They are also known as "kissing bugs" because they often bite people's faces. When one of these bugs bites you, it leaves behind infected waste. You can become infected if you rub the waste in your eyes or nose, the bite wound, or a cut.</p><p>Chagas disease can also spread through contaminated food, a blood transfusion, a donated organ, or from the pregnant parent to the baby during pregnancy.</p>Who is more likely to develop Chagas disease?<p>Kissing bugs can be found throughout the Americas, but they are more common in certain areas. The people who are most at risk for Chagas disease:</p><ul><li>Live in rural areas of Latin America</li><li>Have seen the bugs, especially in those areas</li><li>Have stayed in a house with a thatched roof or with walls that have cracks or crevices</li></ul>What are the symptoms of Chagas disease?<p>In the beginning, there may be no symptoms. Some people do get mild symptoms, such as:</p><ul><li>Fever</li><li>Fatigue</li><li>Body aches</li><li><span class="qt0">Headache</span></li><li>Loss of appetite</li><li>Diarrhea</li><li>Vomiting</li><li>A rash</li><li>A swollen eyelid</li></ul><p>These early symptoms usually go away. However, if you don't treat the infection, it stays in your body. Later, it can cause serious intestinal and heart problems such as:</p><ul><li>An  serious arrhythmia (a problem with the rate or rhythm of your heartbeat) that can cause sudden death</li><li>An enlarged heart that doesn't pump blood well</li><li>Problems with digestion and bowel movements</li><li>An increased chance of having a stroke</li></ul>How is Chagas disease diagnosed?<p>To find out if you have Chagas disease, your health care provider:</p><ul><li>Will do a physical exam</li><li>Will take your medical history, including asking about your symptoms and where you have lived and traveled</li><li>Will run a blood test for Chagas disease</li><li>May run tests to see whether the disease has affected your intestines and heart</li></ul>What are the treatments for Chagas disease?<p>Medicines can kill the parasite, especially early on. You can also treat related problems. For example, a pacemaker can help with some heart complications.</p>Can Chagas disease be prevented?<p>There are no vaccines or medicines to prevent Chagas disease. If you travel to areas where it occurs, you are at higher risk if you sleep outdoors or are staying in poor housing conditions. It is important to use insecticides to prevent bites and practice food safety.</p><p>Centers for Disease Control and Prevention</p></content>

    <document rank="17" url="https://medlineplus.gov/chickenpox.html">
      <content name="title">Chickenpox</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Varicella-Zoster Virus</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Varicella</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>Chickenpox is an infection caused by the varicella-zoster virus. Most cases are in children under age 15, but older children and adults can get it.  It spreads very easily from one person to another.</p><p>The classic symptom of chickenpox is an uncomfortable, itchy rash. The rash turns into fluid-filled blisters and eventually into scabs. It usually shows up on the face, chest, and back and then spreads to the rest of the body. Other symptoms include:</p><ul><li>Fever</li><li><span class="qt0">Headache</span></li><li>Tiredness</li><li>Loss of appetite</li></ul><p>Chickenpox is usually mild and lasts 5 to 10 days. Calamine lotions and oatmeal baths can help with itching. Acetaminophen can treat the fever. Do not use aspirin for chickenpox; that combination can cause Reye syndrome.</p><p>Chickenpox can sometimes cause serious problems. Adults, babies, teenagers, pregnant women, and those with weak immune systems tend to get sicker from it. They may need to take antiviral medicines.</p><p>Once you catch chickenpox, the virus usually stays in your body.  You probably will not get chickenpox again, but the virus can cause shingles in adults. A chickenpox vaccine can help prevent most cases of chickenpox, or make it less severe if you do get it.</p><p>Centers for Disease Control and Prevention</p></content>

    <document rank="18" url="https://medlineplus.gov/chikungunya.html">
      <content name="title">Chikungunya</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>Chikungunya is a virus that spread by the same kinds of mosquitoes that spread dengue and Zika virus. Rarely, the virus can spread from the pregnant  parent to their newborn around the time of birth. It may also spread through contact with infected blood. There have been outbreaks of chikungunya virus in Africa, Asia, Europe, the Indian and Pacific Oceans, the Caribbean, and Central and South America.</p><p>Most people who have chikungunya will have symptoms, which can be severe. They usually start 3-7 days after being bitten by an infected mosquito. The most common symptoms are fever and joint pain. Other symptoms may include <span class="qt0">headache</span>, muscle pain, joint swelling, and rash.</p><p>Most people feel better within a week. In some cases, however, the joint pain may last for months. People at risk for more severe disease include newborns, older adults, and people with diseases such as high blood pressure, diabetes, or heart disease.</p><p>A blood test can show whether you have chikungunya virus. There are no medicines to treat it. Drinking lots of fluids, resting, and taking non-aspirin pain relievers might help you feel better.</p><p>In the U.S., there is a vaccine for adults who are at a higher risk of getting chikungunya. You can also help prevent getting or spreading chikungunya by avoiding mosquito bites:</p><ul><li>Use a U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA)-registered insect repellent when you go outdoors. Make sure to follow the instructions for using the repellant.</li><li>Wear clothes that cover your arms, legs, and feet.</li><li>Stay in places that either have air conditioning (with the windows closed) or that use window and door screens.</li></ul><p>Centers for Disease Control and Prevention</p></content>

    <document rank="19" url="https://medlineplus.gov/commoncold.html">
      <content name="title">Common Cold</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Cold, Common</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is the common cold?<p>The common cold is a mild infection of your upper respiratory tract (which includes your nose and throat). Colds are probably the most common illness. Adults have an average of 2-3 colds per year, and children have even more. Colds are more common in the winter and spring, but you can get them at any time.</p>What causes the common cold?<p>More than 200 different viruses can cause a cold, but rhinoviruses are the most common type. The viruses that cause colds are very contagious. They can spread from person to person through the air and close personal contact. You can also get infected when you touch something that has the virus on it and then touch your eyes, mouth, or nose. For example, you could get a cold after you shake hands with someone who has a cold or touch a doorknob that has the germs on it, and then touch your face.</p>What are the symptoms of the common cold?<p>The symptoms of a common cold usually include:</p><ul><li>Sneezing</li><li>Stuffy nose (congestion)</li><li>Runny nose</li><li>Sore throat</li><li>Coughing</li><li><span class="qt0">Headache</span></li></ul><p>The symptoms usually start a few days after you become infected with the virus. Some symptoms can last for 10-14 days.</p>What are the treatments for the common cold?<p>There is no cure for the common cold. But there are treatments that can make you feel better while you wait for the cold to go away on its own:</p><ul><li>Getting lots of rest.</li><li>Drinking plenty of fluids.</li><li>Using a clean humidifier or cool mist vaporizer.</li><li>Gargling with warm salt water.</li><li>Using saline nose drops or sprays.</li><li>Taking over-the-counter pain or cold and cough medicines. But you need to be careful with certain medicines:
	<ul><li>Children and teens should not take aspirin.</li><li>Some cold and cough medicines contain ingredients that are not recommended for children. Talk with your child's health care provider before giving your child any cold and cough medicines.</li><li>Some cold and cough medicines contain pain relievers. If you also take a separate pain reliever with these medicines, you could be getting a dangerous amount of the pain reliever. Read the labels on the medicines and follow the instructions carefully. If you have questions, ask your provider or a pharmacist.</li></ul>
</li></ul><p>Antibiotics will not help with a cold. Antibiotics help with bacterial infections, not with viral infections such as colds.</p><p>Most people who have a cold will feel better after a week or two. However, some people who get a cold may develop other illnesses, such as bronchitis or pneumonia. This is more common in people with weakened immune systems, asthma, or other respiratory conditions. Contact your provider if you or your child have symptoms that concern you, such as:</p><ul><li>Trouble breathing or fast breathing</li><li>Dehydration</li><li>Fever that lasts longer than 4 days</li><li>Symptoms that last more than 10 days without improvement</li><li>Symptoms, such as fever or cough, that improve but then return or worsen</li><li>Worsening of chronic medical conditions</li></ul>Can the common cold be prevented?<p>There is no vaccine to protect against the common cold. But you may be able to reduce your risk of getting or spreading a cold by:</p><ul><li>Washing your hands often with soap and water for at least 20 seconds.</li><li>Avoiding touching your face, nose, or mouth with unwashed hands.</li><li>Avoiding close contact, such as kissing, shaking hands, and sharing cups and eating utensils, with others if you are sick or they are sick.</li><li>Cleaning and disinfecting surfaces that you frequently touch.</li><li>Covering coughs and sneezes with a tissue. Then throw away the tissue and wash your hands.</li><li>Staying home when sick.</li></ul><p>Centers for Disease Control and Prevention</p></content>

    <document rank="20" url="https://medlineplus.gov/ebola.html">
      <content name="title">Ebola</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Ebola disease</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Ebola hemorrhagic fever</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Ebola virus disease</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is Ebola?<p>Ebola, also known as Ebola virus disease (EVD), is a type of hemorrhagic fever. It is a rare and often deadly disease. It can be caused by several different types of Ebola viruses. There are occasional outbreaks of Ebola, and they mostly occur in Africa.</p>How does Ebola spread?<p>Researchers believe that the virus first spreads from an infected animal to a human. Ebola can then spread from human to human through direct contact with:</p><ul><li>Blood or body fluids of a person who is sick with or has died from Ebola.</li><li>Objects (such as clothes, bedding, needles, and medical equipment) contaminated with body fluids from a person who is sick with or has died from Ebola.</li><li>Semen (through oral, vaginal, or anal sex) from a man who has recovered from Ebola. The virus can remain in semen and other body fluids, even if the person no longer has symptoms.</li></ul><p>When people become infected with Ebola, they do not start developing signs or symptoms right away. They cannot spread Ebola to others until after they develop symptoms.</p>What are the symptoms of Ebola?<p>The symptoms of Ebola may appear anywhere from 2 to 21 days after exposure to the virus. The average is about 8 to 10 days. The symptoms usually include:</p><ul><li>Fever</li><li><span class="qt0">Headache</span></li><li>Joint and muscle aches</li><li>Weakness and fatigue</li><li>Sore throat</li><li>Gastrointestinal symptoms including abdominal (belly) pain, diarrhea, and vomiting</li><li>Loss of appetite</li><li>Unexplained bleeding or bruising</li></ul><p>Other symptoms may include a rash, red eyes, and hiccups.</p>How is Ebola diagnosed?<p>The early symptoms of Ebola are similar to other more common diseases. This makes it difficult to diagnose Ebola in someone who has been infected for only a few days. If a person has the symptoms of Ebola and may have been exposed to the virus, the health care provider can do a blood test for Ebola. It can take a few days after the symptoms start before Ebola shows up in the blood test.</p>What are the treatments for Ebola?<p>In the United States, there are two approved medicines to treat Ebola that is caused by one specific type of virus (Zaire ebolavirus). These medicines have not been evaluated to treat Ebola caused by other types of Ebola viruses. The medicines are monoclonal antibodies, which are immune system proteins that are created in the lab.</p><p>Whether or not there are medicines to treat a case of Ebola, there is supportive care. This type of care can improve the chances of survival, especially when given early. Supportive care includes:</p><ul><li>Giving fluids and electrolytes</li><li>Giving medicines to support blood pressure, reduce vomiting and diarrhea, and to manage fever and pain</li><li>Giving oxygen</li><li>Treating any other complications or infections</li></ul><p>People who recover may still have side effects afterwards, such as fatigue, muscle aches, eye and vision problems, and stomach pain.</p>Can Ebola be prevented?<p>In the United States, there is one vaccine that has been approved to prevent Ebola caused by one specific type of Ebola virus (Zaire ebolavirus). It may be given to health care providers who will be or may be treating Ebola patients and people who work with live Ebola virus in labs.</p><p>If you live in or plan to travel to a region where Ebola virus may be present, you can help protect yourself by avoiding contact with:</p><ul><li>Blood and body fluids of people who are sick</li><li>Semen from a man who has recovered from Ebola, until testing shows that the virus is gone from his semen</li><li>Items that could be contaminated with blood or body fluids from someone who has Ebola</li><li>Bats, forest antelopes, and nonhuman primates (such as monkeys and chimpanzees), including their blood, fluids, or meat</li></ul><p>Centers for Disease Control and Prevention</p></content>

    <document rank="21" url="https://medlineplus.gov/fifthdisease.html">
      <content name="title">Fifth Disease</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Erythema Infectiosum</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Parvovirus B19</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Parvovirus B19 infection</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is fifth disease?<p>Fifth disease, also called erythema infectiosum, is a viral infection caused by parvovirus B19. This virus only infects people; it's not the same type of parvovirus that dogs and cats can get.</p><p>Fifth disease mostly affects children. It often causes a bright red rash on their cheeks (sometimes called a "slapped cheek" rash).</p><p>In the United States, parvovirus B19 infections are more common in late winter, spring, and early summer. There are usually minor outbreaks of fifth disease about every 3 to 4 years.</p>What causes fifth disease?<p>Parvovirus B19, which causes fifth disease, can spread from person to person:</p><ul><li>Through respiratory droplets, such as saliva (spit), sputum, or nasal mucus. The droplets can spread when:
	<ul><li>Someone who has the virus coughs and sneezes</li><li>You touch an item that has the virus on it</li></ul>
</li><li>Through blood or blood products</li><li>From a pregnant parent to the baby during pregnancy</li></ul><p>You are most contagious early in your infection, when you usually only have a fever or cold-like symptoms. You are not likely to be contagious after you get later symptoms such as a rash and joint pain.</p>What are the symptoms of fifth disease?<p>About 2 out of 10 people who get a B19 parvovirus infection will have no symptoms.</p><p>But most people will get the symptoms of fifth disease, which are usually mild and can include:</p><ul><li>Fever</li><li><span class="qt0">Headache</span></li><li>Cough</li><li>Sore throat</li><li>Rashes</li><li>Joint pain</li></ul><p>The "slapped cheek" rash is more common in children. They usually get the rash a few days after they first have other symptoms. The rash can then spread to the arms, legs, chest, back, and buttocks.</p><p>Adults who get fifth disease might also have joint pain and swelling (called polyarthropathy syndrome).</p><p>Sometimes the infection can cause serious health complications affecting the nerves, joints, or blood system. This is more likely to happen if you are pregnant, have a blood disorder such as anemia, or a weakened immune system.</p><p>Because of these risks to you and some possible risks to your baby, contact your health care provider if you are pregnant and:</p><ul><li>You show signs of fifth disease (or a "slapped cheek" rash)</li><li>You may have been exposed to someone who has the infection</li></ul>How is fifth disease diagnosed?<p>There is no routine lab test to diagnose fifth disease. Providers can often diagnose fifth disease just by seeing the "slapped cheek" rash and rashes on the body.</p><p>There is testing to show whether or not you have protection (immunity) against the B19 parvovirus or if you have had a recent infection. This test is usually not needed, but your provider may order the test if you are pregnant or at higher risk for complications of the disease.</p>What are the treatments for fifth disease?<p>There is no specific treatment for fifth disease. It is usually mild and goes away on its own.</p><p>To feel better, get plenty of rest. Taking acetaminophen can help with the fever and pain. Do not give aspirin to children, because it may cause Reye syndrome. This is a rare, serious illness that can affect the brain and liver.</p>Can fifth disease be prevented?<p>There is no vaccine to prevent fifth disease. But you can help protect yourself and others by:</p><ul><li>Washing your hands often with soap and water</li><li>Covering your mouth and nose when you cough or sneeze</li><li>Avoiding touching your eyes, nose, and mouth with unwashed hands</li><li>Avoiding close contact with people who are sick</li><li>Staying home when you are sick</li></ul><p>Most people become immune to the virus after having it once.</p><p>Centers for Disease Control and Prevention</p></content>

    <document rank="22" url="https://medlineplus.gov/flushot.html">
      <content name="title">Flu Shot</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Influenza Vaccine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Flu vaccine</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is the flu?<p>The flu (influenza) is a respiratory infection. It is caused by influenza viruses that infect your nose, throat, and lungs. These viruses can spread from person to person.</p><p>Most people with the flu get better on their own, without medical care. But for some people, the flu is serious. It can cause complications and sometimes even death. People who are 65 and older, under age 5, pregnant, or have certain health conditions are at higher risk of flu complications.</p><p>Getting vaccinated against the flu each year is best way to lower your risk of the flu and its complications.</p>What are the benefits of getting a flu shot?<p>There are many reasons to get a flu shot each year:</p><ul><li>It may keep you from getting sick with the flu.</li><li>If you do get the flu, it may be less severe. You may also be less likely to have complications or need to be hospitalized with the flu.</li><li>If you have certain chronic health conditions, getting a flu shot may help prevent the flu from making your condition worse. For example, if you have COPD or diabetes, the flu can worsen your condition so much that you might need to be hospitalized. Getting a flu shot can lower this risk.</li><li>Getting the vaccine during pregnancy not only helps protect you from the flu during and after pregnancy. It also helps protect your baby from flu during their first few months of life.</li><li>It has been shown to lower the risk of severe life-threatening flu in children.</li><li>It may also protect the people around you, including those who are at higher risk of complications.</li></ul>How do flu shots work?<p>The flu vaccine causes your immune system to make antibodies about two weeks after you get the shot. These antibodies provide protection against infection with the flu viruses that are in the vaccine.</p>Who needs a flu shot?<p>The U.S Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC) recommends annual flu shots for everyone 6 months and older, with a few rare exceptions.</p><p>Flu shots are especially important for people who are at high risk of developing serious complications from the flu.</p><p>There are different types of flu vaccines. There are injectable vaccines (shots) and nasal spray vaccines.  There are also some vaccines especially for people 65 and older. Ask your health care provider which one is right for you.</p>Who should not get a flu shot?<p>There are some people who should not get a flu shot:</p><ul><li>Children younger than 6 months of age.</li><li>Have severe, life-threatening allergies to flu vaccine or any ingredient in the vaccine. This might include gelatin, antibiotics, or other ingredients.</li></ul><p>Some people need to talk to their provider before they get a flu shot. They include people who:</p><ul><li>Have an allergy to any of the ingredients in the vaccine.</li><li>Have ever had Guillain-Barre syndrome(GBS). Some people with a history of GBS should not get a flu vaccine.</li></ul><p>If you are moderately or severely ill, you usually need to wait until you recover before getting a flu shot.</p>Why do I need a flu shot every year?<p>You need to get a flu shot every year because:</p><ul><li>Your immune protection from the vaccine declines over time. You need to get the shot every year to boost that protection.</li><li>Flu viruses are constantly changing, so every year, the flu vaccine is updated. The flu vaccine will include protection against the viruses that will most likely be common during the upcoming flu season.</li></ul>When should I get the flu shot?<p>For most people who need only one dose of the flu vaccine for the season, September and October are usually good times to get it. It's best to be vaccinated by the end of October.</p><p>Some children need two doses of the flu vaccine. They should get their first dose as soon as the vaccine is available. They need to get the second dose at least four weeks after the first.</p><p>If you are pregnant, talk to your provider about when to get the vaccine. When you should get it can vary, based on how far along you are in your pregnancy.</p>Do flu shots cause side effects?<p>Flu vaccines have been shown to be safe. But like any medical product, they can cause side effects. If you have any side effects, they are usually mild and go away on their own within a few days. Common side effects include:</p><ul><li>Soreness, redness, and/or swelling from the shot</li><li><span class="qt0">Headache</span></li><li>Fever</li><li>Nausea</li><li>Muscle aches</li></ul><p>The flu shot, like other injections, can occasionally cause fainting. In rare cases, some people have a serious allergic reaction.</p><p>Centers for Disease Control and Prevention</p></content>
