    <document rank="23" url="https://medlineplus.gov/gastroenteritis.html">
      <content name="title">Gastroenteritis</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Stomach Flu</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is gastroenteritis?<p>Gastroenteritis is an inflammation of the lining of the stomach and intestines. The main symptoms include vomiting and diarrhea. It is usually not serious in healthy people, but it can sometimes lead to dehydration or cause severe symptoms.</p>What causes gastroenteritis?<p>There can be many different causes of gastroenteritis:</p><ul><li>Viruses</li><li>Bacteria</li><li>Parasites</li><li>Chemicals</li><li>Reactions to certain medicines and food</li></ul><p>Viral gastroenteritis is the most common type. It can be caused by many different viruses, including noroviruses and rotaviruses. Some people call viral gastroenteritis the "stomach flu." But this name is not medically correct. It is not caused by flu viruses. The flu is a respiratory infection that affects your nose, throat and lungs.</p><p>When gastroenteritis is caused by consuming foods or drinks contaminated with viruses, bacteria, parasites, or chemicals, this is called food poisoning.</p><p>The viruses, bacteria, and parasites that cause gastroenteritis can also spread from person to person. You could be infected when you touch something that has the germs on it and then touch your eyes, mouth, or nose.</p>What are the symptoms of gastroenteritis?<p>The symptoms of gastroenteritis include:</p><ul><li>Diarrhea</li><li>Pain or cramping in your abdomen (belly)</li><li>Nausea</li><li>Vomiting</li><li>Sometimes fever</li></ul><p>Gastroenteritis is usually not serious. But it can sometimes cause lead to dehydration or cause severe symptoms. Certain people are at higher risk for these problems. They include:</p><ul><li>Pregnant women</li><li>Older adults</li><li>People with weakened immune systems or other serious health conditions</li><li>Infants</li><li>Babies who were born prematurely or have other health conditions</li></ul><p>If you or a family member are at higher risk and have symptoms of gastroenteritis, contact a health care provider right away.
Even if you are not at high risk, it is possible to become dehydrated or have more serious symptoms. There are some warning signs to watch for. It's important to contact a provider right away if you or your child have any of them:</p><ul><li>For adults, they include:
  <ul><li>Change in mental state, such as irritability or lack of energy</li><li>Diarrhea lasting more than 2 days</li><li>High fever</li><li>Vomiting often</li><li>Six or more loose stools in a day</li><li>Severe pain in the abdomen (belly) or rectum</li><li>Stools that are black and tarry or contain blood or pus</li><li>Symptoms of dehydration, such as thirst, dry mouth, <span class="qt0">headache</span>, dark-colored urine, and urinating less than normal</li></ul>
</li><li>For infants and children, they include:
  <ul><li>Change in the child's mental state, such as irritability or lack of energy</li><li>Diarrhea lasting more than a day</li><li>Any fever in infants</li><li>High fever in older children</li><li>Frequent loose stools</li><li>Vomiting often</li><li>Severe pain in the abdomen (belly) or rectum</li><li>Signs or symptoms of dehydration, such as thirst, dry mouth, urinating less than usual or no wet diapers for 3 hours or more, and no tears when crying</li><li>Stools that are black and tarry or contain blood or pus</li></ul>
</li></ul>How is gastroenteritis diagnosed?<p>To find out if you have gastroenteritis, your provider:</p><ul><li>Will do a physical exam</li><li>Will ask about your symptoms</li><li>May do tests of your stool</li></ul>What are the treatments for gastroenteritis?<p>Usually, people with gastroenteritis get better on their own, with rest and plenty of fluids and electrolytes. Your provider may suggest that you take a probiotic. Studies suggest that some probiotics may help shorten a case of diarrhea.</p><p>People with more severe symptoms may need medicines to control nausea or vomiting. Providers may also give other medicines for certain types of gastroenteritis, such as antibiotics for some bacterial types and antiparasitic medicines for some parasitic types.</p>Can gastroenteritis be prevented?<p>Gastroenteritis cannot always be prevented. But proper hand washing, cleaning surfaces that may be infected with germs, and safe food preparation may help prevent some of the infections that can cause gastroenteritis. There are vaccines for infants to protect against rotavirus infections.</p><p>NIH: National Institute of Diabetes and Digestive and Kidney Diseases</p></content>

    <document rank="24" url="https://medlineplus.gov/h1n1fluswineflu.html">
      <content name="title">H1N1 Flu (Swine Flu)</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Swine Flu</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>Swine flu is an infection caused by a virus. It's named for a virus that pigs can get. People do not normally get swine flu, but human infections can and do happen.  In 2009 a strain of swine flu called H1N1 infected many people around the world.</p><p>The virus is contagious and can spread from human to human.   Symptoms of swine flu in people are similar to the symptoms of regular human flu and include fever, cough, sore throat, body aches, <span class="qt0">headache</span>, chills and fatigue.</p><p>There are antiviral medicines you can take to prevent or treat swine flu. There is a vaccine available to protect against swine flu. You can help prevent the spread of germs that cause respiratory illnesses like influenza by:</p><ul><li>Covering your nose and mouth with a tissue when you cough or sneeze. Throw the tissue in the trash after you use it.</li><li>Washing your hands often with soap and water, especially after you cough or sneeze. You can also use alcohol-based hand cleaners.</li><li>Avoiding touching your eyes, nose or mouth. Germs spread this way.</li><li>Trying to avoid close contact with sick people.</li><li>Staying home from work or school if you are sick.</li></ul><p>Centers for Disease Control and Prevention</p></content>

    <document rank="4" url="https://medlineplus.gov/fever.html">
      <content name="title"><span class="qt0">Fever</span></content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Hot (Temperature)</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Pyrexia</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>A <span class="qt0">fever</span> is a body temperature that is higher than normal. A normal temperature can vary from person to person, but it is usually around 98.6 °F (37 °C). A <span class="qt0">fever</span> is not a disease. It is usually a sign that your body is trying to fight an illness or infection.</p><p>Infections cause most <span class="qt0">fevers</span>. You get a <span class="qt0">fever</span> because your body is trying to kill the virus or bacteria that caused the infection. Most of those bacteria and viruses do well when your body is at your normal temperature. But if you have a <span class="qt0">fever</span>, it is harder for them to survive. <span class="qt0">Fever</span> also activates your body's immune system.</p><p>Other causes of <span class="qt0">fevers</span> include:</p><ul><li>Medicines, including some antibiotics, blood pressure medicines, and anti-seizure medicines</li><li>Heat illness</li><li>Cancers</li><li>Autoimmune diseases</li><li>Some childhood vaccines</li></ul><p>Treatment depends on the cause of your <span class="qt0">fever</span>. If the <span class="qt0">fever</span> is very high, your health care provider may recommend taking an over-the-counter medicine such as acetaminophen or ibuprofen. Adults can also take aspirin, but children with <span class="qt0">fevers</span> should not take aspirin. It is also important to drink enough liquids, to prevent dehydration.</p></content>

    <document rank="0" url="https://medlineplus.gov/hemorrhagicfevers.html">
      <content name="title">Hemorrhagic <span class="qt0">Fevers</span></content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Lassa <span class="qt0">Fever</span></content>
      <content name="altTitle">Yellow <span class="qt0">Fever</span></content>
      <content name="altTitle">VHFs</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>Viral hemorrhagic <span class="qt0">fevers</span> (VHFs) are a group of illnesses caused by four families of viruses. These include the Ebola and Marburg, Lassa <span class="qt0">fever</span>, and yellow <span class="qt0">fever</span> viruses. VHFs have common features: they affect many organs, they damage the blood vessels, and they affect the body's ability to regulate itself. Some VHFs cause mild disease, but some, like Ebola or Marburg, cause severe disease and death.</p><p>VHFs are found around the world. Specific diseases are usually limited to areas where the animals that carry them live. For example, Lassa <span class="qt0">fever</span> is limited to rural areas of West Africa where rats and mice carry the virus.</p><p>The risk for travelers is low, but you should avoid visiting areas where there are disease outbreaks. Because there are no effective treatments for some of these viral infections, there is concern about their use in bioterrorism.</p><p>Centers for Disease Control and Prevention</p></content>

    <document rank="1" url="https://medlineplus.gov/streptococcalinfections.html">
      <content name="title">Streptococcal Infections</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Rheumatic <span class="qt0">Fever</span></content>
      <content name="altTitle">Scarlet <span class="qt0">Fever</span></content>
      <content name="altTitle">Strep Throat</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Strep</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>Strep is short for Streptococcus, a type of bacteria. There are several types. Two of them cause most of the strep infections in people: group A and group B.</p><p>Group A strep causes:</p><ul><li>Strep throat - a sore, red throat. Your tonsils may be swollen and have white spots on them.</li><li>Scarlet <span class="qt0">fever</span> - an illness that follows strep throat. It causes a red rash on the body.</li><li>Impetigo - a skin infection</li><li>Toxic shock syndrome</li><li>Cellulitis and necrotizing fasciitis (flesh-eating disease)</li></ul><p>Group B strep can cause blood infections, pneumonia and meningitis in newborns. A screening test during pregnancy can tell if you have it. If you do, intravenous (IV) antibiotics during labor can save your baby's life.  Adults can also get group B strep infections, especially if they are 65 or older or already have health problems. Strep B can cause urinary tract infections, blood infections, skin infections and pneumonia in adults.</p><p>Antibiotics are used to treat strep infections.</p></content>

    <document rank="2" url="https://medlineplus.gov/tickbites.html">
      <content name="title">Tick Bites</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Anaplasmosis</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Babesiosis</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Colorado Tick <span class="qt0">Fever</span></content>
      <content name="altTitle">Ehrlichiosis</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Rocky Mountain Spotted <span class="qt0">Fever</span></content>
      <content name="altTitle">Tickborne Diseases</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Tularemia</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What are ticks?<p>Ticks are small parasites. They may look like insects, but they have eight legs and are related to spiders. Ticks feed on the blood of people and warm-blooded animals. There are many types of ticks in the United States, and they live in different parts of the country.</p><p>Ticks can be different colors and sizes. They can be light-colored, reddish brown, or dark brown. Some ticks are so small that they can be difficult to see. Ticks may get on you if you walk though areas where they live, such as tall grass, leaf litter or shrubs.</p>Why do I need to be worried about tick bites?<p>If you spend time outdoors or have pets that go outdoors, you need to beware of ticks. When they bite, certain types of ticks can pass on germs that cause different diseases. Sometimes the symptoms can be mild. In other cases, you can have serious, long-lasting health problems. Some of the diseases you can get from a tick bite (called tickborne diseases) include:</p><ul><li>Lyme disease</li><li>Alpha-gal syndrome (tick bite red meat allergy)</li><li>Babesiosis</li><li>Ehrlichiosis</li><li>Rocky Mountain spotted <span class="qt0">fever</span></li><li>Tularemia</li></ul>What happens if I get bitten by a tick?<p>You may not feel it when a tick bites you. The tick can stay attached to your body for several days. If that tick is infected, it can pass along any germs to you once it starts sucking your blood. But if you catch it and remove it before it has filled up on your blood, you are less likely to get infected.</p>How do I remove a tick?<p>If you find a tick attached to your skin, remove the tick as soon as you can. You could use a tick removal device or a fine-tipped tweezers:</p><ul><li>Using the tweezers, grab the tick as close to your skin as possible.</li><li>Pull upward with steady, even pressure. Don't twist or jerk the tick. You want to remove the whole tick in one piece if you can. If the mouth-parts of the tick break off and stay in the skin, try to remove them. But if you can't remove them easily, then leave them.</li><li>Thoroughly clean the bite area and your hands with rubbing alcohol or soap and water.</li></ul>When do I need to contact my health care provider about a tick bite?<p>Many tickborne diseases can have similar signs and symptoms. The most common are:</p><ul><li><span class="qt0">Fever</span></li><li>Chills</li><li>Aches and pains</li><li>Rash</li></ul><p>If you develop any of these symptoms within several weeks of removing a tick, contact your provider.</p>How can I prevent tick bites?<p>There are steps you can take to prevent tick bites:</p><ul><li>Avoid wooded, brushy, and grassy areas, especially during warmer months.</li><li>Wear insect repellent with DEET, picaridin or another U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA)-registered insect repellent.</li><li>Wear light-colored protective clothing.</li><li>Treat your clothing and gear with products containing 0.5% permethrin.</li><li>Tuck your pant legs into your socks and your shirt into your pants.</li><li>Remove your clothing after being outdoors. Check your clothing for ticks and remove any ticks that you find. Wash and dry your clothes at high temperatures.</li><li>Check yourself, your children, and your pets daily for ticks and carefully remove any ticks you find.</li></ul></content>

    <document rank="3" url="https://medlineplus.gov/dengue.html">
      <content name="title">Dengue</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Break-bone <span class="qt0">Fever</span></content>
      <content name="altTitle">Dengue <span class="qt0">fever</span></content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is dengue?<p>Dengue is a viral infection. It can be caused by four related viruses. You can get it if an infected mosquito bites you. It is common in warm, wet areas of the world. Although rare, it can be found in some parts of the United States. Outbreaks are more common during rainy seasons.</p>How does dengue spread?<p>Dengue spreads to people through the bite of an infected mosquito. It does not spread directly from person to person. However, the infection can be passed to the baby during pregnancy. In rare cases, it can spread through a blood transfusion, organ transplant, or needle stick injury (an accidental injury from a used needle)..</p>What are the symptoms of dengue and severe dengue?<p>About one in four people infected with dengue will get sick. For those who do get sick, the infection can be mild or severe. The symptoms of dengue include:</p><ul><li>A high <span class="qt0">fever</span></li><li>Nausea and vomiting</li><li>Rash</li><li>Aches and pains (eye pain, usually behind the eyes, and pain in the muscles, joints, or bones)</li></ul><p>The symptoms usually last two to seven days.</p><p>Severe dengue is a serious form of the illness. About 1 in 20 people who get sick with dengue will develop severe dengue. It can cause shock, internal bleeding, and even death. People are more likely to develop severe dengue if they have had dengue before, are pregnant, or are infants.</p><p>Watch for signs and symptoms of severe dengue. Warning signs usually begin about 24 - 48 hours after your <span class="qt0">fever</span> has gone away. If you or your family member have these signs, get immediate medical care:</p><ul><li>Severe abdominal (belly) pain and tenderness</li><li>Vomiting at least 3 times in 24 hours</li><li>Bleeding from the nose or gums</li><li>Vomiting blood, or blood in the stool (poop)</li><li>Feeling tired, restless, or irritable</li></ul>How is dengue diagnosed?<p>There is a blood test which can show if you have been infected with dengue.</p>What are the treatments for dengue?<p>There is no specific medicine to treat dengue. Most people will get better after about a week. But if you think you might have dengue:</p><ul><li>See a health care provider and mention any recent travel.</li><li>Rest as much as possible.</li><li>Take acetaminophen to control <span class="qt0">fever</span> and relieve pain. But do not take aspirin or ibuprofen.</li><li>Drink plenty of fluids to prevent dehydration.</li></ul><p>Severe dengue often requires care at a hospital.</p>Can dengue be prevented?<p>In the United States, a dengue vaccine is approved for children who are ages 9 - 16. They must also have previously had dengue and live in areas where dengue is common. </p><p>You can also help prevent dengue by avoiding mosquito bites:</p><ul><li>Wear insect repellent with DEET or another U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA)-registered insect repellent. Make sure to follow the instructions for using the repellant.</li><li>Wear clothes that cover your arms, legs and feet.</li><li>Close unscreened doors and windows.</li></ul><p>Centers for Disease Control and Prevention</p></content>

    <document rank="5" url="https://medlineplus.gov/hayfever.html">
      <content name="title">Hay <span class="qt0">Fever</span></content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Allergic Rhinitis</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Pollen Allergy</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Seasonal Allergies</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>Each spring, summer, and fall, trees, weeds, and grasses release tiny pollen grains into the air. Some of the pollen ends up in your nose and throat. This can trigger a type of allergy called hay <span class="qt0">fever</span>.</p><p>Symptoms can include:</p><ul><li>Sneezing, often with a runny or clogged nose</li><li>Coughing and postnasal drip</li><li>Itching eyes, nose and throat</li><li>Red and watery eyes</li><li>Dark circles under the eyes</li></ul><p>Your health care provider may diagnose hay <span class="qt0">fever</span> based on a physical exam and your symptoms. Sometimes skin or blood tests are used. Taking medicines and using nasal sprays can relieve symptoms. You can also rinse out your nose, but be sure to use distilled or sterilized water with saline. Allergy shots can help make you less sensitive to pollen and provide long-term relief.</p></content>
      <content name="mesh">Rhinitis, Allergic, Seasonal</content>

      <content name="groupName">Immune System</content>

    <document rank="6" url="https://medlineplus.gov/valleyfever.html">
      <content name="title">Valley <span class="qt0">Fever</span></content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Coccidioidomycosis</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>Valley <span class="qt0">Fever</span> is a disease caused by a fungus (or mold) called Coccidioides. The fungi live in the soil of dry areas like the southwestern U.S. You get it from inhaling the spores of the fungus. The infection cannot spread from person to person.</p><p>Anyone can get Valley <span class="qt0">Fever</span>. But it's most common among older adults, especially those 60 and older. People who have recently moved to an area where it occurs are at highest risk for infection. Other people at higher risk include:</p><ul><li>Workers in jobs that expose them to soil dust.   These include construction workers, agricultural workers, and military forces doing field training.</li><li>African Americans and Asians</li><li>Women in their third trimester of pregnancy</li><li>People with weak immune systems</li></ul><p>Valley <span class="qt0">Fever</span> is often mild, with no symptoms. If you have symptoms, they may include a flu-like illness, with <span class="qt0">fever</span>, cough, headache, rash, and muscle aches. Most people get better within several weeks or months. A small number of people may develop a chronic lung or widespread infection.</p><p>Valley <span class="qt0">Fever</span> is diagnosed by testing your blood, other body fluids, or tissues. Many people with the acute infection get better without treatment. In some cases, doctors may prescribe antifungal drugs for acute infections. Severe infections require antifungal drugs.</p><p>Centers for Disease Control and Prevention</p></content>

    <document rank="7" url="https://medlineplus.gov/ebola.html">
      <content name="title">Ebola</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Ebola disease</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Ebola hemorrhagic <span class="qt0">fever</span></content>
      <content name="altTitle">Ebola virus disease</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is Ebola?<p>Ebola, also known as Ebola virus disease (EVD), is a type of hemorrhagic <span class="qt0">fever</span>. It is a rare and often deadly disease. It can be caused by several different types of Ebola viruses. There are occasional outbreaks of Ebola, and they mostly occur in Africa.</p>How does Ebola spread?<p>Researchers believe that the virus first spreads from an infected animal to a human. Ebola can then spread from human to human through direct contact with:</p><ul><li>Blood or body fluids of a person who is sick with or has died from Ebola.</li><li>Objects (such as clothes, bedding, needles, and medical equipment) contaminated with body fluids from a person who is sick with or has died from Ebola.</li><li>Semen (through oral, vaginal, or anal sex) from a man who has recovered from Ebola. The virus can remain in semen and other body fluids, even if the person no longer has symptoms.</li></ul><p>When people become infected with Ebola, they do not start developing signs or symptoms right away. They cannot spread Ebola to others until after they develop symptoms.</p>What are the symptoms of Ebola?<p>The symptoms of Ebola may appear anywhere from 2 to 21 days after exposure to the virus. The average is about 8 to 10 days. The symptoms usually include:</p><ul><li><span class="qt0">Fever</span></li><li>Headache</li><li>Joint and muscle aches</li><li>Weakness and fatigue</li><li>Sore throat</li><li>Gastrointestinal symptoms including abdominal (belly) pain, diarrhea, and vomiting</li><li>Loss of appetite</li><li>Unexplained bleeding or bruising</li></ul><p>Other symptoms may include a rash, red eyes, and hiccups.</p>How is Ebola diagnosed?<p>The early symptoms of Ebola are similar to other more common diseases. This makes it difficult to diagnose Ebola in someone who has been infected for only a few days. If a person has the symptoms of Ebola and may have been exposed to the virus, the health care provider can do a blood test for Ebola. It can take a few days after the symptoms start before Ebola shows up in the blood test.</p>What are the treatments for Ebola?<p>In the United States, there are two approved medicines to treat Ebola that is caused by one specific type of virus (Zaire ebolavirus). These medicines have not been evaluated to treat Ebola caused by other types of Ebola viruses. The medicines are monoclonal antibodies, which are immune system proteins that are created in the lab.</p><p>Whether or not there are medicines to treat a case of Ebola, there is supportive care. This type of care can improve the chances of survival, especially when given early. Supportive care includes:</p><ul><li>Giving fluids and electrolytes</li><li>Giving medicines to support blood pressure, reduce vomiting and diarrhea, and to manage <span class="qt0">fever</span> and pain</li><li>Giving oxygen</li><li>Treating any other complications or infections</li></ul><p>People who recover may still have side effects afterwards, such as fatigue, muscle aches, eye and vision problems, and stomach pain.</p>Can Ebola be prevented?<p>In the United States, there is one vaccine that has been approved to prevent Ebola caused by one specific type of Ebola virus (Zaire ebolavirus). It may be given to health care providers who will be or may be treating Ebola patients and people who work with live Ebola virus in labs.</p><p>If you live in or plan to travel to a region where Ebola virus may be present, you can help protect yourself by avoiding contact with:</p><ul><li>Blood and body fluids of people who are sick</li><li>Semen from a man who has recovered from Ebola, until testing shows that the virus is gone from his semen</li><li>Items that could be contaminated with blood or body fluids from someone who has Ebola</li><li>Bats, forest antelopes, and nonhuman primates (such as monkeys and chimpanzees), including their blood, fluids, or meat</li></ul><p>Centers for Disease Control and Prevention</p></content>

    <document rank="8" url="https://medlineplus.gov/bacterialinfections.html">
      <content name="title">Bacterial Infections</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Flesh-Eating Bacteria</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Infections, Bacterial</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Necrotizing Fasciitis</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Q <span class="qt0">Fever</span></content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What are bacteria?<p>Bacteria are tiny organisms (living things) that have only one cell. Under a microscope, they look like balls, rods, or spirals. They can be found almost everywhere on Earth. There are lots of bacteria in and on your body. In fact, your body has about 10 times more bacteria cells than human cells.</p><p>There are many different types of bacteria. Most types don't make you sick. Some types are helpful. For example, "good" bacteria in your digestive system can help your body digest food and absorb vitamins and minerals. They can help protect you from getting sick. Bacteria are also used in making foods like yogurt and cheese.</p><p>But some types of bacteria can cause an infection and make you sick. They are called infectious bacteria. These bacteria can reproduce quickly in your body. Many of them give off toxins (poisons) that can damage your cells and make you sick. Other types can move into and damage your tissues. A few examples of bacteria that cause infections include Streptococcus, Staphylococcus, and E. coli.</p>How do bacterial infections spread?<p>There are different ways that bacterial infections may spread:</p><ul><li>Through droplets and particles that are breathed out by someone who has the infection. You might breathe in the droplets or particles, or they could land on your mouth, nose, or eyes. 
</li><li>By touching surfaces or objects that have the bacteria on them and then touching your mouth, nose, or eyes.</li><li>Through cuts, scrapes, and skin-to-skin contact with someone who has the infection.</li><li>From the pregnant parent to the baby during pregnancy.</li><li>Through contaminated food or water.</li><li>By being bitten by an infected insect or animal.</li><li>Through sexual contact (usually vaginal, anal and oral sex) with someone who has the infection.</li></ul>What are the treatments for bacterial infections?<p>Sometimes your immune system may be able to fight off a bacterial infection. But when you do need treatment, it will be with antibiotics.</p><p></p>antibiotic resistance<p></p>Can bacterial infections be prevented?<p>You can help prevent some bacterial infections by:</p><ul><li>Getting vaccines that protect against bacterial infections.</li><li>Having good hygiene habits. These include washing your hands often and covering your mouth and nose when coughing or sneezing.</li><li>Paying attention to food safety.</li><li>Cleaning and disinfecting surfaces that your frequently touch.</li><li>Preventing insect bites by using a U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA)-registered insect repellent. when you go outdoors. If you travel to an area that has a high risk of diseases from insect bites, also wear long pants, shirts, and socks.</li><li>Practicing safe sex. This means using a latex condom every time you have anal, vaginal, or oral sex. If your or your partner is allergic to latex, you can use polyurethane condoms.</li><li>Avoiding close contact with people who are sick.</li></ul></content>

    <document rank="10" url="https://medlineplus.gov/catscratchdisease.html">
      <content name="title">Cat Scratch Disease</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">CSD</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Cat scratch <span class="qt0">fever</span></content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>Cat scratch disease (CSD) is an illness caused by the bacterium Bartonella henselae. Almost half of all cats carry the infection at some point. The infection does not make cats sick. However, the scratch or bite of an infected cat can cause symptoms in people, including :</p><ul><li>Swollen lymph nodes, especially around the head, neck, and upper limbs</li><li><span class="qt0">Fever</span></li><li> Headache</li><li>Fatigue</li><li>Poor appetite </li></ul><p>For people with weak immune systems, CSD may cause more serious problems. The best way to avoid CSD is to avoid rough play with cats that could lead to scratches or bites. If you do get a scratch or bite, wash it well with soap and water. If the bite or scratch gets infected or if you have symptoms of CSD, call your doctor. </p><p>Centers for Disease Control and Prevention</p></content>

    <document rank="11" url="https://medlineplus.gov/infectiousmononucleosis.html">
      <content name="title">Infectious Mononucleosis</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">EBV Infections</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Epstein-Barr Virus Infections</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Glandular <span class="qt0">Fever</span></content>
      <content name="altTitle">Mono</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Mononucleosis</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Kissing disease</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is infectious mononucleosis (mono)?<p>Infectious mononucleosis (mono) is a disease caused by viruses.The most common cause is the Epstein-Barr virus (EBV). Mono is contagious, which means it can spread from person to person. It is common among teenagers and young adults, especially college students.</p>What causes infectious mononucleosis (mono)?<p>Mono can be caused by many different viruses. But it is most often caused by the Epstein-Barr virus (EBV). EBV is found all over the world. Most people get an EBV infection at some point in their lives, but only some of them will get the symptoms of mono. EBV infections often happen during childhood or when someone is a young adult. EBV infections in children usually do not cause symptoms. And when they do, it's hard to tell the difference between the symptoms of mono and the symptoms of other common childhood illnesses such as the flu. Teens and young adults who get EBV are more likely to have symptoms that are typical of mono.</p><p>EBV and the other viruses that cause mono are usually spread through body fluids, especially saliva (spit). This means that you can get it through kissing. That's why mono is sometimes called "the kissing disease." But you can also get these viruses if you share food, drinks, forks, spoons, or lip balm with someone who has mono. Other less common ways of getting an infection are through blood transfusions, organ transplants, and blood and semen during sexual contact.</p>What are the symptoms of infectious mononucleosis (mono)?<p>The symptoms of mono usually start four to six weeks after you get the infection. But they may start sooner in young children. The symptoms often develop slowly, and they may not all happen at the same time. They can include:</p><ul><li>Extreme fatigue</li><li><span class="qt0">Fever</span></li><li>Sore throat</li><li>Head and body aches</li><li>Swollen lymph glands in your neck and armpits</li><li>Rash</li><li>Swollen liver, spleen, or both (these are less common symptoms)</li></ul><p>Most people get better in two to four weeks. However, some people may feel fatigued for several more weeks. Occasionally, the symptoms can last for six months or longer.</p>How is infectious mononucleosis (mono) diagnosed?<p>Your healthcare provider may diagnose mono based on your symptoms and a physical exam. The exam will include checking to see if your lymph nodes, tonsils, liver, or spleen are swollen. In some cases, your provider might also order a mono test  to confirm the diagnosis.</p>What are the treatments for infectious mononucleosis (mono)?<ul><li>Drinking plenty of fluids to avoid dehydration.</li><li>Getting lots of rest.</li><li>Taking over-the-counter medications for pain and <span class="qt0">fever</span>. But don't give aspirin to children or teens because it may cause Reye syndrome. This syndrome is a rare, serious illness that can affect the brain and liver.</li></ul><p>If you have severe symptoms, your provider may suggest additional treatment based on which organs in your body are affected by the mono.</p><p>Antibiotics don't treat viral infections, so they do not help with mono. Some people do get bacterial infections such as strep throat along with mono. In that case, you probably need antibiotics to treat the bacterial infection. But you should not take penicillin antibiotics like ampicillin or amoxicillin. Those antibiotics can cause a rash in people who have mono.</p><p>Mono can cause an enlarged spleen, which could rupture and cause a medical emergency. To try to protect the spleen, providers recommend avoiding intense exercise and contact sports until you fully recover (about a month).</p>Can infectious mononucleosis (mono) be prevented?<p>There is no vaccine to protect against mono. To lower your chance of getting or spreading mono:</p><ul><li>Don't share food, drinks, forks, spoons, or lip balm with anyone, especially if you or the other person has mono.</li><li>Don't kiss someone if you or the other person has mono.</li><li>Wash your hands often with soap and water.</li></ul><p>Centers for Disease Control and Prevention</p></content>

    <document rank="12" url="https://medlineplus.gov/coldsores.html">
      <content name="title">Cold Sores</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle"><span class="qt0">Fever</span> Blister</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Herpes, Oral</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Oral Herpes</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>Cold sores are caused by a contagious virus called herpes simplex virus (HSV). There are two types of HSV. Type 1 usually causes oral herpes, or cold sores. Type 1 herpes virus infects more than half of the U.S. population by the time they reach their 20s. Type 2 usually affects the genital area.</p><p>Some people have no symptoms from the infection. But others develop painful and unsightly cold sores. Cold sores usually occur outside the mouth, on or around the lips. When they are inside the mouth, they are usually on the gums or the roof of the mouth. They are not the same as canker sores, which are not contagious.</p><p>There is no cure for cold sores. They normally go away on their own in a few weeks.  Antiviral medicines can help them heal faster.  They can also help to prevent cold sores in people who often get them. Other medicines can help with the pain and discomfort of the sores. These include ointments that numb the blisters, soften the crusts of the sores, or dry them out. Protecting your lips from the sun with sunblock lip balm can also help.</p></content>
      <content name="mesh">Herpes Labialis</content>
      <content name="groupName">Infections</content>

    <document rank="13" url="https://medlineplus.gov/mosquitobites.html">
      <content name="title">Mosquito Bites</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>Mosquitoes are insects that live all over the world. There are thousands of different species of mosquitoes; about 200 of those live in the United States.</p><p>Female mosquitoes bite animals and humans and drink a very small amount of their blood. They need protein and iron from blood to produce eggs. After drinking blood, they find some standing water and lay their eggs in it. The eggs hatch into larvae, then pupae, and then they become adult mosquitos. The males live for about a week to ten days, and the females can live up to several weeks. Some female mosquitoes can hibernate in the winter, and they can live for months.</p>What health problems can mosquito bites cause?<p>Most mosquito bites are harmless, but there are times when they can be dangerous. The ways that mosquito bites can affect humans include:</p><ul><li>Causing itchy bumps, as an immune system response to the mosquito's saliva. This is the most common reaction. The bumps usually go away after a day or two.</li><li>Causing allergic reactions, including blisters, large hives, and in rare cases, anaphylaxis. Anaphylaxis is a severe allergic reaction that affects the whole body. It is a medical emergency.</li><li>Spreading diseases to humans. Some of these diseases can be serious. Many of them do not have any treatments, and only a few have vaccines to prevent them. These diseases are more of a problem in Africa and other tropical areas of the world, but more of them are spreading to the United States. One factor is climate change, which makes the conditions in some parts of the United States more favorable to certain types of mosquitoes. Other reasons include increased trade with, and travel to, tropical and subtropical areas.</li></ul>Which diseases can mosquitoes spread?<p>Common diseases spread by mosquitoes include:</p><ul><li>Chikungunya, a viral infection that causes symptoms such as <span class="qt0">fever</span> and severe joint pain. The symptoms usually last about a week, but for some, the joint pain may last for months. Most cases of chikungunya in the United States are in people who traveled to other countries. There have been a few cases where it has spread in the United States.</li><li>Dengue, a viral infection that causes a high <span class="qt0">fever</span>, headaches, joint and muscle pain, vomiting, and a rash. Most people get better within a few weeks. In some cases, it can become very severe, even life-threatening. Dengue is rare in the United States.</li><li>Malaria, a parasitic disease that causes serious symptoms such as high <span class="qt0">fevers</span>, shaking chills, and flu-like illness. It can be life-threatening, but there are drugs to treat it. Malaria is a major health problem in many tropical and subtropical areas of the world. Almost all cases of malaria in the United States are in people who traveled to other countries.</li><li>West Nile Virus (WNV), a viral infection that often has no symptoms. In those that do have symptoms, they are usually mild, and include <span class="qt0">fever</span>, headache, and nausea. In rare cases, the virus can enter the brain, and it can be life-threatening. WNV has spread across the continental United States.</li><li>Zika Virus, a viral infection that often does not cause symptoms. One in five infected people do get symptoms, which are usually mild. They include a <span class="qt0">fever</span>, rash, joint pain, and pink eye. Besides being spread by mosquitoes, Zika can spread from mother to baby during pregnancy and cause serious birth defects. It can also spread from one partner to another during sex. There have been a few outbreaks of Zika in the southern United States.</li></ul>Can mosquito bites be prevented?<ul><li>Use an insect repellent when you go outdoors. Choose an Environmental Protection Agency (EPA)-registered insect repellent. They are evaluated to make sure they are safe and effective. Make sure that the repellant has one of these ingredients: DEET, picaridin, IR3535, oil of lemon eucalyptus, or para-menthane-diol. It is important to follow the instructions on the label.</li><li>Cover up. Wear long sleeves, long pants, and socks when outdoors. Mosquitoes may bite through thin fabric, so spray thin clothes with an EPA-registered repellent like permethrin. Don't apply permethrin directly to skin.</li><li>Mosquito-proof your home. Install or repair screens on windows and doors to keep mosquitoes out. Use air conditioning if you have it.</li><li>Get rid of mosquito breeding sites. Regularly empty standing water from your house and yard. The water could be in flowerpots, gutters, buckets, pool covers, pet water dishes, discarded tires, or birdbaths.</li><li>If you plan to travel, get information about the areas you will be going to. Find out whether there is a risk of diseases from mosquitoes, and if so, whether there is a vaccine or medicine to prevent those diseases. See a health care provider familiar with travel medicine, ideally 4 to 6 weeks before your trip.</li></ul></content>

    <document rank="14" url="https://medlineplus.gov/staphylococcalinfections.html">
      <content name="title">Staphylococcal Infections</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Vancomycin-Resistant Staphylococcus aureus</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Staph</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What are Staphylococcal (staph) infections?<p>Staphylococcus (staph) is a group of bacteria. There are more than 30 types. A type called Staphylococcus aureus causes most infections.</p><p>Staph bacteria can cause many different types of infections, including:</p><ul><li>Skin infections, which are the most common types of staph infections.</li><li>Bacteremia, an infection of the bloodstream. This can lead to sepsis, a very serious immune response to infection.</li><li>Bone infections.</li><li>Endocarditis, an infection of the inner lining of the heart chambers and valves.</li><li>Food poisoning.</li><li>Pneumonia.</li><li>Toxic shock syndrome (TSS), a life-threatening condition caused by toxins from certain types of bacteria.</li></ul>What causes staph infections?<p>Some people carry staph bacteria on their skin or in their noses, but they do not get an infection. But if they get a cut or wound, the bacteria can enter the body and cause an infection.</p><p>Staph bacteria can spread from person to person. They can also spread on objects, such as towels, clothing, door handles, athletic equipment, and remotes. If you have staph and do not handle food properly when you are preparing it, you can also spread staph to others.</p>Who is more likely to get a staph infection?<p>Anyone can develop a staph infection, but certain people are more likely to get one, including those who:</p><ul><li>Have a chronic condition such as diabetes, cancer, vascular disease, eczema, and lung disease</li><li>Have a weakened immune system, such as from HIV, medicines to prevent organ rejection, or chemotherapy</li><li>Had surgery</li><li>Are in the hospital</li><li>Use a catheter, breathing tube, or feeding tube</li><li>Have an implanted device, such as a pacemaker or artificial joint or heart valve</li><li>Have burns, especially if they are deep or cover a large area of the body</li><li>Are on dialysis</li><li>Inject illegal drugs</li><li>Do contact sports, since you may have skin-to-skin contact with others or share equipment</li></ul>What are the symptoms of staph infections?<p>The symptoms of a staph infection depend on the type of infection:</p><ul><li>Skin infections can look like pimples or boils. They may be red, swollen, and painful. Sometimes there is pus or other drainage. They can turn into impetigo, which turns into a crust on the skin, or cellulitis, a swollen, red area of skin that feels hot.</li><li>Bone infections can cause pain, swelling, warmth, and redness in the infected area. You may also have chills and a <span class="qt0">fever</span>.</li><li>Endocarditis causes some flu-like symptoms: <span class="qt0">fever</span>, chills, and fatigue. It also causes symptoms such as rapid heartbeat, shortness of breath, and fluid buildup in your arms or legs.</li><li>Food poisoning typically causes nausea and vomiting, diarrhea, and <span class="qt0">fever</span>. If you lose too many fluids, you may also become dehydrated. </li><li>Pneumonia symptoms include a high <span class="qt0">fever</span>, chills, and cough that doesn't get better. You may also have chest pain and shortness of breath.</li><li>Toxic shock syndrome (TSS) causes high <span class="qt0">fever</span>, sudden low blood pressure, vomiting, diarrhea, and confusion. You may have a sunburn-like rash somewhere on your body. TSS can lead to organ failure.</li></ul>How are staph infections diagnosed?<p>Your health care provider will do a physical exam and ask about your symptoms. Often, providers can tell if you have a staph skin infection by looking at it. To check for other types of staph infections, providers may do a culture, with a skin scraping, tissue sample, stool sample, or throat or nasal swabs. There may be other tests, such as imaging tests, depending on the type of infection.</p>What are the treatments for staph infections?<p>Treatment for staph infections is antibiotics. Depending on the type of infection, the antibiotics might be a cream, ointment, medicines (to swallow), or intravenous (IV) medicine. If you have an infected wound, your provider might drain it. Sometimes you may need surgery for bone infections.</p><p>Some staph infections, such as MRSA (methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus), are resistant to many antibiotics. There are still certain antibiotics that can treat these infections.</p>Can staph infections be prevented?<p>Certain steps can help to prevent staph infections:</p><ul><li>Use good hygiene, including washing your hands often.</li><li>Don't share towels, sheets, or clothing with someone who has a staph infection.</li><li>It's best not to share athletic equipment. If you do need to share, make sure that it is properly cleaned and dried before you use it.</li><li>Practice food safety, including not preparing food for others when you have a staph infection.</li><li>If you have a cut or wound, keep it covered.</li></ul></content>

    <document rank="15" url="https://medlineplus.gov/commoninfantandnewbornproblems.html">
      <content name="title">Common Infant and Newborn Problems</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Colic</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Diaper Rash</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>It is hard when your baby is sick.  Common health problems in babies include colds, coughs, <span class="qt0">fevers</span>, and vomiting.  Babies also commonly have skin problems, like diaper rash or cradle cap.</p><p>Many of these problems are not serious. It is important to know how to help your sick baby, and to know the warning signs for more serious problems.  Trust your intuition - if you are worried about your baby, call your health care provider right away.</p></content>

    <document rank="16" url="https://medlineplus.gov/gastroenteritis.html">
      <content name="title">Gastroenteritis</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Stomach Flu</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is gastroenteritis?<p>Gastroenteritis is an inflammation of the lining of the stomach and intestines. The main symptoms include vomiting and diarrhea. It is usually not serious in healthy people, but it can sometimes lead to dehydration or cause severe symptoms.</p>What causes gastroenteritis?<p>There can be many different causes of gastroenteritis:</p><ul><li>Viruses</li><li>Bacteria</li><li>Parasites</li><li>Chemicals</li><li>Reactions to certain medicines and food</li></ul><p>Viral gastroenteritis is the most common type. It can be caused by many different viruses, including noroviruses and rotaviruses. Some people call viral gastroenteritis the "stomach flu." But this name is not medically correct. It is not caused by flu viruses. The flu is a respiratory infection that affects your nose, throat and lungs.</p><p>When gastroenteritis is caused by consuming foods or drinks contaminated with viruses, bacteria, parasites, or chemicals, this is called food poisoning.</p><p>The viruses, bacteria, and parasites that cause gastroenteritis can also spread from person to person. You could be infected when you touch something that has the germs on it and then touch your eyes, mouth, or nose.</p>What are the symptoms of gastroenteritis?<p>The symptoms of gastroenteritis include:</p><ul><li>Diarrhea</li><li>Pain or cramping in your abdomen (belly)</li><li>Nausea</li><li>Vomiting</li><li>Sometimes <span class="qt0">fever</span></li></ul><p>Gastroenteritis is usually not serious. But it can sometimes cause lead to dehydration or cause severe symptoms. Certain people are at higher risk for these problems. They include:</p><ul><li>Pregnant women</li><li>Older adults</li><li>People with weakened immune systems or other serious health conditions</li><li>Infants</li><li>Babies who were born prematurely or have other health conditions</li></ul><p>If you or a family member are at higher risk and have symptoms of gastroenteritis, contact a health care provider right away.
Even if you are not at high risk, it is possible to become dehydrated or have more serious symptoms. There are some warning signs to watch for. It's important to contact a provider right away if you or your child have any of them:</p><ul><li>For adults, they include:
  <ul><li>Change in mental state, such as irritability or lack of energy</li><li>Diarrhea lasting more than 2 days</li><li>High <span class="qt0">fever</span></li><li>Vomiting often</li><li>Six or more loose stools in a day</li><li>Severe pain in the abdomen (belly) or rectum</li><li>Stools that are black and tarry or contain blood or pus</li><li>Symptoms of dehydration, such as thirst, dry mouth, headache, dark-colored urine, and urinating less than normal</li></ul>
</li><li>For infants and children, they include:
  <ul><li>Change in the child's mental state, such as irritability or lack of energy</li><li>Diarrhea lasting more than a day</li><li>Any <span class="qt0">fever</span> in infants</li><li>High <span class="qt0">fever</span> in older children</li><li>Frequent loose stools</li><li>Vomiting often</li><li>Severe pain in the abdomen (belly) or rectum</li><li>Signs or symptoms of dehydration, such as thirst, dry mouth, urinating less than usual or no wet diapers for 3 hours or more, and no tears when crying</li><li>Stools that are black and tarry or contain blood or pus</li></ul>
</li></ul>How is gastroenteritis diagnosed?<p>To find out if you have gastroenteritis, your provider:</p><ul><li>Will do a physical exam</li><li>Will ask about your symptoms</li><li>May do tests of your stool</li></ul>What are the treatments for gastroenteritis?<p>Usually, people with gastroenteritis get better on their own, with rest and plenty of fluids and electrolytes. Your provider may suggest that you take a probiotic. Studies suggest that some probiotics may help shorten a case of diarrhea.</p><p>People with more severe symptoms may need medicines to control nausea or vomiting. Providers may also give other medicines for certain types of gastroenteritis, such as antibiotics for some bacterial types and antiparasitic medicines for some parasitic types.</p>Can gastroenteritis be prevented?<p>Gastroenteritis cannot always be prevented. But proper hand washing, cleaning surfaces that may be infected with germs, and safe food preparation may help prevent some of the infections that can cause gastroenteritis. There are vaccines for infants to protect against rotavirus infections.</p><p>NIH: National Institute of Diabetes and Digestive and Kidney Diseases</p></content>

    <document rank="17" url="https://medlineplus.gov/westnilevirus.html">
      <content name="title">West Nile Virus</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">WNV</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is West Nile Virus?<p>West Nile virus (WNV) is an infectious disease. It is most often spread by the bite of an infected mosquito. Most people have no symptoms, while some may develop mild symptoms. But WNV can sometimes cause serious symptoms if the virus enters your brain.</p>How does West Nile Virus spread?<p>WNV usually spreads to people through the bite of an infected mosquito. However, in rare cases, it can spread from person-to-person. This can happen through an organ transplant or blood transfusion. If you are preganant you can spread WNV to your fetus during pregnancy or delivery. And after delivery, it can spread to the baby during breastfeeding.</p>Who is more likely to develop West Nile Virus?<p>Anyone could be bitten by an infected mosquito and get WNV. But certain people are at higher risk for more serious illness they get a WNV infection. They include people who:</p><ul><li>Are over age 60</li><li>Have certain medical conditions, such as cancer, diabetes, high blood pressure, or kidney disease</li><li>Had an organ transplant</li></ul>What are the symptoms of West Nile Virus?<p>Symptoms can be mild to severe. But most people have no symptoms or mild symptoms.</p><p>Mild symptoms of WNV can include:</p><ul><li><span class="qt0">Fever</span></li><li>Headache</li><li>Body aches</li><li>Vomiting</li><li>Diarrhea</li><li>Skin rash</li></ul><p>These symptoms usually go away on their own within a few days to several weeks. But some people will have weakness or fatigue that lasts for weeks or months.</p><p>Although rare, If West Nile virus enters your brain, it can be life-threatening. It may cause inflammation of the brain (encephalitis) or inflammation of the tissue that surrounds the brain and spinal cord (meningitis).</p><p>Serious symptoms of WNV can include:</p><ul><li>High <span class="qt0">fever</span></li><li>Headache</li><li>Neck stiffness</li><li>Numbness</li><li>Coma</li><li>Muscle weakness</li><li>Vision loss</li></ul>How is West Nile Virus diagnosed?<p>To find out if you have WNV, your health care provider:</p><ul><li>Will do a physical exam</li><li>Will take your medical history, including asking about your symptoms</li><li>May order blood tests</li></ul>What are the treatments for West Nile Virus?<p>There are no specific vaccines or treatments for human WNV disease. But rest, fluids, and over-the-counter pain medicines may relieve some symptoms. You may need to be hospitalized if you have serious symptoms.</p>Can West Nile Virus be prevented?<p>There are no vaccines or medicines to prevent WNV. The best way to avoid WNV is to help prevent mosquito bites:</p><ul><li>Wear insect repellent with DEET or another U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA)-registered insect repellent. Make sure to follow the instructions for using the repellent.</li><li>Wear long-sleeved shirts and pants if you're outside.</li><li>Get rid of mosquito breeding sites by emptying standing water from flowerpots, buckets, or barrels.</li><li>Stay indoors between dusk and dawn, when mosquitoes are most active.</li><li>Use screens on windows to keep mosquitoes out.</li></ul><p>Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC)</p></content>

    <document rank="18" url="https://medlineplus.gov/chikungunya.html">
      <content name="title">Chikungunya</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>Chikungunya is a virus that spread by the same kinds of mosquitoes that spread dengue and Zika virus. Rarely, the virus can spread from the pregnant  parent to their newborn around the time of birth. It may also spread through contact with infected blood. There have been outbreaks of chikungunya virus in Africa, Asia, Europe, the Indian and Pacific Oceans, the Caribbean, and Central and South America.</p><p>Most people who have chikungunya will have symptoms, which can be severe. They usually start 3-7 days after being bitten by an infected mosquito. The most common symptoms are <span class="qt0">fever</span> and joint pain. Other symptoms may include headache, muscle pain, joint swelling, and rash.</p><p>Most people feel better within a week. In some cases, however, the joint pain may last for months. People at risk for more severe disease include newborns, older adults, and people with diseases such as high blood pressure, diabetes, or heart disease.</p><p>A blood test can show whether you have chikungunya virus. There are no medicines to treat it. Drinking lots of fluids, resting, and taking non-aspirin pain relievers might help you feel better.</p><p>In the U.S., there is a vaccine for adults who are at a higher risk of getting chikungunya. You can also help prevent getting or spreading chikungunya by avoiding mosquito bites:</p><ul><li>Use a U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA)-registered insect repellent when you go outdoors. Make sure to follow the instructions for using the repellant.</li><li>Wear clothes that cover your arms, legs, and feet.</li><li>Stay in places that either have air conditioning (with the windows closed) or that use window and door screens.</li></ul><p>Centers for Disease Control and Prevention</p></content>

    <document rank="19" url="https://medlineplus.gov/childrenshealth.html">
      <content name="title">Children's Health</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Pediatrics</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>Your child's health includes physical, mental and social well-being. Most parents know the basics of keeping children healthy, like offering them healthy foods, making sure they get enough sleep and exercise and ensuring their safety. </p><p>It is also important for children to get regular checkups with their health care provider. These visits are a chance to check your child's development. They are also a good time to catch or prevent problems.</p><p>Other than checkups, school-age children should be seen for:</p><ul><li>Significant weight gain or loss</li><li>Sleep problems or change in behavior</li><li><span class="qt0">Fever</span> higher than 102</li><li>Rashes or skin infections</li><li>Frequent sore throats</li><li>Breathing problems</li></ul></content>

    <document rank="20" url="https://medlineplus.gov/fifthdisease.html">
      <content name="title">Fifth Disease</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Erythema Infectiosum</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Parvovirus B19</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Parvovirus B19 infection</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is fifth disease?<p>Fifth disease, also called erythema infectiosum, is a viral infection caused by parvovirus B19. This virus only infects people; it's not the same type of parvovirus that dogs and cats can get.</p><p>Fifth disease mostly affects children. It often causes a bright red rash on their cheeks (sometimes called a "slapped cheek" rash).</p><p>In the United States, parvovirus B19 infections are more common in late winter, spring, and early summer. There are usually minor outbreaks of fifth disease about every 3 to 4 years.</p>What causes fifth disease?<p>Parvovirus B19, which causes fifth disease, can spread from person to person:</p><ul><li>Through respiratory droplets, such as saliva (spit), sputum, or nasal mucus. The droplets can spread when:
	<ul><li>Someone who has the virus coughs and sneezes</li><li>You touch an item that has the virus on it</li></ul>
</li><li>Through blood or blood products</li><li>From a pregnant parent to the baby during pregnancy</li></ul><p>You are most contagious early in your infection, when you usually only have a <span class="qt0">fever</span> or cold-like symptoms. You are not likely to be contagious after you get later symptoms such as a rash and joint pain.</p>What are the symptoms of fifth disease?<p>About 2 out of 10 people who get a B19 parvovirus infection will have no symptoms.</p><p>But most people will get the symptoms of fifth disease, which are usually mild and can include:</p><ul><li><span class="qt0">Fever</span></li><li>Headache</li><li>Cough</li><li>Sore throat</li><li>Rashes</li><li>Joint pain</li></ul><p>The "slapped cheek" rash is more common in children. They usually get the rash a few days after they first have other symptoms. The rash can then spread to the arms, legs, chest, back, and buttocks.</p><p>Adults who get fifth disease might also have joint pain and swelling (called polyarthropathy syndrome).</p><p>Sometimes the infection can cause serious health complications affecting the nerves, joints, or blood system. This is more likely to happen if you are pregnant, have a blood disorder such as anemia, or a weakened immune system.</p><p>Because of these risks to you and some possible risks to your baby, contact your health care provider if you are pregnant and:</p><ul><li>You show signs of fifth disease (or a "slapped cheek" rash)</li><li>You may have been exposed to someone who has the infection</li></ul>How is fifth disease diagnosed?<p>There is no routine lab test to diagnose fifth disease. Providers can often diagnose fifth disease just by seeing the "slapped cheek" rash and rashes on the body.</p><p>There is testing to show whether or not you have protection (immunity) against the B19 parvovirus or if you have had a recent infection. This test is usually not needed, but your provider may order the test if you are pregnant or at higher risk for complications of the disease.</p>What are the treatments for fifth disease?<p>There is no specific treatment for fifth disease. It is usually mild and goes away on its own.</p><p>To feel better, get plenty of rest. Taking acetaminophen can help with the <span class="qt0">fever</span> and pain. Do not give aspirin to children, because it may cause Reye syndrome. This is a rare, serious illness that can affect the brain and liver.</p>Can fifth disease be prevented?<p>There is no vaccine to prevent fifth disease. But you can help protect yourself and others by:</p><ul><li>Washing your hands often with soap and water</li><li>Covering your mouth and nose when you cough or sneeze</li><li>Avoiding touching your eyes, nose, and mouth with unwashed hands</li><li>Avoiding close contact with people who are sick</li><li>Staying home when you are sick</li></ul><p>Most people become immune to the virus after having it once.</p><p>Centers for Disease Control and Prevention</p></content>

    <document rank="21" url="https://medlineplus.gov/hantavirusinfections.html">
      <content name="title">Hantavirus Infections</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">HPS</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Hanta virus</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>Hantavirus pulmonary syndrome (HPS) is a rare but deadly viral infection. It is spread by mice and rats. They shed the virus in their urine, droppings, and saliva. Tiny droplets with the virus can enter the air. People can get the disease if they breathe infected air or come into contact with rodents or their urine or droppings. You cannot catch it from people.</p><p>Early symptoms of HPS include:</p><ul><li>	Fatigue</li><li>	<span class="qt0">Fever</span> </li><li>	Muscle aches, especially in the thighs, hips and back</li><li>	Headaches</li><li>	Chills</li><li>	Dizziness</li><li>	Nausea, vomiting, diarrhea or abdominal pain</li></ul><p>Later symptoms include coughing and shortness of breath. </p><p>Controlling rodents in and around your house is the best way to prevent infection. If you have been around rodents and have symptoms of <span class="qt0">fever</span>, deep muscle aches, and severe shortness of breath, see your doctor immediately.</p><p>There is no specific treatment, cure, or vaccine for HPS. Patients may do better if it is recognized early and they get medical care in an intensive care unit. They often need to use a breathing machine and have oxygen therapy.</p><p>Centers for Disease Control and Prevention</p></content>

    <document rank="22" url="https://medlineplus.gov/heartvalvediseases.html">
      <content name="title">Heart Valve Diseases</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Aortic Stenosis</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Heart Murmur</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Valvular Heart Diseases</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Valvular heart disease</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What are heart valve diseases?<p>Heart valve disease happens when one or more of your heart valves don't work well.</p><p>Your heart has four valves: the tricuspid, pulmonary, mitral, and aortic valves. The valves have flaps that open and close. The flaps make sure that blood flows in the right direction through your heart and to the rest of your body. When your heart beats, the flaps open to let blood through. Between heartbeats they close to stop the blood from flowing backwards.</p><p>If one or more of your heart valves doesn't open or close correctly, it can affect your blood flow and strain your heart. Fortunately, treatment helps most valve diseases.</p>What are the types of heart valve diseases?<p>Heart valves can have three basic kinds of problems:</p><ul><li>Regurgitation, or backflow, happens when the flaps of a valve don't close tightly. This allows the blood to leak backwards. A common cause of regurgitation is prolapse, where the flaps of the valve flop or bulge back. Prolapse most often affects the mitral valve.</li><li>Stenosis happens when the flaps of a valve become thick, stiff, or stuck together. This prevents the heart valve from opening all the way. Not enough blood can pass through the valve. Aortic valve stenosis is a common type of stenosis. It affects the valve that controls blood flow into the large artery that carries blood out of the heart to the body.</li><li>Atresia happens when a heart valve did not form properly and does not have an opening for blood to pass through.</li></ul><p>Sometimes a valve can have both regurgitation and stenosis.</p>What causes heart valve diseases?<p>Some people are born with heart valve disease. This is called congenital heart valve disease. It can happen alone or along with other congenital heart defects. Heart valve disease can also develop over time as you get older or have certain conditions that affect the heart.</p>Who is more likely to develop heart valve diseases?<p>Your chance of having heart valve disease is higher if:</p><ul><li>You are older. With age, the heart valves can become thick and stiff.</li><li>You have or have had other conditions that affect your heart and blood vessels. These include:
	<ul><li>Rheumatic <span class="qt0">fever</span>. An untreated strep throat can become rheumatic <span class="qt0">fever</span>, which can harm the heart valves. The damage may not show up for years. Today, most people take antibiotics to cure strep throat before it can cause heart valve damage.</li><li>Endocarditis. This is a rare infection in the lining of the heart and heart valves. It is usually caused by bacteria in the bloodstream.</li><li>A heart attack.</li><li>Heart failure.</li><li>Coronary artery disease, especially when it affects the aorta (the large artery that carries blood from the heart to the body).</li><li>High blood pressure.</li><li>High blood cholesterol.</li><li>Diabetes.</li><li>Obesity and overweight.</li><li>Lack of physical activity.</li></ul>
</li><li>A family history of early heart disease:<ul><li>A father or brother who had heart disease younger than 55.</li><li>A mother or sister who had heart disease younger than 65.</li></ul></li><li>You were born with an aortic valve that wasn't formed right. Sometimes this will cause problems right away. Other times, the valve may work well enough for years before causing problems.</li></ul>What are the symptoms of heart valve diseases?<p>Many people live their whole lives with a heart valve that doesn't work perfectly and never have any problems. But heart valve disease may get worse slowly over time. You may develop signs and symptoms, such as:</p><ul><li>Shortness of breath (feeling like you can't get enough air)</li><li>Fatigue</li><li>Swelling in your feet, ankles, abdomen (belly), or the veins in your neck</li><li>Chest pain when you're physically active</li><li>Arrhythmia, a problem with the rate or rhythm of your heartbeat</li><li>Dizziness or fainting</li></ul><p>If you don't get treatment for heart valve disease, the symptoms and strain on your heart may keep getting worse.</p>What other problems can heart valve diseases cause?<p>When the valves don't work well, your heart has to pump harder to get enough blood out to the body.  Without treatment, this extra workload on your heart can lead to:</p><ul><li>Heart failure</li><li>Stroke</li><li>Blood clots</li><li>Sudden cardiac arrest or death</li></ul>How is heart valve disease diagnosed?<p>Your health care provider may listen to your heart with a stethoscope and hear that your heart makes abnormal sounds, such as a click or a heart murmur. These sounds may mean a valve isn't working normally. The provider will usually refer you to a cardiologist, a doctor who specializes in heart diseases.</p><p>The doctor will also listen to your heart and will do a physical exam. You will also likely need to have one or more heart tests.</p>What are the treatments for heart valve diseases?<p>Most heart valve problems can be treated successfully. Treatment may include:</p><ul><li>Medicines to control your symptoms and keep your heart pumping well</li><li>Heart-healthy lifestyle changes to treat other related heart conditions</li><li>Surgery to repair or replace a valve</li></ul><p>It's possible that you may need surgery, even if you don't have symptoms. Fixing the valve can help prevent future heart problems.</p><p>There are many ways to do heart valve surgery. You and your doctor can decide what's best for you, based on your valve problem and general health. Heart valve repair surgery has fewer risks than heart valve replacement. So, when repair is possible, it's preferred over valve replacement.</p><p>In some cases, valve replacement is necessary. There are 2 types of replacement valves:</p><ul><li>Biologic valves made from pig, cow, or human tissue. These valves tend to wear out after 10 to 15 years, but some may last longer.</li><li>Mechanical (human-made) valves usually don't wear out. But with a mechanical valve, you usually have to take blood thinners for the rest of your life to prevent blood clots. And your risk of endocarditis (a heart infection) is higher than with a biologic valve.</li></ul><p>NIH: National Heart, Lung, and Blood Institute</p></content>
      <content name="mesh">Heart Valve Diseases</content>
      <content name="groupName">Blood, Heart and Circulation</content>

    <document rank="23" url="https://medlineplus.gov/legionnairesdisease.html">
      <content name="title">Legionnaires' Disease</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Legionellosis</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is Legionnaires' Disease? <p>Legionnaires' disease is a serious type of pneumonia caused by Legionella bacteria. The disease got its name when a group of people at an American Legion convention became ill with this type of pneumonia.</p><p>Legionella is found naturally in freshwater, such as lakes and rivers. It can also be found in soil. But people usually only get sick from it when if it grows and spreads in man-made water systems. These systems can include hot tubs, fountains, and the plumbing systems of large buildings, such as hotels or nursing homes.</p><p>Legionella can also cause Pontiac <span class="qt0">fever</span>. This is a milder, flu-like illness that doesn't infect your lungs.</p><p></p>What causes Legionnaires' disease?<p></p><p>You usually can't get infected by drinking water that contains the bacteria unless you aspirate it (accidentally breathe the water into your lungs). The bacteria don't spread from person to person. Disease outbreaks happen when many people are exposed to the same water source that contains the bacteria.</p>Who is more likely to develop Legionnaires' disease?<p>Most healthy people who come in contact with the bacteria do not become sick. You are more likely to develop an infection if you:</p><ul><li>Are over the age of 50</li><li>Are a current or former smoker</li><li>Have a chronic (long-lasting) disease such as diabetes or kidney failure</li><li>Have a chronic lung disease such as COPD or emphysema</li><li>Have a weakened immune system due to a disease such as HIV or cancer or are taking medicines that suppress your immune system</li><li>Live in a long-term care facility</li><li>Have recently stayed in a hospital or had surgery requiring anesthesia</li></ul>What are the symptoms of Legionnaires' disease? <p>The symptoms usually show up 2 to 14 days after you were exposed to Legionella bacteria. Most of the symptoms are similar to those of other types of pneumonia. But Legionnaires' disease may also cause gastrointestinal symptoms and mental changes.</p><p>The symptoms of Legionnaires' disease may include:</p><ul><li>Cough</li><li>High <span class="qt0">fever</span></li><li>Chills</li><li>Headache</li><li>Chest pain</li><li>Shortness of breath</li><li>Fatigue</li><li>Nausea and vomiting</li><li>Diarrhea</li><li>Confusion</li></ul>What other problems can Legionnaires' disease cause?<p>Legionnaires' disease can cause life-threatening complications such as respiratory failure, kidney failure, and sepsis.</p><p>About 1 out of every 10 people who get sick with Legionnaires' disease will die from complications of the illness.</p>How is Legionnaires' disease diagnosed?<p>To find out if you have Legionnaires' disease, your health care provider will probably order a chest x-ray to see if you have pneumonia. If you do have it, lab tests such as a urine or sputum test can check for the Legionella bacteria. Your provider may also order a blood test to see if you have been exposed to the bacteria.</p>What are the treatments for Legionnaires' disease?<p>If you have Legionnaires' disease, you will likely need care in a hospital. The treatment is with antibiotics. Most people will recover, especially if they were healthy when they got the disease. Getting treatment early can increase the chances of getting better.</p>Can Legionnaires' disease be prevented?<p>You can lower your risk of getting Legionnaires' disease if you:</p><ul><li>Clean all your water-related devices regularly. This could include portable humidifiers, water heaters, air conditioners, and nasal rinses.</li><li>If you haven't used your faucets and showerheads for a week or more, flush them by letting the hot and cold faucets run for at least two minutes before use.</li><li>Avoid exposure to water sources such as decorative foundations or hot tubs if you don't know how well they are maintained.</li><li>Use windshield cleaner in your wiper fluid tank in your car instead of water.</li><li>Ask about how well your buildings' water systems are maintained.</li></ul><p>Centers for Disease Control and Prevention</p></content>
      <content name="mesh">Legionnaires' Disease</content>

This is a milder, flu-like illness that  ... symptoms of Legionnaires' disease may include: Cough  High <span class="qt0">fever</span>  Chills  Headache  Chest pain  Shortness of breath  Fatigue   ... </content>
