    <document rank="24" url="https://medlineplus.gov/pneumonia.html">
      <content name="title">Pneumonia</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Bronchopneumonia</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Walking Pneumonia</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is pneumonia?<p>Pneumonia is an infection in one or both of the lungs. It causes the air sacs of the lungs to fill up with fluid or pus. It can range from mild to severe, depending on the type of germ causing the infection, your age, and your overall health.</p>What causes pneumonia?<p>Bacterial, viral, and fungal infections can cause pneumonia.</p><p>Bacteria are the most common cause. Bacterial pneumonia can occur on its own. It can also develop after you've had certain viral infections such as a cold or the flu. Several different types of bacteria can cause pneumonia, including:</p><ul><li>Streptococcus pneumoniae</li><li>Legionella pneumophila; this pneumonia is often called Legionnaires' disease</li><li>Mycoplasma pneumoniae</li><li>Chlamydia pneumoniae</li><li>Haemophilus influenzae</li></ul><p>Viruses that infect the respiratory tract may cause pneumonia. Viral pneumonia is often mild and goes away on its own within a few weeks. But sometimes it is serious enough that you need to get treatment in a hospital. If you have viral pneumonia, you are at risk of also getting bacterial pneumonia. The different viruses that can cause pneumonia include:</p><ul><li>Respiratory syncytial virus (RSV)</li><li>Some common cold and flu viruses</li><li>SARS-CoV-2, the virus that causes COVID-19</li></ul><p>Fungal pneumonia is more common in people who have chronic health problems or weakened immune systems. Some of the types include:</p><ul><li>Pneumocystis pneumonia (PCP)</li><li>Coccidioidomycosis, which causes valley <span class="qt0">fever</span></li><li>Histoplasmosis</li><li>Cryptococcus</li></ul>Who is at risk for pneumonia?<p>Anyone can get pneumonia, but certain factors can increase your risk:</p><ul><li>Age; the risk is higher for children who are age 2 and under and adults age 65 and older</li><li>Exposure to certain chemicals, pollutants, or toxic fumes</li><li>Lifestyle habits, such as smoking, heavy alcohol use, and malnourishment</li><li>Being in a hospital, especially if you are in the ICU. Being sedated and/or on a ventilator raises the risk even more.</li><li>Having a lung disease</li><li>Having a weakened immune system</li><li>Have trouble coughing or swallowing, from a stroke or other condition</li><li>Recently being sick with a cold or the flu</li></ul>What are the symptoms of pneumonia?<p>The symptoms of pneumonia can range from mild to severe and include:</p><ul><li><span class="qt0">Fever</span></li><li>Chills</li><li>Cough, usually with phlegm (a slimy substance from deep in your lungs)</li><li>Shortness of breath</li><li>Chest pain when you breathe or cough</li><li>Nausea and/or vomiting</li><li>Diarrhea</li></ul><p>The symptoms can vary for different groups. Newborns and infants may not show any signs of the infection. Others may vomit and have a <span class="qt0">fever</span> and cough. They might seem sick, with no energy, or be restless.</p><p>Older adults and people who have serious illnesses or weak immune systems may have fewer and milder symptoms. They may even have a lower than normal temperature. Older adults who have pneumonia sometimes have sudden changes in mental awareness.</p>What other problems can pneumonia cause?<p>Sometimes pneumonia can cause serious complications such as:</p><ul><li>Bacteremia, which happens when the bacteria move into the bloodstream. It is serious and can lead to septic shock.</li><li>Lung abscesses, which are collections of pus in cavities of the lungs</li><li>Pleural disorders, which are conditions that affect the pleura. The pleura is the tissue that covers the outside of the lungs and lines the inside of your chest cavity.</li><li>Kidney failure</li><li>Respiratory failure</li></ul>How is pneumonia diagnosed?<p>Sometimes pneumonia can be hard to diagnose. This is because it can cause some of the same symptoms as a cold or the flu. It may take time for you to realize that you have a more serious condition.</p><p>Your health care provider may use many tools to make a diagnosis:</p><ul><li>A medical history, which includes asking about your symptoms</li><li>A physical exam, including listening to your lungs with a stethoscope</li><li>Various tests, such as
		<ul><li>A chest x-ray</li><li>Blood tests such as a complete blood count (CBC) to see if your immune system is actively fighting an infection</li><li>A Blood culture to find out whether you have a bacterial infection that has spread to your bloodstream</li></ul>
</li></ul><p>If you are in the hospital, have serious symptoms, are older, or have other health problems, you may also have more tests, such as:</p><ul><li>Sputum test, which checks for bacteria in a sample of your sputum (spit) or phlegm (slimy substance from deep in your lungs).</li><li>Chest CT scan to see how much of your lungs is affected. It may also show if you have complications such as lung abscesses or pleural effusions.</li><li>Pleural fluid culture, which checks for bacteria in a fluid sample that was taken from the pleural space</li><li>Pulse oximetry or blood oxygen level test, to check how much oxygen is in your blood</li><li>Bronchoscopy, a procedure used to look inside your lungs' airways</li></ul>What are the treatments for pneumonia?<p>Treatment for pneumonia depends on the type of pneumonia, which germ is causing it, and how severe it is:</p><ul><li>Antibiotics treat bacterial pneumonia and some types of fungal pneumonia. They do not work for viral pneumonia.</li><li>In some cases, your provider may prescribe antiviral medicines for viral pneumonia</li><li>Antifungal medicines treat other types of fungal pneumonia</li></ul><p>You may need to be treated in a hospital if your symptoms are severe or if you are at risk for complications. While there, you may get additional treatments. For example, if your blood oxygen level is low, you may receive oxygen therapy.</p><p>It may take time to recover from pneumonia. Some people feel better within a week. For other people, it can take a month or more.</p>Can pneumonia be prevented?<p>Vaccines can help prevent pneumonia caused by pneumococcal bacteria or the flu virus. Having good hygiene, not smoking, and having a healthy lifestyle may also help prevent pneumonia.</p><p>NIH: National Heart, Lung, and Blood Institute</p></content>

    <document rank="0" url="https://medlineplus.gov/chestpain.html">
      <content name="title"><span class="qt0">Chest</span> <span class="qt2">Pain</span></content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle"><span class="qt2">Pain</span>, <span class="qt0">Chest</span></content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>Having a <span class="qt2">pain</span> in your <span class="qt0">chest</span> can be scary.  It does not always mean that you are having a heart attack.  There can be many other causes, including:</p><ul><li>Other heart problems, such as angina</li><li>Panic attacks</li><li>Digestive problems, such as heartburn or esophagus disorders</li><li>Sore muscles</li><li>Lung diseases, such as pneumonia, pleurisy, or pulmonary embolism</li><li>Costochondritis - an inflammation of joints in your <span class="qt0">chest</span></li></ul><p>Some of these problems can be serious.  Get immediate medical care if you have <span class="qt0">chest</span> <span class="qt2">pain</span> that does not go away, crushing <span class="qt2">pain</span> or pressure in the <span class="qt0">chest, or chest</span> <span class="qt2">pain</span> along with nausea, sweating, dizziness or shortness of breath. Treatment depends on the cause of the <span class="qt2">pain</span>.</p></content>
      <content name="mesh"><span class="qt0">Chest</span> <span class="qt2">Pain</span></content>
      <content name="groupName">Blood, Heart and Circulation</content>
      <content name="groupName">Symptoms</content>
      <content name="snippet"> Having a <span class="qt2">pain</span> in your <span class="qt0">chest</span> can be scary.  It does not always mean that you are having a heart attack.  There can be many other causes, including: Other heart  ... </content>

    <document rank="1" url="https://medlineplus.gov/heartdiseaseinwomen.html">
      <content name="title">Heart Disease in Women</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is heart disease?<p>Heart disease is a general term that includes many types of heart problems. It's also called cardiovascular disease, which means heart and blood vessel disease.</p><p>Coronary artery disease (also called coronary heart disease) is the most common type of heart disease in both men and women. It happens slowly over time when a sticky substance called plaque builds up in the arteries that supply your heart muscle with blood. The plaque narrows or blocks blood flow to the heart muscle and can lead to:</p><ul><li>Angina - <span class="qt0">chest</span> <span class="qt2">pain</span> from lack of blood flow</li><li>Heart attacks - when part of the heart muscle dies from loss of blood flow</li><li>Heart failure - when your heart can't pump enough blood to meet your body's needs</li><li>Arrhythmia - a problem with the rate or rhythm of your heartbeat</li></ul>How does heart disease affect women?<p>In the United States, heart disease is the number one cause of death in women. But women are often not diagnosed with heart disease as quickly as men are. That's because:</p><ul><li>Women are more likely than men to have "silent" heart disease, meaning that they don't have symptoms.</li><li>Health care providers may not recognize heart disease in women because women's symptoms may be different from men's symptoms.</li><li>Women are more likely than men to have certain types of heart disease that can be harder to diagnose.</li></ul><p>A delay in diagnosis may mean a delay in medical care that could help prevent serious problems, such as a heart attack. That's why it's important to learn about your risk for heart disease, the symptoms in women, and how to keep your heart healthy.</p>What types of heart disease do women get?<p>Women can get any type of heart disease. Like men, the most common type of heart disease among women is coronary artery disease. But there are certain types of heart disease which are less common, but affect women more often than men:</p><ul><li>Coronary microvascular disease - <span class="qt0">Chest</span> <span class="qt2">pain</span> from spasms (sudden tightening) in the smallest arteries of the heart that pinch off blood flow. It typically happens during rest or routine activities. This serious condition increases your risk of having a heart attack or other heart diseases.  This type may be harder to diagnose since blockages in smaller arteries can be harder to see on imaging tests.</li><li>Broken heart syndrome - Strong <span class="qt0">chest</span> <span class="qt2">pain</span> or other signs that look like a heart attack. It's caused by the stress of powerful emotions, such as deep grief, anger, or surprise. It can happen even if you're healthy. It mostly affects women after menopause and usually doesn't cause any lasting damage.</li><li>Variant angina - A rare type of strong <span class="qt0">chest</span> <span class="qt2">pain</span> from spasms in the heart arteries. The <span class="qt2">pain</span> usually happens in a pattern during sleep. Variant angina rarely causes a heart attack.</li></ul>Which women are more likely to develop heart disease?<p>Your risk for developing heart disease increases with:</p><ul><li>Menopause. Women can develop heart disease at any age, but your risk increases after your periods stop, usually by age 55. Before menopause, your body makes more estrogen (a female hormone) which helps protect against heart disease. That's why women generally develop coronary artery disease 10 years later than men. During and after menopause, estrogen levels drop and your risk for heart diseases goes up. If your periods stop before age 40, your risk will be higher than other women your age.</li><li>Family history of heart disease. Your risk for heart disease may be higher if your:
	<ul><li>Mother or sister had heart disease before age 65.</li><li>Father or brother had heart disease before age 55.</li></ul>
</li><li>Problems during pregnancy, such as  high blood pressure, gestational diabetes, anemia, and high blood pressure.</li><li>Use of hormonal birth control (pills, patches, or vaginal rings with estrogen and progesterone).</li><li>Endometriosis.</li><li>Polycystic ovary syndrome.</li><li>Inflammatory and autoimmune diseases.</li><li>Metabolic syndrome.</li><li>Mental health issues, such as stress, anxiety, or depression.</li><li>Health risks from a lack physical activity.</li><li>Obesity or being overweight.</li><li>Diabetes.</li><li>Low HDL cholesterol levels.</li><li>Mild to moderate high blood pressure.</li><li>Smoking.</li></ul><p>If you have one or more risks for heart disease, ask your health care provider for help understanding your risk level. Ask if you need any heart tests to help catch heart disease early.</p>What are the symptoms of heart disease and heart attack in women?<p>When women have symptoms of heart disease, they may include:</p><ul><li><span class="qt2">Pain</span> or discomfort in the <span class="qt0">chest</span> that may be dull and heavy or sharp</li><li><span class="qt2">Pain</span> in the neck, jaw, throat, upper belly, or back</li><li>Nausea or vomiting</li><li>Unusual fatigue</li><li>Shortness of breath (feeling like you can't get enough air) during physical activity</li></ul><p>Women who have coronary artery disease are more likely than men to have <span class="qt0">chest</span> <span class="qt2">pain</span> when resting or doing daily activities, rather than during exercise. They're also more likely than men to feel <span class="qt0">chest</span> <span class="qt2">pain</span> from mental stress.</p><p>Symptoms of a heart attack in women may also be different than in men. <span class="qt0">Chest</span> <span class="qt2">pain</span> is the most common symptom for both sexes. It may feel like crushing or squeezing. But women are somewhat less likely than men to have <span class="qt0">chest</span> <span class="qt2">pain</span>.</p><p>During a heart attack, women may feel:</p><ul><li>Pressure or tightness in the <span class="qt0">chest</span></li><li><span class="qt2">Pain</span> in the upper back, arms, neck, jaw or throat</li><li>Dizziness</li><li>Unusual fatigue</li><li>Indigestion, heartburn, nausea, or vomiting </li><li>Stomach <span class="qt2">pain</span></li><li>Shortness of breath</li></ul><p>Heart attacks usually don't look like the sudden, dramatic events we see in the movies. The symptoms may be mild or strong. They may start slowly. They can stop and then come back.</p>Can heart disease in women be prevented?<p>You can help lower your risk by:</p><ul><li>Learning how to prevent heart disease and making heart-healthy habits part of your daily life.</li><li>Asking your provider about your personal risk for heart disease and the best way to manage your heart health.</li></ul><p>Remember, women can have heart disease without symptoms. But if you pay attention to your risk for heart disease, you can take action to prevent problems or keep them from getting worse.</p><p>NIH: National Heart, Lung, and Blood Institute</p></content>
      <content name="mesh">Heart Diseases</content>
      <content name="groupName">Women</content>
      <content name="groupName">Blood, Heart and Circulation</content>

    <document rank="2" url="https://medlineplus.gov/angina.html">
      <content name="title">Angina</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Syndrome X (Cardiac)</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>Angina is <span class="qt0">chest</span> <span class="qt2">pain</span> or discomfort you feel when there is not enough blood flow to your heart muscle. Your heart muscle needs the oxygen that the blood carries. Angina may feel like pressure or a squeezing <span class="qt2">pain</span> in your <span class="qt0">chest</span>. It may feel like indigestion. You may also feel <span class="qt2">pain</span> in your shoulders, arms, neck, jaw, or back. </p><p>Angina is a symptom of coronary artery disease (CAD), the most common heart disease. CAD happens when a sticky substance called plaque builds up in the arteries that supply blood to the heart, reducing blood flow.</p><p>There are three types of angina:</p><ul><li>Stable angina is the most common type. It happens when the heart is working harder than usual. Stable angina has a regular pattern.  Rest and medicines usually help.</li><li>Unstable angina is the most dangerous. It does not follow a pattern and can happen without physical exertion. It does not go away with rest or medicine. It is a sign that you could have a heart attack soon.</li><li>Variant angina is rare. It happens when you are resting. Medicines can help.</li></ul><p>Not all <span class="qt0">chest</span> <span class="qt2">pain</span> or discomfort is angina.  If you have <span class="qt0">chest</span> <span class="qt2">pain</span>, you should see your health care provider.</p><p>NIH: National Heart, Lung, and Blood Institute</p></content>
      <content name="mesh">Angina Pectoris</content>
      <content name="groupName">Older Adults</content>
      <content name="groupName">Blood, Heart and Circulation</content>
      <content name="snippet"> Angina is <span class="qt0">chest</span> <span class="qt2">pain</span> or discomfort you feel when there is not enough blood flow to your heart muscle. Your heart muscle needs the oxygen that the blood carries.  ... </content>

    <document rank="3" url="https://medlineplus.gov/esophagusdisorders.html">
      <content name="title">Esophagus Disorders</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Achalasia</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Barrett's Esophagus</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Cardiospasm</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What are esophagus disorders?<p>Esophagus disorders are problems with how your esophagus works. The esophagus is the tube that carries food from your mouth to your stomach. You usually don't notice it unless you swallow something too big, hot, or cold. You might also notice it if something is wrong. When that happens, you might feel <span class="qt2">pain</span> or have trouble swallowing (dysphagia).</p>What causes esophagus disorders?<p>The cause depends on the type of disorder. Some run in families. Others are linked to allergic reactions to food or substances in your environment. Sometimes, the cause is unknown.</p><p>You may be more likely to develop an esophagus disorder if you:</p><ul><li>Are overweight or have obesity</li><li>Are pregnant</li><li>Take certain medicines</li><li>Have had radiation treatmentt to your neck or <span class="qt0">chest</span></li><li>Smoke or are around secondhand smoke</li></ul>What are common esophagus disorders?<p>The most common esophagus problem is gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD). This happens when the muscle at the bottom of your esophagus doesn't close properly. Stomach acid can move back up and irritate the esophagus.</p><p>Other esophagus disorders include:</p><ul><li>Heartburn is a burning feeling in your <span class="qt0">chest</span> or throat. It is often caused by acid reflux.</li><li>Barrett's Esophagus happens when the lining of your esophagus changes. It is often caused by chronic (long-term) GERD.</li><li>Achalasia is a problem where the lower muscle in your esophagus doesn't relax like it should.</li><li>Esophageal Spasms are muscle contractions that cause <span class="qt0">chest</span> <span class="qt2">pain</span> or swallowing problems.</li><li>Eosinophilic Esophagitis (EoE) is a rare condition where certain white blood cells (eosinophils) build up in the esophagus, causing swelling, <span class="qt2">pain</span>, and trouble swallowing.</li><li>Esophageal Cancer is a type of cancer that starts in the tissues of the esophagus.</li></ul>What are symptoms of esophagus disorders?<p>Symptoms can be different for each condition but may include:</p><ul><li><span class="qt2">Pain</span> in your <span class="qt0">chest</span>, abdomen (belly), or back</li><li>A cough or sore throat that doesn't go away</li><li>Trouble swallowing or feeling like food is stuck</li><li>A hoarse voice or wheezing</li><li>Heartburn or a burning feeling in your stomach</li><li>Tasting acid or food in the back of your mouth</li><li>Food or liquid coming back up into your mouth (regurgitation)</li><li>Losing weight without trying</li></ul><p>Get medical help right away if you have <span class="qt0">chest</span> <span class="qt2">pain</span> with shortness of breath, or <span class="qt2">pain</span> in your jaw or arm. These could be signs of a heart problem.</p>How are esophagus disorders diagnosed?<p>Your health care provider will ask about your symptoms and medical history. They may order one or more tests, such as:</p><ul><li>Upper gastrointestinal (GI) endoscopy. This test uses a tiny camera to look inside your esophagus and stomach.</li><li>Biopsy. This test takes a small tissue sample to check.</li><li>Barium swallow. This test uses imaging to see how you swallow.</li><li>Esophageal manometry. This test checks how well your esophagus moves food to your stomach.</li><li>Esophageal pH test. This test measures how often acid backs up into your esophagus and how long the acid stays there.</li></ul>How are esophagus disorders treated?<p>Treatment depends on what's causing the problem and your overall health. Some esophagus disorders get better with over-the-counter medicines, diet changes, or lifestyle changes. Others may need prescription medicine or surgery.</p><p>Without treatment, some disorders may lead to other problems, such as pneumonia, from food entering the windpipe. GERD, Barrett's esophagus, and achalasia can also raise your risk for esophageal cancer.</p>Can esophagus disorders be prevented?<p>You can help prevent or reduce symptoms by:</p><ul><li>Not smoking and limiting alcohol</li><li>Eating smaller meals</li><li>Avoiding eating close to bedtime</li><li>Wearing loose-fitting clothes</li><li>Sleeping on your left side or raising the head of your bed</li><li>Maintaining a healthy weight</li></ul><p>NIH: National Institute of Diabetes and Digestive and Kidney Diseases</p></content>

      <content name="groupName">Digestive System</content>

    <document rank="4" url="https://medlineplus.gov/diabeticheartdisease.html">
      <content name="title">Diabetic Heart Disease</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is diabetes?<p>Diabetes is a disease in which your blood glucose, or blood sugar, levels are too high. Glucose comes from foods you eat. The cells of your body need glucose for energy. A hormone called insulin helps the glucose get into your cells.</p><p>With type 1 diabetes, your body doesn't make insulin. With type 2 diabetes, your body doesn't make or use insulin well. Without enough insulin, glucose builds up in your blood and causes high blood sugar levels.</p>What is diabetic heart disease?<p>Diabetic heart disease is a term for heart disease in people who have diabetes. If you have diabetes, you are much more likely to develop heart disease or have a stroke than people who don't have diabetes. And you may start having these problems at a younger age.</p><p>Over time, the effects of high blood sugar from diabetes can include damage to the blood vessels and nerves in your heart. This damage increases your chance of developing heart diseases, including:</p><ul><li>Coronary artery disease (CAD). It happens slowly as a sticky material called plaque builds up in the arteries that supply your heart muscle with blood.</li><li>Heart failure. With this condition, your heart can't pump enough oxygen-rich blood to meet the needs of your body.</li><li>Cardiomyopathy. This is a group of diseases in which the heart muscle may become thick or stiff.</li></ul>Who is more likely to develop diabetic heart disease?<p>Diabetes puts you at serious risk for heart disease. It also makes you more likely to develop other conditions that raise your risk even more, including:</p><ul><li>High blood pressure</li><li>High blood levels of LDL "bad" cholesterol</li><li>High triglycerides (a type of fat in your blood) with low blood levels of HDL "good" cholesterol</li></ul><p>Your risk for heart disease is also higher if you</p><ul><li>Are male</li><li>Smoke</li><li>Have obesity</li><li>Have too much belly fat around your waist, even though you're at a healthy weight:
	<ul><li>For men, that's a waist more than 40 inches</li><li>For women, that's a waist more than 35 inches</li></ul>
</li><li>Have a family history of heart disease</li><li>Have chronic kidney disease</li></ul>What are the symptoms of diabetic heart disease?<p>In the early stages, heart disease usually doesn't have any symptoms. But if your heart disease worsens, you can have symptoms. Your symptoms will depend on the type of heart disease you have. They might include:</p><ul><li>Shortness of breath</li><li>Fatigue</li><li>Dizziness or fainting</li><li>Arrhythmia (problem with the rate or rhythm of your heartbeat)</li><li>Swollen feet and ankles</li><li><span class="qt0">Chest</span> <span class="qt2">pain</span></li></ul><p>It's important to know that people with diabetes may not feel <span class="qt0">chest</span> <span class="qt2">pain</span>. That's because diabetes can damage the nerves in your heart. If you have any symptoms that could be heart disease, talk with your health care provider.</p>How is diabetic heart disease diagnosed?<p>To find out if you have diabetic heart disease, your provider will:</p><ul><li>Ask about your medical history, including your symptoms and other health conditions you may have</li><li>Ask about your family history, to find out if you have relatives who have or had heart disease</li><li>Do a physical exam</li><li>Likely run some tests to help understand your personal risk for heart disease, including:
	<ul><li>Blood tests to check your cholesterol, triglycerides, and blood sugar levels</li><li>A blood-pressure check</li><li>Heart tests, if needed</li></ul>
</li></ul><p>Depending on your risk level, your provider may send you to a cardiologist (a doctor who specializes in heart diseases) for care. If you do have heart disease, treatment will depend on the type of heart disease you have.</p>How can I prevent diabetic heart disease?<p>You may be able to prevent heart disease or keep it from getting worse by working with your provider to:</p><ul><li>Control your blood sugar levels</li><li>Manage other conditions you may have that can raise your risk for heart disease </li><li>Take any medicines your provider prescribed</li><li>Make heart-healthy habits part of your daily life</li><li>Follow a healthy eating plan for diabetes</li></ul><p>NIH: National Institute of Diabetes and Digestive and Kidney Diseases</p></content>

      <content name="mesh">Heart Diseases</content>
      <content name="mesh">Diabetic Cardiomyopathies</content>

That's because diabetes can damage the nerves  ... </content>

    <document rank="5" url="https://medlineplus.gov/arrhythmia.html">
      <content name="title">Arrhythmia</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Bradycardia</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Irregular Heartbeat</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Multifocal Atrial Tachycardia</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Tachycardia</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is an arrhythmia?<p>An arrhythmia is a problem with the rate or rhythm of your heartbeat. Your heart beats too quickly, too slowly, or with an irregular pattern. Changes in the electrical signals that control your heartbeat cause arrhythmias.</p><ul><li>When your heart beats faster than normal, it's called tachycardia.</li><li>When your heart beats too slowly, it's called bradycardia.</li><li>When the signal to beat comes too early, it's called a premature or extra heartbeat. It may feel like your heart skipped a beat.</li></ul><p>There are many types of arrhythmias. Arrhythmias may affect the upper or lower chamber of your heart. The most common type of arrhythmia is atrial fibrillation, which causes an irregular and fast heartbeat.</p><p>Some arrhythmias are harmless, such as when your heart rate speeds up during exercise and slows down when you sleep. But if you have a frequent irregular rhythm, it may mean your heart isn't pumping enough blood into your body. Getting treatment and following a heart-healthy lifestyle can help control arrhythmias. It may also help prevent heart damage that can trigger some heart arrhythmias.</p>What causes an arrhythmia?<p>Many factors can affect your heart's rhythm, such as having had a heart attack, smoking, congenital heart defects, and stress. Other factors that could increase your risk for some types of arrhythmias could include if you:</p><ul><li>Have a family history of arrhythmias.</li><li>Have certain health conditions, which can include heart and blood vessel diseases, lung diseases, kidney diseases, obesity, and sleep apnea.</li><li>Are an older adult.</li><li>Have had recent surgery for your heart, lungs, or throat.</li><li>Take certain medicines for other health conditions.</li><li>Use illegal drugs.</li></ul><p>If you have risk factors, some situations that make your heart work harder, raise your blood pressure, or cause strong emotional stress may trigger an arrhythmia.</p>What are the symptoms of an arrhythmia?<p>You may not have any noticeable symptoms of an arrhythmia. Your provider may find an arrhythmia during your routine checkup. Symptoms of an arrhythmia can include:</p><ul><li>Fast or slow heartbeat</li><li>Skipping, fluttering, or pounding heartbeats</li><li><span class="qt0">Chest</span> <span class="qt2">pain</span> or discomfort</li><li>Dizziness or fainting</li><li>Shortness of breath</li><li>Sweating</li><li>Tiredness or weakness</li></ul><p>Seek emergency medical care if you have <span class="qt0">chest</span> <span class="qt2">pain</span>, shortness of breath, or think you are having a heart attack.</p>How is an arrhythmia diagnosed?<p>To find out if you have an arrhythmia, your health care provider may order an electrocardiogram (EKG).  This test records your heart's electrical activity and is the most common for finding an arrhythmia. Your provider may also:</p><ul><li>Ask about your medical history, including your symptoms and lifestyle habits.</li><li>Do a physical exam, which includes checking your heartbeat and pulse.</li><li>Order blood tests and other heart tests.</li><li>Check your legs or feet for swelling.</li><li>Look for signs of other health conditions that could cause an arrythmia, such as thyroid disease.</li></ul>What are the treatments for an arrhythmia?<p>Treatment may include medicines, an implantable cardioverter-defibrillator (ICD) or pacemaker, or sometimes surgery. Your provider may also recommend avoiding activities that may trigger your arrhythmia.</p><p>The goal of treatment is to restore a normal heart rhythm. If not treated, arrhythmias can damage your heart, brain, and other organs and could be life-threatening.</p>Can arrhythmias be prevented?<p>To help prevent an arrhythmia, your provider may suggest that you make heart-healthy lifestyle changes and treat health conditions that may cause arrhythmias.</p><p>NIH: National Heart, Lung, and Blood Institute</p></content>
      <content name="mesh">Arrhythmias, Cardiac</content>
      <content name="groupName">Blood, Heart and Circulation</content>

    <document rank="6" url="https://medlineplus.gov/angioplasty.html">
      <content name="title">Angioplasty</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Balloon angioplasty</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Coronary angioplasty</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Percutaneous coronary intervention (PCI)</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is angioplasty?<p>Angioplasty is a procedure to improve blood flow in coronary arteries that have become narrow or blocked. Your coronary arteries supply oxygen-rich blood to your heart. If you have coronary artery disease, a sticky material called plaque builds up in your coronary arteries. Plaque is made of cholesterol, calcium, and other substances in your blood. Over time, it can narrow your arteries or fully block them. When this happens, some parts of your heart don't get enough blood.</p><p>Angioplasty widens the blocked part of your artery so more blood can get through. It is also called percutaneous coronary intervention (PCI).</p><p>

</p>What conditions does angioplasty treat?<p>Doctors (usually a heart specialist called a cardiologist) use angioplasty to:</p><ul><li>Reduce <span class="qt0">chest</span> <span class="qt2">pain</span> from blockages in the coronary arteries. This type of <span class="qt2">pain</span> is called angina. There are different types of angina. Angioplasty treats certain types.</li><li>Limit damage to the heart during or right after a heart attack. In this case, angioplasty is an emergency treatment.</li></ul><p>Angioplasty does not cure coronary artery disease. To help prevent more plaque blockages, you'll need to take any prescribed medicines, eat healthy foods, and get regular exercise.</p>What happens during angioplasty?<p>Most people have angioplasties in a hospital in a special room called a cardiac catheterization, or cath, lab. You will be awake and lying down. You'll get medicine to help you relax through an intravenous (IV) line. This is a small tube that goes into a vein in your hand or arm.</p><p>Angioplasty is done through a blood vessel in your arm, wrist, or groin. Your doctor will:</p><ul><li>Make a small opening in that area to insert a thin tube (a catheter) into a blood vessel.</li><li>Thread the tube through the vessel to your heart, using x-rays as a guide.</li><li>Inject contrast dye inside your arteries. The dye highlights your heart and blood vessels in the x-rays.</li><li>Replace the first tube with another one that has a small, deflated balloon on the end.</li><li>Guide the balloon inside the blockage and inflate it to push the plaque flat against the artery wall. This makes the artery wider and improves blood flow.</li><li>Sometimes put a small, mesh tube into the artery to help keep it open. The tube is called a stent. Some stents have a coating of medicine that helps prevent blood clots from forming.</li></ul>What happens after an angioplasty?<p>If you had an angioplasty for <span class="qt0">chest</span> <span class="qt2">pain</span>, you'll go to a recovery room for a few hours. You may stay in the hospital overnight. Your doctor will probably prescribe medicines to prevent blood clots. Most people can return to their usual activities after a week.</p><p>If you had an emergency angioplasty for a heart attack, you'll need to stay in the hospital for about a few more days.</p>Are there any risks from angioplasty?<p>Angioplasty is very safe, but every invasive procedure comes with risks. You may get a bruise, feel sore, or have some bleeding where the tubes were inserted. More serious problems don't happen very often, but they are possible. They can include serious bleeding, blood clots, and narrowing of the artery again.</p><p>NIH: National Heart, Lung, and Blood Institute</p></content>

This type  ... an angioplasty? If you had an angioplasty for <span class="qt0">chest</span> <span class="qt2">pain</span>, you'll go to a recovery room for  ... </content>

    <document rank="7" url="https://medlineplus.gov/gerd.html">
      <content name="title">GERD</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Acid Reflux</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Gastroesophageal Reflux</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Gastroesophageal reflux disease</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is GERD?<p>Sometimes, after eating a large meal or lying down too soon after eating, you might feel acid coming up into your throat. This is called gastroesophageal reflux (GER). It happens when the muscle at the end of your esophagus (the tube that carries food from your mouth to your stomach) doesn't close properly. Stomach contents can then move back up and irritate the esophagus.</p><p>Having reflux occasionally is common. But if you have symptoms two or more times a week or if they cause damage to the lining of your esophagus, you may have gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD). GERD is a chronic (long-lasting) condition that can cause discomfort and, over time, lead to other health problems.</p><p>Anyone can have GERD, including infants and children.</p>What causes GERD?<p>GERD can happen when the muscle at the bottom of your esophagus becomes weak or relaxes at the wrong time. You are more likely to have GERD if you:</p><ul><li>Are overweight or have obesity</li><li>Are pregnant</li><li>Smoke or are around secondhand smoke</li><li>Have a hiatal hernia (when part of the stomach pushes up into the <span class="qt0">chest</span>)</li></ul><p>Certain foods, drinks, or medicines can also make GERD worse.</p>What are common symptoms of GERD?<p>The most common symptom of GERD is heartburn. This is a burning feeling in your <span class="qt0">chest</span> or throat. You can also have GERD without having heartburn. Other symptoms may include:</p><ul><li>Tasting acid or food in the back of your mouth</li><li>A dry cough or hoarse voice</li><li>Asthma-like symptoms</li><li>Trouble swallowing</li></ul><p>Get medical help right away if you have <span class="qt0">chest</span> <span class="qt2">pain</span> with shortness of breath, or <span class="qt2">pain</span> in your jaw or arm. These can be signs of a heart problem.</p>How is GERD diagnosed?<p>Your health care provider will ask about your symptoms and medical history. They may recommend tests such as:</p><ul><li>Upper GI endoscopy to look at the esophagus and stomach lining with a tiny camera.</li><li>Biopsy to check a small tissue sample.</li><li>X-Ray to take pictures of the upper digestive system.</li><li>Esophageal pH test to measure how often acid backs up the esophagus and how long the acid stays there.</li><li>Esophageal manometry to check how well your esophagus moves food when you swallow.</li><li>Transnasal esophagoscopy to look for damage to the esophagus by using a small tube with a camera.</li></ul>How is GERD treated?<p>Most people can manage GERD with lifestyle changes and medicine. In rare cases, surgery is needed.</p><p>Lifestyle changes can include:</p><ul><li>Eating smaller meals and avoiding lying down for at least 2-3 hours after eating</li><li>Avoiding foods that trigger symptoms, such as spicy foods, chocolate, caffeine, or fatty foods</li><li>Losing weight if needed</li><li>Raising the head of your bed</li><li>Quitting smoking</li></ul><p>Medicines can include:</p><ul><li>Over-the-counter medicines, such as antacids</li><li>Prescription medicines</li></ul><p>If not treated, GERD can cause problems like inflammation of the esophagus, scarring, or changes in the tissue lining (Barrett's esophagus). It can also worsen asthma or cause chronic (long-term) cough or hoarseness.</p>Can GERD be prevented?<p>You may be able to improve symptoms of GERD by:</p><ul><li>Avoiding alcohol and trigger foods</li><li>Eating smaller meals</li><li>Not eating close to bedtime</li><li>Wearing loose-fitting clothes</li><li>Sleeping on your left side or raising the head of your bed</li><li>Maintaining a healthy weight</li></ul><p>NIH: National Institute of Diabetes and Digestive and Kidney Diseases</p></content>

    <document rank="8" url="https://medlineplus.gov/hiatalhernia.html">
      <content name="title">Hiatal Hernia</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>A hiatal hernia is a condition in which the upper part of your stomach bulges through an opening in your diaphragm. Your diaphragm is the thin muscle that separates your <span class="qt0">chest</span> from your abdomen. Your diaphragm helps keep acid from coming up into your esophagus. When you have a hiatal hernia, it's easier for the acid to come up. This leaking of acid from your stomach into your esophagus is called GERD (gastroesophageal reflux disease). GERD may cause symptoms such as :</p><ul><li>Heartburn</li><li>Problems swallowing</li><li>A dry cough</li><li>Bad breath</li><li>Nausea and/or vomiting</li><li>Breathing problems</li><li>The wearing away of your teeth</li></ul><p>Often, the cause of a hiatal hernia is unknown. It may have to do with weakness in the surrounding muscles. Sometimes the cause is an injury or a birth defect. Your risk of getting a hiatal hernia goes up as you age; they are common in people over age 50. You are also at higher risk if you have obesity or smoke.</p><p>People usually find out that they have a hiatal hernia when they are getting tests for GERD, heartburn, <span class="qt0">chest</span> <span class="qt2">pain</span>, or abdominal <span class="qt2">pain</span>. The tests may be a <span class="qt0">chest</span> x-ray, an x-ray with a barium swallow, or an upper endoscopy.</p><p>You don't need treatment if your hiatal hernia does not cause any symptoms or problems. If you do have symptoms, some lifestyle changes may help. They include eating small meals, avoiding certain foods, not smoking or drinking alcohol, and losing weight. Your health care provider may recommend antacids or other medicines. If these don't help, you may need surgery.</p><p>NIH: National Institute of Diabetes and Digestive and Kidney Diseases</p></content>

    <document rank="9" url="https://medlineplus.gov/sarcoidosis.html">
      <content name="title">Sarcoidosis</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is sarcoidosis? <p>Sarcoidosis is a condition in which groups of cells in your immune system form small, red, and swollen (inflamed) lumps. These lumps are called granulomas. They can form in any organ in the body. But they most commonly affect the lungs and lymph nodes in the <span class="qt0">chest</span>. Over time, sarcoidosis can cause permanent scarring of organs.</p>What causes sarcoidosis?<p>Your immune system creates inflammation to help defend you against germs and sickness. But in sarcoidosis, inflammation goes off track. It causes the cells in your immune system to form granulomas. Studies suggest that this inflammation might be triggered by infections and certain substances in the environment such as insecticides and mold. And your genes may affect how your immune system reacts to a trigger.</p>Who is more likely to develop sarcoidosis?<p>Anyone can develop sarcoidosis, but you are more likely to have it if you:</p><ul><li>Are older, especially if you are over age 55</li><li>Live or work near insecticides, mold, or other substances that may cause inflammation</li><li>Have a close relative who has sarcoidosis</li><li>Take certain medicines, such as some types of HIV medicines and monoclonal antibodies</li><li>Are of African or Scandinavian descent</li><li>Are female</li></ul>What are the symptoms of sarcoidosis?<p>Many people who have sarcoidosis have no symptoms, or they may feel unwell but without any obvious symptoms. If you have symptoms, you may have some general symptoms. And you may have other symptoms, which will depend upon which part of the body is affected.</p><p>The general symptoms can include:</p><ul><li>Fatigue</li><li>Weight loss</li><li>Swollen lymph nodes</li><li>Depression</li><li>Fever</li><li>Night sweats (heavy sweating during sleep)</li></ul><p>The symptoms of sarcoidosis in the lungs can include:</p><ul><li>Wheezing</li><li>Cough</li><li>Shortness of breath</li><li><span class="qt0">Chest</span> <span class="qt2">pain</span></li></ul><p>The symptoms of sarcoidosis in other parts of the body can include:
</p><ul><li>Larger than normal liver or spleen</li><li>Jaundice, which can make your eyes or skin yellow</li><li>Nervous system problems, such as:
  <ul><li><span class="qt2">Headache</span></li><li>Dizziness</li><li>Vision problems</li><li>Seizures</li><li>Numbness or tingling</li></ul></li><li>Heart palpitations or an irregular heartbeat (arrhythmia)</li><li><span class="qt2">Pain</span> and swelling in the joints</li><li>Skin problems such as rashes and growths under the skin</li></ul><p>There is also a set of symptoms that are called Lofgren's syndrome. Some people have Lofgren's syndrome when they first develop sarcoidosis. It usually goes away completely within 2 years. The symptoms of Lofgren's syndrome may include:</p><ul><li>Swollen lymph nodes in your <span class="qt0">chest</span>, neck, chin, armpits, or groin.</li><li>A rash of small, itchy, or painful bumps called erythema nodosum. The rash is most often on your head, neck, or legs.</li><li>Blurred vision, eye <span class="qt2">pain</span> or redness, light sensitivity, or watery eyes.</li><li>Joint <span class="qt2">pain</span>, stiffness, or swelling.</li><li>Fever.</li></ul>What other problems can sarcoidosis cause?<p>If untreated, or if the treatment does not work, sarcoidosis can cause serious health problems called complications. Possible complications include:</p><ul><li>Lung problems</li><li>Heart problems</li><li>Hormone problems</li><li>Brain, nerve, or muscle problems</li><li>Kidney problems</li><li>Severe organ damage</li></ul>How is sarcoidosis diagnosed?<p>There is no single test that can diagnose sarcoidosis, and its symptoms can be similar to the symptoms of many other conditions. That makes it hard to diagnose. To find out if you have sarcoidosis, your health care provider:</p><ul><li>Will ask about your medical history, including your symptoms, lifestyle, and any other health conditions you may have.</li><li>Will do a physical exam, which may include:
  <ul><li>Checking your temperature</li><li>Checking to see if your lymph nodes, spleen, or liver are swollen</li><li>Listening to your <span class="qt0">chest</span> with a stethoscope as you breathe in and out</li><li>Looking for rashes or sores on your body, such as scalp and lower legs</li></ul>
</li><li>Will likely order various tests, such as:
  <ul><li><span class="qt0">Chest</span> x-rays</li><li>A biopsy of the skin, lymph nodes, lungs, or other affected organs</li><li>Blood tests to check your blood counts, hormone levels, and how well your kidneys are working</li><li>Other imaging tests</li></ul>
</li></ul><p>If you are diagnosed with sarcoidosis, your provider may order other tests to look at how sarcoidosis is affecting the body.</p>What are the treatments for sarcoidosis?<p>There is no cure for sarcoidosis. The goal of treatment for sarcoidosis is remission, which means you still have the condition, but it does not cause you problems.</p><p>You may not need treatment, and sometimes the condition goes away on its own. If you do need treatment, which treatment(s) you get will depend on your symptoms, which organs are affected, and whether those organs are working well. You may be given medicines to:</p><ul><li>Lower inflammation</li><li>Treat an overactive immune response</li><li>Treat joint <span class="qt2">pain</span></li><li>Treat skin problems</li></ul><p>If you have complications from sarcoidosis, you may need other treatments, such as other medicines, surgery, oxygen therapy, pulmonary rehabilitation, or an implanted cardiac pacemaker or defibrillator.</p><p>Whether or not you have symptoms from sarcoidosis, it's important to get regular follow-up care from your provider, make healthy lifestyle changes, and contact your provider if you have any new symptoms.</p><p>NIH: National Heart, Lung, and Blood Institute</p></content>

    <document rank="10" url="https://medlineplus.gov/lymphoma.html">
      <content name="title">Lymphoma</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Non-Hodgkin Lymphoma</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Waldenstrom's Macroglobulinemia</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>Lymphoma is a cancer of a part of the immune system called the lymph system.  There are many types of lymphoma.  One type is Hodgkin disease.  The rest are called non-Hodgkin lymphomas.</p><p>Non-Hodgkin lymphomas begin when a type of white blood cell, called a T cell or B cell, becomes abnormal. The cell divides again and again, making more and more abnormal cells. These abnormal cells can spread to almost any other part of the body. Most of the time, doctors don't know why a person gets non-Hodgkin lymphoma. You are at increased risk if you have a weakened immune system or have certain types of infections.</p><p>Non-Hodgkin lymphoma can cause many symptoms, such as :</p><ul><li>Swollen, <span class="qt2">painless</span> lymph nodes in the neck, armpits or groin</li><li>Unexplained weight loss </li><li>Fever </li><li>Soaking night sweats </li><li>Coughing, trouble breathing or <span class="qt0">chest</span> <span class="qt2">pain</span> </li><li>Weakness and tiredness that don't go away </li><li><span class="qt2">Pain</span>, swelling or a feeling of fullness in the abdomen </li></ul><p>Your doctor will diagnose lymphoma with a physical exam, blood tests, a <span class="qt0">chest</span> x-ray, and a biopsy.  Treatments include chemotherapy, radiation therapy, targeted therapy, biological therapy, or therapy to remove proteins from the blood. Targeted therapy uses drugs or other substances that attack specific cancer cells with less harm to normal cells. Biologic therapy boosts your body's own ability to fight cancer. If you don't have symptoms, you may not need treatment right away. This is called watchful waiting.</p><p>NIH: National Cancer Institute</p></content>

    <document rank="11" url="https://medlineplus.gov/heartburn.html">
      <content name="title">Heartburn</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Acid Reflux</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Acid indigestion</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Pyrosis</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>Heartburn is a painful burning feeling in your <span class="qt0">chest</span> or throat. It happens when stomach acid backs up into your esophagus, the tube that carries food from your mouth to your stomach.</p><p>If you have heartburn more than twice a week, you may have GERD. But you can have GERD without having heartburn.</p><p>Pregnancy, certain foods, alcohol, and some medications can bring on heartburn. Treating heartburn is important because over time reflux can damage the esophagus.</p><p>Over-the-counter medicines may help.  If the heartburn continues, you may need prescription medicines or surgery. </p><p>If you have other symptoms such as crushing <span class="qt0">chest</span> <span class="qt2">pain</span>, it could be a heart attack. Get help immediately.</p></content>
      <content name="mesh">Heartburn</content>
      <content name="groupName">Digestive System</content>
      <content name="groupName">Symptoms</content>
      <content name="snippet"> ... you have other symptoms such as crushing <span class="qt0">chest</span> <span class="qt2">pain</span>, it could be a heart attack. Get help immediately.</content>

    <document rank="12" url="https://medlineplus.gov/bloodclots.html">
      <content name="title">Blood Clots</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Thrombophlebitis</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Venous Thrombosis</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Hypercoagulability</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is a blood clot?<p>A blood clot is a mass of blood that forms when platelets, proteins, and cells in the blood stick together. When you get hurt, your body forms a blood clot to stop the bleeding.  After the bleeding stops and healing takes place, your body usually breaks down and removes the blood clot. But sometimes the blood clots form where they shouldn't, your body makes too many blood clots or abnormal blood clots, or the blood clots don't break down like they should. These blood clots can be dangerous and may cause other health problems.</p><p>Blood clots can form in, or travel to, the blood vessels in the limbs, lungs, brain, heart, and kidneys. The types of problems blood clots can cause will depend on where they are:</p><ul><li>Deep vein thrombosis (DVT) is a blood clot in a deep vein, usually in the lower leg, thigh, or pelvis. It can block a vein and cause damage to your leg.</li><li>A pulmonary embolism (PE) can happen when a DVT breaks off and travels through the bloodstream to the lungs. A PE, especially if it is large, can damage your lungs and prevent your other organs from getting enough oxygen.</li><li>Cerebral venous sinus thrombosis (CVST) is a rare blood clot in the venous sinuses in your brain. Normally the venous sinuses drain blood from your brain. CVST blocks the blood from draining and can cause a hemorrhagic stroke.</li><li>Blood clots in other parts of the body can cause problems such as an ischemic stroke, a heart attack, kidney problems, kidney failure, and pregnancy-related problems.</li></ul>Who is more likely to develop blood clots?<p>Certain factors can raise your risk of developing blood clots:</p><ul><li>Atherosclerosis</li><li>Atrial fibrillation</li><li>Being overweight or having obesity</li><li>Cancer and cancer treatments</li><li>Certain genetic disorders</li><li>Certain surgeries</li><li>COVID-19</li><li>Diabetes</li><li>Family history of blood clots</li><li>Overweight and obesity</li><li>Pregnancy and giving birth</li><li>Serious injuries</li><li>Some medicines, including birth control pills</li><li>Smoking</li><li>Staying in one position for a long time, such as being in the hospital or taking a long car or plane ride</li></ul>What are the symptoms of blood clots?<p>The symptoms for blood clots can be different, depending on where the blood clot is:</p><ul><li>In the abdomen: Abdominal <span class="qt2">pain</span>, nausea and vomiting</li><li>In an arm or leg: Sudden or gradual <span class="qt2">pain</span>, swelling, tenderness, and warmth</li><li>In the lungs: Shortness of breath, <span class="qt2">pain</span> with deep breathing, rapid breathing, and increased heart rate</li><li>In the brain: Trouble speaking, vision problems, seizures, weakness on one side of the body, and sudden severe <span class="qt2">headache</span></li><li>In the heart: <span class="qt0">Chest</span> <span class="qt2">pain</span>, sweating, shortness of breath, and <span class="qt2">pain</span> in the left arm</li></ul>How are blood clots diagnosed?<p>To find out if you have one or more blood clots, your health care provider: </p><ul><li>Will do a physical exam.</li><li>Will take your medical history, which includes asking about your symptoms.</li><li>May order blood tests, including a D-dimer test.</li><li>May order imaging tests, such as
    <ul><li>Ultrasound.</li><li>X-rays of the veins (venography) or blood vessels (angiography) that are taken after you get an injection of special dye. The dye shows up on the x-ray and allows the provider to see how the blood flows.</li><li>CT Scan.</li></ul>
</li></ul>What are the treatments for blood clots?<p>Treatments for blood clots depend on where the blood clot is located and how severe it is. Treatments may include:</p><ul><li>Blood thinners.</li><li>Other medicines, including thrombolytics. Thrombolytics are medicines that dissolve blood clots. They are usually used where the blood clots are severe.</li><li>Surgery and other procedures to remove the blood clots</li></ul>Can blood clots be prevented?<p>You may be able to help prevent blood clots by:</p><ul><li>Moving around as soon as possible after having been confined to your bed, such as after surgery, illness, or injury</li><li>Getting up and moving around every few hours when you have to sit for long periods of time, for example if you are on a long flight or car trip</li><li>Wearing compression stockings after surgery or during a long flight</li><li>Regular physical activity</li><li>Not smoking</li><li>Staying at a healthy weight</li></ul><p>Some people at high risk may need to take blood thinners to prevent blood clots.</p></content>

    <document rank="13" url="https://medlineplus.gov/eosinophilicesophagitis.html">
      <content name="title">Eosinophilic Esophagitis</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">EoE</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is eosinophilic esophagitis (EoE)?<p>Eosinophilic esophagitis (EoE) is a chronic disease of the esophagus. Your esophagus is the muscular tube that carries food and liquids from your mouth to the stomach. If you have EoE, white blood cells called eosinophils build up in your esophagus. This causes damage and inflammation, which can cause <span class="qt2">pain</span>, trouble swallowing, and food getting stuck in your throat.</p><p>EoE is rare. But because it is a newly recognized disease, it is being diagnosed more often. Some people who think that they have reflux (GERD) may actually have EoE.</p>What causes eosinophilic esophagitis (EoE)?<p>Researchers are not certain about the exact cause of EoE. They think that it is an immune system/allergic reaction to foods or to substances in your environment, such as dust mites, animal dander, pollen, and molds. Certain genes may also play a role in EoE.</p>Who is at risk for eosinophilic esophagitis(EoE)?<p>EoE can affect anyone, but it is more common in people who:</p><ul><li>Are male</li><li>Have other allergic diseases, such as hay fever, eczema, asthma and food allergies</li><li>Have family members with EoE</li></ul>What are the symptoms of eosinophilic esophagitis (EoE)?<p>The most common symptoms of EoE can depend on your age.</p><p>In infants and toddlers::</p><ul><li>Feeding problems</li><li>Vomiting</li><li>Poor weight gain and growth</li><li>Reflux that does not get better with medicines</li></ul><p>In older children::</p><ul><li>Vomiting</li><li>Abdominal <span class="qt2">pain</span></li><li>Trouble swallowing, especially with solid foods</li><li>Reflux that does not get better with medicines</li><li>Poor appetite</li></ul><p>In adults::</p><ul><li>Trouble swallowing, especially with solid foods</li><li>Food getting stuck in the esophagus</li><li>Reflux that does not get better with medicines</li><li>Heartburn</li><li><span class="qt0">Chest</span> <span class="qt2">pain</span></li></ul>How is eosinophilic esophagitis (EoE) diagnosed?<p>To find out if you have EoE, your doctor will likely:</p><ul><li>Ask about your symptoms and medical history. Since other conditions can have the same symptoms of EoE, it is important for your doctor to take a thorough history.</li><li>Do an upper gastrointestinal (GI) endoscopy. An endoscope is a long, flexible tube with a light and camera at the end of it. Your doctor will run the endoscope down your esophagus and look at it. Some signs that you might have EoE include white spots, rings, narrowing, and inflammation in the esophagus. However, not everyone with EoE has those signs, and sometimes they can be signs of a different esophagus disorder.</li><li>Do a biopsy. During the endoscopy, the doctor will take small tissue samples from your esophagus. The samples will be checked for a high number of eosinophils. This is the only way to make a diagnosis of EoE.</li><li>Do other tests as needed. You may have blood tests to check for other conditions. If you do have EoE, you may have blood or other types of tests to check for specific allergies.</li></ul>What are the treatments for eosinophilic esophagitis (EoE)?<p>There is no cure for EoE. Treatments can manage your symptoms and prevent further damage. The two main types of treatments are medicines and diet.</p><p>Medicines used to treat EoE are:</p><ul><li>Steroids, which can help control inflammation. These are usually topical steroids, which you swallow either from an inhaler or as a liquid. Sometimes doctors prescribe oral steroids (pills) to treat people who have serious swallowing problems or weight loss.</li><li>Acid suppressors such as proton pump inhibitors (PPIs), which may help with reflux symptoms and decrease inflammation.</li><li>Monoclonal antibodies, which can reduce inflammation and may help with swallowing. They can be used in adults and children over age 12.</li></ul><p>Dietary changes for EoE include:</p><ul><li>Elimination diet. If you are on an elimination diet, you stop eating and drinking certain foods and beverages for several weeks. If you are feeling better, you add the foods back to your diet one at a time. You have repeat endoscopies to see whether or not you are tolerating those foods. There are different types of elimination diets:</li><ul><li>With one type, you first have an allergy test. Then you stop eating and drinking the foods you are allergic to.</li><li>For another type, you eliminate foods and drinks that commonly cause allergies, such as dairy products, egg, wheat, soy, peanuts, tree nuts and fish/shellfish.</li></ul><li>Elemental diet. With this diet, you stop eating and drinking all proteins. Instead, you drink an amino acid formula. Some people who do not like the taste of the formula use a feeding tube instead. If your symptoms and inflammation go away completely, you may be able to try adding foods back one at a time, to see whether you can tolerate them.</li></ul><p>Which treatment your doctor suggests depends on different factors, including your age. Some people may use more than one kind of treatment. Researchers are still trying to understand EoE and how best to treat it.</p><p>If your treatment is not working well enough and you have narrowing of the esophagus, you may need dilation. This is a procedure to stretch the esophagus. This makes it easier for you to swallow.</p></content>

    <document rank="14" url="https://medlineplus.gov/thymuscancer.html">
      <content name="title">Thymus Cancer</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Thymoma</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>The thymus is a small organ in your upper <span class="qt0">chest</span>, under your breastbone. Before birth and during childhood, the thymus helps the body make a type of white blood cell. These cells help protect you from infections. </p><p>Cancer of the thymus is rare. You are more likely to get it if you have other diseases such as myasthenia gravis, lupus or rheumatoid arthritis. Sometimes there are no symptoms.  Other times, thymus cancer can cause:</p><ul><li>A cough that doesn't go away </li><li><span class="qt0">Chest</span> <span class="qt2">pain</span></li><li>Trouble breathing</li></ul><p>Doctors use a physical exam, imaging tests, and a biopsy to diagnose thymus cancer. The most common treatment is surgery to remove the tumor. Other options include radiation therapy, chemotherapy, and hormone therapy.</p><p>NIH: National Cancer Institute</p></content>
      <content name="mesh">Thymus Neoplasms</content>
      <content name="groupName">Cancers</content>
      <content name="groupName">Immune System</content>
      <content name="groupName">Endocrine System</content>

    <document rank="15" url="https://medlineplus.gov/alpha1antitrypsindeficiency.html">
      <content name="title">Alpha-1 Antitrypsin Deficiency</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">AATD</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Alpha-1</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Inherited emphysema</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency (AAT deficiency)?<p>Alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency (AAT deficiency, or AATD) is an inherited condition that raises your risk for lung and liver disease. If you have this condition, your body doesn't make enough alpha-1 antitrypsin (AAT).</p><p>AAT is made by your liver. It helps protect your lungs from inflammation and irritating substances you might breathe in, such as smoke. If your liver doesn't make enough AAT, your lungs may be more easily damaged from smoking, pollution, or dust from the environment. This can lead to a serious lung condition called chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD). AAT deficiency may also cause a liver disease called cirrhosis. This is more common in children who have AAT deficiency.</p>What causes alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency (AAT deficiency)?<p>AAT deficiency is a genetic disorder. That means it's caused by changes in your genes, which may also be called gene variants or mutations.</p><p>Your genes carry information that controls what you look like and how your body works. AAT deficiency is caused by changes in the SERPINA1 gene, which carries instructions for making the AAT protein. These gene changes are inherited from your parents, so AAT deficiency tends to run in families:</p><ul><li>If you have two mutated copies of the gene, it means you have a condition called AAT deficiency. People with this disorder have a higher risk of getting lung disease or liver damage before the age of 45.</li><li>If you have one mutated copy of the gene, you are a carrier of AAT deficiency. In these cases, this means you are at slightly higher risk of developing lung disease, especially if you have other risk factors, such as being a smoker. You could still pass the mutated gene on to your children.</li></ul><p>There are a few gene changes that cause AAT deficiency. These gene changes can:</p><ul><li>Decrease the amount of AAT protein your liver makes.</li><li>Prevent your liver from making any AAT.</li><li>Affect the shape of the AAT protein so that it can't move out of your liver to protect your lungs. Over time, AAT builds up in your liver and causes damage.</li></ul>What are the symptoms of alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency (AAT deficiency)?<p>Some people who have AAT deficiency do not have any symptoms. For those who do, symptoms usually appear in people between 20 and 50 years old. These symptoms may include:</p><ul><li>Wheezing</li><li>Shortness of breath, especially after exercise</li><li>Chronic cough with phlegm (mucus)</li><li>Repeated respiratory infections such as colds and the flu</li><li><span class="qt0">Chest</span> <span class="qt2">pain</span></li><li>Fatigue</li><li>Faster-than-normal heartbeat when you stand up</li><li>Vision problems</li><li>Losing weight without trying</li></ul><p>Some people who have AAT deficiency may have liver damage. Signs of liver damage include jaundice (a condition that causes your skin and eyes to turn yellow) and swelling in your legs.</p><p>Rarely, AAT deficiency can cause skin problems, such as painful lumps or patches.</p>How is alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency (AAT deficiency) diagnosed?<p>Your health care provider may test you for AAT deficiency if you have:</p><ul><li>Symptoms of AAT deficiency</li><li>A condition that could be related to AAT deficiency</li><li>Relatives who have AAT deficiency</li><li>Relatives who have a lung or liver disease that could be related to AAT deficiency</li></ul><p>AAT deficiency in babies often affects the liver. Your baby may need AAT testing if he or she has signs of liver disease such as jaundice or abnormal liver enzyme tests.</p><p>A blood test can check the level of AAT protein in your blood. If the level is lower than normal, it is likely that you have AAT deficiency.</p><p>A genetic test is the most certain way to check for AAT deficiency and should be done to confirm the results of the blood test. There are two types of genetic tests:</p><ul><li>A genotype test looks for the more common types of gene changes that can cause AAT deficiency.</li><li>A phenotype test checks for changes in the AAT protein that change how it would normally work.</li></ul><p>If your lungs are affected, you may also have lung function tests to see how well your lungs are working.</p>What are the treatments for alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency (AAT deficiency)?<p>There is no cure for AAT deficiency, but there are treatments to help with the symptoms and slow the lung damage it causes. Treatment options may include:</p><ul><li>Inhaled medicines to help you breathe better.</li><li>Pulmonary rehabilitation.</li><li>Oxygen therapy.</li><li>Augmentation therapy, which is a lifelong treatment. It raises the levels of the AAT protein in your lungs, using ATT protein taken from the blood of donors. It helps slow down lung damage. It cannot prevent liver damage.</li><li>Lung surgery or a lung transplant, if your lungs are severely damaged.</li><li>Liver transplant, if your liver is severely damaged.</li></ul><p>To help prevent or delay lung damage, it is important to quit smoking (if you smoke) and avoid secondhand smoke, dust, and air pollution. Ask your provider If you need to stop drinking alcohol.</p></content>

    <document rank="16" url="https://medlineplus.gov/anaphylaxis.html">
      <content name="title">Anaphylaxis</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Anaphylactic shock</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>Anaphylaxis is a serious allergic reaction. It can begin very quickly, and symptoms may be life-threatening. The most common causes are reactions to foods (especially peanuts), medications, and stinging insects. Other causes include exercise and exposure to latex. Sometimes no cause can be found.</p><p>It can affect many organs:</p><ul><li>Skin - itching, hives, redness, swelling</li><li>Nose - sneezing, stuffy nose, runny nose</li><li>Mouth - itching, swelling of the lips or tongue</li><li>Throat - itching, tightness, trouble swallowing, swelling of the back of the throat</li><li><span class="qt0">Chest</span> - shortness of breath, coughing, wheezing, <span class="qt0">chest</span> <span class="qt2">pain</span> or tightness</li><li>Heart - weak pulse, passing out, shock</li><li>Gastrointestinal tract - vomiting, diarrhea, <span class="qt2">cramps</span></li><li>Nervous system - dizziness or fainting</li></ul><p>If someone is having a serious allergic reaction, call 911. If an auto-injector  is available, give the person the injection right away.</p></content>
      <content name="mesh">Anaphylaxis</content>

    <document rank="17" url="https://medlineplus.gov/atherosclerosis.html">
      <content name="title">Atherosclerosis</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Arteriosclerosis</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Hardening of the Arteries</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Plaque, Atherosclerotic</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is atherosclerosis?<p>Atherosclerosis is a condition in which plaque builds up inside your arteries. Plaque is a sticky substance made up of cholesterol, fat, blood cells, calcium, and other substances found in the blood. Over time, plaque hardens and causes your arteries to narrow. That limits the flow of oxygen-rich blood to your body.</p><p>Some people may confuse atherosclerosis and arteriosclerosis, but they are not the same thing:</p><ul><li>Arteriosclerosis is hardening of the arteries, which means that the arteries thicken and become less flexible. It can have several different causes.</li><li>Atherosclerosis, which develops from plaque buildup, is a common type of arteriosclerosis.</li></ul><p>Atherosclerosis can affect most of the arteries in the body. It has different names, based on which arteries are affected:</p><ul><li>Coronary artery disease (CAD) is plaque buildup in the arteries of your heart.
</li><li>Peripheral artery disease (PAD) is plaque buildup in the arteries that carry blood away from the heart to other parts of the body. It most often affects the arteries of your legs, but it can also affect the arteries of your arms or pelvis.
</li><li>Carotid artery disease is plaque buildup in the neck arteries. It reduces blood flow to the brain.
</li><li>Renal artery stenosis is plaque buildup in the arteries that supply blood to your kidneys.
</li><li>Vertebral artery disease is plaque buildup in the arteries that supply blood to the back of your brain. 
</li><li>Mesenteric artery ischemia is plaque buildup in the arteries that supply your intestines with blood. 
</li></ul>What causes atherosclerosis?<p>Plaque often starts to build up during childhood and gets worse with age. The exact cause is unknown, but researchers believe that this buildup happens when there is damage to the arteries. This damage may be caused by unhealthy lifestyle habits, medical conditions, and your genes.</p>Who is more likely to develop atherosclerosis?<p>You may be more likely to develop atherosclerosis if you:</p><ul><li>Have certain medical conditions, including:
	<ul><li>High blood pressure</li><li>High blood cholesterol</li><li>Diabetes</li><li>Metabolic syndrome</li><li>Inflammatory diseases such as rheumatoid arthritis and psoriasis</li></ul>
</li><li>Have a family history of high blood cholesterol</li><li>Eat a lot of foods high in saturated fats</li><li>Smoke or chew tobacco</li><li>Are older - the risk increases after age 45 men and age 55 in women</li></ul>What are the symptoms of atherosclerosis?<p>In the early stages, atherosclerosis often does not cause any symptoms. You may first notice some symptoms at times when your body needs more oxygen. For example, this could be when you are having physical or emotional stress.</p><p>Your symptoms will depend on which arteries are affected and how much blood flow is blocked:</p><ul><li>With coronary artery disease, the symptoms may include angina (a type of <span class="qt0">chest</span> <span class="qt2">pain</span>), palpitations (racing or pounding heart), and shortness of breath.</li><li>With carotid artery disease, you may have a bruit. This is a whooshing sound that your health care provider hears when using a stethoscope. You could also have a transient ischemic attack (TIA), sometimes called a mini-stroke.</li><li>With peripheral artery disease, you may have <span class="qt2">pain</span>, aching, heaviness, or cramping in the legs when walking or climbing stairs.</li><li>With vertebral artery disease, you may have problems with thinking and memory, weakness or numbness on one side of the body or face, and vision trouble. You could also have a transient ischemic attack.</li><li>With mesenteric artery ischemia, the symptoms can include severe <span class="qt2">pain</span> after meals, weight loss, and diarrhea.</li></ul><p>For men, erectile dysfunction (ED) is an early warning sign that you may be at higher risk for atherosclerosis and its complications. If you have ED, talk with your provider about your risk of plaque buildup.</p>What other problems can atherosclerosis cause?<p>Atherosclerosis can cause other health problems, or complications. For example, if a plaque bursts, a blood clot may form. The clot could block the artery completely or travel to another part of the body. Other possible complications can vary, depending on which arteries are affected. For example, blockages in different parts of the body can lead to complications such as a heart attack, stroke, vascular dementia, or limb loss.</p>How is atherosclerosis diagnosed?<p>To find out if you have atherosclerosis, your provider:</p><ul><li>Will ask about your medical and family health history</li><li>Will ask about your lifestyle and risk factors for plaque buildup in the arteries</li><li>Will do a physical exam, which will include listening to your heart and the blood flow in your arteries</li><li>Will likely order tests, such as blood tests and heart health tests</li></ul>What are the treatments for atherosclerosis?<p>If you have atherosclerosis, your provider will work with you to create a treatment plan that works for you. Your plan will depend on which arteries are affected, how much the blood flow is blocked, and what other medical conditions you have. Possible treatments may include:</p><ul><li>Heart-healthy lifestyle changes.</li><li>Medicines to:
	<ul><li>Manage your risk factors.</li><li>Treat atherosclerosis or its complications.</li><li>Treat any medical conditions you have that can worsen plaque buildup.</li></ul>
</li><li>Procedures or surgeries to treat diseases or complications that were caused by plaque buildup. The specific type of procedure or surgery will depend on which arteries are affected.</li><li>Cardiac rehabilitation, if you have had certain complications from atherosclerosis.</li></ul>Can atherosclerosis be prevented?<p>There are steps you can take to try to prevent atherosclerosis:</p><ul><li>Choose heart-healthy foods, such fruits, vegetables, and whole grains. Limit foods that are high in saturated fats, salt, and added sugars.</li><li>Do regular physical activity. But before you start an exercise program, ask your provider what level of physical activity is right for you.</li><li>Aim for a healthy weight.</li><li>Limit how much alcohol you drink. Drinking less is better for health than drinking more. Men should limit their intake to 2 drinks or less in a day. Women should drink 1 drink or less per day.</li><li>Manage stress.</li><li>If you smoke, quit smoking.</li><li>Avoid secondhand smoke.</li><li>Get enough good-quality sleep.</li></ul><p>NIH: National Heart, Lung, and Blood Institute</p></content>
      <content name="mesh">Atherosclerosis</content>
      <content name="groupName">Blood, Heart and Circulation</content>
