    <document rank="18" url="https://medlineplus.gov/atrialfibrillation.html">
      <content name="title">Atrial Fibrillation</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">AF</content>
      <content name="altTitle">AFib</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is atrial fibrillation (AFib)?<p>Atrial fibrillation, also known as AFib or AF, is one of the most common types of arrhythmias.  Arrhythmias are problems with the rate or rhythm of your heartbeat. They can cause your heart to beat too slowly, too fast, or in an irregular way.</p><p>If you have AFib, your heart beats irregularly and sometimes much faster than normal. Also, your heart's upper and lower chambers do not work together as they should. When this happens, the lower chambers do not fill completely or pump enough blood to your lungs and body. This can cause symptoms such as dizziness, fatigue, and a pounding heartbeat.</p><p>AFib may happen in brief episodes, or it may be a permanent condition. It's very important to treat it, since AFib can put you at risk for stroke and other heart conditions.</p>What causes atrial fibrillation (AFib)?<p>AFib is most often caused by changes to the heart's tissue or the electrical signaling that helps the heartbeat. These changes can happen due to different conditions and factors, such as high blood pressure, coronary artery disease, congenital heart defects, infections, and aging. Sometimes the cause is unknown.</p>Who is more likely to develop atrial fibrillation (AFib)?<p>Anyone can develop AFib, but there are certain things that raise your risk for it:</p><ul><li>Aging. The risk of atrial fibrillation increases as you get older, especially when you are over age 65.</li><li>Family history and genetics. AFib can run in families. So can heart disease, which raises your risk of AFib.</li><li>Some lifestyle choices. Your risk is higher if you drink a lot of alcohol, use certain illegal drugs such as cocaine and methamphetamines, or smoke.</li><li>Having certain health conditions, such as:
  <ul><li>High blood pressure</li><li>Diabetes</li><li>Heart failure</li><li>Heart valve diseases</li><li>Obesity</li><li>Hyperthyroidism</li><li>Chronic kidney disease</li><li>COPD and other lung diseases</li><li>Sleep apnea</li></ul>
</li><li>Race. AFib is more common in people with European ancestry.</li><li>Recent surgery. You may be at risk of atrial fibrillation in the early days and weeks after surgery on your heart, lungs, or esophagus.</li></ul>What are the symptoms of atrial fibrillation (AFib)?<p>Some people who have AFib don't have any symptoms and don't know they have it. If you do have symptoms, you may only notice them once in a while. Or you may have symptoms that are more frequent. And in some cases, the symptoms might be severe. If you have heart disease, you are more likely to notice your symptoms. And those symptoms could get worse if your heart disease gets worse.</p><p>The symptoms of AFib can include:</p><ul><li>Extreme fatigue, which is the most common symptom</li><li>Heart palpitations (the feeling that your heart is skipping a beat, fluttering, pounding, or beating too hard or too fast)</li><li>Trouble breathing, especially when lying down or when exercising</li><li><span class="qt0">Chest</span> <span class="qt2">pain</span></li><li>Dizziness or fainting</li><li>Low blood pressure</li></ul>What other problems can AFib cause?<p>If AFib is not treated, it can lead to serious health problems (complications) such as:</p><ul><li>Stroke</li><li>Heart failure</li><li>Blood clots</li><li>Sudden cardiac arrest (SCA)</li><li>Cognitive impairment and dementia</li></ul><p>To help prevent these problems, it's important to contact your health care provider if you are having symptoms. If you do have AFib, the sooner you are diagnosed and treated, the better.</p>How is atrial fibrillation (AFib) diagnosed?<p>To find out if you have AFib, your provider:</p><ul><li>Will ask about your medical history, including your symptoms, lifestyle, and any other health conditions you may have</li><li>Will ask about your family history, to find out if you have relatives who have or had AFib</li><li>Will do a physical exam</li><li>May order blood tests</li><li>Will likely order heart tests, such as an electrocardiogram (also called an EKG or ECG) and echocardiogram</li><li>May ask you to wear a heart monitor device that records your heart's electrical activity</li></ul>What are the treatments for atrial fibrillation (AFib)?<p>The treatments for AFib may include:</p><ul><li>Blood thinner medicines that help prevent blood clots from forming.</li><li>Medicines to control your heart's rhythm and rate.</li><li>Following heart-healthy lifestyle changes, such as:
  <ul><li>Following a heart-healthy eating plan that limits saturated fats, salt, and cholesterol. An example is the DASH eating plan.</li><li>Limiting or avoiding alcohol, because it can increase your heart rate.</li><li>Aiming for a healthy weight.</li><li>Getting regular physical activity.</li><li>Managing stress.</li><li>Quitting smoking.</li></ul>
</li><li>Procedures such as:
  <ul><li>Electrical cardioversion, which restores your heart rhythm using low-energy shocks to your heart.</li><li>Catheter ablation, which scars the tissue that is causing the arrhythmia. The scar tissue blocks the abnormal heart signals.</li></ul>
</li><li>Surgeries such as:
  <ul><li>Surgery to put in a pacemaker to help control the arrhythmia.</li><li>A Maze procedure, which creates scar tissue in a maze-like pattern in certain parts of the heart.</li><li>Left atrial appendage closure, a surgery on a small sac in the muscle wall of your left atrium (the upper left chamber of your heart). It helps prevent blood clots and can reduce your risk of stroke. This surgery is for people who are not able to take blood thinners.</li></ul>
</li></ul>Can atrial fibrillation (AFib) be prevented?<p>There are steps you can take to help lower your risk of atrial fibrillation, such as:</p><ul><li>Making heart-healthy lifestyle changes:
  <ul><li>Following a heart-healthy eating plan</li><li>Limiting or avoiding alcohol</li><li>Aiming for a healthy weight</li><li>Getting regular physical activity</li><li>Managing stress</li><li>Not smoking</li></ul>
</li><li>Avoiding illegal drugs, such as cocaine and methamphetamines</li><li>Taking antiarrhythmic medicine (medicine to treat arrhythmia) if you are having heart surgery</li><li>Treating any health conditions that could raise your risk of AFib</li></ul><p>NIH: National Heart, Lung, and Blood Institute</p></content>

      <content name="groupName">Blood, Heart and Circulation</content>

    <document rank="19" url="https://medlineplus.gov/carbonmonoxidepoisoning.html">
      <content name="title">Carbon Monoxide Poisoning</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">CO Poisoning</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>Carbon monoxide (CO) is a gas that has no odor or color. But it is very dangerous. It can cause sudden illness and death. CO is found in combustion fumes, such as those made by cars and trucks, lanterns, stoves, gas ranges and heating systems. CO from these fumes can build up in places that don't have a good flow of fresh air.  You can be poisoned by breathing them in.  The most common symptoms of CO poisoning are:</p><ul><li>	<span class="qt2">Headache</span></li><li>	Dizziness</li><li>	Weakness</li><li>	Nausea</li><li>	Vomiting</li><li>	<span class="qt0">Chest</span> <span class="qt2">pain</span></li><li>	Confusion</li></ul><p>It is often hard to tell if someone has CO poisoning, because the symptoms may be like those of other illnesses. People who are sleeping or intoxicated can die from CO poisoning before they have symptoms. A CO detector can warn you if you have high levels of CO in your home. </p><p>Centers for Disease Control and Prevention</p></content>

    <document rank="20" url="https://medlineplus.gov/childrenshealth.html">
      <content name="title">Children's Health</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Pediatrics</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>Your child's health includes physical, mental and social well-being. Most parents know the basics of keeping children healthy, like offering them healthy foods, making sure they get enough sleep and exercise and ensuring their safety. </p><p>It is also important for children to get regular checkups with their health care provider. These visits are a chance to check your child's development. They are also a good time to catch or prevent problems.</p><p>Other than checkups, school-age children should be seen for:</p><ul><li>Significant weight gain or loss</li><li>Sleep problems or change in behavior</li><li>Fever higher than 102</li><li>Rashes or skin infections</li><li>Frequent sore throats</li><li>Breathing problems</li></ul></content>
      <content name="mesh">Pediatrics</content>
      <content name="mesh">Child Health</content>
      <content name="mesh">Child Health Services</content>
      <content name="mesh">Child, Hospitalized</content>
      <content name="mesh">Child Welfare</content>
      <content name="groupName">Children and Teenagers</content>
      <content name="groupName">Population Groups</content>
      <content name="snippet"> Your child's health includes physical, mental and social well-being. Most parents know the basics of keeping children healthy, like offering them healthy  ... </content>
    </document>
    <document rank="21" url="https://medlineplus.gov/cholesterol.html">
      <content name="title">Cholesterol</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Familial Combined Hyperlipidemia</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Familial Dysbetalipoproteinemia</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Familial Hypercholesterolemia</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Hypercholesterolemia</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Hyperlipidemia</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Hyperlipoproteinemia</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is cholesterol?<p>Cholesterol is a waxy, fat-like substance that's found in all the cells in your body. Your body needs some cholesterol to make hormones, vitamin D, and substances that help you digest foods. Your body makes all the cholesterol it needs. Cholesterol is also found in foods from animal sources, such as egg yolks, meat, and cheese.</p><p>If you have too much cholesterol in your blood, it can combine with other substances in the blood to form plaque. Plaque sticks to the walls of your arteries. This buildup of plaque is known as atherosclerosis. It can lead to coronary artery disease, a condition in which your coronary arteries become narrow or even blocked.</p>What are HDL, LDL, and VLDL?<p>HDL, LDL, and VLDL are lipoproteins. They are a combination of fat (lipid) and protein. The lipids need to be attached to the proteins so they can move through the blood. Different types of lipoproteins have different purposes:</p><ul><li>HDL stands for high-density lipoprotein. It is sometimes called "good" cholesterol because it helps your body get rid of cholesterol.  It carries cholesterol from other parts of your body back to your liver. Your liver then removes the cholesterol from your body.</li><li>LDL stands for low-density lipoprotein. It is sometimes called "bad" cholesterol because a high LDL level leads to the buildup of plaque in your arteries.</li><li>VLDL stands for very low-density lipoprotein. Some people also call VLDL a "bad" cholesterol because it too contributes to the buildup of plaque in your arteries. But VLDL and LDL are different; VLDL mainly carries triglycerides and LDL mainly carries cholesterol.</li></ul>What causes high cholesterol?<p>The most common cause of high cholesterol is an unhealthy lifestyle. This can include:</p><ul><li>Unhealthy eating habits, such as eating lots of saturated fats. These fats are found in red meats, full-fat dairy products, chocolate, some baked goods, and many deep-fried and processed foods.  Eating too much saturated fat can raise your LDL (bad) cholesterol.</li><li>Lack of physical activity, with lots of sitting and little exercise. This lowers your HDL (good) cholesterol.</li><li>Smoking, which lowers HDL cholesterol, especially in women. It also raises your LDL cholesterol.</li><li>Stress, which may raise levels of certain hormones such as corticosteroids. These can cause your body to make more cholesterol.</li><li>Drinking too much alcohol, which can raise your total cholesterol level.</li></ul><p>Genetics may also cause people to have high cholesterol. For example, familial hypercholesterolemia (FH) is an inherited condition that causes very high levels of cholesterol in the blood. Other medical conditions and certain medicines may also raise LDL cholesterol levels or lower HDL cholesterol levels.</p>What can raise my risk of high cholesterol?<p>A variety of things can raise your risk of high cholesterol:</p><ul><li>Age. Your cholesterol levels tend to rise as you get older. Even though it is less common, younger people, including children and teens, can also have high cholesterol.</li><li>Sex. Between ages 20 and 39, men have a greater risk of high total cholesterol than women. But after menopause, a woman's risk goes up. This happens because menopause lowers levels of female hormones that may protect against high blood cholesterol.</li><li>Family history. High blood cholesterol can run in families.</li><li>Other health conditions. Conditions such as diabetes, chronic kidney disease, HIV, and lupus can raise your risk of high cholesterol.
</li><li>Medicines. Certain medicines can raise your level of LDL cholesterol or lower your level of HDL cholesterol, including:
  <ul><li>Steroids</li><li>Some chemotherapy medicines</li><li>Medicines taken after an organ transplant</li><li>Medicines for certain heart conditions</li><li>Certain acne medicines</li></ul>
</li><li>Race or ethnicity. People from certain racial or ethnic groups may have an increased risk of high cholesterol. For example, Asian Americans are more likely to have high levels of LDL cholesterol than other groups. And non-Hispanic White people are more likely than other groups to have high levels of total cholesterol.</li><li>Weight. Being overweight or having obesity raises your cholesterol level.</li></ul>What other health problems can high cholesterol cause?<p>Undiagnosed or untreated high blood cholesterol can lead to serious health problems:</p><ul><li>If you have large deposits of plaque in your arteries, an area of plaque can rupture (break open). This can cause a blood clot to form on the surface of the plaque. If the clot becomes large enough, it can mostly or completely block blood flow in a coronary artery.</li><li>If the flow of oxygen-rich blood to your heart muscle is reduced or blocked, it can cause angina (<span class="qt0">chest</span> <span class="qt2">pain</span>) or a heart attack.</li><li>Plaque also can build up in other arteries in your body, including the arteries that bring oxygen-rich blood to your brain and limbs. This can lead to problems such as carotid artery disease, stroke, and peripheral arterial disease.</li></ul>How is high cholesterol diagnosed?<p>There are usually no signs or symptoms that you have high cholesterol. A blood test can measure your cholesterol levels. When and how often you should get this test depends on your age, risk factors, and family history.  The general recommendations are:</p><p>For people who are age 19 or younger:</p><ul><li>The first test should be between ages 9 to 11</li><li>Children should have the test again every 5 years</li><li>Some children may have this test starting at age 2 if there is a family history of high blood cholesterol, heart attack, or stroke</li></ul><p>For people who are ages 20 to 65:</p><ul><li>Younger adults should have the test every 5 years</li><li>Men ages 45 to 65 and women ages 55 to 65 should have it every 1 to 2 years</li></ul><p>For people over age 65:</p><ul><li>They should be tested every year</li></ul>How can I lower my cholesterol?<p>You can lower your cholesterol through heart-healthy lifestyle changes. They include a heart-healthy eating plan, weight management, and regular physical activity.</p><p>If the lifestyle changes alone do not lower your cholesterol enough, you may also need to take medicines. There are several types of cholesterol-lowering medicines available, including statins. If you take medicines to lower your cholesterol, you still should continue with the lifestyle changes.</p><p>Some people with familial hypercholesterolemia (FH) may receive a treatment called lipoprotein apheresis. This treatment uses a filtering machine to remove LDL cholesterol from the blood. Then the machine returns the rest of the blood back to the person.</p><p>NIH: National Heart, Lung, and Blood Institute</p></content>

    <document rank="22" url="https://medlineplus.gov/coronaryarterybypasssurgery.html">
      <content name="title">Coronary Artery Bypass Surgery</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Bypass Surgery</content>
      <content name="altTitle">CABG</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Coronary Artery Bypass Graft</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is coronary artery bypass surgery?<p>Coronary artery bypass surgery creates a new path for blood to flow to your heart. This surgery is sometimes called a coronary artery bypass graft (CABG) or heart bypass. It's the most common heart surgery in adults. You may need this surgery if you have a blocked or partially blocked artery in your heart.</p><p>This surgery is often used to treat coronary artery disease (CAD). Your coronary arteries supply blood to your heart. If you have coronary artery disease, a sticky material called plaque builds up in your coronary arteries. This can cause them to harden and narrow. When this happens, some parts of your heart don't get enough blood.</p><p>Before recommending surgery, your health care provider may try other treatments. These could include lifestyle changes, medicines, or angioplasty, a procedure to open your arteries. If these treatments don't help, you may need coronary artery bypass surgery.</p>Who might benefit from coronary artery bypass surgery?<p>Your provider may recommend surgery if you have obstructive coronary artery disease to help lower your risk of a heart attack. They will consider your overall health before deciding if surgery is your best option.</p><p>You may benefit from surgery if you have:</p><ul><li>Coronary heart disease with angina (<span class="qt0">chest</span> <span class="qt2">pain</span>)</li><li>Diabetes</li><li>Multiple blocked coronary arteries</li><li>Serious heart failure</li></ul><p>You might need emergency coronary artery bypass surgery to treat a severe heart attack.</p>What happens during coronary artery bypass surgery?<p>During surgery, the surgeon takes healthy blood vessels from another part of your body. These blood vessels are attached to blood vessels above and below the blocked artery to let blood bypass (get around) the blockage. You might need more than one bypass if you have a few blocked arteries.</p><p>Methods to perform coronary artery bypass surgery include:</p><ul><li>Traditional CABG. The <span class="qt0">chest</span> is cut open to reach the heart. A machine pumps blood through your body during the surgery. This is the most common type of CABG surgery.</li><li>Off-pump CABG. The <span class="qt0">chest</span> is cut open to reach the heart. A machine for pumping blood is not used.</li><li>Minimally invasive CABG methods. Small cuts are made in the <span class="qt0">chest</span>. A machine for pumping blood is not used.</li><li>Robotic-assisted surgery. This is a type of minimally invasive surgery. The surgeon uses a computer to control tools on the arms of a robot. A machine for pumping blood may be used.</li></ul>Are there any risks from coronary artery bypass surgery?<p>Like all surgery, CABG has risks even though the results are often excellent. The risks can include:</p><ul><li>Arrhythmia</li><li>Bleeding</li><li>Heart attack</li><li>Infection</li><li>Kidney failure</li><li>Stroke</li></ul><p>After coronary artery bypass surgery, many people remain symptom-free for years. You may need surgery again if blockages form in the grafted arteries or veins. Or if blockages happen in arteries that weren't blocked before. Lifestyle changes and medicines may help stop your arteries from becoming clogged again.</p><p>NIH: National Heart, Lung, and Blood Institute</p></content>

    <document rank="23" url="https://medlineplus.gov/coronaryarterydisease.html">
      <content name="title">Coronary Artery Disease</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Arteriosclerosis, Coronary</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Atherosclerosis, Coronary</content>
      <content name="altTitle">CAD</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Coronary Arteriosclerosis</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Coronary atherosclerosis</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Coronary heart disease</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>Coronary artery disease (CAD) is the most common type of heart disease. It is the leading cause of death in the United States in both men and women.</p><p>CAD happens when the arteries that supply blood to heart muscle become hardened and narrowed. This is due to the buildup of cholesterol and other material, called plaque, on their inner walls. This buildup is called atherosclerosis. As it grows, less blood can flow through the arteries. As a result, the heart muscle can't get the blood or oxygen it needs. This can lead to <span class="qt0">chest</span> <span class="qt2">pain</span> (angina) or a heart attack.  Most heart attacks happen when a blood clot suddenly cuts off the hearts' blood supply, causing permanent heart damage.  </p><p>Over time, CAD can also weaken the heart muscle and contribute to heart failure and arrhythmias. Heart failure means the heart can't pump blood well to the rest of the body. Arrhythmias are changes in the normal beating rhythm of the heart.  </p><p>NIH: National Heart, Lung, and Blood Institute</p></content>
      <content name="mesh">Coronary Artery Disease</content>
      <content name="groupName">Older Adults</content>
      <content name="groupName">Blood, Heart and Circulation</content>

    <document rank="24" url="https://medlineplus.gov/endocarditis.html">
      <content name="title">Endocarditis</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is endocarditis?<p>Endocarditis is sometimes called infective endocarditis. It's a rare but life-threatening inflammation of the lining inside your heart's chambers and valves (the endocardium).</p><p>Endocarditis is usually caused by germs that get into your bloodstream and travel to your heart. Once they get inside your heart, the germs can attach to the lining or get trapped in the valves. They start to grow, causing an infection. If not treated quickly, the infection can cause damage to the heart and lead to serious health problems.</p>What causes endocarditis?<p>Bacterial infections cause most endocarditis. Normally, many bacteria live in your mouth, on your skin, or in other parts of the body. Sometimes the bacteria can get into your bloodstream from injuries such cuts or scrapes. Dental work and certain surgeries can also allow small amounts of bacteria to enter your bloodstream.</p><p>In some cases, fungal infections cause endocarditis. The fungi, such as yeast, can live in parts of your body. Fungal infections generally happen in people who have weakened immune systems that can't stop the fungus from growing. This includes people who have HIV.</p>Who is more likely to develop endocarditis?<p>In general, endocarditis is rare. If you're healthy, your immune system usually destroys the germs in your bloodstream before they can cause harm.</p><p>But your chance of developing endocarditis may be higher than most people if you:</p><ul><li>Have certain heart conditions. Damaged or abnormal heart tissue and devices in your heart can trap germs more easily than healthy heart tissue. That means your risk of endocarditis is higher if you have:
	<ul><li>Heart valve disease.</li><li>Certain congenital heart defects.</li><li>A pacemaker or an implantable defibrillator.</li><li>A heart valve replacement.</li></ul>
</li><li>Are older. Age-related changes to the heart valves, such as mitral valve prolapse or calcium deposits in the aortic valve, create places for germs to attach to the heart.</li><li>Inject illegal drugs. Unclean needles may carry bacteria into the bloodstream.</li><li>Have a condition that weakens your immune system.</li><li>Don't take care of your teeth and gums. Poor dental health makes it easier for germs to get into your bloodstream through your gums and mouth.</li><li>Have a long-term central venous line, a tube that stays in a large vein for weeks or months for medical treatment.</li><li>Have already had endocarditis. Endocarditis can damage heart tissue, which increases your risk of getting it again.</li></ul>What are the symptoms of endocarditis?<p>Endocarditis symptoms may be severe or very mild. They may start suddenly or slowly. And they can vary from person to person. The possible symptoms of endocarditis include:</p><ul><li>Fever and chills</li><li>New or worsening heart murmur (an unusual sound heard between heartbeats)</li><li><span class="qt0">Chest</span> <span class="qt2">pain</span></li><li>Cough</li><li>Muscle, joint, and back <span class="qt2">pain</span></li><li>Night sweats (heavy sweating during sleep)</li><li>Shortness of breath (feeling like you can't get enough air)</li><li>Skin changes, including:
	<ul><li>Broken blood vessels</li><li>Painful red or purple bumps</li><li><span class="qt2">Painless</span> flat red spots on the palms of your hands or soles of your feet</li></ul>
</li></ul>What other problems can endocarditis cause?<p>When the germs are in your heart, they can clump together with blood cells. These clumps can break off and travel through your bloodstream. They may block blood flow, spread infection, or damage your organs, including your brain, lungs, kidneys, and spleen.</p><p>Endocarditis may sometimes lead to sepsis, a medical emergency that happens when your body has an extreme response to the infection.</p><p>Endocarditis can also cause serious heart problems including:</p><ul><li>Heart valve damage</li><li>Heart failure</li><li>Arrhythmia (a problem with the rate or rhythm of your heartbeat)</li></ul>How is endocarditis diagnosed?<p>To find out if you have endocarditis, your health care provider will:</p><ul><li>Ask about your medical history, including your symptoms, recent illnesses, and other health conditions that affect your chance of developing endocarditis.</li><li>Do a physical exam.</li><li>Likely order tests, such as:
	<ul><li>Blood tests to check for signs of infection,  bacteria, or fungi in your blood.</li><li><span class="qt0">Chest</span> x-rays.</li><li>Heart tests.</li></ul>
</li></ul>What are the treatments for endocarditis?<p>If you have endocarditis, it's important to get treatment quickly. Treatments may include:</p><ul><li>Medicines:<ul><li>Antibiotics to treat bacterial infections. Antibiotics are usually started through an intravenous (IV) line in the hospital.</li><li>Antifungal medicine to treat fungal infections. Your provider may suggest taking antifungal medicine for the rest of your life to prevent the infection from coming back.</li></ul></li><li>Heart surgery may be needed to repair or replace damaged valves and heart tissue. Surgery may also be done to remove infected tissue.</li><li>Dental care, especially cleanings, can help reduce the amount of bacteria that grows in your mouth.</li></ul><p>Treatment may last weeks, and you may need tests to make sure it's working. Your provider will also check you for problems that could develop from endocarditis, such as heart failure or an irregular heartbeat.</p>Can endocarditis be prevented?<p>If you have a higher chance of developing endocarditis than most people, you can reduce your risk if you:</p><ul><li>Take good care of your teeth and gums every day</li><li>Have dental exams and cleaning at your dentist's office every 6 months</li><li>Make heart-healthy habits part of your daily life to help prevent heart disease</li><li>Call your health care provider right away if you have symptoms that could be endocarditis</li></ul><p>People with the highest risk for bacterial endocarditis need antibiotics before dental visits or certain medical or surgical procedures. Ask your provider if you're part of the highest risk group. If so, let all your providers know about your risk.</p><p>NIH: National Heart, Lung, and Blood Institute</p></content>

    <document rank="0" url="https://medlineplus.gov/backpain.html">
      <content name="title"><span class="qt0">Back</span> <span class="qt1">Pain</span></content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Low <span class="qt0">Back</span> <span class="qt1">Pain</span></content>
      <content name="altTitle">Lumbago</content>
      <content name="altTitle"><span class="qt1">Pain</span>, <span class="qt0">Back</span></content>
      <content name="altTitle"><span class="qt0"><span class="qt1">Backache</span></span></content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>If you've ever groaned, "Oh, my aching <span class="qt0">back</span>!", you are not alone. <span class="qt0">Back</span> <span class="qt1">pain</span> is one of the most common medical problems, affecting 8 out of 10 people at some point during their lives. <span class="qt0">Back</span> <span class="qt1">pain</span> can range from a dull, constant <span class="qt1">ache</span> to a sudden, sharp <span class="qt1">pain</span>. 
Acute <span class="qt0">back</span> <span class="qt1">pain</span> comes on suddenly and usually lasts from a few days to a few weeks. <span class="qt0">Back</span> <span class="qt1">pain</span> is called chronic if it lasts for more than three months.</p><p>Most <span class="qt0">back</span> <span class="qt1">pain</span> goes away on its own, though it may take awhile. Taking over-the-counter <span class="qt1">pain</span> relievers and resting can help. However, staying in bed for more than 1 or 2 days can make it worse. </p><p>If your <span class="qt0">back</span> <span class="qt1">pain</span> is severe or doesn't improve after three days, you should call your health care provider.  You should also get medical attention if you have <span class="qt0">back</span> <span class="qt1">pain</span> following an injury.</p><p>Treatment for <span class="qt0">back</span> <span class="qt1">pain</span> depends on what kind of <span class="qt1">pain</span> you have, and what is causing it. It may include hot or cold packs, exercise, medicines, injections, complementary treatments, and sometimes surgery.</p><p>NIH: National Institute of Arthritis and Musculoskeletal and Skin Diseases</p></content>

      <content name="snippet"> If you've ever groaned, "Oh, my aching <span class="qt0">back</span>!", you are not alone. <span class="qt0">Back</span> <span class="qt1">pain</span> is one of the most common medical problems, affecting 8 out of 10 people at some  ... </content>

    <document rank="1" url="https://medlineplus.gov/backinjuries.html">
      <content name="title"><span class="qt0">Back</span> Injuries</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>Your <span class="qt0">back</span> is made of bones, muscles, and other tissues extending from your neck to your pelvis. <span class="qt0">Back</span> injuries can result from sports injuries, work around the house or in the garden, or a sudden jolt such as a car accident. The lower <span class="qt0">back</span> is the most common site of <span class="qt0">back</span> injuries and <span class="qt0">back</span> <span class="qt1">pain</span>. Common <span class="qt0">back</span> injuries include :</p><ul><li>Sprains and strains</li><li>Herniated disks</li><li>Fractured (broken) vertebrae</li></ul><p>These injuries can cause <span class="qt1">pain</span> and limit your movement. Treatments vary but might include medicines, icing, bed rest, physical therapy, or surgery. You might be able to prevent some <span class="qt0">back</span> injuries by maintaining a healthy weight, lifting objects with your legs, and using lower-<span class="qt0">back</span> support when you sit. </p></content>

    <document rank="2" url="https://medlineplus.gov/nondrugpainmanagement.html">
      <content name="title">Non-Drug <span class="qt1">Pain</span> Management</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is <span class="qt1">pain</span>?<p><span class="qt1">Pain</span> is a signal in your nervous system that something may be wrong. It is an unpleasant feeling, such as a prick, tingle, sting, burn, or <span class="qt1">ache</span>. Each person feels <span class="qt1">pain</span> differently, even if the reason for the <span class="qt1">pain</span> is the same. <span class="qt1">Pain</span> may be sharp or dull. It may come and go, or it may be constant. You may feel <span class="qt1">pain</span> in one area of your body, such as your <span class="qt0">back</span>, abdomen, chest, pelvis, or you may feel <span class="qt1">pain</span> all over.</p><p>There are two types of <span class="qt1">pain</span>:</p><ul><li>Acute <span class="qt1">pain</span> usually comes on suddenly, because of a disease, injury, or inflammation (irritation, redness, and/or swelling). It usually goes away when the cause is treated or healed, though sometimes it can turn into chronic <span class="qt1">pain</span>.</li><li>Chronic <span class="qt1">pain</span> lasts for longer than three months or the time in which you should have healed. It can cause severe problems.</li></ul>What are <span class="qt1">pain</span> relievers?<p><span class="qt1">Pain</span> relievers are medicines that reduce or relieve <span class="qt1">pain</span>. There are many <span class="qt1">pain</span> medicines, each with advantages and risks. Some are over-the-counter (OTC) medicines. Others are stronger medicines, which are available by prescription. The most powerful prescription <span class="qt1">pain</span> relievers are opioids. They are very effective, but people who take them are at risk of addiction and overdose.</p><p>Because of the side effects and risks of <span class="qt1">pain</span> relievers, you may want to try non-drug treatments first. If you need to take medicines, doing some non-drug treatments may allow you to take a lower dose.</p>What are some non-drug treatments for <span class="qt1">pain</span>?<p>Many non-drug treatments can help with <span class="qt1">pain</span>. It is important to check with your health care provider before trying any of them:</p><ul><li>Acupuncture involves stimulating acupuncture points. These are specific points on your body. There are different acupuncture methods. The most common one involves inserting thin needles through the skin. Others include using pressure, electrical stimulation, and heat. Acupuncture is based on the belief that qi (vital energy) flows through the body along paths, called meridians. Practitioners believe that triggering the acupuncture points can rebalance the qi. Research suggests that acupuncture can help manage certain <span class="qt1">pain</span> conditions.</li><li>Biofeedback techniques use electronic devices to measure body functions such as breathing and heart rate. This teaches you to be more aware of your body functions so you can learn to control them. For example, a biofeedback device may show you measurements of your muscle tension. By watching how these measurements change, you can become more aware of when your muscles are tense and learn to relax them. Biofeedback may help to control <span class="qt1">pain</span>, including chronic headaches and <span class="qt0">back</span> <span class="qt1">pain</span>.</li><li>Electrical stimulation involves using a device to send a gentle electric current to your nerves or muscles. This can help treat <span class="qt1">pain</span> by changing or blocking <span class="qt1">pain</span> signals. Types include:
	<ul><li>Transcutaneous electrical stimulation (TENS)</li><li>Implanted electric nerve stimulation</li><li>Deep brain or spinal cord stimulation</li></ul>
</li><li>Massage therapy is a treatment in which the soft tissues of the body are kneaded, rubbed, tapped, and stroked. Among other benefits, it may help people relax, and relieve stress and <span class="qt1">pain</span>.</li><li>Meditation is a mind-body practice in which you focus your attention on something, such as an object, word, phrase, or breathing. This helps you to minimize distracting or stressful thoughts or feelings.</li><li>Physical therapy uses techniques such as heat, cold, exercise, massage, and manipulation. It can help to control <span class="qt1">pain</span>, condition muscles and restore strength.</li><li>Psychotherapy (talk therapy) uses methods such as discussion, listening, and counseling to treat mental and behavioral disorders. It can also help people who have <span class="qt1">pain</span>, especially chronic <span class="qt1">pain</span>, by:
	<ul><li>Teaching them coping skills to better deal with the stress that <span class="qt1">pain</span> can cause</li><li>Addressing negative thoughts and emotions that can make <span class="qt1">pain</span> worse</li><li>Providing them with support</li></ul>
</li><li>Relaxation therapy can help reduce muscle tension and stress, lower blood pressure, and control <span class="qt1">pain</span>. It may involve tensing and relaxing muscles throughout the body. It may be used with guided imagery (focusing the mind on positive images) and meditation.</li><li>Surgery can sometimes be necessary to treat severe <span class="qt1">pain</span>, especially when <span class="qt0">back</span> problems or serious musculoskeletal injuries cause it. There are always risks to getting surgery, and it does not always work to treat <span class="qt1">pain</span>. It is important to go through all the risks and benefits with your provider.</li></ul></content>
      <content name="mesh"><span class="qt1">Pain</span> Management</content>
      <content name="groupName">Complementary and Alternative Therapies</content>

    <document rank="3" url="https://medlineplus.gov/herniateddisk.html">
      <content name="title">Herniated Disk</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Ruptured Disk</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Slipped Disk</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Bulging disk</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Compressed disk</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Herniated intervertebral disk</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Herniated nucleus pulposus</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Prolapsed disk</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>Your backbone, or spine, is made up of 26 bones called vertebrae. In between them are soft disks filled with a jelly-like substance. These disks cushion the vertebrae and keep them in place. As you age, the disks break down or degenerate. As they do, they lose their cushioning ability. This can lead to <span class="qt1">pain</span> if the <span class="qt0">back</span> is stressed.</p><p>A herniated disk is a disk that ruptures. This allows the jelly-like center of the disk to leak, irritating the nearby nerves. This can cause sciatica or <span class="qt0">back</span> <span class="qt1">pain</span>.</p><p>Your doctor will diagnose a herniated disk with a physical exam and, sometimes, imaging tests. With treatment, most people recover. Treatments include rest, <span class="qt1">pain</span> and anti-inflammatory medicines, physical therapy, and sometimes surgery.</p><p>NIH: National Institute of Arthritis and Musculoskeletal and Skin Diseases</p></content>

Your doctor will diagnose a herniated disk with  ... </content>

    <document rank="4" url="https://medlineplus.gov/guidetogoodposture.html">
      <content name="title">Guide to Good Posture</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Posture</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>Good posture is about more than standing up straight so you can look your best. It is an important part of your long-term health. Making sure that you hold your body the right way, whether you are moving or still, can prevent <span class="qt1">pain</span>, injuries, and other health problems.</p>What is posture?<p>Posture is how you hold your body. There are two types:</p><ul><li>Dynamic posture is how you hold yourself when you are moving, like when you are walking, running, or bending over to pick up something.</li><li>Static posture is how you hold yourself when you are not moving, like when you are sitting, standing, or sleeping.</li></ul><p>It is important to make sure that you have good dynamic and static posture.</p><p>The key to good posture is the position of your spine. Your spine has three natural curves - at your neck, mid <span class="qt0">back</span>, and low <span class="qt0">back</span>. Correct posture should maintain these curves, but not increase them. Your head should be above your shoulders, and the top of your shoulder should be over the hips.</p>How can posture affect my health?<p>Poor posture can be bad for your health. Slouching or slumping over can:</p><ul><li>Misalign your musculoskeletal system</li><li>Wear away at your spine, making it more fragile and prone to injury</li><li>Cause neck, shoulder, and <span class="qt0">back</span> <span class="qt1">pain</span></li><li>Decrease your flexibility</li><li>Affect how well your joints move</li><li>Affect your balance and increase your risk of falling</li><li>Make it harder to digest your food</li><li>Make it harder to breathe</li></ul>How can I improve my posture in general?<p>
</p><ul><li>Be mindful of your posture during everyday activities, like watching television, washing dishes, or walking</li><li>Stay active. Any kind of exercise may help improve your posture, but certain types of exercises can be especially helpful. They include yoga, tai chi, and other classes that focus on body awareness. It is also a good idea to do exercises that strengthen your core (muscles around your <span class="qt0">back</span>, abdomen, and pelvis).</li><li>Maintain a healthy weight. Extra weight can weaken your abdominal muscles, cause problems for your pelvis and spine, and contribute to low <span class="qt0">back</span> <span class="qt1">pain</span>. All of these can hurt your posture.</li><li>Wear comfortable, low-heeled shoes. High heels, for example, can throw off your balance and force you to walk differently. This puts more stress on your muscles and harms your posture.</li><li>Make sure work surfaces are at a comfortable height for you, whether you're sitting in front of a computer, making dinner, or eating a meal.</li></ul>How can I improve my posture when sitting?<p>Many Americans spend a lot of their time sitting - either at work, at school, or at home. It is important to sit properly, and to take frequent breaks:</p><ul><li>Switch sitting positions often</li><li>Take brief walks around your office or home</li><li>Gently stretch your muscles every so often to help relieve muscle tension</li><li>Don't cross your legs; keep your feet on the floor, with your ankles in front of your knees</li><li>Make sure that your feet touch the floor, or if that's not possible, use a footrest</li><li>Relax your shoulders; they should not be rounded or pulled backwards</li><li>Keep your elbows in close to your body. They should be bent between 90 and 120 degrees.</li><li>Make sure that your <span class="qt0">back</span> is fully supported. Use a <span class="qt0">back</span> pillow or other <span class="qt0">back</span> support if your chair does not have a backrest that can support your lower <span class="qt0">back</span>'s curve.</li><li>Make sure that your thighs and hips are supported. You should have a well-padded seat, and your thighs and hips should be parallel to the floor.</li></ul>How can I improve my posture when standing?<p>
</p><ul><li>Stand up straight and tall</li><li>Keep your shoulders <span class="qt0">back</span></li><li>Pull your stomach in</li><li>Put your weight mostly on the balls of your feet</li><li>Keep your head level</li><li>Let your arms hang down naturally at your sides</li><li>Keep your feet about shoulder-width apart</li></ul><p>With practice, you can improve your posture; you will look and feel better.</p></content>

    <document rank="5" url="https://medlineplus.gov/chiropractic.html">
      <content name="title">Chiropractic</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>Chiropractic is a health care profession. Chiropractors perform adjustments (manipulations) to the spine or other parts of the body. The goal is to correct alignment problems, ease <span class="qt1">pain</span>, and support the body's natural ability to heal itself. They may also use other treatments including :</p><ul><li>Heat and ice</li><li>Electrical stimulation</li><li>Relaxation techniques</li><li>Rehabilitative and general exercise</li><li>Counseling about diet, weight loss, and other lifestyle factors</li><li>Dietary supplements</li></ul><p>Many people visit chiropractors for treatment of low <span class="qt0">back</span> <span class="qt1">pain</span>, neck <span class="qt1">pain</span>, and headaches.</p><p>NIH: National Center for Complementary and Integrative Health</p></content>

NIH: National Center  ... </content>

    <document rank="6" url="https://medlineplus.gov/healthproblemsinpregnancy.html">
      <content name="title">Health Problems in Pregnancy</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">High Risk Pregnancy</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Pregnancy Complications</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Pregnancy and Health Problems</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Pregnancy, High-Risk</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What are health problems in pregnancy?<p>A health problem in pregnancy is any disease or condition that could affect your health or the health of your fetus. Some health problems may make it more likely that you will have a high-risk pregnancy. A high-risk pregnancy is one in which you, your fetus, or both are at higher risk for health problems than in a typical pregnancy.</p><p>But just because you have health problems, it doesn't mean that you, or your fetus, will have a problem during the pregnancy. Taking care of yourself and getting early and regular prenatal care from a health care provider may help you reduce pregnancy risks from health problems.</p>What raises my risk for health problems during pregnancy?<p>Every pregnancy has some risk of problems, but your lifestyle, as well as factors like certain conditions and health issues, can raise that risk, such as:</p><ul><li>A health condition you had before you got pregnant</li><li>A health condition you develop during pregnancy</li><li>A pregnancy with more than one baby</li><li>A health problem that happened during a previous pregnancy and could happen again</li><li>Substance use during pregnancy</li><li>Being over age 35</li></ul><p>Any of these can affect your health, the health of your fetus, or both.</p>Can chronic health conditions cause problems in pregnancy?<p>Every pregnancy is different. If you have specific risks in one pregnancy, it doesn't mean that you will have them in another. But, if you have a chronic condition, you should talk to your provider about how to minimize your risk before you get pregnant. Once you are pregnant, you may need a health care team to monitor your pregnancy. Some common conditions that can complicate a pregnancy include:</p><ul><li>High blood pressure</li><li>Polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS)</li><li>Kidney problems</li><li>Autoimmune disorders</li><li>Thyroid disease</li><li>Obesity</li><li>HIV</li><li>Cancer</li><li>Infections</li></ul><p>Other conditions that may make pregnancy risky can develop during pregnancy - for example, gestational diabetes and Rh incompatibility.</p>Can health problems in pregnancy be prevented?<p>You may be able to lower your risk of certain health problems by making healthy lifestyle changes before you get pregnant. These can include reaching a healthy weight, not smoking, and managing any health conditions.</p><p>However, health problems during pregnancy are not always preventable. Some chronic conditions can be treated and controlled, while others aren't treatable and carry a higher risk than usual, even if the health problem is well-managed.</p><p>Share your symptoms with your provider for early detection and treatment of health problems. Sometimes, it's hard to know what's normal. Some symptoms, like nausea, <span class="qt0">back</span> <span class="qt1">pain</span>, and fatigue, are common during pregnancy, while other symptoms, like vaginal bleeding or a severe or long-lasting <span class="qt1">headache</span>, can be signs of a problem. Call your provider to let them know if something is bothering or worrying you.</p></content>
      <content name="mesh">Pregnancy, High-Risk</content>
      <content name="mesh">Pregnancy Complications</content>
      <content name="groupName">Pregnancy and Reproduction</content>
      <content name="groupName">Female Reproductive System</content>

    <document rank="7" url="https://medlineplus.gov/chronicpain.html">
      <content name="title">Chronic <span class="qt1">Pain</span></content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle"><span class="qt1">Pain</span>, Chronic</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is chronic <span class="qt1">pain</span>?<p><span class="qt1">Pain</span> is a signal from your nervous system that something may be wrong. It is an unpleasant feeling, such as a prick, tingle, sting, burn, or <span class="qt1">ache. Pain</span> may be sharp or dull. You may feel <span class="qt1">pain</span> in one area of your body or all over. Each person feels <span class="qt1">pain</span> differently, even if the reason for the <span class="qt1">pain</span> is the same.</p><p><span class="qt1">Pain</span> might be acute or chronic. The type of <span class="qt1">pain</span> is based on how long and how often you have <span class="qt1">pain</span>. Acute <span class="qt1">pain</span> starts suddenly and goes away when the cause is treated or healed. This <span class="qt1">pain</span> lets you know that you may be injured or have a problem you need to take care of. Chronic <span class="qt1">pain</span> lasts longer than three months or the time in which you should have healed.</p><p>If you have chronic <span class="qt1">pain</span>, it can occur most days or every day and may last for weeks, months, or even years. It can affect all aspects of daily life, including your mood and relationships. Treatment may not get rid of chronic <span class="qt1">pain</span>, but it can help with your symptoms.</p>What causes chronic <span class="qt1">pain</span>?<p>Sometimes, acute <span class="qt1">pain</span> can become chronic <span class="qt1">pain</span>. The original cause may have been an injury or infection, or you may have an ongoing cause of <span class="qt1">pain</span>, such as arthritis or cancer. In some cases, there is no clear cause. Environmental factors and psychological factors such as mood and stress can make chronic <span class="qt1">pain</span> worse.</p>What are the symptoms of chronic <span class="qt1">pain</span>?<p>Since people feel <span class="qt1">pain</span> in different ways, two people could have the same issue, but each may have different symptoms. Chronic <span class="qt1">pain</span> can occur anywhere in your body and may cause other symptoms such as fatigue, mood changes, or difficulty sleeping. If you have depression or stress, it may make chronic <span class="qt1">pain</span> worse.</p>Who is more likely to get chronic <span class="qt1">pain</span>?<p>Many older adults have chronic <span class="qt1">pain</span>. Women also report having more chronic <span class="qt1">pain</span> than men, and they are at a greater risk for many <span class="qt1">pain</span> conditions. Some people have two or more chronic <span class="qt1">pain</span> conditions. You may be more likely to have chronic <span class="qt1">pain</span> if you have certain medical conditions such as:</p><ul><li>Headaches or migraines</li><li>Cancer</li><li>Fibromyalgia</li><li>Arthritis</li><li>Nerve damage</li><li><span class="qt0">Back</span> problems</li></ul>How is chronic <span class="qt1">pain</span> diagnosed?<p>Chronic <span class="qt1">pain</span> lasts three months or longer or when <span class="qt1">pain</span> continues after your body has healed. If the cause of your <span class="qt1">pain</span> is unknown, your health care provider may:</p><ul><li>Ask you about your medical history</li><li>Ask you to describe the <span class="qt1">pain</span> and how it affects your life</li><li>Do a physical exam</li><li>Order blood tests or other medical tests</li></ul>What are the treatments for chronic <span class="qt1">pain</span>?<p>Chronic <span class="qt1">pain</span> is not always curable, but treatments can help. Treatments may include medicines, including <span class="qt1">pain</span> relievers. There are also non-drug treatments, such as acupuncture, physical therapy, and sometimes surgery.</p><p>Depending on the cause of your <span class="qt1">pain</span> and your symptoms, your provider may also recommend lifestyle changes which may include suggestions for:</p><ul><li>Improving mental health</li><li>Managing stress</li><li>Getting to and staying at a healthy weight</li><li>Adding low-impact exercise</li></ul></content>
      <content name="mesh">Chronic <span class="qt1">Pain</span></content>
      <content name="groupName">Brain and Nerves</content>

Chronic <span class="qt1">pain</span>  ... </content>

    <document rank="8" url="https://medlineplus.gov/ankylosingspondylitis.html">
      <content name="title">Ankylosing Spondylitis</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Rheumatoid Spondylitis</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Spondylitis, Ankylosing</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is ankylosing spondylitis?<p>Ankylosing spondylitis (AS) is a type of arthritis of the spine. It causes inflammation (swelling) between your vertebrae (the bones that make up your spine) and the joints between your spine and pelvis. AS inflammation can cause stiffness and make it difficult to move and bend.</p><p>Symptoms of AS can range from mild to severe <span class="qt0">back</span> <span class="qt1">pain</span>. Over time, AS can fuse (grow together) your vertebrae, limiting movement and causing a hunched posture. In some people, AS can affect other joints or body parts.</p><p>There is no cure for AS, but early treatment can help manage symptoms, slow down the disease, and help you lead a more productive life.</p>Who is more likely to get ankylosing spondylitis?<p>Ankylosing spondylitis usually starts before age 45. Early symptoms may begin between the ages of 15 and 30. Your chance of developing AS can also increase if:</p><ul><li>You're a man. AS is more common and severe in men.</li><li>You have a family history. If a member of your family has AS, you're more likely to get the disease.</li><li>You have other medical conditions that may increase your risk of getting AS. These include Crohn's disease, ulcerative colitis, or psoriasis.</li></ul>What causes ankylosing spondylitis?<p>No one knows what causes ankylosing spondylitis, but the environment and genes likely play a role. Your genes are parts of DNA in your cells that are passed down from your parents. Certain genes may increase your risk of getting the disease, but not everyone with these genes gets AS.</p>What are the symptoms of ankylosing spondylitis?<p>AS affects people differently. The most common symptom is <span class="qt1">pain</span> and stiffness in the lower <span class="qt0">back</span> and/or hips. The <span class="qt1">pain</span> is usually worse during the night or after sitting for a long time.</p><p>Over time, AS may progress to other areas of your spine or body. Symptoms may depend on which areas of your body the disease affects. Some people have symptoms that come and go. Others may have severe, ongoing <span class="qt1">pain</span>.</p><p>Other symptoms of ankylosing spondylitis may include:</p><ul><li><span class="qt1">Pain</span>, stiffness, and inflammation of the joints.</li><li>Difficulty taking a deep breath. This could occur if the joints connecting the ribs are affected.</li><li>Changes in vision.</li><li>Fatigue.</li><li>Loss of appetite.</li><li>Weight loss.</li><li>Skin rashes, such as psoriasis.</li><li>Abdominal (belly) <span class="qt1">pain</span> and loose stools (poop).</li></ul>How is ankylosing spondylitis diagnosed?<p>There is no single test for ankylosing spondylitis. To find out if you have AS your health care provider may:</p><ul><li>Ask about your medical history, including your symptoms.</li><li>Ask about your family health history, including relatives who have had AS.</li><li>Do a physical exam.</li><li>Order blood tests or imaging studies.</li></ul>What are the treatments for ankylosing spondylitis?<p>AS has no cure, but treatment may help relieve symptoms, maintain posture, and slow down the disease. Since other parts of your body can be affected by AS, you will likely work with a team of health care professionals for tests, diagnosis, and care.</p><p>Treatment usually includes medicine to help relieve symptoms and keep the disease from getting worse, as well as physical therapy to improve mobility. If AS is severe, surgery may be needed.</p><p>You can help manage your AS symptoms if you:</p><ul><li>Exercise</li><li>Monitor your symptoms</li><li>Manage your stress</li><li>Use assistive devices as needed</li><li>Follow a healthy diet</li><li>Stop smoking, or don't start</li></ul><p>NIH: National Institute of Arthritis and Musculoskeletal and Skin Disease</p></content>
      <content name="mesh">Spondylitis, Ankylosing</content>
      <content name="groupName">Bones, Joints and Muscles</content>

    <document rank="9" url="https://medlineplus.gov/uterinefibroids.html">
      <content name="title">Uterine Fibroids</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Fibroids</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Uterine Leiomyomata</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What are uterine fibroids?<p>Uterine fibroids are the most common noncancerous (benign) tumors in women of childbearing age. You may have uterine fibroids and not even know it since they often don't cause any symptoms. Fibroids are made of muscle cells and other tissues that grow in and around the wall of the uterus, or womb. They can vary in number and size and grow as one tumor or in clusters.</p><p>Many women with fibroids can get pregnant with no problems. However, some uterine fibroids could cause infertility (not being able to get pregnant), pregnancy loss, or problems with fetal development or delivery.</p>Who is more likely to get uterine fibroids?<p>If you're a female and the age when you're usually able to get pregnant and give birth, you could develop fibroids. Some factors may increase your risk of having uterine fibroids. These can include:</p><ul><li>Age. Women who are older have a higher risk.</li><li>Race/ethnicity. Black/African American women often have fibroids at a younger age. They're also more likely to have larger fibroids with worse symptoms.</li><li>Family history. If your mother or sister had uterine fibroids.</li><li>Other factors. If you started your period before age 10 or have never been pregnant.</li><li>Certain medical conditions. These include obesity or vitamin D deficiency.</li></ul><p>Your risk of getting uterine fibroids lowers with each pregnancy and with long-term use of birth control pills or shots.</p>What causes uterine fibroids?<p>No one knows what causes uterine fibroids. A few things that might play a part in getting uterine fibroids include genetics and growth hormones. Also, other hormones like estrogen and progesterone seem to help fibroids grow. These hormones cause the tissue lining inside the uterus to thicken during each menstrual cycle to prepare for pregnancy.</p>What are the symptoms of uterine fibroids?<p>Many women with fibroids have no symptoms. If you do, the type of symptoms may be due to location, size, and number of fibroids. Symptoms may include:</p><ul><li>Heavy or painful periods or bleeding between periods</li><li>Feeling "full" in the lower abdomen (belly)</li><li>Urinating (peeing) often</li><li><span class="qt1">Pain</span> during sex</li><li>Lower <span class="qt0">back</span> <span class="qt1">pain</span></li><li>Problems getting pregnant, multiple miscarriages, or early labor while pregnant</li></ul>How are uterine fibroids diagnosed?<p>You may not know that you have uterine fibroids unless you have symptoms. Or your health care provider may find the fibroids during a pelvic exam, or with imaging tests.</p><p>Uterine fibroids might be very small, or in extreme cases, they may grow large enough to make it appear like a pregnancy.</p>What are the treatments for uterine fibroids?<p>If you have no symptoms, you may not even need treatment. If you have symptoms, the treatment for uterine fibroids depends on many factors. These can include:</p><ul><li>Your symptoms.</li><li>Your age. Sometimes fibroids shrink during and after menopause due to a drop in hormone levels.</li><li>The location, type, and size of fibroids.</li><li>If you are pregnant or want to get pregnant in the future.</li></ul><p>Treatment may include medicines that can slow or stop fibroid growth. Surgery, such as a hysterectomy (removal of the uterus), may be considered if you are near or past menopause, your fibroids are very large or cause heavy bleeding.</p><p>If you are having trouble getting pregnant, your provider may discuss infertility treatments. They may also recommend an emotional support group if you have chronic symptoms or difficulties getting pregnant or maintaining a pregnancy.</p><p>NIH: National Institute of Child Health and Human Development</p></content>

    <document rank="10" url="https://medlineplus.gov/bladdercancer.html">
      <content name="title">Bladder Cancer</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Urinary Bladder Cancer</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>The bladder is a hollow organ in your lower abdomen that stores urine. Bladder cancer occurs in the lining of the bladder. It is the sixth most common type of cancer in the United States.</p><p>Symptoms include:</p><ul><li>Blood in your urine</li><li>A frequent urge to urinate</li><li><span class="qt1">Pain</span> when you urinate</li><li>Low <span class="qt0">back</span> <span class="qt1">pain</span></li></ul><p>Risk factors for developing bladder cancer include smoking and exposure to certain chemicals in the workplace.  People with a family history of bladder cancer or who are older, White, or male have a higher risk.</p><p>Treatments for bladder cancer include surgery, radiation therapy, chemotherapy, and biologic therapy. Biologic therapy boosts your body's own ability to fight cancer.</p><p>NIH: National Cancer Institute</p></content>
      <content name="mesh">Urinary Bladder Neoplasms</content>
      <content name="groupName">Cancers</content>
      <content name="groupName">Kidneys and Urinary System</content>
      <content name="snippet"> ... in your urine  A frequent urge to urinate  <span class="qt1">Pain</span> when you urinate  Low <span class="qt0">back</span> <span class="qt1">pain</span>  Risk factors for developing bladder cancer include smoking  ... </content>
    </document>
    <document rank="11" url="https://medlineplus.gov/endocarditis.html">
      <content name="title">Endocarditis</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is endocarditis?<p>Endocarditis is sometimes called infective endocarditis. It's a rare but life-threatening inflammation of the lining inside your heart's chambers and valves (the endocardium).</p><p>Endocarditis is usually caused by germs that get into your bloodstream and travel to your heart. Once they get inside your heart, the germs can attach to the lining or get trapped in the valves. They start to grow, causing an infection. If not treated quickly, the infection can cause damage to the heart and lead to serious health problems.</p>What causes endocarditis?<p>Bacterial infections cause most endocarditis. Normally, many bacteria live in your mouth, on your skin, or in other parts of the body. Sometimes the bacteria can get into your bloodstream from injuries such cuts or scrapes. Dental work and certain surgeries can also allow small amounts of bacteria to enter your bloodstream.</p><p>In some cases, fungal infections cause endocarditis. The fungi, such as yeast, can live in parts of your body. Fungal infections generally happen in people who have weakened immune systems that can't stop the fungus from growing. This includes people who have HIV.</p>Who is more likely to develop endocarditis?<p>In general, endocarditis is rare. If you're healthy, your immune system usually destroys the germs in your bloodstream before they can cause harm.</p><p>But your chance of developing endocarditis may be higher than most people if you:</p><ul><li>Have certain heart conditions. Damaged or abnormal heart tissue and devices in your heart can trap germs more easily than healthy heart tissue. That means your risk of endocarditis is higher if you have:
	<ul><li>Heart valve disease.</li><li>Certain congenital heart defects.</li><li>A pacemaker or an implantable defibrillator.</li><li>A heart valve replacement.</li></ul>
</li><li>Are older. Age-related changes to the heart valves, such as mitral valve prolapse or calcium deposits in the aortic valve, create places for germs to attach to the heart.</li><li>Inject illegal drugs. Unclean needles may carry bacteria into the bloodstream.</li><li>Have a condition that weakens your immune system.</li><li>Don't take care of your teeth and gums. Poor dental health makes it easier for germs to get into your bloodstream through your gums and mouth.</li><li>Have a long-term central venous line, a tube that stays in a large vein for weeks or months for medical treatment.</li><li>Have already had endocarditis. Endocarditis can damage heart tissue, which increases your risk of getting it again.</li></ul>What are the symptoms of endocarditis?<p>Endocarditis symptoms may be severe or very mild. They may start suddenly or slowly. And they can vary from person to person. The possible symptoms of endocarditis include:</p><ul><li>Fever and chills</li><li>New or worsening heart murmur (an unusual sound heard between heartbeats)</li><li>Chest <span class="qt1">pain</span></li><li>Cough</li><li>Muscle, joint, and <span class="qt0">back</span> <span class="qt1">pain</span></li><li>Night sweats (heavy sweating during sleep)</li><li>Shortness of breath (feeling like you can't get enough air)</li><li>Skin changes, including:
	<ul><li>Broken blood vessels</li><li>Painful red or purple bumps</li><li><span class="qt1">Painless</span> flat red spots on the palms of your hands or soles of your feet</li></ul>
</li></ul>What other problems can endocarditis cause?<p>When the germs are in your heart, they can clump together with blood cells. These clumps can break off and travel through your bloodstream. They may block blood flow, spread infection, or damage your organs, including your brain, lungs, kidneys, and spleen.</p><p>Endocarditis may sometimes lead to sepsis, a medical emergency that happens when your body has an extreme response to the infection.</p><p>Endocarditis can also cause serious heart problems including:</p><ul><li>Heart valve damage</li><li>Heart failure</li><li>Arrhythmia (a problem with the rate or rhythm of your heartbeat)</li></ul>How is endocarditis diagnosed?<p>To find out if you have endocarditis, your health care provider will:</p><ul><li>Ask about your medical history, including your symptoms, recent illnesses, and other health conditions that affect your chance of developing endocarditis.</li><li>Do a physical exam.</li><li>Likely order tests, such as:
	<ul><li>Blood tests to check for signs of infection,  bacteria, or fungi in your blood.</li><li>Chest x-rays.</li><li>Heart tests.</li></ul>
</li></ul>What are the treatments for endocarditis?<p>If you have endocarditis, it's important to get treatment quickly. Treatments may include:</p><ul><li>Medicines:<ul><li>Antibiotics to treat bacterial infections. Antibiotics are usually started through an intravenous (IV) line in the hospital.</li><li>Antifungal medicine to treat fungal infections. Your provider may suggest taking antifungal medicine for the rest of your life to prevent the infection from coming <span class="qt0">back</span>.</li></ul></li><li>Heart surgery may be needed to repair or replace damaged valves and heart tissue. Surgery may also be done to remove infected tissue.</li><li>Dental care, especially cleanings, can help reduce the amount of bacteria that grows in your mouth.</li></ul><p>Treatment may last weeks, and you may need tests to make sure it's working. Your provider will also check you for problems that could develop from endocarditis, such as heart failure or an irregular heartbeat.</p>Can endocarditis be prevented?<p>If you have a higher chance of developing endocarditis than most people, you can reduce your risk if you:</p><ul><li>Take good care of your teeth and gums every day</li><li>Have dental exams and cleaning at your dentist's office every 6 months</li><li>Make heart-healthy habits part of your daily life to help prevent heart disease</li><li>Call your health care provider right away if you have symptoms that could be endocarditis</li></ul><p>People with the highest risk for bacterial endocarditis need antibiotics before dental visits or certain medical or surgical procedures. Ask your provider if you're part of the highest risk group. If so, let all your providers know about your risk.</p><p>NIH: National Heart, Lung, and Blood Institute</p></content>

    <document rank="12" url="https://medlineplus.gov/painrelievers.html">
      <content name="title"><span class="qt1">Pain</span> Relievers</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle"><span class="qt1">Analgesics</span></content>
      <content name="altTitle">Methadone</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Narcotics</content>
      <content name="altTitle"><span class="qt1">Painkillers</span></content>
      <content name="altTitle"><span class="qt1">Pain</span> medicines</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What are <span class="qt1">pain</span> relievers?<p><span class="qt1">Pain</span> relievers are medicines that reduce or relieve headaches, sore muscles, arthritis, or other aches and <span class="qt1">pains</span>.  There are many different <span class="qt1">pain</span> medicines, and each has advantages and risks. Some types of <span class="qt1">pain</span> respond better to certain medicines than others. Each person may also have a slightly different response to a <span class="qt1">pain</span> reliever.</p>What are the types of over-the-counter <span class="qt1">pain</span> relievers?<p>Over-the-counter (OTC) medicines are good for many types of <span class="qt1">pain</span>. Two types of OTC <span class="qt1">pain</span> medicines are usually recommended for mild to moderate <span class="qt1">pain</span>.</p><ul><li>Acetaminophen (Tylenol) is used in many OTC and prescription medicines. It's often considered safer than other <span class="qt1">pain</span> relievers. Unfortunately, it's common for people to take too much accidentally. Be careful not to take too much each day or use more than one medicine with acetaminophen. Check with your health care provider before taking medicines containing acetaminophen for more than ten days or five days for your child.</li><li>Nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) include aspirin, naproxen (Aleve), and ibuprofen (Advil, Motrin). These <span class="qt1">pain</span> relievers are often most effective if you have <span class="qt1">pain</span> and inflammation (swelling), such as for arthritis or menstrual <span class="qt1">cramps</span>. If you take more than the recommended amount, NSAIDs may cause nausea, stomach <span class="qt1">pain</span>, or ulcers.</li></ul>What are prescription <span class="qt1">pain</span> relievers?<p>If OTC <span class="qt1">pain</span> relievers don't relieve your <span class="qt1">pain</span>, your provider may prescribe something stronger. Many NSAIDs are also available at higher prescription doses.</p><p>The most powerful <span class="qt1">pain</span> relievers are opioids, sometimes called narcotics. They include strong prescription <span class="qt1">pain</span> relievers such as oxycodone, hydrocodone, or morphine. Opioids are sometimes used to treat moderate to severe <span class="qt1">pain</span>. Your provider may give you a prescription to reduce <span class="qt1">pain</span> after you have had a major injury or surgery.</p><p>Opioids are very effective, but they can sometimes have serious side effects. There is also a risk of addiction. Because of the risks, you must use them only under your provider's supervision.</p>What are some non-drug treatments for <span class="qt1">pain</span>?<p><span class="qt1">Pain</span> relievers are just one part of a <span class="qt1">pain</span> treatment plan. Environmental factors, stress, and beliefs about <span class="qt1">pain</span> may affect the way you feel about <span class="qt1">pain</span> and respond to treatment.</p><p>There are many things you can do to help ease <span class="qt1">pain</span>. Treatment depends on the cause and type of <span class="qt1">pain</span>. It's important to check with your provider before trying any of them. A few non-drug treatments for <span class="qt1">pain</span> include:</p><ul><li>Acupuncture</li><li>Hot or cold packs</li><li>Massage therapy</li><li>Physical therapy</li><li>Relaxation techniques</li></ul></content>
