    <document rank="13" url="https://medlineplus.gov/temporomandibulardisorders.html">
      <content name="title">Temporomandibular Disorders</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">TMD</content>
      <content name="altTitle">TMJ</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Temporomandibular Joint Dysfunction</content>
      <content name="altTitle">TMJ disorders</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What are temporomandibular disorders (TMDs)?<p>Temporomandibular disorders (TMDs) are a group of more than 30 conditions that affect your temporomandibular joints (TMJs). You have two TMJs; one on each side of your jaw. They connect your lower jaw to your skull.</p><p>TMDs can cause problems in your jaw joint and your muscles that control jaw movement. These problems can include <span class="qt1">pain</span>, stiffness, and trouble chewing.</p><p>There are three main types of TMDs:</p><ul><li>Disorders of your jaw joints</li><li>Disorders of the muscles you use for chewing</li><li>Headaches from a TMD</li></ul><p>Many TMDs last only a short time and go away on their own. However, in some cases they can become chronic (long lasting)</p>What causes temporomandibular disorders (TMDs)?<p>Sometimes an injury to the jaw or TMJ can cause a TMD. But in most cases, the cause is not known.</p><p>Research suggests that certain factors may play a role in why a TMD starts and whether it will be long lasting:</p><ul><li>Genetics</li><li>Psychological and life stressors</li><li>How someone perceives <span class="qt1">pain</span></li></ul>Who is more likely to develop a temporomandibular disorder (TMD)?<p>Anyone can develop a TMD, but it is more common in women than in men. It is most common in women between 35 and 44 years old.</p>What are the symptoms of temporomandibular disorders (TMDs)?<p>The symptoms of TMDs may include:</p><ul><li><span class="qt1">Pain</span> in the chewing muscles and/or jaw joint. This is the most common symptom.</li><li><span class="qt1">Pain</span> that spreads to the face or neck.</li><li>Jaw stiffness.</li><li>Limited movement or locking of the jaw.</li><li>Painful clicking or popping in your jaw joint when you open or close your mouth. However, clicking or popping without <span class="qt1">pain</span> is common and is not a sign of a TMD.</li><li>Tinnitus (ringing in the ears), hearing loss, or dizziness.</li><li>A change in the way the upper and lower teeth fit together.</li></ul>How are temporomandibular disorders (TMDs) diagnosed?<p>There is no standard test used to diagnose TMDs, and some other conditions can cause some similar symptoms. This can make TMDs hard to diagnose.</p><p>To find out if you have a TMD, your health care provider or dentist:</p><ul><li>Will take a detailed medical history, which includes asking about your symptoms. They will want to know:
	<ul><li>Where the <span class="qt1">pain</span> is</li><li>When you have it</li><li>What makes it better or worse</li><li>If it stays in one area or spreads to other parts of your body</li><li>If you have any other <span class="qt1">pain</span> conditions such as <span class="qt1">headache</span> or <span class="qt0">back</span> <span class="qt1">pain</span></li></ul>
</li><li>Will examine your head, neck, face, and jaw for:
	<ul><li>Tenderness</li><li>Jaw clicking or popping</li><li>Problems moving your jaw</li></ul>
</li><li>May order imaging studies such as an x-ray, MRI, or CT</li></ul>What are the treatments for temporomandibular disorders (TMDs)?<p>For many people, the symptoms of TMD are temporary. To help you feel better, your provider or dentist may suggest that you:</p><ul><li>Eat soft foods.</li><li>Apply heat or cold to the face and do some exercises to gently stretch and strengthen your jaw muscles.</li><li>Take over-the-counter <span class="qt1">pain</span> relievers, such as nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) like ibuprofen.</li><li>Stop clenching your jaw, chewing gum, and biting your nails.</li></ul><p>If those do not help, your provider or dentist may suggest other treatments such as:</p><ul><li>Physical therapy</li><li>Self-management and behavioral health approaches, which can include:
	<ul><li>Cognitive behavioral therapy (CBT), a type of psychotherapy. It can help you identify negative thoughts and change them. It can also teach you coping skills.</li><li>Biofeedback, which involves using electronic devices to teach you to control certain body functions, such as your heartbeat, blood pressure, and muscle tension. For example, it can detect when you tighten your jaw muscles. You can learn how to recognize when this happens and then relax your muscles.</li></ul></li><li>Medicines such as:
	<ul><li>Anti-anxiety medicines</li><li>Antidepressants</li><li>Anti-seizure medicines</li><li>Prescription <span class="qt1">pain</span> relievers</li></ul>
</li><li>An intraoral appliance, which is a device that fits over your teeth. Types of intraoral appliances include oral splints and mouth guards. If you are going to use one, it's important to: 
	<ul><li>Know that there is not a lot of evidence that they improve TMD <span class="qt1">pain</span>.</li><li>Make sure that the appliance you have is not designed to permanently change your bite.</li><li>Let your provider or dentist know if it makes your <span class="qt1">pain</span> worse.</li></ul></li><li>Complementary treatments, which are treatments that are used along with mainstream medical care. There is limited evidence showing that they help with TMD symptoms. But some providers and dentists may recommend acupuncture and transcutaneous electrical stimulation (TENS). TENS involves using a device to send a gentle electric current to your nerves or muscles. It may help treat <span class="qt1">pain</span> by interrupting or blocking the <span class="qt1">pain</span> signals.</li></ul><p>If you are still having severe symptoms from a TMD, your provider or dentist may suggest surgery, including implant surgery, or another procedure.</p><p>It's important to be careful, because sometimes surgery or another procedure may not work or may even make your symptoms worse. Before any surgery or other procedure, it is very important to get opinions from more than one provider and to completely understand the risks. If possible, get an opinion from a surgeon who specializes in treating TMDs.</p><p>NIH: National Institute of Dental and Craniofacial Research</p></content>

    <document rank="14" url="https://medlineplus.gov/sciatica.html">
      <content name="title">Sciatica</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>Sciatica is a symptom of a problem with the sciatic nerve, the largest nerve in the body. It controls muscles in the <span class="qt0">back</span> of your knee and lower leg and provides feeling to the <span class="qt0">back</span> of your thigh, part of your lower leg, and the sole of your foot. When you have sciatica, you have <span class="qt1">pain</span>, weakness, numbness, or tingling. It can start in the lower <span class="qt0">back</span> and extend down your leg to your calf, foot, or even your toes. It's usually on only one side of your body.</p><p>Causes of sciatica include:</p><ul><li>A ruptured intervertebral disk</li><li>Narrowing of the spinal canal that puts pressure on the nerve, called spinal stenosis</li><li>An injury such as a pelvic fracture.</li></ul><p>In many cases no cause can be found.</p><p>Sometimes sciatica goes away on its own. Treatment, if needed, depends on the cause of the problem. It may include exercises, medicines, and surgery.</p></content>

    <document rank="15" url="https://medlineplus.gov/menstruation.html">
      <content name="title">Menstruation</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Amenorrhea</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Heavy Menstrual Bleeding</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Menorrhagia</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Menses</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Menstrual period</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Period</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is menstruation?<p>Menstruation, also called a period, is normal vaginal bleeding that happens as part of the monthly cycle in women and girls. Every month, the body prepares for pregnancy. If pregnancy does not happen, the uterus (womb) sheds its lining. This blood and tissue leave the body through the vagina.</p>What is the menstrual cycle?<p>The menstrual cycle is the monthly process the body goes through to prepare for pregnancy. It is counted from the first day of one period to the first day of the next. Most menstrual cycles last about 28 days, but cycles can be shorter or longer and still be normal.</p>When will my period start?<p>Most girls start having a period between ages 9 and 15, but some may start earlier or later. A period usually lasts three to seven days and can be light or heavy. It may take a few years to become regular.</p><p>Your period may change during times that affect hormones, such as after childbirth or while breastfeeding. Periods usually continue until menopause, which most often happens between ages 45 and 55.</p>What are the symptoms of menstruation?<p>In addition to vaginal bleeding, you may have:</p><ul><li>Cramping <span class="qt1">pain</span> in the lower abdomen (belly) or pelvis</li><li>Lower <span class="qt0">back</span> <span class="qt1">pain</span></li><li>Bloating</li><li>Sore breasts</li><li>Food cravings</li><li>Mood swings or irritability</li><li>Headaches or fatigue</li></ul><p>Premenstrual syndrome (PMS) is a group of physical and emotional symptoms that can happen in the days before a period starts. Many women have some PMS symptoms, which usually go away after their period begins.</p><p>Many women and girls use menstrual products such as pads, tampons, menstrual cups, or period underwear to manage bleeding during their period. Changing or cleaning these products often helps you stay comfortable and clean.</p>What are menstrual irregularities?<p>Menstrual irregularities are changes in the usual menstrual cycle. These may include missed periods, irregular periods, or heavy bleeding. Common menstrual irregularities include:</p><ul><li>Amenorrhea means not having a period.</li><li>Oligomenorrhea means having periods that happen less often.</li><li>Menorrhagia means very heavy menstrual bleeding.</li><li>Prolonged menstrual bleeding occurs when periods regularly last longer than 8 days.</li><li>Dysmenorrhea refers to painful periods that may include severe <span class="qt1">cramps</span>.</li></ul><p>Keep track of your period each month. This can help you understand your usual cycle, know when to expect your next period, and notice changes.</p><p>Talk with your health care provider if you notice major changes in your menstrual cycle, very heavy bleeding, or severe <span class="qt1">pain</span>. These may be signs of other health problems that need treatment.</p><p>NIH: National Institute of Child Health and Human Development</p></content>
      <content name="mesh">Menstruation</content>
      <content name="mesh">Menstruation Disturbances</content>
      <content name="groupName">Women</content>
      <content name="groupName">Endocrine System</content>
      <content name="groupName">Female Reproductive System</content>

    <document rank="16" url="https://medlineplus.gov/periodpain.html">
      <content name="title">Period <span class="qt1">Pain</span></content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Dysmenorrhea</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Menstrual <span class="qt1">Cramps</span></content>
      <content name="altTitle">Menstrual <span class="qt1">pain</span></content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What are painful periods?<p>Menstruation, or period, is normal vaginal bleeding that happens as part of a woman's monthly cycle. Many women have painful periods, also called dysmenorrhea. The <span class="qt1">pain</span> is most often menstrual <span class="qt1">cramps</span>, which are a throbbing, cramping <span class="qt1">pain</span> in your lower abdomen. You may also have other symptoms, such as lower <span class="qt0">back</span> <span class="qt1">pain</span>, nausea, diarrhea, and headaches. Period <span class="qt1">pain</span> is not the same as premenstrual syndrome (PMS). PMS causes many different symptoms, including weight gain, bloating, irritability, and fatigue. PMS often starts one to two weeks before your period starts.</p>What causes painful periods?<p>There are two types of dysmenorrhea: primary and secondary. Each type has different causes.</p><p>Primary dysmenorrhea is the most common kind of period <span class="qt1">pain</span>. It is period <span class="qt1">pain</span> that is not caused by another condition. The cause is usually having too many prostaglandins, which are chemicals that your uterus makes. These chemicals make the muscles of your uterus tighten and relax, and this causes the <span class="qt1">cramps</span>.</p><p>The <span class="qt1">pain</span> can start a day or two before your period. It normally lasts for a few days, though in some women it can last longer.</p><p>You usually first start having period <span class="qt1">pain</span> when you are younger, just after you begin getting periods. Often, as you get older, you have less <span class="qt1">pain. The pain</span> may also get better after you have given birth.</p><p>Secondary dysmenorrhea often starts later in life. It is caused by conditions that affect your uterus or other reproductive organs, such as endometriosis and uterine fibroids. This kind of <span class="qt1">pain</span> often gets worse over time. It may begin before your period starts and continue after your period ends.</p>What can I do about period <span class="qt1">pain</span>?<p>To help ease your period <span class="qt1">pain</span>, you can try:</p><ul><li>Using a heating pad or hot water bottle on your lower abdomen</li><li>Getting some exercise</li><li>Taking a hot bath</li><li>Doing relaxation techniques, including yoga and meditation</li></ul><p>You might also try taking over-the-counter <span class="qt1">pain</span> relievers such as nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs). NSAIDs include ibuprofen and naproxen. Besides relieving <span class="qt1">pain</span>, NSAIDs reduce the amount of prostaglandins that your uterus makes and lessen their effects.  This helps to lessen the <span class="qt1">cramps</span>. You can take NSAIDs when you first have symptoms, or when your period starts. You can keep taking them for a few days. You should not take NSAIDS if you have ulcers or other stomach problems, bleeding problems, or liver disease. You should also not take them if you are allergic to aspirin. Always check with your health care provider if you are not sure whether or not you should take NSAIDs.</p><p>It may also help to get enough rest and avoid using alcohol and tobacco.</p>When should I get medical help for my period <span class="qt1">pain</span>?<p>For many women, some <span class="qt1">pain</span> during your period is normal. However, you should contact your health care provider if:</p><ul><li>NSAIDs and self-care measures don't help, and the <span class="qt1">pain</span> interferes with your life</li><li>Your <span class="qt1">cramps</span> suddenly get worse</li><li>You are over 25 and you get severe <span class="qt1">cramps</span> for the first time</li><li>You have a fever with your period <span class="qt1">pain</span></li><li>You have the <span class="qt1">pain</span> even when you are not getting your period</li></ul>How is the cause of severe period <span class="qt1">pain</span> diagnosed?<p>To diagnose severe period <span class="qt1">pain</span>, your health care provider will ask you about your medical history and do a pelvic exam. You may also have an ultrasound or other imaging test. If your health care provider thinks you have secondary dysmenorrhea, you might have laparoscopy. It is a surgery that lets your health care provider look inside your body.</p>What are treatments for severe period <span class="qt1">pain</span>?<p>If your period <span class="qt1">pain</span> is primary dysmenorrhea and you need medical treatment, your health care provider might suggest using hormonal birth control, such as the pill, patch, ring, or IUD. Another treatment option might be prescription <span class="qt1">pain</span> relievers.</p><p>If you have secondary dysmenorrhea, your treatment depends upon the condition that is causing the problem. In some cases, you may need surgery.</p></content>

      <content name="groupName">Women</content>
      <content name="groupName">Female Reproductive System</content>

You may also have other symptoms, such as lower <span class="qt0">back</span> <span class="qt1">pain</span>, nausea, diarrhea, and headaches. Period <span class="qt1">pain</span> is  ... </content>

    <document rank="17" url="https://medlineplus.gov/pregnancy.html">
      <content name="title">Pregnancy</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>You're going to have a baby! It's an exciting time, but it can also feel a bit overwhelming. You may have a lot of questions, including what you can do to give your baby a healthy start. Taking care of your health while you're pregnant will help your fetus develop into a healthy baby. To do that, it's important to:</p><ul><li>Have regular visits with your health care provider. During prenatal care visits your provider checks your health and carefully monitors your pregnancy until you deliver a healthy baby. And if there are any health problems, your provider can find them early. Getting treatment right away can cure many problems and prevent others.</li><li>Eat healthy and drink plenty of water. Good nutrition during pregnancy includes eating a variety of: 
	<ul><li>Fruits</li><li>Vegetables</li><li>Whole grains</li><li>Lean meats or other protein sources</li><li>Low-fat dairy products</li></ul>
</li><li>Take prenatal vitamins. During pregnancy, you need higher amounts of certain vitamins and minerals, such as folic acid and iron.</li><li>Be careful with medicines. Always check with your provider before you start or stop any medicine. This includes over-the-counter medicines and dietary or herbal supplements.</li><li>Stay active. Physical activity can help you stay strong, feel and sleep better, and prepare your body for birth. Check with your provider about which types of activities are right for you.</li><li>Avoid substances that could hurt your baby, including alcohol, drugs, and tobacco.</li></ul><p>Your body will keep changing as your fetus develops until your baby is ready to be born. It can be hard to know whether a new symptom is normal or could be a sign of a problem. Check with your provider if something is bothering or worrying you.</p></content>
      <content name="mesh">Pregnancy</content>
      <content name="groupName">Women</content>
      <content name="groupName">Pregnancy and Reproduction</content>
      <content name="groupName">Female Reproductive System</content>

    <document rank="18" url="https://medlineplus.gov/sportssafety.html">
      <content name="title">Sports Safety</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Bicycle Safety</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Recreational Safety</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>Playing sports can be fun, but it can also be dangerous if you are not careful. You can help prevent injuries by:</p><ul><li>Getting a physical to make sure you are healthy before you start playing your sport</li><li>Wearing the right shoes, gear, and equipment </li><li>Drinking lots of water </li><li>Warming up and stretching</li></ul><p>If you have already hurt yourself playing a sport, make sure you recover completely before you start up again.  If possible, protect the injured part of your body with padding, a brace, or special equipment. When you do start playing again, start slowly.</p></content>

      <content name="mesh">Safety</content>
      <content name="groupName">Safety Issues</content>
      <content name="groupName">Fitness and Exercise</content>
      <content name="snippet"> Playing sports can be fun, but it can also be dangerous if you are not careful. You can help prevent injuries by: Getting a physical to make sure you are healthy  ... </content>

    <document rank="19" url="https://medlineplus.gov/sportsinjuries.html">
      <content name="title">Sports Injuries</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>Exercising is good for you, but sometimes you can injure yourself when you play sports or exercise. Accidents, poor training practices, or improper gear can cause them. Some people get hurt because they are not in shape. Not warming up or stretching enough can also lead to injuries. </p><p>The most common sports injuries are:</p><ul><li>Sprains and strains</li><li>Knee injuries</li><li> Swollen muscles </li><li> Achilles tendon injuries </li><li> <span class="qt1">Pain</span> along the shin bone </li><li>Rotator cuff injuries</li><li>Fractures (broken bones) </li><li>Dislocations</li></ul><p>If you get hurt, stop playing. Continuing to play or exercise can cause more harm.  Treatment often begins with the RICE (Rest, Ice, Compression, and Elevation) method to relieve <span class="qt1">pain</span>, reduce swelling, and speed healing. Other possible treatments include <span class="qt1">pain</span> relievers, keeping the injured area from moving, rehabilitation, and sometimes surgery.</p><p>NIH: National Institute of Arthritis and Musculoskeletal and Skin Diseases</p></content>
      <content name="mesh">Athletic Injuries</content>
      <content name="groupName">Injuries and Wounds</content>
      <content name="groupName">Wellness and Lifestyle</content>

    <document rank="20" url="https://medlineplus.gov/mriscans.html">
      <content name="title">MRI Scans</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Magnetic Resonance Imaging</content>
      <content name="altTitle">NMR</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Nuclear magnetic resonance</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is an MRI scan?<p>Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) is an imaging test that is usually <span class="qt1">painless</span> and uses magnets, radio waves, and a computer to create detailed pictures of the inside of your body. MRI scans show organs and soft tissues clearly and can help tell healthy from unhealthy tissue in many cases.</p>Who needs an MRI scan?<p>MRI scans are often used to diagnose conditions that affect soft tissues and to monitor treatment.</p><p>MRI may be used to look at:</p><ul><li>The brain, spinal cord, and nerves</li><li>Muscles, ligaments, and tendons</li><li>Joints, such as the knee or shoulder</li></ul><p>MRI does not use x-rays or other radiation. Because of this, it may be used when repeat imaging is needed.</p>How do you prepare for an MRI scan?<p>MRI machines use powerful magnets. Metal objects or implants can interfere with the scan and may be unsafe.</p><p>Before your MRI, you will need to remove items such as:</p><ul><li>Jewelry and watches</li><li>Hairpins</li><li>Hearing aids</li></ul><p>Before your MRI, tell your health care provider if you:</p><ul><li>Are pregnant</li><li>Are breastfeeding, especially if you may receive contrast material (a substance that helps some parts of your body show up more clearly on the images)</li><li>Feel anxious in enclosed spaces (claustrophobic)</li><li>Have kidney problems</li><li>Have metal in your body, such as bullets, shrapnel, or other metal fragments</li><li>Have metal or electronic devices in your body, such as a pacemaker, artificial joint, or other implants</li><li>Have tattoos or permanent makeup, since some darker inks may contain metal</li></ul>How is an MRI scan done?<p>You will usually change into a hospital gown. Sometimes before the test, you may receive contrast, which is usually given through a vein (intravenously or IV).</p><p>During the scan, you lie on a table that slides inside a large, tunnel-like machine. Some facilities use an open MRI machine, which is less enclosed. The machine makes loud noises as it takes pictures. You may be given earplugs or headphones.</p><p>The scan can take some time, depending on the part of your body being examined. You will need to stay very still during the scan.</p>What should I expect after an MRI scan?<p>After most MRI scans, you can return to normal activities right away. If you were given medicine to help you relax, someone will need to take you home.</p><p>A specialist called a radiologist reviews the images and shares them with your provider.</p></content>
      <content name="mesh">Magnetic Resonance Imaging</content>

    <document rank="21" url="https://medlineplus.gov/occupationalhealth.html">
      <content name="title">Occupational Health</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Employee Health</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Occupational Injuries</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>Occupational health problems occur at work or because of the kind of work you do. These problems can include:</p><ul><li>Cuts, fractures (broken bones), and sprains and strains</li><li>Loss of limbs</li><li>Repetitive motion disorders</li><li>Hearing problems caused by exposure to noise</li><li>Vision problems</li><li>Illness caused by breathing, touching, or swallowing unsafe substances</li><li>Illness caused by exposure to radiation</li><li>Exposure to germs in health care settings</li></ul><p>Good job safety and prevention practices can reduce your risk of these problems. Try to stay fit, reduce stress, set up your work area properly, and use the right equipment and gear.</p></content>
      <content name="mesh">Accidents, Occupational</content>
      <content name="mesh">Occupational Stress</content>
      <content name="mesh">Occupational Injuries</content>

    <document rank="22" url="https://medlineplus.gov/rehabilitation.html">
      <content name="title">Rehabilitation</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Occupational Therapy</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Physical Therapy</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Rehab</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is rehabilitation?<p>Rehabilitation is care that can help you get <span class="qt0">back</span>, keep, or improve abilities that you need for daily life. These abilities may be physical, mental, and/or cognitive (thinking and learning). You may have lost them because of a disease or injury, or as a side effect from a medical treatment. Rehabilitation can improve your daily life and functioning.</p>Who needs rehabilitation?<p>Rehabilitation is for people who have lost abilities that they need for daily life. Some of the most common causes include:</p><ul><li>Injuries and trauma, including burns, fractures (broken bones), traumatic brain injury, and spinal cord injuries</li><li>Stroke</li><li>Severe infections</li><li>Major surgery</li><li>Side effects from medical treatments, such as from cancer treatments</li><li>Certain birth defects and genetic disorders</li><li>Developmental disabilities</li><li>Chronic <span class="qt1">pain</span>, including <span class="qt0">back</span> and neck <span class="qt1">pain</span></li></ul>What are the goals of rehabilitation?<p>The overall goal of rehabilitation is to help you get your abilities <span class="qt0">back</span> and regain independence. But the specific goals are different for each person. They depend on what caused the problem, whether the cause is ongoing or temporary, which abilities you lost, and how severe the problem is. For example,:</p><ul><li>A person who has had a stroke may need rehabilitation to be able to dress or bathe without help</li><li>An active person who has had a heart attack may go through cardiac rehabilitation to try to return to exercising</li><li>Someone with a lung disease may get pulmonary rehabilitation to be able to breathe better and improve their quality of life</li></ul>What happens in a rehabilitation program?<p>When you get rehabilitation, you often have a team of different health care providers helping you. They will work with you to figure out your needs, goals, and treatment plan. The types of treatments that may be in a treatment plan include:</p><ul><li>Assistive devices, which are tools, equipment, and products that help people with disabilities move and function</li><li>Cognitive rehabilitation therapy to help you relearn or improve skills such as thinking, learning, memory, planning, and decision making</li><li>Mental health counseling</li><li>Music or art therapy to help you express your feelings, improve your thinking, and develop social connections</li><li>Nutritional counseling</li><li>Occupational therapy to help you with your daily activities</li><li>Physical therapy to help your strength, mobility, and fitness</li><li>Recreational therapy to improve your emotional well-being through arts and crafts, games, relaxation training, and animal-assisted therapy</li><li>Speech-language therapy to help with speaking, understanding, reading, writing and swallowing</li><li>Treatment for <span class="qt1">pain</span></li><li>Vocational rehabilitation to help you build skills for going to school or working at a job</li></ul><p>Depending on your needs, you may have rehabilitation in the providers' offices, a hospital, or an inpatient rehabilitation center. In some cases, a provider may come to your home. If you get care in your home, you will need to have family members or friends who can come and help with your rehabilitation.</p></content>

    <document rank="23" url="https://medlineplus.gov/pain.html">
      <content name="title"><span class="qt1">Pain</span></content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is <span class="qt1">pain</span>?<p><span class="qt1">Pain</span> is a signal in your nervous system that something may be wrong. It is an unpleasant feeling, such as a prick, tingle, sting, burn, or <span class="qt1">ache</span>. Each person feels <span class="qt1">pain</span> differently, even if the reason for the <span class="qt1">pain</span> is the same.  <span class="qt1">Pain</span> may be sharp or dull. It may be mild or severe. <span class="qt1">Pain</span> may come and go, or it may be constant. You may feel <span class="qt1">pain</span> in one area of your body, such as your <span class="qt0">back</span>, abdomen, chest, or pelvis, or you may feel it all over.</p><p><span class="qt1">Pain</span> can help alert you that there is a problem. If you never felt <span class="qt1">pain</span>, you might seriously hurt yourself without knowing it, or you might not realize you have a medical problem that needs treatment. Finding out what's causing your <span class="qt1">pain</span> will help your health care provider determine the best way to manage or treat it.</p>What are the types of <span class="qt1">pain</span>?<p>Patterns and types of <span class="qt1">pain</span> are named based on how long and how often you have <span class="qt1">pain</span>. These include:</p><p>
</p><ul><li>Acute <span class="qt1">pain</span> usually happens suddenly because of a disease, injury, or inflammation (irritation, redness, and/or swelling). This type of <span class="qt1">pain</span> is often sharp and acts as a warning that something is wrong. It usually goes away when the cause is treated or healed, though sometimes it can turn into chronic <span class="qt1">pain</span>. Some examples of causes of acute <span class="qt1">pain</span> include broken bones or dental issues.</li><li>Chronic <span class="qt1">pain</span> lasts for longer than three months or the time in which you should have healed.  It can affect all aspects of daily life, including your mood and relationships. Some conditions that might cause chronic <span class="qt1">pain</span> include arthritis or <span class="qt0">back</span> problems.</li><li>Episodic <span class="qt1">pain</span> can happen from time to time and may occur with long-term medical conditions. Some conditions that might cause episodic <span class="qt1">pain</span> include sickle cell disease or chronic migraines.</li></ul><p><span class="qt1">Pain</span> may also be categorized by what is likely to be the cause of the <span class="qt1">pain. Pain</span> may be described as nociceptive (caused by tissue damage or inflammation), neuropathic (caused by nerve damage), or nociplastic (caused by changes in how your nervous system processes <span class="qt1">pain</span>).</p>What causes <span class="qt1">pain</span>?<p>Understanding what causes <span class="qt1">pain</span> and why people feel it differently may be difficult. Often, it's easier to find the cause of acute <span class="qt1">pain</span> due to an injury than the cause of chronic <span class="qt1">pain</span>, or you may have an ongoing cause of <span class="qt1">pain</span>, such as cancer.</p><p>In some cases, there is no clear cause. Environmental factors and psychological factors such as stress and beliefs about <span class="qt1">pain</span> may affect the way you feel <span class="qt1">pain</span> and respond to treatment.</p>How is <span class="qt1">pain</span> diagnosed?<p>You are the only one who knows how your <span class="qt1">pain</span> feels. Your provider can best measure your <span class="qt1">pain</span> by how you report it. They may ask you:</p><ul><li>Where is the <span class="qt1">pain</span> in your body?</li><li>How long have you had the <span class="qt1">pain</span>?</li><li>How does the <span class="qt1">pain</span> feel, such as stinging, burning, or sharp?</li><li>How often do you have <span class="qt1">pain</span>?</li><li>When do you feel <span class="qt1">pain</span>, such as in the morning, evening, or all the time?</li><li>What relieves the <span class="qt1">pain</span>?</li><li>How does <span class="qt1">pain</span> affect your daily life?</li><li>Is the <span class="qt1">pain</span> mild, moderate, or severe?</li><li>To rate the <span class="qt1">pain</span> on a scale.</li></ul><p>If the cause of your <span class="qt1">pain</span> is unknown, your provider may also do a physical exam and order blood tests or other medical tests to help find the cause.</p>What are the treatments for <span class="qt1">pain</span>?<p><span class="qt1">Pain</span> is not always curable, but there are many ways to manage and treat it. Treatment depends on the cause and type of <span class="qt1">pain</span>. Treatments may include medicines, such as <span class="qt1">pain</span> relievers. There are also non-drug treatments, such as acupuncture, physical therapy, and sometimes surgery.</p><p>Depending on the cause of your <span class="qt1">pain</span> and your symptoms, your provider may recommend lifestyle changes. These may include suggestions for:</p><ul><li>Eating a healthy diet</li><li>Adding exercise</li><li>Losing weight</li><li>Managing stress</li><li>Improving mental health</li></ul><p>NIH: National Institute of Neurological Disorders and Stroke</p></content>
      <content name="mesh"><span class="qt1">Pain</span></content>
      <content name="mesh"><span class="qt1">Pain</span> Perception</content>
      <content name="mesh"><span class="qt1">Pain</span> Management</content>

You may feel <span class="qt1">pain</span> in one area of your body, such as your <span class="qt0">back</span>, abdomen, chest, or pelvis, or you may feel it all over. <span class="qt1">Pain</span> can help alert you that there is a  ... </content>

    <document rank="24" url="https://medlineplus.gov/dislocatedshoulder.html">
      <content name="title">Dislocated Shoulder</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Shoulder Dislocation</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is a dislocated shoulder?<p>Your shoulder joint is made up of three bones: your collarbone, your shoulder blade, and your upper arm bone. The top of your upper arm bone is shaped like a ball. This ball fits into a cuplike socket in your shoulder blade. A shoulder dislocation is an injury that happens when the ball pops out of your socket. A dislocation may be partial, where the ball is only partially out of the socket. It can also be a full dislocation, where the ball is completely out of the socket.</p>What causes a dislocated shoulder?<p>Your shoulders are the most movable joints in your body. They are also the most commonly dislocated joints.</p><p>The most common causes of shoulder dislocations are:</p><ul><li>Sports injuries</li><li>Accidents, including traffic accidents</li><li>Falling on your shoulder or outstretched arm</li><li>Seizures and electric shocks, which can cause muscle contractions that pull the arm out of place</li></ul>Who is at risk for a dislocated shoulder?<p>A dislocated shoulder can happen to anyone, but they are more common in young men, who are more often involved in sports and other physical activities. Older adults, especially women, are also at higher risk because they are more likely to fall.</p>What are the symptoms of a dislocated shoulder?<p>The symptoms of a dislocated shoulder include:</p><ul><li>Severe shoulder <span class="qt1">pain</span></li><li>Swelling and bruising of your shoulder or upper arm</li><li>Numbness and/or weakness in your arm, neck, hand, or fingers</li><li>Trouble moving your arm</li><li>Your arm seems to be out of place</li><li>Muscle spasms in your shoulder</li></ul><p>If you are having these symptoms, get medical treatment right away.</p>How is a dislocated shoulder diagnosed?<p>To make a diagnosis, your health care provider will take a medical history and examine your shoulder. Your provider may also ask you to get an x-ray to confirm the diagnosis.</p>What are the treatments for a dislocated shoulder?<p>The treatment for dislocated shoulder usually involves three steps:</p><ul><li>The first step is a closed reduction, a procedure in which your health care provider puts the ball of your upper arm <span class="qt0">back</span> into the socket. You may first get medicine to relieve the <span class="qt1">pain</span> and relax your shoulder muscles. Once the joint is <span class="qt0">back</span> in place, the severe <span class="qt1">pain</span> should end.</li><li>The second step is wearing a sling or other device to keep your shoulder in place. You will wear it for a few days to several weeks.</li><li>The third step is rehabilitation, once the <span class="qt1">pain</span> and swelling have improved. You will do exercises to improve your range of motion and strengthen your muscles.</li></ul><p>You may need surgery if you injure the tissues or nerves around the shoulder or if you get repeated dislocations.</p><p>A dislocation can make your shoulder unstable. When that happens, it takes less force to dislocate it. This means that there is a higher risk of it happening again. Your health care provider may ask you to continue doing some exercises to prevent another dislocation.</p></content>

  <list num="93" start="0" per="25">
    <document rank="0" url="https://medlineplus.gov/depression.html">
      <content name="title"><span class="qt0">Depression</span></content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Dysthymic Disorder</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Clinical <span class="qt0">depression</span></content>
      <content name="altTitle">Major <span class="qt1">depressive disorder</span></content>
      <content name="altTitle">Unipolar <span class="qt0">depression</span></content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is <span class="qt0">depression</span>?<p><span class="qt0">Depression</span> is more than a feeling of being sad or irritable for a few days. It's a serious mood disorder. As one of the most common mental disorders in the United States, <span class="qt0">depression</span> can affect how you think, feel, and your everyday life. You may not know why you feel this way, but even basic daily activities such as sleeping, eating, or working may become difficult. <span class="qt0">Depression</span> often needs long-term treatment but getting help at the earliest sign of a problem can help manage your symptoms.</p>What are the different types of <span class="qt0">depression</span>?<p>The most common types of <span class="qt0">depression</span> are major <span class="qt0">depression</span> and persistent <span class="qt1">depressive disorder</span>.</p><ul><li>Major <span class="qt0">depression</span> symptoms include a depressed mood or a loss of interest. Symptoms affect your daily activities and last for at least two weeks.</li><li>Persistent <span class="qt1">depressive disorder</span> (also called dysthymia or dysthymic disorder) has less severe depressive symptoms, but they last longer, usually for at least two years.</li></ul><p>Other types of <span class="qt0">depression</span> can include:</p><ul><li>Seasonal affective disorder comes and goes with the seasons. It usually starts in late fall and early winter and goes away during the spring and summer.</li><li>Bipolar disorder is sometimes called manic <span class="qt0">depression</span>. This mood disorder can cause intense mood swings.</li><li><span class="qt0">Depression</span> with symptoms of psychosis is a severe form of <span class="qt0">depression</span> that also has delusions (false beliefs) or hallucinations (seeing, hearing, or feeling something that isn't there).</li></ul>What causes <span class="qt0">depression</span>?<p>There are a variety of things that may cause <span class="qt0">depression</span>, including genetic, biological, environmental, and psychological factors. <span class="qt0">Depression</span> can happen at any age and can affect anyone, but it's more common in women. This is often due to the physical and hormonal changes that women have during menstruation, pregnancy, the postpartum period, and menopause. Other mental disorders or chronic medical conditions such as diabetes, cancer, or heart disease may occur with <span class="qt0">depression</span>. This may make the chronic condition or <span class="qt0">depression</span> worse.</p><p>In some cases, there is no clear cause for <span class="qt0">depression</span>.</p>Who is at risk of <span class="qt0">depression</span>?<p>You may be at a higher risk of <span class="qt0">depression</span> if you have a close relative who has it or another mental health disorder. Going through trauma or stressful life events may raise your risk even more.</p>What are the symptoms of <span class="qt0">depression</span>?<p><span class="qt0">Depression</span> doesn't look the same on everyone. The type and number of symptoms of <span class="qt0">depression</span> can vary and don't always appear like sadness. <span class="qt0">Depression</span> may affect you differently based on your:</p><ul><li>Age. The symptoms of <span class="qt0">depression</span> may be different in a child, who may appear more irritable than sad. Teens may act out or have excessive sleepiness. The symptoms for an older adult may not be as obvious as other age groups.</li><li>Sex. Men often show symptoms and coping behaviors differently than women. Rather than sad, men may appear angry or irritable.</li><li>Medical conditions. Some medicines and medical conditions may cause symptoms that look like <span class="qt0">depression</span>. Chronic conditions may make <span class="qt0">depression</span> worse.</li></ul><p>Symptoms of <span class="qt0">depression</span> are ongoing and affect your day-to-day life. They can include:</p><ul><li>Feeling sad or "empty"</li><li>Losing interest in favorite activities</li><li>Overeating, or not wanting to eat at all</li><li>Sleeping too much, or not enough</li><li>Feeling very tired</li><li>Feeling hopeless, irritable, anxious, or guilty</li><li>Having aches and pains, headaches, cramps, or digestive problems</li><li>Having thoughts of death or suicide</li><li>Changing moods or behaviors</li><li>Increasing use of alcohol or drugs</li><li>Isolating from family and friends</li></ul>How is <span class="qt0">depression</span> diagnosed?<p>If you think you have <span class="qt0">depression</span>, talk to your health care provider or a mental health provider. To be diagnosed with <span class="qt0">depression</span>, your provider may use many tools:</p><ul><li>A medical history will include asking about your symptoms and family history. To be diagnosed with <span class="qt0">depression</span>, your symptoms must occur most of the day, nearly every day, for at least two weeks. One of the symptoms must be a depressed mood or a loss of interest in most activities.</li><li>Medical tests may be done to rule out other medical conditions. Certain medicines and medical conditions (such as viruses or thyroid disorders) may cause symptoms like <span class="qt0">depression</span>.</li><li>A physical exam to make sure another issue isn't causing your symptoms.</li><li>A mental health evaluation by your provider or a mental health provider.</li></ul>What are the treatments for <span class="qt0">depression</span>?<p><span class="qt0">Depression</span> may need long-term treatment. Most treatments include medicines and/or psychotherapy (talk therapy):</p><ul><li>Medicines such as antidepressants may help control your symptoms. You may need to try several medicines to find which works best for you. Medicine may take time to work and can have side effects. Don't stop taking your medicine without first talking with your provider. Contact your provider if you have any concerns about side effects from the medicine.</li><li>Psychotherapy (talk therapy) under the care of a mental health provider can help you recognize and change troubling emotions, thoughts, and behaviors. This may be done one-on-one or in a group setting. It can give you and your family support, education, skills, and coping strategies.</li><li>Light therapy has been shown to be effective for seasonal affective disorder (SAD).</li><li>Other treatments options may be considered if treatment isn't working or if <span class="qt0">depression</span> is severe. These may include:
	<ul><li>Electroconvulsive therapy (ECT) is a brain stimulation procedure that uses a mild electric current when you are under general anesthesia. ECT is most often used for severe <span class="qt0">depression</span> that is not getting better with other treatments.</li><li>Repetitive transcranial magnetic stimulation (rTMS) is a brain stimulation procedure that uses magnetic waves to relieve <span class="qt0">depression</span>. It's not as powerful as ECT, but with rTMS, you don't need anesthesia. It also has a low risk of negative effects on your memory and thinking.</li></ul>
</li></ul>Can <span class="qt0">depression</span> be prevented?<p>Most cases of <span class="qt0">depression</span> can't be prevented, but healthy lifestyle changes can have long-term benefits for your mental health. These can include:</p><ul><li>Getting regular exercise</li><li>Having a consistent sleep schedule</li><li>Controlling stress</li></ul><p>If you, or someone you love is depressed, see your provider or a mental health provider. It's important to get treatment at the earliest sign of a problem.</p><p>If you or someone you know has thoughts of hurting themselves or attempting suicide, get help right away. If it is an emergency, dial 911.</p><p>NIH: National Institute of Mental Health</p></content>
      <content name="mesh"><span class="qt0">Depression</span></content>

    <document rank="1" url="https://medlineplus.gov/postpartumdepression.html">
      <content name="title">Postpartum <span class="qt0">Depression</span></content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Baby Blues</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Post-pregnancy <span class="qt0">depression</span></content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is postpartum <span class="qt0">depression</span>?<p>Having a baby can be exciting, but it can also be stressful and bring many strong emotions. During the postpartum period, which is the time after you give birth, you may have mood swings, crying spells, or feel anxious. These feelings are common and are often called the postpartum "baby blues". The baby blues usually go away within a few days to a week.</p><p>If your symptoms are more severe and last longer than two weeks, you may have postpartum <span class="qt0">depression</span>.</p><p>Postpartum <span class="qt0">depression</span> is a common but serious mood disorder that can begin anytime within the first year after childbirth. Without treatment, symptoms may make it hard to care for your baby or manage daily tasks.</p>What causes postpartum <span class="qt0">depression</span>?<p>The exact cause is unknown, but postpartum <span class="qt0">depression</span> likely results from a combination of factors, including:</p><ul><li>Sudden changes in hormone levels after pregnancy</li><li>Physical changes, such as dropping thyroid hormone levels</li><li>Lifestyle factors, such as fatigue, lack of sleep, limited support, or recent stressful events</li></ul>Who is at risk for postpartum <span class="qt0">depression</span>?<p>Anyone can develop postpartum <span class="qt0">depression</span> after childbirth. You might be at higher risk if you:</p><ul><li>Have a history of <span class="qt0">depression</span>, bipolar disorder, or a family health history of these conditions</li><li>Had a multiple birth (twins, triplets, or more)</li><li>Gave birth while in your teens</li><li>Had health problems during pregnancy</li><li>Had preterm labor or other complications during childbirth</li><li>Have a baby with special needs</li><li>Have ever experienced domestic violence</li><li>Are dealing with financial stress</li><li>Had an unplanned pregnancy</li></ul>What are the symptoms of postpartum <span class="qt0">depression</span>?<p>Postpartum <span class="qt0">depression</span> symptoms are more intense and last longer than the baby blues.</p><p>Symptoms may include:</p><ul><li>Feeling sad or empty most of the time</li><li>Eating too much or too little</li><li>Sleeping too much or too little</li><li>Crying more than usual or for no clear reason</li><li>Feeling unusually angry</li><li>Pulling away from family and friends</li><li>Feeling worried or anxious often</li><li>Feeling little or no interest in your baby</li><li>Constant doubts about your ability to care for your baby</li><li>Thinking of harming yourself or your baby</li></ul><p>If you have thoughts about suicide, or hurting yourself or your baby get help right away:</p><ul><li>Call 911 or go to your local emergency room</li><li>Contact a crisis hotline. In the United States, you can reach the National Suicide and Crisis Lifeline at any time:
	<ul><li>Call or text 988</li><li>Chat online with Lifeline Chat</li><li>TTY users: Use your preferred relay service or dial 711 then 988</li></ul>
</li><li>Veterans can contact the Veterans Crisis Line:
	<ul><li>Call 988, then press 1</li><li>Text 838255</li><li>Chat online</li></ul>
</li><li>Call your mental health provider or other health care provider</li><li>Reach out to a loved one or close friend</li></ul><p>If these symptoms begin during pregnancy and continue after childbirth, it's called perinatal <span class="qt0">depression</span>. Without treatment, it can affect bonding with your baby and may contribute to feeding or sleeping problems.</p><p>Very rarely, a new mother may develop postpartum psychosis, a medical emergency. Symptoms may include confusion, hallucinations (seeing or hearing things that aren't real), or dangerous behaviors. Call 911 or go to the nearest emergency room right away if this happens.</p>How is postpartum <span class="qt0">depression</span> diagnosed?<p>Your provider may diagnose postpartum <span class="qt0">depression</span> using:</p><ul><li>Screening questions or questionnaires, such as those about your mood, sleep, and thoughts.</li><li>A clinical evaluation based on your symptoms.</li><li>Blood tests, if needed, to check for physical conditions such as thyroid disorders, that can cause or worsen <span class="qt0">depression</span>.</li></ul>What is the treatment for postpartum <span class="qt0">depression</span>?<p>If you think you have postpartum <span class="qt0">depression</span>, talk with your provider. Treatments may include:</p><ul><li>Medicines, including antidepressants</li><li>Talk therapy (counseling)</li></ul><p>Tell your provider if you are breastfeeding so they can choose the safest treatment options.</p><p>Along with these treatment options, there are things you can do at home that may help you feel better, such as:</p><ul><li>Asking for help with caring for the baby and household chores</li><li>Taking time for yourself</li><li>Getting physical activity, such as walking</li><li>Resting when the baby rests</li></ul>Can postpartum <span class="qt0">depression</span> be prevented?<p>You may be able to lower your risk for postpartum <span class="qt0">depression</span> by:</p><ul><li>Talking with your provider during pregnancy about any history of <span class="qt0">depression</span></li><li>Completing recommended screening questionnaires during pregnancy and after birth</li><li>Getting support from family, friends, or support groups</li><li>Attending early Physical changes so symptoms can be found and treated as soon as possible</li></ul><p>Dept. of Health and Human Services Office on Women's Health</p></content>
      <content name="mesh"><span class="qt0">Depression</span>, Postpartum</content>
      <content name="groupName">Women</content>
      <content name="groupName">Mental Health and Behavior</content>

      <content name="snippet">What is postpartum <span class="qt0">depression</span>? Having a baby can be exciting, but it can also be stressful and bring many strong emotions. During the postpartum period,  ... </content>

    <document rank="2" url="https://medlineplus.gov/teendepression.html">
      <content name="title">Teen <span class="qt0">Depression</span></content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle"><span class="qt0">Depression</span>, Teen</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is <span class="qt0">depression</span> in teens?<p><span class="qt0">Depression</span> in teens (ages 13-17) is a serious medical illness. It's more than just a feeling of being sad or "blue" for a few days. It is an intense feeling of sadness, hopelessness, and anger or frustration that lasts much longer. These feelings make it hard for you to function normally and do your usual activities. You may also have trouble focusing and have no motivation or energy. <span class="qt0">Depression</span> can make you feel like it is hard to enjoy life or even get through the day.</p>What causes <span class="qt0">depression</span> in teens?<p>Many factors may play a role in <span class="qt0">depression</span>, including:</p><ul><li>Genetics. <span class="qt0">Depression</span> can run in families.</li><li>Brain biology and chemistry.</li><li>Hormones. Hormone changes can contribute to <span class="qt0">depression</span>.</li><li>Stressful childhood events such as trauma, the death of a loved one, bullying, and abuse.</li></ul>Which teens are at risk of <span class="qt0">depression</span>?<p><span class="qt0">Depression</span> can happen at any age, but often begins in the teens or early adulthood. Certain teens are at higher risk of <span class="qt0">depression</span>, such as those who:</p><ul><li>Have other mental health conditions, such as anxiety, eating disorders, and substance use</li><li>Have other diseases, such as diabetes, cancer, and heart disease</li><li>Have family members with mental illness</li><li>Have a dysfunctional family/family conflict</li><li>Have problems with friends or other kids at school</li><li>Have learning disabilities or attention deficit hyperactivity disorder (ADHD)</li><li>Have had trauma in childhood</li><li>Have low self-esteem, a pessimistic outlook, or poor coping skills</li><li>Are members of the LGBTQIA+ community, especially when their families are not supportive</li></ul>What are the symptoms of <span class="qt0">depression</span> in teens?<p>If you have <span class="qt0">depression</span>, you have one or more of these symptoms most of the time:</p><ul><li>Sadness</li><li>Feeling of emptiness</li><li>Hopelessness</li><li>Being angry, irritable, or frustrated, even at minor things</li></ul><p>You also may also have other symptoms, such as:</p><ul><li>No longer caring about things you used to enjoy</li><li>Changes in weight, such as losing weight when you are not dieting or gaining weight from eating too much</li><li>Changes in sleep, such as having trouble falling asleep or staying asleep, or sleeping much more than usual</li><li>Feeling restless or having trouble sitting still</li><li>Feeling very tired or not having energy</li><li>Feeling worthless or very guilty</li><li>Having trouble concentrating, remembering information, or making decisions</li><li>Thinking about dying or suicide</li></ul>How is <span class="qt0">depression</span> in teens diagnosed?<p>If you think you might be depressed, tell someone that you trust, such as your:</p><ul><li>Parents or guardian</li><li>Teacher or counselor</li><li>Doctor</li></ul><p>The next step is to see your doctor for a checkup. Your doctor can first make sure that you do not have another health problem that is causing your <span class="qt0">depression</span>. To do this, you may have a physical exam and lab tests.</p><p>If you don't have another health problem, you will get a psychological evaluation. Your doctor may do it, or you may be referred to a mental health professional to get one. You may be asked about things such as:</p><ul><li>Your thoughts and feelings</li><li>How you are doing at school</li><li>Any changes in your eating, sleeping, or energy level</li><li>Whether you are suicidal</li><li>Whether you use alcohol or drugs</li></ul>How is <span class="qt0">depression</span> in teens treated?<p>Effective treatments for <span class="qt0">depression</span> in teens include talk therapy, or a combination of talk therapy and medicines:</p><p>Talk therapy</p><p>Talk therapy, also called psychotherapy or counseling, can help you understand and manage your moods and feelings. It involves going to see a therapist, such as a psychiatrist, a psychologist, a social worker, or counselor. You can talk out your emotions to someone who understands and supports you. You can also learn how to stop thinking negatively and start to look at the positives in life. This will help you build confidence and feel better about yourself.</p><p>There are many different types of talk therapy. Certain types have been shown to help teens deal with <span class="qt0">depression</span>, including:</p><ul><li>Cognitive behavioral therapy (CBT), which helps you to identify and change negative and unhelpful thoughts. It also helps you build coping skills and change behavioral patterns.</li><li>Interpersonal therapy (IPT), which focuses on improving your relationships. It helps you understand and work through troubled relationships that may contribute to your <span class="qt0">depression</span>. IPT may help you change behaviors that are causing problems.  You also explore major issues that may add to your <span class="qt0">depression</span>, such as grief or life changes.</li></ul><p>Medicines</p><p>In some cases, your doctor will suggest medicines along with talk therapy. There are a few antidepressants that have been widely studied and proven to help teens. If you are taking medicine for <span class="qt0">depression</span>, it is important to see your doctor regularly.</p><p>It is also important to know that it will take some time for you to get relief from antidepressants:</p><ul><li>It can take 3 to 4 weeks until an antidepressant takes effect</li><li>You may have to try more than one antidepressant to find one that works for you</li><li>It can also take some time to find the right dose of an antidepressant</li></ul><p>In some cases, teenagers may have an increase in suicidal thoughts or behavior when taking antidepressants. This risk is higher in the first few weeks after starting the medicine and when the dose is changed. Make sure to tell your parents or guardian if you start feeling worse or have thoughts of hurting yourself.</p><p>You should not stop taking the antidepressants on your own. You need to work with your doctor to slowly and safely decrease the dose before you stop.</p><p>Programs for severe <span class="qt0">depression</span></p><p>Some teens who have severe <span class="qt0">depression</span> or are at risk of hurting themselves may need more intensive treatment. They may go into a psychiatric hospital or do a day program. Both offer counseling, group discussions, and activities with mental health professionals and other patients. Day programs may be full-day or half-day, and they often last for several weeks.</p></content>

      <content name="snippet">What is <span class="qt0">depression</span> in teens? <span class="qt0">Depression</span> in teens (ages 13-17) is a serious medical illness. It's more than just a feeling of being sad or "blue" for a few  ... </content>

    <document rank="3" url="https://medlineplus.gov/bipolardisorder.html">
      <content name="title">Bipolar Disorder</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Bi-polar Disorder</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Manic-Depressive Illness</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Manic <span class="qt0">depression</span></content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is bipolar disorder?<p>Bipolar disorder is a mood disorder that can cause intense mood swings:</p><ul><li>Sometimes you may feel extremely "up," elated, irritable, or energized. This is called a manic episode.</li><li>Other times you may feel "down," sad, indifferent, or hopeless. This is called a depressive episode.</li><li>You may have both manic and depressive symptoms together. This is called a mixed episode.</li></ul><p>Along with mood swings, bipolar disorder causes changes in behavior, energy levels, and activity levels.</p><p>Bipolar disorder used to be called other names, including manic <span class="qt0">depression</span> and manic-<span class="qt1">depressive disorder</span>.</p>What are the types of bipolar disorder?<p>There are three main types of bipolar disorder:</p><ul><li>Bipolar I disorder involves manic episodes that last at least 7 days or manic symptoms so severe that you need immediate hospital care. Depressive episodes are also common. Those often last at least two weeks. This type of bipolar disorder can also involve mixed episodes.</li><li>Bipolar II disorder involves depressive episodes. But instead of full-blown manic episodes, there are episodes of hypomania. Hypomania is a less severe version of mania.</li><li>Cyclothymic disorder, or cyclothymia, also involves hypomanic and depressive symptoms. But they are not as intense or as long-lasting as hypomanic or depressive episodes. The symptoms usually last for at least two years in adults and for one year in children and teenagers.</li></ul><p>With any of these types, having four or more episodes of mania or <span class="qt0">depression</span> in a year is called "rapid cycling."</p>What causes bipolar disorder?<p>The exact cause of bipolar disorder is unknown. Several factors likely play a role in the disorder. They include genetics, brain structure and function, and your environment.</p>Who is at risk for bipolar disorder?<p>You are at higher risk for bipolar disorder if you have a close relative who has it. Going through trauma or stressful life events may raise this risk even more.</p>What are the symptoms of bipolar disorder?<p>The symptoms of bipolar disorder can vary. But they involve mood swings known as mood episodes:</p><ul><li>The symptoms of a manic episode can include
     <ul><li>Feeling very up, high, or elated</li><li>Feeling jumpy or wired, more active than usual</li><li>Having a very short temper or seeming extremely irritable</li><li>Having racing thoughts and talking very fast</li><li>Needing less sleep</li><li>Feeling like you are unusually important, talented, or powerful</li><li>Do risky things that show poor judgment, such as eating and drinking too much, spending or giving away a lot of money, or having reckless sex</li></ul> 
</li><li>The symptoms of a depressive episode can include
    <ul><li>Feeling very sad, hopeless, or worthless</li><li>Feeling lonely or isolating yourself from others</li><li>Talking very slowly, feeling like you have nothing to say, or forgetting a lot</li><li>Having little energy</li><li>Sleeping too much</li><li>Eating too much or too little</li><li>Lack of interest in your usual activities and being unable to do even simple things</li><li>Thinking about death or suicide</li></ul>
</li><li>The symptoms of a mixed episode include both manic and depressive symptoms together. For example, you may feel very sad, empty, or hopeless, while at the same time feeling extremely energized.</li></ul><p>Some people with bipolar disorder may have milder symptoms. For example, you may have hypomania instead of mania. With hypomania, you may feel very good and find that you can get a lot done. You may not feel like anything is wrong. But your family and friends may notice your mood swings and changes in activity levels. They may realize that your behavior is unusual for you. After the hypomania, you might have severe <span class="qt0">depression</span>.</p><p>Your mood episodes may last a week or two or sometimes longer. During an episode, symptoms usually occur every day for most of the day.</p>How is bipolar disorder diagnosed?<p>To diagnose bipolar disorder, your health care provider may use many tools:</p><ul><li>A physical exam</li><li>A medical history, which will include asking about your symptoms, lifetime history, experiences, and family history</li><li>Medical tests to rule out other conditions</li><li>A mental health evaluation. Your provider may do the evaluation or may refer you to a mental health specialist to get one.</li></ul>What are the treatments for bipolar disorder?<p>Treatment can help many people, including those with the most severe forms of bipolar disorder. The main treatments for bipolar disorder include medicines, psychotherapy, or both:</p><ul><li>Medicines can help control the symptoms of bipolar disorder. You may need to try several different medicines to find which one works best for you. Some people need to take more than one medicine. It's important to take your medicine consistently. Don't stop taking it without first talking with your provider.  Contact your provider if you have any concerns about side effects from the medicines.</li><li>Psychotherapy (talk therapy) can help you recognize and change troubling emotions, thoughts, and behaviors. It can give you and your family support, education, skills, and coping strategies. There are several different types of psychotherapy that may help with bipolar disorder.</li><li>Other treatment options include:
    <ul><li>Electroconvulsive therapy (ECT), a brain stimulation procedure that can help relieve symptoms. It uses a mild electric current and is done while you are under general anesthesia. ECT is most often used for severe bipolar disorder that is not getting better with other treatments. It may also be used when someone needs a treatment that will work more quickly than medicines. This might be when a person has a high risk of suicide or is catatonic (unresponsive).</li><li>Repetitive transcranial magnetic stimulation (rTMS), a brain stimulation procedure that uses magnetic waves to relieve <span class="qt0">depression</span>. It is not as powerful as ECT, but with rTMS, you don't need general anesthesia. It also has a low risk of negative effects on your memory and thinking.
  </li><li>Light therapy has been shown to be effective for seasonal affective disorder (SAD). Many people with bipolar disorder also find that their <span class="qt0">depression</span> gets worse during certain seasons, usually in the fall and winter. Light therapy may help with their symptoms.</li><li>Healthy lifestyle changes, such as getting regular exercise, having a consistent sleep schedule, and keeping a mood journal, can also help with your symptoms.</li></ul>    
</li></ul><p>Bipolar disorder is a lifelong illness. But long-term, ongoing treatment can help manage your symptoms and enable you to live a healthy, successful life.</p><p>NIH: National Institute of Mental Health</p></content>

    <document rank="4" url="https://medlineplus.gov/seasonalaffectivedisorder.html">
      <content name="title">Seasonal Affective Disorder</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">SAD</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Seasonal <span class="qt0">depression</span></content>
      <content name="altTitle">Seasonal mood disorder</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>Seasonal affective disorder (SAD) is a type of <span class="qt0">depression</span> that comes and goes with the seasons. It usually starts in the late fall and early winter and goes away during the spring and summer. Some people do have episodes of <span class="qt0">depression</span> that start in the spring or summer, but that is a lot less common. Symptoms of SAD may include:</p><ul><li>Sadness</li><li>Gloomy outlook</li><li>Feeling hopeless, worthless, and irritable</li><li>Loss of interest or pleasure in activities you used to enjoy</li><li>Low energy</li><li>Difficulty sleeping or oversleeping</li><li>Carbohydrate cravings and weight gain</li><li>Thoughts of death or suicide</li></ul><p>SAD is more common in women, young people, and those who live far from the equator. You are also more likely to have SAD if you or your family members have <span class="qt0">depression</span>.</p><p>The exact causes of SAD are unknown. Researchers have found that people with SAD may have an imbalance of serotonin, a brain chemical that affects your mood. Their bodies also make too much melatonin, a hormone that regulates sleep, and not enough vitamin D.</p><p>The main treatment for SAD is light therapy. The idea behind light therapy is to replace the sunshine that you miss during the fall and winter months. You sit in front of a light therapy box every morning to get daily exposure to bright, artificial light. But some people with SAD do not respond to light therapy alone. Antidepressant medicines and talk therapy can reduce SAD symptoms, either alone or combined with light therapy.</p><p>NIH: National Institute of Mental Health</p></content>

    <document rank="5" url="https://medlineplus.gov/antidepressants.html">
      <content name="title">Antidepressants</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">SNRI </content>
      <content name="altTitle">SSRIs</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Tricyclic antidepressants</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What are antidepressants?<p>Antidepressants are prescription medicines to treat <span class="qt0">depression. Depression</span> is more than feeling a little sad or "blue" for a few days. It's a very common, serious medical illness that affects your mood and general mental health It can make you feel tired, hopeless, worried, or fearful. It can change your thinking, sleeping, and eating. <span class="qt0">Depression</span> may make some people think about ending their lives.</p><p>But antidepressants can help many people who have <span class="qt0">depression</span>. Researchers think antidepressants may help improve the way your brain uses certain chemicals that control mood or stress.</p>Are antidepressants used for other conditions?<p>A health care provider may prescribe antidepressants for anxiety, chronic pain, or insomnia. Sometimes providers also prescribe antidepressants for other conditions.</p>What are the different types of antidepressants?<p>There are many types of antidepressants. Each one works differently. Providers usually prescribe newer antidepressants first because they don't cause as many side effects as older types. They also seem to help more kinds of <span class="qt0">depression</span> and anxiety problems.</p><p>Most of the newer antidepressants belong to one of these three groups:</p><ul><li>Selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs)</li><li>Serotonin and norepinephrine reuptake inhibitors (SNRIs)</li><li>Atypical antidepressants, which are newer antidepressants that don't fit into the other groups</li></ul><p>If these antidepressants don't help, your provider might suggest one of the older antidepressants. The older types include tricyclic antidepressants (TCAs), tetracyclics, and monoamine oxidase inhibitors (MAOIs). Even though these antidepressants may cause more serious side effects, the benefits may outweigh the risks for some people.</p>Which type of antidepressant is right for me?<p>Our bodies and brains all work differently. That means one antidepressant won't work for everyone.  You may need to try two or more medicines before you find one that works for you.</p><p>Your provider will work with you to choose the best option to try first. You'll consider questions such as:</p><ul><li>Which symptoms bother you most? Some antidepressants may do a better job helping specific symptoms, such as trouble sleeping.</li><li>What other medicines and supplements do you take? Some antidepressants can cause problems if you take them with certain medicines and herbs.</li><li>Did a certain antidepressant work well for a close relative? An antidepressant that helped a parent, brother, or sister could be a good choice for you, too.</li><li>Do you have other health conditions? Certain antidepressants can make some other conditions better or worse. Any other conditions that you have will be part of choosing your <span class="qt0">depression</span> treatment.</li><li>Are you pregnant, planning for pregnancy, or breastfeeding?  If so, your provider will help you find a way to treat your <span class="qt0">depression</span> that's safe for you and your baby.</li></ul>How long do antidepressants take to work?<p>Antidepressants usually take 4 to 8 weeks to work, so you'll need to be patient. You may notice that some problems, such as sleeping and eating, get better before your mood improves. That's a good sign. You may just need to give the medicine a little more time to do its job.</p><p>Sometimes an antidepressant helps at first, but symptoms return while you're still taking it. But there's usually another one you can try. To get more relief from <span class="qt0">depression</span>, your provider may suggest combining two antidepressants, using another kind of medicine with an antidepressant, or adding talk therapy or other approaches to improve your mental health.</p>How long will I need to take an antidepressant?<p>When an antidepressant starts to work, you and your provider can decide how long you need to stay on it. The typical length of treatment is 6 to 12 months, but some people may stay on antidepressants for much longer.</p>What are the side effects of antidepressants?<p>Not everyone has side effects from antidepressants. But if you do have them, they're usually mild and may get better over time as your body gets used to the new medicine.</p><p>The most common side effects from antidepressants include:</p><ul><li>Nausea and vomiting</li><li>Weight gain</li><li>Diarrhea</li><li>Sleepiness</li><li>Sexual problems, such as a lack of desire or ability to have sex</li></ul><p>When thinking about side effects, it's important to remember that there are also risks from not treating <span class="qt0">depression</span>. Your provider can help you think through the pros and cons of all your options.</p><p>If you have any side effects from antidepressants, your provider may suggest ways to manage them while you wait to see if the antidepressant will work. If the side effects bother you too much, you may need to change antidepressants. But you should never change your dose or stop taking an antidepressant on your own.</p><p>If you have serious problems or notice any changes that worry you, such as new or worsening symptoms, unusual changes in your mood, or you start acting differently, call your provider right away.</p><p>In some cases, children, teenagers, and young adults under 25 may be more likely to think about hurting or killing themselves when starting antidepressants or when the dose is changed. Get medical help right away if this happens.</p>What can I do to take antidepressants safely?<p>Antidepressants are generally safe when you use them correctly:</p><ul><li>Tell your provider about everything you take. That includes medicines, herbs, supplements, and over-the-counter medicines you take, such as pain relievers and decongestants. Be honest about recreational drugs and alcohol, too.</li><li>Try to get all your medicines from the same pharmacy. That way the pharmacist can warn you and your provider if you take medicines that may cause problems when used together.</li><li>Follow all instructions about how to take your medicine.</li><li>Talk with your provider if side effects bother you.</li><li>Never stop taking antidepressants without your provider's help. Stopping too fast may make <span class="qt0">depression</span> come back. You could even make your condition worse. To stop antidepressants safely, you need to give your body time to get used to being without the medicine. Your provider can tell you the safest way to go off an antidepressant.</li></ul><p>NIH: National Institute of Mental Health</p></content>
