    <document rank="21" url="https://medlineplus.gov/traumaticbraininjury.html">
      <content name="title">Traumatic Brain Injury</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">TBI</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Acquired brain injury</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is traumatic brain injury (TBI)?<p>Traumatic brain injury (TBI) is a sudden injury that causes damage to the brain. It may happen when there is a blow, bump, or jolt to the head. This is a closed head injury. A TBI can also happen when an object penetrates the skull. This is a penetrating injury.</p><p>Symptoms of a TBI can be mild, moderate, or severe. Concussions are a type of mild TBI. The effects of a concussion can sometimes be serious, but most people completely recover in time. More severe TBI can lead to serious physical and psychological symptoms, coma, and even death.</p>What causes traumatic brain injury (TBI)?<p>The main causes of TBI depend on the type of head injury:</p><ul><li>Some of the common causes of a closed head injury include 
	<ul><li>Falls. This is the most common cause in adults age 65 and older.</li><li>Motor vehicle crashes. This is the most common cause in young adults.</li><li>Sports injuries</li><li>Being struck by an object</li><li>Child abuse. This is the most common cause in children under age 4.</li><li>Blast injuries due to explosions</li></ul>
</li><li>Some of the common causes of a penetrating injury include 
	<ul><li>Being hit by a bullet or shrapnel</li><li>Being hit by a weapon such as a hammer, knife, or baseball bat</li><li>A head injury that causes a bone fragment to penetrate the skull</li></ul>
</li></ul><p>Some accidents such as explosions, natural disasters, or other extreme events can cause both closed and penetrating TBI in the same person.</p>Who is at risk for traumatic brain injury (TBI)?<p>Certain groups are at higher risk of TBI:</p><ul><li>Men are more likely to get a TBI than women. They are also more likely to have serious TBI.</li><li>Adults aged 65 and older are at the greatest risk for being hospitalized and dying from a TBI</li></ul>What are the symptoms of traumatic brain injury (TBI)?<p>The symptoms of TBI depend on the type of injury and how serious the brain damage is.</p><p>The symptoms of mild TBI can include:</p><ul><li>A brief loss of consciousness in some cases. However, many people with mild TBI remain conscious after the injury.</li><li>Headache</li><li>Confusion</li><li>Lightheadedness</li><li>Dizziness</li><li>Blurred vision or tired eyes</li><li>Ringing in the ears</li><li>Bad taste in the mouth</li><li>Fatigue or lethargy</li><li>A change in sleep patterns</li><li>Behavioral or mood changes</li><li>Trouble with memory, concentration, attention, or thinking</li></ul><p>If you have a moderate or severe TBI, you may have those same symptoms. You may also have other symptoms such as:</p><ul><li>A headache that gets worse or does not go away</li><li>Repeated vomiting or nausea</li><li>Convulsions or seizures</li><li>Not being able to wake up from sleep</li><li>Larger than normal pupil (dark center) of one or both eyes. This is called dilation of the pupil.</li><li>Slurred speech</li><li>Weakness or numbness in the arms and legs</li><li>Loss of coordination</li><li>Increased confusion, restlessness, or agitation</li></ul>How is traumatic brain injury (TBI) diagnosed?<p>If you have a head injury or other trauma that may have caused a TBI, you need to get medical care as soon as possible. To make a diagnosis, your health care provider:</p><ul><li>Will ask about your symptoms and the details of your injury</li><li>Will do a neurologic exam</li><li>May do imaging tests, such as a CT scan or MRI</li><li>May use a tool such as the Glasgow coma scale to determine how severe the TBI is. This scale measures your ability to open your eyes, speak, and move.</li><li>May do neuropsychological tests to check how your brain is functioning</li></ul>What are the treatments for traumatic brain injury (TBI)?<p>The treatments for TBI depend on many factors, including the size, severity, and location of the brain injury.</p><p>For mild TBI, the main treatment is rest. If you have a headache, you can try taking over-the-counter pain relievers. It is important to follow your health care provider's instructions for complete rest and a gradual return to your normal activities. If you start doing too much too soon, it may take longer to recover. Contact your provider if your symptoms are not getting better or if you have new symptoms.</p><p>For moderate to severe TBI, the first thing health care providers will do is stabilize you to prevent further injury. They will manage your blood pressure, check the pressure inside your skull, and make sure that there is enough blood and oxygen getting to your brain.</p><p>Once you are stable, the treatments may include:</p><ul><li>Surgery to reduce additional damage to your brain, for example to
	<ul><li>Remove hematomas (clotted blood)</li><li>Get rid of damaged or dead brain tissue</li><li>Repair skull fractures</li><li>Relieve pressure in the skull</li></ul>
</li><li>Medicines to treat the symptoms of TBI and to lower some of the risks associated with it, such as
	<ul><li>Anti-anxiety medication to lessen feelings of nervousness and fear</li><li>Anticoagulants to prevent blood clots</li><li>Anticonvulsants to prevent seizures</li><li>Antidepressants to treat symptoms of <span class="qt0">depression</span> and mood instability</li><li>Muscle relaxants to reduce muscle spasms</li><li>Stimulants to increase alertness and attention</li></ul>
</li><li>Rehabilitation therapies, which can include therapies for physical, emotional, and cognitive difficulties:
	<ul><li>Physical therapy, to build physical strength, coordination, and flexibility</li><li>Occupational therapy, to help you learn or relearn how to perform daily tasks, such as getting dressed, cooking, and bathing</li><li>Speech therapy, to help you to with speech and other communication skills and treat swallowing disorders</li><li>Psychological counseling, to help you learn coping skills, work on relationships, and improve your emotional well-being</li><li>Vocational counseling, which focuses on your ability to return to work and deal with workplace challenges</li><li>Cognitive therapy, to improve your memory, attention, perception, learning, planning, and judgment</li></ul></li></ul><p>Some people with TBI may have permanent disabilities. A TBI can also put you at risk for other health problems such as anxiety, <span class="qt0">depression</span>, and post-traumatic stress disorder. Treating these problems can improve your quality of life.</p>Can traumatic brain injury (TBI) be prevented?<p>There are steps you can take to prevent head injuries and TBIs:</p><ul><li>Always wear your seatbelt and use car seats and booster seats for children</li><li>Never drive under the influence of drugs or alcohol</li><li>Wear a properly fitting helmet when riding a bicycle, skateboarding, and playing sports like hockey and football</li><li>Prevent falls by
	<ul><li>Making your house safer. For example, you can install railings on the stairs and grab bars in the tub, get rid of tripping hazards, and use window guards and stair safety gates for young children.</li><li>Improving your balance and strength with regular physical activity</li></ul>
</li></ul></content>

Treating these problems  ... </content>

    <document rank="22" url="https://medlineplus.gov/veteransandmilitaryhealth.html">
      <content name="title">Veterans and Military Health</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Agent Orange</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Gulf War Syndrome</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Military Health</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Persian Gulf War</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Veterans Health</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>Military service members and veterans have made sacrifices to our country, and they may face lots of different health issues. Some of the most common health problems they may have include chronic pain, sleep disorders, and mental health disorders.</p><p>But they may also face some different health risks than civilians. Serving to protect our country is a special job, and along with it comes some special risks and job hazards. These include different types of injuries and health problems from exposures.</p>What types of injuries do service members face?<p>During their service, members are at risk for various injuries. These injuries can happen during training or while in combat. Sometimes the injuries are life-threatening or serious enough to cause disability. Others may not be as serious, but they may be painful and can affect daily life.</p><p>Some of the specific types of injuries that service members may face include:</p><ul><li>Sprains and strains, especially in the ankles and knees. These injuries are often caused by exercise and running.</li><li>Back and shoulder injuries, often from lifting and carrying.</li><li>Tinnitus and hearing loss, typically from exposure to noise.</li><li>Head injuries and traumatic brain injuries (TBIs).</li><li>Shrapnel and gunshot wounds.</li><li>Lost limbs.</li></ul>What other special health problems do service members face?<p>There may also be a risk of health problems from exposure to environmental hazards, such as contaminated water, chemicals, infections, and burn pits. Sometimes the health problems caused by exposures don't develop until years later.</p><p>Some service members experience military sexual trauma (MST). This includes sexual assault and sexual harassment.</p>What mental health issues do service members and veterans face?<p>Being in combat and being separated from your family can be stressful. So can readjusting to civilian life after the military. All of this stress can put service members and veterans at risk for mental health problems, including:</p><ul><li><span class="qt0">Depression</span></li><li>Post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD)</li><li>Anxiety</li><li>Alcohol and drug use disorders</li></ul><p>There is also a risk of suicide. Veterans who are in crisis can get help from the Veteran's Crisis line by:</p><ul><li>Calling 988 and then pressing 1</li><li>Texting to 838255</li><li>Chatting with them online</li></ul>What health care services are available to service members and veterans?<p>There are health care services especially for service members and veterans through:</p><ul><li>TRICARE, which is the Department of Defense's health care program. It has insurance plans and other services for uniformed service members, retirees, and their families around the world.</li><li>The Veterans Health Administration, which is part of the Department of Veterans Affairs (VA). It provides medical and social support services to eligible veterans.</li><li>The VA 's expanded health care and benefits for veterans exposed to burn pits, Agent Orange, and other toxic substances.</li></ul><p>To better understand and treat the health needs of veterans in the future, the VA created the Million Veteran Program (MVP). This research program is looking at how genes, lifestyle, military experiences, and exposures affect health and wellness in veterans.</p></content>
      <content name="mesh">Prisoners of War</content>
      <content name="mesh">War Exposure</content>

    <document rank="23" url="https://medlineplus.gov/alcoholusedisorderaud.html">
      <content name="title">Alcohol Use Disorder (AUD)</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Alcohol Abuse</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Alcohol Dependence</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Alcohol Withdrawal</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Alcoholism</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Binge Drinking</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Drinking</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Substance Use</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is alcohol use disorder (AUD)?<p>For most adults, moderate alcohol use is probably not harmful. Your risk of developing an alcohol use disorder (AUD) depends on how much, how often, and how quickly you drink alcohol. Alcohol misuse means that drinking causes distress and harm. Over time alcohol misuse can increase your risk of AUD.</p><p>AUD can range from mild to severe, depending on the symptoms. Severe AUD is sometimes called alcoholism or alcohol dependence.</p><p>AUD is a disease that causes:</p><ul><li>Craving - a strong need to drink</li><li>Loss of control - not being able to stop drinking once you've started</li><li>Negative emotional state - feeling anxious and irritable when you are not drinking</li></ul>What is binge drinking?<p>Binge drinking is a type of alcohol misuse. It means drinking so much at once that your blood alcohol concentration (BAC) level is 0.08% or more. For a man, this usually happens after having 5 or more drinks within a few hours. For a woman, it is after about 4 or more drinks within a few hours. Not everyone who binge drinks has an AUD, but they are at higher risk for getting one.</p>What are the dangers of too much alcohol?<p>Too much alcohol is dangerous. Heavy drinking can increase the risk of certain cancers. It may lead to liver diseases, such as fatty liver disease and cirrhosis. It can also cause damage to the brain and other organs. Drinking during pregnancy can harm your fetus. Alcohol also increases the risk of death from car crashes, injuries, homicide, and suicide.</p>How do I know if I have an alcohol use disorder (AUD)?<p>You may have an AUD if you can answer yes to two or more of these questions:</p><p>In the past year, have you:</p><ul><li>Ended up drinking more or for a longer time than you had planned to?</li><li>Wanted to cut down or stop drinking, or tried to, but couldn't?</li><li>Spent a lot of your time drinking or recovering from drinking?</li><li>Felt a strong need to drink?</li><li>Found that drinking - or being sick from drinking - often interfered with your family life, job, or school?</li><li>Kept drinking even though it was causing trouble with your family or friends?</li><li>Given up or cut back on activities that you enjoyed just so you could drink?</li><li>Gotten into dangerous situations while drinking or after drinking? Some examples are driving drunk and having unsafe sex.</li><li>Kept drinking even though it was making you feel depressed or anxious? Or when it was adding to another health problem?</li><li>Had to drink more and more to feel the effects of the alcohol?</li><li>Had withdrawal symptoms when the alcohol was wearing off? They include trouble sleeping, shakiness, irritability, anxiety, <span class="qt0">depression</span>, restlessness, nausea, and sweating. In severe cases, you could have a fever, seizures, or hallucinations.</li></ul><p>If you have any of these symptoms, your drinking may already be a cause for concern. The more symptoms you have, the more serious the problem is.</p>What should I do if I think that I might have an alcohol use disorder (AUD)?<p>If you think you might have an AUD, see your health care provider for an evaluation. Your provider can help make a treatment plan, prescribe medicines, and if needed, give you treatment referrals.</p><p>NIH: National Institute on Alcohol Abuse and Alcoholism</p></content>

    <document rank="24" url="https://medlineplus.gov/alzheimerscaregivers.html">
      <content name="title">Alzheimer's Caregivers</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Caregivers for Alzheimer's Disease</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>A caregiver gives care to someone who needs help taking care of themselves. It can be rewarding. It may help to strengthen connections to a loved one. You may feel fulfillment from helping someone else. But sometimes caregiving can be stressful and even overwhelming. This can be especially true when caring for someone with Alzheimer's disease (AD).</p><p>AD is an illness that changes the brain. It causes people to lose the ability to remember, think, and use good judgment. They also have trouble taking care of themselves. Over time, as the disease gets worse, they will need more and more help. As a caregiver, it is important for you to learn about AD. You will want to know what happens to the person during the different stages of the disease. This can help you plan for the future, so that you will have all of the resources you will need to be able to take care of your loved one.</p><p>As a caregiver for someone with AD, your responsibilities can include:</p><ul><li>Getting your loved one's health, legal, and financial affairs in order. If possible, include them in the planning while they can still make decisions. Later you will need to take over managing their finances and paying their bills.
</li><li>Evaluating their house and making sure it's safe for their needs</li><li>Monitoring their ability to drive. You may want to hire a driving specialist who can test their driving skills. When it is no longer safe for your loved one to drive, you need to make sure that they stop.</li><li>Encouraging your loved one to get some physical activity. Exercising together may make it more fun for them.</li><li>Making sure that your loved one has a healthy diet</li><li>Helping with daily tasks like bathing, eating, or taking medicine</li><li>Doing housework and cooking</li><li>Running errands such as shopping for food and clothes</li><li>Driving them to appointments</li><li>Providing company and emotional support</li><li>Arranging medical care and making health decisions</li></ul><p>As you care for your loved one with AD, don't ignore your own needs. Caregiving can be stressful, and you need to take care of your own physical and mental health.</p><p>At some point, you will not be able to do everything on your own. Make sure that you get help when you need it. There are many different services available, including:</p><ul><li>Home care services</li><li>Adult day care services</li><li>Respite services, which provide short-term care for the person with AD</li><li>Federal and state government programs that can provide financial support and services</li><li>Assisted living facilities</li><li>Nursing homes, some of which have special memory care units for people with AD</li><li>Palliative and hospice care</li></ul><p>You might consider hiring a geriatric care manager. They are specially trained professionals who can help you to find the right services for your needs.</p><p>NIH: National Institute on Aging</p></content>

    <document rank="0" url="https://medlineplus.gov/anxiety.html">
      <content name="title"><span class="qt0">Anxiety</span></content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Generalized <span class="qt0">Anxiety</span> Disorder</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is <span class="qt0">anxiety</span>?<p><span class="qt0">Anxiety</span> is a feeling of fear, dread, and uneasiness. It might cause you to sweat, feel restless and <span class="qt0">tense</span>, and have a rapid heartbeat.   It can be a normal reaction to <span class="qt0">stress</span>. For example, you might feel <span class="qt0">anxious</span> when faced with a difficult problem at work, before taking a test, or before making an important decision. It can help you to cope. The <span class="qt0">anxiety</span> may give you a boost of energy or help you focus. But for people with <span class="qt0">anxiety</span> disorders, the fear is not temporary and can be overwhelming.</p>What are <span class="qt0">anxiety</span> disorders?<p><span class="qt0">Anxiety</span> disorders are conditions in which you have <span class="qt0">anxiety</span> that does not go away and can get worse over time. The symptoms can interfere with daily activities such as job performance, schoolwork, and relationships.</p>What are the types of <span class="qt0">anxiety</span> disorders?<p>There are several types of <span class="qt0">anxiety</span> disorders, including: </p><ul><li>Generalized <span class="qt0">anxiety</span> disorder (GAD). People with GAD worry about ordinary issues such as health, money, work, and family. But their worries are excessive, and they have them almost every day for at least 6 months.</li><li>Panic disorder. People with panic disorder have panic attacks. These are sudden, repeated periods of intense fear when there is no danger. The attacks come on quickly and can last several minutes or more.</li><li>Phobias. People with phobias have an intense fear of something that poses little or no actual danger. Their fear may be about spiders, flying, going to crowded places, or being in social situations (known as social <span class="qt0">anxiety</span>).</li></ul>What causes <span class="qt0">anxiety</span> disorders?<p>The cause of <span class="qt0">anxiety</span> is unknown. Factors such as genetics, brain biology and chemistry, <span class="qt0">stress</span>, and your environment may play a role.</p>Who is at risk for <span class="qt0">anxiety</span> disorders?<p>The risk factors for the different types of <span class="qt0">anxiety</span> disorders can vary. For example, GAD and phobias are more common in women, but social <span class="qt0">anxiety</span> affects men and women equally. There are some general risk factors for all types of <span class="qt0">anxiety</span> disorders, including:</p><ul><li>Certain personality traits, such as being shy or withdrawn when you are in new situations or meeting new people</li><li>Traumatic events in early childhood or adulthood</li><li>Family history of <span class="qt0">anxiety</span> or other mental disorders</li><li>Some physical health conditions, such as thyroid problems or arrhythmia</li></ul>What are the symptoms of <span class="qt0">anxiety</span> disorders?<p>The different types of <span class="qt0">anxiety</span> disorders can have different symptoms. But they all have a combination of:</p><ul><li><span class="qt0">Anxious</span> thoughts or beliefs that are hard to control. They make you feel restless and <span class="qt0">tense</span> and interfere with your daily life. They do not go away and can get worse over time.</li><li>Physical symptoms, such as a pounding or rapid heartbeat, unexplained aches and pains, dizziness, and shortness of breath</li><li>Changes in behavior, such as avoiding everyday activities you used to do</li></ul><p>Using caffeine, other substances, and certain medicines can make your symptoms worse.</p>How are <span class="qt0">anxiety</span> disorders diagnosed?<p>To diagnose <span class="qt0">anxiety</span> disorders, your health care provider will ask about your symptoms and medical history. You may also have a physical exam and lab tests to make sure that a different health problem is not the cause of your symptoms.</p><p>If you don't have another health problem, you will get a psychological evaluation. Your provider may do it, or you may be referred to a mental health professional to get one.</p>What are the treatments for <span class="qt0">anxiety</span> disorders?<p>The main treatments for <span class="qt0">anxiety</span> disorders are psychotherapy (talk therapy), medicines, or both:</p><ul><li>Psychotherapy can help you recognize and change troubling emotions, thoughts, and behaviors. To be effective, it should focus on your specific <span class="qt0">anxieties</span> and needs. There are many types; some of the types used for <span class="qt0">anxiety</span> disorders include:
  <ul><li>Cognitive behavioral therapy (CBT), which teaches you different ways of thinking and behaving. It can help you change how you react to the things that cause you to feel fear and <span class="qt0">anxiety</span>. It may include exposure therapy. This therapy focuses on having you confront your fears so that you will be able to do the things that you had been avoiding.</li><li>Acceptance and commitment therapy may help with some <span class="qt0">anxiety</span> disorders. It uses strategies such as mindfulness and goal setting to reduce discomfort and <span class="qt0">anxiety</span>.</li></ul> 
</li><li>Medicines to treat <span class="qt0">anxiety</span> disorders include anti-<span class="qt0">anxiety</span> medicines and certain antidepressants. Some types of medicines may work better for specific types of <span class="qt0">anxiety</span> disorders. You should work closely with your provider to identify which medicine is best for you. You may need to try more than one medicine before you can find the right one.</li></ul><p>Support groups and <span class="qt0">stress</span> management techniques may also be helpful.</p><p>NIH: National Institute of Mental Health</p></content>
      <content name="mesh"><span class="qt0">Anxiety</span> Disorders</content>

    <document rank="1" url="https://medlineplus.gov/phobias.html">
      <content name="title">Phobias</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Agoraphobia</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Social <span class="qt0">Anxiety</span> Disorder</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Social Phobia</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>A phobia is a type of <span class="qt0">anxiety</span> disorder.  It is a strong, irrational fear of something that poses little or no real danger.</p><p>There are many specific phobias. Acrophobia is a fear of heights. Agoraphobia is a fear of public places, and claustrophobia is a fear of closed-in places. If you become <span class="qt0">anxious</span> and extremely self-conscious in everyday social situations, you could have a social phobia. Other common phobias involve tunnels, highway driving, water, flying, animals and blood. </p><p>People with phobias try to avoid what they are afraid of. If they cannot, they may experience:</p><ul><li>Panic and fear</li><li>Rapid heartbeat</li><li>Shortness of breath</li><li>Trembling</li><li>A strong desire to get away</li></ul><p>Phobias usually start in children or teens, and continue into adulthood. The causes of specific phobias are not known, but they sometimes run in families. </p><p>Treatment helps most people with phobias. Options include medicines, therapy or both.</p><p>NIH: National Institute of Mental Health</p></content>
      <content name="mesh">Phobic Disorders</content>
      <content name="groupName">Mental Health and Behavior</content>
      <content name="snippet"> A phobia is a type of <span class="qt0">anxiety</span> disorder.  It is a strong, irrational fear of something that poses little or no real danger. There are many specific phobias. Acrophobia  ... </content>

    <document rank="2" url="https://medlineplus.gov/obsessivecompulsivedisorder.html">
      <content name="title">Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Hoarding</content>
      <content name="altTitle">OCD</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is obsessive-compulsive disorder (OCD)?<p>Obsessive-compulsive disorder (OCD) is a mental disorder in which you have thoughts (obsessions) and rituals (compulsions) over and over. They interfere with your life, but you cannot control or stop them.</p>What causes obsessive-compulsive disorder (OCD)?<p>The cause of obsessive-compulsive disorder (OCD) is unknown. Factors such as genetics, brain biology and chemistry, and your environment may play a role.</p>Who is at risk for obsessive-compulsive disorder (OCD)?<p>Obsessive-compulsive disorder (OCD) usually begins when you are a teen or young adult. Boys often develop OCD at a younger age than girls.</p><p>Risk factors for OCD include:</p><ul><li>Family history. People with a first-degree relative (such as a parent, sibling, or child) who has OCD are at higher risk. This is especially true if the relative developed OCD as a child or teen.</li><li>Brain structure and functioning. Imaging studies have shown that people with OCD have differences in certain parts of the brain. Researchers need to do more studies to understand the connection between the brain differences and OCD.</li><li>Childhood trauma, such as child abuse. Some studies have found a link between trauma in childhood and OCD. More research is needed to understand this relationship better.</li></ul><p>In some cases, children may develop OCD or OCD symptoms following a streptococcal infection. This is called Pediatric Autoimmune Neuropsychiatric Disorders Associated with Streptococcal Infections (PANDAS).</p>What are the symptoms of obsessive-compulsive disorder (OCD)?<p>People with OCD may have symptoms of obsessions, compulsions, or both:</p><ul><li>Obsessions are repeated thoughts, urges, or mental images that cause <span class="qt0">anxiety</span>. They may involve things such as
 <ul><li>Fear of germs or contamination</li><li>Fear of losing or misplacing something</li><li>Worries about harm coming towards yourself or others</li><li>Unwanted forbidden thoughts involving sex or religion</li><li>Aggressive thoughts towards yourself or others</li><li>Needing things lined up exactly or arranged in a particular, precise way</li></ul></li><li>Compulsions are behaviors that you feel like you need to do over and over to try to reduce your <span class="qt0">anxiety</span> or stop the obsessive thoughts. Some common compulsions include
 <ul><li>Excessive cleaning and/or handwashing</li><li>Repeatedly checking on things, such as whether the door is locked or the oven is off</li><li>Compulsive counting</li><li>Ordering and arranging things in a particular, precise way</li></ul></li></ul><p>Some people with OCD also have Tourette syndrome or another tic disorder. Tics are sudden twitches, movements, or sounds that people do over and over. People who have tics cannot stop their body from doing these things.</p>How is obsessive-compulsive disorder (OCD) diagnosed?<p>The first step is to talk with your health care provider about your symptoms. It's important to check whether a physical problem is causing your symptoms. So your provider will do a physical exam and will ask you about your medical history. If  your symptoms do not seem to be caused by a physical problem, your provider may do an OCD test or may refer you to a mental health specialist for further evaluation or treatment.</p><p>Obsessive-compulsive disorder (OCD) can sometimes be hard to diagnose. Its symptoms are like those of other mental disorders, such as <span class="qt0">anxiety</span> disorders. It is also possible to have both OCD and another mental disorder.</p><p>Not everyone who has obsessions or compulsions has OCD. Your symptoms would usually be considered OCD when you:</p><ul><li>Can't control your thoughts or behaviors, even when you know that they are excessive</li><li>Spend at least 1 hour a day on these thoughts or behaviors</li><li>Don't get pleasure when performing the behaviors. But doing them may briefly give you relief from the <span class="qt0">anxiety</span>.</li><li>Have significant problems in your daily life because of these thoughts or behaviors</li></ul>What are the treatments for obsessive-compulsive disorder (OCD)?<p>The main treatments for obsessive-compulsive disorder (OCD) are cognitive behavioral therapy, medicines, or both:</p><ul><li>Cognitive behavioral therapy (CBT) is a type of psychotherapy. It teaches you different ways of thinking, behaving, and reacting to your obsessions and compulsions. 
  <ul><li>Exposure and Response Prevention (ERP) s a specific type of CBT. ERP involves gradually exposing you to your fears or obsessions. You learn healthy ways to deal with the <span class="qt0">anxiety</span> they cause.</li></ul>
</li><li>Medicines for OCD include certain types of antidepressants. If those don't work for you, your provider may suggest taking some other type of psychiatric medicine.</li></ul><p>If you have severe OCD that does not get better with these treatments, your provider may suggest a treatment called repetitive transcranial magnetic stimulation (rTMS). It is a brain stimulation procedure that uses magnetic waves. It can target specific brain areas associated with OCD.</p><p>NIH: National Institute of Mental Health </p></content>

    <document rank="3" url="https://medlineplus.gov/panicdisorder.html">
      <content name="title">Panic Disorder</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is panic disorder?<p>Panic disorder is a type of <span class="qt0">anxiety</span> disorder.  It causes repeated panic attacks, which are sudden periods of intense fear, discomfort, or a sense of losing control. These attacks happen even though there is no real danger. They often cause physical symptoms. For example, you may have a rapid or pounding heartbeat and feel like you are having a heart attack.</p><p>If you have panic attacks, it doesn't mean you will develop a panic disorder. Many people only have one or two panic attacks in their lifetime and get better without treatment.</p><p>But some of the people who have panic attacks do develop panic disorder. They have repeated panic attacks. The attacks can happen as often as several times a day or as rarely as a few times a year. People with panic disorder often worry about having another attack. It may cause them to avoid places and situations where they had panic attacks in the past.</p><p>Panic disorder is not life-threatening, but it can be upsetting and affect your quality of life. And if it is not treated, it can sometimes lead to other health conditions, including depression and substance use disorders.</p>What causes panic disorder?<p>The cause of panic disorder is unknown. Researchers think that certain factors may play a role:</p><ul><li>Genetics - panic disorder sometimes runs in families. But no one knows for sure why some family members have it while others don't.</li><li>Brain biology and chemistry.</li><li>Your environment.</li><li>Major <span class="qt0">stress</span>.</li></ul>Who is more likely to develop panic disorder?<p>Panic disorder is more common in women than men. It often starts in the late teens or early adulthood. Sometimes it starts when a person is under a lot of <span class="qt0">stress</span>. People who have had trauma, especially in childhood, are more likely to develop panic disorder.</p>What are the symptoms of panic disorder?<p>People with panic disorder may have:</p><ul><li>Sudden and repeated panic attacks of overwhelming <span class="qt0">anxiety</span> and fear</li><li>A feeling of being out of control or a fear of death during a panic attack</li><li>An intense worry about another panic attack</li><li>A fear or avoidance of places and situations where they had panic attacks in the past</li><li>Physical symptoms during a panic attack, such as:
	<ul><li>Pounding or racing heart</li><li>Sweating or chills</li><li>Trembling or shaking</li><li>Trouble breathing</li><li>The feeling that they are choking</li><li>Weakness or dizziness</li><li>Chest pain</li><li>Stomach pain or nausea</li></ul>
</li></ul><p>Panic attacks can happen anytime, without warning. They can last anywhere from a few minutes to over an hour.</p>How is panic disorder diagnosed?<p>To find out if you have panic disorder, your health care provider:</p><ul><li>Will ask about your medical history and symptoms</li><li>May check if an unrelated physical problem is causing your symptoms, for example with:
	<ul><li>A physical exam</li><li>Blood tests to check for thyroid problems and other possible conditions</li><li>Heart health tests</li></ul>
</li><li>May do a panic disorder test</li><li>May refer you to a mental health provider for the panic disorder test or other types of psychological evaluations</li></ul>What are the treatments for panic disorder?<p>Treatment for panic disorder usually includes one or more of the following:</p><ul><li>Talk therapy, also called psychotherapy or counseling, which can help you understand your feelings.
	<ul><li>It may include cognitive-behavioral therapy (CBT), a type of talk therapy that helps you change your negative thoughts and how you react to things that cause you to feel <span class="qt0">anxiety</span>.</li></ul></li><li>Medicines, including:
	<ul><li>Antidepressants, such as selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs) and serotonin-norepinephrine reuptake inhibitors (SNRIs)</li><li>Anti-<span class="qt0">anxiety</span> medicines</li></ul></li></ul><p>Your provider may also suggest that you follow a healthy lifestyle, which may help with panic disorder. It may include:</p><ul><li>Avoiding alcohol</li><li>Reducing caffeine</li><li>Eating regular meals</li><li>Getting enough sleep</li><li>Getting regular exercise</li></ul><p>Joining a support group may also be helpful. Support groups can make you feel like you are not alone, and you may learn some new tips on how to cope.</p><p>NIH: National Institute of Mental Health</p></content>
      <content name="mesh">Panic Disorder</content>
      <content name="groupName">Mental Health and Behavior</content>

    <document rank="4" url="https://medlineplus.gov/bullyingandcyberbullying.html">
      <content name="title">Bullying and Cyberbullying</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is bullying?<p>Bullying is when a person or group repeatedly harms someone on purpose. It can be physical, social, and/or verbal. It is harmful to both the victims and the bullies, and it always involves:</p><ul><li>Aggressive behavior.</li><li>A difference in power, meaning that the victim is weaker or is seen as weaker. For example, bullies may try to use physical strength, embarrassing information, or popularity to harm others.</li><li>Repetition, meaning it happens more than once or that it probably will happen again</li></ul>What are the types of bullying?<p>There are three types of bullying:</p><ul><li>Physical bullying involves hurting a person's body or belongings. Examples include hitting, kicking, and stealing or breaking someone's stuff.</li><li>Social bullying (also called relational bullying) hurts someone's reputation or relationships. Some examples are spreading rumors, embarrassing someone in public, and making someone feel left out.</li><li>Verbal bullying is saying or writing mean things, including name-calling, taunting, and threatening</li></ul>What is cyberbullying?<p>Cyberbullying is bullying that happens through text messages or online. It could be through emails, social media, forums, or gaming. Some examples are:</p><ul><li>Posting rumors on social media</li><li>Sharing embarrassing pictures or videos online</li><li>Sharing someone else's private information online (doxing)</li><li>Making threats against someone online</li><li>Creating fake accounts and posting information to embarrass someone</li></ul><p>Certain types of cyberbullying can be illegal. The laws on cyberbullying are different from state to state.</p>How is cyberbullying different from bullying?<p>Cyberbullying is a type of bullying, but there are some differences between the two. Cyberbullying can be:</p><ul><li>Anonymous - people can hide their identities when they are online or using a cell phone</li><li>Persistent - people can send messages instantly, at any time of the day or night</li><li>Permanent - a lot of electronic communication is permanent and public, unless it's reported and removed. A bad online reputation can affect getting into college, getting a job, and other areas of life. This applies to the bully as well.</li><li>Hard to notice - teachers and parents may not overhear or see cyberbullying taking place</li></ul>Which children are more likely to be bullied?<p>Children are more likely to be bullied if they:</p><ul><li>Are seen as different from their peers, such as being overweight or underweight, dressing differently, or being of a different race/ethnicity</li><li>Are seen as weak</li><li>Have depression, <span class="qt0">anxiety</span>, or low self-esteem</li><li>Don't have many friends or are less popular</li><li>Don't socialize well with others</li><li>Have an intellectual or developmental disability</li></ul>Which children are more likely to be bullies?<p>There are two types of children who are more likely to bully others:</p><ul><li>Children who are well-connected to peers, have social power, are overly worried about popularity, and like to be in charge of others</li><li>Children who are more isolated from peers, may be depressed or <span class="qt0">anxious</span>, have low self-esteem, are easily pressured by peers, and have trouble understanding other people's feelings</li></ul><p>There are certain factors that make someone more likely to be a bully. They include:</p><ul><li>Being aggressive or easily frustrated</li><li>Having trouble at home, such as violence or bullying in the home or having uninvolved parents</li><li>Having trouble following rules</li><li>Seeing violence positively</li><li>Having friends who bully others</li></ul>What are the effects of bullying?<p>Bullying is a serious problem that causes harm. And it doesn't just hurt the person who is being bullied; it can also be harmful for the bullies and for any kids who witness the bullying.</p><p>Kids who are bullied can have problems at school and with their mental and physical health. They are at risk for:</p><ul><li>Depression, <span class="qt0">anxiety</span>, and low self-esteem. These problems sometimes last into adulthood.</li><li>Health complaints, including headaches and stomachaches</li><li>Lower grades and test scores</li><li>Missing and dropping out of school</li></ul><p>Kids who bully others have a higher risk for substance use, problems in school, and violence later in life.

</p><p>Kids who witness bullying are more likely to abuse drugs or alcohol and have mental health problems. They may also miss or skip school.

</p>What are the signs of being bullied?<p>Often, kids who are being bullied don't report it. They may fear a backlash from the bully, or they may think that no one cares. Sometimes they feel too ashamed to talk about it. So it is important to know the signs of a bullying problem:</p><ul><li>Depression, loneliness, or <span class="qt0">anxiety</span></li><li>Low self-esteem</li><li>Headaches, stomachaches, or poor eating habits</li><li>Disliking school, not wanting to go to school, or getting worse grades than before</li><li>Self-destructive behaviors, such as running away from home, harming themselves, or talking about suicide</li><li>Unexplained injuries</li><li>Lost or destroyed clothing, books, electronics, or jewelry</li><li>Trouble sleeping or frequent nightmares</li><li>Sudden loss of friends or avoidance of social situations</li></ul>How do you help someone who is being bullied?<p>To help a child who is being bullied, support the child and address the bullying behavior:</p><ul><li>Listen and focus on the child. Learn what's been going on and show you want to help.</li><li>Assure the child that bullying is not his/her fault</li><li>Know that kids who are bullied may struggle with talking about it. Consider referring them to a school counselor, psychologist, or other mental health service.</li><li>Give advice about what to do. This may involve role-playing and thinking through how the child might react if the bullying occurs again.</li><li>Work together to resolve the situation and protect the bullied child. The child, parents, and school or organization should be part of the solution.</li><li>Follow up. Bullying may not end overnight. Make sure that the child knows that you are committed to making it stop.</li><li>Make sure that the bully knows that his or her behavior is wrong and harms others</li><li>Show kids that bullying is taken seriously. Make it clear to everyone that the bullying will not be tolerated.</li></ul><p>Department of Health and Human Services</p></content>

    <document rank="5" url="https://medlineplus.gov/childmentalhealth.html">
      <content name="title">Child Mental Health</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Mental Health, Child</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>It's important to recognize and treat mental illnesses in children early on.  Once mental illness develops, it becomes a regular part of your child's behavior. This makes it more difficult to treat.</p><p>But it's not always easy to know when your child has a serious problem. Everyday stresses can cause changes in your child's behavior. For example, getting a new brother or sister or going to a new school may cause a child to temporarily act out. Warning signs that it might be a more serious problem include:</p><ul><li>Problems in more than one setting (at school, at home, with peers)</li><li>Changes in appetite or sleep</li><li>Social withdrawal or fear of things he or she did not used to be not afraid of</li><li>Returning to behaviors more common in younger children, such as bedwetting</li><li>Signs of being upset, such as sadness or tearfulness</li><li>Signs of self-destructive behavior, such as head-banging or suddenly getting hurt often</li><li>Repeated thoughts of death</li></ul><p>To diagnose mental health problems, the doctor or mental health specialist looks at your child's signs and symptoms, medical history, and family history. Treatments include medicines and talk therapy.</p><p>NIH: National Institute of Mental Health</p></content>

    <document rank="6" url="https://medlineplus.gov/depression.html">
      <content name="title">Depression</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Dysthymic Disorder</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Clinical depression</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Major depressive disorder</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Unipolar depression</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is depression?<p>Depression is more than a feeling of being sad or irritable for a few days. It's a serious mood disorder. As one of the most common mental disorders in the United States, depression can affect how you think, feel, and your everyday life. You may not know why you feel this way, but even basic daily activities such as sleeping, eating, or working may become difficult. Depression often needs long-term treatment but getting help at the earliest sign of a problem can help manage your symptoms.</p>What are the different types of depression?<p>The most common types of depression are major depression and persistent depressive disorder.</p><ul><li>Major depression symptoms include a depressed mood or a loss of interest. Symptoms affect your daily activities and last for at least two weeks.</li><li>Persistent depressive disorder (also called dysthymia or dysthymic disorder) has less severe depressive symptoms, but they last longer, usually for at least two years.</li></ul><p>Other types of depression can include:</p><ul><li>Seasonal affective disorder comes and goes with the seasons. It usually starts in late fall and early winter and goes away during the spring and summer.</li><li>Bipolar disorder is sometimes called manic depression. This mood disorder can cause intense mood swings.</li><li>Depression with symptoms of psychosis is a severe form of depression that also has delusions (false beliefs) or hallucinations (seeing, hearing, or feeling something that isn't there).</li></ul>What causes depression?<p>There are a variety of things that may cause depression, including genetic, biological, environmental, and psychological factors. Depression can happen at any age and can affect anyone, but it's more common in women. This is often due to the physical and hormonal changes that women have during menstruation, pregnancy, the postpartum period, and menopause. Other mental disorders or chronic medical conditions such as diabetes, cancer, or heart disease may occur with depression. This may make the chronic condition or depression worse.</p><p>In some cases, there is no clear cause for depression.</p>Who is at risk of depression?<p>You may be at a higher risk of depression if you have a close relative who has it or another mental health disorder. Going through trauma or stressful life events may raise your risk even more.</p>What are the symptoms of depression?<p>Depression doesn't look the same on everyone. The type and number of symptoms of depression can vary and don't always appear like sadness. Depression may affect you differently based on your:</p><ul><li>Age. The symptoms of depression may be different in a child, who may appear more irritable than sad. Teens may act out or have excessive sleepiness. The symptoms for an older adult may not be as obvious as other age groups.</li><li>Sex. Men often show symptoms and coping behaviors differently than women. Rather than sad, men may appear angry or irritable.</li><li>Medical conditions. Some medicines and medical conditions may cause symptoms that look like depression. Chronic conditions may make depression worse.</li></ul><p>Symptoms of depression are ongoing and affect your day-to-day life. They can include:</p><ul><li>Feeling sad or "empty"</li><li>Losing interest in favorite activities</li><li>Overeating, or not wanting to eat at all</li><li>Sleeping too much, or not enough</li><li>Feeling very tired</li><li>Feeling hopeless, irritable, <span class="qt0">anxious</span>, or guilty</li><li>Having aches and pains, headaches, cramps, or digestive problems</li><li>Having thoughts of death or suicide</li><li>Changing moods or behaviors</li><li>Increasing use of alcohol or drugs</li><li>Isolating from family and friends</li></ul>How is depression diagnosed?<p>If you think you have depression, talk to your health care provider or a mental health provider. To be diagnosed with depression, your provider may use many tools:</p><ul><li>A medical history will include asking about your symptoms and family history. To be diagnosed with depression, your symptoms must occur most of the day, nearly every day, for at least two weeks. One of the symptoms must be a depressed mood or a loss of interest in most activities.</li><li>Medical tests may be done to rule out other medical conditions. Certain medicines and medical conditions (such as viruses or thyroid disorders) may cause symptoms like depression.</li><li>A physical exam to make sure another issue isn't causing your symptoms.</li><li>A mental health evaluation by your provider or a mental health provider.</li></ul>What are the treatments for depression?<p>Depression may need long-term treatment. Most treatments include medicines and/or psychotherapy (talk therapy):</p><ul><li>Medicines such as antidepressants may help control your symptoms. You may need to try several medicines to find which works best for you. Medicine may take time to work and can have side effects. Don't stop taking your medicine without first talking with your provider. Contact your provider if you have any concerns about side effects from the medicine.</li><li>Psychotherapy (talk therapy) under the care of a mental health provider can help you recognize and change troubling emotions, thoughts, and behaviors. This may be done one-on-one or in a group setting. It can give you and your family support, education, skills, and coping strategies.</li><li>Light therapy has been shown to be effective for seasonal affective disorder (SAD).</li><li>Other treatments options may be considered if treatment isn't working or if depression is severe. These may include:
	<ul><li>Electroconvulsive therapy (ECT) is a brain stimulation procedure that uses a mild electric current when you are under general anesthesia. ECT is most often used for severe depression that is not getting better with other treatments.</li><li>Repetitive transcranial magnetic stimulation (rTMS) is a brain stimulation procedure that uses magnetic waves to relieve depression. It's not as powerful as ECT, but with rTMS, you don't need anesthesia. It also has a low risk of negative effects on your memory and thinking.</li></ul>
</li></ul>Can depression be prevented?<p>Most cases of depression can't be prevented, but healthy lifestyle changes can have long-term benefits for your mental health. These can include:</p><ul><li>Getting regular exercise</li><li>Having a consistent sleep schedule</li><li>Controlling <span class="qt0">stress</span></li></ul><p>If you, or someone you love is depressed, see your provider or a mental health provider. It's important to get treatment at the earliest sign of a problem.</p><p>If you or someone you know has thoughts of hurting themselves or attempting suicide, get help right away. If it is an emergency, dial 911.</p><p>NIH: National Institute of Mental Health</p></content>
      <content name="mesh">Depression</content>

    <document rank="7" url="https://medlineplus.gov/mentaldisorders.html">
      <content name="title">Mental Disorders</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Psychiatric Disorders</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Mental illness</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What are mental disorders?<p>Mental disorders (or mental illnesses) are conditions that affect your thinking, feeling, mood, and behavior. They may happen over a short period of time or come and go. Some can be chronic (long-lasting). They can affect your ability to relate to others and function each day..</p>What are some types of mental disorders?<p>There are many different types of mental disorders. Some common ones include:</p><ul><li><span class="qt0">Anxiety</span> disorders, including panic disorder, obsessive-compulsive disorder, and phobias</li><li>Depression, bipolar disorder, and other mood disorders</li><li>Eating disorders</li><li>Personality disorders</li><li>Post-traumatic <span class="qt0">stress</span> disorder (PTSD)</li><li>Psychotic disorders, including schizophrenia</li></ul>What causes mental disorders?<p>There is no single cause for mental illness. A number of factors can contribute to risk for mental illness, such as:</p><ul><li>Your genes and family history</li><li>Your life experiences, such as <span class="qt0">stress</span> or a history of abuse, especially if they happen in childhood</li><li>Biological factors such as chemical imbalances in the brain</li><li>A traumatic brain injury (TBI)</li><li>Prenatal (before birth) exposure to viruses, toxic chemicals, or other substances such as alcohol and drugs.</li><li>Use of alcohol or recreational drugs</li><li>Having a serious medical condition like cancer</li><li>Having few friends, and feeling lonely or isolated</li></ul><p>Mental disorders are not caused by character flaws. They have nothing to do with being lazy or weak.</p>Who is at risk for mental disorders?<p>Mental disorders are common. Many Americans will be diagnosed with a mental disorder at some time in their life.</p>How are mental disorders diagnosed?<p>The steps to getting a diagnosis include:</p><ul><li>A medical history</li><li>A physical exam and possibly lab tests, if your provider thinks that other medical conditions could be causing your symptoms</li><li>A psychological evaluation. You will answer questions about your thinking, feelings, and behaviors.</li></ul>What are the treatments for mental disorders?<p>Treatment depends on which mental disorder you have and how serious it is. You and your provider will work on a treatment plan just for you. It usually involves some type of therapy. You may also take medicines. Some people also need social support and education on managing their condition.</p><p>In some cases, you may need more intensive treatment. You may need to go to a psychiatric hospital.  This could be because your mental illness is severe. Or it could be because you are at risk of hurting yourself or someone else. In the hospital, you will get counseling, group discussions, and activities with mental health professionals and other patients.</p></content>
      <content name="mesh">Mental Disorders</content>
      <content name="mesh">Psychiatric Rehabilitation</content>
      <content name="groupName">Mental Health and Behavior</content>
      <content name="snippet"> ... types of mental disorders. Some common ones include: <span class="qt0">Anxiety</span> disorders, including panic disorder, obsessive-compulsive disorder, and  ... </content>

    <document rank="8" url="https://medlineplus.gov/migraine.html">
      <content name="title">Migraine</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Vascular Headache</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What are migraines?<p>Migraines are a recurring type of headache. They cause moderate to severe pain that is throbbing or pulsing. The pain is often on one side of your head. You may also have other symptoms, such as nausea and weakness. You may be sensitive to light and sound.</p>What causes migraines?<p>Researchers believe that migraine has a genetic cause. There are also a number of factors that can trigger a migraine. These factors vary from person to person, and they include:</p><ul><li><span class="qt0">Stress</span></li><li><span class="qt0">Anxiety</span></li><li>Hormonal changes in women</li><li>Bright or flashing lights</li><li>Loud noises</li><li>Strong smells</li><li>Medicines</li><li>Too much or not enough sleep</li><li>Sudden changes in weather or environment</li><li>Overexertion (too much physical activity)</li><li>Tobacco</li><li>Caffeine or caffeine withdrawal</li><li>Skipped meals</li><li>Medication overuse (taking medicine for migraines too often)</li></ul><p>Some people have found that certain foods or ingredients can trigger headaches, especially when they are combined with other triggers. These foods and ingredients include:</p><ul><li>Alcohol</li><li>Chocolate</li><li>Aged cheeses</li><li>Monosodium glutamate (MSG)</li><li>Some fruits and nuts</li><li>Fermented or pickled goods</li><li>Yeast</li><li>Cured or processed meats</li></ul>Who is at risk for migraines?<p>About 12% of Americans get migraines. They can affect anyone, but you are more likely to have them if you:</p><ul><li>Are a woman. Women are three times more likely than men to get migraines.</li><li>Have a family history of migraines. Most people with migraines have family members who have migraines.</li><li>Have other medical conditions, such as depression, <span class="qt0">anxiety</span>, bipolar disorder, sleep disorders, and epilepsy.</li></ul>What are the symptoms of migraines?<p>There are four different phases of migraines. You may not always go through every phase each time you have a migraine.:</p><ul><li>Prodome. This phase starts up to 24 hours before you get the migraine. You have early signs and symptoms, such as food cravings, unexplained mood changes, uncontrollable yawning, fluid retention, and increased urination.</li><li>Aura. If you have this phase, you might see flashing or bright lights or zig-zag lines. You may have muscle weakness or feel like you are being touched or grabbed. An aura can happen just before or during a migraine.</li><li>Headache. A migraine usually starts gradually and then becomes more severe. It typically causes throbbing or pulsing pain, which is often on one side of your head. But sometimes you can have a migraine without a headache. Other migraine symptoms may include</li><ul><li>Increased sensitivity to light, noise, and odors</li><li>Nausea and vomiting</li><li>Worsened pain when you move, cough, or sneeze</li></ul><li>Postdrome (following the headache). You may feel exhausted, weak, and confused after a migraine. This can last up to a day.</li></ul><p>Migraines are more common in the morning; people often wake up with them. Some people have migraines at predictable times, such as before menstruation or on weekends following a stressful week of work.</p>How are migraines diagnosed?<p>To make a diagnosis, your health care provider will:</p><ul><li>Take your medical history</li><li>Ask about your symptoms</li><li>Do a physical and neurological exam</li></ul><p>An important part of diagnosing migraines is to rule out other medical conditions which could be causing the symptoms. So you may also have blood tests, an MRI or CT scan, or other tests.</p>How are migraines treated?<p>There is no cure for migraines. Treatment focuses on relieving symptoms and preventing additional attacks.</p><p>There are different types of medicines to relieve symptoms. They include triptan drugs, ergotamine drugs, and pain relievers. The sooner you take the medicine, the more effective it is.</p><p>There are also other things you can do to feel better:</p><ul><li>Resting with your eyes closed in a quiet, darkened room</li><li>Placing a cool cloth or ice pack on your forehead</li><li>Drinking fluids</li></ul><p>There are some lifestyle changes you can make to prevent migraines:</p><ul><li><span class="qt0">Stress</span> management strategies, such as exercise, relaxation techniques, and biofeedback, may reduce the number and severity of migraines. Biofeedback uses electronic devices to teach you to control certain body functions, such as your heartbeat, blood pressure, and muscle <span class="qt0">tension</span>.</li><li>Make a log of what seems to trigger your migraines. You can learn what you need to avoid, such as certain foods and medicines. It also help you figure out what you should do, such as establishing a consistent sleep schedule and eating regular meals.</li><li>Hormone therapy may help some women whose migraines seem to be linked to their menstrual cycle</li><li>If you have obesity, losing weight may also be helpful</li></ul><p>If you have frequent or severe migraines, you may need to take medicines to prevent further attacks. Talk with your health care provider about which drug would be right for you.</p><p>Certain natural treatments, such as riboflavin (vitamin B2) and coenzyme Q10, may help prevent migraines. If your magnesium level is low, you can try taking magnesium. There is also an herb, butterbur, which some people take to prevent migraines. But butterbur may not be safe for long-term use. Always check with your health care provider before taking any supplements.</p><p>NIH: National Institute of Neurological Disorders and Stroke</p></content>

    <document rank="9" url="https://medlineplus.gov/premenstrualsyndrome.html">
      <content name="title">Premenstrual Syndrome</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">PMS</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is premenstrual syndrome (PMS)?<p>Premenstrual syndrome, or PMS, is a group of physical and emotional symptoms that start one to two weeks before your period. Most women have at least some symptoms of PMS, and the symptoms go away after their periods start. The symptoms may range from mild to severe.</p>What is premenstrual dysphoric disorder (PMDD)?<p>Premenstrual dysphoric disorder (PMDD) is a severe type of PMS. With PMDD, the symptoms are severe enough to interfere with your life.  PMDD much less common than PMS.</p>What causes premenstrual syndrome (PMS)?<p>Researchers don't know exactly what causes PMS. Changes in hormone levels during the menstrual cycle may play a role. These changing hormone levels may affect some women more than others.</p>What are the symptoms of premenstrual syndrome (PMS)?<p>PMS symptoms are different for everyone. You may get physical symptoms, emotional symptoms, or both. Your symptoms may also change throughout your life.</p><p>Physical symptoms may include:</p><ul><li>Breast swelling and tenderness</li><li>Acne</li><li>Bloating and weight gain</li><li>Headache</li><li>Joint pain</li><li>Backache</li><li>Constipation or diarrhea</li><li>Food cravings</li></ul><p>Emotional symptoms may include:</p><ul><li>Irritability</li><li>Mood swings</li><li>Crying spells</li><li>Depression</li><li><span class="qt0">Anxiety</span></li><li>Sleeping too much or too little</li><li>Trouble with concentration and memory</li><li>Less interest in sex</li></ul>How is premenstrual syndrome (PMS) diagnosed?<p>You may wish to see your health care provider if your symptoms bother you or affect your daily life.</p><p>There is no single test for PMS. Your provider will talk with you about your symptoms, including when they happen and how much they affect your life.  To be diagnosed with PMS, your symptoms must:</p><ul><li>Happen in the five days before your period for at least three menstrual cycles in a row</li><li>End within four days after your period starts</li><li>Keep you from enjoying or doing some of your normal activities</li></ul><p>Your provider may wish to do tests to rule out other conditions which may cause similar symptoms.</p>What are the treatments for premenstrual syndrome (PMS)?<p>No single PMS treatment works for everyone. If your symptoms are not severe, you may be able to manage them with: </p><ul><li>Over-the-counter pain relievers such as ibuprofen, aspirin, or naproxen, to help ease cramps, headaches, backaches, and breast tenderness</li><li>Getting regular exercise</li><li>Getting enough sleep</li><li>Eating healthy foods</li><li>Avoiding salt, caffeine, sugar, and alcohol in the two weeks before your period</li></ul><p>Some studies have shown that certain vitamins may help with some symptoms of PMS. They include calcium and vitamin B6.</p><p>Some women take certain herbal supplements for PMS symptoms. But there is not enough evidence to prove that supplements are effective for PMS. Check with your provider before taking any vitamins or supplements.</p><p>If you are not able to manage your PMS symptoms, your provider may suggest prescription medicines. These medicines may also be used to treat PMDD. They include:</p><ul><li>Hormonal birth control, which may help with the physical symptoms of PMS. But sometimes they may make the emotional symptoms worse. You may need to try several different types of birth control before you find the right one.</li><li>Antidepressants, such as selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs), which may help with emotional symptoms.</li><li>Diuretics ("water pills") to reduce symptoms of bloating and breast tenderness.</li><li>Anti-<span class="qt0">anxiety</span> medicine to ease symptoms of <span class="qt0">anxiety</span>.</li></ul><p>Dept. of Health and Human Services Office on Women's Health</p></content>

      <content name="mesh">Premenstrual Syndrome</content>

    <document rank="10" url="https://medlineplus.gov/antidepressants.html">
      <content name="title">Antidepressants</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">SNRI </content>
      <content name="altTitle">SSRIs</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Tricyclic antidepressants</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What are antidepressants?<p>Antidepressants are prescription medicines to treat depression. Depression is more than feeling a little sad or "blue" for a few days. It's a very common, serious medical illness that affects your mood and general mental health It can make you feel tired, hopeless, worried, or fearful. It can change your thinking, sleeping, and eating. Depression may make some people think about ending their lives.</p><p>But antidepressants can help many people who have depression. Researchers think antidepressants may help improve the way your brain uses certain chemicals that control mood or <span class="qt0">stress</span>.</p>Are antidepressants used for other conditions?<p>A health care provider may prescribe antidepressants for <span class="qt0">anxiety</span>, chronic pain, or insomnia. Sometimes providers also prescribe antidepressants for other conditions.</p>What are the different types of antidepressants?<p>There are many types of antidepressants. Each one works differently. Providers usually prescribe newer antidepressants first because they don't cause as many side effects as older types. They also seem to help more kinds of depression and <span class="qt0">anxiety</span> problems.</p><p>Most of the newer antidepressants belong to one of these three groups:</p><ul><li>Selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs)</li><li>Serotonin and norepinephrine reuptake inhibitors (SNRIs)</li><li>Atypical antidepressants, which are newer antidepressants that don't fit into the other groups</li></ul><p>If these antidepressants don't help, your provider might suggest one of the older antidepressants. The older types include tricyclic antidepressants (TCAs), tetracyclics, and monoamine oxidase inhibitors (MAOIs). Even though these antidepressants may cause more serious side effects, the benefits may outweigh the risks for some people.</p>Which type of antidepressant is right for me?<p>Our bodies and brains all work differently. That means one antidepressant won't work for everyone.  You may need to try two or more medicines before you find one that works for you.</p><p>Your provider will work with you to choose the best option to try first. You'll consider questions such as:</p><ul><li>Which symptoms bother you most? Some antidepressants may do a better job helping specific symptoms, such as trouble sleeping.</li><li>What other medicines and supplements do you take? Some antidepressants can cause problems if you take them with certain medicines and herbs.</li><li>Did a certain antidepressant work well for a close relative? An antidepressant that helped a parent, brother, or sister could be a good choice for you, too.</li><li>Do you have other health conditions? Certain antidepressants can make some other conditions better or worse. Any other conditions that you have will be part of choosing your depression treatment.</li><li>Are you pregnant, planning for pregnancy, or breastfeeding?  If so, your provider will help you find a way to treat your depression that's safe for you and your baby.</li></ul>How long do antidepressants take to work?<p>Antidepressants usually take 4 to 8 weeks to work, so you'll need to be patient. You may notice that some problems, such as sleeping and eating, get better before your mood improves. That's a good sign. You may just need to give the medicine a little more time to do its job.</p><p>Sometimes an antidepressant helps at first, but symptoms return while you're still taking it. But there's usually another one you can try. To get more relief from depression, your provider may suggest combining two antidepressants, using another kind of medicine with an antidepressant, or adding talk therapy or other approaches to improve your mental health.</p>How long will I need to take an antidepressant?<p>When an antidepressant starts to work, you and your provider can decide how long you need to stay on it. The typical length of treatment is 6 to 12 months, but some people may stay on antidepressants for much longer.</p>What are the side effects of antidepressants?<p>Not everyone has side effects from antidepressants. But if you do have them, they're usually mild and may get better over time as your body gets used to the new medicine.</p><p>The most common side effects from antidepressants include:</p><ul><li>Nausea and vomiting</li><li>Weight gain</li><li>Diarrhea</li><li>Sleepiness</li><li>Sexual problems, such as a lack of desire or ability to have sex</li></ul><p>When thinking about side effects, it's important to remember that there are also risks from not treating depression. Your provider can help you think through the pros and cons of all your options.</p><p>If you have any side effects from antidepressants, your provider may suggest ways to manage them while you wait to see if the antidepressant will work. If the side effects bother you too much, you may need to change antidepressants. But you should never change your dose or stop taking an antidepressant on your own.</p><p>If you have serious problems or notice any changes that worry you, such as new or worsening symptoms, unusual changes in your mood, or you start acting differently, call your provider right away.</p><p>In some cases, children, teenagers, and young adults under 25 may be more likely to think about hurting or killing themselves when starting antidepressants or when the dose is changed. Get medical help right away if this happens.</p>What can I do to take antidepressants safely?<p>Antidepressants are generally safe when you use them correctly:</p><ul><li>Tell your provider about everything you take. That includes medicines, herbs, supplements, and over-the-counter medicines you take, such as pain relievers and decongestants. Be honest about recreational drugs and alcohol, too.</li><li>Try to get all your medicines from the same pharmacy. That way the pharmacist can warn you and your provider if you take medicines that may cause problems when used together.</li><li>Follow all instructions about how to take your medicine.</li><li>Talk with your provider if side effects bother you.</li><li>Never stop taking antidepressants without your provider's help. Stopping too fast may make depression come back. You could even make your condition worse. To stop antidepressants safely, you need to give your body time to get used to being without the medicine. Your provider can tell you the safest way to go off an antidepressant.</li></ul><p>NIH: National Institute of Mental Health</p></content>

    <document rank="11" url="https://medlineplus.gov/caffeine.html">
      <content name="title">Caffeine</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Energy Drinks</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is caffeine?<p>Caffeine is a bitter substance that occurs naturally in more than 60 plants including:</p><ul><li>Coffee beans</li><li>Tea leaves</li><li>Kola nuts, which are used to flavor soft drink colas</li><li>Cacao pods, which are used to make chocolate products</li></ul><p>There is also synthetic (man-made) caffeine, which is added to some medicines, foods, and drinks. For example, some pain relievers, cold medicines, and over-the-counter medicines for alertness contain synthetic caffeine. So do energy drinks and "energy-boosting" gums and snacks.</p><p>Most people consume caffeine from drinks. The amount of caffeine in different drinks can vary a lot, but it is generally:</p><ul><li>An 8-ounce cup of coffee: 95-200 mg</li><li>A 12-ounce can of cola: 35-45 mg</li><li>An 8-ounce energy drink: 70-100 mg</li><li>An 8-ounce cup of tea: 14-60 mg</li></ul>What are caffeine's effects on the body?<p>Caffeine has many effects on your body's metabolism. It:</p><ul><li>Stimulates your central nervous system, which can make you feel more awake and give you a boost of energy</li><li>Is a diuretic, meaning that it helps your body get rid of extra salt and water by urinating more</li><li>Increases the release of acid in your stomach, sometimes leading to an upset stomach or heartburn</li><li>May interfere with the absorption of calcium in the body</li><li>Increases your blood pressure</li></ul><p>Within one hour of eating or drinking caffeine, it reaches its peak level in your blood. You may continue to feel the effects of caffeine for four to six hours.</p>What are the side effects from too much caffeine?<p>For most people, it is not harmful to consume up to 400mg of caffeine a day. If you do eat or drink too much caffeine, it can cause health problems, such as:</p><ul><li>Restlessness and shakiness</li><li>Insomnia</li><li>Headaches</li><li>Dizziness</li><li>Fast heart rate</li><li>Dehydration</li><li><span class="qt0">Anxiety</span></li><li>Dependency, so you need to take more of it to get the same results</li></ul><p>Some people are more sensitive to the effects of caffeine than others.</p>What are energy drinks, and why can they be a problem?<p>Energy drinks are beverages that have added caffeine. The amount of caffeine in energy drinks can vary widely, and sometimes the labels on the drinks do not give you the actual amount of caffeine in them. Energy drinks may also contain sugars, vitamins, herbs, and supplements.</p><p>Companies that make energy drinks claim that the drinks can increase alertness and improve physical and mental performance. This has helped make the drinks popular with American teens and young adults. There's limited data showing that energy drinks might temporarily improve alertness and physical endurance. There is not enough evidence to show that they enhance strength or power. But what we do know is that energy drinks can be dangerous because they have large amounts of caffeine. And since they have lots of sugar, they can contribute to weight gain and worsen diabetes.</p><p>Sometimes young people mix their energy drinks with alcohol. It is dangerous to combine alcohol and caffeine. Caffeine can interfere with your ability to recognize how drunk you are, which can lead you to drink more. This also makes you more likely to make bad decisions.</p>Who should avoid or limit caffeine?<p>You should check with your health care provider about whether you should limit or avoid caffeine if you:</p><ul><li>Are pregnant, since caffeine passes through the placenta to your baby.</li><li>Are breastfeeding, since a small amount of caffeine that you consume is passed along to your baby.</li><li>Have sleep disorders, including insomnia.</li><li>Have migraines or other chronic headaches.</li><li>Have <span class="qt0">anxiety</span>.</li><li>Have GERD or ulcers.</li><li>Have arrhythmia (a problem with the rate or rhythm of your heartbeat).</li><li>Have high blood pressure.</li><li>Take certain medicines or supplements, including stimulants, certain antibiotics, asthma medicines, and heart medicines. Check with your health care provider about whether there might be interactions between caffeine and any medicines and supplements that you take.</li><li>Are a child or teen. Neither should have as much caffeine as adults. Children can be especially sensitive to the effects of caffeine.</li></ul>What is caffeine withdrawal?<p>If you have been consuming caffeine on a regular basis and then suddenly stop, you may have caffeine withdrawal. Symptoms can include:</p><ul><li>Headaches</li><li>Drowsiness</li><li>Irritability</li><li>Nausea</li><li>Trouble concentrating</li></ul><p>These symptoms usually go away after a couple of days.</p></content>
