    <document rank="12" url="https://medlineplus.gov/cancerlivingwithcancer.html">
      <content name="title">Cancer--Living with Cancer</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Living with Cancer</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">Learning to live with cancer<p>Cancer is a common disease. Many Americans will be diagnosed with cancer during their lifetimes. Even though cancer may be life-threatening, many people have successful treatment. Others live with cancer for a very long time.</p><p>For most people with cancer, learning to live with the disease is one of the biggest challenges they've ever faced. That's because having cancer touches just about every part of your life and the lives of those around you.</p><p>Cancer and its treatment may change:</p><ul><li>Your daily routines and ability to work</li><li>Your important relationships</li><li>The way you look, feel, and think about yourself</li></ul><p>If you learn what to expect, you may feel more in control and prepared to cope with these changes. Your health care provider can help you find information and support services that are right for you.</p>Coping with feelings about having cancer<p>Having cancer may cause a range of strong emotions, such as sadness, anger, fear, worry, or guilt. These feelings are normal, and they're likely to change over time. It's helpful to sort out your feelings in a way that's comfortable for you. You might try:</p><ul><li>Talking openly with someone you trust</li><li>Writing about your feelings</li><li>Using relaxation methods, such as meditation and other complementary and alternative medicine (CAM)</li><li>Doing the things you enjoy,  giving yourself a break from focusing on cancer</li></ul><p>If your emotions seem to take over your life, tell your provider. You may need extra support if you have symptoms of depression, <span class="qt0">stress, anxiety</span>, or panic disorder.</p>Communicating with your health care team<p>During cancer treatment, you usually have a team of providers. Along with doctors and nurses, you may be able to talk with social workers, pharmacists, dietitians, and other health professionals.</p><p>These professionals are prepared to help you deal with the issues that cancer brings up, including concerns about finances. But it's up to you to let your team know what's on your mind.</p><p>Good communication with your providers may help you feel more in control and satisfied with your care. Your communications may be better if you:</p><ul><li>Tell your providers how much you want to know about your cancer and its treatment. Do you want all the details or just the big picture?</li><li>Write down your questions and concerns before your visits.</li><li>Bring a family member or a friend to your visits. This person can help by listening, taking notes, and asking questions.</li></ul>Talking openly with family and friends<p>Cancer changes the daily routines and roles of the people who love and support you. They may need to start doing the things you've always done for them. And you may need their help doing things you've always done for yourself. These changes can be difficult for everyone.</p><p>It may help to have an honest talk about changing roles and needs. If that sounds too hard, ask a social worker or another member of your care team to help you talk with family and friends who are helping with your care.  These caregivers may need some support, too.</p>Dealing with changes in your self-image<p>Cancer and its treatment may cause some big changes in how you look, feel, and think about yourself. For example, you may have:</p><ul><li>Less energy</li><li>Temporary or permanent changes in your body, such as  scars, or hair loss from chemotherapy</li><li>Problems being sexually close or doubts about dating</li></ul><p>Coping with these changes can be hard. But most people find ways to feel more positive over time. If you feel well enough, you might try:</p><ul><li>Exercise. Walking, yoga, or other types of movement may help you feel stronger and more in control of your body. But check with your provider first.</li><li>Staying involved in life and helping others. Think about volunteering, hobbies, or other activities that might make you feel good about yourself.</li><li>Counseling for sexual problems. Talking with a professional, either with a partner or on your own, may help.</li></ul>Adjusting to life after treatment<p>After treatment, you'll have regular cancer follow-up care. Your provider will explain the schedule of checkups and tests you'll need. This is a good time to discuss the challenges you may face ahead. Knowing what to expect may help you make plans as you find a "new normal" with cancer as part of your life.</p><p>NIH: National Cancer Institute</p></content>

    <document rank="13" url="https://medlineplus.gov/dementia.html">
      <content name="title">Dementia</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Senility</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is dementia?<p>Dementia is a loss of mental functions that is severe enough to affect your daily life and activities. These functions include:</p><ul><li>Memory</li><li>Language skills</li><li>Visual perception (your ability to make sense of what you see)</li><li>Problem solving</li><li>Trouble with everyday tasks</li><li>The ability to focus and pay attention</li></ul><p>It is normal to become a bit more forgetful as you age. But dementia is not a normal part of aging. It is a serious disorder that interferes with your daily life.</p>What are the types of dementia?<p>The most common types of dementia are known as neurodegenerative disorders. These are diseases in which the cells of the brain stop working or die. They include:</p><ul><li>Alzheimer's disease, which is the most common form of dementia among older people. People with Alzheimer's have plaques and tangles in their brain. These are abnormal buildups of different proteins. Beta-amyloid protein clumps up and forms plaques in between your brain cells. Tau protein builds up and forms tangles inside the nerve cells of your brain. There is also a loss of connection between nerve cells in the brain.</li><li>Lewy body dementia, which causes movement symptoms along with dementia. Lewy bodies are abnormal deposits of a protein in the brain.</li><li>Frontotemporal disorders, which cause changes to certain parts of the brain:
	<ul><li>Changes in the frontal lobe lead to behavioral symptoms</li><li>Changes in the temporal lobe lead to language and emotional disorders</li></ul>
</li><li>Vascular dementia, which involves changes to the brain's blood supply. It is often caused by a stroke or atherosclerosis (hardening of the arteries) in the brain.</li><li>Mixed dementia, which is a combination of two or more types of dementia. For example, some people have both Alzheimer's disease and vascular dementia.</li></ul><p>Other conditions can cause dementia or dementia-like symptoms, including:</p><ul><li>Creutzfeldt-Jakob disease, a rare brain disorder</li><li>Huntington's disease, an inherited, progressive brain disease</li><li>Chronic traumatic encephalopathy (CTE), caused by repeated traumatic brain injury</li><li>HIV-associated dementia (HAD)</li></ul>Who is at risk for dementia?<p>Certain factors can raise your risk for developing dementia, including:</p><ul><li>Aging. This is the biggest risk factor for dementia.</li><li>Smoking</li><li>Uncontrolled diabetes</li><li>High blood pressure</li><li>Drinking too much alcohol</li><li>Having close family members who have dementia</li></ul>What are the symptoms of dementia?<p>The symptoms of dementia can vary, depending on which parts of the brain are affected. Often, forgetfulness is the first symptom.  Dementia also causes problems with the ability to think, problem solve, and reason. For example, people with dementia may:</p><ul><li>Get lost in a familiar neighborhood</li><li>Use unusual words to refer to familiar objects</li><li>Forget the name of a close family member or friend</li><li>Forget old memories</li><li>Need help doing tasks that they used to do by themselves</li></ul><p>Some people with dementia cannot control their emotions and their personalities may change. They may become apathetic, meaning that they are no longer interested in normal daily activities or events. They may lose their inhibitions and stop caring about other peoples' feelings.</p><p>Certain types of dementia can also cause problems with balance and movement.</p><p>The stages of dementia range from mild to severe. In the mildest stage, it is just beginning to affect a person's functioning. In the most severe stage, the person is completely dependent on others for care.</p>How is dementia diagnosed?<p>Your health care provider may use many tools to make a diagnosis:</p><ul><li>A medical history, which includes asking about your symptoms</li><li>A physical exam</li><li>Tests of your thinking, memory, and language abilities</li><li>Other tests, such as blood tests, genetic tests, and brain scans</li><li>A mental health evaluation to see whether a mental disorder is contributing to your symptoms</li></ul>What are the treatments for dementia?<p>There is no cure for most types of dementia, including Alzheimer's disease and Lewy body dementia. Treatments may help to maintain mental function longer, manage behavioral symptoms, and slow down the symptoms of disease. They may include:</p><ul><li>Medicines, which may temporarily improve memory and thinking or slow down their decline. They only work in some people. Other medicines can treat symptoms such as <span class="qt0">anxiety</span>, depression, sleep problems, and muscle stiffness. Some of these medicines can cause strong side effects in people with dementia. It is important to talk to your health care provider about which medicines will be safe for you.</li><li>Occupational therapy to help find ways to more easily do everyday activities</li><li>Speech therapy to help with swallowing difficulties and trouble speaking loudly and clearly</li><li>Mental health counseling to help people with dementia and their families learn how to manage difficult emotions and behaviors. It can also help them plan for the future.</li><li>Music or art therapy to reduce <span class="qt0">anxiety</span> and improve well-being</li></ul>Can dementia be prevented?<p>Researchers have not found a proven way to prevent dementia. Living a healthy lifestyle might influence some of your risk factors for dementia.</p></content>

    <document rank="14" url="https://medlineplus.gov/eatingdisorders.html">
      <content name="title">Eating Disorders</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Anorexia Nervosa</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Binge Eating</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Bulimia</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Pica</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What are eating disorders?<p>Eating disorders are serious mental health disorders. They involve severe problems with your thoughts about food and your eating behaviors. You may eat much less or much more than you need.</p><p>Eating disorders are medical conditions; they are not a lifestyle choice. They affect your body's ability to get proper nutrition. This can lead to health issues, such as heart and kidney problems, or sometimes even death. But there are treatments that can help.</p>What are the types of eating disorders?<p>Common types of eating disorders include:</p><ul><li>Binge-eating, which is out-of-control eating. People with binge-eating disorder keep eating even after they are full. They often eat until they feel very uncomfortable. Afterward, they usually have feelings of guilt, shame, and distress. Eating too much too often can lead to weight gain and obesity. Binge-eating disorder is the most common eating disorder in the U.S.</li><li>Bulimia nervosa. People with bulimia nervosa also have periods of binge-eating. But afterwards, they purge, by making themselves throw up or using laxatives. They may also over-exercise or fast. People with bulimia nervosa may be slightly underweight, normal weight, or overweight.</li><li>Anorexia nervosa. People with anorexia nervosa avoid food, severely restrict food, or eat very small quantities of only certain foods. They may see themselves as overweight, even when they are dangerously underweight. Anorexia nervosa is the least common of the three eating disorders, but it is often the most serious. It has the highest death rate of any mental disorder.</li></ul>What causes eating disorders?<p>The exact cause of eating disorders is unknown. Researchers believe that eating disorders are caused by a complex interaction of factors. These include genetic, biological, behavioral, psychological, and social factors.</p>Who is at risk for eating disorders?<p>Anyone can develop an eating disorder, but they are more common in women. Eating disorders frequently appear during the teen years or young adulthood. But people can also develop them during childhood or later in life.</p>What are the symptoms of eating disorders?<p>The symptoms of eating disorders vary, depending on the disorder:</p><p>The symptoms of binge-eating include:</p><ul><li>Eating unusually large amounts of food in a specific amount of time, such as a 2-hour period</li><li>Eating even when you're full or not hungry</li><li>Eating fast during binge episodes</li><li>Eating until you're uncomfortably full</li><li>Eating alone or in secret to avoid embarrassment</li><li>Feeling distressed, ashamed, or guilty about your eating</li><li>Frequently dieting, possibly without weight loss</li></ul><p>The symptoms of bulimia nervosa include the same symptoms as binge-eating, plus trying to get rid of the food or weight after binging by:</p><ul><li>Purging, making yourself throw up or using laxatives or enemas to speed up the movement of food through your body</li><li>Doing intensive and excessive exercise</li><li>Fasting</li></ul><p>Over time, bulimia nervosa can cause health problems such as:</p><ul><li>Chronically inflamed and sore throat</li><li>Swollen salivary glands in the neck and jaw area</li><li>Worn tooth enamel and increasingly sensitive and decaying teeth. This is caused by the exposure to stomach acid every time you throw up.</li><li>GERD (acid reflux) and other gastrointestinal problems</li><li>Severe dehydration from purging</li><li>Electrolyte imbalance, which could be too low or too high levels of sodium, calcium, potassium and other minerals. This can lead to a stroke or heart attack.</li></ul><p>The symptoms of anorexia nervosa include:</p><ul><li>Eating very little, to the point of starving yourself</li><li>Intensive and excessive exercise</li><li>Extreme thinness</li><li>Intense fear of gaining weight</li><li>Distorted body image - seeing yourself as overweight even when you are severely underweight</li></ul><p>Over time, anorexia nervosa can cause health problems such as:</p><ul><li>Thinning of the bones (osteopenia or osteoporosis)</li><li>Mild anemia</li><li>Muscle wasting and weakness</li><li>Thin, brittle hair and nails</li><li>Dry, blotchy, or yellowish skin</li><li>Growth of fine hair all over the body</li><li>Severe constipation</li><li>Low blood pressure</li><li>Slowed breathing and pulse</li><li>Feeling cold all the time because of a drop in internal body temperature</li><li>Feeling faint, dizzy, or weak</li><li>Feeling tired all the time</li><li>Infertility</li><li>Damage to the structure and function of the heart</li><li>Brain damage</li><li>Multiorgan failure</li></ul><p>Anorexia nervosa can be fatal. Some people with this disorder die of complications from starvation, and others die of suicide.</p><p>Some people with eating disorders may also have other mental disorders (such as depression or <span class="qt0">anxiety</span>) or problems with substance use.</p>How is eating disorders diagnosed?<p>Because eating disorders can be so serious, it is important to seek help if you or a loved one thinks that you might have a problem. Your health care provider may use many tools to make a diagnosis:</p><ul><li>A medical history, which includes asking about your symptoms. It is important to be honest about your eating and exercise behaviors so your provider can help you.</li><li>A physical exam</li><li>Blood or urine tests to rule out other possible causes of your symptoms</li><li>Other tests to see whether you have any other health problems caused by the eating disorder. These can include kidney function tests and an electrocardiogram (EKG or ECG).</li></ul>What are the treatments for eating disorders?<p>Treatment plans for eating disorders are tailored to individual needs. You will likely have a team of providers helping you, including doctors, nutritionists, nurses, and therapists. The treatments may include:</p><ul><li>Individual, group, and/or family psychotherapy. Individual therapy may include cognitive behavioral approaches, which help you to identify and change negative and unhelpful thoughts. It also helps you build coping skills and change behavioral patterns.</li><li>Medical care and monitoring, including care for the complications that eating disorders can cause</li><li>Nutrition counseling. Doctors, nurses, and counselors will help you eat healthy to reach and maintain a healthy weight.</li><li>Medicines, such as antidepressants, antipsychotics, or mood stabilizers, may help treat some eating disorders. The medicines can also help with the depression and <span class="qt0">anxiety</span> symptoms that often go along with eating disorders.</li></ul><p>Some people with serious eating disorders may need to be in a hospital or in a residential treatment program. Residential treatment programs combine housing and treatment services.</p><p>NIH: National Institute of Mental Health</p></content>
      <content name="mesh">Feeding and Eating Disorders</content>

    <document rank="15" url="https://medlineplus.gov/infantandnewborndevelopment.html">
      <content name="title">Infant and Newborn Development</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>When will my baby take his first step or say her first word? During their first year, babies start to develop skills they will use for the rest of their lives. The normal growth of babies can be broken down into the following areas:</p><ul><li>Gross motor - controlling the head, sitting, crawling, maybe even starting to walk</li><li>Fine motor - holding a spoon, picking up a piece of cereal between thumb and finger</li><li>Sensory - seeing, hearing, tasting, touching and smelling</li><li>Language - starting to make sounds, learning some words,  understanding what people say </li><li>Social - the ability to play with family members and other children</li></ul><p>Babies do not develop at the same rate. There is a wide range of what is considered "normal." Your baby may be ahead in some areas and slightly behind in others. If you are worried about possible delays, talk to your baby's health care provider.</p></content>

    <document rank="16" url="https://medlineplus.gov/lewybodydementia.html">
      <content name="title">Lewy Body Dementia</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Dementia with Lewy Bodies</content>
      <content name="altTitle">LBD</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Lewy body disease</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Parkinson's disease dementia</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is Lewy body dementia (LBD)?<p>Lewy body dementia (LBD) is one of the most common types of dementia in older adults. Dementia is a loss of mental functions that is severe enough to affect your daily life and activities. These functions include:</p><ul><li>Memory</li><li>Language skills</li><li>Visual perception (your ability to make sense of what you see)</li><li>Problem solving</li><li>Trouble with everyday tasks</li><li>The ability to focus and pay attention</li></ul>What are the types of Lewy body dementia (LBD)?<p>There are two types of LBD: dementia with Lewy bodies and Parkinson's disease dementia.</p><p>Both types cause the same changes in the brain. And, over time, they can cause similar symptoms. The main difference is in when the cognitive (thinking) and movement symptoms start.</p><p>Dementia with Lewy bodies causes problems with thinking ability that seem similar to Alzheimer's disease. Later, it also causes other symptoms, such as movement symptoms, visual hallucinations, and certain sleep disorders. It also causes more trouble with mental activities than with memory.</p><p>Parkinson's disease dementia starts as a movement disorder. It first causes the symptoms of Parkinson's disease: slowed movement, muscle stiffness, tremor, and a shuffling walk. Later on, it causes dementia.</p>What causes Lewy body dementia (LBD)?<p>LBD happens when Lewy bodies build up in parts of the brain that control memory, thinking, and movement.  Lewy bodies are abnormal deposits of a protein called alpha-synuclein. Researchers don't know exactly why these deposits form. But they do know that other diseases, such as Parkinson's disease, also involve a build-up of that protein.</p>Who is at risk for Lewy body dementia (LBD)?<p>The biggest risk factor for LBD is age; most people who get it are over age 50. People who have a family history of LBD are also at higher risk.</p>What are the symptoms of Lewy body dementia (LBD)?<p>LBD is a progressive disease. This means that the symptoms start slowly and get worse over time. The most common symptoms include changes in cognition, movement, sleep, and behavior:</p><ul><li>Dementia, which is a loss of mental functions that is severe enough to affect your daily life and activities</li><li>Changes in concentration, attention, alertness, and wakefulness. These changes usually happen from day to day. But sometimes they can also happen throughout the same day.</li><li>Visual hallucinations, which means seeing things that are not there</li><li>Problems with movement and posture, including slowness of movement, difficulty walking, and muscle stiffness. These are called parkinsonian motor symptoms.</li><li>REM sleep behavior disorder, a condition in which a person seems to act out dreams. It may include vivid dreaming, talking in one's sleep, violent movements, or falling out of bed. This may be the earliest symptom of LBD in some people. It can appear several years before any other LBD symptoms.</li><li>Changes in behavior and mood, such as depression, <span class="qt0">anxiety</span>, and apathy (a lack of interest in normal daily activities or events)</li></ul><p>In the early stages of LBD, symptoms can be mild, and people can function fairly normally. As the disease gets worse, people with LBD need more help due to problems with thinking and movement. In the later stages of the disease, they often cannot care for themselves.</p>How is Lewy body dementia (LBD) diagnosed?<p>There isn't one test that can diagnose LBD. It is important to see an experienced doctor to get a diagnosis. This would usually be a specialist such as a neurologist. The doctor will:</p><ul><li>Do a medical history, including taking a detailed account of the symptoms. The doctor will talk to both the patient and caregivers.</li><li>Do physical and neurological exams</li><li>Do tests to rule out other conditions that could cause similar symptoms. These could include blood tests and brain imaging tests.</li><li>Do neuropsychological tests to evaluate memory and other cognitive functions</li></ul><p>LBD can be hard to diagnose, because Parkinson's disease and Alzheimer's disease cause similar symptoms. Scientists think that Lewy body disease might be related to these diseases, or that they sometimes happen together.</p><p>It's also important to know which type of LBD a person has, so the doctor can treat that type's particular symptoms. It also helps the doctor understand how the disease will affect the person over time. The doctor makes a diagnosis based on when certain symptoms start:</p><ul><li>If cognitive symptoms start within a year of movement problems, the diagnosis is dementia with Lewy bodies</li><li>If cognitive problems start more than a year after the movement problems, the diagnosis is Parkinson's disease dementia</li></ul>What are the treatments for Lewy body dementia (LBD)?<p>There is no cure for LBD, but treatments can help with the symptoms:</p><ul><li>Medicines may help with some of the cognitive, movement, and psychiatric symptoms</li><li>Physical therapy can help with movement problems</li><li>Occupational therapy may help find ways to more easily do everyday activities</li><li>Speech therapy may help with swallowing difficulties and trouble speaking loudly and clearly</li><li>Mental health counseling can help people with LBD and their families learn how to manage difficult emotions and behaviors. It can also help them plan for the future.</li><li>Music or art therapy may reduce <span class="qt0">anxiety</span> and improve well-being</li></ul><p>Support groups can also be helpful for people with LBD and their caregivers. Support groups can give emotional and social support. They are also a place where people can share tips about how to deal with day-to-day challenges.</p><p>NIH: National Institute of Neurological Disorders and Stroke</p></content>

    <document rank="17" url="https://medlineplus.gov/marijuana.html">
      <content name="title">Marijuana</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Cannabinoids</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Cannabis</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Medical Marijuana</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Blunt</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Dope</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Ganja</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Grass</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Hash</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Joint</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Mary Jane</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Pot</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Reefer</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Weed</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is marijuana?<p>Marijuana is a green, brown, or gray mix of dried, crumbled parts from the marijuana plant.  The plant contains chemicals which act on your brain and can change your mood or consciousness.</p>How do people use marijuana?<p>There are many different ways that people use marijuana, including:</p><ul><li>Rolling it up and smoking it like a cigarette or cigar</li><li>Smoking it in a pipe</li><li>Mixing it in food and eating it</li><li>Brewing it as a tea</li><li>Smoking oils from the plant ("dabbing")</li><li>Using electronic vaporizers ("vaping")</li></ul>What are the effects of marijuana?<p>Marijuana can cause both short-term and long-term effects.</p><p>Short term:</p><p>While you are high, you may experience:</p><ul><li>Altered senses, such as seeing brighter colors</li><li>Altered sense of time, such as minutes seeming like hours</li><li>Changes in mood</li><li>Problems with body movement</li><li>Trouble with thinking, problem-solving, and memory</li><li>Increased appetite</li></ul><p>Long term:</p><p>In the long term, marijuana can cause health problems, such as:</p><ul><li>Problems with brain development. People who started using marijuana as teenagers may have trouble with thinking, memory, and learning.</li><li>Coughing and breathing problems, if you smoke marijuana frequently</li><li>Problems with child development during and after pregnancy, if a woman smokes marijuana while pregnant</li></ul>Can you overdose on marijuana?<p>It is possible to overdose on marijuana, if you take a very high dose. Symptoms of an overdose include <span class="qt0">anxiety</span>, panic, and a rapid heartbeat. In rare cases, an overdose can cause paranoia and hallucinations. There are no reports of people dying from using just marijuana.</p>Is marijuana addictive?<p>After using marijuana for a while, it is possible to get addicted to it. You are more likely to become addicted if you use marijuana every day or you started using it when you were a teenager. If you are addicted, you will have a strong need to take the drug. You may also need to smoke more and more of it to get the same high. When you try to quit, you may have mild withdrawal symptoms such as:</p><ul><li>Irritability</li><li>Trouble sleeping</li><li>Decreased appetite</li><li><span class="qt0">Anxiety</span></li><li>Cravings</li></ul>What is medical marijuana?<p>The marijuana plant has chemicals that can help with some health problems. More states are making it legal to use the plant as medicine for certain medical conditions. But there isn't enough research to show that the whole plant works to treat or cure these conditions. The U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA) has not approved the marijuana plant as a medicine. Marijuana is still illegal at the national level.</p><p>However, there have been scientific studies of cannabinoids, the chemicals in marijuana. The two main cannabinoids that are of medical interest are THC and CBD. The FDA has approved two drugs that contain THC. These drugs treat nausea caused by chemotherapy and increase appetite in patients who have severe weight loss from AIDS. There is also a liquid drug that contains CBD. It treats two forms of severe childhood epilepsy. Scientists are doing more research with marijuana and its ingredients to treat many diseases and conditions.</p><p>NIH: National Institute on Drug Abuse</p></content>

    <document rank="18" url="https://medlineplus.gov/olderadultmentalhealth.html">
      <content name="title">Older Adult Mental Health</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Emotional Health</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Mental Health, Older Adults</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Seniors' Mental Health</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Senior mental health</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>Mental health includes our emotional, psychological, and social well-being. It affects how we think, feel, and act as we cope with life. It also helps determine how we handle <span class="qt0">stress</span>, relate to others, and make choices. Mental health is important at every stage of life, including as we age.</p><p>Many older adults are at risk for mental health problems. But this does not mean that mental health problems are a normal part of aging. Studies show that most older adults feel satisfied with their lives, even though they may have more illnesses or physical problems.</p><p>Sometimes, however, important life changes can make you feel uneasy, stressed, and sad. These changes could include the death of a loved one, retirement, or dealing with a serious illness. Many older adults will eventually adjust to the changes. But some people will have more trouble adjusting. This can put them at risk for mental disorders such as depression and <span class="qt0">anxiety</span>.</p><p>It's important to recognize and treat mental disorders in older adults. These disorders don't just cause mental suffering. They can also make it harder for you to manage other health problems. This is especially true if those health problems are chronic.</p><p>Some of the warning signs of mental disorders in older adults include:</p><ul><li>Changes in mood or energy level</li><li>A change in your eating or sleeping habits</li><li>Withdrawing from the people and activities you enjoy</li><li>Feeling unusually confused, forgetful, angry, upset, worried, or scared</li><li>Feeling numb or like nothing matters</li><li>Having unexplained aches and pains</li><li>Feeling sadness or hopelessness</li><li>Smoking, drinking, or using drugs more than usual</li><li>Anger, irritability, or aggressiveness</li><li>Having thoughts and memories that you can't get out of your head</li><li>Hearing voices or believing things that are not true</li><li>Thinking of harming yourself or others</li></ul><p>If you think that you may have a mental health problem, get help. Talk therapy and/or medicines can treat mental disorders. If you don't know where to start, contact your primary care provider.</p></content>
      <content name="mesh">Mental Health</content>
      <content name="groupName">Mental Health and Behavior</content>
      <content name="groupName">Older Adults</content>

    <document rank="19" url="https://medlineplus.gov/primaryovarianinsufficiency.html">
      <content name="title">Primary Ovarian Insufficiency</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Ovarian Insufficiency</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Premature Ovarian Failure</content>
      <content name="altTitle">POF</content>
      <content name="altTitle">POI</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is primary ovarian insufficiency (POI)?<p>Primary ovarian insufficiency (POI) happens when your ovaries stop working normally before you are 40.</p><p>As you get older, fertility naturally decreases. This usually starts around age 40 or later.  Menstrual periods may also become irregular as you transition to menopause. If you have POI, these changes happen earlier, before age 40. Sometimes POI can start as early as the teenage years.</p><p>POI is different from premature menopause. With premature menopause, your periods stop before age 40. You can no longer get pregnant. The cause can be natural or it can be a disease, surgery, chemotherapy, or radiation. With POI, some women still have occasional periods. They may even get pregnant.  In most cases of POI, the cause is unknown.</p>What causes primary ovarian insufficiency (POI)?<p>In about 90% of cases, the exact cause of POI is unknown.</p><p>Research shows that POI is related to problems with the follicles. Follicles are small sacs in your ovaries where eggs grow and mature. One type of follicle problem is that you run out of working follicles earlier than normal. Another is that the follicles are not working properly. In most cases, the cause of the follicle problem is unknown. But sometimes the cause may be:</p><ul><li>Genetic disorders such as Fragile X syndrome and Turner syndrome</li><li>A low number of follicles</li><li>Autoimmune diseases, including thyroiditis and Addison disease</li><li>Chemotherapy or radiation therapy</li><li>Metabolic disorders</li><li>Exposure to toxic substances, such as cigarette smoke, chemicals, and pesticides</li></ul>Who is at risk for primary ovarian insufficiency (POI)?<p>Certain factors can raise your risk of POI:</p><ul><li>Family history. If you have a mother or sister with POI, you are more likely to have it.</li><li>Genes. Some changes to genes and genetic conditions put you at higher risk for POI. For example, women with Fragile X syndrome or Turner syndrome are at higher risk.</li><li>Certain diseases, such as autoimmune diseases and some viral infections.</li><li>Cancer treatments, such as chemotherapy and radiation therapy.</li><li>Age.You can get POI when you're young, but it becomes more common between the ages of 35 and 40.</li></ul>What are the symptoms of primary ovarian insufficiency (POI)?<p>The first sign of POI is usually irregular or missed periods. Later symptoms may be like those of natural menopause, including:</p><ul><li>Hot flashes</li><li>Night sweats</li><li>Irritability</li><li>Poor concentration</li><li>Decreased sex drive</li><li>Pain during sex</li><li>Vaginal dryness</li></ul><p>You may see a health care provider because of difficulty getting pregnant or trouble with infertility.</p>What other problems can primary ovarian insufficiency (POI) cause?<p>Since POI causes you to have lower levels of certain hormones, you are at greater risk for other health conditions, including:</p><ul><li><span class="qt0">Anxiety</span> and depression. Hormonal changes caused by POI can contribute to <span class="qt0">anxiety</span> or lead to depression.</li><li>Dry eye syndrome and eye surface disease. Some women with POI have one of these eye conditions. Both can cause discomfort and may lead to blurred vision. If not treated, these conditions can cause permanent eye damage.</li><li>Heart disease. Lower levels of estrogen can affect the muscles lining the arteries and can increase the buildup of cholesterol. These factors increase your risk of atherosclerosis (hardening of the arteries).</li><li>Infertility.</li><li>Low thyroid function. This problem also is called hypothyroidism. The thyroid is a gland that makes hormones that control your body's metabolism and energy level. Low levels of thyroid hormones can affect your metabolism and can cause very low energy, mental sluggishness, and other symptoms.</li><li>Osteoporosis. The hormone estrogen helps keep bones strong. Without enough estrogen, women with POI often develop osteoporosis. It is a bone disease that causes weak, brittle bones that are more likely to break.</li></ul>How is primary ovarian insufficiency (POI) diagnosed?<p>To diagnose POI, your provider may do:</p><ul><li>A medical history, including asking whether you have relatives with POI</li><li>A pregnancy test, to make sure that you are not pregnant</li><li>A physical exam, to look for signs of other disorders that could be causing your symptoms</li><li>Blood tests, to check for certain hormones. You may also have a blood test to do a chromosome analysis. A chromosome is the part of a cell that contains genetic information.</li><li>A pelvic ultrasound, to see whether the ovaries are enlarged or have multiple follicles.</li></ul>What are the treatments for primary ovarian insufficiency (POI)?<p>Currently, there is no proven treatment to restore normal function to the ovaries. But there are treatments for some of the symptoms of POI. There are also ways to lower your health risks and treat the conditions that POI can cause:</p><ul><li>Hormone therapy for menopause (sometimes called hormone replacement therapy or HRT). This is the most common treatment. It gives your body the estrogen and other hormones that your ovaries are not making. Hormone therapy for menopause improves sexual health and decreases the risks for heart disease and osteoporosis.</li><li>Calcium and vitamin D supplements. Because women with POI are at higher risk for osteoporosis, you should take calcium and vitamin D every day.</li><li>In vitro fertilization (IVF). If you have POI and you wish to become pregnant, you may consider trying IVF.</li><li>Regular physical activity and a healthy body weight.Getting regular exercise and controlling your weight can lower your risk for osteoporosis and heart disease.</li><li>Treatments for associated conditions. If you have a condition that is related to POI, it is important to treat that as well. Treatments may involve medicines and hormones.</li></ul><p>NIH: National Institute of Child Health and Human Development</p></content>
      <content name="mesh">Primary Ovarian Insufficiency</content>

    <document rank="20" url="https://medlineplus.gov/sexualassault.html">
      <content name="title">Sexual Assault</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Rape</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is sexual assault?<p>Sexual assault is any kind of sexual activity, contact, or experience that happens without your consent. That means the sexual activity happens even though you don't agree to it.</p><p>Sexual assault may happen with:</p><ul><li>Physical force or threats of force</li><li>Sexual coercion (being pressured, tricked, threatened, or forced in a nonphysical way to make you think you owe sex to someone)</li><li>Alcohol or drugs, including date rape drugs</li></ul><p>Sexual assault may also be called sexual violence or sexual abuse. It affects millions of people each year. Anyone can be a victim of sexual assault. Many victims first experience sexual assault during childhood. Most victims are girls and women. But many boys and men are also victims of sexual assault.</p><p>If you've been sexually assaulted, it's never your fault. It's something that happens to you.</p>What does sexual assault include?<p>Sexual assault is any unwanted sexual activity, including:</p><ul><li>Sexual activity with physical contact, which is everything from sexual touching or kissing to rape or attempted rape</li><li>Sexual activity without physical contact, which includes things that may happen in person, online, or through texts, such as:
	<ul><li>"Flashing" or exhibitionism (when someone shows you their genitals without asking you for permission)</li><li>Being forced to look at sexual images or pose for sexual photos</li><li>Being sent unwanted texts with sexual photos or messages</li><li>Voyeurism (when someone watches private sexual acts without permission)</li><li>Getting sexual threats or being sexually harassed (unwelcome sexual comments, requests for sexual favors, or other sexual behavior that's ongoing)</li></ul></li></ul><p>Any sexual activity that happens without your consent is sexual assault.</p>What does "consent" mean?<p>Giving your consent means that you clearly and freely say "yes" to sexual activity. Your consent means that you:</p><ul><li>Know and understand what's going on</li><li>Can say what you want to do and don't want to do</li><li>Are not underage</li><li>Are not being pressured or tricked to do things you don't want to do</li></ul><p>Giving consent is not:</p><ul><li>Silence. Not saying "no" doesn't mean you're saying "yes."</li><li>Having consented in the past. Having said "yes" to sex with someone in the past doesn't mean that you've agreed to all sexual activity in the future. That's true when you're dating or in a lasting relationship.</li><li>Being passive and not fighting off an attacker.</li></ul><p>You cannot give consent if you are:</p><ul><li>Drugged, drunk, passed out, or asleep.</li><li>Mentally unable to consent due to illness or disability.</li><li>Underage (States have different laws about how old you must be to give legal consent to sexual activity.).</li><li>Sexually coerced. This means that someone is pressuring you to take part in sexual activity. Agreeing to sex under pressure is not giving consent. The pressure may include:
	<ul><li>Abusing power over you, for example, a boss threatening your job.</li><li>Making threats to harm people you care about.</li><li>Making false promises to reward you for sex.</li><li>Threatening to end a relationship or spread rumors about you.</li><li>Wearing you down by constantly asking for sex or making you feel guilty or obligated.</li></ul>
</li></ul>Who commits sexual assault?<p>Both men and women commit sexual assault. A person who commits sexual assault may be a stranger, but more often it's someone you know, for example:</p><ul><li>A friend or acquaintance</li><li>Someone you work with</li><li>A neighbor</li><li>A family member</li><li>A current or past romantic partner, including a spouse</li></ul>What are the possible health effects from sexual assault?<p>Sexual assault may have many health effects. Many of them can affect your lifelong health and well-being. The effects may include:</p><ul><li>Physical effects such as:
	<ul><li>Bruises or genital injuries</li><li>Sexually transmitted infections (STIs)</li><li>Pregnancy</li><li>Ongoing problems with:
			<ul><li>Female reproductive health</li><li>Digestive disorders</li><li>Your heart</li><li>Sexual health</li></ul>
		</li></ul>
</li><li>Psychological effects such as:
	<ul><li>Depression or <span class="qt0">anxiety</span></li><li>Thoughts about suicide</li><li>Post-traumatic <span class="qt0">stress</span> disorder (PTSD)</li></ul>
</li></ul><p>Sexual assault victims are more likely to smoke, have  an alcohol use disorder (AUD), use illegal drugs, and have risky sexual behavior. Girls who experience sexual violence are more likely to become victims of intimate partner violence in adulthood.</p>What can I do if I'm a victim of sexual assault?<p>If you are in danger or need medical care, call 911. If you can, get away from the person who assaulted you and get to a safe place as fast as you can.</p><p>You can call the National Sexual Assault Hotline at 800-656-HOPE (4673) to connect with a sexual assault service provider in your area who can direct you to local resources.</p><p>Getting support after a sexual assault may help you cope better and reduce the long-term effects. Victim services like rape crisis centers may offer a safe, healing place where you can find support.</p><p>Studies show that treatments such as Trauma-Focused Cognitive Behavioral Therapy (TF-CBT) can help victims cope with depression, <span class="qt0">anxiety</span>, and post-traumatic <span class="qt0">stress</span> disorder.</p>Can sexual assault be prevented?<p>Sexual assault is never the victim's fault. No one who has been assaulted should be blamed for something that they did or did not do.</p><p>You can take some steps to try to be safer around others:</p><ul><li>Go to parties with friends. Look out for each other. If a friend seems drunk or drugged, get them to a safe place. Have a plan for how you'll get home, too.</li><li>Keep control of your drink. Someone could add alcohol or put a drug in it without you knowing.</li><li>Meet people for the first time in a public place.</li><li>Listen to your "gut feelings." If you feel uncomfortable around someone, leave.</li><li>Pay attention to your surroundings. When walking alone, stay in busy well-lit areas. And don't wear headphones; you want to be able to hear what's happening around you.</li></ul><p>Centers for Disease and Control and Prevention</p></content>

    <document rank="21" url="https://medlineplus.gov/sleepdisorders.html">
      <content name="title">Sleep Disorders</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Narcolepsy</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Night Terrors</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Sleeping Pills</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is sleep?<p>While you are sleeping  your brain and body functions are still active. Sleep is a natural process that helps your body restore energy, supports learning and memory, and keeps you healthy.</p><p>It's not just the number of hours of sleep you get that matters. The quality of your sleep is also important. During the night, your brain cycles through different stages of non-rapid eye movement (non-REM) sleep and rapid eye movement (REM) sleep. If your sleep is often interrupted, you may not get enough time in certain stages of sleep. Poor-quality sleep does more than make you feel tired. It can affect your physical and mental health, thinking, and daily functioning.</p>What are sleep disorders?<p>Sleep disorders are conditions that disturb your normal sleep patterns. There are many types of sleep disorders. Some general types include:</p><ul><li>Insomnia, which is trouble falling asleep or staying asleep.</li><li>Sleep-related breathing disorders such as sleep apnea, a condition in which you stop breathing for 10 seconds or more during sleep.</li><li>Sleep-related movement disorders such as restless leg syndrome (RLS), which involves tingling or prickling sensations in your legs, and a strong urge to move them.</li><li>Hypersomnia, which is extreme daytime sleepiness. This includes narcolepsy.</li><li>Circadian rhythm sleep-wake disorders, which are problems falling asleep or waking up at the right times. This may be due to shift work or jet lag.</li><li>Parasomnia, which is unusual behaviors during sleep or while falling asleep or waking up. This may include sleepwalking, talking, or eating during sleep.</li></ul><p>Some people who feel tired during the day have a true sleep disorder, while others simply don't get enough sleep. The amount of sleep you need depends on your age, lifestyle, health, and sleep patterns. Most adults need about 7-9 hours each night.</p>What causes sleep disorders?<p>There are different causes for different sleep disorders. They may include:</p><ul><li>Other health conditions, such as heart disease, lung disease, nerve disorders, or pain</li><li>Mental illnesses, including depression and <span class="qt0">anxiety</span></li><li>Medicines</li><li>Genetics</li></ul><p>Sometimes the cause is unknown.</p><p>Other factors that may contribute to sleep problems include:</p><ul><li>Using caffeine or alcohol</li><li>Having an irregular schedule, such as working the night shift</li><li>Aging, since older adults may sleep less, spend less time in deep sleep, and wake easily</li></ul>What are the symptoms of sleep disorders?<p>The symptoms of sleep disorders depend on the specific disorder. Some signs that you may have a sleep disorder include that:</p><ul><li>You regularly take more than 30 minutes to fall asleep.</li><li>You regularly wake up several times and then have trouble falling back to sleep, or you wake up too early in the morning.</li><li>You often feel sleepy during the day, take frequent naps, or fall asleep at the wrong times during the day.</li><li>Your bed partner says that when you sleep, you snore loudly, snort, gasp, make choking sounds, or stop breathing for short periods.</li><li>You have creeping or tingling sensations in your legs or arms that are relieved by moving or massaging them, especially in the evening and when trying to fall asleep.</li><li>Your bed partner notices that your legs or arms jerk often during sleep.</li><li>You have vivid, dreamlike experiences while falling asleep or dozing.</li><li>You have episodes of sudden muscle weakness triggered by strong emotions.</li><li>You feel as though you cannot move or speak for a brief time when you first wake up.</li></ul>How are sleep disorders diagnosed?<p>To diagnose a sleep disorder, your health care provider will review your medical and sleep history and do a physical exam. You may also have a sleep study (polysomnogram). Most sleep studies  monitor and record data about your body during a full night of sleep. The data includes:</p><ul><li>Brain wave activity</li><li>Eye movements</li><li>Breathing rate</li><li>Blood pressure</li><li>Heart rate and electrical activity in the heart and other muscles</li></ul><p>Other types of sleep studies may check how quickly you fall asleep during daytime naps or how well you can stay awake during the day.</p>What are the treatments for sleep disorders?<p>Treatments for sleep disorders depend on which disorder you have. They may include:</p><ul><li>Practicing good sleep habits and other lifestyle changes, such as eating a healthy diet and getting regular exercise</li><li>Reducing <span class="qt0">anxiety</span> about sleep with cognitive behavioral therapy or relaxation techniques</li><li>Using a CPAP (continuous positive airway pressure) machine for sleep apnea</li><li>Using bright light therapy (in the morning)</li><li>Taking sleep aid medicines, or natural products such as melatonin</li></ul><p>Always check with your provider before taking any medicine or supplement. Most sleep aid medicines are generally for short-term use.</p></content>

    <document rank="22" url="https://medlineplus.gov/stress.html">
      <content name="title"><span class="qt0">Stress</span></content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Psychological <span class="qt0">Stress</span></content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is <span class="qt0">stress</span>?<p><span class="qt0">Stress</span> is how your brain and body respond to a challenge or demand. When you are stressed, your body releases certain hormones. Hormones are chemicals that travel in your bloodstream and control how different parts of your body work. The hormones that are released when you are stressed make you alert and ready to act. They can raise your blood pressure, heart rate, and blood glucose (blood sugar) levels. This response is sometimes called a "fight or flight" response.</p><p>Everyone gets stressed from time to time. There are different types of <span class="qt0">stress</span>. It can be short-term or long-term. It can be caused by something that happens once or something that keeps happening.</p><p>Not all <span class="qt0">stress</span> is bad. In fact, it can help you survive in a dangerous situation. For example, one kind of <span class="qt0">stress</span> is the jolt you may feel when a car pulls out in front of you while you are driving. This jolt of hormones helps you quickly hit the brakes to avoid an accident. A little short-term <span class="qt0">stress</span> can sometimes be helpful. For example, the <span class="qt0">stress</span> of having a deadline for school or your job may push you to get your work done on time. Once you finish it, that <span class="qt0">stress</span> goes away.</p><p>But <span class="qt0">stress</span> that lasts a long time can harm your health.</p>What causes long-term <span class="qt0">stress</span>?<p>Long-term <span class="qt0">stress</span>, or chronic <span class="qt0">stress</span>, lasts for weeks, months, or longer. As you go about your life, your body is acting as if you're being threatened.</p><p>Causes of long-term <span class="qt0">stress</span> include:</p><ul><li>Routine <span class="qt0">stress</span> from the demands of work, school, family needs, money problems, and other daily pressures that don't stop.</li><li><span class="qt0">Stress</span> from sudden, difficult changes in your life, such as divorce, illness, losing your job, or other unhappy life events that often have a long impact.</li><li>Traumatic <span class="qt0">stress</span>, which may happen when you're in danger of serious harm or death. Examples include being in a bad accident, a war, a flood, earthquake, or other frightening event. This type of <span class="qt0">stress</span> can cause a long-lasting problem called post-traumatic <span class="qt0">stress</span> disorder (PTSD).</li></ul>How can long term-<span class="qt0">stress</span> harm my health?<p>People respond to <span class="qt0">stress</span> in different ways. If you're stressed for a long time you may notice that you are:</p><ul><li>Getting sick more often than usual because <span class="qt0">stress</span> weakens your body's ability to fight germs</li><li>Having stomach problems or trouble digesting food</li><li>Having trouble sleeping</li><li>Having headaches</li><li>Feeling sad, angry, or easily upset</li></ul><p>When <span class="qt0">stress</span> keeps going, your body acts as if you're always in danger. That's a lot of strain that may play a part in developing serious health problems, including:</p><ul><li>Depression</li><li><span class="qt0">Anxiety</span></li><li>Heart disease</li><li>High blood pressure</li><li>Diabetes</li></ul><p>It's possible to get used to the symptoms of <span class="qt0">stress</span> and not even realize there's a problem. So when there's a lot of <span class="qt0">stress</span> in your life, it's important to pay attention to how it affects you so you can do something about it.</p>How can I manage long-term <span class="qt0">stress</span>?<p>Simple things that improve your mental health may be helpful in managing long-term <span class="qt0">stress</span>, such as:</p><ul><li>Get regular exercise. A 30-minute daily walk can help you feel better and help keep your immune system strong, so you don't get sick.</li><li>Try relaxing activities. You could look for an app or wellness program that uses breathing, meditation, or muscle relaxation exercises.</li><li>Get enough sleep every night.</li><li>Avoid too much caffeine.</li><li>Decide what you need to do now and what can wait. And focus on what you got done each day, not on what you weren't able to do.</li><li>Ask your family or friends for support.</li></ul>When should I ask my health care provider for help with <span class="qt0">stress</span>?<p>Get help if you're having severe symptoms for 2 weeks or more, including:</p><ul><li>Trouble sleeping</li><li>Changes in your eating that cause unwanted changes in your weight</li><li>Troubles getting out of bed because of your mood</li><li>Difficulty focusing your thoughts</li><li>Losing interest in things you usually enjoy</li><li>Not being able to do your usual daily activities</li></ul><p>Always get help right away if <span class="qt0">stress</span> is causing you to:</p><ul><li>Have thoughts of suicide</li><li>Feel you can't cope</li><li>Use drugs or alcohol more often than usual</li></ul><p>Your health care provider may refer you to a mental health professional such as a psychologist or social worker.</p><p>NIH: National Institute of Mental Health</p></content>
      <content name="mesh"><span class="qt0">Stress</span>, Psychological</content>

    <document rank="23" url="https://medlineplus.gov/teenmentalhealth.html">
      <content name="title">Teen Mental Health</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Mental Health, Teen</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>Being a teenager is hard. You're under <span class="qt0">stress</span> to be liked, do well in school, get along with your family, and make big decisions. You can't avoid most of these pressures, and worrying about them is normal. But feeling very sad, hopeless or worthless could be warning signs of a mental health problem.</p><p>Mental health problems are real, painful, and sometimes severe. You might need help if you have the signs mentioned above, or if you:</p><ul><li>Often feel very angry or very worried </li><li>Feel grief for a long time after a loss or death</li><li>Think your mind is controlled or out of control</li><li>Use alcohol or drugs</li><li>Exercise, diet and/or binge-eat obsessively </li><li>Hurt other people or destroy property </li><li>Do reckless things that could harm you or others</li><li>Feel depressed (sad and hopeless)</li></ul><p>Mental health problems can be treated. To find help, talk to your parents, school counselor, or health care provider.</p></content>

    <document rank="24" url="https://medlineplus.gov/toddlerdevelopment.html">
      <content name="title">Toddler Development</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>Mastering new skills such as how to walk, talk, and use the potty are developmental milestones.  It is exciting to watch your toddler learn new skills. The normal development of children aged 1-3 includes several areas:</p><ul><li>Gross motor - walking, running, climbing</li><li>Fine motor - feeding themselves, drawing</li><li>Sensory - seeing, hearing, tasting, touching, and smelling</li><li>Language - saying single words, then sentences</li><li>Social - playing with others, taking turns, doing fantasy play</li></ul><p>Toddlers do not develop at the same rate. There is a wide range of what is considered "normal." Your child may be ahead in some areas and slightly behind in others. If you are worried about possible delays, talk to your child's health care provider.</p></content>

    <document rank="1" url="https://medlineplus.gov/asthma.html">
      <content name="title"><span class="qt0">Asthma</span></content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Bronchial <span class="qt0">Asthma</span></content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is <span class="qt0">asthma</span>?<p><span class="qt0">Asthma</span> is a chronic (long-term) lung disease. It affects your airways, the tubes that carry air in and out of your lungs. When you have <span class="qt0">asthma</span>, your airways can become inflamed and narrowed. This can cause wheezing, coughing, and tightness in your chest. When these symptoms get worse than usual, it is called an <span class="qt0">asthma</span> attack or flare-up.</p>What causes <span class="qt0">asthma</span>?<p>The exact cause of <span class="qt0">asthma</span> is unknown. Genetics and your environment likely play a role in who gets <span class="qt0">asthma</span>.</p><p>An <span class="qt0">asthma</span> attack can happen when you are exposed to an <span class="qt0">asthma</span> trigger. An <span class="qt0">asthma</span> trigger is something that can set off or worsen your <span class="qt0">asthma</span> symptoms. Different triggers can cause different types of <span class="qt0">asthma</span>:</p><ul><li>Allergic <span class="qt0">asthma</span> is caused by allergens. Allergens are substances that cause an allergic reaction. They can include
	<ul><li>Dust mites</li><li>Mold</li><li>Pets</li><li>Pollen from grass, trees, and weeds</li><li>Waste from pests such as cockroaches and mice</li></ul>
</li><li>Nonallergic <span class="qt0">asthma</span> is caused by triggers that are not allergens, such as
	<ul><li>Breathing in cold air</li><li>Certain medicines</li><li>Household chemicals</li><li>Infections such as colds and the flu</li><li>Outdoor air pollution</li><li>Tobacco smoke</li></ul>
</li><li>Occupational <span class="qt0">asthma</span> is caused by breathing in chemicals or industrial dusts at work</li><li>Exercise-induced <span class="qt0">asthma</span> happens during physical exercise, especially when the air is dry</li></ul><p><span class="qt0">Asthma</span> triggers may be different for each person and can change over time.</p>Who is at risk for <span class="qt0">asthma</span>?<p><span class="qt0">Asthma</span> affects people of all ages, but it often starts during childhood. Certain factors can raise your risk of having <span class="qt0">asthma</span>:</p><ul><li>Being exposed to secondhand smoke when your mother is pregnant with you or when you are a small child</li><li>Being exposed to certain substances at work, such as chemical irritants or industrial dusts</li><li>Genetics and family history. You are more likely to have <span class="qt0">asthma</span> if one of your parents has it, especially if it's your mother.</li><li>Race or ethnicity. Black and African Americans and Puerto Ricans are at higher risk of <span class="qt0">asthma</span> than people of other races or ethnicities.</li><li>Having other diseases or conditions such as obesity and allergies</li><li>Often having viral respiratory infections as a young child</li><li>Sex. In children, <span class="qt0">asthma</span> is more common in boys. In teens and adults, it is more common in women.</li></ul>What are the symptoms of <span class="qt0">asthma</span>?<p>The symptoms of <span class="qt0">asthma</span> include:</p><ul><li>Chest tightness</li><li>Coughing, especially at night or early morning</li><li>Shortness of breath</li><li>Wheezing, which causes a whistling sound when you breathe out</li></ul><p>These symptoms can range from mild to severe. You may have them every day or only once in a while.</p><p>When you are having an <span class="qt0">asthma</span> attack, your symptoms get much worse. The attacks may come on gradually or suddenly. Sometimes they can be life-threatening. They are more common in people who have severe <span class="qt0">asthma</span>. If you are having <span class="qt0">asthma</span> attacks, you may need a change in your treatment.</p>How is <span class="qt0">asthma</span> diagnosed?<p>Your health care provider may use many tools to diagnose <span class="qt0">asthma</span>:</p><ul><li>Physical exam</li><li>Medical history</li><li>Lung function tests, including spirometry, to test how well your lungs work</li><li>Tests to measure how your airways react to specific exposures. During this test, you inhale different concentrations of allergens or medicines that may tighten the muscles in your airways. Spirometry is done before and after the test.</li><li>Peak expiratory flow (PEF) tests to measure how fast you can blow air out using maximum effort</li><li>Fractional exhaled nitric oxide (FeNO) tests to measure levels of nitric oxide in your breath when you breathe out. High levels of nitric oxide may mean that your lungs are inflamed.</li><li>Allergy skin or blood tests, if you have a history of allergies. These tests check which allergens cause a reaction from your immune system.</li></ul>What are the treatments for <span class="qt0">asthma</span>?<p>If you have <span class="qt0">asthma</span>, you will work with your health care provider to create a treatment plan. The plan will include ways to manage your <span class="qt0">asthma</span> symptoms and prevent <span class="qt0">asthma</span> attacks. It will include:</p><ul><li>Strategies to avoid triggers. For example, if tobacco smoke is a trigger for you, you should not smoke or allow other people to smoke in your home or car.</li><li>Short-term relief medicines,  also called quick-relief medicines. They help prevent symptoms or relieve symptoms during an <span class="qt0">asthma</span> attack. They include an inhaler to carry with you all the time. It may also include other types of medicines which work quickly to help open your airways.</li><li>Control medicines.  You take them every day to help prevent symptoms. They work by reducing airway inflammation and preventing narrowing of the airways.</li></ul><p>If you have a severe attack and the short-term relief medicines do not work, you will need emergency care.</p><p>Your provider may adjust your treatment until <span class="qt0">asthma</span> symptoms are controlled.</p><p>Sometimes <span class="qt0">asthma</span> is severe and cannot be controlled with other treatments. If you are an adult with uncontrolled <span class="qt0">asthma</span>, in some cases your provider might suggest bronchial thermoplasty. This is a procedure that uses heat to shrink the smooth muscle in the lungs. Shrinking the muscle reduces your airway's ability to tighten and allows you to breathe more easily. The procedure has some risks, so it's important to discuss them with your provider.</p></content>

      <content name="title"><span class="qt0">Asthma</span> in Children</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Childhood <span class="qt0">Asthma</span></content>
      <content name="altTitle">Pediatric <span class="qt0">asthma</span></content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is <span class="qt0">asthma</span>? <p><span class="qt0">Asthma</span> is a chronic (long-term) lung disease. It affects your airways, the tubes that carry air in and out of your lungs. When you have <span class="qt0">asthma</span>, your airways can become inflamed and narrowed. This can cause wheezing, coughing, and tightness in your chest. When these symptoms get worse than usual, it is called an <span class="qt0">asthma</span> attack or flare-up.</p>How does <span class="qt0">asthma</span> affect children? <p><span class="qt0">Asthma</span> often starts during childhood, usually before age 5. Many children have <span class="qt0">asthma</span> - it is the most common chronic disease of childhood. It can cause children to miss school and end up in the hospital. But treatments can help manage <span class="qt0">asthma</span>.</p>What causes <span class="qt0">asthma</span> in children?<p>The exact cause of <span class="qt0">asthma</span> is unknown. Genetics and environment likely play a role in which children get <span class="qt0">asthma</span>.</p><p>An <span class="qt0">asthma</span> attack can happen when your child is exposed to an <span class="qt0">asthma</span> trigger. An <span class="qt0">asthma</span> trigger is something that can set off or worsen <span class="qt0">asthma</span> symptoms. Different triggers can cause different types of <span class="qt0">asthma</span>:</p><ul><li>Allergic <span class="qt0">asthma</span> is caused by allergens. Allergens are substances that cause an allergic reaction. They can include
	<ul><li>Dust mites</li><li>Mold</li><li>Pets</li><li>Pollen from grass, trees, and weeds</li><li>Waste from pests such as cockroaches and mice</li></ul>
</li><li>Nonallergic <span class="qt0">asthma</span> is caused by triggers that are not allergens, such as
	<ul><li>Breathing in cold air</li><li>Certain medicines</li><li>Household chemicals</li><li>Infections such as colds and the flu</li><li>Outdoor air pollution</li><li>Tobacco smoke</li></ul>
</li><li>Exercise-induced <span class="qt0">asthma</span> happens during physical exercise, especially when the air is dry</li></ul><p><span class="qt0">Asthma</span> triggers may be different for each child and can change over time.</p>Which children are at risk for <span class="qt0">asthma</span>?<p>Certain factors raise the risk of <span class="qt0">asthma</span> in children:</p><ul><li>Being exposed to secondhand smoke when their mother is pregnant with them or when they are small children</li><li>Genetics and family history. Children are more likely to have <span class="qt0">asthma</span> if one of their parents has it, especially if it's the mother. </li><li>Race or ethnicity. Black and African Americans and Puerto Ricans are at higher risk of <span class="qt0">asthma</span> than people of other races or ethnicities.</li><li>Having other diseases or conditions such as  obesity and allergies</li><li>Often having viral respiratory infections as young children</li><li>Sex. In children, <span class="qt0">asthma</span> is more common in boys. In teens, it is more common in girls.</li></ul>What are the symptoms of <span class="qt0">asthma</span> in children?<p>The symptoms of <span class="qt0">asthma</span> in children include:</p><ul><li>Chest tightness</li><li>Coughing, especially at night or early morning</li><li>Breathing problems, such as shortness of breath, rapid breathing, or gasping for air</li><li>Feeling tired</li><li>Dark circles under the eyes</li><li>Being irritable</li><li>Wheezing, which causes a whistling sound when they breathe out</li><li>Trouble eating or sucking (in infants)</li></ul><p>These symptoms can range from mild to severe. They may happen often or only once in a while.</p><p>When children have an <span class="qt0">asthma</span> attack, their symptoms get much worse. The attacks may come on gradually or suddenly. Sometimes they can be life-threatening. Warning signs of a severe attack include severe coughing, serious breathing problems, and turning very pale or blue in the face, lips and/or fingernails. If your child has those symptoms, get medical help right away.</p>How is <span class="qt0">asthma</span> in children diagnosed?<p>It can be hard to diagnose <span class="qt0">asthma</span> in children, especially if they are young. <span class="qt0">Asthma</span> has similar symptoms as other childhood conditions. And some children may not have <span class="qt0">asthma</span> symptoms very often, so it may seem like they are having respiratory infections instead.</p><p>Your child's health care provider may use many tools to diagnose <span class="qt0">asthma</span>:</p><ul><li>Physical exam</li><li>Medical history</li><li>Chest x-ray</li><li>Lung function tests, including spirometry, to test how well the lungs work. Younger children are usually not able to do these tests.</li><li>Allergy skin or blood tests, if you have a history of allergies. These tests check which allergens cause a reaction from your immune system.</li></ul><p>If you have a young child who cannot do lung function tests, the provider may suggest doing a trial of <span class="qt0">asthma</span> medicines. The trial involves giving your child the medicines for several weeks to see whether the symptoms get better.</p>What are the treatments for <span class="qt0">asthma</span> in children?<p>If your child has <span class="qt0">asthma</span>, you will work with their health care provider to create a treatment plan. The plan will include ways to manage your child's <span class="qt0">asthma</span> symptoms and prevent <span class="qt0">asthma</span> attacks, such as:</p><ul><li>Strategies to avoid triggers. For example, if tobacco smoke is a trigger for your child, you should not allow anyone to smoke in your home or car.</li><li>Short-term relief medicines, also called quick-relief medicines. They help prevent symptoms or relieve symptoms during an <span class="qt0">asthma</span> attack. They include an inhaler to have for your child at all times. It may also include other types of medicines which work quickly to help open your child's airways.</li><li>Control medicines.  They work by reducing airway inflammation and preventing narrowing of the airways. Not all children will take control medicines. Whether or not your child needs them depends on how severe the <span class="qt0">asthma</span> is and how often your child has symptoms.</li></ul><p>If your child has a severe attack and the short-term relief medicines do not work, get medical help right away.</p><p>Your child's provider may adjust the treatment until the <span class="qt0">asthma</span> symptoms are controlled.</p></content>

    <document rank="2" url="https://medlineplus.gov/allergy.html">
      <content name="title">Allergy</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Allergic Rhinitis</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Hypersensitivity</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>An allergy is a reaction by your immune system to something that does not bother most other people. People who have allergies often are sensitive to more than one thing. Substances that often cause reactions are:</p><ul><li>Pollen</li><li>Dust mites</li><li>Mold spores</li><li>Pet dander</li><li>Food</li><li>Insect stings</li><li>Medicines</li></ul><p>Normally, your immune system fights germs. It is your body's defense system. In most allergic reactions, however, it is responding to a false alarm. Genes and the environment probably both play a role.</p><p>Allergies can cause a variety of symptoms such as a runny nose, sneezing, itching, rashes, swelling, or <span class="qt0">asthma</span>. Allergies can range from minor to severe.  Anaphylaxis is a severe reaction that can be life-threatening.   Doctors use skin and blood tests to diagnose allergies. Treatments include medicines, allergy shots, and avoiding the substances that cause the reactions.</p></content>
      <content name="mesh">Hypersensitivity</content>
      <content name="groupName">Ear, Nose and Throat</content>
      <content name="groupName">Immune System</content>
