    <document rank="3" url="https://medlineplus.gov/copd.html">
      <content name="title">COPD</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is COPD (chronic obstructive pulmonary disease)?<p>COPD (chronic obstructive pulmonary disease) is a group of lung diseases that make it hard to breathe and get worse over time.</p><p>Normally, the airways and air sacs in your lungs are elastic or stretchy. When you breathe in, the airways bring air to the air sacs. The air sacs fill up with air, like a small balloon. When you breathe out, the air sacs deflate, and the air goes out. If you have COPD, less air flows in and out of your airways because of one or more problems:</p><ul><li>The airways and air sacs in your lungs become less elastic</li><li>The walls between many of the air sacs are destroyed</li><li>The walls of the airways become thick and inflamed</li><li>The airways make more mucus than usual and can become clogged</li></ul>What are the types of COPD (chronic obstructive pulmonary disease)?<p>COPD includes two main types:</p><ul><li>Emphysema affects the air sacs in your lungs, as well as the walls between them. They become damaged and are less elastic.</li><li>Chronic bronchitis, in which the lining of your airways is constantly irritated and inflamed. This causes the lining to swell and make mucus.</li></ul><p>Most people with COPD have both emphysema and chronic bronchitis, but how severe each type is can be different from person to person.</p>What causes COPD (chronic obstructive pulmonary disease)?<p>The cause of COPD is usually long-term exposure to irritants that damage your lungs and airways. In the United States, cigarette smoke is the main cause. Pipe, cigar, and other types of tobacco smoke can also cause COPD, especially if you inhale them.</p><p>Exposure to other inhaled irritants can contribute to COPD. These include secondhand smoke, air pollution, and chemical fumes or dusts from the environment or workplace.</p><p>Rarely, a genetic condition called alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency can play a role in causing COPD.</p>Who is at risk for COPD (chronic obstructive pulmonary disease)?<p>The risk factors for COPD include:</p><ul><li>Smoking. This is the main risk factor. Up to 75% of people who have COPD smoke or used to smoke.</li><li>Long-term exposure to other lung irritants, such as secondhand smoke, air pollution, and chemical fumes and dusts from the environment or workplace</li><li>Age. Most people who have COPD are at least 40 years old when their symptoms begin.</li><li>Genetics. This includes alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency, which is a genetic condition. Also, smokers who get COPD are more likely to get it if they have a family history of COPD.</li><li><span class="qt0">Asthma</span>. People who have <span class="qt0">asthma</span> have more risk of developing COPD than people who don't have <span class="qt0">asthma</span>. But most people with <span class="qt0">asthma</span> will not get COPD.</li></ul>What are the symptoms of COPD (chronic obstructive pulmonary disease)?<p>At first, you may have no symptoms or only mild symptoms. As the disease gets worse, your symptoms usually become more severe. They can include:</p><ul><li>Frequent coughing or a cough that produces a lot of mucus</li><li>Wheezing</li><li>A whistling or squeaky sound when you breathe</li><li>Shortness of breath, especially with physical activity</li><li>Tightness in your chest</li></ul><p>Some people with COPD get frequent respiratory infections such as colds and the flu. In severe cases, COPD can cause weight loss, weakness in your lower muscles, and swelling in your ankles, feet, or legs.</p>How is COPD (chronic obstructive pulmonary disease) diagnosed?<p>Your health care provider may use many tools to make a diagnosis:</p><ul><li>A medical history, which includes asking about your symptoms</li><li>A family history</li><li>Various tests, such as lung function tests, a chest x-ray or CT scan, and blood tests</li></ul><p>Your doctor will diagnose COPD based on your signs and symptoms, your medical and family histories, and test results.</p>What are the treatments for COPD (chronic obstructive pulmonary disease)?<p>There is no cure for COPD. However, treatments can help with symptoms, slow the progress of the disease, and improve your ability to stay active. There are also treatments to prevent or treat complications of the disease. Treatments include:</p><ul><li>Lifestyle changes, such as
	<ul><li>Quitting smoking if you are a smoker. This is the most important step you can take to treat COPD.</li><li>Avoiding secondhand smoke and places where you might breathe in other lung irritants</li><li>Ask your health care provider for an eating plan that will meet your nutritional needs. Also ask about how much physical activity you can do. Physical activity can strengthen the muscles that help you breathe and improve your overall wellness.</li></ul></li><li>Medicines, such as
	<ul><li>Bronchodilators, which relax the muscles around your airways. This helps open your airways and makes breathing easier. Most bronchodilators are taken through an inhaler. In more severe cases, the inhaler may also contain steroids to reduce inflammation.</li><li>Vaccines for the flu and pneumococcal pneumonia, since people with COPD are at higher risk for serious problems from these diseases</li><li>Antibiotics if you get a bacterial lung infection</li></ul></li><li>Oxygen therapy, if you have severe COPD and low levels of oxygen in your blood. Oxygen therapy can help you breathe better. You may need extra oxygen all the time or only at certain times.</li><li>Pulmonary rehabilitation, which is a program that helps improve the well-being of people who have chronic breathing problems. It may include
	<ul><li>An exercise program</li><li>Disease management training</li><li>Nutritional counseling</li><li>Psychological counseling</li></ul></li><li>Surgery, usually as a last resort for people who have severe symptoms that have not gotten better with medicines:
	<ul><li>For COPD that is mainly related to emphysema, there are surgeries that: 
			<ul><li>Remove damaged lung tissue</li><li>Remove large air spaces (bullae) that can form when air sacs are destroyed. The bullae can interfere with breathing.</li></ul>
		</li><li>For severe COPD, some people may need lung transplant</li></ul></li></ul><p>If you have COPD, it's important to know when and where to get help for your symptoms. You should get emergency care if you have severe symptoms, such as trouble catching your breath or talking. Call your health care provider if your symptoms are getting worse or if you have signs of an infection, such as a fever.</p>Can COPD (chronic obstructive pulmonary disease) be prevented?<p>Since smoking causes most cases of COPD, the best way to prevent it is to not smoke. It's also important to try to avoid lung irritants such as secondhand smoke, air pollution, chemical fumes, and dusts.</p><p>NIH: National Heart, Lung, and Blood Institute</p></content>

People who have <span class="qt0">asthma</span> have more risk of developing COPD than people who don't have <span class="qt0">asthma</span>. But most people with <span class="qt0">asthma</span> will not get  ... </content>

    <document rank="4" url="https://medlineplus.gov/flu.html">
      <content name="title">Flu</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Grippe</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Influenza</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Seasonal flu</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is the flu?<p>The flu, also called influenza, is a respiratory infection caused by viruses. Each year, millions of Americans get sick with the flu. Sometimes it causes mild illness. But it can also be serious or even deadly, especially for people over 65, newborn babies, and people with certain chronic illnesses.</p>What causes the flu?<p>The flu is caused by flu viruses that spread from person to person. When someone with the flu coughs, sneezes, or talks, they spray tiny droplets. These droplets can land in the mouths or noses of people who are nearby. Less often, a person may get flu by touching a surface or object that has flu virus on it and then touching their own mouth, nose, or possibly their eyes.</p>What are the symptoms of the flu?<p>Symptoms of the flu come on suddenly and may include:</p><ul><li>Fever or feeling feverish/chills</li><li>Cough</li><li>Sore throat</li><li>Runny or stuffy nose</li><li>Muscle or body aches</li><li>Headaches</li><li>Fatigue (tiredness)</li></ul><p>Some people may also have vomiting and diarrhea. This is more common in children.</p><p>Sometimes people have trouble figuring out whether they have a cold or the flu. There are differences between them:</p>Signs and SymptomsColdFluStart of symptomsSlowlySuddenlyFeverRarelyUsuallyAchesSometimes (slight)UsuallyFatigue, weaknessSometimesUsuallyHeadacheRarelyCommonStuffy nose, sneezing, or sore throatCommonSometimes<p>Sometimes people say that they have a "flu" when they really have something else. For example, "stomach flu" isn't the flu; it's gastroenteritis.</p>What other problems can the flu cause?<p>Some people who get the flu will develop complications. Some of these complications can be serious or even life-threatening. They include:</p><ul><li>Bronchitis</li><li>Ear infection</li><li>Sinus infection</li><li>Pneumonia</li><li>Inflammation of the heart (myocarditis), brain (encephalitis), or muscle tissues (myositis, rhabdomyolysis)</li></ul><p>The flu also can make chronic health problems worse. For example, people with <span class="qt0">asthma</span> may have <span class="qt0">asthma</span> attacks while they have flu.</p><p>Certain people are more likely to have complications from the flu, including:</p><ul><li>Adults 65 and older</li><li>Pregnant women</li><li>Children younger than 5</li><li>People with certain chronic health conditions, such as <span class="qt0">asthma</span>, diabetes, and heart disease</li></ul>How is the flu diagnosed?<p>To diagnose the flu, health care providers will first do a medical history and ask about your symptoms. There are several tests for the flu. For the tests, your provider will swipe the inside of your nose or the back of your throat with a swab. Then the swab will be tested for the flu virus.</p><p>Some tests are quick and give results in 15-20 minutes. But these tests are not as accurate as other flu tests. These other tests can give you the results in one hour or several hours.</p>What are the treatments for the flu?<p>Most people with the flu recover on their own without medical care. People with mild cases of the flu should stay home and avoid contact with others, except to get medical care.</p><p>But if you have symptoms of flu and are in a high risk group or are very sick or worried about your illness, contact your health care provider. You might need antiviral medicines to treat your flu. Antiviral medicines can make the illness milder and shorten the time you are sick. They also can prevent serious flu complications. They usually work best when you start taking them within 2 days of getting sick.</p>Can the flu be prevented?<p>The best way to prevent the flu is to get a flu vaccine every year. But it's also important to have good health habits like covering your cough and washing your hands often. This can help stop the spread of germs and prevent the flu.</p><p>Centers for Disease Control and Prevention</p></content>

    <document rank="5" url="https://medlineplus.gov/smoking.html">
      <content name="title">Smoking</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Nicotine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Tobacco Smoking</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Cigar smoking</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Cigarette smoking</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Pipe smoking</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What are the health effects of smoking?<p>There's no way around it; smoking is bad for your health. It harms nearly every organ of the body, even some that you would not expect. Cigarette smoking causes many cancers and other health problems. It is also the cause of nearly one in five deaths in the United States.</p><p>Some of the many health problems that smoking can cause include:</p><ul><li>Cancers. Smoking is the most common cause of lung and oral cancers. But it can also cause cancer in many other parts of your body, such as in your larynx (voice box), esophagus, throat, bladder, kidney, liver, stomach, pancreas, colon and rectum, and cervix. It can also cause acute myeloid leukemia (AML). </li><li>Lung diseases. Smoking is the most common cause of COPD. It can also worsen <span class="qt0">asthma</span> and raise your risk of pneumonia.</li><li>Cardiovascular diseases.  Smoking can damage your blood vessels and make them thicken and grow narrower. This makes your heart  beat faster and raises your blood pressure. Smoking also increases your risk of blood clots and stroke.</li><li>Vision problems. Smoking can raise your risk of  cataracts and cause macular degeneration (AMD).</li></ul><p>You have a greater chance of certain pregnancy problems if you smoke while pregnant.   Your baby is also at higher risk of dying of sudden infant death syndrome (SIDS).</p><p>Smoking also causes addiction to nicotine, a stimulant drug that is in tobacco. Nicotine addiction makes it much harder for people to quit smoking.</p>What are the health risks of secondhand smoke?<p>Your smoke is also bad for other people. If they breathe in your secondhand smoke, they can get many of the same problems as smokers do. These problems can include heart disease and lung cancer. Children exposed to secondhand smoke have a higher risk of ear infections, colds, pneumonia, bronchitis, and more severe <span class="qt0">asthma</span>. If you breathe secondhand smoke while pregnant, you're more likely to have preterm labor and a baby with low birth weight.</p>Are other forms of tobacco also dangerous?<p>Besides cigarettes, there are several other forms of tobacco. Some people smoke tobacco in cigars and water pipes (hookahs). These forms of tobacco also contain harmful chemicals and nicotine. Some cigars contain as much tobacco as an entire pack of cigarettes.</p><p>E-cigarettes often look like cigarettes, but they work differently. They are battery-operated smoking devices. Using an e-cigarette is called vaping. Researchers still have a lot to learn about the health effects of using e-cigarettes. We do know that they contain nicotine, which is highly addictive and a health danger to you and your fetus if you are pregnant, children, and teens. And e-cigarettes also expose non-smokers to secondhand aerosols (rather than secondhand smoke), which contain harmful chemicals.</p><p>Smokeless tobacco, such as chewing tobacco and snuff, is also bad for your health. Smokeless tobacco can cause certain cancers, including oral cancer. It also increases your risk of getting heart disease, gum disease, and oral lesions.</p>Why should I quit?<p>Remember, there is no safe level of tobacco use. Smoking even just one cigarette per day over a lifetime can cause smoking-related cancers and premature death. Quitting smoking can reduce your risk of health problems and add years to your life. The earlier you quit, the greater the benefit. Some immediate benefits of quitting include:</p><ul><li>Lower heart rate and blood pressure</li><li>Less carbon monoxide in the blood (carbon monoxide reduces the blood's ability to carry oxygen)</li><li>Better circulation</li><li>Less coughing and wheezing</li></ul><p>Quitting smoking can be challenging, but it is so important for your health. Contact your health care provider if you need help quitting.</p></content>

    <document rank="6" url="https://medlineplus.gov/hayfever.html">
      <content name="title">Hay Fever</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Allergic Rhinitis</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Pollen Allergy</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Seasonal Allergies</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>Each spring, summer, and fall, trees, weeds, and grasses release tiny pollen grains into the air. Some of the pollen ends up in your nose and throat. This can trigger a type of allergy called hay fever.</p><p>Symptoms can include:</p><ul><li>Sneezing, often with a runny or clogged nose</li><li>Coughing and postnasal drip</li><li>Itching eyes, nose and throat</li><li>Red and watery eyes</li><li>Dark circles under the eyes</li></ul><p>Your health care provider may diagnose hay fever based on a physical exam and your symptoms. Sometimes skin or blood tests are used. Taking medicines and using nasal sprays can relieve symptoms. You can also rinse out your nose, but be sure to use distilled or sterilized water with saline. Allergy shots can help make you less sensitive to pollen and provide long-term relief.</p></content>

      <content name="groupName">Ear, Nose and Throat</content>
      <content name="groupName">Immune System</content>

    <document rank="7" url="https://medlineplus.gov/molds.html">
      <content name="title">Molds</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Fungi</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>Molds are fungi that can be found both outdoors and indoors. They grow best in warm, damp and humid conditions. If you have damp or wet spots in your house, you will probably get mold.</p><p>Molds can cause health problems. Inhaling or touching mold or mold spores may cause allergic reactions or <span class="qt0">asthma</span> attacks in sensitive people. Molds can cause fungal infections. In addition, mold exposure may irritate your eyes, skin, nose, throat, and lungs. </p><p>Centers for Disease Control and Prevention</p></content>

    <document rank="8" url="https://medlineplus.gov/occupationalhealth.html">
      <content name="title">Occupational Health</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Employee Health</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Occupational Injuries</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>Occupational health problems occur at work or because of the kind of work you do. These problems can include:</p><ul><li>Cuts, fractures (broken bones), and sprains and strains</li><li>Loss of limbs</li><li>Repetitive motion disorders</li><li>Hearing problems caused by exposure to noise</li><li>Vision problems</li><li>Illness caused by breathing, touching, or swallowing unsafe substances</li><li>Illness caused by exposure to radiation</li><li>Exposure to germs in health care settings</li></ul><p>Good job safety and prevention practices can reduce your risk of these problems. Try to stay fit, reduce stress, set up your work area properly, and use the right equipment and gear.</p></content>

    <document rank="9" url="https://medlineplus.gov/ozone.html">
      <content name="title">Ozone</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>Ozone is a gas. It can be good or bad, depending on where it is. "Good" ozone occurs naturally about 10 to 30 miles above the Earth's surface. It shields us from the sun's ultraviolet rays. Part of the good ozone layer is gone. Man-made chemicals have destroyed it. Without enough good ozone, people may get too much ultraviolet radiation.  This may increase the risk of skin cancer, cataracts, and immune system problems.</p><p>"Bad" ozone is at ground level. It forms when pollutants from cars, factories, and other sources react chemically with sunlight. It is the main ingredient in smog. It is usually worst in the summer. Breathing bad ozone can be harmful. It can cause coughing, throat irritation, worsening of <span class="qt0">asthma</span>, bronchitis, and emphysema. It can lead to permanent lung damage, if you are regularly exposed to it.</p><p>Environmental Protection Agency</p></content>

    <document rank="10" url="https://medlineplus.gov/schoolhealth.html">
      <content name="title">School Health</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>Your child spends more time at school than anywhere else except home. Schools can have a major effect on children's health. Schools can teach children about health, and promote healthy behaviors. Physical education classes give children a chance to get exercise.</p><p>Schools work to:</p><ul><li>Prevent risky behaviors such as alcohol and tobacco use or bullying and cyberbullying</li><li>Encourage healthy habits like exercise and healthy eating</li><li>Deal with specific health problems in students, such as <span class="qt0">asthma</span>, obesity and infectious diseases</li></ul><p>The school building and environment should be a safe and healthy place for your child.</p></content>

    <document rank="11" url="https://medlineplus.gov/steroids.html">
      <content name="title">Steroids</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Corticosteroids</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Glucocorticoids</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Prednisone</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>You may have heard of anabolic steroids, which can have harmful effects. But there's another type of steroid - sometimes called a corticosteroid - that treats a variety of problems. These steroids are similar to hormones that your adrenal glands make to fight stress associated with illnesses and injuries. They reduce inflammation and affect the immune system. </p><p>You may need to take corticosteroids to treat:</p><ul><li>	Arthritis</li><li>	<span class="qt0">Asthma</span></li><li>	Autoimmune diseases such as lupus and multiple sclerosis</li><li>	Skin conditions such as eczema and rashes</li><li>	Some kinds of cancer</li></ul><p>Steroids are strong medicines, and they can have side effects, including weakened bones and cataracts. Because of this, you usually take them for as short a time as possible.</p></content>

    <document rank="12" url="https://medlineplus.gov/commoncold.html">
      <content name="title">Common Cold</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Cold, Common</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is the common cold?<p>The common cold is a mild infection of your upper respiratory tract (which includes your nose and throat). Colds are probably the most common illness. Adults have an average of 2-3 colds per year, and children have even more. Colds are more common in the winter and spring, but you can get them at any time.</p>What causes the common cold?<p>More than 200 different viruses can cause a cold, but rhinoviruses are the most common type. The viruses that cause colds are very contagious. They can spread from person to person through the air and close personal contact. You can also get infected when you touch something that has the virus on it and then touch your eyes, mouth, or nose. For example, you could get a cold after you shake hands with someone who has a cold or touch a doorknob that has the germs on it, and then touch your face.</p>What are the symptoms of the common cold?<p>The symptoms of a common cold usually include:</p><ul><li>Sneezing</li><li>Stuffy nose (congestion)</li><li>Runny nose</li><li>Sore throat</li><li>Coughing</li><li>Headache</li></ul><p>The symptoms usually start a few days after you become infected with the virus. Some symptoms can last for 10-14 days.</p>What are the treatments for the common cold?<p>There is no cure for the common cold. But there are treatments that can make you feel better while you wait for the cold to go away on its own:</p><ul><li>Getting lots of rest.</li><li>Drinking plenty of fluids.</li><li>Using a clean humidifier or cool mist vaporizer.</li><li>Gargling with warm salt water.</li><li>Using saline nose drops or sprays.</li><li>Taking over-the-counter pain or cold and cough medicines. But you need to be careful with certain medicines:
	<ul><li>Children and teens should not take aspirin.</li><li>Some cold and cough medicines contain ingredients that are not recommended for children. Talk with your child's health care provider before giving your child any cold and cough medicines.</li><li>Some cold and cough medicines contain pain relievers. If you also take a separate pain reliever with these medicines, you could be getting a dangerous amount of the pain reliever. Read the labels on the medicines and follow the instructions carefully. If you have questions, ask your provider or a pharmacist.</li></ul>
</li></ul><p>Antibiotics will not help with a cold. Antibiotics help with bacterial infections, not with viral infections such as colds.</p><p>Most people who have a cold will feel better after a week or two. However, some people who get a cold may develop other illnesses, such as bronchitis or pneumonia. This is more common in people with weakened immune systems, <span class="qt0">asthma</span>, or other respiratory conditions. Contact your provider if you or your child have symptoms that concern you, such as:</p><ul><li>Trouble breathing or fast breathing</li><li>Dehydration</li><li>Fever that lasts longer than 4 days</li><li>Symptoms that last more than 10 days without improvement</li><li>Symptoms, such as fever or cough, that improve but then return or worsen</li><li>Worsening of chronic medical conditions</li></ul>Can the common cold be prevented?<p>There is no vaccine to protect against the common cold. But you may be able to reduce your risk of getting or spreading a cold by:</p><ul><li>Washing your hands often with soap and water for at least 20 seconds.</li><li>Avoiding touching your face, nose, or mouth with unwashed hands.</li><li>Avoiding close contact, such as kissing, shaking hands, and sharing cups and eating utensils, with others if you are sick or they are sick.</li><li>Cleaning and disinfecting surfaces that you frequently touch.</li><li>Covering coughs and sneezes with a tissue. Then throw away the tissue and wash your hands.</li><li>Staying home when sick.</li></ul><p>Centers for Disease Control and Prevention</p></content>

      <content name="groupName">Infections</content>

    <document rank="13" url="https://medlineplus.gov/environmentalhealth.html">
      <content name="title">Environmental Health</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>Our environment affects our health. If parts of the environment, like the air, water, 
or soil become polluted, it can lead to health problems. For example, <span class="qt0">asthma</span> pollutants and chemicals in the air or in the home can trigger <span class="qt0">asthma</span> attacks.</p><p>Some environmental risks are a part of the natural world, like radon in the soil. Others are the result of human activities, like lead poisoning from paint, or exposure to asbestos or mercury from mining or industrial use.</p><p>NIH: National Institute of Environmental Health Sciences</p></content>
      <content name="mesh">Environmental Health</content>
      <content name="groupName">Poisoning, Toxicology, Environmental Health</content>
      <content name="snippet"> ... it can lead to health problems. For example, <span class="qt0">asthma</span> pollutants and chemicals in the air or in the home can trigger <span class="qt0">asthma</span> attacks. Some environmental risks are a part of  ... </content>
    </document>
    <document rank="14" url="https://medlineplus.gov/foodallergy.html">
      <content name="title">Food Allergy</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Allergy, Food</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Egg Allergy</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Milk Allergy</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Nut Allergy</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Peanut Allergy</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Shellfish Allergy</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Wheat Allergy</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is a food allergy?<p>A food allergy is an abnormal immune system reaction to certain foods. Your immune system normally protects you from germs. But if you have a food allergy, your immune system mistakenly reacts to certain foods as if they were harmful. This reaction is usually mild. But in some cases, it can be serious or even life-threatening.</p><p>A food intolerance is different than a food allergy. If you have a food intolerance, you also have symptoms when you eat that food. But these are typically just digestive symptoms such as bloating and gas. Also, an intolerance is not an immune system reaction.</p><p>Most food allergies are caused by:</p><ul><li>Cow's milk</li><li>Chicken eggs</li><li>Crustacean shellfish, such as shrimp, crab, lobster, and crayfish</li><li>Fish</li><li>Peanuts</li><li>Sesame</li><li>Soy</li><li>Tree nuts, such as almonds, walnuts, pecans</li><li>Wheat</li></ul><p>People with a food allergy may be allergic to more than one type of food.</p>Who is more likely to develop a food allergy?<p>Both children and adults can have food allergies. Some children will outgrow their food allergies. And sometimes people can develop food allergies as adult.</p><p>Certain factors can make you more likely to have a food allergy. They include:</p><ul><li>Having eczema, a disease that causes inflammation, redness, and irritation of the skin. Your immune system also plays a role in eczema.</li><li>Having <span class="qt0">asthma</span> or other allergies, such as hay fever.</li><li>Your genes. Certain genes may influence the development of food allergies. And you are more likely to have food allergies if someone in your family has allergies, <span class="qt0">asthma</span>, or eczema.</li></ul>What are the symptoms of food allergies?<p>If you are allergic to a food you have eaten, you may have a variety of symptoms. These symptoms are not always the same for every person. And the symptoms that you get each time you eat the food may not always be the same. For example, your symptoms may sometimes depend on how much of the food you ate.</p><p>Food allergy symptoms usually start within a few minutes to two hours after you eat that food. They may include:</p><ul><li>Hives</li><li>Flushed skin or rash</li><li>Tingling or itchy sensation in the mouth</li><li>Face, tongue, or lip swelling</li><li>Vomiting and/or diarrhea</li><li>Abdominal (belly) cramps</li><li>Coughing or wheezing</li><li>Dizziness and/or lightheadedness</li><li>Swelling of the throat and vocal cords</li><li>Trouble breathing</li></ul><p>In rare cases, a food allergy can cause a life-threatening reaction called anaphylaxis. It may start out with some milder symptoms, but then it becomes more serious. It can lead to:</p><ul><li>Narrowed airways in the lungs</li><li>Severe trouble breathing because of swelling in the throat </li><li>Severe lowering of blood pressure and shock ("anaphylactic shock")</li><li>Loss of consciousness</li></ul><p>This is a medical emergency. Call 911 if someone is having the symptoms of anaphylaxis.</p>How are food allergies diagnosed?<p>To find out if you have a food allergy, your health care provider will</p><ul><li>Ask about your symptoms, family health history, and medical history, including other allergies.</li><li>Likely do a physical exam.</li><li>Do food allergy testing. This could involve skin testing, blood testing, a food elimination diet, and/or an oral food challenge test. An oral food challenge test is the most accurate food allergy test.</li></ul>What are the treatments for food allergies?<p>There is no cure for food allergies. The only way to prevent a reaction is to avoid eating the food that you are allergic to.</p><p>There are medicines for people with food allergies. They include:</p><ul><li>Medicines to reduce food allergy symptoms, including antihistamines and corticosteroids.</li><li>Medicines to reduce allergic reactions, including severe reactions.</li><li>Epinephrine, a medicine to treat anaphylaxis. Your provider may give you a prescription for an epinephrine auto-injector. It is a device used to inject epinephrine when someone is having symptoms of anaphylaxis. If you have been prescribed one, it's important to learn how to use it and to carry it with you at all times.</li></ul><p>If you have a food allergy, it's a good idea to have a medical alert bracelet that says what your allergy is.</p></content>
      <content name="mesh">Food Hypersensitivity</content>
      <content name="groupName">Immune System</content>

    <document rank="15" url="https://medlineplus.gov/gerd.html">
      <content name="title">GERD</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Acid Reflux</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Gastroesophageal Reflux</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Gastroesophageal reflux disease</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is GERD?<p>Sometimes, after eating a large meal or lying down too soon after eating, you might feel acid coming up into your throat. This is called gastroesophageal reflux (GER). It happens when the muscle at the end of your esophagus (the tube that carries food from your mouth to your stomach) doesn't close properly. Stomach contents can then move back up and irritate the esophagus.</p><p>Having reflux occasionally is common. But if you have symptoms two or more times a week or if they cause damage to the lining of your esophagus, you may have gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD). GERD is a chronic (long-lasting) condition that can cause discomfort and, over time, lead to other health problems.</p><p>Anyone can have GERD, including infants and children.</p>What causes GERD?<p>GERD can happen when the muscle at the bottom of your esophagus becomes weak or relaxes at the wrong time. You are more likely to have GERD if you:</p><ul><li>Are overweight or have obesity</li><li>Are pregnant</li><li>Smoke or are around secondhand smoke</li><li>Have a hiatal hernia (when part of the stomach pushes up into the chest)</li></ul><p>Certain foods, drinks, or medicines can also make GERD worse.</p>What are common symptoms of GERD?<p>The most common symptom of GERD is heartburn. This is a burning feeling in your chest or throat. You can also have GERD without having heartburn. Other symptoms may include:</p><ul><li>Tasting acid or food in the back of your mouth</li><li>A dry cough or hoarse voice</li><li><span class="qt0">Asthma</span>-like symptoms</li><li>Trouble swallowing</li></ul><p>Get medical help right away if you have chest pain with shortness of breath, or pain in your jaw or arm. These can be signs of a heart problem.</p>How is GERD diagnosed?<p>Your health care provider will ask about your symptoms and medical history. They may recommend tests such as:</p><ul><li>Upper GI endoscopy to look at the esophagus and stomach lining with a tiny camera.</li><li>Biopsy to check a small tissue sample.</li><li>X-Ray to take pictures of the upper digestive system.</li><li>Esophageal pH test to measure how often acid backs up the esophagus and how long the acid stays there.</li><li>Esophageal manometry to check how well your esophagus moves food when you swallow.</li><li>Transnasal esophagoscopy to look for damage to the esophagus by using a small tube with a camera.</li></ul>How is GERD treated?<p>Most people can manage GERD with lifestyle changes and medicine. In rare cases, surgery is needed.</p><p>Lifestyle changes can include:</p><ul><li>Eating smaller meals and avoiding lying down for at least 2-3 hours after eating</li><li>Avoiding foods that trigger symptoms, such as spicy foods, chocolate, caffeine, or fatty foods</li><li>Losing weight if needed</li><li>Raising the head of your bed</li><li>Quitting smoking</li></ul><p>Medicines can include:</p><ul><li>Over-the-counter medicines, such as antacids</li><li>Prescription medicines</li></ul><p>If not treated, GERD can cause problems like inflammation of the esophagus, scarring, or changes in the tissue lining (Barrett's esophagus). It can also worsen <span class="qt0">asthma</span> or cause chronic (long-term) cough or hoarseness.</p>Can GERD be prevented?<p>You may be able to improve symptoms of GERD by:</p><ul><li>Avoiding alcohol and trigger foods</li><li>Eating smaller meals</li><li>Not eating close to bedtime</li><li>Wearing loose-fitting clothes</li><li>Sleeping on your left side or raising the head of your bed</li><li>Maintaining a healthy weight</li></ul><p>NIH: National Institute of Diabetes and Digestive and Kidney Diseases</p></content>

    <document rank="16" url="https://medlineplus.gov/healthproblemsinpregnancy.html">
      <content name="title">Health Problems in Pregnancy</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">High Risk Pregnancy</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Pregnancy Complications</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Pregnancy and Health Problems</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Pregnancy, High-Risk</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What are health problems in pregnancy?<p>A health problem in pregnancy is any disease or condition that could affect your health or the health of your fetus. Some health problems may make it more likely that you will have a high-risk pregnancy. A high-risk pregnancy is one in which you, your fetus, or both are at higher risk for health problems than in a typical pregnancy.</p><p>But just because you have health problems, it doesn't mean that you, or your fetus, will have a problem during the pregnancy. Taking care of yourself and getting early and regular prenatal care from a health care provider may help you reduce pregnancy risks from health problems.</p>What raises my risk for health problems during pregnancy?<p>Every pregnancy has some risk of problems, but your lifestyle, as well as factors like certain conditions and health issues, can raise that risk, such as:</p><ul><li>A health condition you had before you got pregnant</li><li>A health condition you develop during pregnancy</li><li>A pregnancy with more than one baby</li><li>A health problem that happened during a previous pregnancy and could happen again</li><li>Substance use during pregnancy</li><li>Being over age 35</li></ul><p>Any of these can affect your health, the health of your fetus, or both.</p>Can chronic health conditions cause problems in pregnancy?<p>Every pregnancy is different. If you have specific risks in one pregnancy, it doesn't mean that you will have them in another. But, if you have a chronic condition, you should talk to your provider about how to minimize your risk before you get pregnant. Once you are pregnant, you may need a health care team to monitor your pregnancy. Some common conditions that can complicate a pregnancy include:</p><ul><li>High blood pressure</li><li>Polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS)</li><li>Kidney problems</li><li>Autoimmune disorders</li><li>Thyroid disease</li><li>Obesity</li><li>HIV</li><li>Cancer</li><li>Infections</li></ul><p>Other conditions that may make pregnancy risky can develop during pregnancy - for example, gestational diabetes and Rh incompatibility.</p>Can health problems in pregnancy be prevented?<p>You may be able to lower your risk of certain health problems by making healthy lifestyle changes before you get pregnant. These can include reaching a healthy weight, not smoking, and managing any health conditions.</p><p>However, health problems during pregnancy are not always preventable. Some chronic conditions can be treated and controlled, while others aren't treatable and carry a higher risk than usual, even if the health problem is well-managed.</p><p>Share your symptoms with your provider for early detection and treatment of health problems. Sometimes, it's hard to know what's normal. Some symptoms, like nausea, back pain, and fatigue, are common during pregnancy, while other symptoms, like vaginal bleeding or a severe or long-lasting headache, can be signs of a problem. Call your provider to let them know if something is bothering or worrying you.</p></content>
      <content name="mesh">Pregnancy, High-Risk</content>
      <content name="mesh">Pregnancy Complications</content>

      <content name="groupName">Female Reproductive System</content>

    <document rank="17" url="https://medlineplus.gov/immunesystemanddisorders.html">
      <content name="title">Immune System and Disorders</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Chronic Granulomatous Disease</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Primary Immunodeficiency Diseases</content>
      <content name="altTitle">SCID</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Severe Combined Immunodeficiency</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is the immune system?<p>Your immune system is a complex network of cells, tissues, and organs. Together they help the body fight infections and other diseases.</p><p>When germs such as bacteria or viruses invade your body, they attack and multiply. This is called an infection. The infection causes the disease that makes you sick. Your immune system protects you from the disease by fighting off the germs.</p>What are the parts of the immune system?<p>The immune system has many different parts, including:</p><ul><li>Your skin, which can help prevent germs from getting into the body</li><li>Mucous membranes, which are the moist, inner linings of some organs and body cavities. They make mucus and other substances which can trap and fight germs.</li><li>White blood cells, which fight germs</li><li>Organs and tissues of the lymph system, such as the thymus, spleen, tonsils, lymph nodes, lymph vessels, and bone marrow.  They produce, store, and carry white blood cells.</li></ul>How does the immune system work?<p>Your immune system defends your body against substances it sees as harmful or foreign. These substances are called antigens. They may be germs such as bacteria and viruses. They might be chemicals or toxins. They could also be cells that are damaged from things like cancer or sunburn.</p><p>When your immune system recognizes an antigen, it attacks it. This is called an immune response. Part of this response is to make antibodies. Antibodies are proteins that work to attack, weaken, and destroy antigens. Your body also makes other cells to fight the antigen.</p><p>Afterwards, your immune system remembers the antigen. If it sees the antigen again, it can recognize it. It will quickly send out the right antibodies, so in most cases, you don't get sick. This protection against a certain disease is called immunity.</p>What are the types of immunity?<p>There are three different types of immunity:</p><ul><li>Innate immunity is the protection that you are born with. It is your body's first line of defense. It includes barriers such as the skin and mucous membranes. They keep harmful substances from entering the body. It also includes some cells and chemicals which can attack foreign substances.</li><li>Active immunity, also called adaptive immunity, develops when you are infected with or vaccinated against a foreign substance. Active immunity is usually long-lasting. For many diseases, it can last your entire life.</li><li>Passive immunity happens when you receive antibodies to a disease instead of making them through your own immune system. For example, newborn babies have antibodies from their mothers. People can also get passive immunity through blood products that contain antibodies. This kind of immunity gives you protection right away. But it only lasts a few weeks or months.</li></ul>What can go wrong with the immune system?<p>Sometimes a person may have an immune response even though there is no real threat. This can lead to problems such as allergies, <span class="qt0">asthma</span>, and autoimmune diseases. If you have an autoimmune disease, your immune system attacks healthy cells in your body by mistake.</p><p>Other immune system problems happen when your immune system does not work correctly. These problems include immunodeficiency diseases. If you have an immunodeficiency disease, you get sick more often. Your infections may last longer and can be more serious and harder to treat. They are often genetic disorders.</p><p>There are other diseases that can affect your immune system. For example, HIV is a virus that harms your immune system by destroying your white blood cells. If HIV is not treated, it can lead to AIDS (acquired immunodeficiency syndrome). People with AIDS have badly damaged immune systems. They get an increasing number of severe illnesses.</p></content>
      <content name="mesh">Immune System Diseases</content>
      <content name="mesh">Immune System</content>
      <content name="groupName">Immune System</content>

    <document rank="18" url="https://medlineplus.gov/refluxinchildren.html">
      <content name="title">Reflux in Children</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">GERD in Children</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Gastroesophageal Reflux in Children</content>
      <content name="altTitle">GER in Children</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is reflux (GER) and GERD?<p>Gastroesophageal reflux (GER), often called reflux, occurs when food or stomach acid flows back from your child's stomach into the esophagus. The esophagus is the tube that carries food from your mouth to your stomach. This can sometimes cause irritation or a burning sensation. Occasional reflux is common and usually not a problem.</p><p>Gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD) is a more serious and long-lasting type of reflux. GERD can cause repeated symptoms or damage to the lining of the esophagus. Having reflux two or more times a week may be a sign of GERD. Your child's health care provider may diagnose GERD if the reflux causes pain, feeding problems, or irritation of the esophagus.</p>What causes reflux and GERD in children?<p>A small muscle called the lower esophageal sphincter acts as a valve between the esophagus and stomach. When your child swallows, this muscle relaxes to let food pass from the esophagus to the stomach. This muscle normally stays closed, so the stomach contents and acid don't flow back into the esophagus. This muscle, along with the diaphragm (the large muscle between the chest and abdomen), usually prevents reflux. It's normal for children to have reflux occasionally.</p><p>In children who have GERD, the lower esophageal sphincter may be weak or relax when it shouldn't. That allows stomach contents into the esophagus. This can happen because of:</p><ul><li>A hiatal hernia, a condition in which the upper part of the stomach pushes up into the chest</li><li>Increased pressure on the abdomen (belly) from being overweight or having obesity</li><li>Certain medicines, such as some used to treat <span class="qt0">asthma</span>, allergies, depression, or pain</li><li>Smoking or exposure to secondhand smoke</li><li>Previous surgery on the esophagus or upper abdomen</li><li>Developmental delays or certain neurological conditions, such as cerebral palsy</li><li>Lung conditions, such as cystic fibrosis</li></ul>How common are reflux and GERD in children?<p>Many children have occasional reflux, especially after large meals or physical activity. GERD is less common, and most symptoms improve with time and lifestyle changes. </p>What are the symptoms of reflux and GERD in children?<p>Symptoms may vary by age. Some children might not even notice reflux, while others may taste food or stomach acid in the back of their mouth.</p><p>In children, GERD can cause:</p><ul><li>Heartburn, a burning feeling in the chest or throat (more common in older children and teens)</li><li>Bad breath</li><li>Nausea or vomiting</li><li>Trouble or pain when swallowing</li><li>Cough, hoarseness, or breathing problems</li><li>Wearing away of tooth enamel from stomach acid</li></ul><p>Other conditions can cause similar symptoms. Talk to your child's provider if symptoms happen often or make eating, sleeping, or daily activities difficult.</p>How are reflux and GERD diagnosed in children?<p>In most cases, your child's provider can tell if it's reflux by learning about your child's symptoms and health history. Tests are usually needed only if symptoms don't get better with lifestyle changes or medicines, or if another health problem is suspected.</p><p>Common tests include:</p><ul><li>Upper GI series, which looks at the shape of your child's upper GI (gastrointestinal) tract. Your child will drink or eat a chalky-tasting liquid called barium. For young children, the barium is mixed in with a bottle or other food. Several x-rays are taken to track the barium as it goes through your child's esophagus and stomach.</li><li>Esophageal pH and impedance monitoring, which measures the amount of acid or liquid in your child's esophagus. A thin flexible tube is placed through their nose into the stomach. The end of the tube in the esophagus measures when and how much acid comes up into the esophagus. The other end of the tube attaches to a monitor that records the measurements. Your child will wear the tube for 24 hours. They may need to stay in the hospital during the test.</li><li>Upper gastrointestinal (GI) endoscopy and biopsy, which uses an endoscope, a long, flexible tube with a light and camera at the end of it. An endoscope is inserted down your child's esophagus, stomach, and first part of the small intestine. While looking at the pictures from the endoscope, tissue samples (biopsy) may be taken.</li></ul>What lifestyle changes can help treat my child's reflux or GERD?<p>Simple lifestyle changes can often improve symptoms. Examples include:</p><ul><li>Maintaining a healthy weight.</li><li>Eating smaller meals.</li><li>Avoiding high-fat foods or other trigger foods that cause symptoms.</li><li>Wearing loose-fitting clothing around the abdomen (belly).</li><li>Staying upright for 3 hours after meals and avoiding slouching when sitting.</li><li>Sleeping with the head of the bed raised 6 to 8 inches.</li></ul>What are the treatments for my child's GERD?<p>If lifestyle changes aren't enough, your provider may recommend medicine to reduce stomach acid. The medicines work by lowering the amount of acid your child's stomach makes. Some are available over-the-counter, while others need a prescription. Do not give your child any medicine unless your provider recommends it.</p><p>If symptoms don't get better or are severe, your provider may refer you to a doctor who treats stomach and digestion problems in children. In rare cases, surgery may be considered.</p><p>If GERD isn't treated, it can cause problems such as inflammation or scarring in the esophagus, changes in its lining (Barrett's esophagus), or make breathing problems, like <span class="qt0">asthma</span>, worse.</p><p>NIH: National Institute of Diabetes and Digestive and Kidney Diseases</p></content>
      <content name="mesh">Gastroesophageal Reflux</content>
      <content name="groupName">Digestive System</content>
      <content name="groupName">Children and Teenagers</content>

NIH: National Institute of Diabetes and Digestive  ... </content>

    <document rank="19" url="https://medlineplus.gov/secondhandsmoke.html">
      <content name="title">Secondhand Smoke</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Environmental Tobacco Smoke</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Passive Smoking</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Smoking, Passive</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Tobacco smoke pollution</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>Secondhand smoke is a mixture of the smoke that comes from the burning end of a cigarette, cigar, or pipe, and the smoke breathed out by the smoker.  It contains more than 7,000 chemicals. Hundreds of those chemicals are toxic and about 70 can cause cancer.</p><p>Health effects of secondhand smoke include:</p><ul><li>Ear infections in children</li><li>More frequent and severe <span class="qt0">asthma</span> attacks in children</li><li>Heart disease and lung cancer in adults who have never smoked</li></ul><p>There is no safe amount of secondhand smoke. Even low levels of it can be harmful. The only way to fully protect nonsmokers from secondhand smoke is not to allow smoking indoors.</p><p>Centers for Disease Control and Prevention</p></content>
      <content name="mesh">Tobacco Smoke Pollution</content>
      <content name="groupName">Poisoning, Toxicology, Environmental Health</content>
      <content name="snippet"> ... Ear infections in children  More frequent and severe <span class="qt0">asthma</span> attacks in children  Heart disease and lung cancer  ... </content>

    <document rank="20" url="https://medlineplus.gov/smokingandyouth.html">
      <content name="title">Smoking and Youth</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Teen Smoking</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>Smoking cigarettes has many health risks for everyone. However, the younger you are when you start smoking, the more problems it can cause. People who start smoking before the age of 21 have the hardest time quitting. Teens who smoke are also more likely to use alcohol and illegal drugs.</p><p>The problem is not just cigarettes.  Spit tobacco, e-cigarettes, and cigars are not safe alternatives to cigarettes. Low-tar and additive-free tobacco products are not safe either.</p><p>Young people who do not start using tobacco by age 18 will most likely never start.</p><p>Centers for Disease Control and Prevention</p></content>

      <content name="groupName">Children and Teenagers</content>

    <document rank="21" url="https://medlineplus.gov/alcoholusedisorderaudtreatment.html">
      <content name="title">Alcohol Use Disorder (AUD) Treatment</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Alcohol Abuse Treatment</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Alcoholism Treatment</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is an alcohol use disorder?<p>An alcohol use disorder (AUD) is drinking that causes distress and harm. It is a medical condition in which you:</p><ul><li>Drink alcohol compulsively</li><li>Can't control how much you drink</li><li>Feel anxious, irritable, and/or stressed when you are not drinking</li></ul><p>An AUD can range from mild to severe, depending on the symptoms. Severe AUD is sometimes called alcoholism or alcohol dependence.</p>What are the treatments for alcohol use disorder?<p>Most people with an alcohol use disorder can benefit from some form of treatment. Medical treatments include medicines and behavioral therapies. For many people, using both types gives them the best results. People who are getting treatment for AUD may also find it helpful to go to a support group such as Alcoholics Anonymous (AA). If you have an AUD and a mental illness, it is important to get treatment for both.</p><p>Some people may need intensive treatment for AUD. They may go to a residential treatment center for rehabilitation (rehab). Treatment there is highly structured. It usually includes several different kinds of behavioral therapies. It may also include medicines for detox (medical treatment for alcohol withdrawal) and/or for treating the AUD.</p>Which medicines can treat alcohol use disorder?<p>Three medicines are approved to treat AUD:</p><ul><li>Disulfiram causes unpleasant symptoms such as nausea and skin flushing whenever you drink alcohol. Knowing that drinking will cause these unpleasant effects may help you stay away from alcohol.</li><li>Naltrexone blocks the receptors in your brain that make you feel good when you drink alcohol. It can also reduce your craving for alcohol. This can help you cut back on your drinking.</li><li>Acamprosate helps you avoid alcohol after you have quit drinking. It works on multiple brain systems to reduce your cravings, especially just after you have quit drinking.</li></ul><p>Your health care provider can help you figure out if one of these medicines is right for you. They are not addictive, so you don't have to worry about trading one addiction for another. They are not a cure, but they can help you manage AUD. This is just like taking medicines to manage a chronic disease such as <span class="qt0">asthma</span> or diabetes.</p>Which behavioral therapies can treat alcohol use disorder?<p>Another name for behavioral therapies for AUD is alcohol counseling. It involves working with a health care professional to identify and help change the behaviors that lead to your heavy drinking.:</p><ul><li>Cognitive-behavioral therapy (CBT) helps you identify the feelings and situations that can lead to heavy drinking. It teaches you coping skills, including how to manage stress and how to change the thoughts that cause you to want to drink. You may get CBT one-on-one with a therapist or in small groups.</li><li>Motivational enhancement therapy helps you build and strengthen the motivation to change your drinking behavior. It includes about four sessions over a short period of time. The therapy starts with identifying the pros and cons of seeking treatment. Then you and your therapist work on forming a plan for making changes in your drinking. The next sessions focus on building up your confidence and developing the skills you need to be able to stick to the plan.</li><li>Marital and family counseling includes spouses and other family members. It can help to repair and improve your family relationships. Studies show that strong family support through family therapy may help you to stay away from drinking.</li><li>Brief interventions are short, one-on-one or small-group counseling sessions. It includes one to four sessions. The counselor gives you information about your drinking pattern and potential risks. The counselor works with you to set goals and provide ideas that may help you make a change.</li></ul>Is treatment for alcohol use disorder effective?<p>For most people, treatment for an AUD is helpful. But overcoming an alcohol use disorder is an ongoing process, and you may relapse (start drinking again). You should look at relapse as a temporary setback, and keep trying. Many people repeatedly try to cut back or quit drinking, have a setback, then try to quit again. Having a relapse does not mean that you cannot recover. If you do relapse, it is important to return to treatment right away, so you can learn more about your relapse triggers and improve your coping skills. This may help you be more successful the next time.</p><p>NIH: National Institute on Alcohol Abuse and Alcoholism</p></content>

    <document rank="22" url="https://medlineplus.gov/aspergillosis.html">
      <content name="title">Aspergillosis</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">ABPA</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Allergic Bronchopulmonary Aspergillosis</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>Aspergillosis is a disease caused by a fungus (or mold) called Aspergillus.  The fungus is very common in both indoors and outdoors. Most people breathe in the spores of the fungus every day without being affected. But some people get the disease. It usually occurs in people with lung diseases or weakened immune systems.</p><p>There are different kinds of aspergillosis. One kind is allergic bronchopulmonary aspergillosis (also called ABPA).  Symptoms of ABPA include wheezing and coughing. ABPA can affect healthy people but it is most common in people with <span class="qt0">asthma</span> or cystic fibrosis.</p><p>Another kind is invasive aspergillosis, which damages tissues in the body. It usually affects the lungs. Sometimes it can also cause infection in other organs and spread throughout the body. It affects people who have immune system problems, such as people who have had a transplant, are taking high doses of steroids, or getting chemotherapy for some cancers.</p><p>Your doctor might do a variety of tests to make the diagnosis, including a chest x-ray, CT scan of the lungs, and  an examination of tissues for signs of the fungus. Treatment is with antifungal drugs. If you have ABPA, you may also take steroids.</p><p>Centers for Disease Control and Prevention</p></content>

    <document rank="23" url="https://medlineplus.gov/breathingproblems.html">
      <content name="title">Breathing Problems</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Dyspnea</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Shortness of Breath</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Tachypnea</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>When you're short of breath, it's hard or uncomfortable for you to take in the oxygen your body needs. You may feel as if you're not getting enough air. Sometimes you can have mild breathing problems because of a stuffy nose or intense exercise. But shortness of breath can also be a sign of a serious disease.</p><p>Many conditions can make you feel short of breath:</p><ul><li>Lung conditions such as <span class="qt0">asthma</span>, emphysema, or pneumonia</li><li>Problems with your trachea or bronchi, which are part of your airway system</li><li>Heart disease can make you feel breathless if your heart cannot pump enough blood to supply oxygen to your body</li><li>Anxiety and panic attacks</li><li>Allergies</li></ul><p>If you often have trouble breathing, it is important to find out the cause.</p></content>
      <content name="mesh">Tachypnea</content>
      <content name="mesh">Dyspnea</content>

    <document rank="24" url="https://medlineplus.gov/caffeine.html">
      <content name="title">Caffeine</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Energy Drinks</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is caffeine?<p>Caffeine is a bitter substance that occurs naturally in more than 60 plants including:</p><ul><li>Coffee beans</li><li>Tea leaves</li><li>Kola nuts, which are used to flavor soft drink colas</li><li>Cacao pods, which are used to make chocolate products</li></ul><p>There is also synthetic (man-made) caffeine, which is added to some medicines, foods, and drinks. For example, some pain relievers, cold medicines, and over-the-counter medicines for alertness contain synthetic caffeine. So do energy drinks and "energy-boosting" gums and snacks.</p><p>Most people consume caffeine from drinks. The amount of caffeine in different drinks can vary a lot, but it is generally:</p><ul><li>An 8-ounce cup of coffee: 95-200 mg</li><li>A 12-ounce can of cola: 35-45 mg</li><li>An 8-ounce energy drink: 70-100 mg</li><li>An 8-ounce cup of tea: 14-60 mg</li></ul>What are caffeine's effects on the body?<p>Caffeine has many effects on your body's metabolism. It:</p><ul><li>Stimulates your central nervous system, which can make you feel more awake and give you a boost of energy</li><li>Is a diuretic, meaning that it helps your body get rid of extra salt and water by urinating more</li><li>Increases the release of acid in your stomach, sometimes leading to an upset stomach or heartburn</li><li>May interfere with the absorption of calcium in the body</li><li>Increases your blood pressure</li></ul><p>Within one hour of eating or drinking caffeine, it reaches its peak level in your blood. You may continue to feel the effects of caffeine for four to six hours.</p>What are the side effects from too much caffeine?<p>For most people, it is not harmful to consume up to 400mg of caffeine a day. If you do eat or drink too much caffeine, it can cause health problems, such as:</p><ul><li>Restlessness and shakiness</li><li>Insomnia</li><li>Headaches</li><li>Dizziness</li><li>Fast heart rate</li><li>Dehydration</li><li>Anxiety</li><li>Dependency, so you need to take more of it to get the same results</li></ul><p>Some people are more sensitive to the effects of caffeine than others.</p>What are energy drinks, and why can they be a problem?<p>Energy drinks are beverages that have added caffeine. The amount of caffeine in energy drinks can vary widely, and sometimes the labels on the drinks do not give you the actual amount of caffeine in them. Energy drinks may also contain sugars, vitamins, herbs, and supplements.</p><p>Companies that make energy drinks claim that the drinks can increase alertness and improve physical and mental performance. This has helped make the drinks popular with American teens and young adults. There's limited data showing that energy drinks might temporarily improve alertness and physical endurance. There is not enough evidence to show that they enhance strength or power. But what we do know is that energy drinks can be dangerous because they have large amounts of caffeine. And since they have lots of sugar, they can contribute to weight gain and worsen diabetes.</p><p>Sometimes young people mix their energy drinks with alcohol. It is dangerous to combine alcohol and caffeine. Caffeine can interfere with your ability to recognize how drunk you are, which can lead you to drink more. This also makes you more likely to make bad decisions.</p>Who should avoid or limit caffeine?<p>You should check with your health care provider about whether you should limit or avoid caffeine if you:</p><ul><li>Are pregnant, since caffeine passes through the placenta to your baby.</li><li>Are breastfeeding, since a small amount of caffeine that you consume is passed along to your baby.</li><li>Have sleep disorders, including insomnia.</li><li>Have migraines or other chronic headaches.</li><li>Have anxiety.</li><li>Have GERD or ulcers.</li><li>Have arrhythmia (a problem with the rate or rhythm of your heartbeat).</li><li>Have high blood pressure.</li><li>Take certain medicines or supplements, including stimulants, certain antibiotics, <span class="qt0">asthma</span> medicines, and heart medicines. Check with your health care provider about whether there might be interactions between caffeine and any medicines and supplements that you take.</li><li>Are a child or teen. Neither should have as much caffeine as adults. Children can be especially sensitive to the effects of caffeine.</li></ul>What is caffeine withdrawal?<p>If you have been consuming caffeine on a regular basis and then suddenly stop, you may have caffeine withdrawal. Symptoms can include:</p><ul><li>Headaches</li><li>Drowsiness</li><li>Irritability</li><li>Nausea</li><li>Trouble concentrating</li></ul><p>These symptoms usually go away after a couple of days.</p></content>

    <document rank="2" url="https://medlineplus.gov/arthritis.html">
      <content name="title"><span class="qt0">Arthritis</span></content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is <span class="qt0">arthritis</span>?<p>If you feel pain and stiffness in your body or have trouble moving around, you might have <span class="qt0">arthritis</span>. Any disorder that affects the joints is often called <span class="qt0">arthritis</span>. Joints are places where two bones meet, such as your elbow or knee.</p><p>Most types of <span class="qt0">arthritis</span> can cause joint pain and inflammation (swelling). Over time, a swollen joint can become severely damaged. Some kinds of <span class="qt0">arthritis</span> can also cause problems in your organs, such as your eyes or skin. Treatment will depend on the type of <span class="qt0">arthritis</span> you have.</p>What are the types of <span class="qt0">arthritis</span>?<p>Common types of <span class="qt0">arthritis</span> include:</p><ul><li><span class="qt0">Osteoarthritis</span> is the most common type of <span class="qt0">arthritis</span>. It's often related to aging or injury.</li><li>Autoimmune <span class="qt0">arthritis</span> happens when your body's immune system attacks healthy cells in your body by mistake.  Rheumatoid <span class="qt0">arthritis</span> is the most common form of this type of <span class="qt0">arthritis</span>.</li><li>Juvenile <span class="qt0">arthritis</span> is a type of <span class="qt0">arthritis</span> that happens in children.</li><li>Infectious <span class="qt0">arthritis</span> is an infection that has spread from another part of your body to the joint. Reactive <span class="qt0">arthritis</span> is one type.</li><li>Psoriatic <span class="qt0">arthritis</span> affects people with psoriasis (itchy or sore scaly red and white skin patches).</li><li>Gout is a painful type of <span class="qt0">arthritis</span> that happens when too much uric acid builds up in your body. It often starts in the big toe. Other joints can also be affected.</li><li>Ankylosing spondylitis is a type of <span class="qt0">arthritis</span> of the spine that can cause stiffness and make it difficult to move and bend.</li></ul>What are the symptoms of <span class="qt0">arthritis</span>?<p>Pain, redness, warmth, and joint inflammation are common <span class="qt0">arthritis</span> symptoms. You may not be able to move your joint as well as it should. Other symptoms could include fever, weight loss, breathing difficulties, or a rash.</p><p>Symptoms often get worse as you age. Some symptoms of <span class="qt0">arthritis</span> may be signs of other illnesses.</p>Who is more likely to get <span class="qt0">arthritis</span>?<p>A few things that might increase your chance of getting <span class="qt0">arthritis</span> include:</p><ul><li>Family history. You may be more likely to get certain types of <span class="qt0">arthritis</span> if a member of your family has the disorder.</li><li>Age. The risk of getting many types of <span class="qt0">arthritis</span> increases with age.</li><li>Sex. Certain types of <span class="qt0">arthritis</span> are more common in women, while other types are more common in men.</li></ul><p>Other factors that might increase your risk of getting <span class="qt0">arthritis</span> include having a previous joint injury, obesity, or lupus.</p>How is <span class="qt0">arthritis</span> diagnosed?<p>To find out if you have <span class="qt0">arthritis</span>, your health care provider may:</p><ul><li>Ask you about your medical history, including your symptoms.</li><li>Do a physical exam.</li><li>Order blood tests or take x-rays.</li></ul><p>Your provider may refer you to a rheumatologist (a doctor who specializes in <span class="qt0">arthritis</span> care) for tests, diagnosis, and care.</p>What are the treatments for <span class="qt0">arthritis</span>?<p>Treatment depends on the type of <span class="qt0">arthritis</span> you have. It may include medicine or surgery. Your provider will work with you to improve your symptoms and quality of life.</p><p>A few ways to help manage your symptoms include:</p><ul><li>Hot or cold packs</li><li>Relaxation techniques</li><li>Use of splints, braces, and/or assistive devices</li></ul><p>NIH: National Institute of <span class="qt0">Arthritis</span> and Musculoskeletal and Skin Diseases</p></content>
      <content name="mesh"><span class="qt0">Arthritis</span></content>
      <content name="groupName">Bones, Joints and Muscles</content>

    <document rank="0" url="https://medlineplus.gov/juvenilearthritis.html">
      <content name="title">Juvenile <span class="qt0">Arthritis</span></content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Juvenile Rheumatoid <span class="qt0">Arthritis</span></content>
      <content name="altTitle">Still's Disease</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Childhood <span class="qt0">arthritis</span></content>
      <content name="altTitle">JRA</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Juvenile idiopathic <span class="qt0">arthritis</span></content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is juvenile <span class="qt0">arthritis</span>?<p>Juvenile <span class="qt0">arthritis</span> (JA) is <span class="qt0">arthritis</span> that happens in children. It causes joint inflammation (swelling), pain, stiffness, and loss of motion. Joints are places where two bones meet, such as your elbow or knee. It can affect any joint, but it is more common in the knees, hands, and feet.</p><p>JA can affect your child's growth and development, and in some cases, it can also affect other organs. Finding JA early and starting treatment can help manage symptoms and reduce joint damage.</p>Who is more likely to get juvenile <span class="qt0">arthritis</span>?<p>There are several other forms of <span class="qt0">arthritis</span> affecting children. The most common type of JA in children under age 16 is juvenile idiopathic <span class="qt0">arthritis</span> (JIA). There are multiple types of JIA. Symptoms and the number of joints affected help determine the type. Some types of JIA are more common in girls.</p>What causes juvenile <span class="qt0">arthritis</span>?<p>The exact cause of JA is unknown. Most types are autoimmune disorders. This means that your immune system, which normally helps your body fight infection, attacks your body's tissues.</p>What are the symptoms of juvenile <span class="qt0">arthritis</span>?<p>Symptoms can vary depending on the type of JA. The symptoms of most types include joint pain, swelling, warmth, and stiffness. Your child may not complain of pain or stiffness, but one early sign of JA may be limping or clumsiness in the morning or after resting. Larger joints, such as the knee, may be swollen.</p><p>Certain types of JA can cause a high fever, swollen lymph nodes, a rash, growth problems, or eye inflammation in some children. Symptoms can come and go. Some children have just one or two flare-ups. Others have symptoms that never go away.</p>How is juvenile <span class="qt0">arthritis</span> diagnosed?<p>There is no single test for JA, so it can be hard to diagnose. Your health care provider may:</p><ul><li>Ask about medical history and symptoms.</li><li>Do a physical exam.</li><li>Order blood tests or imaging studies.</li></ul>What are the treatments for juvenile <span class="qt0">arthritis</span>?<p>A team of providers usually treats JA. Treatment will depend on your child's age, the type of JA, and how bad it is. Treatment can include medicines and physical therapy to help maintain movement and reduce swelling and pain.</p><p>NIH: National Institute of <span class="qt0">Arthritis</span> and Musculoskeletal and Skin Diseases</p></content>
      <content name="mesh"><span class="qt0">Arthritis</span>, Juvenile</content>
      <content name="groupName">Children and Teenagers</content>
      <content name="groupName">Bones, Joints and Muscles</content>
      <content name="groupName">Immune System</content>
      <content name="snippet">What is juvenile <span class="qt0">arthritis</span>? Juvenile <span class="qt0">arthritis</span> (JA) is <span class="qt0">arthritis</span> that happens in children. It causes joint inflammation (swelling), pain, stiffness, and  ... </content>
