    <document rank="1" url="https://medlineplus.gov/infectiousarthritis.html">
      <content name="title">Infectious <span class="qt0">Arthritis</span></content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Reactive <span class="qt0">Arthritis</span></content>
      <content name="altTitle">Reiter's Syndrome</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Septic <span class="qt0">Arthritis</span></content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>Most kinds of <span class="qt0">arthritis</span> cause pain and swelling in your joints. Joints are places where two bones meet, such as your elbow or knee.  Infectious <span class="qt0">arthritis</span> is an infection in the joint. The infection comes from a bacterial, viral, or fungal infection that spreads from another part of the body.  Symptoms of infectious <span class="qt0">arthritis</span> include:</p><ul><li>Intense pain in the joint</li><li>Joint redness and swelling</li><li>Chills and fever</li><li>Inability to move the area with the infected joint</li></ul><p>One type of infectious <span class="qt0">arthritis</span> is reactive <span class="qt0">arthritis</span>. The reaction is to an infection somewhere else in your body. The joint is usually the knee, ankle, or toe.  Sometimes, reactive <span class="qt0">arthritis</span> is set off by an infection in the bladder, or in the urethra, which carries urine out of the body. In women, an infection in the vagina can cause the reaction. For both men and women, it can start with bacteria passed on during sex. Another form of reactive <span class="qt0">arthritis</span> starts with eating food or handling something that has bacteria on it.</p><p>To diagnose infectious <span class="qt0">arthritis</span>, your health care provider may do tests of your blood, urine, and joint fluid. Treatment includes medicines and sometimes surgery.</p></content>
      <content name="mesh"><span class="qt0">Arthritis</span>, Infectious</content>
      <content name="groupName">Bones, Joints and Muscles</content>
      <content name="groupName">Infections</content>
      <content name="snippet"> Most kinds of <span class="qt0">arthritis</span> cause pain and swelling in your joints. Joints are places where two bones meet, such as your elbow or knee.  Infectious <span class="qt0">arthritis</span> is  ... </content>
    </document>
    <document rank="3" url="https://medlineplus.gov/psoriaticarthritis.html">
      <content name="title">Psoriatic <span class="qt0">Arthritis</span></content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>Psoriasis is a skin disease that causes itchy or sore patches of thick, red skin with silvery scales. You usually get them on your elbows, knees, scalp, back, face, palms and feet, but they can show up on other parts of your body.</p><p>Some people with psoriasis have psoriatic <span class="qt0">arthritis</span>.  It causes pain, stiffness, and swelling of the joints.  It is often mild, but can sometimes be serious and affect many joints. The joint and skin problems don't always happen at the same time.</p><p>Your doctor will do a physical exam and imaging tests to diagnose psoriatic <span class="qt0">arthritis</span>. There is no cure, but medicines can help control inflammation and pain. In rare cases, you might need surgery to repair or replace damaged joints.</p></content>
      <content name="mesh"><span class="qt0">Arthritis</span>, Psoriatic</content>
      <content name="groupName">Bones, Joints and Muscles</content>
      <content name="snippet"> Psoriasis is a skin disease that causes itchy or sore patches of thick, red skin with silvery scales. You usually get them on your elbows, knees, scalp, back,  ... </content>
    </document>
    <document rank="4" url="https://medlineplus.gov/rheumatoidarthritis.html">
      <content name="title">Rheumatoid <span class="qt0">Arthritis</span></content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">RA</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is rheumatoid <span class="qt0">arthritis</span>?<p>Rheumatoid <span class="qt0">arthritis</span> (RA) is a form of <span class="qt0">arthritis</span> that causes pain, swelling, and stiffness in your joints. RA is an autoimmune disease. Although it is most common in the wrist and fingers, this disease can cause your immune system to attack any joint tissue. The inflammation (swelling) that comes with RA can also affect other body parts. Inflammation could cause medical issues in your eyes, skin, heart, nerves, blood, or lungs.</p><p>RA differs from <span class="qt0">osteoarthritis</span>, a common <span class="qt0">arthritis</span> that often comes with age. RA affects the lining of your joints and damages the tissue that covers the ends of the bones in a joint. Eventually, this might cause your joints to not work as well.</p><p>There is no cure for RA, but early treatment can help you manage symptoms, reduce joint damage, and lead a productive life.</p>Who is more likely to get rheumatoid <span class="qt0">arthritis</span>?<p>Rheumatoid <span class="qt0">arthritis</span> can happen at any age, but your risk increases as you become an older adult. Your chance of developing RA can also increase if:</p><ul><li>You're a woman. Women are more likely to get RA than men.</li><li>You have a family history. If a member of your family has RA, you're more likely to get the disease.</li><li>You're a smoker.Smoking over a long period of time increases your risk of getting RA and how serious the disease can be.</li><li>You have other medical conditions that may increase your risk of getting RA. These include obesity, gum disease, and lung disease.</li></ul>What causes rheumatoid <span class="qt0">arthritis</span>?<p>No one knows what causes rheumatoid <span class="qt0">arthritis</span>. A few things that might play a part in getting RA include:</p><ul><li>Your genes are parts of DNA in your cells that are passed down from your parents. Certain genes may increase your risk of getting rheumatoid <span class="qt0">arthritis</span>, but not everyone with these genes gets RA.</li><li>The environment, which includes things such as cigarette smoke, sometimes triggers RA symptoms.</li><li>Hormones are chemical messengers in your bloodstream that control the actions of certain cells or organs. Researchers think that sex hormones might play a role in getting RA. Women are more likely to develop the disease, and RA symptoms may change during and after pregnancy.</li></ul>What are the symptoms of rheumatoid <span class="qt0">arthritis</span>?<p>Your immune system may start turning against your body's joints and tissues years before you notice any symptoms of RA. The changes from this type of immune malfunction usually settle in your joints.</p><p>RA affects people differently. You might have the disease for only a short time, or your symptoms might come and go. The severe form can be chronic (long-lasting) and may last a lifetime.</p><p>Symptoms of rheumatoid <span class="qt0">arthritis</span> may include:</p><ul><li>Joint pain or stiffness when moving. This is usually worse in the morning or after inactivity. RA usually affects both joints. If one of your hands or knees is affected, so is the other.</li><li>Joint tenderness, redness, and warmth.</li><li>Joint swelling may make daily activities hard. These could include things such as combing your hair, buttoning your clothes, or bending your knees.</li><li>Fatigue, fever, and a loss of appetite.</li><li>Hard bumps (rheumatoid nodules) under your skin near the joints.</li></ul>How is rheumatoid <span class="qt0">arthritis</span> diagnosed?<p>There's no single test for rheumatoid <span class="qt0">arthritis</span>. The disease develops over time. At first, you may only have a few symptoms, making it hard to diagnose RA in its early stages. To find out if you have RA, your health care provider may:</p><ul><li>Ask about your medical history, including your symptoms.</li><li>Ask about your family health history, including relatives who have had RA.</li><li>Do a physical exam, which includes checking your joints, looking for rashes or nodules, and listening to your chest for signs of inflammation in your lungs.</li><li>Order blood tests or imaging studies (x-ray or ultrasound) to make sure other health conditions aren't causing your symptoms since they may be like other types of <span class="qt0">arthritis</span> and joint conditions.</li></ul><p>Your provider may refer you to a rheumatologist (a doctor who specializes in <span class="qt0">arthritis</span> care) for tests, diagnosis, and care.</p>What are the treatments for rheumatoid <span class="qt0">arthritis</span>?<p>There is no cure for RA, but early treatment can help prevent your symptoms from getting worse and damaging your joints. Treatment can include medicine, lifestyle changes, and surgery. These may slow or stop joint damage and reduce pain and swelling.</p><p>Your provider may use a combination of treatments. Your treatment may change based on your symptoms.</p><p>You can help manage your RA symptoms if you:</p><ul><li>Are physically active</li><li>Keep a healthy weight</li><li>Avoid injuries to your joints</li><li>Avoid activities that have repetitive motions, like bending your knee over and over</li><li>Stop smoking, or don't start</li></ul><p>NIH: National Institute of <span class="qt0">Arthritis</span> and Musculoskeletal and Skin Diseases</p></content>
      <content name="mesh"><span class="qt0">Arthritis</span>, Rheumatoid</content>
      <content name="groupName">Bones, Joints and Muscles</content>
      <content name="groupName">Immune System</content>
      <content name="snippet">What is rheumatoid <span class="qt0">arthritis</span>? Rheumatoid <span class="qt0">arthritis</span> (RA) is a form of <span class="qt0">arthritis</span> that causes pain, swelling, and stiffness in your joints. RA is an autoimmune  ... </content>
    </document>
    <document rank="5" url="https://medlineplus.gov/gout.html">
      <content name="title">Gout</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Chondrocalcinosis</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Hyperuricemia</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Pseudogout</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Gouty <span class="qt0">arthritis</span></content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is gout?<p>Gout is a common type of inflammatory <span class="qt0">arthritis</span>. It causes pain, swelling, and redness in one or more joints. It usually happens as a flare, which can last for a week or two and then gets better. The flares often begin in your big toe or a lower limb.</p>What causes gout?<p>Gout happens when too much uric acid (urate) builds up in your body over a long time. Uric acid is a waste product your body makes when it breaks down purines. Purines are substances that are in your body's tissues and in many foods.</p><p>When your body breaks down old cells or digests foods that contain purine, most of the uric acid that's made dissolves in your blood. Your kidneys filter the uric acid out of your blood, and it leaves your body in your urine (pee).</p><p>However, sometimes your body can make too much uric acid or does not remove enough of it. Then the uric acid levels build up in your body, including in your blood. Having too much uric acid in the blood is called hyperuricemia. It does not cause health problems for everyone. But in some people, uric acid forms needle-like crystals. They can form in your joints, which causes gout. The crystals can also cause kidney stones.</p><p>Calcium pyrophosphate <span class="qt0">arthritis</span>, sometimes called pseudogout, is a related disease. It causes similar symptoms and is sometimes confused with gout. But it is caused by a buildup of calcium phosphate, not uric acid.</p>Who is more likely to develop gout? <p>Many people develop gout. You are more likely to get it if you:</p><ul><li>Are male.</li><li>Are older; it usually develops in middle age.</li><li>Have obesity.</li><li>Have certain health conditions, such as:
  <ul><li>Heart failure</li><li>High blood pressure</li><li>Metabolic syndrome</li><li>Chronic kidney disease</li><li>Conditions that cause your cells to break down more quickly, such as psoriasis or some cancers</li><li>Rare genetic conditions that lead to increased uric acid</li></ul>
</li><li>Have a family history of gout.</li><li>Have an unhealthy diet and eat foods that are rich in purines, such as red meat, organ meats, certain seafoods.</li><li>Drink alcohol.</li><li>Eat and drink lots of foods and beverages that contain fructose (a type of sugar).</li><li>Take certain medicines, such as diuretics (water pills), low-dose aspirin, and some medicines that weaken your immune system.</li><li>Take high amounts of niacin (vitamin B-3).</li></ul>What are the symptoms of gout?<p>Gout usually happens in only one joint at a time. It is often found in the big toe, but can also affect other joints, including your other toes, ankle, and knee.</p><p>Gout flares often start suddenly at night, and the symptoms in the affected joint often include:</p><ul><li>Intense pain, which may be bad enough to wake you up</li><li>Swelling</li><li>Redness</li><li>Warmth</li></ul><p>The flares typically get better within a week or two. In between flares, you usually don't have symptoms. Some people may have flares often, while others may not have another flare for years. But over time, if left untreated, your flares may happen more often and last longer. </p><p>And if gout is untreated over long periods of time, you can develop tophi. Tophi are hard, uric acid deposits under the skin. They start out as painless, but over time, they can become painful. They can also cause bone and soft tissue damage and misshapen joints.</p>How is gout diagnosed?<p>To find out if you have gout, your health care provider:</p><ul><li>Will take your medical history, which includes asking about your symptoms.</li><li>Will do a physical exam, including examining the affected joint(s).</li><li>May order various tests, such as:
  <ul><li>A test of a sample of fluid from one of your painful joints. The fluid is examined under a microscope and is checked for uric acid crystals.</li><li>A uric acid blood or urine test.</li><li>An ultrasound or special CT scan to look for uric acid crystal buildup in the affected joint and check for other conditions that may be causing the symptoms.</li></ul>
</li></ul>What are the treatments for gout?<p>There are effective treatments for gout. Which treatment you get will depend on your symptoms and the cause of your gout. The goals of your treatment will be to:</p><ul><li>Reduce the pain from gout flares, by using medicines such as:
  <ul><li>Nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) like ibuprofen.</li><li>Acetaminophen and the anti-inflammatory drug colchicine.</li><li>Oral or injected corticosteroids.</li></ul>
</li><li>Prevent future flares, for example by:
  <ul><li>Making lifestyle changes such as losing weight, limiting alcohol, and avoiding foods high in purines. If you are taking medicines that can cause high uric acid in the blood, your provider may suggest stopping or changing those medicines.</li><li>If needed, taking medicines to lower uric acid in the blood.</li></ul>
</li><li>Prevent tophi and kidney stones, for example with medicines that lower uric acid in the blood.</li></ul><p>With early diagnosis, treatment, and lifestyle changes, gout is one of the most controllable forms of <span class="qt0">arthritis</span>. Treatment and lifestyle changes may help people avoid gout flares, lessen their symptoms, and sometimes even become gout free.</p><p>NIH: National Institute of <span class="qt0">Arthritis</span> and Musculoskeletal and Skin Diseases</p></content>

    <document rank="6" url="https://medlineplus.gov/osteoarthritis.html">
      <content name="title"><span class="qt0">Osteoarthritis</span></content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Degenerative Joint Disease</content>
      <content name="altTitle">OA</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Osteoarthrosis</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is <span class="qt0">osteoarthritis</span>?<p><span class="qt0">Osteoarthritis</span>, sometimes called OA, is a type of <span class="qt0">arthritis</span> that only affects the joints, usually in the hands, knees, hips, neck, and lower back. It's the most common type of <span class="qt0">arthritis</span>.</p><p>In a healthy joint, the ends of the bones are covered with a smooth, slippery tissue called cartilage. The cartilage pads the bones and helps them glide easily when you move the joint. With <span class="qt0">osteoarthritis</span>, the cartilage breaks down and becomes rough. Sometimes, all the cartilage wears away and the bones rub together. Bumps of extra bone called bone spurs may grow in the joint area.</p><p>There is no cure for <span class="qt0">osteoarthritis</span>. It usually gets worse slowly. But there's a lot you can do to manage the symptoms.</p>What are the symptoms of <span class="qt0">osteoarthritis</span>?<p><span class="qt0">Osteoarthritis</span> affects people in different ways, and not everyone has pain. The most common symptoms are:</p><ul><li>Pain when you move, which often gets better with rest</li><li>Stiffness, especially for the first 30 minutes after you get up from resting</li><li>Swollen joints, especially after using the joint a lot</li><li>Less movement in the joint than normal</li><li>A joint that feels loose or unstable</li></ul>What causes <span class="qt0">osteoarthritis</span>?<p>Researchers aren't sure what causes <span class="qt0">osteoarthritis</span>. They think that it could be caused by a combination of factors in the body and the environment. Your chance of developing <span class="qt0">osteoarthritis</span> increases with age. They also know that some people are more likely to develop it than others.</p>Who is more likely to develop <span class="qt0">osteoarthritis</span>?<p>Things that make you more likely to develop <span class="qt0">osteoarthritis</span> include:</p><ul><li>Aging. <span class="qt0">Osteoarthritis</span> can happen at any age, but the chance of getting it increases in middle-aged adults and older. After age 50, it is more common in women than in men.</li><li>Being overweight. Extra weight puts more stress on your joints.</li><li>Having a past injury or surgery on a joint. This is often the cause of <span class="qt0">osteoarthritis</span> in younger adults.</li><li>Doing a lot of activities that overuse the joint. This includes sports with a lot of jumping, twisting, running, or throwing.</li><li>Having a joint that doesn't line up correctly.</li><li>A family history of <span class="qt0">osteoarthritis</span>. Some people inherit genetic changes that increase their chance of developing <span class="qt0">osteoarthritis</span>.</li></ul>How is <span class="qt0">osteoarthritis</span> diagnosed?<p>There is no specific test for <span class="qt0">osteoarthritis</span>. To find out if you have <span class="qt0">osteoarthritis</span>, your provider:</p><ul><li>Will ask about your symptoms and medical history</li><li>Will do a physical exam</li><li>May use x-rays or other imaging tests to look at your joints</li><li>May order lab tests to make sure that a different problem isn't causing your symptoms</li></ul>What are the treatments for <span class="qt0">osteoarthritis</span>?<p>The goal of treating <span class="qt0">osteoarthritis</span> is to ease your pain, help you move better, and stop it from getting worse.</p><p>Treatment usually begins with:</p><ul><li>Exercises to improve strength, flexibility and balance</li><li>Weight loss, if needed, to improve pain, especially in your hips or knees</li><li>Braces or shoe inserts (orthotics) that a health care provider fits for you</li></ul><p>You can buy some pain relievers and <span class="qt0">arthritis</span> creams without a prescription. They can be helpful, but it's best to talk to your provider about using them. If they don't help enough, your provider may prescribe injections (shots) into the joint or prescription pain relievers.</p><p>Complementary therapies may help some people. Massage can increase blood flow and bring warmth to the area. Some research shows that acupuncture may help relieve <span class="qt0">osteoarthritis</span> pain. Simple things like heat and ice can help, too.</p><p>If none of these treatments help enough, surgery may be an option. You and your provider can decide if it's right for you.</p><p>NIH: National Institute of <span class="qt0">Arthritis</span> and Musculoskeletal and Skin Diseases</p></content>
      <content name="mesh"><span class="qt0">Osteoarthritis</span></content>
      <content name="groupName">Older Adults</content>
      <content name="groupName">Bones, Joints and Muscles</content>
      <content name="snippet">What is <span class="qt0">osteoarthritis? Osteoarthritis</span>, sometimes called OA, is a type of <span class="qt0">arthritis</span> that only affects the joints, usually in the hands, knees, hips, neck,  ... </content>

    <document rank="7" url="https://medlineplus.gov/jointdisorders.html">
      <content name="title">Joint Disorders</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Arthrography</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Arthroplasty</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Arthroscopy</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What are joints?<p>Your joints are places where two or more bones come together. Your shoulders, elbows, hips, knees, and knuckles are all joints. Your spine has joints, too.</p><p>But joints are more than bones. They include the soft tissues around them, such as cartilage, tendons and ligaments. Cartilage is the hard slippery flexible tissue that covers the ends of your bones at a joint. Tendons are tough, flexible bands that connect your muscles to your bones so you can move your joints. Ligaments connect the bones of the joint to each other to keep them stable when you move.</p>What are joint disorders?<p>Joint disorders are diseases or injuries that affect your joints. Injuries can happen because of overuse of a joint. Or you could have a sudden injury, such as an accident or a sports injury.</p>What diseases can affect the joints?<p>Many diseases can affect the joints. They often cause joint pain and make your joints stiff, red, or swollen. Most of them are chronic. That means they last a long time. Some may never go away completely. Some of the diseases that affect the joints include:</p><ul><li><span class="qt0">Arthritis. Arthritis</span> may cause joint pain and swelling. There are many types of this disease. <span class="qt0">Osteoarthritis</span> is the most common type. Over time, <span class="qt0">arthritis</span> can cause severe joint damage. It can affect people of all ages. A joint injury when you're young may cause <span class="qt0">osteoarthritis</span> later in life.</li><li>Lupus. This autoimmune disease affects many parts of the body and can cause joint and muscle pain. Some types of lupus often cause <span class="qt0">arthritis</span>.</li><li>Sjögren's Syndrome. This autoimmune disease affects glands that make moisture in many parts of the body. The main symptoms are dry eyes and mouth, but it often causes joint pain, too.</li></ul><p>Treatments are different depending on the disease. But most treatments include medicines and therapies to relieve pain and other symptoms.</p>What types of joint disorders happen from sudden injuries?<p>Joint disorders from sudden injuries include:</p><ul><li>Sprains and strains. Sprains are stretched or torn ligaments. Acute strains are stretched or torn muscles or tendons that happen from a sudden injury or movement, such as lifting a heavy object.</li><li>Dislocated joints. A joint is dislocated when the bones are pushed or pulled out of position. A joint dislocation is a medical emergency.</li></ul><p>Treatment depends on the type of injury. You can treat many sports injuries at home. But you should call your health care provider if you:</p><ul><li>Have a lot of joint pain, swelling or numbness</li><li>Can't put weight on the joint</li><li>Have pain from an old injury with more swelling, an unstable joint, or a joint that isn't normal in another way</li></ul>What types of joint disorders happen from overuse?<p>Overuse injuries usually damage the soft tissues of the joint. They can happen when you work a joint too hard by doing the same movements over and over. For example, you could get an overuse injury from playing a musical instrument, playing sports, or doing certain jobs, such as carpentry or painting.</p><p>Joint overuse injuries include:</p><ul><li>Bursitis. The bursa is a small fluid-filled sac. It works as a pad between the bones of a joint and the moving parts around it, such as muscles, tendons and skin. With bursitis, the bursa becomes irritated and swollen with extra fluid. Overuse is the most common cause, but injuries, infections and other conditions, such as <span class="qt0">arthritis</span>, can cause bursitis.</li><li>Tendinitis. This condition happens when you overuse a tendon. It swells and makes the joint painful to move.</li><li>Chronic strain. A strain becomes chronic when your muscles or tendons stretch or tear slowly over time from repeating the same movements.</li></ul><p>The treatments for bursitis, tendinitis, and chronic strain are often the same. They usually include rest, keeping the injured joint higher than your heart, and taking medicine to reduce swelling. Your provider may recommend gentle exercise and other treatment. In some cases, your provider may suggest an injection (a shot) of medicine into the joint. If these do not help, you may need surgery.</p>How can I keep my joints healthy?<p>Getting enough physical activity is one of the most important things you can do to prevent or slow joint disorders. Activity strengthens the muscles around your joints and helps them work better.</p><p>When you play sports, wear the right equipment to protect your joints, such as knee pads. If you already have joint problems, ask your provider what type of activities are best for you.</p><p>NIH: National Institute of <span class="qt0">Arthritis</span> and Musculoskeletal and Skin Diseases</p></content>

    <document rank="8" url="https://medlineplus.gov/pagetsdiseaseofbone.html">
      <content name="title">Paget's Disease of Bone</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Osteitis Deformans</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is Paget's disease of bone?<p>Paget's disease of bone is a chronic bone disorder. Normally, there is a process in which your bones break down and then regrow. In Paget's disease, this process is abnormal. There is excessive breakdown and regrowth of bone. Because the bones regrow too quickly, they are bigger and softer than normal. They may be misshapen and easily fractured (broken). Paget's usually affects just one or a few bones.</p>What causes Paget's disease of bone?<p>Researchers do not know for sure what causes Paget's disease. Environmental factors may play a role. In some cases, the disease runs in families, and several genes have been linked to the disease.</p>Who is at risk for Paget's disease of bone?<p>The disease is more common in older people and those of northern European heritage. If you have a close relative who has Paget's, you are much more likely to have it.</p>What are the symptoms of Paget's disease of bone?<p>Many people do not know that they have Paget's, because it often has no symptoms. When there are symptoms, they are similar to those of <span class="qt0">arthritis</span> and other disorders. The symptoms include:</p><ul><li>Pain, which may be due to the disease or to <span class="qt0">arthritis</span>, which can be a complication of Paget's</li><li>Headaches and hearing loss, which can happen when Paget's disease affects the skull</li><li>Pressure on the nerves, which can happen when Paget's disease affects the skull or spine</li><li>Increased head size, bowing of a limb, or curvature of the spine. This can happen in advanced cases.</li><li>Hip pain, if Paget's disease affects the pelvis or thighbone</li><li>Damage to the cartilage of your joints, which may lead to <span class="qt0">arthritis</span></li></ul><p>Usually, Paget's disease gets worse slowly over time. It does not spread to normal bones.</p>What other problems can Paget's disease of bone cause?<p>Paget's disease can lead to other complications, such as:</p><ul><li><span class="qt0">Arthritis</span>, because the misshapen bones can cause increased pressure and more wear and tear on the joints</li><li>Heart failure. In severe Paget's disease, the heart has to work harder to pump blood to affected bones. Heart failure is more likely if you also have hardening of the arteries.</li><li>Kidney stones, which can happen when the excessive breakdown of the bone leads to extra calcium in the body</li><li>Nervous system problems, since the bones can cause pressure on the brain, spinal cord, or nerves. There may also be reduced blood flow to the brain and spinal cord.</li><li>Osteosarcoma, cancer of the bone</li><li>Loose teeth, if Paget's disease affects the facial bones</li><li>Vision loss, if Paget's disease in the skull affects the nerves. This is rare.</li></ul>How is Paget's disease of bone diagnosed?<p>Your health care provider may use many tools to make a diagnosis:</p><ul><li>A medical history, which includes asking about your symptoms</li><li>A physical exam</li><li>An x-ray of the affected bones. Paget's disease is almost always diagnosed using x-rays.</li><li>An alkaline phosphatase blood test</li><li>A bone scan</li></ul><p>Sometimes the disease is found by accident when one of these tests is done for another reason.</p>What are the treatments for Paget's disease of bone?<p>To avoid complications, it is important to find and treat Paget's disease early. The treatments include:</p><ul><li>Medicines. There are several different medicines to treat Paget's disease. The most common type is bisphosphonates. They help reduce bone pain and stop or slow down the progress of the disease.</li><li>Surgery is sometimes needed for certain complications of the disease. There are surgeries to
	<ul><li>Allow fractures (broken bones) to heal in a better position</li><li>Replace joints such as the knee and hip when there is severe <span class="qt0">arthritis</span></li><li>Realign a deformed bone to reduce the pain in weight-bearing joints, especially the knees</li><li>Reduce pressure on a nerve, if enlargement of the skull or spine injuries effects the nervous system</li></ul>
</li></ul><p>Diet and exercise do not treat Paget's, but they can help to keep your skeleton healthy. If you do not have kidney stones, you should make sure to get enough calcium and vitamin D through your diet and supplements. Besides keeping your skeleton healthy, exercise can prevent weight gain and maintain the mobility of your joints. Talk with your health care provider before you start a new exercise program. You need to make sure that the exercise does not put too much stress on the affected bones.</p><p>NIH: National Institute of <span class="qt0">Arthritis</span> and Musculoskeletal and Skin Diseases</p></content>

    <document rank="9" url="https://medlineplus.gov/psoriasis.html">
      <content name="title">Psoriasis</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>Psoriasis is a skin disease that causes itchy or sore patches of thick, red skin with silvery scales. You usually get the patches on your elbows, knees, scalp, back, face, palms and feet, but they can show up on other parts of your body.  Some people who have psoriasis also get a form of <span class="qt0">arthritis</span> called psoriatic <span class="qt0">arthritis</span>.</p><p>A problem with your immune system causes psoriasis. In a process called cell turnover, skin cells that grow deep in your skin rise to the surface. Normally, this takes a month. In psoriasis, it happens in just days because your cells rise too fast. </p><p>Psoriasis can be hard to diagnose because it can look like other skin diseases. Your doctor might need to look at a small skin sample under a microscope.</p><p>Psoriasis can last a long time, even a lifetime. Symptoms come and go. Things that make them worse include:</p><ul><li>Infections</li><li>Stress</li><li>Dry skin</li><li>Certain medicines</li></ul><p>Psoriasis usually occurs in adults. It sometimes runs in families. Treatments include creams, medicines, and light therapy.</p><p>NIH: National Institute of <span class="qt0">Arthritis</span> and Musculoskeletal and Skin Diseases </p></content>
      <content name="mesh">Psoriasis</content>
      <content name="groupName">Skin, Hair and Nails</content>

A problem with your immune system causes psoriasis.  ... medicines, and light therapy. NIH: National Institute of <span class="qt0">Arthritis</span> and Musculoskeletal and Skin Diseases </content>

    <document rank="10" url="https://medlineplus.gov/wristinjuriesanddisorders.html">
      <content name="title">Wrist Injuries and Disorders</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Ganglion Cyst</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>Your wrist connects your hand to your forearm. It is not one big joint; it has several small joints. This makes it flexible and allows you to move your hand in different ways. The wrist has two big forearm bones and eight small bones known as carpals. It also has tendons and ligaments, which are connective tissues. Tendons connect muscles to bones. Ligaments connect bones to each other.</p>What are the types of wrist injuries and disorders?<p>Some of the more common types of wrist injuries and disorders are:</p><ul><li>Carpal tunnel syndrome, which happens when a nerve that runs from your forearm into your palm becomes squeezed at the wrist</li><li>Ganglion cysts, which are noncancerous lumps or masses</li><li>Gout, which is a form of <span class="qt0">arthritis</span> caused by a buildup of uric acid in your joints</li><li>Fractures (broken bones)</li><li><span class="qt0">Osteoarthritis</span>, the most common type of <span class="qt0">arthritis</span>. It is caused by wear and tear of the joints.</li><li>Sprains and strains, which are injuries to ligaments and injuries to muscles or tendons</li><li>Tendinitis, inflammation of a tendon, usually due to overuse</li></ul>Who is at risk for wrist injuries and disorders?<p>Certain things can put you at higher risk of having a wrist problem, including:</p><ul><li>Doing sports, which can put you at risk for injuries and puts stress on your wrist. For example, you may fall on your outstretched hand when you are skating or snowboarding. Your wrist could be injured while doing contact sports. And other sports such as gymnastics and basketball can strain your wrists.</li><li>Doing repetitive wrist motions, such as typing on a keyboard, working on an assembly line, or using power tools.</li><li>Having certain diseases. For example, rheumatoid <span class="qt0">arthritis</span> can cause wrist pain.</li></ul>What are the symptoms of wrist injuries and disorders?<p>The symptoms of a wrist problem can vary, depending on the problem. A common symptom is wrist pain. Some other possible symptoms include swelling, a decrease in wrist strength, and sudden numbness or tingling.</p>How are wrist injuries and disorders diagnosed?<p>Your health care provider may use many tools to make a diagnosis:</p><ul><li>A medical history, which includes asking about your symptoms</li><li>A physical exam, including checking your wrist strength and range of motion</li><li>An x-ray or other imaging test</li><li>Blood tests</li></ul>What are the treatments for wrist injuries and disorders?<p>Treatments for wrist pain depends on the type of injury or disorder. They may include:</p><ul><li>Resting your wrist</li><li>Wearing a wrist brace or cast</li><li>Pain relievers</li><li>Cortisone shots</li><li>Physical therapy</li><li>Surgery</li></ul>Can wrist injuries and disorders be prevented?<p>To try to prevent wrist problems, you can:</p><ul><li>Use wrist guards, when doing sports that put you at risk for wrist injuries</li><li>In the workplace, perform stretching exercises and take frequent rest breaks. You should also pay attention to ergonomics to make sure that you are using the proper wrist position while working.</li><li>Make sure that you get enough calcium and vitamin D to keep your bones strong</li></ul></content>

    <document rank="11" url="https://medlineplus.gov/fingerinjuriesanddisorders.html">
      <content name="title">Finger Injuries and Disorders</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Dupuytren Contracture</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Thumb Injuries</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>You use your fingers and thumbs to do everything from grasping objects to playing musical instruments to typing. When there is something wrong with them, it can make life difficult. Common problems include:</p><ul><li>Injuries that result in fractures (broken bones), ruptured ligaments and dislocations</li><li><span class="qt0">Osteoarthritis</span> - wear-and-tear <span class="qt0">arthritis</span>. It can also cause deformity.</li><li>Tendinitis - irritation of the tendons</li><li>Dupuytren's contracture - a hereditary thickening of the tough tissue that lies just below the skin of your palm. It causes the fingers to stiffen and bend.</li><li>Trigger finger - an irritation of the sheath that surrounds the flexor tendons. It can cause the tendon to catch and release like a trigger.</li></ul></content>
      <content name="mesh">Finger Injuries</content>
      <content name="groupName">Bones, Joints and Muscles</content>
      <content name="groupName">Injuries and Wounds</content>
      <content name="snippet"> ... ruptured ligaments and dislocations  <span class="qt0">Osteoarthritis</span> - wear-and-tear <span class="qt0">arthritis</span>. It can also cause deformity.  Tendinitis - irritation of  ... </content>
    </document>
    <document rank="12" url="https://medlineplus.gov/scleroderma.html">
      <content name="title">Scleroderma</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Morphea</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Systemic Sclerosis</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Circumscribed scleroderma</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Dermatosclerosis</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is scleroderma? <p>Scleroderma means "hard skin." It's the name of an autoimmune disease that causes inflammation and thickening in the skin and other areas of the body.  This inflammation causes you to have areas of tight, hard skin.  Scleroderma may affect just one area of your body, or it can affect many systems in your body.</p>What are the types of scleroderma?<p>There are two main types of scleroderma:</p><ul><li>Localized scleroderma only affects your skin and the muscles and tissues just under your skin.</li><li>Systemic scleroderma, which is also called systemic sclerosis, is a more serious type. It affects many parts of your body and can damage your blood vessels and internal organs, such as your heart, lungs, and kidneys.</li></ul>What causes scleroderma?<p>The exact cause of scleroderma is unknown. Researchers think that several factors may play a part in causing the disease:</p><ul><li>Your genetics. Certain genes can increase the chance that you will develop scleroderma. They may also play a role in which the type of scleroderma you have. Scleroderma is not passed from parent to child, but you are more likely to develop it if a close relative has it.</li><li>Your environment. Exposure to certain things in the environment, such as viruses or chemicals, may trigger scleroderma.</li><li>Immune system changes. When your immune system changes, it can trigger your cells to make too much collagen in the body. Too much collagen causes patches of tight, hard skin.</li><li>Hormones. Hormonal or immune system differences between women and men might play a part in the disease.</li></ul>Who is more likely to develop scleroderma?<p>Anyone can get scleroderma, but certain factors may make you more likely to develop it:</p><ul><li>Your sex. Scleroderma is more common in women than in men.</li><li>Your age. The disease usually appears between the ages of 30 and 50.</li><li>Your race. Scleroderma can affect people of all races and ethnic groups, but the disease can affect African Americans more severely.</li></ul>What are the symptoms of scleroderma?<p>The symptoms of scleroderma are different for each person, depending on the type of scleroderma you have:</p><ul><li>Localized scleroderma usually causes patches of thick, hard skin in one of two patterns:
  <ul><li>Patches in firm, oval shapes that stay in one area or spread to other areas of skin. This is called morphea.</li><li>Lines of thickened or different colored skin that run down your arm, leg, and, rarely, on the forehead. This is called linear scleroderma.</li></ul>
</li><li>Systemic scleroderma can cause problems with your internal organs as well as your skin. It can cause symptoms such as:
  <ul><li>Thick, tight skin on your fingers</li><li>Fatigue</li><li>Raynaud's phenomenon, a narrowing of blood vessels in the hands or feet</li><li>Damage to your internal organs, including your digestive system, lungs, kidneys, and heart</li></ul>
</li></ul>How is scleroderma diagnosed?<p>There is no single test for scleroderma. The symptoms can vary from person to person and can be similar to those of other diseases. This can make scleroderma hard to diagnose.</p><p>To find out if you have scleroderma, your health care provider:</p><ul><li>Will ask about your symptoms and medical history</li><li>Will do a physical exam</li><li>May order blood tests, including an ANA (antinuclear antibody) test</li><li>May do a skin biopsy</li><li>May do other tests, such as imaging tests, to check for organ damage</li></ul>What are the treatments for scleroderma?<p>There is no cure for scleroderma, but treatments can help control your symptoms and limit damage. The treatments may include:</p><ul><li>Medicines to help decrease swelling, manage pain, control other symptoms, and prevent complications.</li><li>Physical or occupational therapy to help with pain, improve muscle strength, and teach you ways to help with daily living.</li><li>Regular dental care, because scleroderma can make your mouth dry and damage connective tissues in your mouth. These problems can speed up tooth decay and cause your teeth to become loose.</li></ul><p>You may need to see specialists to help treat your disease. Many people with scleroderma will see a rheumatologist. This is a doctor who specializes in rheumatic diseases such as <span class="qt0">arthritis</span> and other inflammatory or autoimmune disorders. Dermatologists, who specialize in conditions of the skin, hair, and nails, may also play an important role in treating the disease. And if you have organ damage, you may need to see other specialists.</p><p>You can also help manage some of your symptoms, for example by:</p><ul><li>Dressing warm and avoiding cold or wet environments</li><li>Quitting smoking (if you smoke)</li><li>Putting on sunscreen before you go outdoors</li><li>Using moisturizers on your skin to help lessen stiffness</li><li>Avoiding hot baths and showers, harsh soaps, and household cleaners</li><li>Getting regular physical activity</li></ul><p>NIH: National Institute of <span class="qt0">Arthritis</span> and Musculoskeletal and Skin Diseases</p></content>

    <document rank="13" url="https://medlineplus.gov/shoulderinjuriesanddisorders.html">
      <content name="title">Shoulder Injuries and Disorders</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Clavicle Injuries</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Collarbone Injuries</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Frozen Shoulder</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Scapula Injuries</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>Your shoulder joint is composed of three bones: the clavicle (collarbone), the scapula (shoulder blade), and the humerus (upper arm bone). Your shoulders are the most movable joints in your body. They can also be unstable because the ball of the upper arm is larger than the shoulder socket that holds it. To remain in a stable or normal position, the shoulder must be anchored by muscles, tendons, and ligaments.</p><p>Because your shoulder can be unstable, it can be easily injured. Common problems include:</p><ul><li>Sprains and strains</li><li>Dislocations</li><li>Separations</li><li>Tendinitis</li><li>Bursitis</li><li>Torn rotator cuffs</li><li>Frozen shoulder</li><li>Fractures (broken bones)</li><li><span class="qt0">Arthritis</span></li></ul><p>Health care providers diagnose shoulder problems by using your medical history, a physical exam, and imaging tests.</p><p>Often, the first treatment for shoulder problems is RICE. This stands for Rest, Ice, Compression, and Elevation. Other treatments include exercise and medicines to reduce pain and swelling. If those don't work, you may need surgery.</p><p>NIH: National Institute of <span class="qt0">Arthritis</span> and Musculoskeletal and Skin Diseases</p></content>

    <document rank="14" url="https://medlineplus.gov/spinalstenosis.html">
      <content name="title">Spinal Stenosis</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>Your spine, or backbone, protects your spinal cord and allows you to stand and bend. Spinal stenosis causes narrowing in your spine. The narrowing puts pressure on your nerves and spinal cord and can cause pain.</p><p>Spinal stenosis occurs mostly in people older than 50. Younger people with a spine injury or a narrow spinal canal are also at risk. Diseases such as <span class="qt0">arthritis</span> and scoliosis can cause spinal stenosis, too. Symptoms might appear gradually or not at all. They include:</p><ul><li>Pain in your neck or back</li><li>Numbness, weakness, cramping, or pain in your arms or legs</li><li>Pain going down the leg</li><li>Foot problems</li></ul><p>Doctors diagnose spinal stenosis with a physical exam and imaging tests. Treatments include medications, physical therapy, braces, and surgery.</p><p>NIH: National Institute of <span class="qt0">Arthritis</span> and Musculoskeletal and Skin Diseases</p></content>
      <content name="mesh">Spinal Stenosis</content>
      <content name="groupName">Bones, Joints and Muscles</content>

Symptoms  ... therapy, braces, and surgery. NIH: National Institute of <span class="qt0">Arthritis</span> and Musculoskeletal and Skin Diseases</content>

    <document rank="15" url="https://medlineplus.gov/ankylosingspondylitis.html">
      <content name="title">Ankylosing Spondylitis</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Rheumatoid Spondylitis</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Spondylitis, Ankylosing</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is ankylosing spondylitis?<p>Ankylosing spondylitis (AS) is a type of <span class="qt0">arthritis</span> of the spine. It causes inflammation (swelling) between your vertebrae (the bones that make up your spine) and the joints between your spine and pelvis. AS inflammation can cause stiffness and make it difficult to move and bend.</p><p>Symptoms of AS can range from mild to severe back pain. Over time, AS can fuse (grow together) your vertebrae, limiting movement and causing a hunched posture. In some people, AS can affect other joints or body parts.</p><p>There is no cure for AS, but early treatment can help manage symptoms, slow down the disease, and help you lead a more productive life.</p>Who is more likely to get ankylosing spondylitis?<p>Ankylosing spondylitis usually starts before age 45. Early symptoms may begin between the ages of 15 and 30. Your chance of developing AS can also increase if:</p><ul><li>You're a man. AS is more common and severe in men.</li><li>You have a family history. If a member of your family has AS, you're more likely to get the disease.</li><li>You have other medical conditions that may increase your risk of getting AS. These include Crohn's disease, ulcerative colitis, or psoriasis.</li></ul>What causes ankylosing spondylitis?<p>No one knows what causes ankylosing spondylitis, but the environment and genes likely play a role. Your genes are parts of DNA in your cells that are passed down from your parents. Certain genes may increase your risk of getting the disease, but not everyone with these genes gets AS.</p>What are the symptoms of ankylosing spondylitis?<p>AS affects people differently. The most common symptom is pain and stiffness in the lower back and/or hips. The pain is usually worse during the night or after sitting for a long time.</p><p>Over time, AS may progress to other areas of your spine or body. Symptoms may depend on which areas of your body the disease affects. Some people have symptoms that come and go. Others may have severe, ongoing pain.</p><p>Other symptoms of ankylosing spondylitis may include:</p><ul><li>Pain, stiffness, and inflammation of the joints.</li><li>Difficulty taking a deep breath. This could occur if the joints connecting the ribs are affected.</li><li>Changes in vision.</li><li>Fatigue.</li><li>Loss of appetite.</li><li>Weight loss.</li><li>Skin rashes, such as psoriasis.</li><li>Abdominal (belly) pain and loose stools (poop).</li></ul>How is ankylosing spondylitis diagnosed?<p>There is no single test for ankylosing spondylitis. To find out if you have AS your health care provider may:</p><ul><li>Ask about your medical history, including your symptoms.</li><li>Ask about your family health history, including relatives who have had AS.</li><li>Do a physical exam.</li><li>Order blood tests or imaging studies.</li></ul>What are the treatments for ankylosing spondylitis?<p>AS has no cure, but treatment may help relieve symptoms, maintain posture, and slow down the disease. Since other parts of your body can be affected by AS, you will likely work with a team of health care professionals for tests, diagnosis, and care.</p><p>Treatment usually includes medicine to help relieve symptoms and keep the disease from getting worse, as well as physical therapy to improve mobility. If AS is severe, surgery may be needed.</p><p>You can help manage your AS symptoms if you:</p><ul><li>Exercise</li><li>Monitor your symptoms</li><li>Manage your stress</li><li>Use assistive devices as needed</li><li>Follow a healthy diet</li><li>Stop smoking, or don't start</li></ul><p>NIH: National Institute of <span class="qt0">Arthritis</span> and Musculoskeletal and Skin Disease</p></content>

      <content name="groupName">Bones, Joints and Muscles</content>

    <document rank="16" url="https://medlineplus.gov/bonedensity.html">
      <content name="title">Bone Density</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Bone Loss</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Osteopenia</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Bone mass</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is bone density?<p>Your bones are made of living tissue. To keep them strong, your body breaks down old bone and replaces it with new bone. But when more bone is broken down than replaced, you lose bone density, also called bone mass. Bone density is a measure of the amount of calcium and other minerals in bone. Bones containing more minerals are denser. They tend to be stronger and less likely to fracture (break).</p><p>Losing bone density can weaken your bones. In some cases, it can lead to osteoporosis. This is a disease which can make your bones more likely to break. Low bone density that is not low enough to be osteoporosis is sometimes called osteopenia.</p>What can make you lose bone density?<p>Your bones become less dense as you age. Other factors can also make you more likely to lose bone mass, such as:</p><ul><li>A family history of osteoporosis or low bone mass</li><li>Not developing good bone mass when you are young</li><li>Having certain medical conditions, such as:
	<ul><li>Endocrine diseases</li><li>Certain digestive diseases</li><li>Rheumatoid <span class="qt0">arthritis</span></li><li>Certain types of cancer</li><li>HIV</li><li>Anorexia nervosa, a type of eating disorder</li></ul>
</li><li>Taking certain medicines, such as: 
	<ul><li>Corticosteroids</li><li>Proton pump inhibitors (which treat GERD)</li><li>Medicines to treat epilepsy</li></ul>
</li><li>Smoking tobacco</li><li>Long-term heavy alcohol use</li><li>Physical inactivity or prolonged periods of bedrest</li></ul><p>Not everyone who has low bone mass gets osteoporosis, but they are at higher risk for developing it.</p>Is there a test to measure your bone health?<p>A bone density scan is the best way to measure your bone health. It compares your bone density, or mass, to that of a healthy person who is the same age and sex as you are. It can show:</p><ul><li>Whether you have low bone density</li><li>Whether you have osteoporosis, a disease that makes your bones weak</li><li>Your risk for breaking bones</li><li>Whether your osteoporosis treatment is working</li></ul>How can I keep my bones strong?<p>To help keep your bones strong and help prevent osteoporosis, the best thing to do is to eat a healthy diet rich in calcium and vitamin D. Getting regular physical activity, limiting alcohol, and not smoking can also help.  In some cases, your health care provider may prescribe medicines to prevent osteoporosis.</p><p>NIH: National Institute of <span class="qt0">Arthritis</span> and Musculoskeletal and Skin Diseases</p></content>
      <content name="mesh">Bone Density</content>
      <content name="groupName">Bones, Joints and Muscles</content>

	 Endocrine diseases  Certain digestive diseases  Rheumatoid <span class="qt0">arthritis</span>  Certain types of cancer  HIV  Anorexia nervosa, a  ... medicines to prevent osteoporosis. NIH: National Institute of <span class="qt0">Arthritis</span> and Musculoskeletal and Skin Diseases</content>

    <document rank="17" url="https://medlineplus.gov/carpaltunnelsyndrome.html">
      <content name="title">Carpal Tunnel Syndrome</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Median nerve entrapment</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is carpal tunnel syndrome?<p>Carpal tunnel syndrome is the name for a group of problems that includes numbness, tingling, weakness, or pain in your wrist or hand. It's a very common condition that happens when a nerve in your wrist becomes squeezed.</p><p>The carpal tunnel is a narrow opening between the bones in your wrist and the ligament that holds them together.  A nerve, called the median nerve, runs from your lower arm through the carpal tunnel and into your hand. This nerve provides feeling to your thumb and first three fingers.  It also helps you move your thumb.</p><p>Tendons also run from your lower arm, through the carpal tunnel, and into your hand. The tendons connect your finger bones to muscles in your arm, so you can bend your fingers.</p><p>If the tendons become swollen or irritated inside the carpal tunnel, they may press on the nerve. The squeezed nerve may not work well, which can lead to numbness, weakness, and other symptoms of carpal tunnel syndrome.</p>What causes carpal tunnel syndrome?<p>In certain cases, the cause of carpal tunnel syndrome is unknown. But it usually happens from a combination of things that affect the structures in your wrist, such as:</p><ul><li>Injury to your wrist that causes swelling, such as a sprain or broken bone</li><li>Health conditions that can affect your nerves, joints, or other parts of your wrist, for example:
	<ul><li>Diabetes.</li><li>Rheumatoid <span class="qt0">arthritis</span>.</li><li>A cyst or tumor in your wrist.</li><li>Changes in  certain hormones, which may cause swelling from extra fluid in your body. Examples include an underactive thyroid gland (hypothyroidism), pregnancy and menopause.</li><li>Problems with the way your wrist is formed.</li></ul>
</li><li>Doing the same wrist and hand movements over and over, such as: 
	<ul><li>Assembly line work.</li><li>Carpentry or using tools that vibrate.</li><li>Knitting, gardening, or sports, such as golf.</li></ul>
</li></ul><p>Researchers aren't sure whether long-term typing or computer use leads to carpal tunnel syndrome.</p>Who is more likely to develop carpal tunnel syndrome?<p>Carpal tunnel syndrome usually happens only in adults. You're more likely to develop it if you:</p><ul><li>Are a woman. Women are three times more likely to have carpal tunnel syndrome than men.</li><li>Have a health condition that may affect structures in your wrist.</li><li>Do activities with repeated hand motions.</li><li>Have had a wrist injury.</li><li>Have a close relative who has had carpal tunnel syndrome, such as a parent, brother or sister.</li></ul>What are the symptoms of carpal tunnel syndrome?<p>You may feel numbness, tingling or burning in your fingers, especially in your thumb, index, and middle fingers. Symptoms often affect both hands.</p><p>In the early stages, symptoms usually:</p><ul><li>Start slowly in your dominant hand (the one you write with)</li><li>Happen at night if you sleep with your wrists bent</li></ul><p>As symptoms get worse, you may notice:</p><ul><li>Tingling, pain, or weakness with certain activities, such as driving or holding a phone</li><li>Difficulty grasping or holding small things</li><li>Feeling like your fingers are swollen when they're not</li></ul><p>Without treatment, you could lose feeling in some fingers and have permanent weakness in your thumb. But early diagnosis and treatment can help prevent lasting damage.</p><p> 


</p>How is carpal tunnel syndrome diagnosed?<p>To find out if you have carpal tunnel syndrome, your health care provider will:</p><ul><li>Ask about your medical history</li><li>Ask about your family health history</li><li>Do a physical exam</li><li>Order tests if needed, such as:
	<ul><li>Lab tests to look for diseases that may damage nerves</li><li>X-rays to look for broken bones or <span class="qt0">arthritis</span></li><li>Tests to measure the electrical activity in your nerves and muscles</li><li>Ultrasound to check the nerve in the carpal tunnel</li></ul></li></ul>What are the treatments for carpal tunnel syndrome?<p>It's easier to treat carpal tunnel syndrome early on, so you should start treatment as soon as possible. Your provider will first treat any health conditions you have that may cause your symptoms. The next step would be treatments for carpal tunnel syndrome. They may include:</p><ul><li>Wrist splints (usually worn at night).</li><li>Rest.</li><li>Medicines to reduce swelling and pain:</li><ul><li>Over-the-counter pain relievers, such as aspirin, ibuprofen, and naproxen sodium.</li><li>Prescriptionsteroids, either pills or an injection (shot) into your wrist.</li></ul><li>Other therapies (complementary and integrative medicine). Research shows that yoga may help with pain and weakness. Some people may find acupuncture and chiropractic care helpful, but research hasn't shown that these therapies can improve carpal tunnel syndrome. If you want to try other therapies, talk with your provider first.</li><li>Surgery to make more space in the carpal tunnel. Surgery may be an option if your symptoms are severe and other treatments don't work. It involves cutting the wrist ligament to take pressure off the nerve.  Recovery may take months. Carpal tunnel syndrome rarely comes back after surgery, but mild symptoms may last.</li></ul>Can carpal tunnel syndrome be prevented?<p>You may help prevent carpal tunnel syndrome if you protect your wrists:</p><ul><li>Use good posture to keep your wrists in a natural position.</li><li>Keep your hands warm to keep your muscles flexible. Fingerless gloves may help.</li><li>Take work breaks and vary your tasks.</li><li>Stretch and exercise your hands and wrists.</li><li>Organize your workspace and tools to reduce strain on your hands and wrists.</li></ul></content>
      <content name="mesh">Carpal Tunnel Syndrome</content>

    <document rank="18" url="https://medlineplus.gov/cartilagedisorders.html">
      <content name="title">Cartilage Disorders</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Costochondritis</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Polychondritis</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>Cartilage is the tough but flexible tissue that covers the ends of your bones at a joint. It also gives shape and support to other parts of your body, such as your ears, nose and windpipe. Healthy cartilage helps you move by allowing your bones to glide over each other. It also protects bones by preventing them from rubbing against each other. </p><p>Injured, inflamed, or damaged cartilage can cause symptoms such as pain and limited movement. It can also lead to joint damage and deformity. Causes of cartilage problems include :</p><ul><li>	Tears and injuries, such as sports injuries</li><li>	Genetic factors </li><li>	Other disorders, such as some types of <span class="qt0">arthritis</span></li></ul><p><span class="qt0">Osteoarthritis</span> results from breakdown of cartilage.</p><p>NIH: National Institute of <span class="qt0">Arthritis</span> and Musculoskeletal and Skin Diseases</p></content>
      <content name="mesh">Cartilage Diseases</content>
      <content name="groupName">Bones, Joints and Muscles</content>
      <content name="snippet"> ... factors   	Other disorders, such as some types of <span class="qt0">arthritis  Osteoarthritis</span> results from breakdown of cartilage. NIH: National Institute of <span class="qt0">Arthritis</span> and Musculoskeletal and Skin Diseases</content>
    </document>
    <document rank="19" url="https://medlineplus.gov/chlamydiainfections.html">
      <content name="title">Chlamydia Infections</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is chlamydia?<p>Chlamydia is a common sexually transmitted infection (STI). It is caused by bacteria called Chlamydia trachomatis. Anyone can get chlamydia. It often doesn't cause symptoms, so people may not know that they have it. Antibiotics can cure it. But if it's not treated, chlamydia can cause serious health problems. </p>How is chlamydia spread?<p>You can get chlamydia during oral, vaginal, or anal sex with someone who has chlamydia. Chlamydia can also be passed to the baby during  childbirth.</p><p>If you've had chlamydia and were treated in the past, you can get re-infected if you have unprotected sex with someone who has it.</p>Who is more likely to get chlamydia?<p>Chlamydia is more common in young people, especially young women. You are more likely to get infected with chlamydia if you don't consistently use a condom or if you have multiple partners.</p>What are the symptoms of chlamydia?<p>Chlamydia doesn't usually cause any symptoms. So you may not realize that you have it. But even if you don't have symptoms, you can still pass the infection to others.</p><p>If you do have symptoms, they may not appear until several weeks after you have sex with someone who has chlamydia.</p><p>Symptoms in women include:</p><ul><li>Abnormal vaginal discharge, which may have a strong smell</li><li>A burning sensation when urinating</li></ul><p>If the infection spreads, you might get lower abdominal (belly) pain, pain during sex, nausea, and fever.</p><p>Symptoms in men include:</p><ul><li>Discharge from your penis</li><li>A burning sensation when urinating (peeing)</li><li>Pain and swelling in one or both testicles (although this is less common)</li></ul><p>If the chlamydia infects the rectum (in men or women), it can cause rectal pain, discharge, and bleeding.</p>How is chlamydia diagnosed?<p>There are lab tests to diagnose chlamydia. Your health care provider may ask you to provide a urine sample. Or your provider may use (or ask you to use) a cotton swab to get a sample from your vagina to test for chlamydia.</p>Who should be tested for chlamydia?<p>You should go to your provider for a test if you have symptoms of chlamydia or if you have a partner who has an STI. If you are pregnant, you should get a test when you go to your first prenatal visit.</p><p>People at higher risk should get checked for chlamydia every year:</p><ul><li>Sexually active women 25 and younger</li><li>Older women who have new or multiple sex partners or a sex partner who has an STI</li><li>Men who have sex with men (MSM)</li></ul>What other problems can chlamydia cause?<p>In women, an untreated infection can spread to your uterus and fallopian tubes, causing pelvic inflammatory disease (PID). PID can cause permanent damage to your reproductive system. This can lead to long-term pelvic pain, infertility, and ectopic pregnancy. Women who have had chlamydia infections more than once are at higher risk of serious reproductive health complications.</p><p>Men often don't have health problems from chlamydia. Sometimes it can infect the epididymis (the tube that carries sperm). This can cause pain, fever, and, rarely, infertility.</p><p>Both men and women can develop reactive <span class="qt0">arthritis</span> because of a chlamydia infection. Reactive <span class="qt0">arthritis</span> is a type of <span class="qt0">arthritis</span> that happens as a "reaction" to an infection in the body.</p><p>Babies born to infected mothers can get eye infections and pneumonia from chlamydia. It may also make it more likely for your baby to be born too early.</p><p>Untreated chlamydia may also increase your chances of getting or giving HIV.</p>What are the treatments for chlamydia?<p>Antibiotics will cure the infection. You may get a one-time dose of the antibiotics, or you may need to take medicine every day for 7 days. It is important to take all the medicine that your provider prescribed for you. Antibiotics cannot repair any permanent damage that the disease has caused.</p><p>To prevent spreading the disease to your partner, you should not have sex until the infection has cleared up. If you got a one-time dose of antibiotics, you should wait 7 days after taking the medicine to have sex again. If you have to take medicine every day for 7 days, you should not have sex again until you have finished taking all of the doses of your medicine.</p><p>It is common to get a repeat infection, so you need to get tested again about three months after treatment.</p>Can chlamydia be prevented?<p>The only sure way to prevent chlamydia is to not have vaginal, anal, or oral sex.</p><p>Correct usage of latex condoms greatly reduces, but does not eliminate, the risk of catching or spreading chlamydia. If your or your partner is allergic to latex, you can use polyurethane condoms.</p><p>Centers for Disease Control and Prevention</p></content>

    <document rank="20" url="https://medlineplus.gov/handinjuriesanddisorders.html">
      <content name="title">Hand Injuries and Disorders</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>No matter how old you are or what you do for a living, you are always using your hands. 
When there is something wrong with them, you may not be able to do your regular activities. 
</p><p>Hand problems include  :</p><ul><li>Carpal tunnel syndrome - compression of a nerve as it 
goes through the wrist, often making your fingers feel numb </li><li>Injuries that result in fractures (broken bones), ruptured ligaments 
and dislocations </li><li><span class="qt0">Osteoarthritis</span> - wear-and-tear <span class="qt0">arthritis</span>, which can 
also cause deformity    </li><li>Tendinitis - irritation of the tendons    </li><li>Disorders and injuries of your fingers and thumb  </li></ul></content>

    <document rank="21" url="https://medlineplus.gov/lupus.html">
      <content name="title">Lupus</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Cutaneous Lupus</content>
      <content name="altTitle">SLE</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Subacute Cutaneous Lupus</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Systemic Lupus Erythematosus</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Discoid lupus</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is lupus?<p>Lupus is a chronic (long-lasting) type of autoimmune disease.Autoimmune diseases happen when your immune system attacks healthy cells and tissues by mistake. This attack causes inflammation. It can also damage many parts of the body, including the joints, skin, kidneys, heart, lungs, and brain.</p><p>There are several types of lupus:</p><ul><li>Systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) is the most common type. It can be mild or severe and can affect many parts of the body.</li><li>Cutaneous lupus affects the skin. It causes a rash or sores, usually after your skin is exposed to sunlight. The two major types of cutaneous lupus are discoid lupus and subacute cutaneous lupus.</li><li>Drug-induced lupus is caused by a reaction to some medicines. The symptoms may start 3 to 6 months after starting the medicine. The symptoms usually go away when you stop taking the medicine.</li><li>Neonatal lupus, which is rare, affects newborns. It is caused by certain antibodies that are passed from the pregnant parent to the baby.</li></ul>What causes lupus?<p>The cause of lupus is unknown. Researchers are studying what might cause or trigger the disease, such as:</p><ul><li>Genetics</li><li>Environmental factors, such as viral infections, sunlight, certain medicines, and smoking</li><li>Problems with the immune system</li></ul>Who is more likely to get lupus?<p>Anyone can get lupus, but women get it much more often than men.</p><p>Lupus is more common in African Americans than in White people. It is also more common in people of American Indian and Asian descent.  African American and Hispanic women are more likely to have severe forms of lupus.</p>What are the symptoms of lupus?<p>Lupus can have many symptoms, and they differ from person to person. Some of the more common ones are:</p><ul><li><span class="qt0">Arthritis</span>, causing painful and swollen joints and morning stiffness</li><li>Fever</li><li>Fatigue or feeling tired often</li><li>Red rashes, most often on the face (also called the "butterfly rash")</li><li>Chest pain when taking a deep breath</li><li>Hair loss</li><li>Pale or purple fingers or toes, from cold and stress (Raynaud phenomenon) </li><li>Sensitivity to the sun</li><li>Swelling in legs or around eyes</li><li>Mouth ulcers</li><li>Swollen glands</li><li>Headache and dizziness</li><li>Confusion and memory problems</li></ul><p>Symptoms may come and go. When you are having symptoms, it is called a flare.  Flares can range from mild to severe. New symptoms may appear at any time.</p>What other problems can lupus cause?<p>Lupus causes inflammation throughout your body. This can cause problems in your organs, including:</p><ul><li>Kidney damage (lupus nephritis).</li><li>Heart problems, including inflammation in the heart (myocarditis), heart valves, or lining of the heart muscle (pericarditis).</li><li>Inflammation of blood vessels (vasculitis).</li><li>Blood clots</li><li>Inflammation of the tissue that surrounds the lungs (pleurisy). This can make it painful to breathe.</li></ul><p>Some people with lupus may be more likely to develop other conditions, such as coronary artery disease (CAD) and atherosclerosis.</p>How is lupus diagnosed?<p>There is no specific test for lupus, and it's often mistaken for other diseases that cause similar symptoms. So it may take a while to get a diagnosis. To find out if you have lupus, your health care provider: </p><ul><li>Will ask about your symptoms, medical history, and family health history</li><li>Will do a complete physical exam</li><li>May order blood tests, such as ANA (antinuclear antibody), antibodies, complete blood count, and complement  tests</li><li>May order other tests, such as urine tests</li><li>May do biopsies:
  <ul><li>Skin biopsy (looking at skin samples under a microscope)</li><li>Kidney biopsy (looking at tissue from your kidney under a microscope)</li></ul>
</li></ul>What are the treatments for lupus?<p>There is no cure for lupus, but medicines and lifestyle changes can help control it.</p><p>People with lupus often need to see different providers. You will most likely have a primary care provider and a rheumatologist (a doctor who specializes in diseases of the joints and muscles). Which other specialists you will depend on how lupus affects your body. For example, if lupus is damaging your heart or blood vessels, you would see a cardiologist (a doctor who specializes in heart diseases).</p><p>Your primary care provider should coordinate care between all of your other providers and treat other problems as they come up. You and your primary care provider will develop a treatment plan to fit your needs. You will both review the plan often to make sure that it is working. You should report new symptoms to your provider right away so that your treatment plan can be changed, if needed.</p><p></p><p>The goals of a treatment plan are to:</p><ul><li>Prevent flares</li><li>Treat flares when they occur</li><li>Reduce organ damage and other problems</li><li>Improve your quality of life</li></ul><p>Treatments may include drugs to:</p><ul><li>Reduce fever, swelling, and pain</li><li>Reduce inflammation in your body</li><li>Prevent or reduce flares</li><li>Reduce or prevent damage to joints</li><li>Suppress (lower) the activity of your immune system</li></ul><p>Besides taking medicines for lupus, you may need to take medicines for problems that are related to lupus such as high cholesterol, high blood pressure, or infection.</p><p>Alternative and complementary therapies are therapies that are not part of standard treatments. Some people try alternative and complementary therapies to improve their lupus symptoms. But research has not clearly shown whether these treatments may help or treat lupus. Talk to your provider before trying any new treatments.</p>How can I cope with lupus?<p>It is important to take an active role in your treatment. It helps to learn more about lupus - being able to spot the warning signs of a flare can help you prevent the flare or make the symptoms less severe.</p><p>It is also important to find ways to cope with the stress of having lupus. Exercising and finding ways to relax may make it easier for you to cope. A good support system can also help.</p><p>NIH: National Institute of <span class="qt0">Arthritis</span> and Musculoskeletal and Skin Diseases</p></content>
      <content name="mesh">Lupus Erythematosus, Systemic</content>
      <content name="mesh">Lupus Erythematosus, Discoid</content>
      <content name="groupName">Immune System</content>
      <content name="snippet"> ... person. Some of the more common ones are: <span class="qt0">Arthritis</span>, causing painful and swollen joints and morning stiffness   ... system can also help. NIH: National Institute of <span class="qt0">Arthritis</span> and Musculoskeletal and Skin Diseases</content>

    <document rank="22" url="https://medlineplus.gov/osteonecrosis.html">
      <content name="title">Osteonecrosis</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Aseptic Necrosis</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Avascular Necrosis</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Legg-Calve-Perthes Disease</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Ischemic necrosis</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>Osteonecrosis is a disease caused by reduced blood flow to bones in the joints. In people with healthy bones, new bone is always replacing old bone. In osteonecrosis, the lack of blood causes the bone to break down faster than the body can make enough new bone. The bone starts to die and may break down.</p><p>You can have osteonecrosis in one or several bones. It is most common in the upper leg. Other common sites are your upper arm and your knees, shoulders and ankles. The disease can affect men and women of any age, but it usually strikes in your thirties, forties or fifties. </p><p>At first, you might not have any symptoms. As the disease gets worse, you will probably have joint pain that becomes more severe. You may not be able to bend or move the affected joint very well. </p><p>No one is sure what causes the disease. Risk factors include :</p><ul><li>	Long-term steroid treatment</li><li>	Alcohol misuse</li><li>	Joint injuries</li><li>	Having certain diseases, including <span class="qt0">arthritis</span> and cancer  </li></ul><p>Doctors use imaging tests and other tests to diagnose osteonecrosis. Treatments include medicines, using crutches, limiting activities that put weight on the affected joints, electrical stimulation and surgery.</p><p>NIH: National Institute of <span class="qt0">Arthritis</span> and Musculoskeletal and Skin Diseases</p></content>
      <content name="mesh">Osteonecrosis</content>
      <content name="groupName">Bones, Joints and Muscles</content>
      <content name="snippet"> ... Alcohol misuse  	Joint injuries  	Having certain diseases, including <span class="qt0">arthritis</span> and cancer    Doctors use imaging tests and other  ... electrical stimulation and surgery. NIH: National Institute of <span class="qt0">Arthritis</span> and Musculoskeletal and Skin Diseases</content>
