    <document rank="23" url="https://medlineplus.gov/osteoporosis.html">
      <content name="title">Osteoporosis</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Bone Loss</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is osteoporosis?<p>Osteoporosis is a disease in which your bones become weak and are likely to fracture (break). The disease can develop when your bone mineral density and bone mass decrease. It can also happen if the structure and strength of your bones change.</p><p>Osteoporosis is called a "silent" disease because it doesn't usually cause symptoms. You may not even know you have the disease until you break a bone. This could happen with any bone, but it's most common in the bones of your hip, vertebrae in the spine, and wrist.</p>What causes osteoporosis?<p>Your bones are made of living tissue. To keep them strong, your body breaks down old bone and replaces it with new bone. Osteoporosis develops when more bone is broken down than replaced. You lose bone mass and changes happen in the structure of your bone tissue. This can happen as you get older. Other risk factors can also lead to the development of osteoporosis or increase your chance of developing the disease.</p>Who is more likely to develop osteoporosis?<p>Anyone can develop osteoporosis, but you are more likely to develop it if you have one or more risk factors:</p><ul><li>Your sex. Osteoporosis is more common in women, especially after menopause (postmenopausal).</li><li>Your age. Your risk increases as you get older. It is most common in people over age 50.</li><li>Your body size. It is more common in people who are slim and thin boned.</li><li>Your race:<ul><li>White and Asian women are at highest risk.</li><li>African American and Mexican American women have a lower risk.</li><li>White men are at higher risk than African American and Mexican American men.</li></ul></li><li>Family history. Your risk of osteoporosis may be higher if one of your parents has osteoporosis or broke their hip.</li><li>Changes to hormones. Low levels of certain hormones can increase your chance of developing osteoporosis.</li><li>Diet. A diet that is low in calcium and/or vitamin D or does not include enough protein can raise your risk.</li><li>Long-term use of certain medicines, such as:
	<ul><li>Corticosteroids</li><li>Proton pump inhibitors (which treat GERD)</li><li>Medicines to treat epilepsy</li></ul>
</li><li>Having other medical conditions, such as:
	<ul><li>Endocrine diseases</li><li>Certain digestive diseases</li><li>Rheumatoid <span class="qt0">arthritis</span></li><li>Certain types of cancer</li><li>HIV</li><li>Anorexia nervosa, a type of eating disorder</li></ul>
</li><li>Your lifestyle. Certain lifestyle factors can contribute to bone loss, such as:
	<ul><li>Smoking tobacco</li><li>Long-term heavy alcohol use</li><li>Physical inactivity or prolonged periods of bedrest</li></ul>
</li></ul>What are the symptoms of osteoporosis?<p>Osteoporosis usually doesn't cause symptoms. You may not know that you have it until you break a bone.</p>How is osteoporosis diagnosed?<p>Health care providers often diagnose osteoporosis during routine screening for the disease. The U.S. Preventive Services Task Force recommends screening for:</p><ul><li>Women age 65 and older</li><li>Postmenopausal women under age 65 who have factors that increase the chance of developing osteoporosis</li></ul><p>For men, it isn't clear yet whether regular screening is helpful. More research is needed to know for sure.</p><p>To find out if you have osteoporosis, your provider:</p><ul><li>Will ask about your medical history and whether you have ever broken a bone</li><li>May do a physical exam, which could include checking for:
	<ul><li>A loss of height and/or weight</li><li>Changes in your posture</li><li>Balance and gait (the way you walk)</li><li>Your muscle strength</li></ul>
</li><li>Will likely order a bone density scan</li><li>May do a fracture risk assessment, which is a short questionnaire that helps estimate your risk of breaking a bone in the next 10 years
</li><li>Will likely order a bone density scan</li><li>May do a fracture risk assessment, which is a short questionnaire that helps estimate your risk of breaking a bone in the next 10 years</li></ul>What are the treatments for osteoporosis?<p>The goals for treating osteoporosis are to slow or stop bone loss and to prevent fractures. Your provider may recommend:</p><ul><li>A healthy, balanced diet that includes enough calcium, vitamin D, and protein</li><li>Lifestyle changes such as quitting smoking and limiting alcohol</li><li>Regular physical activity including weight-bearing exercise (like walking), strength training, and balance exercises</li><li>Fall prevention to help prevent fractures</li><li>Medicines, such as:
	<ul><li>Medicines that slow down bone loss</li><li>Medicines that help rebuild bone</li></ul>
</li></ul><p>In addition to managing your osteoporosis, it's important to avoid activities that may cause a fracture. These can include movements that involve:</p><ul><li>Twisting your spine, like swinging a golf club</li><li>Bending forward from the waist, like sit ups and toe touches</li></ul><p>You can also help reduce the risk of breaking a bone by preventing falls.</p>Can osteoporosis be prevented?<p>To help keep bones strong and help prevent osteoporosis, the best thing to do is to eat a healthy diet rich in calcium and vitamin D. Getting regular physical activity, limiting alcohol, and not smoking can also help.</p><p>NIH: National Institute of <span class="qt0">Arthritis</span> and Musculoskeletal and Skin Diseases</p></content>
      <content name="mesh">Osteoporosis</content>
      <content name="mesh">Osteoporotic Fractures</content>

      <content name="groupName">Older Adults</content>

	 Endocrine diseases  Certain digestive diseases  Rheumatoid <span class="qt0">arthritis</span>  Certain types of cancer  HIV  Anorexia nervosa, a  ... smoking can also help. NIH: National Institute of <span class="qt0">Arthritis</span> and Musculoskeletal and Skin Diseases</content>

    <document rank="24" url="https://medlineplus.gov/raynaudphenomenon.html">
      <content name="title">Raynaud Phenomenon</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Raynaud's Disease</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Raynaud's Syndrome</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is Raynaud phenomenon? <p>Raynaud phenomenon is a condition that affects your blood vessels (the tubes that your blood flows through). It causes your blood vessels to narrow, which decreases blood flow. This is called a Raynaud episode or "attack." The attacks usually affect your fingers and toes, causing them to become cold and numb. They may also change color, usually to white or blue. These attacks happen in response to cold temperatures or stress.</p><p>Raynaud phenomenon may also be called Raynaud disease or Raynaud syndrome.</p>What are the types of Raynaud phenomenon?<p>There are two types of Raynaud phenomenon:</p><ul><li>Primary Raynaud phenomenon is the more common type. Its cause is unknown.</li><li>Secondary Raynaud phenomenon is usually caused by another disease or problem, such as lupus or scleroderma. Other causes may be exposure to cold or certain chemicals. The type can be more serious than the primary type.</li></ul>What causes Raynaud phenomenon?<p>Researchers don't know exactly why some people develop Raynaud phenomenon. But they do understand how the attacks happen. When you are exposed to cold, your body tries to slow the loss of heat and maintain its temperature. To do this, the blood vessels in the top layer of your skin constrict (narrow). This moves blood from those vessels near the surface to vessels deeper in the body. But when you have Raynaud phenomenon, the blood vessels in your hands and feet react to cold or stress by narrowing quickly. They also stay narrowed for a long time.</p>Who is more likely to develop Raynaud phenomenon? <p>Anyone can develop Raynaud phenomenon, but some people are more likely to develop it:</p><ul><li>Primary Raynaud phenomenon has been linked to:
  <ul><li>Your sex. Women get it more often than men.</li><li>Your age. It usually develops in people younger than age 30. It often starts in the teenage years.</li><li>A family history of Raynaud phenomenon. You are more likely to develop Raynaud phenomenon if you have a family member who has it.</li></ul>
</li><li>Secondary Raynaud phenomenon has been linked to:
  <ul><li>Certain diseases. These include lupus, scleroderma, rheumatoid <span class="qt0">arthritis</span> (RA), carpal tunnel syndrome, and connective tissue disorders.</li><li>Certain medicines. Medicines that treat high blood pressure, migraines, and attention deficit hyperactivity disorder (ADHD) may cause similar symptoms to Raynaud phenomenon or make your symptoms worse.</li><li>Work-related exposures, such as repeated use of vibrating machinery (such as a jackhammer), or exposure to cold or certain chemicals.</li></ul>
</li></ul>What are the symptoms of Raynaud phenomenon?<p>Raynaud attacks most often happen when you get cold, for example when you grab something cold from the freezer or go into an air-conditioned building on a warm day. Attacks usually affect your fingers and toes. But sometimes they can affect other parts of your body, such as your ears, nose, lips, or nipples.</p><p>An attack causes the skin to become cold and numb. Your skin may also turn white or blue due to a lack of oxygen. As the blood flow returns, your skin may tingle, throb, or turn red. An attack may last a few minutes or a few hours. If you have darker skin, you may not be able to easily see the skin color changes.</p><p>For many people, especially those with the primary type, the symptoms are mild. People with the secondary type often have more severe symptoms. They may develop skin ulcers (open sores caused by poor blood flow) or skin infections.</p>How is Raynaud phenomenon diagnosed?<p>There is no specific test to diagnose Raynaud phenomenon. To find out if you have it, your health care provider:</p><ul><li>Will take your medical history and ask about your symptoms.</li><li>Will do a physical exam.</li><li>May order blood and other lab tests to check for other conditions which could be causing your symptoms and/or to help decide which type of Raynaud phenomenon you have.</li></ul>What are the treatments for Raynaud phenomenon?<p>Most people with Raynaud phenomenon can keep their symptoms under control by avoiding getting cold. But if this is not enough, medicines and, in some cases, surgical procedures can help.</p><p>Secondary Raynaud phenomenon is more likely to be serious and to need more treatments. It's also important to treat the condition or problem that is causing your Raynaud phenomenon. </p><p>You may need to see a specialist such as a rheumatologist, a doctor who treats diseases of the joints, muscles, and bones.</p>Can Raynaud phenomenon be prevented?<p>Raynaud phenomenon cannot be prevented, but you can help prevent attacks and manage your symptoms by:</p><ul><li>Placing your hands or feet in a warm place when you have an attack. This could mean putting them under warm (not hot) water or under a heating pad.</li><li>Keeping your body, especially your hands and feet, warm in cold weather.</li><li>Avoiding triggers, such as certain medicines and stress.</li><li>Quitting smoking (or not starting smoking).</li><li>Managing stress.</li></ul><p>NIH: National Institute of <span class="qt0">Arthritis</span> and Musculoskeletal and Skin Diseases</p></content>
      <content name="mesh">Raynaud Disease</content>
      <content name="groupName">Blood, Heart and Circulation</content>
      <content name="groupName">Symptoms</content>
      <content name="snippet"> ... to:
   Certain diseases. These include lupus, scleroderma, rheumatoid <span class="qt0">arthritis</span> (RA), carpal tunnel syndrome, and connective tissue disorders.   ... starting smoking).  Managing stress.  NIH: National Institute of <span class="qt0">Arthritis</span> and Musculoskeletal and Skin Diseases</content>

    <document rank="0" url="https://medlineplus.gov/heartvalvediseases.html">
      <content name="title"><span class="qt0">Heart</span> Valve <span class="qt1">Diseases</span></content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Aortic Stenosis</content>
      <content name="altTitle"><span class="qt0">Heart</span> Murmur</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Valvular <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">Heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">Diseases</span></span></content>
      <content name="altTitle">Valvular <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span></content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What are <span class="qt0">heart</span> valve <span class="qt1">diseases</span>?<p><span class="qt0">Heart</span> valve <span class="qt1">disease</span> happens when one or more of your <span class="qt0">heart</span> valves don't work well.</p><p>Your <span class="qt0">heart</span> has four valves: the tricuspid, pulmonary, mitral, and aortic valves. The valves have flaps that open and close. The flaps make sure that blood flows in the right direction through your <span class="qt0">heart</span> and to the rest of your body. When your <span class="qt0">heart</span> beats, the flaps open to let blood through. Between heartbeats they close to stop the blood from flowing backwards.</p><p>If one or more of your <span class="qt0">heart</span> valves doesn't open or close correctly, it can affect your blood flow and strain your <span class="qt0">heart</span>. Fortunately, treatment helps most valve <span class="qt1">diseases</span>.</p>What are the types of <span class="qt0">heart</span> valve <span class="qt1">diseases</span>?<p><span class="qt0">Heart</span> valves can have three basic kinds of problems:</p><ul><li>Regurgitation, or backflow, happens when the flaps of a valve don't close tightly. This allows the blood to leak backwards. A common cause of regurgitation is prolapse, where the flaps of the valve flop or bulge back. Prolapse most often affects the mitral valve.</li><li>Stenosis happens when the flaps of a valve become thick, stiff, or stuck together. This prevents the <span class="qt0">heart</span> valve from opening all the way. Not enough blood can pass through the valve. Aortic valve stenosis is a common type of stenosis. It affects the valve that controls blood flow into the large artery that carries blood out of the <span class="qt0">heart</span> to the body.</li><li>Atresia happens when a <span class="qt0">heart</span> valve did not form properly and does not have an opening for blood to pass through.</li></ul><p>Sometimes a valve can have both regurgitation and stenosis.</p>What causes <span class="qt0">heart</span> valve <span class="qt1">diseases</span>?<p>Some people are born with <span class="qt0">heart</span> valve <span class="qt1">disease</span>. This is called congenital <span class="qt0">heart</span> valve <span class="qt1">disease</span>. It can happen alone or along with other congenital <span class="qt0">heart</span> defects. <span class="qt0">Heart</span> valve <span class="qt1">disease</span> can also develop over time as you get older or have certain conditions that affect the <span class="qt0">heart</span>.</p>Who is more likely to develop <span class="qt0">heart</span> valve <span class="qt1">diseases</span>?<p>Your chance of having <span class="qt0">heart</span> valve <span class="qt1">disease</span> is higher if:</p><ul><li>You are older. With age, the <span class="qt0">heart</span> valves can become thick and stiff.</li><li>You have or have had other conditions that affect your <span class="qt0">heart</span> and blood vessels. These include:
	<ul><li>Rheumatic fever. An untreated strep throat can become rheumatic fever, which can harm the <span class="qt0">heart</span> valves. The damage may not show up for years. Today, most people take antibiotics to cure strep throat before it can cause <span class="qt0">heart</span> valve damage.</li><li>Endocarditis. This is a rare infection in the lining of the <span class="qt0">heart and heart</span> valves. It is usually caused by bacteria in the bloodstream.</li><li>A <span class="qt0">heart</span> attack.</li><li><span class="qt0">Heart</span> failure.</li><li><span class="qt0">Coronary</span> artery <span class="qt1">disease</span>, especially when it affects the aorta (the large artery that carries blood from the <span class="qt0">heart</span> to the body).</li><li>High blood pressure.</li><li>High blood cholesterol.</li><li>Diabetes.</li><li>Obesity and overweight.</li><li>Lack of physical activity.</li></ul>
</li><li>A family history of early <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span>:<ul><li>A father or brother who had <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span> younger than 55.</li><li>A mother or sister who had <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span> younger than 65.</li></ul></li><li>You were born with an aortic valve that wasn't formed right. Sometimes this will cause problems right away. Other times, the valve may work well enough for years before causing problems.</li></ul>What are the symptoms of <span class="qt0">heart</span> valve <span class="qt1">diseases</span>?<p>Many people live their whole lives with a <span class="qt0">heart</span> valve that doesn't work perfectly and never have any problems. But <span class="qt0">heart</span> valve <span class="qt1">disease</span> may get worse slowly over time. You may develop signs and symptoms, such as:</p><ul><li>Shortness of breath (feeling like you can't get enough air)</li><li>Fatigue</li><li>Swelling in your feet, ankles, abdomen (belly), or the veins in your neck</li><li>Chest pain when you're physically active</li><li>Arrhythmia, a problem with the rate or rhythm of your heartbeat</li><li>Dizziness or fainting</li></ul><p>If you don't get treatment for <span class="qt0">heart</span> valve <span class="qt1">disease</span>, the symptoms and strain on your <span class="qt0">heart</span> may keep getting worse.</p>What other problems can <span class="qt0">heart</span> valve <span class="qt1">diseases</span> cause?<p>When the valves don't work well, your <span class="qt0">heart</span> has to pump harder to get enough blood out to the body.  Without treatment, this extra workload on your <span class="qt0">heart</span> can lead to:</p><ul><li><span class="qt0">Heart</span> failure</li><li>Stroke</li><li>Blood clots</li><li>Sudden <span class="qt0">cardiac arrest</span> or death</li></ul>How is <span class="qt0">heart</span> valve <span class="qt1">disease</span> diagnosed?<p>Your health care provider may listen to your <span class="qt0">heart</span> with a stethoscope and hear that your <span class="qt0">heart</span> makes abnormal sounds, such as a click or a <span class="qt0">heart</span> murmur. These sounds may mean a valve isn't working normally. The provider will usually refer you to a cardiologist, a doctor who specializes in <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">diseases</span></span>.</p><p>The doctor will also listen to your <span class="qt0">heart</span> and will do a physical exam. You will also likely need to have one or more <span class="qt0">heart</span> tests.</p>What are the treatments for <span class="qt0">heart</span> valve <span class="qt1">diseases</span>?<p>Most <span class="qt0">heart</span> valve problems can be treated successfully. Treatment may include:</p><ul><li>Medicines to control your symptoms and keep your <span class="qt0">heart</span> pumping well</li><li><span class="qt0">Heart</span>-healthy lifestyle changes to treat other related <span class="qt0">heart</span> conditions</li><li>Surgery to repair or replace a valve</li></ul><p>It's possible that you may need surgery, even if you don't have symptoms. Fixing the valve can help prevent future <span class="qt0">heart</span> problems.</p><p>There are many ways to do <span class="qt0">heart</span> valve surgery. You and your doctor can decide what's best for you, based on your valve problem and general health. <span class="qt0">Heart</span> valve repair surgery has fewer risks than <span class="qt0">heart</span> valve replacement. So, when repair is possible, it's preferred over valve replacement.</p><p>In some cases, valve replacement is necessary. There are 2 types of replacement valves:</p><ul><li>Biologic valves made from pig, cow, or human tissue. These valves tend to wear out after 10 to 15 years, but some may last longer.</li><li>Mechanical (human-made) valves usually don't wear out. But with a mechanical valve, you usually have to take blood thinners for the rest of your life to prevent blood clots. And your risk of endocarditis (a <span class="qt0">heart</span> infection) is higher than with a biologic valve.</li></ul><p>NIH: National <span class="qt0">Heart</span>, Lung, and Blood Institute</p></content>

Your <span class="qt0">heart</span> has four valves: the tricuspid,  ... </content>

    <document rank="1" url="https://medlineplus.gov/howtopreventheartdisease.html">
      <content name="title">How to Prevent <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">Heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">Disease</span></span></content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle"><span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">Heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">Disease</span></span> Prevention</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p><span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">Heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span> is the leading cause of the death in the United States. It is also a major cause of disability. But you can take steps to improve your health and help prevent <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span>. The first step is understanding your risk of <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span>. Your risk depends on many factors, some of which are changeable and others that are not. Learning about them and working on the things that you can change can lower your risk of <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span>.</p>What are the <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span> risk factors that I cannot change?<p>There are some risk factors for <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span> that you cannot change:</p><ul><li>Your age. Your risk of <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span> increases as you get older. The risk is higher in:
  <ul><li>Men age 45 and older</li><li>Women age 55 and older</li></ul>
</li><li>Your sex. Some risk factors may affect <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span> risk differently in women than in men. For example: 
  <ul><li>The hormone estrogen provides women some protection against <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span></li><li>Diabetes raises the risk of <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span> more in women than in men.</li></ul>
</li><li>Your race or ethnicity. Certain groups have higher risks than others. For example:
  <ul><li>African Americans are more likely than Whites to die of <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span>, while Hispanic Americans are less likely to die of it</li><li>Asian Americans as a group have lower rates of <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span> than other groups, but South Asian Americans have higher rates</li></ul>
</li><li>Your family history. You have a greater risk if you have a close family member who had <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span> at an early age, for example if:
  <ul><li>Your father or brother was diagnosed before age 55</li><li>Your mother or sister was diagnosed before age 65</li></ul>
</li></ul>What are the <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span> risk factors I can change and what can I do to lower my risk?<p>There are many <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span> risk factors that you can change.  If you make these changes, you don't just help protect your <span class="qt0">heart</span>. You can also improve your overall health and well-being.</p><p>You may have a lot of changes to make. If you need to, you can make the changes gradually, one at a time. What's most important is that you make them. Depending on your lifestyle, these changes could include:</p><ul><li>Controlling your blood pressure.High blood pressure is a major risk factor for <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span>. It is important to get your blood pressure checked regularly - at least once a year for most adults, and more often if you have high blood pressure. You can also take steps, including lifestyle changes, to prevent or control high blood pressure.</li><li>Keeping your cholesterol and triglyceride levels under control:<ul><li>High levels of cholesterol can clog your arteries and raise your risk of <span class="qt0">coronary</span> artery <span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span><span class="qt2"> and </span><span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span> attack. Lifestyle changes and medicines (if needed) can lower your cholesterol.</li><li>Triglycerides are another type of fat in the blood. High  levels of triglyceride may also raise the risk of <span class="qt0">coronary</span> artery <span class="qt1">disease</span>, especially in women.</li></ul></li><li>Staying at a healthy weight. Being overweight or having obesity can increase your risk of <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span>. This is mostly because they are linked to other <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span> risk factors, including high blood cholesterol and triglyceride levels, high blood pressure, and diabetes. Controlling your weight can lower these risks.</li><li>Eating a healthy diet. Try to limit saturated fats, foods high in sodium (salt), and added sugars. Instead, eat plenty of fresh fruit, vegetables, and whole grains. The DASH diet is an example of an eating plan that can help you to lower your blood pressure and cholesterol, two things that can lower your risk of <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span>.</li><li>Getting regular physical activity. Regular physical activity has many benefits, including strengthening your <span class="qt0">heart</span> and improving your circulation. It can also help you maintain a healthy weight and lower cholesterol and blood pressure. All of these can lower your risk of <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span>.</li><li>Limiting alcohol. Drinking too much alcohol can raise your blood pressure. It also adds extra calories, which may cause weight gain. Both of those raise your risk of <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span>. It's best not to drink, but if you do:
  <ul><li>Have no more than 2 drinks per day if you are a man.</li><li>Have no more than 1 drink per day if you are a woman.</li></ul>
</li><li>Not smoking. Cigarette smoking raises your blood pressure and puts you at higher risk of <span class="qt0">heart</span> attack and stroke. 
  <ul><li>If you do not smoke, don't start.</li><li> If you do smoke, quitting will lower your risk of <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span>. You can talk with your health care provider for help in finding the best way for you to quit.</li></ul>
</li><li>Managing stress.Stress is linked to <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span> in many ways. It can raise your blood pressure. Extreme stress can be a "trigger" for a <span class="qt0">heart</span> attack. Also, some common ways of coping with stress, such as overeating, heavy drinking, and smoking, are bad for your <span class="qt0">heart</span>. Some ways to help manage your stress include exercise, listening to music, focusing on something calm or peaceful, and meditating.</li><li>Managing diabetes. Having diabetes doubles your risk of diabetic <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span>. That is because over time, high blood glucose (blood sugar) from diabetes can damage your blood vessels and the nerves that control your <span class="qt0">heart</span> and blood vessels. So, it is important to get tested for diabetes, and if you have it, to keep it under control.</li><li>Getting enough sleep. If you don't get enough sleep, you raise your risk of high blood pressure, obesity, and diabetes. Those three things can raise your risk of <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span>. To improve your sleep:
  <ul><li>If you are an adult, try to get 7 to 9 hours of sleep per night.</li><li>Make sure that you have good sleep habits, such as keeping a regular sleep schedule and creating a good sleeping environment in your bedroom.</li><li> If you have frequent sleep problems, contact your health care provider. One problem, sleep apnea, causes people to briefly stop breathing many times during sleep. This interferes with your ability to get a good rest and can raise your risk of <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span>. If you think you might have sleep apnea, ask your provider about having a sleep study. And if you do have sleep apnea, make sure that you get treatment for it.</li></ul>
</li></ul></content>

    <document rank="2" url="https://medlineplus.gov/coronaryarterydisease.html">
      <content name="title"><span class="qt0">Coronary</span> Artery <span class="qt1">Disease</span></content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Arteriosclerosis, <span class="qt0">Coronary</span></content>
      <content name="altTitle">Atherosclerosis, <span class="qt0">Coronary</span></content>
      <content name="altTitle">CAD</content>
      <content name="altTitle"><span class="qt0">Coronary</span> Arteriosclerosis</content>
      <content name="altTitle"><span class="qt0">Coronary</span> atherosclerosis</content>
      <content name="altTitle"><span class="qt0">Coronary <span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span></content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p><span class="qt0">Coronary</span> artery <span class="qt1">disease</span> (CAD) is the most common type of <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span>. It is the leading cause of death in the United States in both men and women.</p><p>CAD happens when the arteries that supply blood to <span class="qt0">heart</span> muscle become hardened and narrowed. This is due to the buildup of cholesterol and other material, called plaque, on their inner walls. This buildup is called atherosclerosis. As it grows, less blood can flow through the arteries. As a result, the <span class="qt0">heart</span> muscle can't get the blood or oxygen it needs. This can lead to chest pain (angina) or a <span class="qt0">heart</span> attack.  Most <span class="qt0">heart</span> attacks happen when a blood clot suddenly cuts off the <span class="qt0">hearts</span>' blood supply, causing permanent <span class="qt0">heart</span> damage.  </p><p>Over time, CAD can also weaken the <span class="qt0">heart</span> muscle and contribute to <span class="qt0">heart</span> failure and arrhythmias. <span class="qt0">Heart</span> failure means the <span class="qt0">heart</span> can't pump blood well to the rest of the body. Arrhythmias are changes in the normal beating rhythm of the <span class="qt0">heart</span>.  </p><p>NIH: National <span class="qt0">Heart</span>, Lung, and Blood Institute</p></content>

It is the leading cause of death in the United States in both men and women. CAD happens  ... </content>

    <document rank="3" url="https://medlineplus.gov/diabeticheartdisease.html">
      <content name="title">Diabetic <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">Heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">Disease</span></span></content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is diabetes?<p>Diabetes is a <span class="qt1">disease</span> in which your blood glucose, or blood sugar, levels are too high. Glucose comes from foods you eat. The cells of your body need glucose for energy. A hormone called insulin helps the glucose get into your cells.</p><p>With type 1 diabetes, your body doesn't make insulin. With type 2 diabetes, your body doesn't make or use insulin well. Without enough insulin, glucose builds up in your blood and causes high blood sugar levels.</p>What is diabetic <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span>?<p>Diabetic <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span> is a term for <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span> in people who have diabetes. If you have diabetes, you are much more likely to develop <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span> or have a stroke than people who don't have diabetes. And you may start having these problems at a younger age.</p><p>Over time, the effects of high blood sugar from diabetes can include damage to the blood vessels and nerves in your <span class="qt0">heart</span>. This damage increases your chance of developing <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">diseases</span></span>, including:</p><ul><li><span class="qt0">Coronary</span> artery <span class="qt1">disease</span> (CAD). It happens slowly as a sticky material called plaque builds up in the arteries that supply your <span class="qt0">heart</span> muscle with blood.</li><li><span class="qt0">Heart</span> failure. With this condition, your <span class="qt0">heart</span> can't pump enough oxygen-rich blood to meet the needs of your body.</li><li>Cardiomyopathy. This is a group of <span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">diseases</span></span><span class="qt2"> in which the </span><span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span> muscle may become thick or stiff.</li></ul>Who is more likely to develop diabetic <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span>?<p>Diabetes puts you at serious risk for <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span>. It also makes you more likely to develop other conditions that raise your risk even more, including:</p><ul><li>High blood pressure</li><li>High blood levels of LDL "bad" cholesterol</li><li>High triglycerides (a type of fat in your blood) with low blood levels of HDL "good" cholesterol</li></ul><p>Your risk for <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span> is also higher if you</p><ul><li>Are male</li><li>Smoke</li><li>Have obesity</li><li>Have too much belly fat around your waist, even though you're at a healthy weight:
	<ul><li>For men, that's a waist more than 40 inches</li><li>For women, that's a waist more than 35 inches</li></ul>
</li><li>Have a family history of <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span></li><li>Have chronic kidney <span class="qt1">disease</span></li></ul>What are the symptoms of diabetic <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span>?<p>In the early stages, <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span> usually doesn't have any symptoms. But if your <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span> worsens, you can have symptoms. Your symptoms will depend on the type of <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span> you have. They might include:</p><ul><li>Shortness of breath</li><li>Fatigue</li><li>Dizziness or fainting</li><li>Arrhythmia (problem with the rate or rhythm of your heartbeat)</li><li>Swollen feet and ankles</li><li>Chest pain</li></ul><p>It's important to know that people with diabetes may not feel chest pain. That's because diabetes can damage the nerves in your <span class="qt0">heart</span>. If you have any symptoms that could be <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span>, talk with your health care provider.</p>How is diabetic <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span> diagnosed?<p>To find out if you have diabetic <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span>, your provider will:</p><ul><li>Ask about your medical history, including your symptoms and other health conditions you may have</li><li>Ask about your family history, to find out if you have relatives who have or had <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span></li><li>Do a physical exam</li><li>Likely run some tests to help understand your personal risk for <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span>, including:
	<ul><li>Blood tests to check your cholesterol, triglycerides, and blood sugar levels</li><li>A blood-pressure check</li><li><span class="qt0">Heart</span> tests, if needed</li></ul>
</li></ul><p>Depending on your risk level, your provider may send you to a cardiologist (a doctor who specializes in <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">diseases</span></span>) for care. If you do have <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span>, treatment will depend on the type of <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span> you have.</p>How can I prevent diabetic <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span>?<p>You may be able to prevent <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span> or keep it from getting worse by working with your provider to:</p><ul><li>Control your blood sugar levels</li><li>Manage other conditions you may have that can raise your risk for <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span> </li><li>Take any medicines your provider prescribed</li><li>Make <span class="qt0">heart</span>-healthy habits part of your daily life</li><li>Follow a healthy eating plan for diabetes</li></ul><p>NIH: National Institute of Diabetes and Digestive and Kidney <span class="qt1">Diseases</span></p></content>
      <content name="mesh">Diabetes Mellitus</content>

    <document rank="4" url="https://medlineplus.gov/heartdiseaseinwomen.html">
      <content name="title"><span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">Heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">Disease</span></span> in Women</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span>?<p><span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">Heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span> is a general term that includes many types of <span class="qt0">heart</span> problems. It's also called <span class="qt0">cardiovascular</span> <span class="qt1">disease</span>, which means <span class="qt0">heart</span> and blood vessel <span class="qt1">disease</span>.</p><p><span class="qt0">Coronary</span> artery <span class="qt1">disease</span> (also called <span class="qt0">coronary <span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span>) is the most common type of <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span> in both men and women. It happens slowly over time when a sticky substance called plaque builds up in the arteries that supply your <span class="qt0">heart</span> muscle with blood. The plaque narrows or blocks blood flow to the <span class="qt0">heart</span> muscle and can lead to:</p><ul><li>Angina - chest pain from lack of blood flow</li><li><span class="qt0">Heart</span> attacks - when part of the <span class="qt0">heart</span> muscle dies from loss of blood flow</li><li><span class="qt0">Heart</span> failure - when your <span class="qt0">heart</span> can't pump enough blood to meet your body's needs</li><li>Arrhythmia - a problem with the rate or rhythm of your heartbeat</li></ul>How does <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span> affect women?<p>In the United States, <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span> is the number one cause of death in women. But women are often not diagnosed with <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span> as quickly as men are. That's because:</p><ul><li>Women are more likely than men to have "silent" <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span>, meaning that they don't have symptoms.</li><li>Health care providers may not recognize <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span> in women because women's symptoms may be different from men's symptoms.</li><li>Women are more likely than men to have certain types of <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span> that can be harder to diagnose.</li></ul><p>A delay in diagnosis may mean a delay in medical care that could help prevent serious problems, such as a <span class="qt0">heart</span> attack. That's why it's important to learn about your risk for <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span>, the symptoms in women, and how to keep your <span class="qt0">heart</span> healthy.</p>What types of <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span> do women get?<p>Women can get any type of <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span>. Like men, the most common type of <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span> among women is <span class="qt0">coronary</span> artery <span class="qt1">disease</span>. But there are certain types of <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span> which are less common, but affect women more often than men:</p><ul><li><span class="qt0">Coronary</span> microvascular <span class="qt1">disease</span> - Chest pain from spasms (sudden tightening) in the smallest arteries of the <span class="qt0">heart</span> that pinch off blood flow. It typically happens during rest or routine activities. This serious condition increases your risk of having a <span class="qt0">heart</span> attack or other <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">diseases</span></span>.  This type may be harder to diagnose since blockages in smaller arteries can be harder to see on imaging tests.</li><li>Broken <span class="qt0">heart</span> syndrome - Strong chest pain or other signs that look like a <span class="qt0">heart</span> attack. It's caused by the stress of powerful emotions, such as deep grief, anger, or surprise. It can happen even if you're healthy. It mostly affects women after menopause and usually doesn't cause any lasting damage.</li><li>Variant angina - A rare type of strong chest pain from spasms in the <span class="qt0">heart</span> arteries. The pain usually happens in a pattern during sleep. Variant angina rarely causes a <span class="qt0">heart</span> attack.</li></ul>Which women are more likely to develop <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span>?<p>Your risk for developing <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span> increases with:</p><ul><li>Menopause. Women can develop <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span> at any age, but your risk increases after your periods stop, usually by age 55. Before menopause, your body makes more estrogen (a female hormone) which helps protect against <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span>. That's why women generally develop <span class="qt0">coronary</span> artery <span class="qt1">disease</span> 10 years later than men. During and after menopause, estrogen levels drop and your risk for <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">diseases</span></span> goes up. If your periods stop before age 40, your risk will be higher than other women your age.</li><li>Family history of <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span>. Your risk for <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span> may be higher if your:
	<ul><li>Mother or sister had <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span> before age 65.</li><li>Father or brother had <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span> before age 55.</li></ul>
</li><li>Problems during pregnancy, such as  high blood pressure, gestational diabetes, anemia, and high blood pressure.</li><li>Use of hormonal birth control (pills, patches, or vaginal rings with estrogen and progesterone).</li><li>Endometriosis.</li><li>Polycystic ovary syndrome.</li><li>Inflammatory and autoimmune <span class="qt1">diseases</span>.</li><li>Metabolic syndrome.</li><li>Mental health issues, such as stress, anxiety, or depression.</li><li>Health risks from a lack physical activity.</li><li>Obesity or being overweight.</li><li>Diabetes.</li><li>Low HDL cholesterol levels.</li><li>Mild to moderate high blood pressure.</li><li>Smoking.</li></ul><p>If you have one or more risks for <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span>, ask your health care provider for help understanding your risk level. Ask if you need any <span class="qt0">heart</span> tests to help catch <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span> early.</p>What are the symptoms of <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span><span class="qt2"> and </span><span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span> attack in women?<p>When women have symptoms of <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span>, they may include:</p><ul><li>Pain or discomfort in the chest that may be dull and heavy or sharp</li><li>Pain in the neck, jaw, throat, upper belly, or back</li><li>Nausea or vomiting</li><li>Unusual fatigue</li><li>Shortness of breath (feeling like you can't get enough air) during physical activity</li></ul><p>Women who have <span class="qt0">coronary</span> artery <span class="qt1">disease</span> are more likely than men to have chest pain when resting or doing daily activities, rather than during exercise. They're also more likely than men to feel chest pain from mental stress.</p><p>Symptoms of a <span class="qt0">heart</span> attack in women may also be different than in men. Chest pain is the most common symptom for both sexes. It may feel like crushing or squeezing. But women are somewhat less likely than men to have chest pain.</p><p>During a <span class="qt0">heart</span> attack, women may feel:</p><ul><li>Pressure or tightness in the chest</li><li>Pain in the upper back, arms, neck, jaw or throat</li><li>Dizziness</li><li>Unusual fatigue</li><li>Indigestion, heartburn, nausea, or vomiting </li><li>Stomach pain</li><li>Shortness of breath</li></ul><p><span class="qt0">Heart</span> attacks usually don't look like the sudden, dramatic events we see in the movies. The symptoms may be mild or strong. They may start slowly. They can stop and then come back.</p>Can <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span> in women be prevented?<p>You can help lower your risk by:</p><ul><li>Learning how to prevent <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span> and making <span class="qt0">heart</span>-healthy habits part of your daily life.</li><li>Asking your provider about your personal risk for <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span> and the best way to manage your <span class="qt0">heart</span> health.</li></ul><p>Remember, women can have <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span> without symptoms. But if you pay attention to your risk for <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span>, you can take action to prevent problems or keep them from getting worse.</p><p>NIH: National <span class="qt0">Heart</span>, Lung, and Blood Institute</p></content>

    <document rank="6" url="https://medlineplus.gov/congenitalheartdefects.html">
      <content name="title">Congenital <span class="qt0">Heart</span> Defects</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle"><span class="qt0">Heart</span> Defects</content>
      <content name="altTitle"><span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">Heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">Diseases</span></span>, Congenital</content>
      <content name="altTitle"><span class="qt0">Heart</span> Murmur</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What are congenital <span class="qt0">heart</span> defects?<p>Congenital <span class="qt0">heart</span> defects (CHDs) are problems with the structure of the <span class="qt0">heart</span>. "Congenital" means that that the problems are present at birth. These defects happen when a fetus's <span class="qt0">heart</span> doesn't develop normally during pregnancy. Congenital <span class="qt0">heart</span> defects are the most common type of birth defect.</p><p>Congenital <span class="qt0">heart</span> defects can change the way the <span class="qt0">heart</span> pumps blood. They may make blood flow too slowly, go the wrong way, or block it completely.</p><p>There are many types of congenital <span class="qt0">heart</span> defects. They can happen in one or more parts of the <span class="qt0">heart</span>. The most common types are:</p><ul><li>Septal defects (<span class="qt0">"hole in the heart"</span>) - openings in the wall between the left and right sides of the <span class="qt0">heart</span></li><li><span class="qt0">Heart</span> valve defects - problems with the valves that control the flow of blood through the <span class="qt0">heart</span></li><li>Defects in the large blood vessels that carry blood in and out of the <span class="qt0">heart</span></li></ul><p>Congenital <span class="qt0">heart</span> defects can range from very mild problems that never need treatment to life-threatening problems at birth. The most serious congenital <span class="qt0">heart</span> defects are called critical congenital <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span>. Babies with these defects usually need surgery in the first year of life. But the symptoms of milder <span class="qt0">heart</span> defects may not show up until childhood or adulthood.</p>What causes congenital <span class="qt0">heart</span> defects?<p>Researchers often don't know what causes congenital <span class="qt0">heart</span> defects. They do know that changes in a baby's genes sometimes cause a <span class="qt0">heart</span> defect. The changed genes may come from the parents, or the changes may happen during pregnancy.</p>Who is more likely to have a baby with a congenital <span class="qt0">heart</span> defect?<p>Several things may increase the chance that your baby has a congenital <span class="qt0">heart</span> defect, such as:</p><ul><li>Your health before and during pregnancy, including:
	<ul><li>Having diabetes before pregnancy or developing it in the first 3 months of pregnancy (diabetes that develops later in pregnancy isn't a major risk for <span class="qt0">heart</span> defects). Carefully controlling your blood glucose, also called blood sugar, before and during pregnancy can lower your baby's risk of congenital <span class="qt0">heart</span> defects.</li><li>Having phenylketonuria (PKU), a rare inherited disorder that affects how the body uses a protein in foods. If you have PKU, eating a low-protein diet before getting pregnant can lower your baby's risk of having a congenital <span class="qt0">heart</span> defect.</li><li>Having rubella (German measles) during pregnancy.</li></ul></li><li>Your contact with certain substances during pregnancy, including:
	<ul><li>Smoking or secondhand smoke (breathing smoke from another smoker).</li><li>Certain medicines, such as angiotensin-converting (ACE) inhibitors for high blood pressure and retinoic acids for acne. If you're pregnant or plan to get pregnant, talk with your health care provider about all the medicines you take.</li></ul></li><li>Your family history and genetics. In most cases, congenital <span class="qt0">heart</span> defects don't run in families. But your chance of having a baby with a congenital <span class="qt0">heart</span> defect does go up if you or the other parent has a congenital <span class="qt0">heart</span> defect, or if you already have a child with a congenital <span class="qt0">heart</span> defect.</li></ul>What are the symptoms of congenital <span class="qt0">heart</span> defects?<p>Congenital <span class="qt0">heart</span> defects don't cause pain. The signs and symptoms are different, depending on the type and number of defects and how serious they are.</p><p>Common signs and symptoms of congenital <span class="qt0">heart</span> defects include:</p><ul><li>Cyanosis - a bluish color to the skin, lips, and fingernails. It happens when there isn't enough oxygen in the blood.</li><li>Fatigue - your baby may be unusually sleepy and may become very tired during feedings.</li><li>Poor blood flow.</li><li>Fast or difficult breathing.</li><li><span class="qt0">Heart</span> murmur - an unusual sound between heartbeats.</li></ul>What other problems do congenital <span class="qt0">heart</span> defects cause?<p>Congenital <span class="qt0">heart</span> defects don't always cause other problems. If they do, which problems you have would depend on the type and number of defects and how serious the defects are.</p><p>Children with congenital <span class="qt0">heart</span> defects are more likely to:</p><ul><li>Be smaller than other children</li><li>Have problems or delays in mental, and emotional growth, and behavior, such as:
	<ul><li>Speech and language problems</li><li>Attention deficit hyperactivity disorder (ADHD)</li></ul>
</li></ul><p>People with congenital <span class="qt0">heart</span> defects may develop other health conditions, including:</p><ul><li>Endocarditis - an infection of lining of the <span class="qt0">heart</span> and valves</li><li>Arrhythmia - a problem with the rate or rhythm of your heartbeat</li><li><span class="qt0">Heart</span> failure - when your <span class="qt0">heart</span> can't pump enough blood to the body</li><li>Pulmonary hypertension - high blood pressure in your lungs</li><li>Kidney and liver <span class="qt1">disease</span></li></ul>How are congenital <span class="qt0">heart</span> defects diagnosed?<ul><li>Before a baby is born, your provider may use ultrasound pictures of the fetus's <span class="qt0">heart</span> to look for <span class="qt0">heart</span> defects. This is called a fetal echocardiogram. It's done between weeks 18 and 22 of pregnancy.</li><li>During the first few days after birth, all newborns are checked for congenital <span class="qt0">heart</span> defects. A pulse oximeter is clipped to your baby's hands or feet to measure blood oxygen. If it shows low levels of blood oxygen, more tests will be needed to find out if your baby has a <span class="qt0">heart</span> defect.</li><li>To diagnose congenital <span class="qt0">heart</span> defects in babies, children, and adults, a provider may use many tools, including:
	<ul><li>A physical exam.</li><li>Certain <span class="qt0">heart</span> tests to see how the <span class="qt0">heart</span> is working.</li><li>Genetic testing to see if certain gene problems caused the defect.</li></ul></li></ul>What are the treatments for congenital <span class="qt0">heart</span> defects?<p>Treatment depends on the type of congenital <span class="qt0">heart</span> defect and how serious it is. Possible treatments include:</p><ul><li><span class="qt0">Cardiac</span> catheterization to repair simple defects, such as a small hole in the inside wall of the <span class="qt0">heart</span>. A catheterization uses a thin tube guided through a vein and into the <span class="qt0">heart</span>.</li><li><span class="qt0">Heart</span> surgery may be needed to:
	<ul><li>Repair defects in the <span class="qt0">heart</span> and blood vessels.</li><li>Repair or replace a <span class="qt0">heart</span> valve.</li><li>Place a device in the chest to help the <span class="qt0">heart</span> pump blood.</li><li>Do a <span class="qt0">heart</span> transplant</li></ul>
</li><li>Medicine is often used if your baby has a specific type of congenital <span class="qt0">heart</span> defect called patent ductus arteriosus.</li></ul><p>All children and adults who have congenital <span class="qt0">heart</span> defects need regular follow-up care from a cardiologist (a doctor who specializes in <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">diseases</span></span>) throughout their life, even if their defect was repaired.</p><p>Some people may need several <span class="qt0">heart</span> surgeries or catheterizations over the years. They may also need to take medicines to help their <span class="qt0">hearts</span> work as well as possible.</p><p>NIH: National <span class="qt0">Heart</span>, Lung, and Blood Institute</p></content>

      <content name="FullSummary">What is <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span>?<p><span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">Heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span> is a general term that includes many types of <span class="qt0">heart</span> problems. It's a type of <span class="qt0">cardiovascular</span> <span class="qt1">disease</span>, which means <span class="qt0">heart</span> and blood vessel <span class="qt1">disease</span>.</p><p><span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">Heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span> is the leading cause of death in the United States, but there are ways to prevent and manage many types of <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span>.</p>What are the types of <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span>?<p>There are many different types of <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span>. Some you may be born with, called congenital <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span>. Other types develop during your lifetime.</p><p><span class="qt0">Coronary</span> artery <span class="qt1">disease</span> (also called <span class="qt0">coronary <span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span>) is the most common type of <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span>. It happens slowly over time when a sticky substance called plaque builds up in the arteries that supply your <span class="qt0">heart</span> muscle with blood. The plaque narrows or blocks blood flow to the <span class="qt0">heart</span> muscle and can lead to other <span class="qt0">heart</span> problems:</p><ul><li>Angina - chest pain from lack of blood flow</li><li><span class="qt0">Heart</span> attacks - when part of the <span class="qt0">heart</span> muscle dies from loss of blood flow</li><li><span class="qt0">Heart</span> failure - when your <span class="qt0">heart</span> can't pump enough blood to meet your body's needs</li><li>Arrhythmia - a problem with the rate or rhythm of your heartbeat</li></ul><p>Other types of <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">diseases</span></span> may affect your <span class="qt0">heart</span> valves or <span class="qt0">heart</span> muscle (cardiomyopathy).</p>What causes <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">diseases</span></span>?<p>The causes of <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span> depend on the type of <span class="qt1">disease</span>. Some possible causes include lifestyle, genetics, infections, medicines, and other <span class="qt1">diseases</span>.</p>Who is more likely to develop <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">diseases</span></span>?<p>There are many different factors that can make you more likely to develop <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span>. Some of these factors you can change, but others you cannot.</p><ul><li>Age. Your risk of <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span> goes up as you get older.</li><li>Sex. Some factors may affect <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span> risk differently in women than in men.</li><li>Family history and genetics. A family history of early <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span> raises your risk. And research has shown that some genes are linked to a higher risk of certain <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">diseases</span></span>.</li><li>Race/ethnicity. <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">Heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span> is the leading cause of death in most racial and ethnic groups in the United States. However, certain groups have higher risks than others. This includes Black people, White people, and American Indian/Alaska Native people.</li><li>Lifestyle habits. Over time, unhealthy lifestyle habits can raise your risk of <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span>. These can include: 
	<ul><li>Eating a diet high in saturated fats, refined carbohydrates, and salt.</li><li>Not getting enough physical activity.</li><li>Drinking too much alcohol.</li><li>Smoking and exposure to secondhand smoke.</li><li>Too much stress.</li></ul>
</li><li>Having other medical conditions can raise your risk of <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">diseases</span></span>. These conditions include:
	<ul><li>High blood pressure.</li><li>High cholesterol levels.</li><li>Diabetes.</li><li>Obesity.</li><li>Autoimmune and inflammatory <span class="qt1">diseases</span>.</li><li>Chronic kidney <span class="qt1">disease</span>.</li><li>Metabolic syndrome.</li></ul>
</li></ul>What are the symptoms of <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span>?<p>Your symptoms will depend on the type of <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span> you have. You may not have symptoms at first. In some cases, you may not know you have <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span> until you have a complication such as a <span class="qt0">heart</span> attack.</p>How are <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">diseases</span></span> diagnosed?<p>To find out if you have <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span>, your health care provider will:</p><ul><li>Ask about your medical history, including your symptoms</li><li>Ask about your family health history, including relatives who have had <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span></li><li>Do a physical exam</li><li>Likely run <span class="qt0">heart</span> tests and blood tests</li></ul><p>In some cases, your provider may refer you to a cardiologist (a doctor who specializes in <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">diseases</span></span>) for tests, diagnosis, and care.</p>What are the treatments for <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span>?<p>Treatment plans for <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span> depend on the type of <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span> you have, how serious your symptoms are, and what other health conditions you have. Possible treatments may include:</p><ul><li><span class="qt0">Heart</span>-healthy lifestyle changes</li><li>Medicines</li><li>Procedures or surgeries</li><li><span class="qt0">Cardiac</span> rehabilitation</li></ul>Can <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">diseases</span></span> be prevented?<p>You may be able to lower your risk of certain <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">diseases</span></span> by making <span class="qt0">heart</span>-healthy lifestyle changes  and managing any other medical conditions you have.</p><p>NIH: National <span class="qt0">Heart</span>, Lung, and Blood Institute</p></content>

    <document rank="7" url="https://medlineplus.gov/cardiacrehabilitation.html">
      <content name="title"><span class="qt0">Cardiac</span> Rehabilitation</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle"><span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">Heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">Diseases</span></span>--Rehabilitation</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p><span class="qt0">Cardiac</span> rehabilitation (rehab) is a medically supervised program to help people who have:</p><ul><li>A <span class="qt0">heart</span> attack</li><li>Angioplasty or <span class="qt0">coronary</span> artery bypass grafting for <span class="qt0">coronary <span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span></li><li>A <span class="qt0">heart</span> valve repair or replacement</li><li>A <span class="qt0">heart</span> transplant or a lung transplant</li><li>Angina</li><li><span class="qt0">Heart</span> failure</li></ul><p>The goal is to help you return to an active life, and to reduce the risk of further <span class="qt0">heart</span> problems.  A team of specialists will create a plan for you that includes exercise training, education on <span class="qt0">heart</span> healthy living, and counseling to reduce stress.  You will learn how to reduce your risk factors, such as high blood pressure, high blood cholesterol, depression, and diabetes. Being overweight, having obesity, smoking, and not exercising are other risk factors. </p><p>NIH: National <span class="qt0">Heart</span>, Lung, and Blood Institute</p></content>
