    <document rank="8" url="https://medlineplus.gov/hearthealthtests.html">
      <content name="title"><span class="qt0">Heart</span> Health Tests</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle"><span class="qt0">Cardiac</span> Test</content>
      <content name="altTitle"><span class="qt0">Heart</span> Test</content>
      <content name="altTitle"><span class="qt0">Cardiac</span> Diagnostic Tests</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p><span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">Heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">diseases</span></span> are the number one killer in the United States. They are also a major cause of disability. If you do have <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span>, it is important to find it early, when it is easier to treat. Blood tests and <span class="qt0">heart</span> health tests can help find <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">diseases</span></span> or identify problems that can lead to <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">diseases</span></span>. There are several different types of <span class="qt0">heart</span> health tests. Your health care provider will decide which test or tests you need, based on your symptoms (if any), risk factors, and medical history.</p><span class="qt0">Cardiac</span> Catheterization<p><span class="qt0">Cardiac</span> catheterization is a medical procedure used to diagnose and treat some <span class="qt0">heart</span> conditions. For the procedure, your provider puts a catheter (a long, thin, flexible tube) into a blood vessel in your arm, groin, or neck, and threads it to your <span class="qt0">heart</span>. The provider can use the catheter to:</p><ul><li>Do a <span class="qt0">coronary</span> angiography. This involves putting a special type of dye in the catheter, so the dye can flow through your bloodstream to your <span class="qt0">heart</span>. Then your provider takes x-rays of your <span class="qt0">heart</span>. The dye allows your provider to see your <span class="qt0">coronary</span> arteries on the x-ray, and to check for <span class="qt0">coronary</span> artery <span class="qt1">disease</span> (CAD). CAD is caused by plaque buildup in the arteries.</li><li>Take samples of your blood and <span class="qt0">heart</span> muscle.</li><li>Examine your <span class="qt0">heart</span> valves.</li><li>Do procedures such as angioplasty or minor <span class="qt0">heart</span> surgeries to repair congenital <span class="qt0">heart</span> defects or replace <span class="qt0">heart</span> valves.</li></ul><span class="qt0">Cardiac</span> CT Scan<p>A <span class="qt0">cardiac</span> CT (computed tomography) scan is a painless imaging test that uses x-rays to take detailed pictures of your <span class="qt0">heart</span> and its blood vessels. Computers can combine these pictures to create a three-dimensional (3D) model of your whole <span class="qt0">heart</span>. This test can help providers detect or evaluate various <span class="qt0">heart</span> problems, including:</p><ul><li><span class="qt0">Coronary</span> artery <span class="qt1">disease</span></li><li>Calcium buildup in the <span class="qt0">coronary</span> arteries</li><li>Congenital <span class="qt0">heart</span> defects</li><li>Problems with the aorta (the main artery that carries blood away from the <span class="qt0">heart</span>)</li><li>Problems with <span class="qt0">heart</span> function and valves</li><li>Pericardial <span class="qt1">diseases</span></li></ul><p>Before you have the test, you get an injection of contrast dye. The dye highlights your <span class="qt0">heart</span> and blood vessels in the pictures. The CT scanner is a large, tunnel-like machine. You lie still on a table that slides you into the scanner, and the scanner takes the pictures.</p><span class="qt0">Cardiac</span> MRI<p><span class="qt0">Cardiac</span> MRI (magnetic resonance imaging) is a painless imaging test that uses radio waves, magnets, and a computer to create detailed pictures of your <span class="qt0">heart</span>. It can help your provider figure out whether you have <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span>, and if so, how severe it is. A <span class="qt0">cardiac</span> MRI can also help your provider decide the best way to treat <span class="qt0">heart</span> problems such as:</p><ul><li>Congenital <span class="qt0">heart</span> defects</li><li><span class="qt0">Coronary</span> artery <span class="qt1">disease</span></li><li><span class="qt0">Heart</span> valve problems</li><li>Pericarditis</li><li><span class="qt0">Cardiac</span> tumors</li><li>Damage from a <span class="qt0">heart</span> attack</li></ul><p>The MRI is a large, tunnel-like machine. You lie still on a table that slides you into the MRI machine. The machine makes loud noises as it takes pictures of your <span class="qt0">heart</span>. Sometimes before the test, you might get an injection of contrast dye. The dye highlights your <span class="qt0">heart</span> and blood vessels in the pictures.</p>Chest X-Ray<p>A chest x-ray creates pictures of the organs and structures inside your chest, such as your <span class="qt0">heart</span>, lungs, and blood vessels. It can reveal signs of <span class="qt0">heart</span> failure, as well as lung disorders and other causes of symptoms not related to <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span>.</p><span class="qt0">Coronary</span> Angiography<p><span class="qt0">Coronary</span> angiography (angiogram) is a procedure that uses contrast dye and x-ray pictures to look at the insides of your arteries. It can show whether plaque is blocking your arteries and how severe the blockage is. Providers use this procedure to diagnose <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">diseases</span></span> after chest pain, sudden <span class="qt0">cardiac arrest</span> (SCA), or abnormal results from other <span class="qt0">heart</span> tests such as an EKG or a stress test.</p><p>You usually have a <span class="qt0">cardiac</span> catheterization to inject the dye into your <span class="qt0">coronary</span> arteries. Then the provider will take special x-rays while the dye is flowing through your <span class="qt0">coronary</span> arteries. The dye lets your provider study the flow of blood through your <span class="qt0">heart</span> and blood vessels.</p>Echocardiography<p>Echocardiography, or echo, is a painless test that uses sound waves to create moving pictures of your <span class="qt0">heart</span>. The pictures show the size and shape of your <span class="qt0">heart</span>. They also show how well your <span class="qt0">heart</span>'s chambers and valves are working. Providers use an echo to diagnose many different <span class="qt0">heart</span> problems, and to check how severe they are.</p><p>There are several different types of echocardiography. For transthoracic echocardiography (the most common type), a technician applies gel to your chest. The gel helps sound waves reach your <span class="qt0">heart</span>. The technician moves a transducer (wand-like device) across your chest. The transducer connects to a computer. It transmits ultrasound waves into your chest, and the waves bounce (echo) back. The computer converts the echoes into pictures of your <span class="qt0">heart</span>. </p>Electrocardiogram (EKG), (ECG)<p>An electrocardiogram, also called an ECG or EKG, is a painless test that detects and records your <span class="qt0">heart</span>'s electrical activity. It shows how fast your <span class="qt0">heart</span> is beating and whether its rhythm is steady or irregular.</p><p>An EKG may be part of a routine exam to screen for <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span>. Or you may get it to detect and study <span class="qt0">heart</span> problems such as <span class="qt0">heart</span> attacks, arrhythmia, and <span class="qt0">heart</span> failure.</p><p>For the test, you lie still on a table and a nurse or technician attaches electrodes (patches that have sensors) to the skin on your chest, arms, and legs. Wires connect the electrodes to a machine that records your <span class="qt0">heart</span>'s electrical activity.</p>Stress Testing<p>Stress testing looks at how your <span class="qt0">heart</span> works during physical stress. It can help to diagnose <span class="qt0">coronary</span> artery <span class="qt1">disease</span>, and to check how severe it is. It can also check for other problems, including <span class="qt0">heart</span> valve <span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span><span class="qt2"> and </span><span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span> failure.</p><p>For the test, you exercise (or are given medicine if you are unable to exercise) to make your <span class="qt0">heart</span> work hard and beat fast. While this is happening, you get an EKG and blood pressure monitoring. Before or after the test, you might also have an echocardiogram, or other imaging tests such as a nuclear scan. For the nuclear scan, you get an injection of a tracer (a radioactive substance), which travels to your <span class="qt0">heart</span>. Special cameras detect the energy from the tracer to craeate pictures of your <span class="qt0">heart</span>. You have pictures taken after you exercise, and then after you rest.</p><p>NIH: National <span class="qt0">Heart</span>, Lung, and Blood Institute</p></content>

      <content name="snippet"> <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">Heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">diseases</span></span> are the number one killer in the United States. They are also a major cause of disability. If you do have <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span>, it is important to  ... </content>

    <document rank="9" url="https://medlineplus.gov/heartfailure.html">
      <content name="title"><span class="qt0">Heart</span> Failure</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">CHF</content>
      <content name="altTitle"><span class="qt0">Cardiac</span> Failure</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Congestive <span class="qt0">Heart</span> Failure</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Low <span class="qt0">Cardiac</span> Output</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Left-sided <span class="qt0">heart</span> failure</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Right-sided <span class="qt0">heart</span> failure</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is <span class="qt0">heart</span> failure?<p><span class="qt0">Heart</span> failure means that your <span class="qt0">heart</span> can't pump enough oxygen-rich blood to meet your body's needs. <span class="qt0">Heart</span> failure doesn't mean that your <span class="qt0">heart</span> has stopped or is about to stop beating. But without enough blood flow, your organs may not work well, which can cause serious problems.</p><p><span class="qt0">Heart</span> failure can affect one or both sides of your <span class="qt0">heart</span>:</p><ul><li>With right-sided <span class="qt0">heart</span> failure, your <span class="qt0">heart</span> is too weak to pump enough blood to your lungs to get oxygen.</li><li>With left-sided <span class="qt0">heart</span> failure, your <span class="qt0">heart</span> can't pump enough oxygen-rich blood out to your body. This happens when the left side of your <span class="qt0">heart</span> becomes either:
	<ul><li>Too weak to pump enough blood.</li><li>Too thick or stiff to relax and fill with enough blood.</li></ul></li></ul><p>Left-sided <span class="qt0">heart</span> failure is more common than right-sided <span class="qt0">heart</span> failure.</p>What causes <span class="qt0">heart</span> failure?<p><span class="qt0">Heart</span> failure can start suddenly after a medical condition or injury damages your <span class="qt0">heart</span> muscle. But in most cases, <span class="qt0">heart</span> failure develops slowly from long-term medical conditions.</p><p>Conditions that can cause <span class="qt0">heart</span> failure include:</p><ul><li>Arrhythmia (a problem with the rate or rhythm of your heartbeat)</li><li>Cardiomyopathy</li><li>Congenital <span class="qt0">heart</span> defects or other types of <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">diseases</span></span> that you are born with</li><li><span class="qt0">Coronary</span> artery <span class="qt1">disease</span></li><li>Endocarditis</li><li><span class="qt0">Heart</span> attack</li><li><span class="qt0">Heart</span> valve <span class="qt1">diseases</span></li><li>High blood pressure</li><li>A blood clot in your lung</li><li>Diabetes</li><li>Certain severe lung <span class="qt1">diseases</span>, such as <span class="qt1">COPD</span> (chronic obstructive pulmonary <span class="qt1">disease</span>)</li><li>Obesity</li></ul><p>Over time, left-sided <span class="qt0">heart</span> failure can lead to right-sided <span class="qt0">heart</span> failure.</p>Who is more likely to develop <span class="qt0">heart</span> failure?<p><span class="qt0">Heart</span> failure can happen at any age. It happens to both men and women, but men often develop it at a younger age than women.  Your chance of developing <span class="qt0">heart</span> failure increases if:</p><ul><li>You're 65 years old or older. Aging can weaken and stiffen your <span class="qt0">heart</span> muscle.</li><li>Your family health history includes relatives who have or have had <span class="qt0">heart</span> failure.</li><li>You have changes in your genes that affect your <span class="qt0">heart</span> tissue.</li><li>You have habits that can harm your <span class="qt0">heart</span>, including:
	<ul><li>Smoking</li><li>Eating foods high in fat, cholesterol, and sodium (salt)</li><li>Having an inactive lifestyle</li><li>Alcohol use disorder (AUD)</li><li>Illegal drug use</li></ul></li><li>You have other medical conditions that can affect your <span class="qt0">heart</span>, including: 
	<ul><li>Any <span class="qt0">heart</span> or blood vessel conditions, including high blood pressure</li><li>Serious lung <span class="qt1">diseases</span></li><li>Infection, such as HIV or COVID-19</li><li>Obesity</li><li>Diabetes</li><li>Sleep apnea</li><li>Chronic kidney <span class="qt1">disease</span></li><li>Anemia</li><li>Thyroid <span class="qt1">disease</span></li><li>Iron overload <span class="qt1">disease</span></li><li>Cancer treatments that can harm your <span class="qt0">heart</span>, such as radiation and chemotherapy</li></ul></li><li>You are African American. African Americans are more likely to develop <span class="qt0">heart</span> failure and have more serious cases at younger ages than people of other races. Factors such as stigma, discrimination, income, education, and geographic region can also affect their risk of <span class="qt0">heart</span> failure.</li></ul>What are the symptoms of <span class="qt0">heart</span> failure?<p>The symptoms of <span class="qt0">heart</span> failure depend on which side of your <span class="qt0">heart</span> is affected and how serious your condition has become.  Most symptoms are caused by reduced blood flow to your organs and fluid buildup in your body.</p><p>Fluid buildup happens because the flow of blood through your <span class="qt0">heart</span> is too slow. As a result, blood backs up in the vessels that return the blood to your <span class="qt0">heart</span>. Fluid may leak from the blood vessels and collect in the tissues of your body, causing swelling (edema) and other problems.</p><p>Symptoms of <span class="qt0">heart</span> failure may include:</p><ul><li>Feeling short of breath (like you can't get enough air) when you do things like climbing stairs. This may be one of the first symptoms you notice.</li><li>Fatigue or weakness even after rest.</li><li>Coughing.</li><li>Swelling and weight gain from fluid in your ankles, lower legs, or abdomen (belly).</li><li>Difficulty sleeping when lying flat.</li><li>Nausea and loss of appetite.</li><li>Swelling in the veins of your neck.</li><li>Needing to urinate (pee) often.</li></ul><p>At first you may have no symptoms or mild symptoms. As the <span class="qt1">disease</span> gets worse, your symptoms will usually bother you more.</p>What other problems does <span class="qt0">heart</span> failure cause?<p>Fluid buildup and reduced blood flow to your organs can lead to serious problems, including:</p><ul><li>Breathing problems from fluid in and around your lungs (also called congestive <span class="qt0">heart</span> failure)</li><li>Kidney or liver damage, including cirrhosis</li><li>Malnutrition if fluid buildup makes eating uncomfortable or if your stomach doesn't get enough blood flow to digest food properly</li><li>Other <span class="qt0">heart</span> conditions, such as irregular heartbeat and sudden <span class="qt0">cardiac arrest</span></li><li>Pulmonary hypertension</li></ul>How is <span class="qt0">heart</span> failure diagnosed?<p>To find out if you have <span class="qt0">heart</span> failure, your health care provider will</p><p> 
</p><ul><li>Ask about your medical history, including your symptoms</li><li>Ask about your family health history, including relatives who have had <span class="qt0">heart</span> failure</li><li>Do a physical exam</li><li>Likely run <span class="qt0">heart</span> tests and blood tests, including a brain natriuretic peptide (BNP) test</li></ul><p>In some cases, your provider may refer you to a cardiologist (a doctor who specializes in <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">diseases</span></span>) for tests, diagnosis, and care.</p>What are the treatments for <span class="qt0">heart</span> failure?<p>Your treatment will depend on the type of <span class="qt0">heart</span> failure you have and how serious it is. There's no cure for <span class="qt0">heart</span> failure. But treatment can help you live longer with fewer symptoms.</p><p>Even with treatment, <span class="qt0">heart</span> failure usually gets worse over time, so you'll likely need treatment for the rest of your life.</p><p>Most treatment plans include:</p><ul><li>Taking medicine</li><li>Eating less sodium and drinking less liquid to control fluid buildup</li><li>Making other changes, such as quitting smoking, managing stress, and getting as much physical activity as your provider recommends</li><li>Treating any conditions that may make <span class="qt0">heart</span> failure worse</li></ul><p>You may need <span class="qt0">heart</span> surgery if:</p><ul><li>You have a congenital <span class="qt0">heart</span> defect or damage to your <span class="qt0">heart</span> that can be fixed.</li><li>The left side of your <span class="qt0">heart</span> is getting weaker and putting a device in your chest could help. Devices include:
	<ul><li>An implantable cardioverter defibrillator.</li><li>A biventricular pacemaker (<span class="qt0">cardiac</span> resynchronization therapy).</li><li>A mechanical <span class="qt0">heart</span> pump (a ventricular assist device (VAD) or a total artificial <span class="qt0">heart</span>).</li></ul>
</li><li>Your <span class="qt0">heart</span> doctor recommends a <span class="qt0">heart</span> transplant because your <span class="qt0">heart</span> failure is life-threatening and nothing else is helping.</li></ul><p>As part of your treatment, you'll need to pay close attention to your symptoms, because <span class="qt0">heart</span> failure can worsen suddenly. Your provider may suggest a <span class="qt0">cardiac</span> rehabilitation program to help you learn how to manage your condition.</p>Can <span class="qt0">heart</span> failure be prevented?<p>You may be able to prevent or delay <span class="qt0">heart</span> failure if you:</p><ul><li>Work with your provider to manage any health conditions that increase your risk of developing <span class="qt0">heart</span> failure</li><li>Make healthy changes in your eating, exercise, and other daily habits to help prevent <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span></li></ul><p>NIH: National <span class="qt0">Heart</span>, Lung, and Blood Institute</p></content>
      <content name="mesh"><span class="qt0">Heart</span> Failure</content>

      <content name="snippet">What is <span class="qt0">heart</span> failure? <span class="qt0">Heart</span> failure means that your <span class="qt0">heart</span> can't pump enough oxygen-rich blood to meet your body's needs. <span class="qt0">Heart</span> failure doesn't mean that  ... </content>

    <document rank="10" url="https://medlineplus.gov/stroke.html">
      <content name="title">Stroke</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Brain Attack</content>
      <content name="altTitle">CVA</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Cerebrovascular <span class="qt1">Disease</span></content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is a stroke?<p>A stroke happens when there is a loss of blood flow to part of the brain. Your brain cells cannot get the oxygen and nutrients they need from blood, and they start to die within a few minutes. This can cause lasting brain damage, long-term disability, or even death.</p><p>If you think that you or someone else is having a stroke, call 911 right away. Immediate treatment may save someone's life and increase the chances for successful rehabilitation and recovery.</p>What are the types of stroke?<p>There are two types of stroke:</p><ul><li>Ischemic stroke is caused by a blood clot that blocks or plugs a blood vessel in the brain. This is the most common type; about 80% of strokes are ischemic.</li><li>Hemorrhagic stroke is caused by a blood vessel that breaks and bleeds into the brain.</li></ul><p>Another condition that's similar to a stroke is a transient ischemic attack (TIA). It's sometimes called a "mini-stroke." TIAs happen when the blood supply to the brain is blocked for a short time. The damage to the brain cells isn't permanent, but having a TIA puts you at much higher risk of having a stroke.</p>Who is at risk for a stroke?<p>Certain factors can raise your risk of a stroke. The major risk factors include:</p><ul><li>High blood pressure. This is the primary risk factor for a stroke.</li><li>Diabetes.</li><li><span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">Heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">diseases</span></span>.Atrial fibrillation and other <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">diseases</span></span> can cause blood clots that lead to stroke.</li><li>Smoking. When you smoke, you damage your blood vessels and raise your blood pressure.</li><li>A personal or family history of stroke or TIA</li><li>Age. Your risk of stroke increases as you get older.</li><li>Race and ethnicity. People who are African Americans or  Hispanic have a higher risk of stroke.</li></ul><p>There are also other factors that are linked to a higher risk of stroke, such as:</p><ul><li>Alcohol and illegal drug use</li><li>Not getting enough physical activity</li><li>High cholesterol</li><li>Unhealthy diet</li><li>Having obesity</li></ul>What are the symptoms of a stroke?<p>The symptoms of a stroke often happen quickly. They include:</p><ul><li>Sudden numbness or weakness of the face, arm, or leg (especially on one side of the body)</li><li>Sudden confusion, trouble speaking, or understanding speech</li><li>Sudden trouble seeing in one or both eyes</li><li>Sudden difficulty walking, dizziness, loss of balance or coordination</li><li>Sudden severe headache with no known cause</li></ul><p>The F.A.S.T. test can help you remember what to look for if you think someone is having a stroke. Think "FAST" and look for:</p><ul><li>Face drooping on one side when smiling.</li><li>Arm weakness occurs when the arms are raised, and one arm drifts downward.</li><li>Speech is slurred or strange.</li><li>Time to call 911.</li></ul><p>If you think that you or someone else is having a stroke, call 911 right away. Every minute counts during a stroke.</p>How are strokes diagnosed?<p>To make a diagnosis, your health care provider will</p><ul><li>Ask about your symptoms and medical history</li><li>Do a physical exam, including a check of
	<ul><li>Your mental alertness</li><li>Your coordination and balance</li><li>Any numbness or weakness in your face, arms, and legs</li><li>Any trouble speaking and seeing clearly</li></ul>
</li><li>Run some tests, which may include
	<ul><li>Diagnostic imaging of the brain, such as a CT scan or MRI.</li><li><span class="qt0">Heart</span> tests, which can help detect <span class="qt0">heart</span> problems or blood clots that may have led to a stroke. Possible tests include an electrocardiogram (EKG) and an echocardiography.</li></ul>
</li></ul>What are the treatments for stroke?<p>Treatments for stroke include medicines, surgery, and rehabilitation. Which treatments you get depend on the type of stroke and the stage of treatment. The different stages are:</p><ul><li>Acute treatment, to try to stop a stroke while it is happening</li><li>Post-stroke rehabilitation, to overcome the disabilities caused by the stroke</li><li>Prevention, to prevent a first stroke or, if you have already had one, prevent another stroke</li></ul><p>Acute treatments for ischemic stroke are usually medicines:</p><ul><li>You may get tPA, (tissue plasminogen activator), a medicine to dissolve the blood clot. You can only get this medicine within 4 hours of when your symptoms started. The sooner you can get it, the better your chance of recovery.</li><li>If you cannot get that medicine, you may get medicine that helps stop platelets from clumping together to form blood clots. Or you may get a blood thinner to keep existing clots from getting bigger.</li><li>If you have carotid artery <span class="qt1">disease</span>, you may also need a procedure to open your blocked carotid artery.</li></ul><p>Acute treatments for hemorrhagic stroke focus on stopping the bleeding. The first step is to find the cause of bleeding in the brain. The next step is to control it:</p><ul><li>If high blood pressure is the cause of bleeding, you may be given blood pressure medicines.</li><li>If an aneurysm is the cause, you may need aneurysm clipping or coil embolization. These are surgeries to prevent further leaking of blood from the aneurysm. It also can help prevent the aneurysm from bursting again.</li><li>If an arteriovenous malformation (AVM) is the cause of a stroke, you may need an AVM repair. An AVM is a tangle of faulty arteries and veins that can rupture within the brain. An AVM repair may be done through</li><ul><li>Surgery</li><li>Injecting a substance into the blood vessels of the AVM to block blood flow</li><li>Radiation to shrink the blood vessels of the AVM</li></ul></ul><p>Stroke rehabilitation can help you relearn skills you lost because of the damage. The goal is to help you become as independent as possible and to have the best possible quality of life.</p><p>Prevention of another stroke is also important, since having a stroke increases the risk of getting another one. Prevention may include <span class="qt0">heart</span>-healthy lifestyle changes and medicines.</p>Can strokes be prevented?<p>If you have already had a stroke or are at risk of having a stroke, you can make some <span class="qt0">heart</span>-healthy lifestyle changes to try to prevent a future stroke:</p><ul><li>Eating a <span class="qt0">heart</span>-healthy diet</li><li>Aiming for a healthy weight</li><li>Managing stress</li><li>Getting regular physical activity</li><li>Quitting smoking</li><li>Managing your blood pressure and cholesterol levels</li></ul><p>If these changes aren't enough, you may need medicine to control your risk factors.</p><p>NIH: National Institute of Neurological Disorders and Stroke</p></content>

    <document rank="11" url="https://medlineplus.gov/suddencardiacarrest.html">
      <content name="title">Sudden <span class="qt0">Cardiac Arrest</span></content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Automated External Defibrillators</content>
      <content name="altTitle"><span class="qt0">Cardiac Arrest</span></content>
      <content name="altTitle">SCA</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Sudden <span class="qt0">Cardiac</span> Death</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is sudden <span class="qt0">cardiac arrest</span> (SCA)?<p>Sudden <span class="qt0">cardiac arrest</span> (SCA) is a condition in which the <span class="qt0">heart</span> suddenly stops beating. When that happens, blood stops flowing to the brain and other vital organs. If it is not treated, SCA usually causes death within minutes. But quick treatment with a defibrillator may be lifesaving.</p>How is sudden <span class="qt0">cardiac arrest</span> (SCA) different from a <span class="qt0">heart</span> attack?<p>A <span class="qt0">heart</span> attack is different from an SCA. A <span class="qt0">heart</span> attack happens when blood flow to the <span class="qt0">heart</span> is blocked. During a <span class="qt0">heart</span> attack, the <span class="qt0">heart</span> usually doesn't suddenly stop beating. With an SCA, the <span class="qt0">heart</span> stops beating.</p><p>Sometimes an SCA can happen after or during recovery from a <span class="qt0">heart</span> attack.</p>What causes sudden <span class="qt0">cardiac arrest</span> (SCA)?<p>Your <span class="qt0">heart</span> has an electrical system that controls the rate and rhythm of your heartbeat.  An SCA can happen when the <span class="qt0">heart</span>'s electrical system is not working right and causes irregular heartbeats. Irregular heartbeats are called arrhythmias. There are different types. They may cause the <span class="qt0">heart</span> to beat too fast, too slow, or with an irregular rhythm. Some can cause the <span class="qt0">heart</span> to stop pumping blood to the body; this is the type that causes SCA.</p><p>Certain <span class="qt1">diseases</span> and conditions can cause the electrical problems that lead to SCA. They include:</p><ul><li>Ventricular fibrillation, a type of arrhythmia where the ventricles (the <span class="qt0">heart</span>'s lower chambers) don't beat normally. Instead, they beat very fast and very irregularly. They can't pump blood to the body. This causes most SCAs.</li><li><span class="qt0">Coronary</span> artery <span class="qt1">disease</span> (CAD), also called ischemic <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span>. CAD happens when the arteries of the <span class="qt0">heart</span> cannot deliver enough oxygen-rich blood to the <span class="qt0">heart</span>. It is often caused by the buildup of plaque, a waxy substance, inside the lining of larger <span class="qt0">coronary</span> arteries. The plaque blocks some or all of the blood flow to the <span class="qt0">heart</span>.</li><li>Some types of physical stress can cause your <span class="qt0">heart</span>'s electrical system to fail, such as
	<ul><li>Intense physical activity in which your body releases the hormone adrenaline. This hormone can trigger SCA in people who have <span class="qt0">heart</span> problems.</li><li>Very low blood levels of potassium or magnesium. These minerals play an important role in your <span class="qt0">heart</span>'s electrical system.</li><li>Major blood loss</li><li>Severe lack of oxygen</li></ul>
</li><li>Certain inherited disorders which can cause arrhythmias or problems with the structure of your <span class="qt0">heart</span></li><li>Structural changes in the <span class="qt0">heart</span>, such as an enlarged <span class="qt0">heart</span> due to high blood pressure or advanced <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span><span class="qt2">. </span><span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">Heart</span></span> infections can also cause changes to the structure of the <span class="qt0">heart</span>.</li></ul>Who is at risk for sudden <span class="qt0">cardiac arrest</span> (SCA)?<p>You are at higher risk for SCA if you:</p><ul><li>Have <span class="qt0">coronary</span> artery <span class="qt1">disease</span> (CAD). Most people with SCA have CAD. But CAD usually doesn't cause symptoms, so they may not know that they have it.</li><li>Are older; your risk increases with age</li><li>Are a man; it is more common in men than women</li><li>Are Black or African American, especially if you have other conditions such as diabetes, high blood pressure, <span class="qt0">heart</span> failure, or chronic kidney <span class="qt1">disease</span></li><li>Have a personal history of heartbeats that aren't regular (arrhythmia)</li><li>Have a personal or family history of SCA or inherited disorders that can cause arrhythmia</li><li>Have a problem with drug or alcohol use</li><li>Have had a <span class="qt0">heart</span> attack</li><li>Have <span class="qt0">heart</span> failure</li></ul>What are the symptoms of sudden <span class="qt0">cardiac arrest</span> (SCA)?<p>Usually, the first sign of SCA is loss of consciousness (fainting). This happens when the <span class="qt0">heart</span> stops beating.</p><p>Some people may have a racing heartbeat or feel dizzy or light-headed just before they faint. And sometimes people have chest pain, shortness of breath, nausea, or vomiting in the hour before they have an SCA.</p>How is sudden <span class="qt0">cardiac arrest</span> (SCA) diagnosed?<p>SCA happens without warning and requires emergency treatment. Health care providers rarely diagnose SCA with medical tests as it's happening. Instead, it is usually diagnosed after it happens. Providers do this by ruling out other causes of a person's sudden collapse.</p><p>If you are at high risk for SCA, your provider may refer you to a cardiologist, a doctor who specializes in <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">diseases</span></span>. The cardiologist may ask you to get various <span class="qt0">heart</span> health tests to see how well you <span class="qt0">heart</span> is working. He or she will work with you to decide whether you need treatment to prevent SCA.</p>What are the treatments for sudden <span class="qt0">cardiac arrest</span> (SCA)?<p>SCA is an emergency. A person having SCA needs to be treated with a defibrillator right away. A defibrillator is a device sends an electric shock to the <span class="qt0">heart</span>. The electric shock can restore a normal rhythm to a <span class="qt0">heart</span> that's stopped beating. To work well, it needs to be done within minutes of the SCA.</p><p>Most police officers, emergency medical technicians, and other first responders are trained and equipped to use a defibrillator. Call 9-1-1 right away if someone has signs or symptoms of SCA. The sooner you call for help, the sooner lifesaving treatment can begin.</p>What should I do if I think that someone has had an SCA?<p>Many public places such as schools, businesses, and airports have automated external defibrillators (AEDs). AEDs are special defibrillators that untrained people can use if they think that someone has had SCA. AEDS are programmed to give an electric shock if they detect a dangerous arrhythmia. This prevents giving a shock to someone who may have fainted but isn't having SCA.</p><p>If you see someone who you think has had SCA, you should give cardiopulmonary resuscitation (CPR) until defibrillation can be done.</p><p>People who are at risk for SCA may want to consider having an AED at home. Ask your cardiologist to help you decide whether having an AED in your home might help you.</p>What are the treatments after surviving sudden <span class="qt0">cardiac arrest</span> (SCA)?<p>If you survive SCA, you'll likely be admitted to a hospital for ongoing care and treatment. In the hospital, your medical team will closely watch your <span class="qt0">heart</span>. They may give you medicines to try to reduce the risk of another SCA.</p><p>They will also try to find out what caused your SCA. If you're diagnosed with <span class="qt0">coronary</span> artery <span class="qt1">disease</span>, you may have an angioplasty or <span class="qt0">coronary</span> artery bypass surgery. These procedures help restore blood flow through narrowed or blocked <span class="qt0">coronary</span> arteries.</p><p>Often, people who have had SCA get a device called an implantable cardioverter defibrillator (ICD). This small device is surgically placed under the skin in your chest or abdomen. An ICD uses electric pulses or shocks to help control dangerous arrhythmias.</p>Can sudden <span class="qt0">cardiac arrest</span> (SCA) be prevented?<p>You may be able to lower your risk of SCA by following a <span class="qt0">heart</span>-healthy lifestyle. If you have <span class="qt0">coronary</span> artery <span class="qt1">disease</span> or another <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span>, treating that <span class="qt1">disease</span> can also lower your risk of SCA. If you have had an SCA, getting an implantable cardioverter defibrillator (ICD) can lower your chance of having another SCA.</p><p>NIH: National <span class="qt0">Heart</span>, Lung, and Blood Institute</p></content>
      <content name="mesh"><span class="qt0">Heart</span> Arrest</content>
      <content name="groupName">Blood, <span class="qt0">Heart</span> and Circulation</content>
      <content name="snippet">What is sudden <span class="qt0">cardiac arrest</span> (SCA)? Sudden <span class="qt0">cardiac arrest</span> (SCA) is a condition in which the <span class="qt0">heart</span> suddenly stops beating. When that happens, blood stops  ... </content>

    <document rank="12" url="https://medlineplus.gov/benefitsofexercise.html">
      <content name="title">Benefits of Exercise</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Exercise Benefits</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>We have heard it many times before -- regular exercise is good for you, and it can help you manage your weight. But if you are like many people, you are busy, you sit most of the day at your job, and you haven't yet changed your exercise habits. The good news is that it's never too late to start. You can start slowly and find ways to fit more physical activity into your life. To get the most benefit, you should try to get the recommended amount of physical activity for your age. If you can do that, the payoff is that you will feel better, may help prevent or control many <span class="qt1">diseases</span>, and will likely even live longer.</p>What are the health benefits of exercise?<p>Regular exercise and physical activity may:</p><ul><li>Help you get to and stay at a healthy weight. Along with diet, exercise plays an important role in maintaining a healthy weight and preventing obesity. If you are at a healthy weight, you can maintain it if the calories you eat and drink are equal to the amount of energy you burn. To lose weight, you need to use more calories than you eat and drink.</li><li>Reduce your risk of <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">diseases</span></span>. Exercise strengthens your <span class="qt0">heart</span> and improves your circulation. The increased blood flow raises the oxygen levels in your body. This helps lower your risk of <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">diseases</span></span> such as <span class="qt0">coronary</span> artery <span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span><span class="qt2"> and </span><span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span> attack. Regular exercise can also lower high blood pressure, cholesterol, and triglyceride levels, all of which are risk factors for <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span>.</li><li>Help your body manage blood glucose (blood sugar) and insulin levels. Exercise can lower your blood glucose levels and help your insulin work better. This can reduce your risk of metabolic syndrome and type 2 diabetes. And if you already have one of these <span class="qt1">diseases</span>, exercise can help you manage it.</li><li>Help you manage a chronic health condition. For example, regular physical activity may help reduce pain and improve function in adults with arthritis. It can also help support daily living activities for people with disabilities. This may help them be more independent.</li><li>Help you quit smoking. Exercise may make it easier to quit smoking by reducing your cravings and withdrawal symptoms. It can also help limit the weight you might gain when you stop smoking.</li><li>Improve your mental health and mood. During exercise, your body releases chemicals that can improve your mood and make you feel more relaxed. This can help you deal with stress, manage anxiety, and reduce your risk of depression.</li><li>Help keep your thinking, learning, and judgment skills sharp as you age. Exercise stimulates your body to release proteins and other chemicals that improve the structure and function of your brain.</li><li>Strengthen your bones and muscles. Regular exercise can help kids and teens build strong bones. Later in life, it can also slow the loss of bone density that comes with age. Doing muscle-strengthening activities can help you increase or maintain your muscle mass and strength. For older adults, this can mean staying independent, like being able to get in and out of a chair or bed without help.</li><li>Reduce your risk of some cancers, including colon, breast, uterine, bladder, esophageal, kidney, stomach, and lung cancer. There are different ways that physical activity may lower the risk of these cancers. For example, it can strengthen your immune system and reduce inflammation in your body. It also helps prevent obesity, which is a risk factor for many cancers.</li><li>Reduce your risk of falls. For older adults, doing balance and muscle-strengthening activities can help reduce your risk of falling.</li><li>Improve your sleep. Exercise can help you fall asleep faster and stay asleep longer.</li><li>Boost your energy. Exercise helps your <span class="qt0">heart</span> and lungs work better, which can leave you feeling more energetic throughout the day.</li><li>Improve your sexual health. Regular exercise may lower the risk of erectile dysfunction (ED) in men. For those who already have ED, exercise may help improve their sexual function. In women, exercise may increase sexual arousal.</li><li>Increase your chances of living longer. Studies show that physical activity can reduce your risk of dying early from the leading causes of death, like <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span> and certain cancers.</li></ul>How can I make exercise a part of my regular routine?<p>Here are some tips to help you make exercise part of your daily routine:</p><ul><li>Make everyday activities more active. Even small changes can help. You can take the stairs instead of the elevator. Walk down the hall to a coworker's office instead of sending an email. Wash the car yourself. Park further away from your destination.</li><li>Be active with friends and family. Having a workout partner may make you more likely to enjoy exercise. You can also plan social activities that involve exercise. Consider joining an exercise group or class, such as a dance class, hiking club, or volleyball team.</li><li>Keep track of your progress. Keeping a log of your activity or using a fitness tracker may help you set goals and stay motivated.</li><li>Make exercise more fun. Try listening to music or watching TV while you exercise. Also, mix things up a little bit - if you stick with just one type of exercise, you might get bored. Try doing a combination of activities.</li><li>Find activities that you can do even when the weather is bad. You can walk in a mall, climb stairs, or work out in a gym even if the weather stops you from exercising outside.</li><li>Remember that some physical activity is better than none.  Do what you can! Even 5 minutes of physical activity has real health benefits.</li></ul></content>
      <content name="mesh">Exercise</content>
      <content name="groupName">Wellness and Lifestyle</content>
      <content name="groupName">Fitness and Exercise</content>
      <content name="snippet"> We have heard it many times before -- regular exercise is good for you, and it can help you manage your weight. But if you are like many people, you are busy,  ... </content>

    <document rank="13" url="https://medlineplus.gov/pulmonaryhypertension.html">
      <content name="title">Pulmonary Hypertension</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Cor Pulmonale</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Hypertension, Pulmonary</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Primary Pulmonary Hypertension</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Pulmonary Arterial Hypertension</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is pulmonary hypertension? <p>Pulmonary hypertension, sometimes called PH, is a serious condition that affects the blood vessels in the lungs. It develops when the blood pressure in your lungs is higher than normal.</p><p>Your <span class="qt0">heart</span> pumps blood to your lungs so they can add oxygen to the blood. The blood goes back to your <span class="qt0">heart</span>. From there, it travels to the rest of your body so that your tissues can get the oxygen that they need.</p><p>The blood moves from your <span class="qt0">heart</span> to your lungs through blood vessels called pulmonary arteries. If the pulmonary arteries become damaged, narrowed, or blocked, the blood does not flow through them as well. This can increase the blood pressure in the arteries and cause pulmonary hypertension.</p><p>There are different types of pulmonary hypertension, including pulmonary arterial hypertension (PAH). The different types are based on what caused the <span class="qt1">disease</span>.</p>What causes pulmonary hypertension?<p>Pulmonary hypertension can develop on its own or be caused by another condition. Sometimes the cause is unknown or is not clear.</p><p>Some of the possible causes include:</p><ul><li><span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">Heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">diseases</span></span>, including left-sided <span class="qt0">heart</span> failure and congenital <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span></li><li>Lung <span class="qt1">diseases</span> such as <span class="qt1">COPD</span> (chronic obstructive pulmonary <span class="qt1">disease</span>), interstitial lung <span class="qt1">disease</span>, emphysema, and sleep apnea</li><li>Other medical conditions such as:
  <ul><li>Liver <span class="qt1">diseases</span></li><li>Sickle cell <span class="qt1">disease</span></li><li>Pulmonary embolism (blood clots in the lungs)</li><li>Connective tissue disorders like scleroderma</li></ul>
</li></ul>Who is more likely to develop pulmonary hypertension? <p>Certain factors can make you more likely to develop pulmonary hypertension, such as:</p><ul><li>Your age. The risk increases as you get older. The condition is usually diagnosed between ages 30 and 60.</li><li>Your environment. Being exposed to asbestos or having certain infections caused by parasites can raise your risk.</li><li>Your family history and genetics. Certain genetic disorders, such as Down syndrome, congenital <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span>, and Gaucher <span class="qt1">disease</span>, can raise your risk of pulmonary hypertension. So can a family history of blood clots.</li><li>Your lifestyle habits.Smoking and illegal drug use can raise your risk of developing pulmonary hypertension.</li><li>Certain medicines. For example, some medicines used to treat cancer and depression can make you more likely to develop pulmonary hypertension.</li><li>Your sex. Pulmonary hypertension is more common in women than in men.</li></ul>What are the symptoms of pulmonary hypertension?<p>The symptoms of pulmonary hypertension are sometimes hard to recognize and are similar to the symptoms of other medical conditions. So sometimes it can take years for someone to get diagnosed with pulmonary hypertension.</p><p>The symptoms of pulmonary hypertension may include:</p><ul><li>Shortness of breath</li><li>Chest pain or pressure</li><li>Dizziness that may lead to fainting</li><li>Fatigue</li><li>Swelling of the abdomen, legs, or feet</li><li><span class="qt0">Heart</span> palpitations (the feeling that your <span class="qt0">heart</span> is pounding or beating too fast)</li></ul>What other problems can pulmonary hypertension cause?<p>Pulmonary hypertension can get worse over time and lead to serious problems, including:</p><ul><li>Anemia, which can cause your body to not get enough oxygen-rich blood</li><li>Arrhythmias, problems with the rate or rhythm of your heartbeat</li><li>Blood clots in the pulmonary arteries</li><li>Bleeding in the lungs</li><li><span class="qt0">Heart</span> failure</li><li>Liver damage</li><li>Pericardial effusion, a collection of fluid around the <span class="qt0">heart</span></li><li>Serious pregnancy complications</li></ul>How is pulmonary hypertension diagnosed?<p>To find out if you have pulmonary hypertension, your health care provider:</p><ul><li>Will ask about your medical history and symptoms</li><li>Will do a physical exam, which may include checking your blood oxygen, listening to your <span class="qt0">heart</span> and lungs, and checking your blood pressure</li><li>Will likely order some tests, such as:
  <ul><li>Blood tests to look for blood clots, stress on the <span class="qt0">heart</span>, or anemia</li><li><span class="qt0">Heart</span> imaging tests, such as a <span class="qt0">cardiac</span> MRI</li><li>Lung imaging tests, such as chest x-ray</li><li>An electrocardiogram (ECG or EKG)</li></ul>
</li></ul>What are the treatments for pulmonary hypertension?<p>Often there is no cure for pulmonary hypertension, but treatments can help manage your symptoms. Your provider will work with you to come up with a treatment plan. It will be based on your needs and the cause of the pulmonary hypertension. The plan may include:</p><ul><li>Healthy lifestyle changes, such as:
  <ul><li>Healthy eating, which includes eating less salt</li><li>Regular physical activity, which may be done through pulmonary rehabilitation</li></ul>
</li><li>Medicines, such as:
  <ul><li>Blood thinners</li><li>Medicines to control the rate blood is pumped throughout the body</li><li>Medicines to relax blood vessels and allow the blood to flow better</li><li>Medicine to reduce swelling (diuretics)</li></ul>
</li><li>Oxygen therapy</li><li>Procedures to reduce pressure in the <span class="qt0">heart</span> or pulmonary artery</li><li>In some severe cases, a lung transplant</li></ul><p>NIH: National <span class="qt0">Heart</span>, Lung, and Blood Institute</p></content>
      <content name="mesh">Hypertension, Pulmonary</content>

    <document rank="14" url="https://medlineplus.gov/howtolowercholesterolwithdiet.html">
      <content name="title">How to Lower Cholesterol with Diet</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Low Cholesterol Diet</content>
      <content name="altTitle">TLC Diet</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Therapeutic Lifestyle Changes Diet</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is cholesterol?<p>Cholesterol is a waxy, fat-like substance that's found in all the cells in your body. Your body needs some cholesterol to work properly. But if you have too much of it in your blood, it can stick to the walls of your arteries and narrow or even block them. This puts you at risk for <span class="qt0">coronary</span> artery <span class="qt1">disease</span> and other <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">diseases</span></span>.</p><p>Cholesterol is made by your liver. It travels through the blood on proteins called lipoproteins. One type of lipoprotein, LDL, is sometimes called the "bad" cholesterol. A high LDL level leads to a buildup of cholesterol in your arteries. Another type, HDL, is sometimes called the "good" cholesterol. It carries cholesterol from other parts of your body back to your liver. Then your liver removes the cholesterol from your body.</p>What are the treatments for high cholesterol?<p>The main treatments for high cholesterol are <span class="qt0">heart</span>-healthy lifestyle changes and medicines. The lifestyle changes include healthy eating, weight management, and regular physical activity.</p>How can I lower cholesterol with diet?<p><span class="qt0">Heart</span>-healthy lifestyle changes include a diet to lower your cholesterol. The DASH eating plan is one example. Another is the Therapeutic Lifestyle Changes (TLC) diet, which recommends that you:</p><p>Choose healthier fats.You should limit both total fat and saturated fat. No more than 25 to 35% of your daily calories should come from dietary fats, and less than 7% of your daily calories should come from saturated fat. Depending upon how many calories you eat per day, here are the maximum amounts of saturated fats that you should eat:</p>Calories per DaySaturated Fat1,2008 grams1,50010 grams2,00013 grams2,50017 grams<p>Saturated fat is a bad fat because it raises your LDL (bad cholesterol) level more than anything else in your diet. It is found in some meats, dairy products, chocolate, baked goods, and deep-fried and processed foods.</p><p>Instead of saturated fats, switch to foods with healthier fats, such as lean meat, nuts, and unsaturated oils like canola, olive, and safflower oils.</p><p>Limit foods with cholesterol. If you are trying to lower your cholesterol, you should have less than 200 mg a day of cholesterol. Cholesterol is in foods from animals, such as liver and other organ meats, egg yolks, shrimp, and whole milk dairy products.</p><p>Eat plenty of soluble fiber. Soluble fiber helps prevent your digestive tract from absorbing cholesterol. You should try to get 10 to 25 grams of it per day. Foods that are high in soluble fiber include:</p><ul><li>Whole-grain cereals such as oatmeal and oat bran</li><li>Fruits such as apples, bananas, oranges, pears, and prunes</li><li>Legumes such as kidney beans, lentils, chickpeas, black-eyed peas, and lima beans</li></ul><p>Eat lots of fruits and vegetables. They are important sources of vitamins, fiber, and other nutrients.</p><p>Increase plant stanols and sterols in your diet.These are substances that also help prevent your digestive tract from absorbing cholesterol. You should try to get 2 grams of them per day. They can be found in whole grains, nuts, legumes, and oils, such as olive oil and avocado oil. They are also added to some foods, such as certain types of orange juice, margarine, and bread.</p><p>Eat foods that are high in omega-3 fatty acids. These special fats can lower LDL and triglyceride levels. They can also help control blood pressure and may lower your risk of <span class="qt0">heart</span> rhythm problems. If you have <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span>, they may lower your risk of <span class="qt0">heart</span> attack.  Omega-3 fatty acids can be found in:</p><ul><li>Fish such as salmon, tuna (canned or fresh), and mackerel. Most people should try to eat these fish two times a week. But if you are pregnant or breastfeeding, you should avoid fish that could be high in mercury, such as mackerel.</li><li>Plant sources such as walnuts, flaxseed, chia seeds, and canola and soybean oils. But they have smaller amounts of omega-3 acids than the fish sources.</li><li>Fortified foods, such as certain brands of eggs, yogurt, juices, milk, soy beverages, and infant formulas.</li></ul><p>Limit salt. You should try to limit the amount of sodium (salt) that you eat to no more than 2,300 milligrams (about 1 teaspoon of salt) a day. That includes all the sodium you eat, whether it's added in cooking, used at the table, or already present in food products. Limiting salt won't lower your cholesterol, but it can lower your risk of <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">diseases</span></span> by helping to lower your blood pressure. You can reduce your sodium by instead choosing low-salt and "no added salt" foods and seasonings at the table or while cooking.</p><p>Limit alcohol. Alcohol adds extra calories, which can lead to weight gain. Being overweight can raise your LDL level and lower your HDL level. Too much alcohol can also increase your risk of <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">diseases</span></span> because it can raise your blood pressure and triglyceride level. It's best not to drink, but if you do: </p><ul><li>Have no more than 2 drinks per day if you are a man</li><li>Have no more than 1 drink per day if you are a woman</li></ul><p>Nutrition labels can help you figure out how much fat, saturated fat, cholesterol, fiber, and sodium is in the foods that you buy. This can help you eat healthier and lower your cholesterol.</p><p>NIH: National <span class="qt0">Heart</span>, Lung, and Blood Institute</p></content>
      <content name="mesh">Hypercholesterolemia</content>
      <content name="groupName">Blood, <span class="qt0">Heart</span> and Circulation</content>
      <content name="groupName">Food and Nutrition</content>
      <content name="snippet"> ... eat healthier and lower your cholesterol. NIH: National <span class="qt0">Heart</span>, Lung, and Blood Institute</content>

    <document rank="15" url="https://medlineplus.gov/howtolowercholesterol.html">
      <content name="title">How to Lower Cholesterol</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is cholesterol?<p>Cholesterol is a waxy, fat-like substance that's found in all the cells in your body. Your body needs some cholesterol to work properly.  But if you have too much of it in your blood, it can stick to the walls of your arteries and narrow or even block them. This puts you at risk for <span class="qt0">coronary</span> artery <span class="qt1">disease</span> and other <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">diseases</span></span>.</p><p>Cholesterol is made by your liver. It travels through the blood on proteins called lipoproteins. One type of lipoprotein, LDL, is sometimes called the "bad" cholesterol. A high LDL level leads to a buildup of cholesterol in your arteries. Another type, HDL, is sometimes called the "good" cholesterol. It carries cholesterol from other parts of your body back to your liver. Then your liver removes the cholesterol from your body.</p><p>There are steps that you can take to lower your LDL (bad) cholesterol and raise your HDL (good) cholesterol. By keeping your cholesterol levels in range, you can lower your risk of <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">diseases</span></span>.</p>What are the main treatments for high cholesterol?<p>The main treatments for high cholesterol are lifestyle changes and medicines.</p>Lifestyle changes to lower cholesterol<p><span class="qt0">Heart</span>-healthy lifestyle changes that can help you lower or control your cholesterol include:</p><ul><li><span class="qt0">Heart</span>-healthy eating. A <span class="qt0">heart</span>-healthy eating plan limits the amount of saturated and trans fats that you eat. It recommends that you eat and drink only enough calories to stay at a healthy weight and avoid weight gain. It also encourages you to choose a variety of nutritious foods, including fruits, vegetables, whole grains, and lean meats. Examples of eating plans that can lower your cholesterol include the Therapeutic Lifestyle Changes diet and the DASH eating plan. </li><li>Aiming for a healthy weight. If you are overweight or have obesity, losing weight can help lower your LDL cholesterol and raise your HDL cholesterol. This is especially important for people with metabolic syndrome. Metabolic syndrome is a group of conditions that together raise your risk of <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">diseases</span></span> and other health problems.</li><li>Getting regular physical activity. Studies have shown that physical activity can lower LDL cholesterol and triglycerides and raise your HDL cholesterol.  Before you start an exercise program, ask your health care provider what level of physical activity is right for you.</li><li>Managing stress. Research has shown that chronic stress can sometimes raise your LDL cholesterol and lower your HDL cholesterol.</li><li>Quitting smoking. Quitting smoking can raise your HDL cholesterol. Since HDL helps to remove LDL cholesterol from your arteries, having more HDL can help to lower your LDL cholesterol.</li><li>Getting enough good-quality sleep. Getting 7 to 9 hours of sleep every night lowers your risk of high LDL  cholesterol and total cholesterol levels.</li></ul>Medicines to lower cholesterol<p>For some people, making lifestyle changes alone does not their lower cholesterol enough. They may also need to take cholesterol medicines. There are several types. They work in different ways and can have different side effects. If your provider recommends that you take cholesterol medicines, ask them which medicine would be right for you.</p><p>Even if you take medicines to lower your cholesterol, you still need to continue with lifestyle changes.</p>What are some other possible treatments for high cholesterol?<p>Some other possible treatments for high cholesterol may include:</p>Lipoprotein apheresis to lower cholesterol<p>Familial hypercholesterolemia (FH) is an inherited condition that causes very high levels of cholesterol in the blood. Some people who have FH may get a treatment called lipoprotein apheresis. This treatment uses a filtering machine to remove LDL cholesterol from your blood. Then the machine returns the rest of your blood back to your body.</p>Supplements to lower cholesterol<p>Many companies sell supplements that they say can lower cholesterol. For many of these supplements, there isn't enough evidence to show that they can lower cholesterol levels. But for some supplements, research has found that they have cholesterol-lowering effects. For example:</p><ul><li>Plant stanols and sterols supplements, when taken with meals, can reduce cholesterol levels.</li><li>Some soy products have a small cholesterol-lowering effect. But research has found that eating soy foods gives you more of a benefit than taking supplements.</li><li>Whole flaxseed and flaxseed lignans may help to lower cholesterol levels. However, flaxseed oil does not.</li><li>Garlic supplements may lower cholesterol levels, but the effect is small when compared to the effects of cholesterol medicines.</li></ul><p>Supplements may cause side effects and interactions with medicines. Always check with your provider before you take any supplements.</p></content>

    <document rank="16" url="https://medlineplus.gov/cholesterollevelswhatyouneedtoknow.html">
      <content name="title">Cholesterol Levels: What You Need to Know</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Familial Combined Hyperlipidemia</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Familial Dysbetalipoproteinemia</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Familial Hypercholesterolemia</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Hypercholesterolemia</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Hyperlipidemia</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is cholesterol?<p>Cholesterol is a waxy, fat-like substance that's found in all the cells in your body. Your body needs some cholesterol to work properly.  But if you have too much of it in your blood, it can stick to the walls of your arteries and narrow or even block them. This puts you at risk of <span class="qt0">coronary</span> artery <span class="qt1">disease</span> and other <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">diseases</span></span>.</p>How do you measure cholesterol levels?<p>A blood test called a lipoprotein or lipid panel can measure your cholesterol levels. Before the test, you'll need to fast (not eat or drink anything but water) for 9 to 12 hours. The test gives information about your:</p><ul><li>Total cholesterol. This is a measure of the total amount of cholesterol in your blood. It includes both low-density lipoprotein (LDL) cholesterol and high-density lipoprotein (HDL) cholesterol.</li><li>LDL cholesterol. LDL is often called "bad" cholesterol because it is the main source of cholesterol buildup and blockage in the arteries.</li><li>HDL cholesterol. HDL is often called "good" cholesterol because it helps remove cholesterol from your arteries.</li><li>Non-HDL. This number is your total cholesterol minus your HDL. Your non-HDL includes LDL and other types of cholesterol such as VLDL (very-low-density lipoprotein).</li><li>Triglycerides. This is another type of fat in your blood that can raise the risk of <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span>, especially in women.</li></ul>What do my cholesterol numbers mean?<p>Cholesterol numbers are measured in milligrams per deciliter (mg/dL). Talk with your healthcare provider about what your cholesterol numbers should be. The numbers that are best for you may depend on your age, race, blood pressure, weight, family history, and more. However, here are some general guidelines showing the desirable levels (levels that are healthy for most people):</p><p>Anyone age 19 or younger:</p>Type of CholesterolHealthy LevelTotal CholesterolLess than 170 mg/dLNon-HDLLess than 120 mg/dLLDLLess than 110 mg/dLHDLMore than 45 mg/dL<p>Men age 20 or older:</p>Type of CholesterolHealthy LevelTotal CholesterolLess than 200 mg/dLNon-HDLLess than 130 mg/dLLDLLess than 100 mg/dLHDLGreater than or equal to 60 mg/dL is best. Levels less than 40 mg/dL are considered low.<p>Women age 20 or older:</p>Type of CholesterolHealthy LevelTotal CholesterolLess than 200 mg/dLNon-HDLLess than 130 mg/dLLDLLess than 100 mg/dLHDLGreater than or equal to 60 mg/dL is best. Levels less than 50 mg/dL are considered low.<p>Triglycerides are not a type of cholesterol, but they are also measured in this test. A normal triglyceride level is below 150 mg/dL. You might need treatment if you have triglyceride levels that are borderline high (150-199 mg/dL) or high (200 mg/dL or more).</p>How often should I get a cholesterol test?<p>When and how often you should get a cholesterol test depends on your age, risk factors, and family history. The general recommendations are:</p><p>For people who are age 19 or younger:</p><ul><li>The first test should be between ages 9 to 11</li><li>Children should have the test again every 5 years</li><li>Some children may have this test starting at age 2 if there is a family history of high cholesterol, <span class="qt0">heart</span> attack, or stroke</li></ul><p>For people who are ages 20 to 65:</p><ul><li>Younger adults should have the test every 5 years</li><li>Men ages 45 to 65 and women ages 55 to 65 should have it every 1 to 2 years</li></ul><p>For people older than 65::</p><ul><li>They should be tested every year</li></ul>What affects my cholesterol levels?<p>Many factors can affect your cholesterol levels. Some of them are things you can change:</p><ul><li>Diet. Saturated fat and cholesterol in the food you eat make your blood cholesterol level rise. Saturated fat is the main problem, but cholesterol in foods also matters. Reducing the amount of saturated fat in your diet helps lower your blood cholesterol level. Foods that have high levels of saturated fats include red meats, full-fat dairy products, chocolate, some baked goods, and deep-fried and processed foods.</li><li>Weight. Being overweight or having obesity are risk factors for <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span>. They also tend to increase your cholesterol</li><li>Lack of physical activity. Not being physically active is a risk factor for <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span>.</li><li>Smoking. Cigarette smoking lowers your HDL cholesterol. Since HDL helps to remove cholesterol from your arteries, having a lower HDL level can contribute to a higher cholesterol level. </li></ul><p>Some factors that you cannot change can also affect cholesterol levels, such as:</p><ul><li>Age. Your cholesterol levels tend to rise as you get older. Even though it is less common, younger people, including children and teens, can also have high cholesterol.</li><li>Sex. Between ages 20 and 39, men have a greater risk of high total cholesterol than women. But after menopause, a woman's risk goes up. This happens because menopause lowers levels of female hormones that may protect against high blood cholesterol.</li><li>Family history. Your genes partly determine how much cholesterol your body makes. High cholesterol can run in families.</li><li>Race or ethnicity. People from certain racial or ethnic groups may have an increased risk of high cholesterol. For example, Asian Americans are more likely to have high levels of LDL cholesterol than other groups. And non-Hispanic White people are more likely than other groups to have high levels of total cholesterol. </li></ul>How can I lower my cholesterol?<p>You can lower your cholesterol through <span class="qt0">heart</span>-healthy lifestyle changes. They include a <span class="qt0">heart</span>-healthy eating plan, weight management, and regular physical activity.</p><p>If the lifestyle changes alone do not lower your cholesterol enough, you may also need to take medicines. There are several types of cholesterol-lowering medicines available, including statins. If you take medicines to lower your cholesterol, you still should continue with the lifestyle changes.</p><p>NIH: National <span class="qt0">Heart</span>, Lung, and Blood Institute</p></content>
      <content name="mesh">Cholesterol</content>
      <content name="mesh">Hypercholesterolemia</content>
      <content name="groupName">Blood, <span class="qt0">Heart</span> and Circulation</content>
      <content name="groupName">Food and Nutrition</content>
      <content name="snippet"> ... should continue with the lifestyle changes. NIH: National <span class="qt0">Heart</span>, Lung, and Blood Institute</content>

    <document rank="17" url="https://medlineplus.gov/metabolicsyndrome.html">
      <content name="title">Metabolic Syndrome</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Insulin Resistance</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Syndrome X (Metabolic)</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Insulin resistance syndrome</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Metabolic syndrome X</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is metabolic syndrome?<p>Metabolic syndrome is the name for a group of risk factors for <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span>, diabetes, and other health problems. You can have just one risk factor, but people often have several of them together. When you have at least three of them, it is called metabolic syndrome. These risk factors include:</p><ul><li>A large waistline, also called abdominal obesity or "having an apple shape." Too much fat around the stomach is a greater risk factor for <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span> than too much fat in other parts of the body.</li><li>Having a high triglyceride level. Triglycerides are a type of fat found in the blood.</li><li>Having a low HDL cholesterol level. HDL is sometimes called the "good" cholesterol because it helps remove cholesterol from your arteries.</li><li>Having high blood pressure. If your blood pressure stays high over time, it can damage your <span class="qt0">heart</span> and lead to other health problems.</li><li>Having a high fasting blood sugar. Mildly high blood sugar may be an early sign of diabetes.</li></ul><p>The more factors you have, the higher your risk for <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span>, diabetes, and stroke is.</p>What causes metabolic syndrome?<p>Metabolic syndrome has several causes that act together:</p><ul><li>Overweight and obesity</li><li>An inactive lifestyle</li><li>Insulin resistance, a condition in which the body can't use insulin properly. Insulin is a hormone that helps move blood sugar into your cells to give them energy. Insulin resistance can lead to high blood sugar levels.</li><li>Age - your risk goes up as get older</li><li>Genetics - ethnicity and family history</li></ul><p>People who have metabolic syndrome often also have excessive blood clotting and inflammation throughout the body. Researchers don't know whether these conditions cause metabolic syndrome or worsen it.</p>Who is at risk for metabolic syndrome?<p>The most important risk factors for metabolic syndrome are:</p><ul><li>Abdominal obesity (a large waistline)</li><li>An inactive lifestyle</li><li>Insulin resistance</li></ul><p>There are certain groups of people who have an increased risk of metabolic syndrome:</p><ul><li>Some racial and ethnic groups. Mexican Americans have the highest rate of metabolic syndrome, followed by White and Black people.</li><li>People who have diabetes</li><li>People who have a sibling or parent who has diabetes</li><li>Women with polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS)</li><li>People who take medicines that cause weight gain or changes in blood pressure, blood cholesterol, and blood sugar levels</li></ul>What are the symptoms of metabolic syndrome?<p>Most of the metabolic risk factors have no obvious signs or symptoms, except for a large waistline.</p>How is metabolic syndrome diagnosed?<p>Your health care provider will diagnose metabolic syndrome based on the results of a physical exam and blood tests. You must have at least three of the risk factors to be diagnosed with metabolic syndrome:</p><ul><li>A large waistline, which means a waist measurement of 
		<ul><li>35 inches or more for women</li><li>40 inches or more for men</li></ul></li><li>A high triglyceride level, which is 150 mg/dL or higher</li><li>A low HDL cholesterol level, which is
		<ul><li>Less than 50 mg/dL for women</li><li>Less than 40 mg/dL for men</li></ul></li><li>High blood pressure, which is a reading of 130/85 mmHg or higher.</li><li>A high fasting blood sugar, which is 100 mg/dL or higher</li></ul>What are the treatments for metabolic syndrome?<p>The most important treatment for metabolic syndrome is a <span class="qt0">heart</span>-healthy lifestyle, which includes:</p><ul><li>A <span class="qt0">heart</span>-healthy eating plan, which limits the amount of saturated and trans fats that you eat. It encourages you to choose a variety of nutritious foods, including fruits, vegetables, whole grains, and lean meats.</li><li>Aiming for a healthy weight</li><li>Managing stress</li><li>Getting regular physical activity</li><li>Quitting smoking (or not starting if you don't already smoke)</li></ul><p>If making lifestyle changes is not enough, you may need to take medicines. For example, you may need medicines to lower cholesterol or blood pressure.</p>Can metabolic syndrome be prevented?<p>The best way to prevent metabolic syndrome is through the <span class="qt0">heart</span>-healthy lifestyle changes.</p><p>NIH: National <span class="qt0">Heart</span>, Lung, and Blood Institute</p></content>

You can have just one risk factor, but people often have several of them together. When ...  a greater risk factor for <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span> than too much fat in other parts  ... </content>
