    <document rank="18" url="https://medlineplus.gov/atrialfibrillation.html">
      <content name="title">Atrial Fibrillation</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">AF</content>
      <content name="altTitle">AFib</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is atrial fibrillation (AFib)?<p>Atrial fibrillation, also known as AFib or AF, is one of the most common types of arrhythmias.  Arrhythmias are problems with the rate or rhythm of your heartbeat. They can cause your <span class="qt0">heart</span> to beat too slowly, too fast, or in an irregular way.</p><p>If you have AFib, your <span class="qt0">heart</span> beats irregularly and sometimes much faster than normal. Also, your <span class="qt0">heart</span>'s upper and lower chambers do not work together as they should. When this happens, the lower chambers do not fill completely or pump enough blood to your lungs and body. This can cause symptoms such as dizziness, fatigue, and a pounding heartbeat.</p><p>AFib may happen in brief episodes, or it may be a permanent condition. It's very important to treat it, since AFib can put you at risk for stroke and other <span class="qt0">heart</span> conditions.</p>What causes atrial fibrillation (AFib)?<p>AFib is most often caused by changes to the <span class="qt0">heart</span>'s tissue or the electrical signaling that helps the heartbeat. These changes can happen due to different conditions and factors, such as high blood pressure, <span class="qt0">coronary</span> artery <span class="qt1">disease</span>, congenital <span class="qt0">heart</span> defects, infections, and aging. Sometimes the cause is unknown.</p>Who is more likely to develop atrial fibrillation (AFib)?<p>Anyone can develop AFib, but there are certain things that raise your risk for it:</p><ul><li>Aging. The risk of atrial fibrillation increases as you get older, especially when you are over age 65.</li><li>Family history and genetics. AFib can run in families. So can <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span>, which raises your risk of AFib.</li><li>Some lifestyle choices. Your risk is higher if you drink a lot of alcohol, use certain illegal drugs such as cocaine and methamphetamines, or smoke.</li><li>Having certain health conditions, such as:
  <ul><li>High blood pressure</li><li>Diabetes</li><li><span class="qt0">Heart</span> failure</li><li><span class="qt0">Heart</span> valve <span class="qt1">diseases</span></li><li>Obesity</li><li>Hyperthyroidism</li><li>Chronic kidney <span class="qt1">disease</span></li><li><span class="qt1">COPD</span> and other lung <span class="qt1">diseases</span></li><li>Sleep apnea</li></ul>
</li><li>Race. AFib is more common in people with European ancestry.</li><li>Recent surgery. You may be at risk of atrial fibrillation in the early days and weeks after surgery on your <span class="qt0">heart</span>, lungs, or esophagus.</li></ul>What are the symptoms of atrial fibrillation (AFib)?<p>Some people who have AFib don't have any symptoms and don't know they have it. If you do have symptoms, you may only notice them once in a while. Or you may have symptoms that are more frequent. And in some cases, the symptoms might be severe. If you have <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span>, you are more likely to notice your symptoms. And those symptoms could get worse if your <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span> gets worse.</p><p>The symptoms of AFib can include:</p><ul><li>Extreme fatigue, which is the most common symptom</li><li><span class="qt0">Heart</span> palpitations (the feeling that your <span class="qt0">heart</span> is skipping a beat, fluttering, pounding, or beating too hard or too fast)</li><li>Trouble breathing, especially when lying down or when exercising</li><li>Chest pain</li><li>Dizziness or fainting</li><li>Low blood pressure</li></ul>What other problems can AFib cause?<p>If AFib is not treated, it can lead to serious health problems (complications) such as:</p><ul><li>Stroke</li><li><span class="qt0">Heart</span> failure</li><li>Blood clots</li><li>Sudden <span class="qt0">cardiac arrest</span> (SCA)</li><li>Cognitive impairment and dementia</li></ul><p>To help prevent these problems, it's important to contact your health care provider if you are having symptoms. If you do have AFib, the sooner you are diagnosed and treated, the better.</p>How is atrial fibrillation (AFib) diagnosed?<p>To find out if you have AFib, your provider:</p><ul><li>Will ask about your medical history, including your symptoms, lifestyle, and any other health conditions you may have</li><li>Will ask about your family history, to find out if you have relatives who have or had AFib</li><li>Will do a physical exam</li><li>May order blood tests</li><li>Will likely order <span class="qt0">heart</span> tests, such as an electrocardiogram (also called an EKG or ECG) and echocardiogram</li><li>May ask you to wear a <span class="qt0">heart</span> monitor device that records your <span class="qt0">heart</span>'s electrical activity</li></ul>What are the treatments for atrial fibrillation (AFib)?<p>The treatments for AFib may include:</p><ul><li>Blood thinner medicines that help prevent blood clots from forming.</li><li>Medicines to control your <span class="qt0">heart</span>'s rhythm and rate.</li><li>Following <span class="qt0">heart</span>-healthy lifestyle changes, such as:
  <ul><li>Following a <span class="qt0">heart</span>-healthy eating plan that limits saturated fats, salt, and cholesterol. An example is the DASH eating plan.</li><li>Limiting or avoiding alcohol, because it can increase your <span class="qt0">heart</span> rate.</li><li>Aiming for a healthy weight.</li><li>Getting regular physical activity.</li><li>Managing stress.</li><li>Quitting smoking.</li></ul>
</li><li>Procedures such as:
  <ul><li>Electrical cardioversion, which restores your <span class="qt0">heart</span> rhythm using low-energy shocks to your <span class="qt0">heart</span>.</li><li>Catheter ablation, which scars the tissue that is causing the arrhythmia. The scar tissue blocks the abnormal <span class="qt0">heart</span> signals.</li></ul>
</li><li>Surgeries such as:
  <ul><li>Surgery to put in a pacemaker to help control the arrhythmia.</li><li>A Maze procedure, which creates scar tissue in a maze-like pattern in certain parts of the <span class="qt0">heart</span>.</li><li>Left atrial appendage closure, a surgery on a small sac in the muscle wall of your left atrium (the upper left chamber of your <span class="qt0">heart</span>). It helps prevent blood clots and can reduce your risk of stroke. This surgery is for people who are not able to take blood thinners.</li></ul>
</li></ul>Can atrial fibrillation (AFib) be prevented?<p>There are steps you can take to help lower your risk of atrial fibrillation, such as:</p><ul><li>Making <span class="qt0">heart</span>-healthy lifestyle changes:
  <ul><li>Following a <span class="qt0">heart</span>-healthy eating plan</li><li>Limiting or avoiding alcohol</li><li>Aiming for a healthy weight</li><li>Getting regular physical activity</li><li>Managing stress</li><li>Not smoking</li></ul>
</li><li>Avoiding illegal drugs, such as cocaine and methamphetamines</li><li>Taking antiarrhythmic medicine (medicine to treat arrhythmia) if you are having <span class="qt0">heart</span> surgery</li><li>Treating any health conditions that could raise your risk of AFib</li></ul><p>NIH: National <span class="qt0">Heart</span>, Lung, and Blood Institute</p></content>
      <content name="mesh">Atrial Fibrillation</content>
      <content name="groupName">Blood, <span class="qt0">Heart</span> and Circulation</content>

    <document rank="19" url="https://medlineplus.gov/cholesterolmedicines.html">
      <content name="title">Cholesterol Medicines</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is cholesterol?<p>Cholesterol is a waxy, fat-like substance that's found in all the cells in your body. Your body needs some cholesterol to work properly.  But if you have too much of it in your blood, it can stick to the walls of your arteries and narrow or even block them. This puts you at risk for <span class="qt0">coronary</span> artery <span class="qt1">disease</span> and other <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">diseases</span></span>.</p><p>Cholesterol is made by your liver. It travels through the blood on proteins called lipoproteins. One type of lipoprotein, LDL, is sometimes called the "bad" cholesterol. A high LDL level leads to a buildup of cholesterol in your arteries. Another type, HDL, is sometimes called the "good" cholesterol. It carries cholesterol from other parts of your body back to your liver. Then your liver removes the cholesterol from your body.</p>What are the treatments for high cholesterol?<p>If you have high cholesterol, lifestyle changes can help you lower your cholesterol levels. But sometimes the lifestyle changes are not enough, and you need to take cholesterol medicines. But you should still continue with the lifestyle changes even though you are taking medicines.</p>Who needs cholesterol medicines?<p>Your health care provider may prescribe cholesterol medicines for you if:</p><ul><li>You have already had a <span class="qt0">heart</span> attack or stroke, or you have peripheral arterial <span class="qt1">disease</span></li><li>Your LDL (bad) cholesterol level is 190 mg/dL or higher</li><li>You are 40-75 years old, you have diabetes, and your LDL cholesterol level is 70 mg/dL or higher</li><li>You are 40-75 years old, you have a high risk of developing <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span> or stroke, and your LDL cholesterol level is 70 mg/dL or higher</li></ul>What are the different types of cholesterol medicines?<p>There are several types of cholesterol-lowering drugs available, including:</p><ul><li>Statins. They reduce the amount of cholesterol made in the liver. They also increase your liver's ability to remove LDL cholesterol that is already in the blood. Statins are the most common medicines used to treat high cholesterol.</li><li>Bile acid sequestrants.  They keep bile acids, which digest fats and oils, from being absorbed into the body.  But your body needs bile acids, so your liver will make more of them. It makes them by breaking down LDL cholesterol, so this helps lower your LDL cholesterol. Your provider may prescribe these medicines if you cannot take statins or if statins alone are not lowering your cholesterol enough.</li><li>Cholesterol absorption inhibitors.  They block how cholesterol is absorbed into the body. They are also used if you cannot take statins or if statins alone are not lowering your cholesterol enough.</li><li>Nicotinic acid (niacin).  It is a type of B vitamin. It reduces the amount of fats made by your liver, so it lowers your LDL cholesterol and triglycerides. Niacin also raises HDL cholesterol. Even though you can buy niacin without a prescription, you should talk to your provider before taking it to lower your cholesterol. High doses of niacin can cause serious side effects.</li><li>PCSK9 inhibitors.  These are medicines that you inject under your skin. They block a protein called PCSK9. This helps your liver remove and clear LDL cholesterol from your blood. Your provider may prescribe a PCSK9 inhibitor and a statin if you are at high risk of complications like <span class="qt0">heart</span> attack or stroke, or if you have familial hypercholesterolemia (FH). FH is an inherited disorder that causes very high levels of cholesterol in the blood.</li><li>Fibrates.  They lower triglycerides in several complex ways, including reducing the amount of triglycerides made by the liver. They may also help increase HDL cholesterol and may lower LDL levels a bit. If you take them with statins, it might increase the risk of side effects.</li><li>Combination medicines. They include more than one type of cholesterol-lowering medicine. There are also combination medicines that can treat both high blood pressure and cholesterol.</li></ul><p>There are also a few other cholesterol medicines (lomitapide and mipomersen) that are only for people who have FH.</p>How does my health care provider decide which cholesterol medicine I should take?<p>When deciding which medicine you should take and which dose you need, your provider will consider:</p><ul><li>Your cholesterol levels.</li><li>Your risk for <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span> and stroke.</li><li>Your age.</li><li>Any other health problems you have.</li><li>Possible side effects of the medicines. Higher doses are more likely to cause side effects, especially over time.</li></ul><p>Medicines can help control your cholesterol, but they don't cure it. You need to keep taking your medicines and get regular cholesterol checks to make sure that your cholesterol levels are in a healthy range.</p></content>

    <document rank="20" url="https://medlineplus.gov/highcholesterolinchildrenandteens.html">
      <content name="title">High Cholesterol in Children and Teens</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is cholesterol?<p>Cholesterol is a waxy, fat-like substance that's found in all the cells in the body. The liver makes cholesterol, and it is also in some foods, such as meat and dairy products. The body needs some cholesterol to work properly. But if your child or teen has high cholesterol (too much cholesterol in the blood), it can stick to the walls of their arteries and narrow or even block the arteries. This can put your child or teen at risk of <span class="qt0">coronary</span> artery <span class="qt1">disease</span> and other <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">diseases</span></span>.</p>What causes high cholesterol in children and teens?<p>Three main factors contribute to high cholesterol in children and teens:</p><ul><li>An unhealthy diet, especially one that is high in saturated fats</li><li>A family history of high cholesterol, especially when one or both parents have high cholesterol</li><li>Having obesity</li></ul><p>Some <span class="qt1">diseases</span>, such as diabetes, kidney <span class="qt1">disease</span>, and certain thyroid <span class="qt1">diseases</span>, can also cause high cholesterol in children and teens.</p>What are the symptoms of high cholesterol in children and teens?<p>There are usually no signs or symptoms that your child or teen has high cholesterol.</p>How do I know if my child or teen has high cholesterol?<p>There is a blood test to measure cholesterol levels. The test gives information about:</p><ul><li>Total cholesterol. This is a measure of the total amount of cholesterol in your blood. It includes both low-density lipoprotein (LDL) cholesterol and high-density lipoprotein (HDL) cholesterol.</li><li>LDL cholesterol. LDL is often called "bad" cholesterol because it is the main source of cholesterol buildup and blockage in the arteries.</li><li>HDL cholesterol. HDL is often called "good" cholesterol because it helps remove cholesterol from your arteries.</li><li>Non-HDL. This number is your total cholesterol minus your HDL. Your non-HDL includes LDL and other types of cholesterol such as VLDL (very-low-density lipoprotein).</li><li>Triglycerides. This is another type of fat in your blood that can raise the risk of <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span>.</li></ul><p>For anyone aged 19 or younger, the healthy levels of cholesterol are</p>Type of CholesterolHealthy LevelTotal CholesterolLess than 170 mg/dLNon-HDLLess than 120 mg/dLLDLLess than 110 mg/dLHDLMore than 45 mg/dL<p>When and how often your child or teen should get this test depends on their age, risk factors, and family history. The general recommendations are:</p><ul><li>The first test should be between ages 9 to 11</li><li>Children should have the test again every 5 years</li><li>Some children may have this test starting at age 2 if there is a family history of high cholesterol, <span class="qt0">heart</span> attack, or stroke</li></ul>What are the treatments for high cholesterol in children and teens?<p>Lifestyle changes are the main treatment for high cholesterol in children and teens. These changes include:</p><ul><li>Being more active. This includes getting regular physical activity and spending less time sitting (in front of a television, at a computer, on a phone or tablet, etc.).</li><li>Healthy eating. A diet to lower cholesterol includes limiting foods that are high in saturated fat, sugar, and cholesterol. It is also important to eat plenty of fresh fruits, vegetables, and whole grains.</li><li>Losing weight, if your child or teen is overweight or has obesity.</li></ul><p>If everyone in the family makes these changes, it will be easier for your child or teen to stick to them. It is also an opportunity to improve your health, and the health of the rest of your family.</p><p>Sometimes these lifestyle changes are not enough to lower your child or teen's cholesterol. Their health care provider may consider giving your child or teen cholesterol medicines if they:</p><ul><li>Are at least 10 years old.</li><li>Have an LDL (bad) cholesterol level that is higher than 190 mg/dL, even after six months of diet and exercise changes.</li><li>Have an LDL (bad) cholesterol level that is higher than 160 mg/dL AND are at high risk for <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span>.</li><li>Have familial hypercholesterolemia (FH). FH is an inherited disorder that causes very high levels of cholesterol in the blood.</li></ul></content>

    <document rank="21" url="https://medlineplus.gov/primaryovarianinsufficiency.html">
      <content name="title">Primary Ovarian Insufficiency</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Ovarian Insufficiency</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Premature Ovarian Failure</content>
      <content name="altTitle">POF</content>
      <content name="altTitle">POI</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is primary ovarian insufficiency (POI)?<p>Primary ovarian insufficiency (POI) happens when your ovaries stop working normally before you are 40.</p><p>As you get older, fertility naturally decreases. This usually starts around age 40 or later.  Menstrual periods may also become irregular as you transition to menopause. If you have POI, these changes happen earlier, before age 40. Sometimes POI can start as early as the teenage years.</p><p>POI is different from premature menopause. With premature menopause, your periods stop before age 40. You can no longer get pregnant. The cause can be natural or it can be a <span class="qt1">disease</span>, surgery, chemotherapy, or radiation. With POI, some women still have occasional periods. They may even get pregnant.  In most cases of POI, the cause is unknown.</p>What causes primary ovarian insufficiency (POI)?<p>In about 90% of cases, the exact cause of POI is unknown.</p><p>Research shows that POI is related to problems with the follicles. Follicles are small sacs in your ovaries where eggs grow and mature. One type of follicle problem is that you run out of working follicles earlier than normal. Another is that the follicles are not working properly. In most cases, the cause of the follicle problem is unknown. But sometimes the cause may be:</p><ul><li>Genetic disorders such as Fragile X syndrome and Turner syndrome</li><li>A low number of follicles</li><li>Autoimmune <span class="qt1">diseases</span>, including thyroiditis and Addison <span class="qt1">disease</span></li><li>Chemotherapy or radiation therapy</li><li>Metabolic disorders</li><li>Exposure to toxic substances, such as cigarette smoke, chemicals, and pesticides</li></ul>Who is at risk for primary ovarian insufficiency (POI)?<p>Certain factors can raise your risk of POI:</p><ul><li>Family history. If you have a mother or sister with POI, you are more likely to have it.</li><li>Genes. Some changes to genes and genetic conditions put you at higher risk for POI. For example, women with Fragile X syndrome or Turner syndrome are at higher risk.</li><li>Certain <span class="qt1">diseases</span>, such as autoimmune <span class="qt1">diseases</span> and some viral infections.</li><li>Cancer treatments, such as chemotherapy and radiation therapy.</li><li>Age.You can get POI when you're young, but it becomes more common between the ages of 35 and 40.</li></ul>What are the symptoms of primary ovarian insufficiency (POI)?<p>The first sign of POI is usually irregular or missed periods. Later symptoms may be like those of natural menopause, including:</p><ul><li>Hot flashes</li><li>Night sweats</li><li>Irritability</li><li>Poor concentration</li><li>Decreased sex drive</li><li>Pain during sex</li><li>Vaginal dryness</li></ul><p>You may see a health care provider because of difficulty getting pregnant or trouble with infertility.</p>What other problems can primary ovarian insufficiency (POI) cause?<p>Since POI causes you to have lower levels of certain hormones, you are at greater risk for other health conditions, including:</p><ul><li>Anxiety and depression. Hormonal changes caused by POI can contribute to anxiety or lead to depression.</li><li>Dry eye syndrome and eye surface <span class="qt1">disease</span>. Some women with POI have one of these eye conditions. Both can cause discomfort and may lead to blurred vision. If not treated, these conditions can cause permanent eye damage.</li><li><span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">Heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span>. Lower levels of estrogen can affect the muscles lining the arteries and can increase the buildup of cholesterol. These factors increase your risk of atherosclerosis (hardening of the arteries).</li><li>Infertility.</li><li>Low thyroid function. This problem also is called hypothyroidism. The thyroid is a gland that makes hormones that control your body's metabolism and energy level. Low levels of thyroid hormones can affect your metabolism and can cause very low energy, mental sluggishness, and other symptoms.</li><li>Osteoporosis. The hormone estrogen helps keep bones strong. Without enough estrogen, women with POI often develop osteoporosis. It is a bone <span class="qt1">disease</span> that causes weak, brittle bones that are more likely to break.</li></ul>How is primary ovarian insufficiency (POI) diagnosed?<p>To diagnose POI, your provider may do:</p><ul><li>A medical history, including asking whether you have relatives with POI</li><li>A pregnancy test, to make sure that you are not pregnant</li><li>A physical exam, to look for signs of other disorders that could be causing your symptoms</li><li>Blood tests, to check for certain hormones. You may also have a blood test to do a chromosome analysis. A chromosome is the part of a cell that contains genetic information.</li><li>A pelvic ultrasound, to see whether the ovaries are enlarged or have multiple follicles.</li></ul>What are the treatments for primary ovarian insufficiency (POI)?<p>Currently, there is no proven treatment to restore normal function to the ovaries. But there are treatments for some of the symptoms of POI. There are also ways to lower your health risks and treat the conditions that POI can cause:</p><ul><li>Hormone therapy for menopause (sometimes called hormone replacement therapy or HRT). This is the most common treatment. It gives your body the estrogen and other hormones that your ovaries are not making. Hormone therapy for menopause improves sexual health and decreases the risks for <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span> and osteoporosis.</li><li>Calcium and vitamin D supplements. Because women with POI are at higher risk for osteoporosis, you should take calcium and vitamin D every day.</li><li>In vitro fertilization (IVF). If you have POI and you wish to become pregnant, you may consider trying IVF.</li><li>Regular physical activity and a healthy body weight.Getting regular exercise and controlling your weight can lower your risk for osteoporosis and <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span>.</li><li>Treatments for associated conditions. If you have a condition that is related to POI, it is important to treat that as well. Treatments may involve medicines and hormones.</li></ul><p>NIH: National Institute of Child Health and Human Development</p></content>
      <content name="mesh">Primary Ovarian Insufficiency</content>
      <content name="groupName">Women</content>
      <content name="groupName">Pregnancy and Reproduction</content>
      <content name="groupName">Endocrine System</content>

Both can cause discomfort and may lead to blurred vision. If not treated, these conditions can cause permanent eye damage.  <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">Heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span>. Lower levels of estrogen can affect the  ... </content>

    <document rank="22" url="https://medlineplus.gov/prediabetes.html">
      <content name="title">Prediabetes</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Insulin Resistance</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is prediabetes?<p>Prediabetes means that your blood glucose, or blood sugar, levels are higher than normal but not high enough to be called diabetes. Glucose comes from the foods you eat. Too much glucose in your blood can damage your body over time.</p><p>If you have prediabetes, you are more likely to develop type 2 diabetes, <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span>, and stroke. But if you make some lifestyle changes now, you may be able to delay or prevent type 2 diabetes.</p>What causes prediabetes?<p>Prediabetes usually happens when your body has a problem with insulin. Insulin is a hormone that helps the glucose get into your cells to give them energy. A problem with insulin could be:</p><ul><li>Insulin resistance, a condition in which the body can't use its insulin properly. It makes it hard for your cells to get glucose from your blood. This can cause your blood sugar levels to rise.</li><li>Your body can't make enough insulin to keep your blood sugar levels at a healthy level</li></ul><p>Researchers think that being overweight and not getting regular physical activity are major factors in causing prediabetes.</p>Who is at risk for prediabetes?<p>About 1 out of every 3 adults has prediabetes. It is more common in people who:</p><ul><li>Are overweight or have obesity</li><li>Are age 45 or older</li><li>Have a parent, brother, or sister with diabetes</li><li>Are African American, Alaska Native, American Indian, Asian American, Hispanic/Latino, Native Hawaiian, or Pacific Islander American</li><li>Are not physically active</li><li>Have health conditions such as high blood pressure and high cholesterol</li><li>Have had gestational diabetes (diabetes in pregnancy)</li><li>Have a history of <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">heart</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span> or stroke</li><li>Have metabolic syndrome</li><li>Have polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS)</li></ul>What are the symptoms of prediabetes?<p>Most people don't know they have prediabetes because usually there are no symptoms.</p><p>Some people with prediabetes may have darkened skin in the armpit or on the back and sides of the neck. They may also have many small skin growths in those same areas.</p>How is prediabetes diagnosed?<p>There are a few different blood tests that can diagnose prediabetes. The most common ones are:</p><ul><li>Fasting plasma glucose (FPG) test, which measures your blood sugar at a single point in time. You need to fast (not eat or drink) for at least 8 hours before the test. The results of the test are given in mg/dL (milligrams per deciliter):
		<ul><li>A normal level is 99 or below</li><li>Prediabetes is 100 to 125</li><li>Type 2 diabetes is 126 and above</li></ul>
</li><li>A1C test, which measures your average blood sugar over the past 3 months. The results of an A1C test are given as a percentage. The higher the percentage, the higher your blood sugar levels have been. 
		<ul><li>A normal level is below 5.7%</li><li>Prediabetes is between 5.7 to 6.4%</li><li>Type 2 diabetes is above 6.5%</li></ul></li></ul>If I have prediabetes, will I get diabetes?<p>If you have prediabetes, you may be able to delay or prevent type 2 diabetes through lifestyle changes:</p><ul><li>Losing weight, if you are overweight</li><li>Getting regular physical activity</li><li>Following a healthy, reduced-calorie eating plan</li></ul><p>In some cases, your health care provider may also recommend taking diabetes medicines.</p>Can prediabetes be prevented?<p>If you are at risk for prediabetes, those same lifestyle changes (losing weight, regular physical activity, and a healthy eating plan) may prevent you from getting it.</p><p>NIH: National Institute of Diabetes and Digestive and Kidney <span class="qt1">Diseases</span></p></content>
      <content name="mesh">Prediabetic State</content>
      <content name="groupName">Endocrine System</content>
      <content name="groupName">Diabetes Mellitus</content>
      <content name="snippet"> ... National Institute of Diabetes and Digestive and Kidney <span class="qt1">Diseases</span></content>

    <document rank="23" url="https://medlineplus.gov/heartattack.html">
      <content name="title"><span class="qt0">Heart</span> Attack</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">MI</content>
      <content name="altTitle"><span class="qt0">Myocardial Infarction</span></content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>Each year almost 800,000 Americans have a <span class="qt0">heart</span> attack. A <span class="qt0">heart</span> attack happens when blood flow to the <span class="qt0">heart</span> suddenly becomes blocked. Without the blood coming in, the <span class="qt0">heart</span> can't get oxygen. If not treated quickly, the <span class="qt0">heart</span> muscle begins to die. But if you do get quick treatment, you may be able to prevent or limit damage to the <span class="qt0">heart</span> muscle. That's why it's important to know the symptoms of a <span class="qt0">heart</span> attack and call 911 if you or someone else is having them. You should call, even if you are not sure that it is a <span class="qt0">heart</span> attack.</p><p>The most common symptoms in men and women are:</p><ul><li>Chest discomfort. It is often in center or left side of the chest. It usually lasts more than a few minutes. It may go away and come back. It can feel like pressure, squeezing, fullness, or pain. It also can feel like heartburn or indigestion.</li><li>Shortness of breath. Sometimes this is your only symptom. You may get it before or during the chest discomfort. It can happen when you are resting or doing a little bit of physical activity.</li><li>Discomfort in the upper body. You may feel pain or discomfort in one or both arms, the back, shoulders, neck, jaw, or upper part of the stomach.</li></ul><p>You may also have other symptoms, such as nausea, vomiting, dizziness, and lightheadedness. You may break out in a cold sweat. Sometimes women will have different symptoms then men. For example, they are more likely to feel tired for no reason.</p><p>The most common cause of <span class="qt0">heart</span> attacks is <span class="qt0">coronary</span> artery <span class="qt1">disease</span> (CAD). With CAD, there is a buildup of cholesterol and other material, called plaque, on their inner walls or the arteries. This is atherosclerosis. It can build up for years. Eventually an area of plaque can rupture (break open). A blood clot can form around the plaque and block the artery.</p><p>A less common cause of <span class="qt0">heart</span> attack is a severe spasm (tightening) of a <span class="qt0">coronary</span> artery. The spasm cuts off blood flow through the artery.</p><p>At the hospital, health care providers make a diagnosis based on your symptoms, blood tests, and different <span class="qt0">heart</span> health tests. Treatments may include medicines and medical procedures such as <span class="qt0">coronary</span> angioplasty. After a <span class="qt0">heart</span> attack, <span class="qt0">cardiac</span> rehabilitation and lifestyle changes can help you recover.</p><p>NIH: National <span class="qt0">Heart</span>, Lung, and Blood Institute</p></content>
      <content name="mesh"><span class="qt0">Myocardial Infarction</span></content>
      <content name="groupName">Blood, <span class="qt0">Heart</span> and Circulation</content>

With CAD, there is a buildup of cholesterol and other material, called plaque, on their inner walls or the ...  A less common cause of <span class="qt0">heart</span> attack is a severe spasm (tightening) of a  ... </content>

    <document rank="24" url="https://medlineplus.gov/atherosclerosis.html">
      <content name="title">Atherosclerosis</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Arteriosclerosis</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Hardening of the Arteries</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Plaque, Atherosclerotic</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is atherosclerosis?<p>Atherosclerosis is a condition in which plaque builds up inside your arteries. Plaque is a sticky substance made up of cholesterol, fat, blood cells, calcium, and other substances found in the blood. Over time, plaque hardens and causes your arteries to narrow. That limits the flow of oxygen-rich blood to your body.</p><p>Some people may confuse atherosclerosis and arteriosclerosis, but they are not the same thing:</p><ul><li>Arteriosclerosis is hardening of the arteries, which means that the arteries thicken and become less flexible. It can have several different causes.</li><li>Atherosclerosis, which develops from plaque buildup, is a common type of arteriosclerosis.</li></ul><p>Atherosclerosis can affect most of the arteries in the body. It has different names, based on which arteries are affected:</p><ul><li><span class="qt0">Coronary</span> artery <span class="qt1">disease</span> (CAD) is plaque buildup in the arteries of your <span class="qt0">heart</span>.
</li><li>Peripheral artery <span class="qt1">disease</span> (PAD) is plaque buildup in the arteries that carry blood away from the <span class="qt0">heart</span> to other parts of the body. It most often affects the arteries of your legs, but it can also affect the arteries of your arms or pelvis.
</li><li>Carotid artery <span class="qt1">disease</span> is plaque buildup in the neck arteries. It reduces blood flow to the brain.
</li><li>Renal artery stenosis is plaque buildup in the arteries that supply blood to your kidneys.
</li><li>Vertebral artery <span class="qt1">disease</span> is plaque buildup in the arteries that supply blood to the back of your brain. 
</li><li>Mesenteric artery ischemia is plaque buildup in the arteries that supply your intestines with blood. 
</li></ul>What causes atherosclerosis?<p>Plaque often starts to build up during childhood and gets worse with age. The exact cause is unknown, but researchers believe that this buildup happens when there is damage to the arteries. This damage may be caused by unhealthy lifestyle habits, medical conditions, and your genes.</p>Who is more likely to develop atherosclerosis?<p>You may be more likely to develop atherosclerosis if you:</p><ul><li>Have certain medical conditions, including:
	<ul><li>High blood pressure</li><li>High blood cholesterol</li><li>Diabetes</li><li>Metabolic syndrome</li><li>Inflammatory <span class="qt1">diseases</span> such as rheumatoid arthritis and psoriasis</li></ul>
</li><li>Have a family history of high blood cholesterol</li><li>Eat a lot of foods high in saturated fats</li><li>Smoke or chew tobacco</li><li>Are older - the risk increases after age 45 men and age 55 in women</li></ul>What are the symptoms of atherosclerosis?<p>In the early stages, atherosclerosis often does not cause any symptoms. You may first notice some symptoms at times when your body needs more oxygen. For example, this could be when you are having physical or emotional stress.</p><p>Your symptoms will depend on which arteries are affected and how much blood flow is blocked:</p><ul><li>With <span class="qt0">coronary</span> artery <span class="qt1">disease</span>, the symptoms may include angina (a type of chest pain), palpitations (racing or pounding <span class="qt0">heart</span>), and shortness of breath.</li><li>With carotid artery <span class="qt1">disease</span>, you may have a bruit. This is a whooshing sound that your health care provider hears when using a stethoscope. You could also have a transient ischemic attack (TIA), sometimes called a mini-stroke.</li><li>With peripheral artery <span class="qt1">disease</span>, you may have pain, aching, heaviness, or cramping in the legs when walking or climbing stairs.</li><li>With vertebral artery <span class="qt1">disease</span>, you may have problems with thinking and memory, weakness or numbness on one side of the body or face, and vision trouble. You could also have a transient ischemic attack.</li><li>With mesenteric artery ischemia, the symptoms can include severe pain after meals, weight loss, and diarrhea.</li></ul><p>For men, erectile dysfunction (ED) is an early warning sign that you may be at higher risk for atherosclerosis and its complications. If you have ED, talk with your provider about your risk of plaque buildup.</p>What other problems can atherosclerosis cause?<p>Atherosclerosis can cause other health problems, or complications. For example, if a plaque bursts, a blood clot may form. The clot could block the artery completely or travel to another part of the body. Other possible complications can vary, depending on which arteries are affected. For example, blockages in different parts of the body can lead to complications such as a <span class="qt0">heart</span> attack, stroke, vascular dementia, or limb loss.</p>How is atherosclerosis diagnosed?<p>To find out if you have atherosclerosis, your provider:</p><ul><li>Will ask about your medical and family health history</li><li>Will ask about your lifestyle and risk factors for plaque buildup in the arteries</li><li>Will do a physical exam, which will include listening to your <span class="qt0">heart</span> and the blood flow in your arteries</li><li>Will likely order tests, such as blood tests and <span class="qt0">heart</span> health tests</li></ul>What are the treatments for atherosclerosis?<p>If you have atherosclerosis, your provider will work with you to create a treatment plan that works for you. Your plan will depend on which arteries are affected, how much the blood flow is blocked, and what other medical conditions you have. Possible treatments may include:</p><ul><li><span class="qt0">Heart</span>-healthy lifestyle changes.</li><li>Medicines to:
	<ul><li>Manage your risk factors.</li><li>Treat atherosclerosis or its complications.</li><li>Treat any medical conditions you have that can worsen plaque buildup.</li></ul>
</li><li>Procedures or surgeries to treat <span class="qt1">diseases</span> or complications that were caused by plaque buildup. The specific type of procedure or surgery will depend on which arteries are affected.</li><li><span class="qt0">Cardiac</span> rehabilitation, if you have had certain complications from atherosclerosis.</li></ul>Can atherosclerosis be prevented?<p>There are steps you can take to try to prevent atherosclerosis:</p><ul><li>Choose <span class="qt0">heart</span>-healthy foods, such fruits, vegetables, and whole grains. Limit foods that are high in saturated fats, salt, and added sugars.</li><li>Do regular physical activity. But before you start an exercise program, ask your provider what level of physical activity is right for you.</li><li>Aim for a healthy weight.</li><li>Limit how much alcohol you drink. Drinking less is better for health than drinking more. Men should limit their intake to 2 drinks or less in a day. Women should drink 1 drink or less per day.</li><li>Manage stress.</li><li>If you smoke, quit smoking.</li><li>Avoid secondhand smoke.</li><li>Get enough good-quality sleep.</li></ul><p>NIH: National <span class="qt0">Heart</span>, Lung, and Blood Institute</p></content>
      <content name="mesh">Atherosclerosis</content>
      <content name="groupName">Blood, <span class="qt0">Heart</span> and Circulation</content>

    <document rank="2" url="https://medlineplus.gov/stroke.html">
      <content name="title"><span class="qt0">Stroke</span></content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Brain Attack</content>
      <content name="altTitle">CVA</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Cerebrovascular Disease</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is a <span class="qt0">stroke</span>?<p>A <span class="qt0">stroke</span> happens when there is a loss of blood flow to part of the brain. Your brain cells cannot get the oxygen and nutrients they need from blood, and they start to die within a few minutes. This can cause lasting brain damage, long-term disability, or even death.</p><p>If you think that you or someone else is having a <span class="qt0">stroke</span>, call 911 right away. Immediate treatment may save someone's life and increase the chances for successful rehabilitation and recovery.</p>What are the types of <span class="qt0">stroke</span>?<p>There are two types of <span class="qt0">stroke</span>:</p><ul><li>Ischemic <span class="qt0">stroke</span> is caused by a blood clot that blocks or plugs a blood vessel in the brain. This is the most common type; about 80% of <span class="qt0">strokes</span> are ischemic.</li><li>Hemorrhagic <span class="qt0">stroke</span> is caused by a blood vessel that breaks and bleeds into the brain.</li></ul><p>Another condition that's similar to a <span class="qt0">stroke</span> is a transient ischemic attack (TIA). It's sometimes called a "mini-<span class="qt0">stroke</span>." TIAs happen when the blood supply to the brain is blocked for a short time. The damage to the brain cells isn't permanent, but having a TIA puts you at much higher risk of having a <span class="qt0">stroke</span>.</p>Who is at risk for a <span class="qt0">stroke</span>?<p>Certain factors can raise your risk of a <span class="qt0">stroke</span>. The major risk factors include:</p><ul><li>High blood pressure. This is the primary risk factor for a <span class="qt0">stroke</span>.</li><li>Diabetes.</li><li>Heart diseases.Atrial fibrillation and other heart diseases can cause blood clots that lead to <span class="qt0">stroke</span>.</li><li>Smoking. When you smoke, you damage your blood vessels and raise your blood pressure.</li><li>A personal or family history of <span class="qt0">stroke</span> or TIA</li><li>Age. Your risk of <span class="qt0">stroke</span> increases as you get older.</li><li>Race and ethnicity. People who are African Americans or  Hispanic have a higher risk of <span class="qt0">stroke</span>.</li></ul><p>There are also other factors that are linked to a higher risk of <span class="qt0">stroke</span>, such as:</p><ul><li>Alcohol and illegal drug use</li><li>Not getting enough physical activity</li><li>High cholesterol</li><li>Unhealthy diet</li><li>Having obesity</li></ul>What are the symptoms of a <span class="qt0">stroke</span>?<p>The symptoms of a <span class="qt0">stroke</span> often happen quickly. They include:</p><ul><li>Sudden numbness or weakness of the face, arm, or leg (especially on one side of the body)</li><li>Sudden confusion, trouble speaking, or understanding speech</li><li>Sudden trouble seeing in one or both eyes</li><li>Sudden difficulty walking, dizziness, loss of balance or coordination</li><li>Sudden severe headache with no known cause</li></ul><p>The F.A.S.T. test can help you remember what to look for if you think someone is having a <span class="qt0">stroke</span>. Think "FAST" and look for:</p><ul><li>Face drooping on one side when smiling.</li><li>Arm weakness occurs when the arms are raised, and one arm drifts downward.</li><li>Speech is slurred or strange.</li><li>Time to call 911.</li></ul><p>If you think that you or someone else is having a <span class="qt0">stroke</span>, call 911 right away. Every minute counts during a <span class="qt0">stroke</span>.</p>How are <span class="qt0">strokes</span> diagnosed?<p>To make a diagnosis, your health care provider will</p><ul><li>Ask about your symptoms and medical history</li><li>Do a physical exam, including a check of
	<ul><li>Your mental alertness</li><li>Your coordination and balance</li><li>Any numbness or weakness in your face, arms, and legs</li><li>Any trouble speaking and seeing clearly</li></ul>
</li><li>Run some tests, which may include
	<ul><li>Diagnostic imaging of the brain, such as a CT scan or MRI.</li><li>Heart tests, which can help detect heart problems or blood clots that may have led to a <span class="qt0">stroke</span>. Possible tests include an electrocardiogram (EKG) and an echocardiography.</li></ul>
</li></ul>What are the treatments for <span class="qt0">stroke</span>?<p>Treatments for <span class="qt0">stroke</span> include medicines, surgery, and rehabilitation. Which treatments you get depend on the type of <span class="qt0">stroke</span> and the stage of treatment. The different stages are:</p><ul><li>Acute treatment, to try to stop a <span class="qt0">stroke</span> while it is happening</li><li>Post-<span class="qt0">stroke</span> rehabilitation, to overcome the disabilities caused by the <span class="qt0">stroke</span></li><li>Prevention, to prevent a first <span class="qt0">stroke</span> or, if you have already had one, prevent another <span class="qt0">stroke</span></li></ul><p>Acute treatments for ischemic <span class="qt0">stroke</span> are usually medicines:</p><ul><li>You may get tPA, (tissue plasminogen activator), a medicine to dissolve the blood clot. You can only get this medicine within 4 hours of when your symptoms started. The sooner you can get it, the better your chance of recovery.</li><li>If you cannot get that medicine, you may get medicine that helps stop platelets from clumping together to form blood clots. Or you may get a blood thinner to keep existing clots from getting bigger.</li><li>If you have carotid artery disease, you may also need a procedure to open your blocked carotid artery.</li></ul><p>Acute treatments for hemorrhagic <span class="qt0">stroke</span> focus on stopping the bleeding. The first step is to find the cause of bleeding in the brain. The next step is to control it:</p><ul><li>If high blood pressure is the cause of bleeding, you may be given blood pressure medicines.</li><li>If an aneurysm is the cause, you may need aneurysm clipping or coil embolization. These are surgeries to prevent further leaking of blood from the aneurysm. It also can help prevent the aneurysm from bursting again.</li><li>If an arteriovenous malformation (AVM) is the cause of a <span class="qt0">stroke</span>, you may need an AVM repair. An AVM is a tangle of faulty arteries and veins that can rupture within the brain. An AVM repair may be done through</li><ul><li>Surgery</li><li>Injecting a substance into the blood vessels of the AVM to block blood flow</li><li>Radiation to shrink the blood vessels of the AVM</li></ul></ul><p><span class="qt0">Stroke</span> rehabilitation can help you relearn skills you lost because of the damage. The goal is to help you become as independent as possible and to have the best possible quality of life.</p><p>Prevention of another <span class="qt0">stroke</span> is also important, since having a <span class="qt0">stroke</span> increases the risk of getting another one. Prevention may include heart-healthy lifestyle changes and medicines.</p>Can <span class="qt0">strokes</span> be prevented?<p>If you have already had a <span class="qt0">stroke</span> or are at risk of having a <span class="qt0">stroke</span>, you can make some heart-healthy lifestyle changes to try to prevent a future <span class="qt0">stroke</span>:</p><ul><li>Eating a heart-healthy diet</li><li>Aiming for a healthy weight</li><li>Managing stress</li><li>Getting regular physical activity</li><li>Quitting smoking</li><li>Managing your blood pressure and cholesterol levels</li></ul><p>If these changes aren't enough, you may need medicine to control your risk factors.</p><p>NIH: National Institute of Neurological Disorders and <span class="qt0">Stroke</span></p></content>

    <document rank="0" url="https://medlineplus.gov/ischemicstroke.html">
      <content name="title">Ischemic <span class="qt0">Stroke</span></content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Carotid Endarterectomy</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Embolic <span class="qt0">Stroke</span></content>
      <content name="altTitle">Thrombotic <span class="qt0">stroke</span></content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is an ischemic <span class="qt0">stroke</span>?<p>A <span class="qt0">stroke</span> happens when there is a loss of blood flow to part of the brain. Your brain cells cannot get the oxygen and nutrients they need from blood, and they start to die within a few minutes. This can cause lasting brain damage, long-term disability, or even death.</p><p>There are two main types of <span class="qt0">strokes</span>, ischemic and hemorrhagic. Ischemic <span class="qt0">stroke</span> is the more common type. An ischemic <span class="qt0">stroke</span> happens when a blood clot blocks a blood vessel in the brain. Quick treatment can save a life and improve the chances of recovery.</p>What causes an ischemic <span class="qt0">stroke</span>?<p>Ischemic <span class="qt0">strokes</span> are usually caused by one or more of the following:</p><ul><li>A blood clot. A clot can form in an artery that supplies the brain (thrombosis), or it can travel from another part of the body (embolism).</li><li>Atherosclerosis. A disease in which plaque, a sticky substance made up of cholesterol, fat, and other substances, builds up inside your arteries.</li><li>Irregular heart rhythms. Conditions like atrial fibrillation can cause clots to form in the heart, which may travel to the brain.</li><li>Other heart and blood conditions. Such as heart valve diseases or sickle cell disease.</li></ul><p>A transient ischemic attack (TIA) is caused by a brief blockage of blood flow to the brain. It usually lasts a few minutes. Having a TIA can mean you are at risk for having a more serious <span class="qt0">stroke</span>.</p>What are the symptoms of ischemic <span class="qt0">stroke</span>?<p>Symptoms of <span class="qt0">stroke</span> often happen suddenly. They include:</p><ul><li>Sudden numbness or weakness of the face, arm or leg (especially on one side of the body)</li><li>Sudden confusion, trouble speaking or understanding speech</li><li>Sudden trouble seeing in one or both eyes</li><li>Sudden trouble walking, dizziness, loss of balance or coordination</li><li>Sudden severe headache with no known cause</li></ul><p>If you think someone is having a <span class="qt0">stroke</span>, the F.A.S.T. test can help you remember what to look for. Think "FAST" and look for:</p><ul><li>Face drooping on one side when smiling.</li><li>Arm weakness that occurs when the arms are raised, and one arm drifts downward.</li><li>Speech is slurred or strange.</li><li>Time to call 911.</li></ul>How is an ischemic <span class="qt0">stroke</span> diagnosed?<p>To make a diagnosis, your health care provider may do a physical exam, ask about your symptoms and medical history, and use imaging tests. Other heart tests may be done to help find the cause of the <span class="qt0">stroke</span>.</p>How is an ischemic <span class="qt0">stroke</span> treated?<p>It is important to treat <span class="qt0">strokes</span> as quickly as possible. The most common treatments include:</p><ul><li>Medicines such as blood thinners or thrombolytics (which break up and dissolve existing clots) may be used to prevent or treat ischemic <span class="qt0">stroke</span>.</li><li>Surgery or stenting may be used to open narrowed arteries or remove plaque build-up from the carotid arteries, which supply blood to the brain.</li></ul><p>After treatment, rehabilitation can help you regain lost skills and independence. Your provider may also order other medicines to manage your blood pressure,cholesterol, or other health problems such as diabetes that may increase your risk for <span class="qt0">stroke</span>.</p>Can an ischemic <span class="qt0">stroke</span> be prevented?<p>The best way to prevent <span class="qt0">stroke</span> is by making heart-healthy lifestyle changes to lower your risk. If lifestyle changes aren't enough, your provider may prescribe medicine to help manage your risk factors.</p><p>NIH: National Institute of Neurological Disorders and <span class="qt0">Stroke</span></p></content>

    <document rank="1" url="https://medlineplus.gov/hemorrhagicstroke.html">
      <content name="title">Hemorrhagic <span class="qt0">Stroke</span></content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Brain Hemorrhage </content>
      <content name="altTitle">Cerebral Hemorrhage </content>
      <content name="altTitle">Subarachnoid Hemorrhage </content>
      <content name="altTitle">Intracerebral Hemorrhage</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Subarachnoid Hemorrhage</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is a hemorrhagic <span class="qt0">stroke</span>?<p>A <span class="qt0">stroke</span> happens when there is a loss of blood flow to part of the brain. Your brain cells cannot get the oxygen and nutrients they need from blood, and they start to die within a few minutes. This can cause lasting brain damage, long-term disability, or even death.</p><p>There are two main types of <span class="qt0">strokes</span>, ischemic and hemorrhagic. A hemorrhagic <span class="qt0">stroke</span> is less common but often more severe. It occurs when a blood vessel in the brain bursts, causing bleeding (hemorrhage) in or around the brain. This bleeding increases pressure inside the skull and damages brain tissue. Within minutes, brain cells begin to die.</p>What are the types of hemorrhagic <span class="qt0">stroke</span>?<p>Hemorrhagic <span class="qt0">strokes</span> are classified by where the bleeding happens:</p><ul><li>Intracerebral hemorrhage. Bleeding occurs directly into the brain tissue. This is the most common type of hemorrhagic <span class="qt0">stroke</span>.</li><li>Subarachnoid hemorrhage. Bleeding occurs in the space between the brain and the thin layers of tissues covering it. This often happens when a weak spot in an artery (called an aneurysm) bursts on the brain's surface.</li></ul>What causes hemorrhagic <span class="qt0">stroke</span>?<p>Common causes include:</p><ul><li>Aneurysm. A bulge or "ballooning" in a weakened artery wall that can burst.</li><li>Arteriovenous malformation (AVM). A tangled group of abnormal blood vessels that can rupture inside the brain.</li><li>High blood pressure (hypertension). Over time, it can weaken blood vessel walls and increase the risk of them breaking open.</li><li>Head trauma. Brain injury can cause bleeding.</li></ul>What are the symptoms of a hemorrhagic <span class="qt0">stroke</span>?<p><span class="qt0">Stroke</span> symptoms often happen suddenly. They include:</p><ul><li>Sudden numbness or weakness of the face, arm or leg (especially on one side of the body)</li><li>Sudden confusion, trouble speaking, or understanding speech</li><li>Sudden trouble seeing in one or both eyes</li><li>Sudden trouble walking, dizziness, loss of balance or coordination</li><li>Sudden severe headache with no known cause</li></ul><p>The F.A.S.T. test can help you remember what to look for if you think someone is having a 
<span class="qt0">stroke</span>. Think "FAST" and look for:</p><ul><li>Face drooping on one side when smiling.</li><li>Arm weakness occurs when the arms are raised, and one arm drifts downward.</li><li>Speech is slurred or strange.</li><li>Time to call 911.</li></ul>How is a hemorrhagic <span class="qt0">stroke</span> diagnosed?<p>To make a diagnosis, your health care provider may do a physical exam, ask about your symptoms and medical history, and use imaging tests to check for bleeding in your brain.</p>How is hemorrhagic <span class="qt0">stroke</span> treated?<p>It is important to treat <span class="qt0">strokes</span> as quickly as possible. For a hemorrhagic <span class="qt0">stroke</span>, the first step is to find the cause of bleeding in the brain and stop it. Treatment may involve medicines or, in some cases, surgery to stop the bleeding or relieve pressure on the brain.</p><p>After the <span class="qt0">stroke</span>, rehabilitation can help people recover lost abilities and regain independence.</p>Can a hemorrhagic <span class="qt0">stroke</span> be prevented?<p>The best way to prevent <span class="qt0">stroke</span> is by making heart-healthy lifestyle changes to lower your risk. If lifestyle changes aren't enough, your provider may prescribe medicine to help manage your risk factors.</p><p>NIH: National Institute of Neurological Disorders and <span class="qt0">Stroke</span></p></content>
      <content name="mesh">Hemorrhagic <span class="qt0">Stroke</span></content>

    <document rank="3" url="https://medlineplus.gov/strokerehabilitation.html">
      <content name="title"><span class="qt0">Stroke</span> Rehabilitation</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is <span class="qt0">stroke</span> rehabilitation?<p><span class="qt0">Stroke</span> rehabilitation is a program for people who have had a <span class="qt0">stroke. A stroke</span> happens when there is a loss of blood flow to part of the brain. Your brain cells cannot get the oxygen and nutrients they need from blood, and they start to die within a few minutes. This can cause lasting brain damage, long-term disability, or even death.</p><p><span class="qt0">Stroke</span> rehabilitation can help you relearn skills you lost because of the damage. It can help with movement, speech, strength, and daily living skills. The goal is to help you become as independent as possible.</p>Who needs <span class="qt0">stroke</span> rehabilitation?<p>Rehabilitation is for people who have lost abilities needed for daily life. <span class="qt0">Stroke</span> rehabilitation is recommended for anyone affected by <span class="qt0">stroke</span>. Most people who take part in a treatment program recover better than those who don't.</p><p>The effects of a <span class="qt0">stroke</span> depend on the parts of your brain that were affected. Your health care provider may recommend care based on your needs.</p><p>The types of disabilities a <span class="qt0">stroke</span> can cause include:</p><ul><li>Paralysis or problems controlling movement</li><li>Pain or other problems with your senses</li><li>Problems using or understanding language</li><li>Problems with thinking and memory</li><li>Difficulty with controlling or expressing emotions</li></ul>When should <span class="qt0">stroke</span> rehabilitation begin?<p>The sooner you begin rehabilitation, the more likely you are to regain lost skills and abilities. Treatment may start while you're still in the hospital. Most likely it will begin within 24 to 48 hours after the <span class="qt0">stroke</span>.</p>What does <span class="qt0">stroke</span> rehabilitation include?<p>The goal of rehabilitation is to help you get your abilities back and regain independence. But the specific goals vary for each person. Your treatment plan will depend on what part of your body or type of ability was affected by the <span class="qt0">stroke</span>. It may include teaching you new ways to work with disabilities, such as using assistive devices to make it easier to do your usual activities.</p><p>A plan for <span class="qt0">stroke</span> rehabilitation can involve many kinds of health professionals. They will work with you to figure out your needs, goals, and make a treatment plan. The types of treatments can include:</p><ul><li>Speech-language therapy. To help with speaking, understanding, reading, writing, and swallowing.</li><li>Physical therapy. To help you relearn movement and coordination skills, regain strength, and stretch your muscles.</li><li>Occupational therapy. To help improve your ability to perform daily living skills such as eating, drinking, bathing, and dressing.</li><li>Cognitive therapy. To help you relearn or improve skills such as thinking, learning, memory, planning, and decision making.</li><li>Mental health counseling. Therapy, medicine, or joining a patient support group may be recommended to help you cope with emotional or behavioral issues.</li><li>Medicine. To treat pain, other health issues, or to help with depression or other mental health conditions following a <span class="qt0">stroke</span>.</li><li>Vocational rehabilitation. To help you build skills for going to school or working at a job.</li></ul>Where does <span class="qt0">stroke</span> rehabilitation take place?<p>Rehabilitation may start while you're in the hospital. Once you're ready for discharge, you and your family will work with your provider to determine the best setting for your specific needs. These can include:</p><ul><li>An inpatient rehabilitation clinic</li><li>A home-based program</li><li>An outpatient unit</li><li>A skilled nursing facility</li></ul>How long does <span class="qt0">stroke</span> rehabilitation last?<p>A <span class="qt0">stroke</span> can cause various complications, and each person may recover at a different pace. The length of rehabilitation will depend on how bad the <span class="qt0">stroke</span> was and any related complications.</p><p>Some people recover quickly after a <span class="qt0">stroke</span>, but most people need some type of long-term rehabilitation. This can change as you progress and your needs change. Your willingness to work toward improvement and the support of your family and health care team will help you get the best results.</p><p>NIH: National Institute of Neurological Disorders and <span class="qt0">Stroke</span></p></content>
      <content name="mesh"><span class="qt0">Stroke</span> Rehabilitation</content>

    <document rank="4" url="https://medlineplus.gov/transientischemicattack.html">
      <content name="title">Transient Ischemic Attack</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Mini-<span class="qt0">Stroke</span></content>
      <content name="altTitle">TIA</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>A transient ischemic attack (TIA) is a <span class="qt0">stroke</span> that lasts only a few minutes. It happens when the blood supply to part of the brain is briefly blocked. Symptoms of a TIA are like other <span class="qt0">stroke</span> symptoms, but do not last as long. They happen suddenly, and include:</p><ul><li>Numbness or weakness, especially on one side of the body</li><li>Confusion or trouble speaking or understanding speech</li><li>Trouble seeing in one or both eyes</li><li>Difficulty walking</li><li>Dizziness</li><li>Loss of balance or coordination</li></ul><p>Most symptoms of a TIA disappear within an hour, although they may last for up to 24 hours. Because you cannot tell if these symptoms are from a TIA or a <span class="qt0">stroke</span>, you should go to the hospital right away.</p><p>TIAs are often a warning sign for future <span class="qt0">strokes</span>. Taking medicine, such as blood thinners, may reduce your risk of a <span class="qt0">stroke</span>. Your doctor might also recommend surgery. You can also help lower your risk by having a healthy lifestyle. This includes not smoking, not drinking too much, eating a healthy diet, and exercising. It is also important to control other health problems, such as high blood pressure and cholesterol.</p><p>NIH: National Institute of Neurological Disorders and <span class="qt0">Stroke</span></p></content>
      <content name="mesh">Ischemic Attack, Transient</content>
      <content name="groupName">Blood, Heart and Circulation</content>

    <document rank="5" url="https://medlineplus.gov/heatillness.html">
      <content name="title">Heat Illness</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Heat Exhaustion</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Hot (Temperature)</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Sunstroke</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Heat <span class="qt0">stroke</span></content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>Your body normally cools itself by sweating.  During hot weather, especially when it is very humid, sweating just isn't enough to cool you off. Your body temperature can rise to dangerous levels and you can develop a heat illness.</p><p>Most heat illnesses happen when you stay out in the heat too long. Exercising and working outside in high heat can also lead to heat illness. Older adults, young children, and those who are sick or overweight are most at risk. Taking certain medicines or drinking alcohol can also raise your risk.</p><p>Heat-related illnesses include:</p><ul><li>Heat <span class="qt0">stroke</span> - a life-threatening illness in which body temperature may rise above 106 °F (41 °C) in minutes. Symptoms include dry skin, a rapid, strong pulse, dizziness, nausea, and confusion. If you see any of these signs, get medical help right away.</li><li>Heat exhaustion -  an illness that can happen after several days of exposure to high temperatures and not enough fluids. Symptoms include heavy sweating, rapid breathing, and a fast, weak pulse. If it is not treated, it can turn into heat <span class="qt0">stroke</span>.</li><li>Heat cramps - muscle pains or spasms that happen during heavy exercise. You usually get them in your abdomen, arms, or legs.</li><li>Heat rash - skin irritation from excessive sweating. It is more common in young children.</li></ul><p>You can lower your risk of heat illness by drinking fluids to prevent dehydration, replacing lost salt and minerals, and limiting your time in the heat.</p><p>Centers for Disease Control and Prevention</p></content>

    <document rank="6" url="https://medlineplus.gov/heartdiseases.html">
      <content name="title">Heart Diseases</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Cardiac Diseases</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Cardiovascular Diseases</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is heart disease?<p>Heart disease is a general term that includes many types of heart problems. It's a type of cardiovascular disease, which means heart and blood vessel disease.</p><p>Heart disease is the leading cause of death in the United States, but there are ways to prevent and manage many types of heart disease.</p>What are the types of heart disease?<p>There are many different types of heart disease. Some you may be born with, called congenital heart disease. Other types develop during your lifetime.</p><p>Coronary artery disease (also called coronary heart disease) is the most common type of heart disease. It happens slowly over time when a sticky substance called plaque builds up in the arteries that supply your heart muscle with blood. The plaque narrows or blocks blood flow to the heart muscle and can lead to other heart problems:</p><ul><li>Angina - chest pain from lack of blood flow</li><li>Heart attacks - when part of the heart muscle dies from loss of blood flow</li><li>Heart failure - when your heart can't pump enough blood to meet your body's needs</li><li>Arrhythmia - a problem with the rate or rhythm of your heartbeat</li></ul><p>Other types of heart diseases may affect your heart valves or heart muscle (cardiomyopathy).</p>What causes heart diseases?<p>The causes of heart disease depend on the type of disease. Some possible causes include lifestyle, genetics, infections, medicines, and other diseases.</p>Who is more likely to develop heart diseases?<p>There are many different factors that can make you more likely to develop heart disease. Some of these factors you can change, but others you cannot.</p><ul><li>Age. Your risk of heart disease goes up as you get older.</li><li>Sex. Some factors may affect heart disease risk differently in women than in men.</li><li>Family history and genetics. A family history of early heart disease raises your risk. And research has shown that some genes are linked to a higher risk of certain heart diseases.</li><li>Race/ethnicity. Heart disease is the leading cause of death in most racial and ethnic groups in the United States. However, certain groups have higher risks than others. This includes Black people, White people, and American Indian/Alaska Native people.</li><li>Lifestyle habits. Over time, unhealthy lifestyle habits can raise your risk of heart disease. These can include: 
	<ul><li>Eating a diet high in saturated fats, refined carbohydrates, and salt.</li><li>Not getting enough physical activity.</li><li>Drinking too much alcohol.</li><li>Smoking and exposure to secondhand smoke.</li><li>Too much stress.</li></ul>
</li><li>Having other medical conditions can raise your risk of heart diseases. These conditions include:
	<ul><li>High blood pressure.</li><li>High cholesterol levels.</li><li>Diabetes.</li><li>Obesity.</li><li>Autoimmune and inflammatory diseases.</li><li>Chronic kidney disease.</li><li>Metabolic syndrome.</li></ul>
</li></ul>What are the symptoms of heart disease?<p>Your symptoms will depend on the type of heart disease you have. You may not have symptoms at first. In some cases, you may not know you have heart disease until you have a complication such as a heart attack.</p>How are heart diseases diagnosed?<p>To find out if you have heart disease, your health care provider will:</p><ul><li>Ask about your medical history, including your symptoms</li><li>Ask about your family health history, including relatives who have had heart disease</li><li>Do a physical exam</li><li>Likely run heart tests and blood tests</li></ul><p>In some cases, your provider may refer you to a cardiologist (a doctor who specializes in heart diseases) for tests, diagnosis, and care.</p>What are the treatments for heart disease?<p>Treatment plans for heart disease depend on the type of heart disease you have, how serious your symptoms are, and what other health conditions you have. Possible treatments may include:</p><ul><li>Heart-healthy lifestyle changes</li><li>Medicines</li><li>Procedures or surgeries</li><li>Cardiac rehabilitation</li></ul>Can heart diseases be prevented?<p>You may be able to lower your risk of certain heart diseases by making heart-healthy lifestyle changes  and managing any other medical conditions you have.</p><p>NIH: National Heart, Lung, and Blood Institute</p></content>
      <content name="mesh">Heart Diseases</content>
      <content name="groupName">Older Adults</content>
      <content name="groupName">Blood, Heart and Circulation</content>

    <document rank="7" url="https://medlineplus.gov/carotidarterydisease.html">
      <content name="title">Carotid Artery Disease</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Carotid Endarterectomy</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>Your carotid arteries are two large blood vessels in your neck. They supply your brain and head with blood.  If you have carotid artery disease, the arteries become narrow or blocked, usually because of atherosclerosis. Atherosclerosis is the buildup of plaque, which is made up of fat, cholesterol, calcium, and other substances found in the blood.</p><p>Carotid artery disease is serious because it can block the blood flow to your brain, causing a <span class="qt0">stroke</span>. Too much plaque in the artery can cause a blockage. You can also have a blockage when a piece of plaque or a blood clot breaks off the wall of an artery. The plaque or clot can travel through the bloodstream and get stuck in one of your brain's smaller arteries.</p><p>Carotid artery disease often does not cause symptoms until the blockage or narrowing is severe.  One sign may be a bruit (whooshing sound) that your doctor hears when listening to your artery with a stethoscope. Another sign is a transient ischemic attack (TIA), a "mini-<span class="qt0">stroke</span>." A TIA is like a <span class="qt0">stroke</span>, but it only lasts a few minutes, and the symptoms usually go away within an hour. <span class="qt0">Stroke</span> is another sign.</p><p>Imaging tests can confirm whether you have carotid artery disease.</p><p>Treatments may include:</p><ul><li>Healthy lifestyle changes</li><li>Medicines</li><li>Carotid endarterectomy, surgery to remove the plaque</li><li>Angioplasty, a procedure to place a balloon and stent into the artery to open it and hold it open</li></ul><p>NIH: National Heart, Lung, and Blood Institute</p></content>
      <content name="mesh">Carotid Artery Diseases</content>
      <content name="groupName">Blood, Heart and Circulation</content>

Too much plaque in the artery can cause  ... is a transient ischemic attack (TIA), a "mini-<span class="qt0">stroke</span>." A TIA is like a <span class="qt0">stroke</span>, but it  ... </content>

    <document rank="8" url="https://medlineplus.gov/heartattack.html">
      <content name="title">Heart Attack</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">MI</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Myocardial Infarction</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>Each year almost 800,000 Americans have a heart attack. A heart attack happens when blood flow to the heart suddenly becomes blocked. Without the blood coming in, the heart can't get oxygen. If not treated quickly, the heart muscle begins to die. But if you do get quick treatment, you may be able to prevent or limit damage to the heart muscle. That's why it's important to know the symptoms of a heart attack and call 911 if you or someone else is having them. You should call, even if you are not sure that it is a heart attack.</p><p>The most common symptoms in men and women are:</p><ul><li>Chest discomfort. It is often in center or left side of the chest. It usually lasts more than a few minutes. It may go away and come back. It can feel like pressure, squeezing, fullness, or pain. It also can feel like heartburn or indigestion.</li><li>Shortness of breath. Sometimes this is your only symptom. You may get it before or during the chest discomfort. It can happen when you are resting or doing a little bit of physical activity.</li><li>Discomfort in the upper body. You may feel pain or discomfort in one or both arms, the back, shoulders, neck, jaw, or upper part of the stomach.</li></ul><p>You may also have other symptoms, such as nausea, vomiting, dizziness, and lightheadedness. You may break out in a cold sweat. Sometimes women will have different symptoms then men. For example, they are more likely to feel tired for no reason.</p><p>The most common cause of heart attacks is coronary artery disease (CAD). With CAD, there is a buildup of cholesterol and other material, called plaque, on their inner walls or the arteries. This is atherosclerosis. It can build up for years. Eventually an area of plaque can rupture (break open). A blood clot can form around the plaque and block the artery.</p><p>A less common cause of heart attack is a severe spasm (tightening) of a coronary artery. The spasm cuts off blood flow through the artery.</p><p>At the hospital, health care providers make a diagnosis based on your symptoms, blood tests, and different heart health tests. Treatments may include medicines and medical procedures such as coronary angioplasty. After a heart attack, cardiac rehabilitation and lifestyle changes can help you recover.</p><p>NIH: National Heart, Lung, and Blood Institute</p></content>
      <content name="mesh">Myocardial Infarction</content>
      <content name="groupName">Blood, Heart and Circulation</content>
      <content name="snippet"> Each year almost 800,000 Americans have a heart attack. A heart attack happens when blood flow to the heart suddenly becomes blocked. Without the blood coming  ... </content>
