    <document rank="9" url="https://medlineplus.gov/atrialfibrillation.html">
      <content name="title">Atrial Fibrillation</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">AF</content>
      <content name="altTitle">AFib</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is atrial fibrillation (AFib)?<p>Atrial fibrillation, also known as AFib or AF, is one of the most common types of arrhythmias.  Arrhythmias are problems with the rate or rhythm of your heartbeat. They can cause your heart to beat too slowly, too fast, or in an irregular way.</p><p>If you have AFib, your heart beats irregularly and sometimes much faster than normal. Also, your heart's upper and lower chambers do not work together as they should. When this happens, the lower chambers do not fill completely or pump enough blood to your lungs and body. This can cause symptoms such as dizziness, fatigue, and a pounding heartbeat.</p><p>AFib may happen in brief episodes, or it may be a permanent condition. It's very important to treat it, since AFib can put you at risk for <span class="qt0">stroke</span> and other heart conditions.</p>What causes atrial fibrillation (AFib)?<p>AFib is most often caused by changes to the heart's tissue or the electrical signaling that helps the heartbeat. These changes can happen due to different conditions and factors, such as high blood pressure, coronary artery disease, congenital heart defects, infections, and aging. Sometimes the cause is unknown.</p>Who is more likely to develop atrial fibrillation (AFib)?<p>Anyone can develop AFib, but there are certain things that raise your risk for it:</p><ul><li>Aging. The risk of atrial fibrillation increases as you get older, especially when you are over age 65.</li><li>Family history and genetics. AFib can run in families. So can heart disease, which raises your risk of AFib.</li><li>Some lifestyle choices. Your risk is higher if you drink a lot of alcohol, use certain illegal drugs such as cocaine and methamphetamines, or smoke.</li><li>Having certain health conditions, such as:
  <ul><li>High blood pressure</li><li>Diabetes</li><li>Heart failure</li><li>Heart valve diseases</li><li>Obesity</li><li>Hyperthyroidism</li><li>Chronic kidney disease</li><li>COPD and other lung diseases</li><li>Sleep apnea</li></ul>
</li><li>Race. AFib is more common in people with European ancestry.</li><li>Recent surgery. You may be at risk of atrial fibrillation in the early days and weeks after surgery on your heart, lungs, or esophagus.</li></ul>What are the symptoms of atrial fibrillation (AFib)?<p>Some people who have AFib don't have any symptoms and don't know they have it. If you do have symptoms, you may only notice them once in a while. Or you may have symptoms that are more frequent. And in some cases, the symptoms might be severe. If you have heart disease, you are more likely to notice your symptoms. And those symptoms could get worse if your heart disease gets worse.</p><p>The symptoms of AFib can include:</p><ul><li>Extreme fatigue, which is the most common symptom</li><li>Heart palpitations (the feeling that your heart is skipping a beat, fluttering, pounding, or beating too hard or too fast)</li><li>Trouble breathing, especially when lying down or when exercising</li><li>Chest pain</li><li>Dizziness or fainting</li><li>Low blood pressure</li></ul>What other problems can AFib cause?<p>If AFib is not treated, it can lead to serious health problems (complications) such as:</p><ul><li><span class="qt0">Stroke</span></li><li>Heart failure</li><li>Blood clots</li><li>Sudden cardiac arrest (SCA)</li><li>Cognitive impairment and dementia</li></ul><p>To help prevent these problems, it's important to contact your health care provider if you are having symptoms. If you do have AFib, the sooner you are diagnosed and treated, the better.</p>How is atrial fibrillation (AFib) diagnosed?<p>To find out if you have AFib, your provider:</p><ul><li>Will ask about your medical history, including your symptoms, lifestyle, and any other health conditions you may have</li><li>Will ask about your family history, to find out if you have relatives who have or had AFib</li><li>Will do a physical exam</li><li>May order blood tests</li><li>Will likely order heart tests, such as an electrocardiogram (also called an EKG or ECG) and echocardiogram</li><li>May ask you to wear a heart monitor device that records your heart's electrical activity</li></ul>What are the treatments for atrial fibrillation (AFib)?<p>The treatments for AFib may include:</p><ul><li>Blood thinner medicines that help prevent blood clots from forming.</li><li>Medicines to control your heart's rhythm and rate.</li><li>Following heart-healthy lifestyle changes, such as:
  <ul><li>Following a heart-healthy eating plan that limits saturated fats, salt, and cholesterol. An example is the DASH eating plan.</li><li>Limiting or avoiding alcohol, because it can increase your heart rate.</li><li>Aiming for a healthy weight.</li><li>Getting regular physical activity.</li><li>Managing stress.</li><li>Quitting smoking.</li></ul>
</li><li>Procedures such as:
  <ul><li>Electrical cardioversion, which restores your heart rhythm using low-energy shocks to your heart.</li><li>Catheter ablation, which scars the tissue that is causing the arrhythmia. The scar tissue blocks the abnormal heart signals.</li></ul>
</li><li>Surgeries such as:
  <ul><li>Surgery to put in a pacemaker to help control the arrhythmia.</li><li>A Maze procedure, which creates scar tissue in a maze-like pattern in certain parts of the heart.</li><li>Left atrial appendage closure, a surgery on a small sac in the muscle wall of your left atrium (the upper left chamber of your heart). It helps prevent blood clots and can reduce your risk of <span class="qt0">stroke</span>. This surgery is for people who are not able to take blood thinners.</li></ul>
</li></ul>Can atrial fibrillation (AFib) be prevented?<p>There are steps you can take to help lower your risk of atrial fibrillation, such as:</p><ul><li>Making heart-healthy lifestyle changes:
  <ul><li>Following a heart-healthy eating plan</li><li>Limiting or avoiding alcohol</li><li>Aiming for a healthy weight</li><li>Getting regular physical activity</li><li>Managing stress</li><li>Not smoking</li></ul>
</li><li>Avoiding illegal drugs, such as cocaine and methamphetamines</li><li>Taking antiarrhythmic medicine (medicine to treat arrhythmia) if you are having heart surgery</li><li>Treating any health conditions that could raise your risk of AFib</li></ul><p>NIH: National Heart, Lung, and Blood Institute</p></content>
      <content name="mesh">Atrial Fibrillation</content>
      <content name="groupName">Blood, Heart and Circulation</content>

    <document rank="10" url="https://medlineplus.gov/cholesterolmedicines.html">
      <content name="title">Cholesterol Medicines</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is cholesterol?<p>Cholesterol is a waxy, fat-like substance that's found in all the cells in your body. Your body needs some cholesterol to work properly.  But if you have too much of it in your blood, it can stick to the walls of your arteries and narrow or even block them. This puts you at risk for coronary artery disease and other heart diseases.</p><p>Cholesterol is made by your liver. It travels through the blood on proteins called lipoproteins. One type of lipoprotein, LDL, is sometimes called the "bad" cholesterol. A high LDL level leads to a buildup of cholesterol in your arteries. Another type, HDL, is sometimes called the "good" cholesterol. It carries cholesterol from other parts of your body back to your liver. Then your liver removes the cholesterol from your body.</p>What are the treatments for high cholesterol?<p>If you have high cholesterol, lifestyle changes can help you lower your cholesterol levels. But sometimes the lifestyle changes are not enough, and you need to take cholesterol medicines. But you should still continue with the lifestyle changes even though you are taking medicines.</p>Who needs cholesterol medicines?<p>Your health care provider may prescribe cholesterol medicines for you if:</p><ul><li>You have already had a heart attack or <span class="qt0">stroke</span>, or you have peripheral arterial disease</li><li>Your LDL (bad) cholesterol level is 190 mg/dL or higher</li><li>You are 40-75 years old, you have diabetes, and your LDL cholesterol level is 70 mg/dL or higher</li><li>You are 40-75 years old, you have a high risk of developing heart disease or <span class="qt0">stroke</span>, and your LDL cholesterol level is 70 mg/dL or higher</li></ul>What are the different types of cholesterol medicines?<p>There are several types of cholesterol-lowering drugs available, including:</p><ul><li>Statins. They reduce the amount of cholesterol made in the liver. They also increase your liver's ability to remove LDL cholesterol that is already in the blood. Statins are the most common medicines used to treat high cholesterol.</li><li>Bile acid sequestrants.  They keep bile acids, which digest fats and oils, from being absorbed into the body.  But your body needs bile acids, so your liver will make more of them. It makes them by breaking down LDL cholesterol, so this helps lower your LDL cholesterol. Your provider may prescribe these medicines if you cannot take statins or if statins alone are not lowering your cholesterol enough.</li><li>Cholesterol absorption inhibitors.  They block how cholesterol is absorbed into the body. They are also used if you cannot take statins or if statins alone are not lowering your cholesterol enough.</li><li>Nicotinic acid (niacin).  It is a type of B vitamin. It reduces the amount of fats made by your liver, so it lowers your LDL cholesterol and triglycerides. Niacin also raises HDL cholesterol. Even though you can buy niacin without a prescription, you should talk to your provider before taking it to lower your cholesterol. High doses of niacin can cause serious side effects.</li><li>PCSK9 inhibitors.  These are medicines that you inject under your skin. They block a protein called PCSK9. This helps your liver remove and clear LDL cholesterol from your blood. Your provider may prescribe a PCSK9 inhibitor and a statin if you are at high risk of complications like heart attack or <span class="qt0">stroke</span>, or if you have familial hypercholesterolemia (FH). FH is an inherited disorder that causes very high levels of cholesterol in the blood.</li><li>Fibrates.  They lower triglycerides in several complex ways, including reducing the amount of triglycerides made by the liver. They may also help increase HDL cholesterol and may lower LDL levels a bit. If you take them with statins, it might increase the risk of side effects.</li><li>Combination medicines. They include more than one type of cholesterol-lowering medicine. There are also combination medicines that can treat both high blood pressure and cholesterol.</li></ul><p>There are also a few other cholesterol medicines (lomitapide and mipomersen) that are only for people who have FH.</p>How does my health care provider decide which cholesterol medicine I should take?<p>When deciding which medicine you should take and which dose you need, your provider will consider:</p><ul><li>Your cholesterol levels.</li><li>Your risk for heart disease and <span class="qt0">stroke</span>.</li><li>Your age.</li><li>Any other health problems you have.</li><li>Possible side effects of the medicines. Higher doses are more likely to cause side effects, especially over time.</li></ul><p>Medicines can help control your cholesterol, but they don't cure it. You need to keep taking your medicines and get regular cholesterol checks to make sure that your cholesterol levels are in a healthy range.</p></content>

    <document rank="11" url="https://medlineplus.gov/arteriovenousmalformations.html">
      <content name="title">Arteriovenous Malformations</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">AVM</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What are arteriovenous malformations (AVMs)?<p>Arteriovenous malformations (AVMs) are defects in your vascular system.  Your vascular system is your body's network of blood vessels. It includes your:</p><ul><li>Arteries, which carry oxygen-rich blood from your heart to your tissues and organs.</li><li>Veins, which carry the blood and waste products back to your heart.</li><li>Capillaries, which are tiny blood vessels that connect your small arteries to your small veins.</li></ul><p>An AVM is an abnormal tangle of arteries and veins. They are connected to each other, with no capillaries between them. Without the capillaries, the blood flow from the arteries goes directly into the veins at a faster rate than normal. Because of this, the nearby tissue does not get all the oxygen it would normally get. This lack of oxygen can lead to tissue damage and the death of nerve cells and other cells. The fast rate of blood flow can also increase the blood pressure inside the arteries and veins. This can weaken the arteries and veins. A weakened artery or blood vessel could burst or leak blood.</p>What causes arteriovenous malformations (AVMs)?<p>AVMs are rare. The cause of AVMs is unknown. Most of the time, people are born with them. But sometimes they can appear shortly after birth or later in life.</p>What are the symptoms of arteriovenous malformations (AVMs)?<p>The symptoms of AVM will depend on where the AVM is located. They can happen anywhere in the body, but they are more common in the brain or spinal cord. Most people with brain or spinal cord AVMs have few, if any, major symptoms. But if a weakened blood vessel bursts, it can spill blood into the brain (called a hemorrhage). Severe hemorrhages can cause a <span class="qt0">stroke</span> and brain damage.</p><p>If an AVM is causing symptoms, they can include:</p><ul><li>Seizures</li><li>Headache</li><li>Pain in the area where the AVM is located</li><li>Vision problems</li><li>Muscle weakness</li><li>Problems with movement and speech</li><li>Confusion</li><li>Dizziness</li><li>Loss of consciousness</li></ul>How are arteriovenous malformations (AVMs) diagnosed?<p>To find out if you have an AVM, your health care provider:</p><ul><li>Will ask about your symptoms and medical history.</li><li>Will do a physical exam. This may include listening for a bruit. A bruit is whooshing sound caused by the rapid blood flow through the arteries and veins of an AVM.</li><li>May order imaging tests, such as a CT scan, MRI, ultrasound, or cerebral angiography. For a cerebral angiography, you are injected with a special dye that helps the blood vessels in your brain show up on x-rays.</li></ul>What are the treatments for arteriovenous malformations (AVMs)?<p>Treatments for AVMs will depend on factors such as:</p><ul><li>Where the AVM is located</li><li>Its size</li><li>Your symptoms</li><li>Your overall health</li><li>Your risk of bleeding</li></ul><p>The treatment options may include:</p><ul><li>Monitoring for any signs that may mean you have an increased risk of hemorrhage.</li><li>Medicines to help with the symptoms from AVMs.</li><li>Surgery, which may be done if you have a higher-than-usual risk of bleeding. There are a few different types of surgery for AVMs. In some cases, you may have more than one type. Surgery can be risky, especially when it's done on the brain or spinal cord. So you and your provider will need to discuss the risks and benefits of doing the surgery before you make a decision. </li></ul><p>NIH: National Institute of Neurological Disorders and <span class="qt0">Stroke</span></p></content>
      <content name="mesh">Arteriovenous Malformations</content>
      <content name="groupName">Blood, Heart and Circulation</content>

    <document rank="12" url="https://medlineplus.gov/atherosclerosis.html">
      <content name="title">Atherosclerosis</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Arteriosclerosis</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Hardening of the Arteries</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Plaque, Atherosclerotic</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is atherosclerosis?<p>Atherosclerosis is a condition in which plaque builds up inside your arteries. Plaque is a sticky substance made up of cholesterol, fat, blood cells, calcium, and other substances found in the blood. Over time, plaque hardens and causes your arteries to narrow. That limits the flow of oxygen-rich blood to your body.</p><p>Some people may confuse atherosclerosis and arteriosclerosis, but they are not the same thing:</p><ul><li>Arteriosclerosis is hardening of the arteries, which means that the arteries thicken and become less flexible. It can have several different causes.</li><li>Atherosclerosis, which develops from plaque buildup, is a common type of arteriosclerosis.</li></ul><p>Atherosclerosis can affect most of the arteries in the body. It has different names, based on which arteries are affected:</p><ul><li>Coronary artery disease (CAD) is plaque buildup in the arteries of your heart.
</li><li>Peripheral artery disease (PAD) is plaque buildup in the arteries that carry blood away from the heart to other parts of the body. It most often affects the arteries of your legs, but it can also affect the arteries of your arms or pelvis.
</li><li>Carotid artery disease is plaque buildup in the neck arteries. It reduces blood flow to the brain.
</li><li>Renal artery stenosis is plaque buildup in the arteries that supply blood to your kidneys.
</li><li>Vertebral artery disease is plaque buildup in the arteries that supply blood to the back of your brain. 
</li><li>Mesenteric artery ischemia is plaque buildup in the arteries that supply your intestines with blood. 
</li></ul>What causes atherosclerosis?<p>Plaque often starts to build up during childhood and gets worse with age. The exact cause is unknown, but researchers believe that this buildup happens when there is damage to the arteries. This damage may be caused by unhealthy lifestyle habits, medical conditions, and your genes.</p>Who is more likely to develop atherosclerosis?<p>You may be more likely to develop atherosclerosis if you:</p><ul><li>Have certain medical conditions, including:
	<ul><li>High blood pressure</li><li>High blood cholesterol</li><li>Diabetes</li><li>Metabolic syndrome</li><li>Inflammatory diseases such as rheumatoid arthritis and psoriasis</li></ul>
</li><li>Have a family history of high blood cholesterol</li><li>Eat a lot of foods high in saturated fats</li><li>Smoke or chew tobacco</li><li>Are older - the risk increases after age 45 men and age 55 in women</li></ul>What are the symptoms of atherosclerosis?<p>In the early stages, atherosclerosis often does not cause any symptoms. You may first notice some symptoms at times when your body needs more oxygen. For example, this could be when you are having physical or emotional stress.</p><p>Your symptoms will depend on which arteries are affected and how much blood flow is blocked:</p><ul><li>With coronary artery disease, the symptoms may include angina (a type of chest pain), palpitations (racing or pounding heart), and shortness of breath.</li><li>With carotid artery disease, you may have a bruit. This is a whooshing sound that your health care provider hears when using a stethoscope. You could also have a transient ischemic attack (TIA), sometimes called a mini-<span class="qt0">stroke</span>.</li><li>With peripheral artery disease, you may have pain, aching, heaviness, or cramping in the legs when walking or climbing stairs.</li><li>With vertebral artery disease, you may have problems with thinking and memory, weakness or numbness on one side of the body or face, and vision trouble. You could also have a transient ischemic attack.</li><li>With mesenteric artery ischemia, the symptoms can include severe pain after meals, weight loss, and diarrhea.</li></ul><p>For men, erectile dysfunction (ED) is an early warning sign that you may be at higher risk for atherosclerosis and its complications. If you have ED, talk with your provider about your risk of plaque buildup.</p>What other problems can atherosclerosis cause?<p>Atherosclerosis can cause other health problems, or complications. For example, if a plaque bursts, a blood clot may form. The clot could block the artery completely or travel to another part of the body. Other possible complications can vary, depending on which arteries are affected. For example, blockages in different parts of the body can lead to complications such as a heart attack, <span class="qt0">stroke</span>, vascular dementia, or limb loss.</p>How is atherosclerosis diagnosed?<p>To find out if you have atherosclerosis, your provider:</p><ul><li>Will ask about your medical and family health history</li><li>Will ask about your lifestyle and risk factors for plaque buildup in the arteries</li><li>Will do a physical exam, which will include listening to your heart and the blood flow in your arteries</li><li>Will likely order tests, such as blood tests and heart health tests</li></ul>What are the treatments for atherosclerosis?<p>If you have atherosclerosis, your provider will work with you to create a treatment plan that works for you. Your plan will depend on which arteries are affected, how much the blood flow is blocked, and what other medical conditions you have. Possible treatments may include:</p><ul><li>Heart-healthy lifestyle changes.</li><li>Medicines to:
	<ul><li>Manage your risk factors.</li><li>Treat atherosclerosis or its complications.</li><li>Treat any medical conditions you have that can worsen plaque buildup.</li></ul>
</li><li>Procedures or surgeries to treat diseases or complications that were caused by plaque buildup. The specific type of procedure or surgery will depend on which arteries are affected.</li><li>Cardiac rehabilitation, if you have had certain complications from atherosclerosis.</li></ul>Can atherosclerosis be prevented?<p>There are steps you can take to try to prevent atherosclerosis:</p><ul><li>Choose heart-healthy foods, such fruits, vegetables, and whole grains. Limit foods that are high in saturated fats, salt, and added sugars.</li><li>Do regular physical activity. But before you start an exercise program, ask your provider what level of physical activity is right for you.</li><li>Aim for a healthy weight.</li><li>Limit how much alcohol you drink. Drinking less is better for health than drinking more. Men should limit their intake to 2 drinks or less in a day. Women should drink 1 drink or less per day.</li><li>Manage stress.</li><li>If you smoke, quit smoking.</li><li>Avoid secondhand smoke.</li><li>Get enough good-quality sleep.</li></ul><p>NIH: National Heart, Lung, and Blood Institute</p></content>

      <content name="groupName">Blood, Heart and Circulation</content>

    <document rank="13" url="https://medlineplus.gov/cholesterol.html">
      <content name="title">Cholesterol</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Familial Combined Hyperlipidemia</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Familial Dysbetalipoproteinemia</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Familial Hypercholesterolemia</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Hypercholesterolemia</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Hyperlipidemia</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Hyperlipoproteinemia</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is cholesterol?<p>Cholesterol is a waxy, fat-like substance that's found in all the cells in your body. Your body needs some cholesterol to make hormones, vitamin D, and substances that help you digest foods. Your body makes all the cholesterol it needs. Cholesterol is also found in foods from animal sources, such as egg yolks, meat, and cheese.</p><p>If you have too much cholesterol in your blood, it can combine with other substances in the blood to form plaque. Plaque sticks to the walls of your arteries. This buildup of plaque is known as atherosclerosis. It can lead to coronary artery disease, a condition in which your coronary arteries become narrow or even blocked.</p>What are HDL, LDL, and VLDL?<p>HDL, LDL, and VLDL are lipoproteins. They are a combination of fat (lipid) and protein. The lipids need to be attached to the proteins so they can move through the blood. Different types of lipoproteins have different purposes:</p><ul><li>HDL stands for high-density lipoprotein. It is sometimes called "good" cholesterol because it helps your body get rid of cholesterol.  It carries cholesterol from other parts of your body back to your liver. Your liver then removes the cholesterol from your body.</li><li>LDL stands for low-density lipoprotein. It is sometimes called "bad" cholesterol because a high LDL level leads to the buildup of plaque in your arteries.</li><li>VLDL stands for very low-density lipoprotein. Some people also call VLDL a "bad" cholesterol because it too contributes to the buildup of plaque in your arteries. But VLDL and LDL are different; VLDL mainly carries triglycerides and LDL mainly carries cholesterol.</li></ul>What causes high cholesterol?<p>The most common cause of high cholesterol is an unhealthy lifestyle. This can include:</p><ul><li>Unhealthy eating habits, such as eating lots of saturated fats. These fats are found in red meats, full-fat dairy products, chocolate, some baked goods, and many deep-fried and processed foods.  Eating too much saturated fat can raise your LDL (bad) cholesterol.</li><li>Lack of physical activity, with lots of sitting and little exercise. This lowers your HDL (good) cholesterol.</li><li>Smoking, which lowers HDL cholesterol, especially in women. It also raises your LDL cholesterol.</li><li>Stress, which may raise levels of certain hormones such as corticosteroids. These can cause your body to make more cholesterol.</li><li>Drinking too much alcohol, which can raise your total cholesterol level.</li></ul><p>Genetics may also cause people to have high cholesterol. For example, familial hypercholesterolemia (FH) is an inherited condition that causes very high levels of cholesterol in the blood. Other medical conditions and certain medicines may also raise LDL cholesterol levels or lower HDL cholesterol levels.</p>What can raise my risk of high cholesterol?<p>A variety of things can raise your risk of high cholesterol:</p><ul><li>Age. Your cholesterol levels tend to rise as you get older. Even though it is less common, younger people, including children and teens, can also have high cholesterol.</li><li>Sex. Between ages 20 and 39, men have a greater risk of high total cholesterol than women. But after menopause, a woman's risk goes up. This happens because menopause lowers levels of female hormones that may protect against high blood cholesterol.</li><li>Family history. High blood cholesterol can run in families.</li><li>Other health conditions. Conditions such as diabetes, chronic kidney disease, HIV, and lupus can raise your risk of high cholesterol.
</li><li>Medicines. Certain medicines can raise your level of LDL cholesterol or lower your level of HDL cholesterol, including:
  <ul><li>Steroids</li><li>Some chemotherapy medicines</li><li>Medicines taken after an organ transplant</li><li>Medicines for certain heart conditions</li><li>Certain acne medicines</li></ul>
</li><li>Race or ethnicity. People from certain racial or ethnic groups may have an increased risk of high cholesterol. For example, Asian Americans are more likely to have high levels of LDL cholesterol than other groups. And non-Hispanic White people are more likely than other groups to have high levels of total cholesterol.</li><li>Weight. Being overweight or having obesity raises your cholesterol level.</li></ul>What other health problems can high cholesterol cause?<p>Undiagnosed or untreated high blood cholesterol can lead to serious health problems:</p><ul><li>If you have large deposits of plaque in your arteries, an area of plaque can rupture (break open). This can cause a blood clot to form on the surface of the plaque. If the clot becomes large enough, it can mostly or completely block blood flow in a coronary artery.</li><li>If the flow of oxygen-rich blood to your heart muscle is reduced or blocked, it can cause angina (chest pain) or a heart attack.</li><li>Plaque also can build up in other arteries in your body, including the arteries that bring oxygen-rich blood to your brain and limbs. This can lead to problems such as carotid artery disease, <span class="qt0">stroke</span>, and peripheral arterial disease.</li></ul>How is high cholesterol diagnosed?<p>There are usually no signs or symptoms that you have high cholesterol. A blood test can measure your cholesterol levels. When and how often you should get this test depends on your age, risk factors, and family history.  The general recommendations are:</p><p>For people who are age 19 or younger:</p><ul><li>The first test should be between ages 9 to 11</li><li>Children should have the test again every 5 years</li><li>Some children may have this test starting at age 2 if there is a family history of high blood cholesterol, heart attack, or <span class="qt0">stroke</span></li></ul><p>For people who are ages 20 to 65:</p><ul><li>Younger adults should have the test every 5 years</li><li>Men ages 45 to 65 and women ages 55 to 65 should have it every 1 to 2 years</li></ul><p>For people over age 65:</p><ul><li>They should be tested every year</li></ul>How can I lower my cholesterol?<p>You can lower your cholesterol through heart-healthy lifestyle changes. They include a heart-healthy eating plan, weight management, and regular physical activity.</p><p>If the lifestyle changes alone do not lower your cholesterol enough, you may also need to take medicines. There are several types of cholesterol-lowering medicines available, including statins. If you take medicines to lower your cholesterol, you still should continue with the lifestyle changes.</p><p>Some people with familial hypercholesterolemia (FH) may receive a treatment called lipoprotein apheresis. This treatment uses a filtering machine to remove LDL cholesterol from the blood. Then the machine returns the rest of the blood back to the person.</p><p>NIH: National Heart, Lung, and Blood Institute</p></content>

How is high cholesterol  ... history of high blood cholesterol, heart attack, or <span class="qt0">stroke</span>  For people who are ages 20 to 65:  ... </content>

    <document rank="14" url="https://medlineplus.gov/diabetescomplications.html">
      <content name="title">Diabetes Complications</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Diabetic complications</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is diabetes?<p>If you have diabetes, your blood glucose, or blood sugar, levels are too high. Glucose comes from the foods you eat. A hormone called insulin helps the glucose get into your cells to give them energy.</p><p> With type 1 diabetes, your body does not make insulin. With type 2 diabetes, your body does not make or use insulin well. Without enough insulin, the glucose stays in your blood.</p>What health problems can diabetes cause?<p>Over time, having too much glucose in your blood can cause complications, including:</p><ul><li>Eye disease, due to changes in fluid levels, swelling in the tissues, and damage to the blood vessels in the eyes.</li><li>Foot problems, caused by damage to the nerves and reduced blood flow to your feet.</li><li>Gum disease and other dental problems, because a high amount of glucose in your saliva helps harmful bacteria grow in your mouth. The bacteria combine with food to form a soft, sticky film called plaque. Plaque also comes from eating foods that contain sugars or starches. Some types of plaque cause gum disease and bad breath. Other types cause tooth decay and cavities.</li><li>Heart disease and <span class="qt0">stroke</span>, caused by damage to your blood vessels and the nerves that control your heart and blood vessels.</li><li>Kidney disease, due to damage to the blood vessels in your kidneys. Many people with diabetes develop high blood pressure. That can also damage your kidneys.</li><li>Nerve problems (diabetic neuropathy), caused by damage to the nerves and the small blood vessels that nourish your nerves with oxygen and nutrients.</li><li>Sexual and bladder problems, caused by damage to the nerves and reduced blood flow in the genitals and bladder.</li><li>Skin conditions, some of which are caused by changes in the small blood vessels and reduced circulation. People with diabetes are also more likely to have infections, including skin infections.</li></ul>What other problems can people with diabetes have?<p>If you have diabetes, you need to watch out for blood glucose levels that are too high (hyperglycemia) or too low for you (hypoglycemia). These can happen quickly and can become dangerous. Some of the causes include having another illness or infection and certain medicines. They can also happen if you don't get the right amount of diabetes medicines. To try to prevent these problems, make sure to take your diabetes medicines correctly, follow your diabetic diet, and check your blood glucose regularly.</p><p>NIH: National Institute of Diabetes and Digestive and Kidney Diseases</p></content>
      <content name="mesh">Diabetes Complications</content>
      <content name="groupName">Endocrine System</content>
      <content name="groupName">Metabolic Problems</content>
      <content name="groupName">Diabetes Mellitus</content>
      <content name="snippet"> ... cause tooth decay and cavities.  Heart disease and <span class="qt0">stroke</span>, caused by damage to your blood vessels and  ... </content>

    <document rank="15" url="https://medlineplus.gov/diabeticheartdisease.html">
      <content name="title">Diabetic Heart Disease</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is diabetes?<p>Diabetes is a disease in which your blood glucose, or blood sugar, levels are too high. Glucose comes from foods you eat. The cells of your body need glucose for energy. A hormone called insulin helps the glucose get into your cells.</p><p>With type 1 diabetes, your body doesn't make insulin. With type 2 diabetes, your body doesn't make or use insulin well. Without enough insulin, glucose builds up in your blood and causes high blood sugar levels.</p>What is diabetic heart disease?<p>Diabetic heart disease is a term for heart disease in people who have diabetes. If you have diabetes, you are much more likely to develop heart disease or have a <span class="qt0">stroke</span> than people who don't have diabetes. And you may start having these problems at a younger age.</p><p>Over time, the effects of high blood sugar from diabetes can include damage to the blood vessels and nerves in your heart. This damage increases your chance of developing heart diseases, including:</p><ul><li>Coronary artery disease (CAD). It happens slowly as a sticky material called plaque builds up in the arteries that supply your heart muscle with blood.</li><li>Heart failure. With this condition, your heart can't pump enough oxygen-rich blood to meet the needs of your body.</li><li>Cardiomyopathy. This is a group of diseases in which the heart muscle may become thick or stiff.</li></ul>Who is more likely to develop diabetic heart disease?<p>Diabetes puts you at serious risk for heart disease. It also makes you more likely to develop other conditions that raise your risk even more, including:</p><ul><li>High blood pressure</li><li>High blood levels of LDL "bad" cholesterol</li><li>High triglycerides (a type of fat in your blood) with low blood levels of HDL "good" cholesterol</li></ul><p>Your risk for heart disease is also higher if you</p><ul><li>Are male</li><li>Smoke</li><li>Have obesity</li><li>Have too much belly fat around your waist, even though you're at a healthy weight:
	<ul><li>For men, that's a waist more than 40 inches</li><li>For women, that's a waist more than 35 inches</li></ul>
</li><li>Have a family history of heart disease</li><li>Have chronic kidney disease</li></ul>What are the symptoms of diabetic heart disease?<p>In the early stages, heart disease usually doesn't have any symptoms. But if your heart disease worsens, you can have symptoms. Your symptoms will depend on the type of heart disease you have. They might include:</p><ul><li>Shortness of breath</li><li>Fatigue</li><li>Dizziness or fainting</li><li>Arrhythmia (problem with the rate or rhythm of your heartbeat)</li><li>Swollen feet and ankles</li><li>Chest pain</li></ul><p>It's important to know that people with diabetes may not feel chest pain. That's because diabetes can damage the nerves in your heart. If you have any symptoms that could be heart disease, talk with your health care provider.</p>How is diabetic heart disease diagnosed?<p>To find out if you have diabetic heart disease, your provider will:</p><ul><li>Ask about your medical history, including your symptoms and other health conditions you may have</li><li>Ask about your family history, to find out if you have relatives who have or had heart disease</li><li>Do a physical exam</li><li>Likely run some tests to help understand your personal risk for heart disease, including:
	<ul><li>Blood tests to check your cholesterol, triglycerides, and blood sugar levels</li><li>A blood-pressure check</li><li>Heart tests, if needed</li></ul>
</li></ul><p>Depending on your risk level, your provider may send you to a cardiologist (a doctor who specializes in heart diseases) for care. If you do have heart disease, treatment will depend on the type of heart disease you have.</p>How can I prevent diabetic heart disease?<p>You may be able to prevent heart disease or keep it from getting worse by working with your provider to:</p><ul><li>Control your blood sugar levels</li><li>Manage other conditions you may have that can raise your risk for heart disease </li><li>Take any medicines your provider prescribed</li><li>Make heart-healthy habits part of your daily life</li><li>Follow a healthy eating plan for diabetes</li></ul><p>NIH: National Institute of Diabetes and Digestive and Kidney Diseases</p></content>

    <document rank="16" url="https://medlineplus.gov/encephalitis.html">
      <content name="title">Encephalitis</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is encephalitis? <p>Encephalitis is inflammation (swelling) of the brain. It can happen when an infection or medical condition that affects the brain activates your immune system. The inflammation can cause a wide range of symptoms. In extreme cases, it can lead to brain damage, <span class="qt0">stroke</span>, or even death.</p>What causes encephalitis?<p>There are different types of encephalitis, based on what the cause is. The two main types are infectious encephalitis and autoimmune encephalitis.</p><p>Infectious encephalitis is usually caused by a virus. In fact, viruses are the most common cause of encephalitis. Some of the different viruses that cause it include:</p><ul><li>Herpes viruses, including herpes simplex (HSV), the Epstein-Barr virus (which causes infectious mononucleosis) and the varicella-zoster virus (which causes chickenpox).</li><li>Viruses you can get if you are bitten by an infected tick, such as tick-borne encephalitis (TBE virus) and Powassan virus.</li><li>Viruses you can get if you are bitten by an infected mosquito, such as eastern equine encephalitis virus, West Nile virus, and La Crosse virus.</li><li>Enteroviruses, which are a common group of viruses that mostly cause mild illness or respiratory infection. These infections usually happen in the summer and fall.</li></ul><p>Bacteria, fungi, and parasites can also cause infectious encephalitis. But this is not common.</p><p>Autoimmune encephalitis happens when your immune system mistakenly attacks healthy brain cells. It can be triggered by conditions such as certain cancers, benign tumors, and infections. Sometimes the cause is not known.</p>Who is more likely to get encephalitis?<p>Anyone can get encephalitis, but you are more likely to get it if you:</p><ul><li>Have a weakened immune system, for example from having HIV or taking certain medicines. These could include medicines taken after an organ transplant, certain chemotherapy medicines, and specialized treatments for certain autoimmune diseases.</li><li>Are a young child or older adult.</li><li>Live in areas where there are ticks and mosquitoes that carry viruses that can cause encephalitis.</li></ul>What are the symptoms of encephalitis?<p>The symptoms of encephalitis can vary a lot, depending on how severe it is. Many people do not have any symptoms. Others may have mild flu-like symptoms such as fever, fatigue, headache, or body aches. If encephalitis becomes more serious, it can cause:</p><ul><li>Severe headache</li><li>Stiff neck</li><li>Vomiting</li><li>Seizures</li><li>Behavior changes</li><li>Drowsiness</li><li>Muscle weakness</li><li>Partial paralysis in your arms and legs</li><li>Coma</li></ul><p>Encephalitis can be dangerous in infants. Their symptoms may include:</p><ul><li>Fever</li><li>Lethargy (weakness or drowsiness)</li><li>Poor feeding</li><li>Vomiting</li><li>Body stiffness</li><li>Unusual irritability or crying</li><li>A full or bulging fontanel (the soft spot on the top of the head)</li></ul><p>If you or your child is having symptoms of encephalitis, it's important to get medical care right away.</p>How is encephalitis diagnosed?<p>To find out if you have encephalitis, your health care provider:</p><ul><li>Will do a physical exam</li><li>Will take your medical history, which includes asking about your symptoms</li><li>May do a neurologic exam</li><li>May order imaging tests, such as a brain CT scan or MRI</li><li>May order an EEG (electroencephalography), which use small electric sensors to measure your brain activity</li><li>May order blood and cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) tests</li></ul>What are the treatments for encephalitis?<p>Most people with encephalitis will need treatment in the hospital. Depending on the cause, treatments may include antiviral medicines, antibiotics, corticosteroids, and other medicines.</p><p>For some types of encephalitis, there is no medicine to treat it. But rest, nutrition, and fluids can help your body fight the infection and relieve symptoms. </p><p>Some people may need physical, speech, and occupational therapy once the illness is under control.</p>Can encephalitis be prevented?<p>There are steps you can take to help prevent encephalitis that is caused by infections:</p><ul><li>Use good hygiene, including washing your hands often with soap and water.</li><li>Don't share food, drinks, utensils, and glasses with other people.</li><li>Get vaccines for viruses that can cause encephalitis.</li><li>Avoid mosquito and tick bites, for example by:
  <ul><li>Wearing insect repellent with DEET or another U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA)-registered insect repellent. Make sure to follow the instructions for using the repellant.</li><li>Wearing clothes that cover your arms, legs and feet.</li><li>Treating your clothing and gear with products containing 0.5% permethrin before you go in grassy or woody areas.</li></ul>
</li></ul><p>NIH: National Institute of Neurological Disorders and <span class="qt0">Stroke</span></p></content>
      <content name="mesh">Encephalitis</content>
      <content name="groupName">Brain and Nerves</content>

  NIH: National Institute of Neurological Disorders and <span class="qt0">Stroke</span></content>

    <document rank="17" url="https://medlineplus.gov/highbloodpressure.html">
      <content name="title">High Blood Pressure</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Blood Pressure</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Hypertension</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Benign essential hypertension</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Essential hypertension</content>
      <content name="altTitle">HBP</content>
      <content name="altTitle">HTN</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is blood pressure?<p>Blood pressure is the force of your blood pushing against the walls of your arteries. Each time your heart beats, it pumps blood into the arteries. Your blood pressure is highest when your heart beats, pumping the blood. This is called systolic pressure. When your heart is at rest, between beats, your blood pressure falls. This is called diastolic pressure.</p><p>Your blood pressure reading uses these two numbers. Usually the systolic number comes before or above the diastolic number. For example, 120/80 means a systolic of 120 and a diastolic of 80.</p>How is high blood pressure diagnosed?<p>High blood pressure usually has no symptoms. So the only way to find out if you have it is to get regular blood pressure checks from your health care provider. Your provider will use a gauge, a stethoscope or electronic sensor, and a blood pressure cuff. They will take two or more readings at separate appointments before making a diagnosis.</p>Blood Pressure CategorySystolic Blood PressureDiastolic Blood PressureNormalLess than 120andLess than 80Elevated120 - 129andLess than 80High Blood Pressure Stage 1130 - 139or80 - 89High Blood Pressure Stage 2140 or higheror90 or higherHypertensive Crisis (dangerously high blood pressure - seek medical care right away)Higher than 180andHigher than 120<p>For children and teens, the health care provider compares the blood pressure reading to what is normal for other kids who are the same age, height, and sex.</p>What are the different types of high blood pressure?<p>There are two main types of high blood pressure:</p><ul><li>Primary, or essential, high blood pressure. This is the most common type of high blood pressure. For most people who get this kind of blood pressure, it develops over time as you get older.</li><li>Secondary high blood pressure. This is caused by another medical condition or use of certain medicines. It usually gets better after you treat that condition or stop taking the medicines that are causing it.</li></ul>Why do I need to worry about high blood pressure?<p>When your blood pressure stays high over time, it causes the heart to pump harder and work overtime, possibly leading to serious health problems such as heart attack, <span class="qt0">stroke</span>, heart failure, and kidney failure.</p>What are the treatments for high blood pressure?<p>Treatments for high blood pressure include heart-healthy lifestyle changes and medicines.</p><p>You will work with your provider to come up with a treatment plan. It may include only the lifestyle changes. These changes, such as heart-healthy eating and exercise, can be very effective. But sometimes the changes do not control or lower your high blood pressure. Then you may need to take medicine. There are different types of blood pressure medicines. Some people need to take more than one type.</p><p>If your high blood pressure is caused by another medical condition or medicine, treating that condition or stopping the medicine may lower your blood pressure.</p><p>NIH: National Heart, Lung, and Blood Institute</p></content>

    <document rank="18" url="https://medlineplus.gov/hydrocephalus.html">
      <content name="title">Hydrocephalus</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Water on the brain</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>Hydrocephalus is the buildup of too much cerebrospinal fluid in the brain. Normally, this fluid cushions your brain. When you have too much, though, it puts harmful pressure on your brain.</p><p>Hydrocephalus can be congenital, or present at birth. Causes include genetic problems and problems with how the fetus develops. An unusually large head is the main sign of congenital hydrocephalus.</p><p>Hydrocephalus can also happen after birth. This is called acquired hydrocephalus. It can occur at any age. Causes can include head injuries, <span class="qt0">strokes</span>, infections, tumors, and bleeding in the brain. Symptoms include:</p><ul><li>Headache</li><li>Vomiting and nausea</li><li>Blurry vision</li><li>Balance problems</li><li>Bladder control problems</li><li>Thinking and memory problems</li></ul><p>Hydrocephalus can permanently damage the brain, causing problems with physical and mental development. If untreated, it is usually fatal. With treatment, many people lead normal lives with few limitations. Treatment usually involves surgery to insert a shunt. A shunt is a flexible but sturdy plastic tube. The shunt moves the cerebrospinal fluid to another area of the body where it can be absorbed. Medicine and rehabilitation therapy can also help.</p><p>NIH: National Institute of Neurological Disorders and <span class="qt0">Stroke</span></p></content>
      <content name="mesh">Hydrocephalus</content>
      <content name="groupName">Brain and Nerves</content>
      <content name="groupName">Genetics/Birth Defects</content>

    <document rank="19" url="https://medlineplus.gov/meningitis.html">
      <content name="title">Meningitis</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Arachnoiditis</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Aseptic Meningitis</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Spinal meningitis</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>Meningitis is inflammation of the thin tissue that surrounds the brain and spinal cord, called the meninges. There are several types of meningitis. The most common is viral meningitis. You get it when a virus enters the body through the nose or mouth and travels to the brain. Bacterial meningitis is rare but can be deadly. It usually starts with bacteria that cause a cold-like infection. It can cause <span class="qt0">stroke</span>, hearing loss, and brain damage. It can also harm other organs. Pneumococcal infections and meningococcal infections are the most common causes of bacterial meningitis.</p><p>Anyone can get meningitis, but it is more common in people with weak immune systems. Meningitis can get serious very quickly. You should get medical care right away if you have:</p><ul><li>A sudden high fever </li><li>A severe headache </li><li>A stiff neck </li><li>Nausea or vomiting</li></ul><p>Early treatment can help prevent serious problems, including death. Tests to diagnose meningitis include blood tests, imaging tests, and a spinal tap to test cerebrospinal fluid. Antibiotics can treat bacterial meningitis. Antiviral medicines may help some types of viral meningitis. Other medicines can help treat symptoms.</p><p>There are vaccines to prevent some of the bacterial infections that cause meningitis.</p><p>NIH: National Institute of Neurological Disorders and <span class="qt0">Stroke</span></p></content>
      <content name="mesh">Meningitis</content>
      <content name="groupName">Infections</content>
      <content name="groupName">Brain and Nerves</content>

    <document rank="20" url="https://medlineplus.gov/prediabetes.html">
      <content name="title">Prediabetes</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Insulin Resistance</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is prediabetes?<p>Prediabetes means that your blood glucose, or blood sugar, levels are higher than normal but not high enough to be called diabetes. Glucose comes from the foods you eat. Too much glucose in your blood can damage your body over time.</p><p>If you have prediabetes, you are more likely to develop type 2 diabetes, heart disease, and <span class="qt0">stroke</span>. But if you make some lifestyle changes now, you may be able to delay or prevent type 2 diabetes.</p>What causes prediabetes?<p>Prediabetes usually happens when your body has a problem with insulin. Insulin is a hormone that helps the glucose get into your cells to give them energy. A problem with insulin could be:</p><ul><li>Insulin resistance, a condition in which the body can't use its insulin properly. It makes it hard for your cells to get glucose from your blood. This can cause your blood sugar levels to rise.</li><li>Your body can't make enough insulin to keep your blood sugar levels at a healthy level</li></ul><p>Researchers think that being overweight and not getting regular physical activity are major factors in causing prediabetes.</p>Who is at risk for prediabetes?<p>About 1 out of every 3 adults has prediabetes. It is more common in people who:</p><ul><li>Are overweight or have obesity</li><li>Are age 45 or older</li><li>Have a parent, brother, or sister with diabetes</li><li>Are African American, Alaska Native, American Indian, Asian American, Hispanic/Latino, Native Hawaiian, or Pacific Islander American</li><li>Are not physically active</li><li>Have health conditions such as high blood pressure and high cholesterol</li><li>Have had gestational diabetes (diabetes in pregnancy)</li><li>Have a history of heart disease or <span class="qt0">stroke</span></li><li>Have metabolic syndrome</li><li>Have polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS)</li></ul>What are the symptoms of prediabetes?<p>Most people don't know they have prediabetes because usually there are no symptoms.</p><p>Some people with prediabetes may have darkened skin in the armpit or on the back and sides of the neck. They may also have many small skin growths in those same areas.</p>How is prediabetes diagnosed?<p>There are a few different blood tests that can diagnose prediabetes. The most common ones are:</p><ul><li>Fasting plasma glucose (FPG) test, which measures your blood sugar at a single point in time. You need to fast (not eat or drink) for at least 8 hours before the test. The results of the test are given in mg/dL (milligrams per deciliter):
		<ul><li>A normal level is 99 or below</li><li>Prediabetes is 100 to 125</li><li>Type 2 diabetes is 126 and above</li></ul>
</li><li>A1C test, which measures your average blood sugar over the past 3 months. The results of an A1C test are given as a percentage. The higher the percentage, the higher your blood sugar levels have been. 
		<ul><li>A normal level is below 5.7%</li><li>Prediabetes is between 5.7 to 6.4%</li><li>Type 2 diabetes is above 6.5%</li></ul></li></ul>If I have prediabetes, will I get diabetes?<p>If you have prediabetes, you may be able to delay or prevent type 2 diabetes through lifestyle changes:</p><ul><li>Losing weight, if you are overweight</li><li>Getting regular physical activity</li><li>Following a healthy, reduced-calorie eating plan</li></ul><p>In some cases, your health care provider may also recommend taking diabetes medicines.</p>Can prediabetes be prevented?<p>If you are at risk for prediabetes, those same lifestyle changes (losing weight, regular physical activity, and a healthy eating plan) may prevent you from getting it.</p><p>NIH: National Institute of Diabetes and Digestive and Kidney Diseases</p></content>

    <document rank="21" url="https://medlineplus.gov/tremor.html">
      <content name="title">Tremor</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Essential Tremor</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is a tremor?<p>A tremor is a neurological condition that includes shaking or trembling movements in one or more parts of your body. It most often affects the hands. But it can also affect the arms, legs, head, vocal cords, and torso (trunk). A tremor is involuntary, meaning that you cannot control it. It happens because of muscle contractions.</p><p>A tremor may come and go, or it may be constant. It can happen on its own or be caused by another disorder. It is not life threatening, but it may cause challenges. It can make it hard to do daily life tasks such as writing, typing, eating, and dressing. In some cases, a tremor can even lead to disabilities.</p>What are the types of tremor?<p>There are several types of tremor, including:</p><ul><li>Essential tremor, sometimes called benign essential tremor or familial tremor. This is the most common type. It usually affects both your hands and arms while you are moving them. It can also affect your head, voice, or legs. </li><li>Parkinsonian tremor, which is a common symptom in people who have Parkinson's disease. It usually affects one or both hands when they are at rest, but it can affect the chin, lips, face, and legs.</li><li>Dystonic tremor, which happens in people who have dystonia. Dystonia is a movement disorder in which you have involuntary muscle contractions. The contractions cause you to have twisting and repetitive movements. It can affect any muscle in the body.</li></ul>What causes tremor?<p>Generally, tremor is caused by a problem in the deep parts of the brain that control movements. For most types, the cause is unknown. Some types are inherited and run in families. There can also be other causes, such as:</p><ul><li>Neurologic disorders, including multiple sclerosis, Parkinson's disease, <span class="qt0">stroke</span>, and traumatic brain injury</li><li>Certain medicines, such as asthma medicines, chemotherapy, corticosteroids, and medicines used for certain psychiatric and neurological disorders</li><li>Alcohol use disorder (AUD) or alcohol withdrawal</li><li>Poisoning from certain toxic substances, including pesticides and heavy metals such as mercury and lead</li><li>Hyperthyroidism (overactive thyroid)</li><li>Liver or kidney failure</li><li>Anxiety or panic</li><li>Too much caffeine</li></ul>Who is at risk for tremor?<p>Anyone can get tremor, but it is most common in middle-aged and older adults. For certain types of tremor, having a family history raises your risk of getting it.</p>What are the symptoms of tremor?<p>Symptoms of tremor may include:</p><ul><li>Rhythmic shaking in the hands, arms, head, legs, or torso</li><li>Shaky voice</li><li>Difficulty writing or drawing</li><li>Problems holding and controlling utensils, such as a spoon</li></ul>How is tremor diagnosed?<p>Your health care provider may use many tools to make a diagnosis:</p><ul><li>A medical history, which includes asking about your symptoms </li><li>A physical exam, which includes checking:
		<ul><li>Whether the tremor happens when the muscles are at rest or in action</li><li>The location of the tremor</li><li>How often you have the tremor and how strong it is</li></ul>
</li><li>A neurological exam, including checking for:
		<ul><li>Problems with balance</li><li>Problems with speech</li><li>Increased muscle stiffness</li></ul>
</li><li>Blood or urine tests to look for the cause</li><li>Imaging tests to help figure out if the cause is damage to your brain</li><li>Tests that check your abilities to do daily tasks such as handwriting and holding a fork or cup</li><li>An electromyogram, a test that measures involuntary muscle activity and how your muscles respond to nerve stimulation</li></ul>What are the treatments for tremor?<p>There is no cure for most forms of tremor, but there are treatments to help manage symptoms. In some cases, the symptoms may be so mild that you do not need treatment.</p><p>Finding the right treatment depends on getting the right diagnosis of the cause. Tremor caused by another medical condition may get better or go away when you treat that condition. If your tremor is caused by a certain medicine, stopping that medicine usually makes the tremor go away.</p><p>Treatments for tremor can include:</p><ul><li>Medicines. There are different medicines for the specific types of tremor. Another option is Botox injections, which can treat several different types of tremor.</li><li>Surgery may be used for severe cases that do not get better with medicines. The most common type is deep brain stimulation (DBS). DBS uses electrodes that are implanted in the brain. The electrodes send electrical pulses to the parts of the brain that are causing the tremor.</li><li>Physical, speech-language, and occupational therapy, which may help to control tremor and deal with the daily challenges caused by the tremor.</li></ul><p>If you find that caffeine and other stimulants trigger your tremors, it may be helpful to cut them from your diet.</p><p>NIH: National Institute of Neurological Disorders and <span class="qt0">Stroke</span></p></content>
      <content name="mesh">Tremor</content>

      <content name="groupName">Brain and Nerves</content>

NIH: National Institute of Neurological Disorders and <span class="qt0">Stroke</span></content>

    <document rank="22" url="https://medlineplus.gov/amyotrophiclateralsclerosis.html">
      <content name="title">Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">ALS</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Lou Gehrig's Disease</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS) is a nervous system disease that attacks nerve cells called neurons in your brain and spinal cord. These neurons transmit messages from your brain and spinal cord to your voluntary muscles - the ones you can control, like in your arms and legs. At first, this causes mild muscle problems. Some people notice:</p><ul><li>	Trouble walking or running </li><li>	Trouble writing </li><li>	Speech problems</li></ul><p>Eventually, you lose your strength and cannot move. When muscles in your chest fail, you cannot breathe. A breathing machine can help, but most people with ALS die from respiratory failure. </p><p>The disease usually strikes between age 40 and 60. More men than women get it. No one knows what causes ALS. It can run in families, but usually it strikes at random. There is no cure. Medicines can relieve symptoms and, sometimes, prolong survival. </p><p>NIH: National Institute of Neurological Disorders and <span class="qt0">Stroke</span></p></content>
      <content name="mesh">Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis</content>
      <content name="groupName">Brain and Nerves</content>

    <document rank="24" url="https://medlineplus.gov/ataxiatelangiectasia.html">
      <content name="title">Ataxia Telangiectasia</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>Ataxia-telangiectasia (A-T) is a rare, inherited disease. It affects the nervous system, immune system, and other body systems.  Symptoms appear in young children, usually before age 5. They include:</p><ul><li>Ataxia - trouble coordinating movements</li><li>Poor balance </li><li>Slurred speech</li><li>Tiny, red spider veins, called telangiectasias, on the skin and eyes</li><li>Lung infections</li><li>Delayed physical and sexual development</li></ul><p>People with A-T have an increased risk of developing diabetes and cancers, especially lymphoma and leukemia. Although it affects the brain, people with A-T usually have normal or high intelligence.</p><p>A-T has no cure. Treatments might improve some symptoms. They include injections to strengthen the immune system, physical and speech therapy, and high-dose vitamins. </p><p>NIH: National Institute of Neurological Disorders and <span class="qt0">Stroke</span></p></content>
      <content name="mesh">Ataxia Telangiectasia</content>
      <content name="groupName">Brain and Nerves</content>

      <content name="snippet"> ... vitamins.  NIH: National Institute of Neurological Disorders and <span class="qt0">Stroke</span></content>

    <document rank="0" url="https://medlineplus.gov/kidneycysts.html">
      <content name="title"><span class="qt0">Kidney</span> Cysts</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Cystic <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">Kidney</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">Diseases</span></span></content>
      <content name="altTitle">Polycystic <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">Kidney</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">Disease</span></span></content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p> A cyst is a fluid-filled sac. You may get simple <span class="qt0">kidney</span> cysts as you age; they are usually harmless. There are also some <span class="qt1">diseases</span> which cause <span class="qt0">kidney</span> cysts. One type is polycystic <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">kidney</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span> (PKD). It runs in families. In PKD, many cysts grow in the <span class="qt0">kidneys</span>. This can enlarge the <span class="qt0">kidneys</span> and make them work poorly. About half of people with the most common type of PKD end up with <span class="qt0">kidney</span> failure. PKD also causes cysts in other parts of the body, such as the liver. </p><p>Often, there are no symptoms at first. Later, symptoms include:</p><ul><li>Pain in the back and lower sides</li><li>Headaches</li><li>Blood in the urine</li></ul><p>Doctors diagnose PKD with imaging tests and family history. There is no cure. Treatments can help with symptoms and complications. They include medicines and lifestyle changes, and if there is <span class="qt0">kidney</span> failure, dialysis or <span class="qt0">kidney</span> transplants.</p><p>Acquired cystic <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">kidney</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span> (ACKD) happens in people who have chronic <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">kidney</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span>, especially if they are on dialysis. Unlike PKD, the <span class="qt0">kidneys</span> are normal sized, and cysts do not form in other parts of the body. ACKD often has no symptoms. Usually, the cysts are harmless and do not need treatment. If they do cause complications, treatments include medicines, draining the cysts, or surgery.</p><p>NIH: National Institute of Diabetes and Digestive and <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">Kidney</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">Diseases</span></span></p></content>
      <content name="mesh"><span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">Kidney</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">Diseases</span></span>, Cystic</content>
      <content name="groupName"><span class="qt0">Kidneys</span> and Urinary System</content>
      <content name="snippet">  A cyst is a fluid-filled sac. You may get simple <span class="qt0">kidney</span> cysts as you age; they are usually harmless. There are also some <span class="qt1">diseases</span> which cause <span class="qt0">kidney</span> cysts.  ... </content>

    <document rank="1" url="https://medlineplus.gov/chronickidneydisease.html">
      <content name="title">Chronic <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">Kidney</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">Disease</span></span></content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">CKD</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>You have two <span class="qt0">kidneys</span>, each about the size of your fist.  Their main job is to filter your blood. They remove wastes and extra water, which become urine. They also keep the body's chemicals balanced, help control blood pressure, and make hormones.</p><p>Chronic <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">kidney</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span> (CKD) means that your <span class="qt0">kidneys</span> are damaged and can't filter blood as they should. This damage can cause wastes to build up in your body. It can also cause other problems that can harm your health. Diabetes and high blood pressure are the most common causes of CKD.</p><p>The <span class="qt0">kidney</span> damage occurs slowly over many years. Many people don't have any symptoms until their <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">kidney</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span> is very advanced. Blood and urine tests are the only way to know if you have <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">kidney</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span>.</p><p>Treatments cannot cure <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">kidney</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span>, but they may slow <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">kidney</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span>. They include medicines to lower blood pressure, control blood sugar, and lower cholesterol. CKD may still get worse over time. Sometimes it can lead to <span class="qt0">kidney</span> failure. If your <span class="qt0">kidneys</span> fail, you will need  dialysis or a <span class="qt0">kidney</span> transplantation.</p><p>You can take steps to keep your <span class="qt0">kidneys</span> healthier longer:</p><ul><li>Choose foods with less  salt (sodium)</li><li>Control your blood pressure; your health care provider can tell you what your blood pressure should be</li><li>Keep your blood sugar in the target range, if you have diabetes</li><li>Limit the amount of alcohol you drink</li><li>Choose foods that are healthy for your heart: fruits, vegetables, whole grains, and low-fat dairy foods</li><li>Lose weight if you are overweight</li><li>Be physically active</li><li>Don't smoke</li></ul><p>NIH: National Institute of Diabetes and Digestive and <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">Kidney</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">Diseases</span></span></p></content>

    <document rank="2" url="https://medlineplus.gov/kidneydiseases.html">
      <content name="title"><span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">Kidney</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">Diseases</span></span></content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Nephritis</content>
      <content name="altTitle"><span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">Renal</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">Diseases</span></span></content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>You have two <span class="qt0">kidneys</span>, each about the size of your fist. They are near the middle of your back, just below the rib cage. Inside each <span class="qt0">kidney</span> there are about a million tiny structures called nephrons. They filter your blood. They remove wastes and extra water, which become urine.  The urine flows through tubes called ureters. It goes to your bladder, which stores the urine until you go to the bathroom.</p><p>Most <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">kidney</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">diseases</span></span> attack the nephrons. This damage may leave <span class="qt0">kidneys</span> unable to remove wastes. Causes can include genetic problems, injuries, or medicines. You have a higher risk of <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">kidney</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span> if you have diabetes, high blood pressure, or a close family member with <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">kidney</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span>. Chronic <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">kidney</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span> damages the nephrons slowly over several years. Other <span class="qt0">kidney</span> problems include:</p><ul><li>Cancer</li><li>Cysts</li><li>Stones</li><li>Infections</li></ul><p>Your doctor can do blood and urine tests to check if you have <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">kidney</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span>. If your <span class="qt0">kidneys</span> fail, you will need dialysis or a <span class="qt0">kidney</span> transplant.</p><p>NIH: National Institute of Diabetes and Digestive and <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">Kidney</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">Diseases</span></span></p></content>
      <content name="mesh"><span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">Kidney</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">Diseases</span></span></content>
      <content name="groupName"><span class="qt0">Kidneys</span> and Urinary System</content>

    <document rank="26" url="https://medlineplus.gov/urinarytractinfections.html">
      <content name="title">Urinary Tract Infections</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Bladder Infections</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Infections, Bladder</content>
      <content name="altTitle"><span class="qt0">Kidney</span> Infections</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Pyelonephritis</content>
      <content name="altTitle">UTI</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>The urinary system is the body's drainage system for removing wastes and extra water. It includes two <span class="qt0">kidneys</span>, two ureters, a bladder, and a urethra. Urinary tract infections (UTIs) are the second most common type of infection in the body.</p><p>You may have a UTI if you notice:</p><ul><li>Pain or burning when you urinate</li><li>Fever, tiredness, or shakiness</li><li>An urge to urinate often</li><li>Pressure in your lower belly </li><li>Urine that smells bad or looks cloudy or reddish</li><li>Pain in your back or side below the ribs</li></ul><p>People of any age or sex can get UTIs. But about four times as many women get UTIs as men. You're also at higher risk if you have diabetes, need a tube to drain your bladder, or have a spinal cord injury.</p><p>If you think you have a UTI it is important to see your doctor. Your doctor can tell if you have a UTI with a urine test.  Treatment is with antibiotics. </p><p>NIH: National Institute of Diabetes and Digestive and <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">Kidney</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">Diseases</span></span></p></content>
      <content name="mesh">Urinary Tract Infections</content>
      <content name="groupName"><span class="qt0">Kidneys</span> and Urinary System</content>
      <content name="groupName">Infections</content>
      <content name="snippet"> ... National Institute of Diabetes and Digestive and <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">Kidney</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">Diseases</span></span></content>

    <document rank="3" url="https://medlineplus.gov/kidneyfailure.html">
      <content name="title"><span class="qt0">Kidney</span> Failure</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">ESRD</content>
      <content name="altTitle">End-Stage <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">Renal</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">Disease</span></span></content>
      <content name="altTitle"><span class="qt0">Renal</span> Failure</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>Healthy <span class="qt0">kidneys</span> clean your blood by removing excess fluid, minerals, and wastes. They also make hormones that keep your bones strong and your blood healthy. But if the <span class="qt0">kidneys</span> are damaged, they don't work properly. Harmful wastes can build up in your body. Your blood pressure may rise. Your body may retain excess fluid and not make enough red blood cells. This is called <span class="qt0">kidney</span> failure.</p><p>If your <span class="qt0">kidneys</span> fail, you need treatment to replace the work they normally do. The treatment options are dialysis or a <span class="qt0">kidney</span> transplant. Each treatment has benefits and drawbacks. No matter which treatment you choose, you'll need to make some changes in your life, including how you eat and plan your activities. But with the help of health care providers, family, and friends, most people with <span class="qt0">kidney</span> failure can lead full and active lives.</p><p>NIH: National Institute of Diabetes and Digestive and <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">Kidney</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">Diseases</span></span></p></content>
      <content name="mesh"><span class="qt0">Kidney</span> Failure, Chronic</content>
      <content name="groupName"><span class="qt0">Kidneys</span> and Urinary System</content>
      <content name="snippet"> Healthy <span class="qt0">kidneys</span> clean your blood by removing excess fluid, minerals, and wastes. They also make hormones that keep your bones strong and your blood healthy.  ... </content>
