    <document rank="4" url="https://medlineplus.gov/kidneytests.html">
      <content name="title"><span class="qt0">Kidney</span> Tests</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle"><span class="qt0">Kidney</span> Biopsy</content>
      <content name="altTitle"><span class="qt0">Kidney</span> Function Tests</content>
      <content name="altTitle"><span class="qt0">Kidney</span> Function Panel</content>
      <content name="altTitle"><span class="qt0">Kidney</span> Panel</content>
      <content name="altTitle"><span class="qt0">Renal</span> Function Panel</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>You have two <span class="qt0">kidneys</span>. They are fist-sized organs on either side of your backbone above your waist. Your <span class="qt0">kidneys</span> filter and clean your blood, taking out waste products and making urine. <span class="qt0">Kidney</span> tests check to see how well your <span class="qt0">kidneys</span> are working. They include blood, urine, and imaging tests.</p><p>Early <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">kidney</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span> usually does not have signs or symptoms. Testing is the only way to know how your <span class="qt0">kidneys</span> are doing. It is important for you to get checked for <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">kidney</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span> if you have the key risk factors - diabetes, high blood pressure, heart <span class="qt1">disease</span>, or a family history of <span class="qt0">kidney</span> failure.</p><p>Specific <span class="qt0">kidney</span> tests include:</p><ul><li>Glomerular filtration rate (GFR) - one of the most common blood tests to check for chronic <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">kidney</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span>. It tells how well your <span class="qt0">kidneys</span> are filtering.</li><li>Creatinine blood and urine tests - check the levels of creatinine, a waste product that your <span class="qt0">kidneys</span> remove from your blood</li><li>Albumin urine test - checks for albumin, a protein that can pass into the urine if the <span class="qt0">kidneys</span> are damaged</li><li>Imaging tests, such as an ultrasound - provide pictures of the <span class="qt0">kidneys</span>. The pictures help the health care provider see the size and shape of the <span class="qt0">kidneys</span>, and check for anything unusual.</li><li><span class="qt0">Kidney</span> biopsy - a procedure that involves taking a small piece of <span class="qt0">kidney</span> tissue for examination with a microscope. It checks for the cause of <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">kidney</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span> and how damaged your <span class="qt0">kidneys</span> are.</li></ul><p>NIH: National Institute of Diabetes and Digestive and <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">Kidney</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">Diseases</span></span></p></content>
      <content name="mesh"><span class="qt0">Kidney</span> Function Tests</content>
      <content name="groupName"><span class="qt0">Kidneys</span> and Urinary System</content>
      <content name="groupName">Diagnostic Tests</content>

    </document>
    <document rank="5" url="https://medlineplus.gov/diabetickidneyproblems.html">
      <content name="title">Diabetic <span class="qt0">Kidney</span> Problems</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Diabetic Nephropathy</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>If you have diabetes, your blood glucose, or blood sugar, levels are too high.  Over time, this can damage your <span class="qt0">kidneys</span>. Your <span class="qt0">kidneys</span> clean your blood. If they are damaged, waste and fluids build up in your blood instead of leaving your body.</p><p><span class="qt0">Kidney</span> damage from diabetes is called diabetic nephropathy. It begins long before you have symptoms. People with diabetes should get regular screenings for <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">kidney</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span>. Tests include a urine test to detect protein in your urine and a blood test to show how well your <span class="qt0">kidneys</span> are working.</p><p>If the damage continues, your <span class="qt0">kidneys</span> could fail. In fact, diabetes is the most common cause of <span class="qt0">kidney</span> failure in the United States. People with <span class="qt0">kidney</span> failure need either dialysis or a <span class="qt0">kidney</span> transplant.</p><p>You can slow down <span class="qt0">kidney</span> damage or keep it from getting worse. Controlling your blood sugar and blood pressure, taking your medicines and not eating too much protein can help.</p><p>NIH: National Institute of Diabetes and Digestive and <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">Kidney</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">Diseases</span></span></p></content>
      <content name="mesh">Diabetic Nephropathies</content>
      <content name="groupName"><span class="qt0">Kidneys</span> and Urinary System</content>
      <content name="groupName">Endocrine System</content>
      <content name="groupName">Diabetes Mellitus</content>
      <content name="snippet"> If you have diabetes, your blood glucose, or blood sugar, levels are too high.  Over time, this can damage your <span class="qt0">kidneys</span>. Your <span class="qt0">kidneys</span> clean your blood. If  ... </content>
    </document>
    <document rank="6" url="https://medlineplus.gov/kidneytransplantation.html">
      <content name="title"><span class="qt0">Kidney</span> Transplantation</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle"><span class="qt0">Renal</span> Transplantation</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>A <span class="qt0">kidney</span> transplant is an operation that places a healthy <span class="qt0">kidney</span> in your body. The transplanted <span class="qt0">kidney</span> takes over the work of the two <span class="qt0">kidneys</span> that failed, so you no longer need dialysis. </p><p>During a transplant, the surgeon places the new <span class="qt0">kidney</span> in your lower abdomen and connects the artery and vein of the new <span class="qt0">kidney</span> to your artery and vein. Often, the new <span class="qt0">kidney</span> will start making urine as soon as your blood starts flowing through it. But sometimes it takes a few weeks to start working. </p><p>Many transplanted <span class="qt0">kidneys</span> come from donors who have died. Some come from a living family member. The wait for a new <span class="qt0">kidney</span> can be long.</p><p>If you have a transplant, you must take drugs for the rest of your life, to keep your body from rejecting the new <span class="qt0">kidney</span>.</p><p>NIH: National Institute of Diabetes and Digestive and <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">Kidney</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">Diseases</span></span></p></content>
      <content name="mesh"><span class="qt0">Kidney</span> Transplantation</content>
      <content name="groupName"><span class="qt0">Kidneys</span> and Urinary System</content>
      <content name="groupName">Transplantation and Donation</content>
      <content name="snippet"> ... National Institute of Diabetes and Digestive and <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">Kidney</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">Diseases</span></span></content>
    </document>
    <document rank="7" url="https://medlineplus.gov/dialysis.html">
      <content name="title">Dialysis</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Hemodialysis</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Peritoneal Dialysis</content>
      <content name="altTitle"><span class="qt0">Renal</span> Dialysis</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>When your <span class="qt0">kidneys</span> are healthy, they clean your blood. They also make hormones that keep 
your bones strong and your blood healthy. When your <span class="qt0">kidneys</span> fail, you need treatment to 
replace the work your <span class="qt0">kidneys</span> used to do. Unless you have a <span class="qt0">kidney</span> transplant, you will need a treatment called dialysis.</p><p>There are two main types of dialysis. Both types filter your blood to rid your body of harmful wastes, extra salt, and water.:</p><ul><li>Hemodialysis uses a machine. It is sometimes called an artificial <span class="qt0">kidney</span>. You usually go to a special clinic for treatments several times a week.</li><li>Peritoneal dialysis uses the lining of your abdomen, called the peritoneal membrane, to filter your blood.</li></ul><p>NIH: National Institute of Diabetes and Digestive and <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">Kidney</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">Diseases</span></span></p></content>
      <content name="mesh"><span class="qt0">Renal</span> Dialysis</content>
      <content name="groupName"><span class="qt0">Kidneys</span> and Urinary System</content>
      <content name="snippet"> ... National Institute of Diabetes and Digestive and <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">Kidney</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">Diseases</span></span></content>
    </document>
    <document rank="8" url="https://medlineplus.gov/bloodglucose.html">
      <content name="title">Blood Glucose</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Blood Sugar</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Continuous Glucose Monitoring</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Glucose</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is blood glucose?<p>Blood glucose, or blood sugar, is the main sugar found in your blood. It is your body's primary source of energy. It comes from the food you eat. Your body breaks down most of that food into glucose and releases it into your bloodstream. When your blood glucose goes up, it signals your pancreas to release insulin. Insulin is a hormone that helps the glucose get into your cells to be used for energy.</p>What is diabetes?<p>Diabetes is a <span class="qt1">disease</span> in which your blood glucose levels are too high. When you have diabetes, your body doesn't make enough insulin, can't use it as well as it should, or both. Too much glucose stays in your blood and doesn't reach your cells. Over time, having too much glucose in your blood can cause serious health problems (diabetes complications). So if you have diabetes, it's important to keep your blood glucose levels within your target range.

</p>What are blood glucose targets?<p>If you have diabetes, your blood glucose target is the range you try to reach as much as possible. The typical targets are:</p><ul><li>Before a meal: 80 to 130 mg/dL</li><li>Two hours after the start of a meal: Less than 180 mg/dL</li></ul><p>Your blood glucose targets may be different, depending on your age, any additional health problems you have, and other factors. Talk with your health care team  about the best target range for you.</p>When and how should I check my blood glucose?<p>If you have diabetes, you'll likely need to check your blood glucose every day to make sure that your blood glucose numbers are in your target range. Some people may need to check their blood glucose several times a day. Ask your health care team how often you need to check it.</p><p>The most common way to check your blood glucose level at home is with a blood glucose meter. A blood glucose meter measures the amount of glucose in a small sample of blood, usually from your fingertip.</p><p>Continuous glucose monitoring (CGM) is another way to check your glucose levels. Most CGM systems use a tiny sensor that is inserted under your skin. The sensor measures your glucose level every few minutes. It can show changes in your glucose level throughout the day and night. A CGM system is especially useful for people who take insulin and have problems with low blood glucose.</p><p>Your provider will also check your blood glucose with a blood test called an A1C.  It checks your average blood glucose level over the past three months. People with diabetes usually have an A1C test at least twice a year. But you may need the test more often if you aren't meeting your diabetes treatment goals.</p>What happens if my blood glucose level becomes too high?<p>High blood glucose is called hyperglycemia. Symptoms that your blood glucose levels may be too high include:</p><ul><li>Feeling thirsty</li><li>Feeling tired or weak</li><li>Headaches</li><li>Urinating (peeing) often</li><li>Blurred vision</li></ul><p>If you often have high blood glucose levels or symptoms of high blood glucose, talk with your health care team. You may need a change in your diabetes meal plan, physical activity plan, or diabetes medicines.</p><p>High blood glucose may also be caused by other conditions that can affect insulin or glucose levels in your blood. These conditions include problems with your pancreas or adrenal glands.</p>What happens if my blood glucose level becomes low for me?<p>Hypoglycemia, also called low blood glucose, happens when your blood glucose level drops below what is healthy for you. For many people with diabetes, this means a blood glucose reading lower than 70 mg/dL. Your number might be different, so check with your health care team to find out what blood glucose level is low for you.</p><p>Symptoms of low blood glucose tend to come on quickly. The symptoms can be different for everyone, but they may include:</p><ul><li>Shaking</li><li>Sweating</li><li>Nervousness or anxiety</li><li>Irritability or confusion</li><li>Dizziness</li><li>Hunger</li></ul><p>Low blood glucose levels can be common in people with type 1 diabetes and people with type 2 diabetes who take certain diabetes medicines. If you think you may have low blood glucose, check your level, even if you don't have symptoms. Low blood glucose can be dangerous and should be treated as soon as possible.</p><p>Although it's rare, you can still get low blood glucose without having diabetes. The causes can include conditions such as liver <span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span><span class="qt2">, </span><span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">kidney</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span>, and hormone deficiencies (lack of certain hormones). Some medicines, such as certain heart medicines and antibiotics, can also cause it. See your provider to find out the cause of your low blood glucose and how to treat it.</p><p>NIH: National Institute of Diabetes and Digestive and <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">Kidney</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">Diseases</span></span></p></content>

    <document rank="9" url="https://medlineplus.gov/weightcontrol.html">
      <content name="title">Weight Control</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">BMI</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">Why is weight control important?<p>If you are struggling with your weight, you are not alone. In the United States, more than 70 percent of adults are overweight or have obesity. Having this extra weight raises your risk for many health conditions, such as type 2 diabetes, heart <span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span><span class="qt2">, </span><span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">kidney</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span>, and certain cancers.</p><p>Reaching and staying at a healthy weight can be challenging. But having a healthy lifestyle, including healthy eating patterns and regular physical activity, can help you lose weight. It can also lower your chance of developing weight-related health conditions.</p>What factors affect weight and health?<p>You gain weight when you take in more calories (through food and drinks) than you use up from physical activity and daily living. But there are many different factors that can affect weight gain, such as:</p><ul><li>The world around you. Your home, community, and workplace all may affect how you make daily lifestyle choices. For example:
	<ul><li>It is often easier to find food and beverages high in calories, sugar, and fat. For instance, vending machines, cafeterias, and special events may not offer healthy, lower calorie options.</li><li>Less healthy foods may be cheaper than healthier foods.</li><li>Many people are getting less physical activity because they are spending more time using smartphones and other devices.</li></ul>
</li><li>Families. Overweight and obesity tend to run in families. This suggests that genes may play a role in weight gain. Families may also share eating and lifestyle habits. For example, some families may often have foods and drinks that are high in calories, sugar, and fat. And some families may tend to be less active and spend more time doing things like sitting and watching TV or using computers.</li><li>Not enough sleep. People who don't get enough sleep may eat more calories and snack more.</li><li>Emotions. Some people eat when they feel bored, sad, or stressed, even if they are not hungry.</li><li>Medicines and health conditions. Taking certain medicines, such as steroids and certain antidepressants, can lead to weight gain. Some chronic health problems can also cause you to gain weight. A few examples are Cushing's syndrome and polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS).</li></ul>How can I get to and stay at a healthy weight?<p>Getting to and staying at a healthy weight involves finding a balance of food and activity. To lose weight, you need to take in fewer calories than you use up. Some ways to do this are:</p><ul><li>Eating more nutrient-rich foods, such as foods with lots of vitamins, minerals, and fiber.</li><li>Eating and drinking less of the foods and beverages that have lots of calories, salt, sugar, and fat.</li><li>Limiting alcohol.</li><li>Finding healthier ways to cook, such as using healthier oils to cook with and baking or grilling instead of frying foods.</li><li>Getting more physical activity. The general recommendation is for adults to get 150 minutes of physical activity each week. This time can be broken down into short bursts of activity throughout your day. Types of physical activity include: 
	<ul><li>Aerobic activity, which is also called cardio. It uses your large muscle groups (chest, legs, and back) to speed up your heart rate and breathing.</li><li>Muscle-strengthening activity, which is also called strength training. It works your muscles by making you push or pull against something.</li></ul>
</li></ul><p>You may decide to do these lifestyle changes on your own, or you may decide to try a weight-loss diet or program. Before you start, it's important to check with your health care provider first. Your provider can tell you what a healthy weight is for you, help you set goals, and give you tips on how to lose weight.</p>What are weight management medicines?<p>If making lifestyle changes or doing a weight-loss program are not enough to help you lose weight, your provider may prescribe medicines. These medicines are meant to help people who have weight-related health conditions and should be used along with healthy eating and physical activity. The prescription medicines to treat overweight and obesity work in different ways. Some may help you feel less hungry or full sooner. Others may make it harder for your body to absorb fat from the foods you eat.</p><p>Another treatment is weight loss surgery. Your provider may recommend the surgery if you have severe obesity or serious obesity-related health problems and you have not been able to lose enough weight.</p>How can I maintain my weight loss?<p>Maintaining weight loss can be challenging, and it's normal to slip back into old habits sometimes. The key is to keep a healthy balance of food and activity that works for you. Checking your weight regularly can help you notice small changes early and stay on track. And if you have a setback, don't be discouraged, simply refocus on your healthy habits and keep going.</p><p>NIH: National Institute of Diabetes and Digestive and <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">Kidney</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">Diseases</span></span></p></content>

      <content name="mesh">Weight Reduction Programs</content>
      <content name="mesh">Body Weight Maintenance</content>

Reaching and staying at a healthy weight can be challenging. But having a healthy lifestyle, including ...     <document rank="10" url="https://medlineplus.gov/diabeticheartdisease.html">
      <content name="title">Diabetic Heart <span class="qt1">Disease</span></content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is diabetes?<p>Diabetes is a <span class="qt1">disease</span> in which your blood glucose, or blood sugar, levels are too high. Glucose comes from foods you eat. The cells of your body need glucose for energy. A hormone called insulin helps the glucose get into your cells.</p><p>With type 1 diabetes, your body doesn't make insulin. With type 2 diabetes, your body doesn't make or use insulin well. Without enough insulin, glucose builds up in your blood and causes high blood sugar levels.</p>What is diabetic heart <span class="qt1">disease</span>?<p>Diabetic heart <span class="qt1">disease</span> is a term for heart <span class="qt1">disease</span> in people who have diabetes. If you have diabetes, you are much more likely to develop heart <span class="qt1">disease</span> or have a stroke than people who don't have diabetes. And you may start having these problems at a younger age.</p><p>Over time, the effects of high blood sugar from diabetes can include damage to the blood vessels and nerves in your heart. This damage increases your chance of developing heart <span class="qt1">diseases</span>, including:</p><ul><li>Coronary artery <span class="qt1">disease</span> (CAD). It happens slowly as a sticky material called plaque builds up in the arteries that supply your heart muscle with blood.</li><li>Heart failure. With this condition, your heart can't pump enough oxygen-rich blood to meet the needs of your body.</li><li>Cardiomyopathy. This is a group of <span class="qt1">diseases</span> in which the heart muscle may become thick or stiff.</li></ul>Who is more likely to develop diabetic heart <span class="qt1">disease</span>?<p>Diabetes puts you at serious risk for heart <span class="qt1">disease</span>. It also makes you more likely to develop other conditions that raise your risk even more, including:</p><ul><li>High blood pressure</li><li>High blood levels of LDL "bad" cholesterol</li><li>High triglycerides (a type of fat in your blood) with low blood levels of HDL "good" cholesterol</li></ul><p>Your risk for heart <span class="qt1">disease</span> is also higher if you</p><ul><li>Are male</li><li>Smoke</li><li>Have obesity</li><li>Have too much belly fat around your waist, even though you're at a healthy weight:
	<ul><li>For men, that's a waist more than 40 inches</li><li>For women, that's a waist more than 35 inches</li></ul>
</li><li>Have a family history of heart <span class="qt1">disease</span></li><li>Have chronic <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">kidney</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span></li></ul>What are the symptoms of diabetic heart <span class="qt1">disease</span>?<p>In the early stages, heart <span class="qt1">disease</span> usually doesn't have any symptoms. But if your heart <span class="qt1">disease</span> worsens, you can have symptoms. Your symptoms will depend on the type of heart <span class="qt1">disease</span> you have. They might include:</p><ul><li>Shortness of breath</li><li>Fatigue</li><li>Dizziness or fainting</li><li>Arrhythmia (problem with the rate or rhythm of your heartbeat)</li><li>Swollen feet and ankles</li><li>Chest pain</li></ul><p>It's important to know that people with diabetes may not feel chest pain. That's because diabetes can damage the nerves in your heart. If you have any symptoms that could be heart <span class="qt1">disease</span>, talk with your health care provider.</p>How is diabetic heart <span class="qt1">disease</span> diagnosed?<p>To find out if you have diabetic heart <span class="qt1">disease</span>, your provider will:</p><ul><li>Ask about your medical history, including your symptoms and other health conditions you may have</li><li>Ask about your family history, to find out if you have relatives who have or had heart <span class="qt1">disease</span></li><li>Do a physical exam</li><li>Likely run some tests to help understand your personal risk for heart <span class="qt1">disease</span>, including:
	<ul><li>Blood tests to check your cholesterol, triglycerides, and blood sugar levels</li><li>A blood-pressure check</li><li>Heart tests, if needed</li></ul>
</li></ul><p>Depending on your risk level, your provider may send you to a cardiologist (a doctor who specializes in heart <span class="qt1">diseases</span>) for care. If you do have heart <span class="qt1">disease</span>, treatment will depend on the type of heart <span class="qt1">disease</span> you have.</p>How can I prevent diabetic heart <span class="qt1">disease</span>?<p>You may be able to prevent heart <span class="qt1">disease</span> or keep it from getting worse by working with your provider to:</p><ul><li>Control your blood sugar levels</li><li>Manage other conditions you may have that can raise your risk for heart <span class="qt1">disease</span> </li><li>Take any medicines your provider prescribed</li><li>Make heart-healthy habits part of your daily life</li><li>Follow a healthy eating plan for diabetes</li></ul><p>NIH: National Institute of Diabetes and Digestive and <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">Kidney</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">Diseases</span></span></p></content>

      <content name="mesh">Heart <span class="qt1">Diseases</span></content>

NIH: National Institute of Diabetes and Digestive and <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">Kidney</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">Diseases</span></span></content>

    <document rank="11" url="https://medlineplus.gov/gout.html">
      <content name="title">Gout</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Chondrocalcinosis</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Hyperuricemia</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Pseudogout</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Gouty arthritis</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is gout?<p>Gout is a common type of inflammatory arthritis. It causes pain, swelling, and redness in one or more joints. It usually happens as a flare, which can last for a week or two and then gets better. The flares often begin in your big toe or a lower limb.</p>What causes gout?<p>Gout happens when too much uric acid (urate) builds up in your body over a long time. Uric acid is a waste product your body makes when it breaks down purines. Purines are substances that are in your body's tissues and in many foods.</p><p>When your body breaks down old cells or digests foods that contain purine, most of the uric acid that's made dissolves in your blood. Your <span class="qt0">kidneys</span> filter the uric acid out of your blood, and it leaves your body in your urine (pee).</p><p>However, sometimes your body can make too much uric acid or does not remove enough of it. Then the uric acid levels build up in your body, including in your blood. Having too much uric acid in the blood is called hyperuricemia. It does not cause health problems for everyone. But in some people, uric acid forms needle-like crystals. They can form in your joints, which causes gout. The crystals can also cause <span class="qt0">kidney</span> stones.</p><p>Calcium pyrophosphate arthritis, sometimes called pseudogout, is a related <span class="qt1">disease</span>. It causes similar symptoms and is sometimes confused with gout. But it is caused by a buildup of calcium phosphate, not uric acid.</p>Who is more likely to develop gout? <p>Many people develop gout. You are more likely to get it if you:</p><ul><li>Are male.</li><li>Are older; it usually develops in middle age.</li><li>Have obesity.</li><li>Have certain health conditions, such as:
  <ul><li>Heart failure</li><li>High blood pressure</li><li>Metabolic syndrome</li><li>Chronic <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">kidney</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span></li><li>Conditions that cause your cells to break down more quickly, such as psoriasis or some cancers</li><li>Rare genetic conditions that lead to increased uric acid</li></ul>
</li><li>Have a family history of gout.</li><li>Have an unhealthy diet and eat foods that are rich in purines, such as red meat, organ meats, certain seafoods.</li><li>Drink alcohol.</li><li>Eat and drink lots of foods and beverages that contain fructose (a type of sugar).</li><li>Take certain medicines, such as diuretics (water pills), low-dose aspirin, and some medicines that weaken your immune system.</li><li>Take high amounts of niacin (vitamin B-3).</li></ul>What are the symptoms of gout?<p>Gout usually happens in only one joint at a time. It is often found in the big toe, but can also affect other joints, including your other toes, ankle, and knee.</p><p>Gout flares often start suddenly at night, and the symptoms in the affected joint often include:</p><ul><li>Intense pain, which may be bad enough to wake you up</li><li>Swelling</li><li>Redness</li><li>Warmth</li></ul><p>The flares typically get better within a week or two. In between flares, you usually don't have symptoms. Some people may have flares often, while others may not have another flare for years. But over time, if left untreated, your flares may happen more often and last longer. </p><p>And if gout is untreated over long periods of time, you can develop tophi. Tophi are hard, uric acid deposits under the skin. They start out as painless, but over time, they can become painful. They can also cause bone and soft tissue damage and misshapen joints.</p>How is gout diagnosed?<p>To find out if you have gout, your health care provider:</p><ul><li>Will take your medical history, which includes asking about your symptoms.</li><li>Will do a physical exam, including examining the affected joint(s).</li><li>May order various tests, such as:
  <ul><li>A test of a sample of fluid from one of your painful joints. The fluid is examined under a microscope and is checked for uric acid crystals.</li><li>A uric acid blood or urine test.</li><li>An ultrasound or special CT scan to look for uric acid crystal buildup in the affected joint and check for other conditions that may be causing the symptoms.</li></ul>
</li></ul>What are the treatments for gout?<p>There are effective treatments for gout. Which treatment you get will depend on your symptoms and the cause of your gout. The goals of your treatment will be to:</p><ul><li>Reduce the pain from gout flares, by using medicines such as:
  <ul><li>Nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) like ibuprofen.</li><li>Acetaminophen and the anti-inflammatory drug colchicine.</li><li>Oral or injected corticosteroids.</li></ul>
</li><li>Prevent future flares, for example by:
  <ul><li>Making lifestyle changes such as losing weight, limiting alcohol, and avoiding foods high in purines. If you are taking medicines that can cause high uric acid in the blood, your provider may suggest stopping or changing those medicines.</li><li>If needed, taking medicines to lower uric acid in the blood.</li></ul>
</li><li>Prevent tophi and <span class="qt0">kidney</span> stones, for example with medicines that lower uric acid in the blood.</li></ul><p>With early diagnosis, treatment, and lifestyle changes, gout is one of the most controllable forms of arthritis. Treatment and lifestyle changes may help people avoid gout flares, lessen their symptoms, and sometimes even become gout free.</p><p>NIH: National Institute of Arthritis and Musculoskeletal and Skin <span class="qt1">Diseases</span></p></content>
      <content name="mesh">Gout</content>
      <content name="groupName">Bones, Joints and Muscles</content>
      <content name="snippet"> ... National Institute of Arthritis and Musculoskeletal and Skin <span class="qt1">Diseases</span></content>

    <document rank="12" url="https://medlineplus.gov/diabetescomplications.html">
      <content name="title">Diabetes Complications</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Diabetic complications</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is diabetes?<p>If you have diabetes, your blood glucose, or blood sugar, levels are too high. Glucose comes from the foods you eat. A hormone called insulin helps the glucose get into your cells to give them energy.</p><p> With type 1 diabetes, your body does not make insulin. With type 2 diabetes, your body does not make or use insulin well. Without enough insulin, the glucose stays in your blood.</p>What health problems can diabetes cause?<p>Over time, having too much glucose in your blood can cause complications, including:</p><ul><li>Eye <span class="qt1">disease</span>, due to changes in fluid levels, swelling in the tissues, and damage to the blood vessels in the eyes.</li><li>Foot problems, caused by damage to the nerves and reduced blood flow to your feet.</li><li>Gum <span class="qt1">disease</span> and other dental problems, because a high amount of glucose in your saliva helps harmful bacteria grow in your mouth. The bacteria combine with food to form a soft, sticky film called plaque. Plaque also comes from eating foods that contain sugars or starches. Some types of plaque cause gum <span class="qt1">disease</span> and bad breath. Other types cause tooth decay and cavities.</li><li>Heart <span class="qt1">disease</span> and stroke, caused by damage to your blood vessels and the nerves that control your heart and blood vessels.</li><li><span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">Kidney</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span>, due to damage to the blood vessels in your <span class="qt0">kidneys</span>. Many people with diabetes develop high blood pressure. That can also damage your <span class="qt0">kidneys</span>.</li><li>Nerve problems (diabetic neuropathy), caused by damage to the nerves and the small blood vessels that nourish your nerves with oxygen and nutrients.</li><li>Sexual and bladder problems, caused by damage to the nerves and reduced blood flow in the genitals and bladder.</li><li>Skin conditions, some of which are caused by changes in the small blood vessels and reduced circulation. People with diabetes are also more likely to have infections, including skin infections.</li></ul>What other problems can people with diabetes have?<p>If you have diabetes, you need to watch out for blood glucose levels that are too high (hyperglycemia) or too low for you (hypoglycemia). These can happen quickly and can become dangerous. Some of the causes include having another <span class="qt1">illness</span> or infection and certain medicines. They can also happen if you don't get the right amount of diabetes medicines. To try to prevent these problems, make sure to take your diabetes medicines correctly, follow your diabetic diet, and check your blood glucose regularly.</p><p>NIH: National Institute of Diabetes and Digestive and <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">Kidney</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">Diseases</span></span></p></content>
      <content name="mesh">Diabetes Complications</content>
      <content name="groupName">Endocrine System</content>
      <content name="groupName">Metabolic Problems</content>
      <content name="groupName">Diabetes Mellitus</content>

Heart <span class="qt1">disease</span> and stroke, caused by damage to your blood vessels and the nerves that control your heart and blood vessels.  <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">Kidney</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span>, due to damage to the blood vessels  ... </content>

    <document rank="13" url="https://medlineplus.gov/parathyroiddisorders.html">
      <content name="title">Parathyroid Disorders</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Hyperparathyroidism</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Hypoparathyroidism</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Multiple Endocrine Neoplasia</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>Most people have four pea-sized glands, called parathyroid glands, on the thyroid gland in the neck. Though their names are similar, the thyroid and parathyroid glands are completely different. The parathyroid glands make parathyroid hormone (PTH), which helps your body keep the right balance of calcium and phosphorous.</p><p>If your parathyroid glands make too much or too little hormone, it disrupts this balance. If they secrete extra PTH, you have hyperparathyroidism, and your blood calcium rises. In many cases, a benign tumor on a parathyroid gland makes it overactive. Or, the extra hormones can come from enlarged parathyroid glands. Very rarely, the cause is cancer.</p><p>If you do not have enough PTH, you have hypoparathyroidism. Your blood will have too little calcium and too much phosphorous. Causes include injury to the glands, endocrine disorders, or genetic conditions. Treatment is aimed at restoring the balance of calcium and phosphorous.</p><p>NIH: National Institute of Diabetes and Digestive and <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">Kidney</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">Diseases</span></span></p></content>
      <content name="mesh">Parathyroid <span class="qt1">Diseases</span></content>
      <content name="groupName">Endocrine System</content>
      <content name="snippet"> ... National Institute of Diabetes and Digestive and <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">Kidney</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">Diseases</span></span></content>

    <document rank="14" url="https://medlineplus.gov/diabetes.html">
      <content name="title">Diabetes</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Diabetes Mellitus</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Sugar Diabetes</content>
      <content name="altTitle">DM</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is diabetes?<p>Diabetes, also known as diabetes mellitus, is a <span class="qt1">disease</span> in which your blood glucose, or blood sugar, levels are too high. Glucose is your body's main source of energy. Your body can make glucose, but it also comes from the food you eat. Insulin is a hormone made by your pancreas. Insulin helps move glucose from your bloodstream into your cells, where it can be used for energy.</p><p>If you have diabetes, your body can't make insulin, can't use insulin as well as it should, or both. Too much glucose stays in your blood and doesn't reach your cells. This can cause glucose levels to get too high. Over time, high blood glucose levels can lead to serious health conditions. But you can take steps to manage your diabetes and try to prevent these health problems.</p>What are the types of diabetes?<p>There are different types of diabetes:</p><ul><li>Type 1 diabetes. If you have type 1 diabetes, your body makes little or no insulin. It happens when your immune system attacks and destroys the cells that produce insulin. </li><li>Type 2 diabetes. This is the most common form of diabetes. If you have type 2 diabetes, your body may still be able to make insulin, but your cells don't respond well to insulin. They can't easily take up enough glucose from your blood.</li><li>Gestational diabetes. This is a form of diabetes that develops during pregnancy. It happens when your body can't make the extra insulin it needs during pregnancy.</li></ul>What causes diabetes?<p>The different types of diabetes have different causes:</p><ul><li>Researchers think type 1 diabetes is caused by genes and factors in the environment that might trigger the <span class="qt1">disease</span>.</li><li>Type 2 diabetes is caused by several factors, including lifestyle factors and genes. The lifestyle factors include not being physically active and being overweight or having obesity.</li><li>Researchers think gestational diabetes is caused by the hormonal changes of pregnancy along with genetic and lifestyle factors.</li></ul>Who is more likely to develop diabetes?<p>The different types of diabetes have different risk factors:</p><ul><li>You can develop type 1 diabetes at any age, but it most often starts in childhood. Having a parent or sibling with type 1 diabetes may increase your chance of developing it.</li><li>You are at higher risk of developing type 2 diabetes if you:</li><ul><li>Are overweight or have obesity.</li><li>Are over age 35. Children, teenagers, and younger adults can get diabetes, but it is more common in middle-aged and older adults.</li><li>Have a family history of diabetes.</li><li>Have prediabetes. This means that your blood glucose is higher than normal, but it's not high enough to be called diabetes.</li><li>Had gestational diabetes.</li><li>Have given birth to a baby weighing 9 pounds or more.</li><li>Are African American, American Indian, Asian American, Hispanic/Latino, or Pacific Islander.</li><li>Are not physically active.</li><li>Have certain other health conditions, such as high blood pressure or polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS).</li></ul><li>You are at higher risk of developing gestational diabetes if you:</li><ul><li>Are overweight or have obesity.</li><li>Have a family history of diabetes.</li><li>Had gestational diabetes in a previous pregnancy.</li><li>Have given birth to a baby weighing 9 pounds or more.</li><li>Have polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS).</li><li>Are African American, Hispanic/Latino, American Indian, Alaska Native, Native Hawaiian, or Pacific Islander.</li></ul></ul>What are the symptoms of diabetes?<p>The symptoms of diabetes may include:</p><ul><li>Feeling very thirsty</li><li>Feeling very hungry</li><li>Urinating (peeing) more often, including at night</li><li>Fatigue</li><li>Blurry vision</li><li>Numbness or tingling in the feet or hands</li><li>Sores that do not heal</li><li>Losing weight without trying</li></ul><p>But it's important to know that your symptoms may vary, depending on which type you have:</p><ul><li>The symptoms of type 1 diabetes usually come on quickly and can be severe.</li><li>With type 2 diabetes, the symptoms often develop slowly, over several years. The symptoms can be so mild that you might not even notice them.</li><li>Gestational diabetes often has no symptoms. If you do have symptoms, they may be mild. If you are pregnant, you will usually be screened for this condition between 24 and 28 weeks of pregnancy. </li></ul>How is diabetes diagnosed?<p>To find out if you have diabetes, your health care provider will use one or more glucose blood tests. There are several types, including the A1C test.</p>What are the treatments for diabetes?<p>Treatment for diabetes involves managing your blood glucose levels:</p><ul><li>If you have type 1 diabetes, you will need to take daily doses of insulin, either by injection or through a special pump. Some people also need to take another type of diabetes medicine that works with insulin.</li><li>If you have type 2 diabetes, you may be able to manage or even reverse it by making lifestyle changes. These include eating a healthy diet, staying at healthy weight, and getting regular physical activity. Some people also need to take diabetes medicines to manage their diabetes.</li><li>If you have gestational diabetes, you may be able to lower your glucose levels by eating a healthy diet and getting regular exercise. But be sure to talk to your provider about your treatment options. Gestational diabetes usually goes away after you give birth. But you will have a higher risk of developing type 2 diabetes later.</li></ul><p>Checking your blood glucose levels is also an important part of managing your diabetes. Ask your provider about the best way to check your blood glucose level and how often you should check it.</p>Can diabetes be prevented?<p>Type 1 diabetes can't be prevented.</p><p>You may be able to delay or prevent type 2 diabetes through the same lifestyle changes that are used to manage diabetes (eating a healthy diet, staying at a healthy weight, and getting regular physical activity). These lifestyle changes may also help prevent gestational diabetes.</p><p>NIH: National Institute of Diabetes and Digestive and <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">Kidney</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">Diseases</span></span></p></content>

    <document rank="15" url="https://medlineplus.gov/hypoglycemia.html">
      <content name="title">Hypoglycemia</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Low Blood Sugar</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is blood glucose?<p>Blood glucose, or blood sugar, is the main sugar found in your blood. It is your body's primary source of energy. It comes from the food you eat. Your body breaks down most of that food into glucose and releases it into your bloodstream. When your blood glucose goes up, it signals your pancreas to release insulin. Insulin is a hormone that helps the glucose get into your cells to be used for energy.</p><p>For people with diabetes, your body doesn't make enough insulin, can't use it as well as it should, or both. Too much glucose stays in your blood and doesn't reach your cells.</p>What is hypoglycemia?<p>Hypoglycemia means low glucose. It happens when the level of glucose in your blood drops below what is healthy for you:</p><ul><li>For many people with diabetes, this means a blood glucose level lower than 70 mg/dL. Your number might be different, so check with your health care team to find out what blood glucose level is too low for you.</li><li>For people who don't have diabetes, hypoglycemia is typically a blood glucose level lower than 55 mg/dL.</li></ul>What causes hypoglycemia?<p>Hypoglycemia is common in people who have diabetes type 1 or who have diabetes type 2 and take insulin or other diabetes medicines. It can happen:</p><ul><li>As a side effect of insulin or some other medicines that help your pancreas release insulin into your blood. These medicines can lower your blood glucose level.</li><li>If you don't eat or drink enough carbohydrates (carbs). Carbs are the main source of glucose for your body.</li><li>If you get a lot more physical activity than usual.</li><li>If you drink too much alcohol without enough food.</li><li>When you are sick and can't eat enough food or keep food down.</li></ul><p>Although it's rare, you can still get low blood glucose without having diabetes. The causes can include conditions such as liver <span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span><span class="qt2">, </span><span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">kidney</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span>, and hormone deficiencies (lack of certain hormones). It can also happen in people who have had certain types of weight loss surgery. Some medicines, such as certain heart medicines and antibiotics, can also cause it. See your health care provider to find out the cause of your low blood glucose and how to treat it.</p>What are the symptoms of hypoglycemia?<p>The symptoms of low blood glucose tend to come on quickly. The symptoms can be different for everyone, but they may include:</p><ul><li>Shaking</li><li>Sweating</li><li>Nervousness or anxiety</li><li>Irritability or confusion</li><li>Dizziness</li><li>Hunger</li></ul>How is hypoglycemia diagnosed?<p>If you have diabetes, you'll most likely need to check your blood glucose every day and make sure that it's not too low. You can do this with a blood glucose meter or continuous glucose monitoring (CGM) system.</p><p>There are also blood tests that providers can use to check if your blood glucose is too low.</p><p>If you don't have diabetes and you have hypoglycemia, your provider will likely order other tests to try to figure out the cause.</p>What are the treatments for hypoglycemia?<p>If you have mild or moderate hypoglycemia, eating or drinking something with carbohydrates can help. But severe hypoglycemia can cause serious complications, including passing out, coma, or even death. Severe hypoglycemia can be treated with glucagon, a hormone that raises blood glucose levels. It can be given as nasal spray or injection. If you have diabetes, your provider can prescribe you a glucagon kit for use in case of an emergency.</p><p>If you have diabetes and you often have mild or moderate low blood glucose, your health care team may make changes to your diabetes meal plan, physical activity plan, and/or diabetes medicines.</p><p>If you don't have diabetes and you keep having low blood glucose, the treatment will depend on what is causing it to happen.</p>Can hypoglycemia be prevented?<p>If you have diabetes and you take insulin or other medicines that lower blood glucose, you can help prevent hypoglycemia if you:</p><ul><li>Follow your diabetes meal plan. Eat and drink enough carbs to keep your blood glucose in your target range. Also carry a source of fast-acting carbohydrate, such as glucose tablets or a juice box, with you in case your blood glucose gets too low.</li><li>Be safe during physical activity. Check your blood glucose before and afterwards. You may need to eat a snack before your physical activity.</li><li>If you take diabetes medicines, make sure to take them correctly.</li></ul></content>

    <document rank="16" url="https://medlineplus.gov/ureteraldisorders.html">
      <content name="title">Ureteral Disorders</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Ureteral Cancer</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>Your <span class="qt0">kidneys</span> make urine by filtering wastes and extra water from your blood.  The urine travels from the <span class="qt0">kidneys</span> to the bladder in two thin tubes called ureters.</p><p>The ureters are about 8 to 10 inches long. Muscles in the ureter walls tighten and relax to force urine down and away from the <span class="qt0">kidneys</span>. Small amounts of urine flow from the ureters into the bladder about every 10 to 15 seconds.</p><p>Sometimes the ureters can become blocked or injured. This can block the flow of urine to the bladder. If urine stands still or backs up the ureter, you may get a urinary tract infection.</p><p>Doctors diagnose problems with the ureters using different tests. These include urine tests, x-rays, and examination of the ureter with a scope called a cystoscope. Treatment depends on the cause of the problem. It may include medicines and, in severe cases, surgery.</p><p>NIH: National Institute of Diabetes and Digestive and <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">Kidney</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">Diseases</span></span></p></content>
      <content name="mesh">Ureteral <span class="qt1">Diseases</span></content>
      <content name="groupName"><span class="qt0">Kidneys</span> and Urinary System</content>
      <content name="snippet"> ... National Institute of Diabetes and Digestive and <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">Kidney</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">Diseases</span></span></content>

    <document rank="17" url="https://medlineplus.gov/kidneystones.html">
      <content name="title"><span class="qt0">Kidney</span> Stones</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Nephrolithiasis</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>A <span class="qt0">kidney</span> stone is a solid piece of material that forms in the <span class="qt0">kidney</span> from substances in the urine. It may be as small as a grain of sand or as large as a pearl. Most <span class="qt0">kidney</span> stones pass out of the body without help from a doctor. But sometimes a stone will not go away. It may get stuck in the urinary tract, block the flow of urine and cause great pain. </p><p>The following may be signs of <span class="qt0">kidney</span> stones that need a doctor's help:</p><ul><li>	Extreme pain in your back or side that will not go away</li><li>	Blood in your urine</li><li>	Fever and chills</li><li>	Vomiting</li><li>	Urine that smells bad or looks cloudy</li><li>	A burning feeling when you urinate</li></ul><p>Your doctor will diagnose a <span class="qt0">kidney</span> stone with urine, blood, and imaging tests.</p><p>If you have a stone that won't pass on its own, you may need treatment. It can be done with shock waves; with a scope inserted through the tube that carries urine out of the body, called the urethra; or with surgery.</p><p>NIH: National Institute of Diabetes and Digestive and <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">Kidney</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">Diseases</span></span></p></content>
      <content name="mesh">Nephrolithiasis</content>
      <content name="groupName"><span class="qt0">Kidneys</span> and Urinary System</content>
      <content name="snippet"> ... National Institute of Diabetes and Digestive and <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">Kidney</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">Diseases</span></span></content>

    <document rank="18" url="https://medlineplus.gov/wilsondisease.html">
      <content name="title">Wilson <span class="qt1">Disease</span></content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Copper Storage <span class="qt1">Disease</span></content>
      <content name="altTitle">Hepatolenticular Degeneration</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>Wilson <span class="qt1">disease</span> is a rare inherited disorder that prevents your body from getting rid of extra copper.  You need a small amount of copper from food to stay healthy. Too much copper is poisonous.</p><p>Normally, your liver releases extra copper into bile, a digestive fluid.  With Wilson <span class="qt1">disease</span>, the copper builds up in your liver, and it releases the copper directly into your bloodstream. This can cause damage to your brain, <span class="qt0">kidneys</span>, and eyes.</p><p>Wilson <span class="qt1">disease</span> is present at birth, but symptoms usually start between ages 5 and 35. It first attacks the liver, the central nervous system or both. The most characteristic sign is a rusty brown ring around the cornea of the eye. A physical exam and laboratory tests can diagnose it.</p><p>Treatment is with drugs to remove the extra copper from your body.  You need to take medicine and follow a low-copper diet for the rest of your life. Don't eat shellfish or liver, as these foods may contain high levels of copper.  At the beginning of treatment, you'll also need to avoid chocolate, mushrooms, and nuts. Have your drinking water checked for copper content and don't take multivitamins that contain copper.</p><p>With early detection and proper treatment, you can enjoy good health.</p><p>NIH: National Institute of Diabetes and Digestive and <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">Kidney</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">Diseases</span></span></p></content>

      <content name="groupName">Genetics/Birth Defects</content>

This can cause damage to your brain, <span class="qt0">kidneys</span>, and eyes. Wilson <span class="qt1">disease</span> is present at birth,  ... </content>

    <document rank="19" url="https://medlineplus.gov/heartdiseases.html">
      <content name="title">Heart <span class="qt1">Diseases</span></content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Cardiac <span class="qt1">Diseases</span></content>
      <content name="altTitle">Cardiovascular <span class="qt1">Diseases</span></content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is heart <span class="qt1">disease</span>?<p>Heart <span class="qt1">disease</span> is a general term that includes many types of heart problems. It's a type of cardiovascular <span class="qt1">disease</span>, which means heart and blood vessel <span class="qt1">disease</span>.</p><p>Heart <span class="qt1">disease</span> is the leading cause of death in the United States, but there are ways to prevent and manage many types of heart <span class="qt1">disease</span>.</p>What are the types of heart <span class="qt1">disease</span>?<p>There are many different types of heart <span class="qt1">disease</span>. Some you may be born with, called congenital heart <span class="qt1">disease</span>. Other types develop during your lifetime.</p><p>Coronary artery <span class="qt1">disease</span> (also called coronary heart <span class="qt1">disease</span>) is the most common type of heart <span class="qt1">disease</span>. It happens slowly over time when a sticky substance called plaque builds up in the arteries that supply your heart muscle with blood. The plaque narrows or blocks blood flow to the heart muscle and can lead to other heart problems:</p><ul><li>Angina - chest pain from lack of blood flow</li><li>Heart attacks - when part of the heart muscle dies from loss of blood flow</li><li>Heart failure - when your heart can't pump enough blood to meet your body's needs</li><li>Arrhythmia - a problem with the rate or rhythm of your heartbeat</li></ul><p>Other types of heart <span class="qt1">diseases</span> may affect your heart valves or heart muscle (cardiomyopathy).</p>What causes heart <span class="qt1">diseases</span>?<p>The causes of heart <span class="qt1">disease</span> depend on the type of <span class="qt1">disease</span>. Some possible causes include lifestyle, genetics, infections, medicines, and other <span class="qt1">diseases</span>.</p>Who is more likely to develop heart <span class="qt1">diseases</span>?<p>There are many different factors that can make you more likely to develop heart <span class="qt1">disease</span>. Some of these factors you can change, but others you cannot.</p><ul><li>Age. Your risk of heart <span class="qt1">disease</span> goes up as you get older.</li><li>Sex. Some factors may affect heart <span class="qt1">disease</span> risk differently in women than in men.</li><li>Family history and genetics. A family history of early heart <span class="qt1">disease</span> raises your risk. And research has shown that some genes are linked to a higher risk of certain heart <span class="qt1">diseases</span>.</li><li>Race/ethnicity. Heart <span class="qt1">disease</span> is the leading cause of death in most racial and ethnic groups in the United States. However, certain groups have higher risks than others. This includes Black people, White people, and American Indian/Alaska Native people.</li><li>Lifestyle habits. Over time, unhealthy lifestyle habits can raise your risk of heart <span class="qt1">disease</span>. These can include: 
	<ul><li>Eating a diet high in saturated fats, refined carbohydrates, and salt.</li><li>Not getting enough physical activity.</li><li>Drinking too much alcohol.</li><li>Smoking and exposure to secondhand smoke.</li><li>Too much stress.</li></ul>
</li><li>Having other medical conditions can raise your risk of heart <span class="qt1">diseases</span>. These conditions include:
	<ul><li>High blood pressure.</li><li>High cholesterol levels.</li><li>Diabetes.</li><li>Obesity.</li><li>Autoimmune and inflammatory <span class="qt1">diseases</span>.</li><li>Chronic <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">kidney</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span>.</li><li>Metabolic syndrome.</li></ul>
</li></ul>What are the symptoms of heart <span class="qt1">disease</span>?<p>Your symptoms will depend on the type of heart <span class="qt1">disease</span> you have. You may not have symptoms at first. In some cases, you may not know you have heart <span class="qt1">disease</span> until you have a complication such as a heart attack.</p>How are heart <span class="qt1">diseases</span> diagnosed?<p>To find out if you have heart <span class="qt1">disease</span>, your health care provider will:</p><ul><li>Ask about your medical history, including your symptoms</li><li>Ask about your family health history, including relatives who have had heart <span class="qt1">disease</span></li><li>Do a physical exam</li><li>Likely run heart tests and blood tests</li></ul><p>In some cases, your provider may refer you to a cardiologist (a doctor who specializes in heart <span class="qt1">diseases</span>) for tests, diagnosis, and care.</p>What are the treatments for heart <span class="qt1">disease</span>?<p>Treatment plans for heart <span class="qt1">disease</span> depend on the type of heart <span class="qt1">disease</span> you have, how serious your symptoms are, and what other health conditions you have. Possible treatments may include:</p><ul><li>Heart-healthy lifestyle changes</li><li>Medicines</li><li>Procedures or surgeries</li><li>Cardiac rehabilitation</li></ul>Can heart <span class="qt1">diseases</span> be prevented?<p>You may be able to lower your risk of certain heart <span class="qt1">diseases</span> by making heart-healthy lifestyle changes  and managing any other medical conditions you have.</p><p>NIH: National Heart, Lung, and Blood Institute</p></content>
      <content name="mesh">Heart <span class="qt1">Diseases</span></content>
      <content name="groupName">Older Adults</content>

    <document rank="20" url="https://medlineplus.gov/bladderdiseases.html">
      <content name="title">Bladder <span class="qt1">Diseases</span></content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Neurogenic Bladder</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>The bladder is a hollow organ in your lower abdomen that stores urine. Many conditions can affect your bladder. Some common ones are:</p><ul><li>Cystitis - inflammation of the bladder, often from an infection</li><li>Urinary incontinence - loss of bladder control</li><li>Overactive bladder - a condition in which the bladder squeezes urine out at the wrong time</li><li>Interstitial cystitis - a chronic problem that causes bladder pain and frequent, urgent urination</li><li>Bladder cancer</li></ul><p>Doctors diagnose bladder <span class="qt1">diseases</span> using different tests. These include urine tests, x-rays, and an examination of the bladder wall with a scope called a cystoscope. Treatment depends on the cause of the problem. It may include medicines and, in severe cases, surgery. </p><p>NIH: National Institute of Diabetes and Digestive and <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">Kidney</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">Diseases</span></span></p></content>

      <content name="groupName"><span class="qt0">Kidneys</span> and Urinary System</content>

    <document rank="21" url="https://medlineplus.gov/ulcerativecolitis.html">
      <content name="title">Ulcerative Colitis</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Colitis</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Inflammatory Bowel <span class="qt1">Disease</span></content>
      <content name="altTitle">Distal colitis</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Pancolitis</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Ulcerative proctitis</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>Ulcerative colitis (UC) is a <span class="qt1">disease</span> that causes inflammation and sores, called ulcers, in the lining of the rectum and colon. It is one of a group of <span class="qt1">diseases</span> called inflammatory bowel <span class="qt1">disease</span>.</p><p>UC can happen at any age, but it usually starts between the ages of 15 and 30. It tends to run in families. The most common symptoms are pain in the abdomen and blood or pus in diarrhea. Other symptoms may include:</p><ul><li>Anemia</li><li>Severe tiredness</li><li>Weight loss</li><li>Loss of appetite</li><li>Bleeding from the rectum</li><li>Sores on the skin</li><li>Joint pain</li><li>Growth failure in children</li></ul><p>About half of people with UC have mild symptoms.</p><p>Doctors use blood tests, stool tests, colonoscopy or sigmoidoscopy, and imaging tests to diagnose UC. Several types of drugs can help control it. Some people have long periods of remission, when they are free of symptoms. In severe cases, doctors must remove the colon.</p><p>NIH: National Institute of Diabetes and Digestive and <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">Kidney</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">Diseases</span></span></p></content>
      <content name="mesh">Colitis, Ulcerative</content>
      <content name="groupName">Digestive System</content>
      <content name="snippet"> ... of a group of <span class="qt1">diseases</span> called inflammatory bowel <span class="qt1">disease</span>. UC can happen at any ...  NIH: National Institute of Diabetes and Digestive and <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">Kidney</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">Diseases</span></span></content>

    <document rank="22" url="https://medlineplus.gov/diabetesinsipidus.html">
      <content name="title">Diabetes Insipidus</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">DI</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>Diabetes insipidus (DI) causes frequent urination. You become extremely thirsty, so you drink. Then you urinate. This cycle can keep you from sleeping or even make you wet the bed. Your body produces lots of urine that is almost all water.</p><p>DI is different from diabetes mellitus (DM), which involves insulin problems and high blood sugar. The symptoms can be similar. However, DI is related to how your <span class="qt0">kidneys</span> handle fluids. It's much less common than DM. Urine and blood tests can show which one you have.</p><p>Usually, DI is caused by a problem with your pituitary gland or your <span class="qt0">kidneys</span>. Treatment depends on the cause of the problem.  Medicines can often help.</p><p>NIH: National Institute of Diabetes and Digestive and <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">Kidney</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">Diseases</span></span></p></content>

      <content name="snippet"> ... National Institute of Diabetes and Digestive and <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">Kidney</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">Diseases</span></span></content>

    <document rank="23" url="https://medlineplus.gov/crohnsdisease.html">
      <content name="title">Crohn's <span class="qt1">Disease</span></content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Ileocolitis</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Inflammatory Bowel <span class="qt1">Disease</span></content>
      <content name="altTitle">Regional Enteritis</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Regional Ileitis</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is Crohn's <span class="qt1">disease</span>?<p>Crohn's <span class="qt1">disease</span> is a chronic (long-lasting) <span class="qt1">disease</span> that causes inflammation in your digestive tract. It can affect any part of your digestive tract, which runs from your mouth to your anus. But it usually affects your small intestine and the beginning of your large intestine.</p><p>Crohn's <span class="qt1">disease</span> is an inflammatory bowel <span class="qt1">disease</span> (IBD). Ulcerative colitis and microscopic colitis are other common types of IBD.</p>What causes Crohn's <span class="qt1">disease</span>?<p>The cause of Crohn's <span class="qt1">disease</span> is unknown. Researchers think that an autoimmune reaction may be one cause. An autoimmune reaction happens when your immune system attacks healthy cells in your body. Genetics may also play a role, since Crohn's <span class="qt1">disease</span> can run in families.</p><p>Stress and eating certain foods don't cause the <span class="qt1">disease</span>, but they can make your symptoms worse.</p>Who is more likely to develop Crohn's <span class="qt1">disease</span>?<p>There are certain factors that may raise your risk of Crohn's <span class="qt1">disease</span>:</p><ul><li>Family history of the <span class="qt1">disease</span>. Having a parent, child, or sibling with the <span class="qt1">disease</span> puts you at higher risk.</li><li>Smoking. This may double your risk of developing Crohn's <span class="qt1">disease</span>.</li><li>Certain medicines, such as antibiotics, birth-control pills, and nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) such as aspirin or ibuprofen. These may slightly increase your chance of developing Crohn's.</li><li>A high-fat diet. This may also slightly increase your risk of Crohn's.</li></ul>What are the symptoms of Crohn's <span class="qt1">disease</span>?<p>The symptoms of Crohn's <span class="qt1">disease</span> can vary, depending where and how severe your inflammation is. The most common symptoms include:</p><ul><li>Diarrhea</li><li>Cramping and pain in your abdomen</li><li>Weight loss</li></ul><p>Some other possible symptoms are:</p><ul><li>Anemia, a condition in which you have fewer red blood cells than normal</li><li>Eye redness or pain</li><li>Fatigue</li><li>Fever</li><li>Joint pain or soreness</li><li>Nausea or loss of appetite</li><li>Skin changes that involve red, tender bumps under the skin</li></ul><p>Stress and eating certain foods such as carbonated (fizzy) drinks and high-fiber foods may make some people's symptoms worse.</p>What other problems can Crohn's <span class="qt1">disease</span> cause?<p>Crohn's <span class="qt1">disease</span> can cause other problems, including:</p><ul><li>Intestinal obstruction, a blockage in the intestine</li><li>Fistulas, abnormal connections between two parts inside of the body</li><li>Abscesses, pus-filled pockets of infection</li><li>Anal fissures, small tears in your anus that may cause itching, pain, or bleeding</li><li>Ulcers, open sores in your mouth, intestines, anus, or perineum</li><li>Malnutrition, when your body does not get the right amount of vitamins, minerals, and nutrients it needs</li><li>Inflammation in other areas of your body, such as your joints, eyes, and skin</li></ul>How is Crohn's <span class="qt1">disease</span> diagnosed?<p>Your health care provider may use many tools to make a diagnosis:</p><ul><li>A medical history, which includes asking about your symptoms</li><li>A family history</li><li>A physical exam, including:
	<ul><li>Checking for bloating in your abdomen.</li><li>Listening to sounds within your abdomen using a stethoscope.</li><li>Tapping on your abdomen to check for tenderness and pain and to see if your liver or spleen is abnormal or enlarged.</li></ul>
</li><li>Various tests, including:
	<ul><li>Blood and stool tests.</li><li>A colonoscopy.</li><li>An upper GI endoscopy, a procedure in which your provider uses a scope to look inside your mouth, esophagus, stomach, and small intestine.</li><li>Diagnostic imaging tests, such as a CT scan or an upper GI series. An upper GI series uses a special liquid called barium and x-rays. Drinking the barium will make your upper GI tract more visible on an x-ray.</li></ul>
</li></ul>What are the treatments for Crohn's <span class="qt1">disease</span>?<p>There is no cure for Crohn's <span class="qt1">disease</span>, but treatments can decrease inflammation in your intestines, relieve symptoms, and prevent complications. Treatments include medicines, bowel rest, and surgery. No single treatment works for everyone. You and your provider can work together to figure out which treatment is best for you:</p><ul><li>Medicines for Crohn's include various medicines that decrease the inflammation. Many of these medicines do this by reducing the activity of your immune system. Certain medicines can also help with symptoms or complications, such as nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs and anti-diarrheal medicines. If your Crohn's causes an infection, you may need antibiotics.</li><li>Bowel rest involves drinking only certain liquids or not eating or drinking anything. This allows your intestines to rest. You may need to do this if your Crohn's <span class="qt1">disease</span> symptoms are severe. You get your nutrients through drinking a liquid, a feeding tube, or an intravenous (IV) tube. You may need to do bowel rest in the hospital, or you may be able to do it at home. It will last for a few days or up to several weeks.</li><li>Surgery can treat complications and reduce symptoms when other treatments are not helping enough. The surgery will involve removing a damaged part of your digestive tract to treat:
	<ul><li>Fistulas</li><li>Bleeding that is life threatening</li><li>Intestinal obstructions</li><li>Side effects from medicines when they threaten your health</li><li>Symptoms when medicines do not improve your condition</li></ul>
</li></ul><p>Changing your diet can help reduce symptoms. Your provider may recommend that you make changes to your diet, such as:</p><ul><li>Avoiding carbonated drinks</li><li>Avoiding popcorn, vegetable skins, nuts, and other high-fiber foods</li><li>Drinking more liquids</li><li>Eating smaller meals more often</li><li>Keeping a food diary to help identify foods that cause problems</li></ul><p>In some cases, your provider may ask you to go on a special diet, such as a diet that is:</p><ul><li>High calorie</li><li>Lactose free</li><li>Low fat</li><li>Low fiber</li><li>Low salt</li></ul><p>If you are not absorbing enough nutrients, you may need to take nutritional supplements and vitamins.</p><p>National Institute of Diabetes and Digestive and <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">Kidney</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">Diseases</span></span></p></content>
