      <content name="altTitle">Alcoholic Fatty <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">Liver</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">Disease</span></span></content>
      <content name="altTitle">Metabolic Dysfunction-Associated Steatotic <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">Liver</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">Disease</span></span></content>
      <content name="altTitle">Nonalcoholic Fatty <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">Liver</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">Disease</span></span></content>
      <content name="altTitle">Steatotic <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">Liver</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">Disease</span></span></content>
      <content name="altTitle">ALD</content>
      <content name="altTitle">MASLD</content>
      <content name="altTitle">NAFLD</content>
      <content name="altTitle">SLD</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is fatty <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">liver</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span>?<p>Your <span class="qt0">liver</span> is the largest organ inside your body. It helps your body digest food, store energy, and remove poisons. Fatty <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">liver</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span> is a condition in which fat builds up in your <span class="qt0">liver</span>. There are two main types:</p><ul><li>Nonalcoholic fatty <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">liver</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span> (NAFLD)</li><li>Alcoholic fatty <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">liver</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span>, also called alcoholic steatohepatitis</li></ul>What is nonalcoholic fatty <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">liver</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span> (NAFLD)?<p>NAFLD is a type of fatty <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">liver</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span> that is not related to heavy alcohol use. There are two kinds:</p><ul><li>Simple fatty <span class="qt0">liver</span>, in which you have fat in your <span class="qt0">liver</span> but little or no inflammation or <span class="qt0">liver</span> cell damage. Simple fatty <span class="qt0">liver</span> typically does not get bad enough to cause <span class="qt0">liver</span> damage or complications.</li><li>Nonalcoholic steatohepatitis (NASH), in which you have inflammation and <span class="qt0">liver</span> cell damage, as well as fat in your <span class="qt0">liver</span>. Inflammation and <span class="qt0">liver</span> cell damage can cause fibrosis, or scarring, of the <span class="qt0">liver</span>. NASH may lead to cirrhosis or <span class="qt0">liver</span> cancer.</li></ul>What is alcoholic fatty <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">liver</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span>?<p>Alcoholic fatty <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">liver</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span> is due to heavy alcohol use. Your <span class="qt0">liver</span> breaks down most of the alcohol you drink, so it can be removed from your body. But the process of breaking it down can generate harmful substances. These substances can damage <span class="qt0">liver</span> cells, promote inflammation, and weaken your body's natural defenses. The more alcohol that you drink, the more you damage your <span class="qt0">liver</span>.  Alcoholic fatty <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">liver</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span> is the earliest stage of alcohol-related <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">liver</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span>. The next stages are alcoholic <span class="qt0">hepatitis</span> and cirrhosis.</p>Who is at risk for fatty <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">liver</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span>?<p>The cause of nonalcoholic fatty <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">liver</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span> (NAFLD) is unknown. Researchers do know that it is more common in people who:</p><ul><li>Have type 2 diabetes and prediabetes</li><li>Have obesity</li><li>Are middle aged or older (although children can also get it)</li><li>Are Hispanic, followed by non-Hispanic Whites. It is less common in African Americans.</li><li>Have high levels of fats in the blood, such as cholesterol and triglycerides</li><li>Have high blood pressure</li><li>Take certain drugs, such as corticosteroids and some cancer drugs</li><li>Have certain metabolic disorders, including metabolic syndrome</li><li>Have rapid weight loss</li><li>Have certain infections, such as <span class="qt0">hepatitis</span> C</li><li>Have been exposed to some toxins</li></ul><p>NAFLD affects about 25% of people in the world. As the rates of obesity, type 2 diabetes, and high cholesterol are rising in the United States, so is the rate of NAFLD. NAFLD is the most common chronic <span class="qt0">liver</span> disorder in the United States.</p><p>Alcoholic fatty <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">liver</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span> only happens in people who are heavy drinkers, especially those who have been drinking for a long period of time. The risk is higher for heavy drinkers who are women, have obesity, or have certain genetic mutations.</p>What are the symptoms of fatty <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">liver</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span>?<p>Both NAFLD and alcoholic fatty <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">liver</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span> are usually silent <span class="qt1">diseases</span> with few or no symptoms. If you do have symptoms, you may feel tired or have discomfort in the upper right side of your abdomen.</p>How is fatty <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">liver</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span> diagnosed?<p>Because there are often no symptoms, it is not easy to find fatty <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">liver</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span>. Your doctor may suspect that you have it if you get abnormal results on <span class="qt0">liver</span> tests that you had for other reasons. To make a diagnosis, your doctor will use:</p><ul><li>Your medical history</li><li>A physical exam</li><li>Various tests, including blood and imaging tests, and sometimes a biopsy</li></ul><p>As part of the medical history, your doctor will ask about your alcohol use, to find out whether fat in your <span class="qt0">liver</span> is a sign of alcoholic fatty <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">liver</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span> or nonalcoholic fatty <span class="qt0">liver</span> (NAFLD). He or she will also ask which medicines you take, to try to determine whether a medicine is causing your NAFLD.</p><p>During the physical exam, your doctor will examine your body and check your weight and height. Your doctor will look for signs of fatty <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">liver</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span>, such as:</p><ul><li>An enlarged <span class="qt0">liver</span></li><li>Signs of cirrhosis, such as jaundice, a condition that causes your skin and whites of your eyes to turn yellow</li></ul><p>You will likely have blood tests, including <span class="qt0">liver</span> function tests and blood count tests. In some cases you may also have imaging tests, like those that check for fat in the <span class="qt0">liver</span> and the stiffness of your <span class="qt0">liver. Liver</span> stiffness can mean fibrosis, which is scarring of the <span class="qt0">liver</span>. In some cases you may also need a <span class="qt0">liver</span> biopsy to confirm the diagnosis, and to check how bad the <span class="qt0">liver</span> damage is.</p>What are the treatments for fatty <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">liver</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span>?<p>Doctors recommend weight loss for nonalcoholic fatty <span class="qt0">liver</span>. Weight loss can reduce fat in the <span class="qt0">liver</span>, inflammation, and fibrosis. If your doctor thinks that a certain medicine is the cause of your NAFLD, you should stop taking that medicine.  But check with your doctor before stopping the medicine. You may need to get off the medicine gradually, and you might need to switch to another medicine instead.</p><p>There are no medicines that have been approved to treat NAFLD. Studies are investigating whether a certain diabetes medicine or Vitamin E can help, but more studies are needed.</p><p>The most important part of treating alcohol-related fatty <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">liver</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span> is to stop drinking alcohol. If you need help doing that, you may want to see a therapist or participate in an alcohol recovery program. There are also medicines that can help, either by reducing your cravings or making you feel sick if you drink alcohol.</p><p>Both alcoholic fatty <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">liver</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span> and one type of nonalcoholic fatty <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">liver</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span> (nonalcoholic steatohepatitis) can lead to cirrhosis. Doctors can treat the health problems caused by cirrhosis with medicines, operations, and other medical procedures. If the cirrhosis leads to <span class="qt0">liver</span> failure, you may need a <span class="qt0">liver</span> transplant.</p>What are some lifestyle changes that can help with fatty <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">liver</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span>?<p>If you have any of the types of fatty <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">liver</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span>, there are some lifestyle changes that can help:</p><ul><li>Eat a healthy diet, limiting salt and sugar, plus eating lots of fruits, vegetables, and whole grains</li><li>Get vaccinations for <span class="qt0">hepatitis</span> A and B, the flu and pneumococcal <span class="qt1">disease</span>. If you get <span class="qt0">hepatitis</span> A or B along with fatty <span class="qt0">liver</span>, it is more likely to lead to <span class="qt0">liver</span> failure. People with chronic <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">liver</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span> are more likely to get infections, so the other two vaccinations are also important.</li><li>Get regular exercise, which can help you lose weight and reduce fat in the <span class="qt0">liver</span></li><li>Talk with your doctor before using dietary supplements, such as vitamins, or any complementary or alternative medicines or medical practices. Some herbal remedies can damage your <span class="qt0">liver</span>.</li></ul></content>

      <content name="FullSummary"><p>Your <span class="qt0">liver</span> is the largest organ inside your body. It helps your body digest food, store energy, and remove poisons.</p><p>There are many kinds of <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">liver</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">diseases</span></span>:</p><ul><li><span class="qt1">Diseases</span> caused by  viruses, such as <span class="qt0">hepatitis A, hepatitis</span> B, and <span class="qt0">hepatitis</span> C</li><li><span class="qt1">Diseases</span> caused by drugs, poisons, or too much alcohol. Examples include fatty <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">liver</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span> and cirrhosis.</li><li><span class="qt0">Liver</span> cancer</li><li>Inherited <span class="qt1">diseases</span>, such as hemochromatosis and Wilson <span class="qt1">disease</span></li></ul><p>Symptoms of <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">liver</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span> can vary, but they often include swelling of the abdomen and legs, bruising easily, changes in the color of your stool and urine, and jaundice, or yellowing of the skin and eyes. Sometimes there are no symptoms. Tests such as imaging tests and <span class="qt0">liver</span> function tests can check for <span class="qt0">liver</span> damage and help to diagnose <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">liver</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">diseases</span></span>.</p></content>

      <content name="title">Alpha-1 Antitrypsin Deficiency</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">AATD</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Alpha-1</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Inherited emphysema</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency (AAT deficiency)?<p>Alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency (AAT deficiency, or AATD) is an inherited condition that raises your risk for lung and <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">liver</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span>. If you have this condition, your body doesn't make enough alpha-1 antitrypsin (AAT).</p><p>AAT is made by your <span class="qt0">liver</span>. It helps protect your lungs from inflammation and irritating substances you might breathe in, such as smoke. If your <span class="qt0">liver</span> doesn't make enough AAT, your lungs may be more easily damaged from smoking, pollution, or dust from the environment. This can lead to a serious lung condition called chronic obstructive pulmonary <span class="qt1">disease (COPD</span>). AAT deficiency may also cause a <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">liver</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span> called cirrhosis. This is more common in children who have AAT deficiency.</p>What causes alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency (AAT deficiency)?<p>AAT deficiency is a genetic disorder. That means it's caused by changes in your genes, which may also be called gene variants or mutations.</p><p>Your genes carry information that controls what you look like and how your body works. AAT deficiency is caused by changes in the SERPINA1 gene, which carries instructions for making the AAT protein. These gene changes are inherited from your parents, so AAT deficiency tends to run in families:</p><ul><li>If you have two mutated copies of the gene, it means you have a condition called AAT deficiency. People with this disorder have a higher risk of getting lung <span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span><span class="qt2"> or </span><span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">liver</span></span> damage before the age of 45.</li><li>If you have one mutated copy of the gene, you are a carrier of AAT deficiency. In these cases, this means you are at slightly higher risk of developing lung <span class="qt1">disease</span>, especially if you have other risk factors, such as being a smoker. You could still pass the mutated gene on to your children.</li></ul><p>There are a few gene changes that cause AAT deficiency. These gene changes can:</p><ul><li>Decrease the amount of AAT protein your <span class="qt0">liver</span> makes.</li><li>Prevent your <span class="qt0">liver</span> from making any AAT.</li><li>Affect the shape of the AAT protein so that it can't move out of your <span class="qt0">liver</span> to protect your lungs. Over time, AAT builds up in your <span class="qt0">liver</span> and causes damage.</li></ul>What are the symptoms of alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency (AAT deficiency)?<p>Some people who have AAT deficiency do not have any symptoms. For those who do, symptoms usually appear in people between 20 and 50 years old. These symptoms may include:</p><ul><li>Wheezing</li><li>Shortness of breath, especially after exercise</li><li>Chronic cough with phlegm (mucus)</li><li>Repeated respiratory infections such as colds and the flu</li><li>Chest pain</li><li>Fatigue</li><li>Faster-than-normal heartbeat when you stand up</li><li>Vision problems</li><li>Losing weight without trying</li></ul><p>Some people who have AAT deficiency may have <span class="qt0">liver</span> damage. Signs of <span class="qt0">liver</span> damage include jaundice (a condition that causes your skin and eyes to turn yellow) and swelling in your legs.</p><p>Rarely, AAT deficiency can cause skin problems, such as painful lumps or patches.</p>How is alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency (AAT deficiency) diagnosed?<p>Your health care provider may test you for AAT deficiency if you have:</p><ul><li>Symptoms of AAT deficiency</li><li>A condition that could be related to AAT deficiency</li><li>Relatives who have AAT deficiency</li><li>Relatives who have a lung or <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">liver</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span> that could be related to AAT deficiency</li></ul><p>AAT deficiency in babies often affects the <span class="qt0">liver</span>. Your baby may need AAT testing if he or she has signs of <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">liver</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span> such as jaundice or abnormal <span class="qt0">liver</span> enzyme tests.</p><p>A blood test can check the level of AAT protein in your blood. If the level is lower than normal, it is likely that you have AAT deficiency.</p><p>A genetic test is the most certain way to check for AAT deficiency and should be done to confirm the results of the blood test. There are two types of genetic tests:</p><ul><li>A genotype test looks for the more common types of gene changes that can cause AAT deficiency.</li><li>A phenotype test checks for changes in the AAT protein that change how it would normally work.</li></ul><p>If your lungs are affected, you may also have lung function tests to see how well your lungs are working.</p>What are the treatments for alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency (AAT deficiency)?<p>There is no cure for AAT deficiency, but there are treatments to help with the symptoms and slow the lung damage it causes. Treatment options may include:</p><ul><li>Inhaled medicines to help you breathe better.</li><li>Pulmonary rehabilitation.</li><li>Oxygen therapy.</li><li>Augmentation therapy, which is a lifelong treatment. It raises the levels of the AAT protein in your lungs, using ATT protein taken from the blood of donors. It helps slow down lung damage. It cannot prevent <span class="qt0">liver</span> damage.</li><li>Lung surgery or a lung transplant, if your lungs are severely damaged.</li><li><span class="qt0">Liver</span> transplant, if your <span class="qt0">liver</span> is severely damaged.</li></ul><p>To help prevent or delay lung damage, it is important to quit smoking (if you smoke) and avoid secondhand smoke, dust, and air pollution. Ask your provider If you need to stop drinking alcohol.</p></content>
      <content name="mesh">alpha 1-Antitrypsin Deficiency</content>

    <document rank="3" url="https://medlineplus.gov/alcoholusedisorderaud.html">
      <content name="title">Alcohol Use Disorder (AUD)</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Alcohol Abuse</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Alcohol Dependence</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Alcohol Withdrawal</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Alcoholism</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Binge Drinking</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Drinking</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Substance Use</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is alcohol use disorder (AUD)?<p>For most adults, moderate alcohol use is probably not harmful. Your risk of developing an alcohol use disorder (AUD) depends on how much, how often, and how quickly you drink alcohol. Alcohol misuse means that drinking causes distress and harm. Over time alcohol misuse can increase your risk of AUD.</p><p>AUD can range from mild to severe, depending on the symptoms. Severe AUD is sometimes called alcoholism or alcohol dependence.</p><p>AUD is a <span class="qt1">disease</span> that causes:</p><ul><li>Craving - a strong need to drink</li><li>Loss of control - not being able to stop drinking once you've started</li><li>Negative emotional state - feeling anxious and irritable when you are not drinking</li></ul>What is binge drinking?<p>Binge drinking is a type of alcohol misuse. It means drinking so much at once that your blood alcohol concentration (BAC) level is 0.08% or more. For a man, this usually happens after having 5 or more drinks within a few hours. For a woman, it is after about 4 or more drinks within a few hours. Not everyone who binge drinks has an AUD, but they are at higher risk for getting one.</p>What are the dangers of too much alcohol?<p>Too much alcohol is dangerous. Heavy drinking can increase the risk of certain cancers. It may lead to <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">liver</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">diseases</span></span>, such as fatty <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">liver</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span> and cirrhosis. It can also cause damage to the brain and other organs. Drinking during pregnancy can harm your fetus. Alcohol also increases the risk of death from car crashes, injuries, homicide, and suicide.</p>How do I know if I have an alcohol use disorder (AUD)?<p>You may have an AUD if you can answer yes to two or more of these questions:</p><p>In the past year, have you:</p><ul><li>Ended up drinking more or for a longer time than you had planned to?</li><li>Wanted to cut down or stop drinking, or tried to, but couldn't?</li><li>Spent a lot of your time drinking or recovering from drinking?</li><li>Felt a strong need to drink?</li><li>Found that drinking - or being sick from drinking - often interfered with your family life, job, or school?</li><li>Kept drinking even though it was causing trouble with your family or friends?</li><li>Given up or cut back on activities that you enjoyed just so you could drink?</li><li>Gotten into dangerous situations while drinking or after drinking? Some examples are driving drunk and having unsafe sex.</li><li>Kept drinking even though it was making you feel depressed or anxious? Or when it was adding to another health problem?</li><li>Had to drink more and more to feel the effects of the alcohol?</li><li>Had withdrawal symptoms when the alcohol was wearing off? They include trouble sleeping, shakiness, irritability, anxiety, depression, restlessness, nausea, and sweating. In severe cases, you could have a fever, seizures, or hallucinations.</li></ul><p>If you have any of these symptoms, your drinking may already be a cause for concern. The more symptoms you have, the more serious the problem is.</p>What should I do if I think that I might have an alcohol use disorder (AUD)?<p>If you think you might have an AUD, see your health care provider for an evaluation. Your provider can help make a treatment plan, prescribe medicines, and if needed, give you treatment referrals.</p><p>NIH: National Institute on Alcohol Abuse and Alcoholism</p></content>

    <document rank="4" url="https://medlineplus.gov/hepatitisc.html">
      <content name="title"><span class="qt0">Hepatitis</span> C</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">HCV</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is <span class="qt0">hepatitis</span> C?<p><span class="qt0">Hepatitis</span> is inflammation of the <span class="qt0">liver</span>. Inflammation is swelling that happens when tissues of the body are injured or infected. Inflammation can damage organs.</p><p>There are different types of <span class="qt0">hepatitis</span>. One type, <span class="qt0">hepatitis</span> C, is caused by the <span class="qt0">hepatitis</span> C virus (HCV). <span class="qt0">Hepatitis</span> C can range from a mild <span class="qt1">illness</span> lasting a few weeks to a serious, lifelong <span class="qt1">illness</span>.</p><p><span class="qt0">Hepatitis</span> C can be acute or chronic:</p><ul><li>Acute <span class="qt0">hepatitis</span> C is a short-term infection. The symptoms can last up to 6 months. Sometimes your body is able to fight off the infection and the virus goes away. But for most people, an acute infection leads to chronic infection.</li><li>Chronic <span class="qt0">hepatitis</span> C is a long-lasting infection. If it is not treated, it can last for a lifetime and cause serious health problems, including <span class="qt0">liver</span> damage, cirrhosis (scarring of the <span class="qt0">liver), liver</span> cancer, and even death.</li></ul>How is <span class="qt0">hepatitis</span> C spread?<p><span class="qt0">Hepatitis</span> C spreads through contact with the blood of someone who has HCV. This contact may be through:</p><ul><li>Sharing drug needles or other drug materials with someone who has HCV. In the United States, this is the most common way that people get <span class="qt0">hepatitis</span> C.</li><li>Getting an accidental stick with a needle that was used on someone who has HCV. This can happen in health care settings.</li><li>Being tattooed or pierced with tools or inks that were not sterilized after being used on someone who has HCV.</li><li>Having contact with the blood or open sores of someone who has HCV.</li><li>Sharing personal care items that may have come in contact with another person's blood, such as razors or toothbrushes.</li><li>Being born to a mother with HCV.</li><li>Having unprotected sex with someone who has HCV.</li></ul><p>Before 1992, <span class="qt0">hepatitis</span> C was also commonly spread through blood transfusions and organ transplants. Since then, there has been routine testing of the U.S. blood supply for HCV. It is now very rare for someone to get HCV this way.</p>Who is more likely to get <span class="qt0">hepatitis</span> C?<p>You are more likely to get <span class="qt0">hepatitis</span> C if you:</p><ul><li>Have injected drugs</li><li>Had a blood transfusion or organ transplant before July 1992</li><li>Have hemophilia and received clotting factor before 1987</li><li>Have been on kidney dialysis</li><li>Have been in contact with blood or infected needles at work</li><li>Have had tattoos or body piercings</li><li>Have worked or lived in a prison</li><li>Were born to a mother with <span class="qt0">hepatitis</span> C</li><li>Have HIV</li><li>Have had more than one sex partner in the last 6 months</li><li>Have had a sexually transmitted infection (STI)</li><li>Are a man who has had sex with men (MSM)</li></ul><p>If you are at high risk for <span class="qt0">hepatitis</span> C, your health care provider will likely recommend that you get tested for it.</p>What are the symptoms of <span class="qt0">hepatitis</span> C?<p>Most people with <span class="qt0">hepatitis</span> C have no symptoms. Some people with acute <span class="qt0">hepatitis</span> C do have symptoms within 1 to 3 months after they are exposed to the virus. These symptoms may include:</p><ul><li>Dark yellow urine</li><li>Fatigue</li><li>Fever</li><li>Gray- or clay-colored stools</li><li>Joint pain</li><li>Loss of appetite</li><li>Nausea and/or vomiting</li><li>Pain in your abdomen (belly)</li><li>Jaundice (yellowish eyes and skin)</li></ul><p>If you have chronic <span class="qt0">hepatitis</span> C, you probably will not have symptoms until it causes complications. This can happen decades after you were infected. For this reason, <span class="qt0">hepatitis</span> C screening is important, even if you have no symptoms.</p>What other problems can <span class="qt0">hepatitis</span> C cause?<p>Without treatment, <span class="qt0">hepatitis</span> C may lead to cirrhosis, <span class="qt0">liver</span> failure, and <span class="qt0">liver</span> cancer. Early diagnosis and treatment of <span class="qt0">hepatitis</span> C can prevent these complications.</p>How is <span class="qt0">hepatitis</span> C diagnosed?<p>Providers diagnose <span class="qt0">hepatitis</span> C based on your medical history, a physical exam, and blood tests.</p><p>If you do have <span class="qt0">hepatitis</span> C, you may need additional tests to check for <span class="qt0">liver</span> damage. These tests may include other blood tests, an ultrasound of the <span class="qt0">liver, and a liver</span> biopsy.</p>What are the treatments for <span class="qt0">hepatitis</span> C?<p>Treatment for <span class="qt0">hepatitis</span> C is with antiviral medicines. They can cure the <span class="qt1">disease</span> in most cases.</p><p>If you have acute <span class="qt0">hepatitis</span> C, your provider may wait to see if your infection becomes chronic before starting treatment.</p><p>If your <span class="qt0">hepatitis</span> C causes cirrhosis, you should see a doctor who specializes in <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">liver</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">diseases</span></span>. Treatments for health problems related to cirrhosis include medicines, surgery, and other medical procedures. If your <span class="qt0">hepatitis</span> C leads to <span class="qt0">liver</span> failure or <span class="qt0">liver</span> cancer, you may need a <span class="qt0">liver</span> transplant.</p>Can <span class="qt0">hepatitis</span> C be prevented?<p>There is no vaccine for <span class="qt0">hepatitis</span> C. But you can help protect yourself from <span class="qt0">hepatitis</span> C infection by:</p><ul><li>Not sharing drug needles or other drug materials.</li><li>Wearing gloves if you have to touch another person's blood or open sores.</li><li>Making sure your tattoo artist or body piercer uses sterile tools and unopened ink.</li><li>Not sharing personal items such toothbrushes, razors, or nail clippers.</li><li>Using a latex condom during sex. If your or your partner is allergic to latex, you can use polyurethane condoms.</li></ul><p>NIH: National Institute of Diabetes and Digestive and Kidney <span class="qt1">Diseases</span></p></content>
      <content name="mesh"><span class="qt0">Hepatitis</span> C</content>
      <content name="groupName">Digestive System</content>
      <content name="groupName">Infections</content>

    <document rank="5" url="https://medlineplus.gov/cirrhosis.html">
      <content name="title">Cirrhosis</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle"><span class="qt0">Liver</span> Cirrhosis</content>
      <content name="altTitle"><span class="qt0">Hepatic</span> fibrosis</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>Cirrhosis is scarring of the <span class="qt0">liver</span>. Scar tissue forms because of injury or long-term <span class="qt1">disease</span>. Scar tissue cannot do what healthy <span class="qt0">liver</span> tissue does - make protein, help fight infections, clean the blood, help digest food and store energy.   Cirrhosis can lead to  :</p><ul><li>Easy bruising or bleeding, or nosebleeds</li><li>Swelling of the abdomen or legs </li><li>Extra sensitivity to medicines</li><li>High blood pressure in the vein entering the <span class="qt0">liver</span></li><li>Enlarged veins called varices in the esophagus and stomach. Varices can bleed suddenly.</li><li> Kidney failure</li><li>Jaundice</li><li>Severe itching</li><li>Gallstones</li></ul><p>A small number of people with cirrhosis get <span class="qt0">liver</span> cancer.</p><p>Your doctor will diagnose cirrhosis with blood tests, imaging tests, or a biopsy.</p><p>Cirrhosis has many causes. In the United States, the most common causes are chronic alcoholism and <span class="qt0">hepatitis</span>. Nothing will make the scar tissue disappear, but treating the cause can keep it from getting worse. If too much scar tissue forms, you may need to consider a <span class="qt0">liver</span> transplant.  </p><p>NIH: National Institute of Diabetes and Digestive and Kidney <span class="qt1">Diseases</span></p></content>
      <content name="mesh"><span class="qt0">Liver</span> Cirrhosis</content>
      <content name="groupName">Digestive System</content>
      <content name="snippet"> ... National Institute of Diabetes and Digestive and Kidney <span class="qt1">Diseases</span></content>

    <document rank="6" url="https://medlineplus.gov/livertransplantation.html">
      <content name="title"><span class="qt0">Liver</span> Transplantation</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle"><span class="qt0">Hepatic</span> Transplantation</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>Your <span class="qt0">liver</span> is the largest organ inside your body. It helps your body digest food, store energy, and remove poisons. You cannot live without a <span class="qt0">liver</span> that works. If your <span class="qt0">liver</span> fails, your doctor may put you on a waiting list for a <span class="qt0">liver</span> transplant. Doctors do <span class="qt0">liver</span> transplants when other treatment cannot keep a damaged <span class="qt0">liver</span> working.</p><p>During a <span class="qt0">liver</span> transplantation, the surgeon removes the diseased <span class="qt0">liver</span> and replaces it with a healthy one. Most transplant <span class="qt0">livers</span> come from a donor who has died.  Sometimes there is a living donor.  This is when a healthy person donates part of his or her <span class="qt0">liver</span> for a specific patient.</p><p>The most common reason for a transplant in adults is cirrhosis. This is scarring of the <span class="qt0">liver</span>, caused by injury or long-term <span class="qt1">disease</span>. The most common reason in children is biliary atresia, a <span class="qt1">disease</span> of the bile ducts.</p><p>If you have a transplant, you must take drugs the rest of your life to help keep your body from rejecting the new <span class="qt0">liver</span>.</p><p>NIH: National Institute of Diabetes and Digestive and Kidney <span class="qt1">Diseases</span></p></content>
      <content name="mesh"><span class="qt0">Liver</span> Transplantation</content>
      <content name="groupName">Digestive System</content>
      <content name="groupName">Transplantation and Donation</content>
      <content name="snippet"> ... National Institute of Diabetes and Digestive and Kidney <span class="qt1">Diseases</span></content>

    <document rank="7" url="https://medlineplus.gov/alcohol.html">
      <content name="title">Alcohol</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Alcohol Consumption</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Drinking</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>Many Americans drink alcohol at least occasionally. The  Dietary Guidelines for Americans say that adults of legal drinking age should either not drink or drink in moderation. Drinking less is better for your health than drinking more. Also, there are some people who should not drink at all.</p><p>If you are going to drink, it's important to know how alcohol affects you and how much is too much.</p>How does alcohol affect the body?<p>Alcohol is a central nervous system depressant. This means that it is a drug that slows down brain activity. It can change your mood, behavior, and self-control. It can cause problems with memory and thinking clearly. Alcohol can also affect your coordination and physical control.</p><p>Alcohol also has effects on the other organs in your body. For example, it can raise your blood pressure and heart rate. If you drink too much at once, it could make you throw up.</p>Why are the effects of alcohol different from person to person?<p>Alcohol's effects vary from person to person, depending on a variety of factors, including:</p><ul><li>How much you drink</li><li>How often you drink</li><li>Your age</li><li>Your sex</li><li>Your genetics</li><li>Your overall health</li><li>Whether or not you have a family history of alcohol problems</li></ul>What is moderate drinking?<ul><li>For most women, moderate drinking is no more than 1 standard drink a day</li><li>For most men, moderate drinking is no more than 2 standard drinks a day</li></ul><p>Even though moderate drinking may be safe for many people, there are still risks. Moderate drinking can raise the risk of death from certain cancers and heart <span class="qt1">diseases</span>.</p>What is a standard drink?<p>In the United States, a standard drink is one that contains about 14 grams of pure alcohol, which is found in:</p><ul><li>12 ounces of regular beer (5% alcohol content)</li><li>5 ounces of wine (12% alcohol content)</li><li>1.5 ounces or a "shot" of distilled spirits or liquor (40% alcohol content)</li></ul>Who should not drink alcohol?<p>Some people should not drink alcohol at all, including those who:</p><ul><li>Are in recovery from an alcohol use disorder (AUD)</li><li> Are are unable to control the amount they drink</li><li>Are under age 21</li><li>Are pregnant or trying to become pregnant</li><li>Are taking medicines that can interact with alcohol</li><li>Have medical conditions that get can worse if you drink alcohol</li><li>Are planning on driving</li><li>Will be operating machinery or doing activities that require skill, coordination, and alertness</li></ul><p>If you have questions about whether it is safe for you to drink, talk with your health care provider.</p>What is excessive drinking?<p>Excessive drinking includes binge drinking and heavy alcohol use:</p><ul><li>Binge drinking is drinking so much at once that your blood alcohol concentration (BAC) level is 0.08% or more: 
  <ul><li>For men, this usually happens after having 5 or more drinks within a few hours.</li><li>For women, it is usually after about 4 or more drinks within a few hours.</li></ul>
</li><li>Heavy alcohol use means:
  <ul><li>For men, having more than 5 drinks on any day or more than 15 drinks per week.</li><li>For women, having more than 4 drinks on any day or more than 8 drinks per week.</li></ul>
</li></ul><p>Binge drinking raises your risk of injuries, car crashes, and alcohol overdose. It also puts you at risk of becoming violent or being the victim of violence.</p><p>Heavy alcohol use over a long period of time may cause health problems such as:</p><ul><li>Alcohol use disorder (AUD)</li><li><span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">Liver</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">diseases</span></span>, including cirrhosis and fatty <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">liver</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span></li><li>Heart <span class="qt1">diseases</span></li><li>Increased risk of certain cancers</li><li>Increased risk of injuries</li></ul><p>Heavy alcohol use can also cause problems at home, at work, and with friends. But treatment can help.</p><p>NIH: National Institute on Alcohol Abuse and Alcoholism</p></content>
      <content name="mesh">Alcohol Drinking</content>
      <content name="groupName">Substance Use and Disorders</content>
      <content name="groupName">Food and Nutrition</content>

(AUD)  <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">Liver</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">diseases</span></span>, including cirrhosis and fatty <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">liver</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span>  Heart  ... </content>

    <document rank="8" url="https://medlineplus.gov/bodyweight.html">
      <content name="title">Body Weight</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">BMI</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Overweight</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Underweight</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is body weight?<p>Body weight is the total mass of your body. Keeping track of your weight is one way to check for changes in your health, but it can't tell you the whole story. That's because your weight includes muscle, bone, fat, and water. Also, everyday factors can play a role in your weight, such as your:</p><ul><li>Diet</li><li>Activity level</li><li>Stress level</li><li>Sleep habits</li><li>Family history and genetics</li><li>Age</li></ul>What factors help determine a healthy body weight?<p>A healthy weight is more than just a number on the scale. It's about feeling good, having energy, and lowering your risk of <span class="qt1">disease</span>. Your health care provider can help you determine a healthy body weight based on several factors. These include your:</p><ul><li>Body Mass Index (BMI). BMI is a measure of how much you weigh compared to how tall you are. The higher your BMI, the higher your risk for certain <span class="qt1">diseases</span>. BMI does not distinguish between fat, muscle, and bone mass. It also may overestimate body fat in athletes or those with a muscular build, or underestimate body fat in older persons or others who have lost muscle.</li><li>Waist circumference. If most of your body fat is around your abdomen (belly) rather than your hips, you may be at a higher risk for heart <span class="qt1">disease</span> and type 2 diabetes.</li><li>Health history. Your health care provider will likely consider your risk factors for <span class="qt1">diseases</span> and health conditions, such as having high cholesterol, in determining your healthy body weight.</li></ul>What are body weight ranges?<p>Having a healthy body weight may lower your chance of developing certain health problems. Carrying too much or too little weight can affect your health. Ranges of body weight include:</p><ul><li>Underweight. Your weight is lower than it should be for your health.</li><li>Normal. Your weight is within a healthy range.</li><li>Overweight. Your weight is greater than it should be for your health.</li><li>Obese. Your body fat is greater than it should be for your health.</li></ul><p>Body weight needs can be different at different ages. For children, weight and growth are tracked over time based on their age.  For older adults, keeping muscle and strength is key, and sudden weight loss should always be checked by your provider.</p>What if I have a sudden gain or loss of weight?<p>Talk to your provider if you're losing or gaining weight without trying. A sudden, unexpected change in weight can be a sign of a medical problem.</p><p>Causes for sudden weight loss can include:</p><ul><li>Thyroid problems</li><li>Cancer</li><li>Infectious <span class="qt1">diseases</span></li><li>Digestive <span class="qt1">diseases</span></li><li>Certain medicines</li></ul><p>Causes for sudden weight gain can include:</p><ul><li>Certain medicines</li><li>Thyroid problems</li><li>Heart failure</li><li>Kidney <span class="qt1">disease</span></li><li><span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">Liver</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span></li></ul>How can I maintain a healthy body weight?<p>Maintaining a healthy body weight can improve energy, sleep, and overall wellbeing. Talk to your provider about what a healthy weight would be for you. If they recommend that you gain or lose weight, they might refer you to a dietician for support. A dietician is a professional who has special training to help you learn the best way to eat.</p><ul><li>Good nutrition and exercise can help in losing weight. Even a small change in weight can lower your risk of developing certain <span class="qt1">diseases</span>.</li><li>Eating extra calories within a well-balanced diet and treating any underlying medical problems can help you gain weight.</li></ul><p>Focus on your health, not just the number on the scale. With daily healthy habits and self-compassion, you can support lasting improvements in your overall wellbeing.</p><p>NIH: National Heart, Lung, and Blood Institute</p></content>

    <document rank="9" url="https://medlineplus.gov/jaundice.html">
      <content name="title">Jaundice</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Icterus</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Kernicterus</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>Jaundice causes your skin and the whites of your eyes to turn yellow. Too much bilirubin causes jaundice.  Bilirubin is a yellow chemical in hemoglobin, the substance that carries oxygen in your red blood cells. As red blood cells break down, your body builds new cells to replace them. The old ones are processed by the <span class="qt0">liver</span>. If the <span class="qt0">liver</span> cannot handle the blood cells as they break down, bilirubin builds up in the body and your skin may look yellow. </p><p>Many healthy babies have some jaundice during the first week of life.  It usually goes away.   However, jaundice can happen at any age and may be a sign of a problem.  Jaundice can happen for many reasons, such as:</p><ul><li>	Blood <span class="qt1">diseases</span></li><li>	Genetic syndromes</li><li>	<span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">Liver</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">diseases</span></span>, such as <span class="qt0">hepatitis</span> or cirrhosis</li><li>	Blockage of bile ducts </li><li>	Infections </li><li>	Medicines </li></ul></content>
      <content name="mesh">Hyperbilirubinemia</content>
      <content name="groupName">Digestive System</content>

    <document rank="10" url="https://medlineplus.gov/bleedingdisorders.html">
      <content name="title">Bleeding Disorders</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Blood Coagulation Disorders</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Clotting disorders</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>Normally, if you get hurt, your body forms a blood clot to stop the bleeding. For blood to clot, your body needs cells called platelets and proteins known as clotting factors. If 
you have a bleeding disorder, you either do not have enough platelets or clotting factors 
or they don't work the way they should. </p><p>Bleeding disorders can be the result of other <span class="qt1">diseases</span>, such as severe <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">liver</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span> or a lack of vitamin K. They can also be inherited. Hemophilia is an inherited bleeding disorder. Bleeding disorders can also be a side effect of medicines such as blood thinners.</p><p>Various blood tests can check for a bleeding disorder. You will also have a physical exam and history. Treatments depend on the cause. They may include medicines and transfusions of blood, platelets, or clotting factor.</p></content>

    <document rank="11" url="https://medlineplus.gov/statins.html">
      <content name="title">Statins</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">HMG-CoA reductase inhibitors</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is cholesterol?<p>Cholesterol is a waxy, fat-like substance that's found in all the cells in your body. Your body needs some cholesterol to work properly.  But if you have too much of it in your blood, it can stick to the walls of your arteries and narrow or even block them. This puts you at risk for coronary artery <span class="qt1">disease</span> and other heart <span class="qt1">diseases</span>.</p><p>Cholesterol is made by your <span class="qt0">liver</span>. It travels through the blood on proteins called lipoproteins. One type of lipoprotein, LDL, is sometimes called the "bad" cholesterol. A high LDL level leads to a buildup of cholesterol in your arteries. Another type, HDL, is sometimes called the "good" cholesterol. It carries cholesterol from other parts of your body back to your <span class="qt0">liver</span>. Then your <span class="qt0">liver</span> removes the cholesterol from your body.</p>What are statins?<p>Statins are a type of cholesterol medicine. Your health care provider may prescribe them if you have high cholesterol and making lifestyle changes does not lower your cholesterol enough. Statins are the most common medicines used to treat high cholesterol.</p>How do statins lower cholesterol?<p>Statins lower LDL cholesterol by slowing down how much cholesterol the <span class="qt0">liver</span> makes. They also increase your <span class="qt0">liver</span>'s ability to remove LDL cholesterol that is already in the blood. This can slow the formation of plaques in your arteries. Studies have shown that statins lower the risk of heart attack and stroke in people with high LDL cholesterol.</p><p>Statins can lower your triglyceride level as well. Triglycerides are another type of fat in your blood that can raise the risk of heart <span class="qt1">disease</span>. Statins may also increase your HDL cholesterol.</p>What are the risks of taking statins?<p>Statins usually don't cause side effects, but they may raise the risk of type 2 diabetes. However, this mainly happens in people already at high risk of diabetes, such as those who are overweight or have obesity, prediabetes, or metabolic syndrome.</p><p>Statins may also cause abnormal results on <span class="qt0">liver</span> enzymes tests, but actual <span class="qt0">liver</span> damage is very rare. Another uncommon side effect is muscle damage. In rare cases, this muscle damage can lead to muscle pain and kidney damage.</p><p>You should not take statins if you are pregnant or breastfeeding. They are also not recommended for people who have certain types of <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">liver</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span>.</p>How can I safely take statins?<p>To safely take statins, you should:</p><ul><li>Take your statin as prescribed. You should not stop taking this medicine on your own, since that can lead to a serious problem or, in rare cases, even cause death. Talk to your provider if you have any concerns about your statin or if you would like to stop or change to a different treatment.</li><li>Ask your provider which other medicines, supplements, or foods you should avoid. Some of these can interact with statins and cause serious side effects or make statins less effective. For example, grapefruit (fresh or as juice) affects how your <span class="qt0">liver</span> breaks down some statins.</li><li>Tell your provider about any symptoms or side effects you are having. Sometimes, people report muscle problems while taking statins. If you start having muscle pain, your provider may order a blood test to look look for muscle damage. The pain may go away if you switch to a different statin. Muscle damage with statins is rare, and your muscles may heal when you switch to a different medicine.</li></ul></content>
      <content name="mesh">Hydroxymethylglutaryl-CoA Reductase Inhibitors</content>
      <content name="groupName">Drug Therapy</content>
      <content name="snippet"> ... grapefruit (fresh or as juice) affects how your <span class="qt0">liver</span> breaks down some ...  rare, and your muscles may heal when you switch to a different medicine. </content>

    <document rank="12" url="https://medlineplus.gov/hemochromatosis.html">
      <content name="title">Hemochromatosis</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Iron Overload <span class="qt1">Disease</span></content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>Hemochromatosis is a <span class="qt1">disease</span> in which too much iron builds up in your body. Your body needs iron but too much of it is toxic. If you have hemochromatosis, you absorb more iron than you need. Your body has no natural way to get rid of the extra iron. It stores it in body tissues, especially the <span class="qt0">liver</span>, heart, and pancreas. The extra iron can damage your organs. Without treatment, it can cause your organs to fail.</p><p>There are two types of hemochromatosis. Primary hemochromatosis is an inherited <span class="qt1">disease</span>. Secondary hemochromatosis is usually the result of something else, such as anemia, thalassemia, <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">liver</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span>, or blood transfusions.</p><p>Many symptoms of hemochromatosis are similar to those of other <span class="qt1">diseases</span>. Not everyone has symptoms. If you do, you may have joint pain, fatigue, general weakness, weight loss, and stomach pain.</p><p>Your doctor will diagnose hemochromatosis based on your medical and family histories, a physical exam, and the results from tests and procedures. Treatments include removing blood (and iron) from your body, medicines, and changes in your diet.</p><p>NIH: National Heart, Lung, and Blood Institute</p></content>
      <content name="mesh">Hemochromatosis</content>
      <content name="groupName">Digestive System</content>
      <content name="groupName">Genetics/Birth Defects</content>
      <content name="groupName">Metabolic Problems</content>

    <document rank="13" url="https://medlineplus.gov/autoimmunediseases.html">
      <content name="title">Autoimmune <span class="qt1">Diseases</span></content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Autoinflammatory <span class="qt1">Diseases</span></content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What are autoimmune <span class="qt1">diseases</span>?<p>Your immune system protects you from <span class="qt1">disease</span> and infection by attacking germs that get into your body, such as viruses and bacteria. Your immune system can tell that the germs aren't part of you, so it destroys them. If you have an autoimmune <span class="qt1">disease</span>, your immune system attacks the healthy cells of your organs and tissues by mistake.</p><p>There are more than 80 types of autoimmune <span class="qt1">diseases</span>. They can affect almost any part of your body. For example, alopecia areata is an autoimmune <span class="qt1">disease</span> of the skin that causes hair loss. Autoimmune <span class="qt0">hepatitis</span> affects the <span class="qt0">liver</span>. In type 1 diabetes, the immune system attacks the pancreas.  And in rheumatoid arthritis, the immune system can attack many parts of the body, including the joints, lungs, and eyes.</p>What causes autoimmune <span class="qt1">diseases</span>?<p>No one is sure why autoimmune <span class="qt1">diseases</span> happen. But you can't catch them from other people.</p><p>Autoimmune <span class="qt1">diseases</span> do tend to run in families, which means that certain genes may make some people more likely to develop a problem. Viruses, certain chemicals, and other things in the environment may trigger an autoimmune <span class="qt1">disease</span> if you already have the genes for it.</p>Who is at risk for autoimmune <span class="qt1">diseases</span>?<p>Millions of Americans of all ages have autoimmune <span class="qt1">diseases</span>. Women develop many types of autoimmune <span class="qt1">diseases</span> much more often than men. And if you have one autoimmune <span class="qt1">disease</span>, you are more likely to get another.</p>What are the symptoms of autoimmune <span class="qt1">diseases</span>?<p>The symptoms of an autoimmune <span class="qt1">disease</span> depend on the part of your body that's affected. Many types of autoimmune <span class="qt1">diseases</span> cause redness, swelling, heat, and pain, which are the signs and symptoms of inflammation.  But other illnesses can cause the same symptoms.</p><p>The symptoms of autoimmune <span class="qt1">diseases</span> can come and go. During a flare-up, your symptoms may get severe for a while. Later on, you may have a remission, which means that your symptoms get better or disappear for a period of time.</p>How are autoimmune <span class="qt1">diseases</span> diagnosed?<p>Doctors often have a hard time diagnosing autoimmune <span class="qt1">diseases</span>. There's usually not a specific test to show whether you have a certain autoimmune <span class="qt1">disease</span>. And the symptoms can be confusing. That's because many autoimmune <span class="qt1">diseases</span> have similar symptoms. And some symptoms, such as muscle aches, are common in many other illnesses. So it can take a long time and some visits to different types of doctors to get a diagnosis.</p><p>To help your doctor find out if an autoimmune <span class="qt1">disease</span> is causing your symptoms,:</p><ul><li>Learn about the health conditions in your family history.  What health problems did your grandparents, aunts, uncles, and cousins have? Write down what you learn and share it with your doctor.</li><li>Keep track of your symptoms, including how long they last and what makes them better or worse. Share your notes with your doctor.</li><li>See a specialist who deals with the symptoms that bother you most. For example, if you have rash, see a dermatologist (skin doctor).</li></ul>What are the treatments for autoimmune <span class="qt1">diseases</span>?<p>The treatment depends on the <span class="qt1">disease</span>. In most cases, the goal of treatment is to suppress (slow down) your immune system, and ease swelling, redness, and pain from inflammation. Your doctor may give you corticosteroids or other medicines to help you feel better. For some <span class="qt1">diseases</span>, you may need treatment for the rest of your life.</p></content>

Autoimmune <span class="qt0">hepatitis</span> affects the <span class="qt0">liver</span>. In type 1 diabetes, the immune system attacks  ... </content>

    <document rank="14" url="https://medlineplus.gov/peripheralnervedisorders.html">
      <content name="title">Peripheral Nerve Disorders</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Neuropathy</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Neuritis</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Peripheral neuritis</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Peripheral neuropathy</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What are peripheral nerves?<p>Nerves are like wires that carry messages back and forth between your brain and your body. Your peripheral nerves branch off from your brain and spinal cord and connect to all parts of your body, including your muscles and organs. Peripheral nerves carry messages from your brain that control your movement, breathing, heartbeat, digestion, and more. They also carry messages from your body to your brain, so you can feel things, such as pain, heat, and cold.</p>What are peripheral nerve disorders?<p>Peripheral nerve disorders happen when one or more peripheral nerves are damaged. Damaged nerves may not carry messages correctly, or they may not work at all. As a result, you may have pain, trouble walking, or a variety of other problems, depending on which nerves are involved.</p><p>Peripheral nerve disorders are very common. There are more than 100 different types.</p>What causes peripheral nerve disorders?<p>Many things can damage nerves and lead to peripheral nerve disorders:</p><ul><li>Diabetes is the most common cause of peripheral nerve disorders. Most people with diabetes will develop diabetic nerve problems.</li><li>Physical injury (trauma) that stretches, crushes, squeezes, cuts, or puts pressure on one or more nerves. Some examples of peripheral nerve disorders from physical injury include  complex regional pain syndrome and brachial plexus injuries.</li><li>Health conditions, including:
	<ul><li>Certain cancers and their treatment (chemotherapy and radiation therapy).</li><li>Infections, such as HIV and Lyme <span class="qt1">disease</span>.</li><li>Problems with blood or blood vessels.</li><li>Autoimmune <span class="qt1">diseases</span>, such as rheumatoid arthritis and lupus.</li><li>Kidney or <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">liver</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span>.</li></ul></li><li>Certain medicines.</li><li>Contact with certain toxic substances, such as lead or mercury.</li><li>Alcohol use disorder (AUD) and smoking.</li><li>Vitamin imbalances, especially a lack of vitamin B12.</li><li>Your genes, including changes in your genes or conditions that you inherit from your parents, such as Charcot-Marie-Tooth <span class="qt1">disease</span>.</li></ul><p>In certain cases, the cause of peripheral nerve disorder is not known.</p>What are the symptoms of peripheral nerve disorders?<p>The symptoms of peripheral nerve disorders depend on which nerves are affected, what is causing the damage, and how serious it is:</p>Types of nervesPossible symptoms of nerve damageMotor nerves control your muscles and all your movement, such as walking, talking, and using your hands.<ul><li>Weak or aching muscles</li><li>Problems with balance, walking, or using your arms and hands</li><li>Cramps or twitching muscles</li><li>Muscle shrinking</li></ul>Sensory nerves carry messages to your brain from your senses, including touch, hot and cold, and pain.<ul><li>Tingling, numbness, or pain often in the hands and feet</li><li>Not being able to feel heat, cold, or pain, such as a cut on your foot</li><li>Pain from even light touch</li></ul>Autonomic nerves send messages to your organs to control breathing, digestion, and other body functions that happen without thinking about them.<ul><li>A heartbeat that's too fast or too slow</li><li>Trouble swallowing</li><li>Sweating too much or too little</li><li>Vomiting, diarrhea, or constipation</li><li>Problems with urination or sexual function</li></ul><p>Symptoms may range from mild to very strong. They may develop quickly over days or slowly over months and years. But they are rarely life-threatening.</p>How are peripheral nerve disorders diagnosed?<p>To find out if you have a peripheral nerve disorder, your provider will:</p><ul><li>Ask about your medical history</li><li>Ask about your family health history</li><li>Do a physical exam</li><li>Order tests, which may include:
	<ul><li>Blood tests</li><li>Genetic tests</li><li>Nerve tests that measure:
			<ul><li>Electrical activity in your nerves and muscles</li><li>How well your autonomic nerves are working</li></ul>
		</li><li>A biopsy of nerve or skin tissue</li><li>CT or MRI scan to see what may be pressing on your nerves</li></ul>
</li></ul>What are the treatments for peripheral nerve disorders?<p>It's important to treat any conditions that are causing nerve damage. In certain cases, that will allow your nerves to heal over time.</p><p>Treatment for symptoms depends on the type of peripheral nerve disorder you have, where it is, and how severe. Treatment options include:</p><ul><li>Braces or splints</li><li>Over-the-counter patches and skin creams</li><li>Prescription medicines</li><li>Non-drug pain management, such as electrical stimulation or relaxation therapy</li><li>Surgery to relieve pressure on a nerve</li></ul>Can peripheral nerve disorders be prevented?<p>You can help prevent peripheral nerve disorders by:</p><ul><li>Managing health conditions that may cause nerve damage, especially diabetes</li><li>Preventing falls and accidents</li><li>Avoiding toxic substances</li><li>Being careful to avoid repeated motions and body positions that press on your nerves</li><li>Eating a balanced diet, exercising, limiting alcohol, and not smoking</li></ul><p>NIH: National Institute of Neurological Disorders and Stroke</p></content>
      <content name="mesh">Peripheral Nerve Injuries</content>
      <content name="mesh">Peripheral Nervous System <span class="qt1">Diseases</span></content>

Kidney or <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">liver</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span>.   Certain medicines.  Contact with certain toxic substances,  ... </content>

    <document rank="15" url="https://medlineplus.gov/pulmonaryhypertension.html">
      <content name="title">Pulmonary Hypertension</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Cor Pulmonale</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Hypertension, Pulmonary</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Primary Pulmonary Hypertension</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Pulmonary Arterial Hypertension</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is pulmonary hypertension? <p>Pulmonary hypertension, sometimes called PH, is a serious condition that affects the blood vessels in the lungs. It develops when the blood pressure in your lungs is higher than normal.</p><p>Your heart pumps blood to your lungs so they can add oxygen to the blood. The blood goes back to your heart. From there, it travels to the rest of your body so that your tissues can get the oxygen that they need.</p><p>The blood moves from your heart to your lungs through blood vessels called pulmonary arteries. If the pulmonary arteries become damaged, narrowed, or blocked, the blood does not flow through them as well. This can increase the blood pressure in the arteries and cause pulmonary hypertension.</p><p>There are different types of pulmonary hypertension, including pulmonary arterial hypertension (PAH). The different types are based on what caused the <span class="qt1">disease</span>.</p>What causes pulmonary hypertension?<p>Pulmonary hypertension can develop on its own or be caused by another condition. Sometimes the cause is unknown or is not clear.</p><p>Some of the possible causes include:</p><ul><li>Heart <span class="qt1">diseases</span>, including left-sided heart failure and congenital heart <span class="qt1">disease</span></li><li>Lung <span class="qt1">diseases</span> such as <span class="qt1">COPD</span> (chronic obstructive pulmonary <span class="qt1">disease</span>), interstitial lung <span class="qt1">disease</span>, emphysema, and sleep apnea</li><li>Other medical conditions such as:
  <ul><li><span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">Liver</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">diseases</span></span></li><li>Sickle cell <span class="qt1">disease</span></li><li>Pulmonary embolism (blood clots in the lungs)</li><li>Connective tissue disorders like scleroderma</li></ul>
</li></ul>Who is more likely to develop pulmonary hypertension? <p>Certain factors can make you more likely to develop pulmonary hypertension, such as:</p><ul><li>Your age. The risk increases as you get older. The condition is usually diagnosed between ages 30 and 60.</li><li>Your environment. Being exposed to asbestos or having certain infections caused by parasites can raise your risk.</li><li>Your family history and genetics. Certain genetic disorders, such as Down syndrome, congenital heart <span class="qt1">disease</span>, and Gaucher <span class="qt1">disease</span>, can raise your risk of pulmonary hypertension. So can a family history of blood clots.</li><li>Your lifestyle habits.Smoking and illegal drug use can raise your risk of developing pulmonary hypertension.</li><li>Certain medicines. For example, some medicines used to treat cancer and depression can make you more likely to develop pulmonary hypertension.</li><li>Your sex. Pulmonary hypertension is more common in women than in men.</li></ul>What are the symptoms of pulmonary hypertension?<p>The symptoms of pulmonary hypertension are sometimes hard to recognize and are similar to the symptoms of other medical conditions. So sometimes it can take years for someone to get diagnosed with pulmonary hypertension.</p><p>The symptoms of pulmonary hypertension may include:</p><ul><li>Shortness of breath</li><li>Chest pain or pressure</li><li>Dizziness that may lead to fainting</li><li>Fatigue</li><li>Swelling of the abdomen, legs, or feet</li><li>Heart palpitations (the feeling that your heart is pounding or beating too fast)</li></ul>What other problems can pulmonary hypertension cause?<p>Pulmonary hypertension can get worse over time and lead to serious problems, including:</p><ul><li>Anemia, which can cause your body to not get enough oxygen-rich blood</li><li>Arrhythmias, problems with the rate or rhythm of your heartbeat</li><li>Blood clots in the pulmonary arteries</li><li>Bleeding in the lungs</li><li>Heart failure</li><li><span class="qt0">Liver</span> damage</li><li>Pericardial effusion, a collection of fluid around the heart</li><li>Serious pregnancy complications</li></ul>How is pulmonary hypertension diagnosed?<p>To find out if you have pulmonary hypertension, your health care provider:</p><ul><li>Will ask about your medical history and symptoms</li><li>Will do a physical exam, which may include checking your blood oxygen, listening to your heart and lungs, and checking your blood pressure</li><li>Will likely order some tests, such as:
  <ul><li>Blood tests to look for blood clots, stress on the heart, or anemia</li><li>Heart imaging tests, such as a cardiac MRI</li><li>Lung imaging tests, such as chest x-ray</li><li>An electrocardiogram (ECG or EKG)</li></ul>
</li></ul>What are the treatments for pulmonary hypertension?<p>Often there is no cure for pulmonary hypertension, but treatments can help manage your symptoms. Your provider will work with you to come up with a treatment plan. It will be based on your needs and the cause of the pulmonary hypertension. The plan may include:</p><ul><li>Healthy lifestyle changes, such as:
  <ul><li>Healthy eating, which includes eating less salt</li><li>Regular physical activity, which may be done through pulmonary rehabilitation</li></ul>
</li><li>Medicines, such as:
  <ul><li>Blood thinners</li><li>Medicines to control the rate blood is pumped throughout the body</li><li>Medicines to relax blood vessels and allow the blood to flow better</li><li>Medicine to reduce swelling (diuretics)</li></ul>
</li><li>Oxygen therapy</li><li>Procedures to reduce pressure in the heart or pulmonary artery</li><li>In some severe cases, a lung transplant</li></ul><p>NIH: National Heart, Lung, and Blood Institute</p></content>
      <content name="mesh">Hypertension, Pulmonary</content>
      <content name="groupName">Blood, Heart and Circulation</content>
      <content name="groupName">Lungs and Breathing</content>
      <content name="snippet"> ... pulmonary arteries  Bleeding in the lungs  Heart failure  <span class="qt0">Liver</span> damage  ...  cases, a lung transplant  NIH: National Heart, Lung, and Blood Institute</content>

    <document rank="16" url="https://medlineplus.gov/bileductcancer.html">
      <content name="title">Bile Duct Cancer</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Cholangiocarcinoma</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>Your <span class="qt0">liver</span> makes a digestive juice called bile. Your gallbladder stores it between meals. When you eat, your gallbladder pushes the bile into tubes called bile ducts. They carry the bile to your small intestine. The bile helps break down fat. It also helps the <span class="qt0">liver</span> get rid of toxins and wastes.</p><p>Bile duct cancer is rare. It can happen in the parts of the bile ducts that are outside or inside the <span class="qt0">liver</span>. Cancer of the bile duct outside of the <span class="qt0">liver</span> is much more common. Risk factors include having inflammation of the bile duct, ulcerative colitis, and some <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">liver</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">diseases</span></span>.</p><p>Symptoms can include:</p><ul><li>Jaundice</li><li>Itchy skin</li><li>Fever</li><li>Abdominal pain</li></ul><p>Tests to diagnose bile duct cancer may include a physical exam, imaging tests of the <span class="qt0">liver</span> and bile ducts, blood tests, and a biopsy.</p><p>Treatments include surgery, radiation therapy, and chemotherapy.</p><p>NIH: National Cancer Institute</p></content>

    <document rank="17" url="https://medlineplus.gov/naildiseases.html">
      <content name="title">Nail <span class="qt1">Diseases</span></content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Fingernails</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Ingrown Nail</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Onychomycosis</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Paronychia</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Toenails</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>Your toenails and fingernails protect the tissues of your toes and fingers. They are made up of layers of a hardened protein called keratin, which is also in your hair and skin. The health of your nails can be a clue to your overall health. Healthy nails are usually smooth and consistent in color. Specific types of nail discoloration and changes in growth rate can be signs of lung, heart, kidney, and <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">liver</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">diseases</span></span>, as well as diabetes and anemia. White spots and vertical ridges are harmless.</p><p>Nail problems that sometimes require treatment include:</p><ul><li>Bacterial and fungal infections</li><li>Ingrown nails</li><li>Tumors</li><li>Warts</li></ul><p>Keeping your nails clean, dry, and trimmed can help you avoid some problems. Do not remove the cuticle, which can cause infection.</p></content>
      <content name="mesh">Nail <span class="qt1">Diseases</span></content>
      <content name="groupName">Skin, Hair and Nails</content>
