Do not remove the cuticle, which can cause infection.</content>
    </document>
    <document rank="18" url="https://medlineplus.gov/vitaminddeficiency.html">
      <content name="title">Vitamin D Deficiency</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Osteomalacia </content>
      <content name="altTitle">Hypovitaminosis D</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Low Vitamin D</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is vitamin D deficiency?<p>Vitamin D deficiency means that your body is not getting enough vitamin D to stay healthy.</p>Why do I need vitamin D and how do I get it?<p>Vitamin D helps your body absorb calcium. Calcium is one of the main building blocks of bone. Vitamin D also has a role in your nervous, muscle, and immune systems.</p><p>You can get vitamin D in three ways: through your skin, from your diet, and from supplements. Your body forms vitamin D naturally after exposure to sunlight. But too much sun exposure can lead to skin aging and skin cancer, so many people try to get their vitamin D from other sources.</p>How much vitamin D do I need?<p>The amount of vitamin D you need each day depends on your age.  The recommended amounts, in international units (IU), are:</p><ul><li>Birth to 12 months: 400 IU</li><li>Children 1-13 years: 600 IU</li><li>Teens 14-18 years: 600 IU</li><li>Adults 19-70 years: 600 IU</li><li>Adults 71 years and older: 800 IU</li><li>Pregnant and breastfeeding women: 600 IU</li></ul><p>People at high risk of vitamin D deficiency may need more. Check with your health care provider about how much you need.</p>What causes vitamin D deficiency?<p>You can become deficient in vitamin D for different reasons:</p><ul><li>You don't get enough vitamin D in your diet</li><li>You don't absorb enough vitamin D from food (a malabsorption problem)</li><li>You don't get enough exposure to sunlight</li><li>Your <span class="qt0">liver</span> or kidneys cannot convert vitamin D to its active form in the body</li><li>You take medicines that interfere with your body's ability to convert or absorb vitamin D</li></ul>Who is at risk of vitamin D deficiency?<p>Some people are at higher risk of vitamin D deficiency:</p><ul><li>Breastfed infants, because human milk is a poor source of vitamin D. If you are breastfeeding, give your infant a supplement of 400 IU of vitamin D every day.</li><li>Older adults, because your skin doesn't make vitamin D when exposed to sunlight as efficiently as when you were young, and your kidneys are less able to convert vitamin D to its active form.</li><li>People with dark skin, which has less ability to produce vitamin D from the sun.</li><li>People with conditions that make it difficult to absorb nutrients from food, such as Crohn's <span class="qt1">disease</span>, ulcerative colitis, and celiac <span class="qt1">disease</span>.</li><li>People who have obesity, because their body fat binds to some vitamin D and prevents it from getting into the blood.</li><li>People who have had gastric bypass surgery, a type of weight loss surgery which creates a bypass of part of the small intestine. Since vitamin D is absorbed there, bypassing part of it makes it harder to absorb enough vitamin D. </li><li>People with chronic kidney or <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">liver</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span>, which can affect your ability to change vitamin D into a form your body can use.</li><li>People who take medicines that affect vitamin D levels, including certain cholesterol, anti-seizure, steroid, and weight-loss medicines.</li></ul><p>Talk with your provider if you are at risk for vitamin D deficiency.  There is a blood test that can measure how much vitamin D is in your body.</p>What problems does vitamin D deficiency cause?<p>Vitamin D deficiency can lead to a loss of bone density, which can contribute to osteoporosis and fractures (broken bones).</p><p>Severe vitamin D deficiency can also lead to other <span class="qt1">diseases</span>:</p><ul><li>In children, it can cause rickets. Rickets is a rare <span class="qt1">disease</span> that causes the bones to become soft and bend. African American infants and children are at higher risk of getting rickets.</li><li>In adults, severe vitamin D deficiency leads to osteomalacia. Osteomalacia causes weak bones, bone pain, and muscle weakness.</li></ul>How can I get more vitamin D?<p>There are a few foods that naturally have some vitamin D:</p><ul><li>Fatty fish such as salmon, tuna, and mackerel</li><li>Beef <span class="qt0">liver</span></li><li>Cheese</li><li>Mushrooms</li><li>Egg yolks</li></ul><p>You can also get vitamin D from fortified foods. You can check the food labels to find out whether a food has vitamin D. Foods that often have added vitamin D include:</p><ul><li>Milk</li><li>Breakfast cereals</li><li>Orange juice</li><li>Other dairy products, such as yogurt</li><li>Soy drinks</li></ul><p>Vitamin D is in many multivitamins. There are also vitamin D supplements, both in pills and in a liquid for babies.</p><p>If you have vitamin D deficiency, the treatment is with supplements. Check with your provider about how much you need to take, how often you need to take it, and how long you need to take it.</p>Can too much vitamin D be harmful?<p>Getting too much vitamin D (known as vitamin D toxicity) can be harmful. Signs of toxicity include nausea and vomiting, poor appetite, constipation, weakness, and weight loss.</p><p>Very high levels of vitamin D can damage the kidneys. It also raises the level of calcium in your blood. High levels of blood calcium (hypercalcemia) can cause confusion, kidney failure, and irregular heartbeat (arrhythmia).</p><p>Most cases of vitamin D toxicity happen when someone overuses vitamin D supplements. You cannot get too much vitamin D from sun exposure because the skin limits the amount of vitamin D it makes.</p></content>
      <content name="mesh">Vitamin D Deficiency</content>
      <content name="groupName">Food and Nutrition</content>

    <document rank="19" url="https://medlineplus.gov/braindiseases.html">
      <content name="title">Brain <span class="qt1">Diseases</span></content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>Your brain is the control center of your body. It controls your thoughts, memory, speech, and movement. It regulates the function of many organs. It's part of your nervous system, which also includes your spinal cord and peripheral nerves. The nervous system sends signals between your brain and the rest of the body. Your nerves take in information from your senses and send it to the brain to be processed. Your brain and nerves also communicate to help you move and to control your body's functions.</p><p>When the brain is healthy, it works quickly and automatically. But when you have a brain <span class="qt1">disease</span>, it may affect how  well you can function and do your daily activities. Some common brain <span class="qt1">diseases</span> include:</p><ul><li>Brain tumors, which can press on nerves and affect brain function.</li><li>Degenerative nerve <span class="qt1">diseases</span>, which can affect many of your body's activities, such as balance, movement, talking, breathing, and heart function. Types include Alzheimer's <span class="qt1">disease</span> and Parkinson's <span class="qt1">disease</span>.</li><li>Encephalitis (inflammation in the brain), which can lead to problems such as vision loss, weakness, and paralysis.</li><li>Genetic brain disorders, which are caused by changes in genes (also called variants or mutations). These disorders can affect the development and function of the brain.</li><li>Strokes, which can cause a loss of brain cells and can affect your ability to think clearly.</li><li>Traumatic brain injuries (TBIs), which can affect brain function. They may range from mild to severe. The effects of a TBI may be temporary or permanent.</li></ul><p>The symptoms of brain <span class="qt1">diseases</span> vary widely, depending on the specific problem. In some cases, damage is permanent. In other cases, treatments such as surgery, medicines, or therapies such as physical, occupational, and speech therapies, may cure the <span class="qt1">disease</span> or improve the symptoms.</p><p> 
</p></content>
      <content name="mesh">Brain <span class="qt1">Diseases</span></content>

    <document rank="20" url="https://medlineplus.gov/veteransandmilitaryhealth.html">
      <content name="title">Veterans and Military Health</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Agent Orange</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Gulf War Syndrome</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Military Health</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Persian Gulf War</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Veterans Health</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>Military service members and veterans have made sacrifices to our country, and they may face lots of different health issues. Some of the most common health problems they may have include chronic pain, sleep disorders, and mental health disorders.</p><p>But they may also face some different health risks than civilians. Serving to protect our country is a special job, and along with it comes some special risks and job hazards. These include different types of injuries and health problems from exposures.</p>What types of injuries do service members face?<p>During their service, members are at risk for various injuries. These injuries can happen during training or while in combat. Sometimes the injuries are life-threatening or serious enough to cause disability. Others may not be as serious, but they may be painful and can affect daily life.</p><p>Some of the specific types of injuries that service members may face include:</p><ul><li>Sprains and strains, especially in the ankles and knees. These injuries are often caused by exercise and running.</li><li>Back and shoulder injuries, often from lifting and carrying.</li><li>Tinnitus and hearing loss, typically from exposure to noise.</li><li>Head injuries and traumatic brain injuries (TBIs).</li><li>Shrapnel and gunshot wounds.</li><li>Lost limbs.</li></ul>What other special health problems do service members face?<p>There may also be a risk of health problems from exposure to environmental hazards, such as contaminated water, chemicals, infections, and burn pits. Sometimes the health problems caused by exposures don't develop until years later.</p><p>Some service members experience military sexual trauma (MST). This includes sexual assault and sexual harassment.</p>What mental health issues do service members and veterans face?<p>Being in combat and being separated from your family can be stressful. So can readjusting to civilian life after the military. All of this stress can put service members and veterans at risk for mental health problems, including:</p><ul><li>Depression</li><li>Post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD)</li><li>Anxiety</li><li>Alcohol and drug use disorders</li></ul><p>There is also a risk of suicide. Veterans who are in crisis can get help from the Veteran's Crisis line by:</p><ul><li>Calling 988 and then pressing 1</li><li>Texting to 838255</li><li>Chatting with them online</li></ul>What health care services are available to service members and veterans?<p>There are health care services especially for service members and veterans through:</p><ul><li>TRICARE, which is the Department of Defense's health care program. It has insurance plans and other services for uniformed service members, retirees, and their families around the world.</li><li>The Veterans Health Administration, which is part of the Department of Veterans Affairs (VA). It provides medical and social support services to eligible veterans.</li><li>The VA 's expanded health care and benefits for veterans exposed to burn pits, Agent Orange, and other toxic substances.</li></ul><p>To better understand and treat the health needs of veterans in the future, the VA created the Million Veteran Program (MVP). This research program is looking at how genes, lifestyle, military experiences, and exposures affect health and wellness in veterans.</p></content>

    <document rank="21" url="https://medlineplus.gov/vitamina.html">
      <content name="title">Vitamin A</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Retinol</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>Vitamins are substances that your body needs to grow and develop normally. Vitamin A plays a role in your:</p><ul><li>Vision</li><li>Bone growth</li><li>Reproduction</li><li>Cell functions</li><li>Immune system</li></ul><p>Vitamin A is an antioxidant. It can come from plant or animal sources.  Plant sources include colorful fruits and vegetables.  Animal sources include <span class="qt0">liver</span> and whole milk.  Vitamin A is also added to foods like cereals.</p><p>Vegetarians, young children, and alcoholics may need extra Vitamin A. You might also need more if you have certain conditions, such as <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">liver</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">diseases</span></span>, cystic fibrosis, and Crohn's <span class="qt1">disease</span>.  Check with your health care provider to see if you need to take vitamin A supplements.</p><p>NIH: National Institutes of Health Office of Dietary Supplements</p></content>

Check with your health care provider to see if you need to take vitamin A supplements. NIH: National Institutes of Health Office of Dietary Supplements</content>

    <document rank="22" url="https://medlineplus.gov/anabolicsteroids.html">
      <content name="title">Anabolic Steroids</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Doping</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Performance-Enhancing Drugs</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Steroids, Anabolic</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Anabolic-androgenic steroids</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What are anabolic steroids?<p>Anabolic steroids are synthetic (man-made) versions of testosterone. Testosterone is the main sex hormone in men. It is needed to develop and maintain male sex characteristics, such as facial hair, deep voice, and muscle growth. Women do have some testosterone in their bodies, but in much smaller amounts.</p>What are anabolic steroids used for?<p>Health care providers use anabolic steroids to treat some hormone problems in men, delayed puberty, and muscle loss from some <span class="qt1">diseases</span>. But some people misuse anabolic steroids by taking them in a way that's different than their provider prescribed.</p>Why do people misuse anabolic steroids?<p>Some bodybuilders and athletes use anabolic steroids to build muscles and improve athletic performance. They may take steroids by swallowing tablets or capsules, inject them into muscles, or apply them to the skin as a gel or cream. These doses may be 10 to 100 times higher than doses used to treat medical conditions. Using them this way, without a prescription from a provider, is not legal or safe.</p>What are the health effects of misusing anabolic steroids?<p>Misuse of anabolic steroids, especially over a long period of time, has been linked with many health problems, including:</p><ul><li>Acne</li><li>Stunted growth in teens</li><li>High blood pressure</li><li>Changes in cholesterol</li><li>Heart problems, including heart attack</li><li><span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">Liver</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span>, including cancer</li><li>Kidney damage</li><li>Mood changes such as irritability and aggressive behavior</li></ul><p>In men, it can also cause:</p><ul><li>Baldness</li><li>Breast growth</li><li>Low sperm count/infertility</li><li>Shrinking of the testicles</li><li>Testicular cancer</li></ul><p>In women, it can also cause:</p><ul><li>Changes in your menstrual cycle (period)</li><li>Growth of body and facial hair</li><li>Male-pattern baldness</li><li>Voice deepening</li><li>Decreased breast size</li></ul>Are anabolic steroids addictive?<p>Even though they don't cause a high, anabolic steroids can lead to addiction. You can have withdrawal symptoms if you stop using them, including:</p><ul><li>Fatigue</li><li>Restlessness</li><li>Loss of appetite</li><li>Sleep problems</li><li>Decreased sex drive</li><li>Steroid cravings</li><li>Depression, which can sometimes be serious and even lead to suicide attempts</li></ul><p>Behavioral therapy and medicines can be helpful in treating anabolic steroid addiction</p><p>NIH: National Institute on Drug Abuse</p></content>

    <document rank="23" url="https://medlineplus.gov/vitamine.html">
      <content name="title">Vitamin E</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Alpha-tocopherol</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>Vitamins are substances that your body needs to grow and develop normally. Vitamin E is an antioxidant. It plays a role in your immune system and metabolic processes.</p><p>Good sources of vitamin E include:</p><ul><li>Vegetable oils</li><li>Margarine</li><li>Nuts and seeds</li><li>Leafy greens</li></ul><p>Vitamin E is also added to foods like cereals. Most people get enough vitamin E from the foods they eat. People with certain disorders, such as <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">liver</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">diseases</span></span>, cystic fibrosis, and Crohn's <span class="qt1">disease</span> may need extra vitamin E. </p><p>Vitamin E supplements may be harmful for people who take blood thinners and other medicines. Check with your health care provider before taking the supplements.</p><p>NIH: National Institutes of Health Office of Dietary Supplements</p></content>

NIH: National Institutes of Health Office of Dietary Supplements</content>

    <document rank="24" url="https://medlineplus.gov/hormonereplacementtherapy.html">
      <content name="title">Hormone Replacement Therapy</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">ERT</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Estrogen Replacement Therapy</content>
      <content name="altTitle">HRT</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Menopausal Hormone Therapy</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>Menopause is the time in a woman's life when her period stops. It is a normal part of aging.  In the years before and during menopause, the levels of female hormones can go up and down. This can cause symptoms such as hot flashes, night sweats, pain during sex, and vaginal dryness. For some women, the symptoms are mild, and they go away on their own. Other women take hormone replacement therapy (HRT), also called menopausal hormone therapy, to relieve these symptoms. HRT may also protect against osteoporosis. </p><p>HRT is not for everyone. You should not use HRT if you:</p><ul><li>Think that you are pregnant</li><li>Have problems with vaginal bleeding</li><li>Have had certain kinds of cancers</li><li>Have had a stroke or heart attack</li><li>Have had blood clots</li><li>Have <span class="qt0"><span class="qt2">liver</span></span><span class="qt2"> </span><span class="qt1"><span class="qt2">disease</span></span></li></ul><p>There are different types of HRT. Some have only one hormone, while others have two. Most are pills that you take every day, but there are also skin patches, vaginal creams, gels, and rings.</p><p>Taking HRT has some risks. For some women, hormone therapy may increase their chances of getting blood clots, heart attacks, strokes, breast cancer, and gallbladder <span class="qt1">disease</span>. Certain types of HRT have a higher risk, and each woman's own risks can vary, depending upon her medical history and lifestyle. You and your health care provider need to discuss the risks and benefits for you. If you do decide to take HRT, it should be the lowest dose that helps and for the shortest time needed. You should check if you still need to take HRT every 3-6 months.</p><p>Food and Drug Administration</p></content>

    <document rank="4" url="https://medlineplus.gov/thyroiddiseases.html">
      <content name="title"><span class="qt0">Thyroid</span> Diseases</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Goiter</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>Your <span class="qt0">thyroid</span> is a small, butterfly-shaped gland in the front of your neck. It makes hormones that control the way the body uses energy. These hormones affect nearly every organ in your body and control many of your body's most important functions. For example, they affect your breathing, heart rate, weight, digestion, and moods.</p><p><span class="qt0">Thyroid</span> diseases cause your <span class="qt0">thyroid</span> to make either too much or too little of the hormones. Some of the different <span class="qt0">thyroid</span> diseases include:</p><ul><li>Goiter, an enlargement of the <span class="qt0">thyroid</span> gland</li><li>Hyperthyroidism, which happens when your <span class="qt0">thyroid</span> gland makes more <span class="qt0">thyroid</span> hormones than your body needs</li><li>Hypothyroidism, which happens when your <span class="qt0">thyroid</span> gland does not make enough <span class="qt0">thyroid</span> hormones</li><li><span class="qt0">Thyroid</span> cancer</li><li><span class="qt0">Thyroid</span> nodules, lumps in the <span class="qt0">thyroid</span> gland</li><li>Thyroiditis, swelling of the <span class="qt0">thyroid</span></li></ul><p>To diagnose <span class="qt0">thyroid</span> diseases, your health care provider may use a medical history, physical exam, and <span class="qt0">thyroid</span> tests. In some cases, your provider may also do a biopsy.</p><p>Treatment depends on the problem, how severe it is, and what your symptoms are. Possible treatments may include medicines, radioiodine therapy, or <span class="qt0">thyroid</span> surgery.</p><p>Dept. of Health and Human Services Office on Women's Health</p></content>

    <document rank="0" url="https://medlineplus.gov/thyroidtests.html">
      <content name="title"><span class="qt0">Thyroid</span> Tests</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle"><span class="qt0">Thyroid</span> Panel</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>Your <span class="qt0">thyroid</span> is a small, butterfly-shaped gland in the front of your neck. It makes hormones that control the way the body uses energy. These hormones affect nearly every organ in your body and control many of your body's most important functions. For example, they affect your breathing, heart rate, weight, digestion, and moods.</p><p><span class="qt0">Thyroid</span> tests check how well your <span class="qt0">thyroid</span> is working. They are also used to diagnose and help find the cause of <span class="qt0">thyroid</span> diseases such as hyperthyroidism and hypothyroidism. <span class="qt0">Thyroid</span> tests include blood tests and imaging tests.</p><p>Blood tests for your <span class="qt0">thyroid</span> include:</p><ul><li>TSH. It measures <span class="qt0">thyroid</span>-stimulating hormone. This is usually the first test your healthcare provider will order.</li><li>T3 and T4.They measure the level of the different <span class="qt0">thyroid</span> hormones in your blood.</li><li><span class="qt0">Thyroid</span> antibodies test.  It measures certain <span class="qt0">thyroid</span> antibodies (markers in the blood). This test may help diagnose autoimmune <span class="qt0">thyroid</span> disorders.</li></ul><p>Imaging tests include:</p><ul><li>CT scans</li><li>Ultrasound</li><li>Nuclear medicine tests, including: 
  <ul><li><span class="qt0">Thyroid</span> scan. It uses small amounts of radioactive material to create a picture of the <span class="qt0">thyroid</span>, showing its size, shape, and position. It can help find the cause of hyperthyroidism and check for <span class="qt0">thyroid</span> nodules (lumps in the <span class="qt0">thyroid</span>).</li><li>Radioactive iodine uptake test, or <span class="qt0">thyroid</span> uptake test. It checks how well your <span class="qt0">thyroid</span> is working and can help find the cause of hyperthyroidism.</li></ul>
</li></ul><p>NIH: National Institute of Diabetes and Digestive and Kidney Diseases</p></content>
      <content name="mesh"><span class="qt0">Thyroid</span> Function Tests</content>
      <content name="groupName">Endocrine System</content>
      <content name="groupName">Diagnostic Tests</content>
      <content name="snippet"> Your <span class="qt0">thyroid</span> is a small, butterfly-shaped gland in the front of your neck. It makes hormones that control the way the body uses energy. These hormones affect  ... </content>

    <document rank="1" url="https://medlineplus.gov/hyperthyroidism.html">
      <content name="title">Hyperthyroidism</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Basedow's Disease</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Graves' Disease</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Overactive <span class="qt0">Thyroid</span></content>
      <content name="altTitle">Thyrotoxicosis</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is hyperthyroidism?<p>Hyperthyroidism, or overactive <span class="qt0">thyroid</span>, happens when your <span class="qt0">thyroid</span> gland makes more <span class="qt0">thyroid</span> hormones than your body needs.</p><p>Your <span class="qt0">thyroid</span> is a small, butterfly-shaped gland in the front of your neck. It makes hormones that control the way the body uses energy. These hormones affect nearly every organ in your body and control many of your body's most important functions. For example, they affect your breathing, heart rate, weight, digestion, and moods. If not treated, hyperthyroidism can cause serious problems with your heart, bones, muscles, menstrual cycle, and fertility. But there are treatments that can help.</p>What causes hyperthyroidism?<p>Hyperthyroidism has several causes. They include:</p><ul><li>Graves' disease, an autoimmune disorder in which your immune system attacks your <span class="qt0">thyroid</span> and causes it to make too much hormone. This is the most common cause.</li><li><span class="qt0">Thyroid</span> nodules, which are growths on your <span class="qt0">thyroid</span>. They are usually benign (not cancer). But they may become overactive and make too much <span class="qt0">thyroid</span> hormone. <span class="qt0">Thyroid</span> nodules are more common in older adults.</li><li>Thyroiditis, inflammation of the <span class="qt0">thyroid</span>. It causes stored <span class="qt0">thyroid</span> hormone to leak out of your <span class="qt0">thyroid</span> gland.</li><li>Too much iodine. Iodine is found in some medicines, cough syrups, seaweed and seaweed-based supplements. Taking too much of them can cause your <span class="qt0">thyroid</span> to make too much <span class="qt0">thyroid</span> hormone.</li><li>Too much <span class="qt0">thyroid</span> medicine. This can happen if people who take <span class="qt0">thyroid</span> hormone medicine for hypothyroidism (underactive <span class="qt0">thyroid</span>) take too much of it.</li></ul>Who is at risk for hyperthyroidism?<p>You are at higher risk for hyperthyroidism if you:</p><ul><li>Are a woman</li><li>Are older than age 60</li><li>Have been pregnant or had a baby within the past 6 months</li><li>Have had <span class="qt0">thyroid</span> surgery or a <span class="qt0">thyroid</span> problem, such as goiter</li><li>Have a family history of <span class="qt0">thyroid</span> disease</li><li>Have pernicious anemia, in which the body cannot make enough healthy red blood cells because it does not have enough vitamin B12</li><li>Have type 1 diabetes or primary adrenal insufficiency, a hormonal disorder</li><li>Get too much iodine, from eating large amounts of foods containing iodine or using iodine-containing medicines or supplements</li></ul>What are the symptoms of hyperthyroidism?<p>The symptoms of hyperthyroidism can vary from person to person and may include:</p><ul><li>Nervousness or irritability</li><li>Fatigue</li><li>Muscle weakness</li><li>Trouble tolerating heat</li><li>Trouble sleeping</li><li>Tremor, usually in your hands</li><li>Rapid and irregular heartbeat</li><li>Frequent bowel movements or diarrhea</li><li>Weight loss</li><li>Mood swings</li><li>Goiter, an enlarged <span class="qt0">thyroid</span> that may cause your neck to look swollen. Sometimes it can cause trouble with breathing or swallowing.</li></ul><p>Adults over age 60 may have different symptoms than younger adults. For example, they may lose their appetite or withdraw from other people. Sometimes this can be mistaken for depression or dementia.</p>What other problems can hyperthyroidism cause?<p>If hyperthyroidism isn't treated, it can cause some serious health problems, including:</p><ul><li>An irregular heartbeat that can lead to blood clots, stroke, heart failure, and other heart problems</li><li>An eye disease called Graves' ophthalmopathy. It can cause double vision, light sensitivity, and eye pain. In rare cases, it can lead to vision loss.</li><li>Thinning bones and osteoporosis</li><li>Fertility problems in women</li><li>Complications in pregnancy, such as premature birth, low birth weight, high blood pressure in pregnancy, and miscarriage</li></ul>How is hyperthyroidism diagnosed?<p>Your health care provider may use many tools to make a diagnosis:</p><ul><li>A medical history, including asking about symptoms</li><li>A physical exam</li><li><span class="qt0">Thyroid</span> tests, such as
		<ul><li>TSH, T3, T4, and <span class="qt0">thyroid</span> antibody  blood tests</li><li>Imaging tests, such as a <span class="qt0">thyroid</span> scan, ultrasound, or radioactive iodine uptake test.  A radioactive iodine uptake test measures how much radioactive iodine your <span class="qt0">thyroid</span> takes up from your blood after you swallow a small amount of it.</li></ul></li></ul>What are the treatments for hyperthyroidism?<p>The treatments for hyperthyroidism include medicines, radioiodine therapy, and <span class="qt0">thyroid</span> surgery:</p><ul><li>Medicines for hyperthyroidism include
		<ul><li>Antithyroid medicines, which cause your <span class="qt0">thyroid</span> to make less <span class="qt0">thyroid</span> hormone. You probably need to take the medicines for 1 to 2 years. In some cases, you might need to take the medicines for several years. This is the simplest treatment, but it is often not a permanent cure.</li><li>Beta blocker medicines, which can reduce symptoms such as tremors, rapid heartbeat, and nervousness. They work quickly and can help you feel better until other treatments take effect.</li></ul>
</li><li>Radioiodine therapy is a common and effective treatment for hyperthyroidism. It involves taking radioactive iodine by mouth as a capsule or liquid. This slowly destroys the cells of the <span class="qt0">thyroid</span> gland that produce <span class="qt0">thyroid</span> hormone. It does not affect other body tissues. Almost everyone who has radioactive iodine treatment later develops hypothyroidism. This is because the <span class="qt0">thyroid</span> hormone-producing cells have been destroyed. But hypothyroidism is easier to treat and causes fewer long-term health problems than hyperthyroidism.</li><li>Surgery to remove part or most of the <span class="qt0">thyroid</span> gland is done in rare cases. It might be an option for people with large goiters or pregnant women who cannot take antithyroid medicines. If you have all of your <span class="qt0">thyroid</span> removed, you will need to take <span class="qt0">thyroid</span> medicines for the rest of your life. Some people who have part of their <span class="qt0">thyroid</span> removed also need to take medicines.</li></ul><p>If you have hyperthyroidism, it's important not to get too much iodine. Talk to your health care provider about which foods, supplements, and medicines you need to avoid.</p><p>NIH: National Institute of Diabetes and Digestive and Kidney Diseases</p></content>

      <content name="snippet">What is hyperthyroidism? Hyperthyroidism, or overactive <span class="qt0">thyroid</span>, happens when your <span class="qt0">thyroid</span> gland makes more <span class="qt0">thyroid</span> hormones than your body needs. Your  ... </content>

    <document rank="2" url="https://medlineplus.gov/hypothyroidism.html">
      <content name="title">Hypothyroidism</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Autoimmune Thyroiditis</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Hashimoto's Disease</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Underactive <span class="qt0">Thyroid</span></content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is hypothyroidism?<p>Hypothyroidism, or underactive <span class="qt0">thyroid</span>, happens when your <span class="qt0">thyroid</span> gland doesn't make enough <span class="qt0">thyroid</span> hormones to meet your body's needs.</p><p>Your <span class="qt0">thyroid</span> is a small, butterfly-shaped gland in the front of your neck. It makes hormones that control the way the body uses energy. These hormones affect nearly every organ in your body and control many of your body's most important functions. For example, they affect your breathing, heart rate, weight, digestion, and moods. Without enough <span class="qt0">thyroid</span> hormones, many of your body's functions slow down. But there are treatments that can help.</p>What causes hypothyroidism?<p>Hypothyroidism has several causes. They include:</p><ul><li>Hashimoto's disease, an autoimmune disorder where your immune system attacks your <span class="qt0">thyroid</span>. This is the most common cause.</li><li>Thyroiditis, inflammation of the <span class="qt0">thyroid</span></li><li>Congenital hypothyroidism, hypothyroidism that is present at birth</li><li>Surgical removal of part or all of the <span class="qt0">thyroid</span></li><li>Radiation treatment of the <span class="qt0">thyroid</span></li><li>Certain medicines</li><li>In rare cases, a pituitary disease or too much or too little iodine in your diet</li></ul>Who is at risk for hypothyroidism?<p>You are at higher risk for hypothyroidism if you:</p><ul><li>Are a woman</li><li>Are older than age 60</li><li>Have had a <span class="qt0">thyroid</span> problem before, such as a goiter</li><li>Have had surgery to correct a <span class="qt0">thyroid</span> problem</li><li>Have received radiation treatment to the <span class="qt0">thyroid</span>, neck, or chest</li><li>Have a family history of <span class="qt0">thyroid</span> disease</li><li>Were pregnant or had a baby in the past 6 months</li><li>Have Turner syndrome, a genetic disorder that affects females</li><li>Have pernicious anemia, in which the body cannot make enough healthy red blood cells because it does not have enough vitamin B12</li><li>Have Sjogren's syndrome, a disease that causes dry eyes and mouth</li><li>Have type 1 diabetes</li><li>Have rheumatoid arthritis, an autoimmune disease that affects the joints</li><li>Have lupus, a chronic autoimmune disease</li></ul>What are the symptoms of hypothyroidism?<p>The symptoms of hypothyroidism can vary from person to person and may include:</p><ul><li>Fatigue</li><li>Weight gain</li><li>A puffy face</li><li>Trouble tolerating cold</li><li>Joint and muscle pain</li><li>Constipation</li><li>Dry skin</li><li>Dry, thinning hair</li><li>Decreased sweating</li><li>Heavy or irregular menstrual periods</li><li>Fertility problems in women</li><li>Depression</li><li>Slowed heart rate</li><li>Goiter, an enlarged <span class="qt0">thyroid</span> that may cause your neck to look swollen. Sometimes it can cause trouble with breathing or swallowing.</li></ul><p>Because hypothyroidism develops slowly, many people don't notice symptoms of the disease for months or even years.</p>What other problems can hypothyroidism cause?<p>Hypothyroidism can contribute to high cholesterol. In rare cases, untreated hypothyroidism can cause myxedema coma. This is a condition in which your body's functions slow down to the point that it becomes life-threatening.</p><p>During pregnancy, hypothyroidism can cause complications, such as premature birth, high blood pressure in pregnancy, and miscarriage. It can also slow the baby's growth and development.</p>How is hypothyroidism diagnosed?<p>Your health care provider may use many tools to make a diagnosis:</p><ul><li>A medical history, including asking about your symptoms</li><li>A physical exam</li><li><span class="qt0">Thyroid</span> tests, such as
		<ul><li>TSH, T3, T4, and <span class="qt0">thyroid</span> antibody  blood tests</li><li>Imaging tests, such as a <span class="qt0">thyroid</span> scan, ultrasound, or radioactive iodine uptake test. A radioactive iodine uptake test measures how much radioactive iodine your <span class="qt0">thyroid</span> takes up from your blood after you swallow a small amount of it.</li></ul></li></ul>What are the treatments for hypothyroidism?<p>The treatment for hypothyroidism is medicine to replace the hormone that your own <span class="qt0">thyroid</span> can no longer make. About 6 to 8 weeks after you start taking the medicine, you will get a blood test to check your <span class="qt0">thyroid</span> hormone level. Your health care provider will adjust your dose if needed. Each time your dose is adjusted, you'll have another blood test. Once you find the right dose, you will probably get a blood test in 6 months. After that, you will need the test once a year.</p><p>If you take your medicine according to the instructions, you usually should be able to control the hypothyroidism. You should never stop taking your medicine without talking with your health care provider first.</p><p>If you have Hashimoto's disease or other types of autoimmune <span class="qt0">thyroid</span> disorders, you may be sensitive to harmful side effects from iodine. Talk to your health care provider about which foods, supplements, and medicines you need to avoid.</p><p>Women need more iodine when they are pregnant because the baby gets iodine from the mother's diet. If you are pregnant, talk with your health care provider about how much iodine you need.</p><p>NIH: National Institute of Diabetes and Digestive and Kidney Diseases</p></content>

    <document rank="3" url="https://medlineplus.gov/thyroidcancer.html">
      <content name="title"><span class="qt0">Thyroid</span> Cancer</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is <span class="qt0">thyroid</span> cancer?<p><span class="qt0">Thyroid</span> cancer is a cancer that starts in the tissues of the <span class="qt0">thyroid</span>. Your <span class="qt0">thyroid</span> is a small, butterfly-shaped gland in the front of your neck. It makes hormones that control the way the body uses energy. These hormones affect nearly every organ in your body and control many of your body's most important functions. For example, they affect your breathing, heart rate, weight, digestion, and moods.</p>What are the different types of <span class="qt0">thyroid</span> cancer?<p>There are different types of <span class="qt0">thyroid</span> cancer; the main ones include:</p><ul><li>Differentiated <span class="qt0">thyroid</span> cancer, which includes papillary cancer and follicular cancer</li><li>Medullary <span class="qt0">thyroid</span> cancer</li><li>Anaplastic <span class="qt0">thyroid</span> cancer</li></ul>Who is more likely to develop <span class="qt0">thyroid</span> cancer?<p>Certain people are more likely to develop <span class="qt0">thyroid</span> cancer. Your risk is higher if you:</p><ul><li>Are between ages 25 and 65</li><li>Are a woman</li><li>Are Asian</li><li>Were exposed to certain types of radiation, including from radiation treatments to your head or neck as a child or from a radiation emergency</li><li>Have had a goiter (enlarged <span class="qt0">thyroid</span>)</li><li>Having certain genetic conditions, including certain types of multiple endocrine neoplasia</li><li>Have a family history of <span class="qt0">thyroid</span> cancer or <span class="qt0">thyroid</span> disease</li></ul>What are the symptoms of <span class="qt0">thyroid</span> cancer?<p><span class="qt0">Thyroid</span> cancer may not cause symptoms at first. It is sometimes found during a routine physical exam. You may get signs or symptoms as the cancer gets bigger. The symptoms may include:</p><ul><li>A lump (nodule) in the neck</li><li>Trouble breathing</li><li>Trouble swallowing</li><li>Pain when swallowing</li><li>Hoarseness or other changes to your voice that do not get better</li></ul>How is <span class="qt0">thyroid</span> cancer diagnosed?<p>To find out if you have <span class="qt0">thyroid</span> cancer, your health care provider may use:</p><ul><li>A physical exam, including checking your neck for swelling, lumps, or anything that seems unusual</li><li>A medical history</li><li><span class="qt0">Thyroid</span> tests</li><li>Other blood or imaging tests</li><li>A biopsy</li></ul>What are the treatments for <span class="qt0">thyroid</span> cancer?<p>Treatment for <span class="qt0">thyroid</span> cancer depends on the type of cancer you have and whether the cancer has spread. Often, more than one type of treatment may be needed. The treatment options include:</p><ul><li>Surgery.</li><li>Radiation therapy, including radioactive iodine therapy.</li><li>Chemotherapy.</li><li><span class="qt0">Thyroid</span> hormone therapy.</li><li>Targeted therapy, which uses drugs or other substances that attack specific cancer cells with less harm to normal cells.</li><li>Watchful waiting, which means that you don't get treatment right away. Your regularly checks to see if your signs or symptoms appear or change.</li></ul><p>NIH: National Cancer Institute</p></content>

Your <span class="qt0">thyroid</span> is a small, butterfly-shaped gland in the front  ... </content>

    <document rank="5" url="https://medlineplus.gov/bodyweight.html">
      <content name="title">Body Weight</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">BMI</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Overweight</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Underweight</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is body weight?<p>Body weight is the total mass of your body. Keeping track of your weight is one way to check for changes in your health, but it can't tell you the whole story. That's because your weight includes muscle, bone, fat, and water. Also, everyday factors can play a role in your weight, such as your:</p><ul><li>Diet</li><li>Activity level</li><li>Stress level</li><li>Sleep habits</li><li>Family history and genetics</li><li>Age</li></ul>What factors help determine a healthy body weight?<p>A healthy weight is more than just a number on the scale. It's about feeling good, having energy, and lowering your risk of disease. Your health care provider can help you determine a healthy body weight based on several factors. These include your:</p><ul><li>Body Mass Index (BMI). BMI is a measure of how much you weigh compared to how tall you are. The higher your BMI, the higher your risk for certain diseases. BMI does not distinguish between fat, muscle, and bone mass. It also may overestimate body fat in athletes or those with a muscular build, or underestimate body fat in older persons or others who have lost muscle.</li><li>Waist circumference. If most of your body fat is around your abdomen (belly) rather than your hips, you may be at a higher risk for heart disease and type 2 diabetes.</li><li>Health history. Your health care provider will likely consider your risk factors for diseases and health conditions, such as having high cholesterol, in determining your healthy body weight.</li></ul>What are body weight ranges?<p>Having a healthy body weight may lower your chance of developing certain health problems. Carrying too much or too little weight can affect your health. Ranges of body weight include:</p><ul><li>Underweight. Your weight is lower than it should be for your health.</li><li>Normal. Your weight is within a healthy range.</li><li>Overweight. Your weight is greater than it should be for your health.</li><li>Obese. Your body fat is greater than it should be for your health.</li></ul><p>Body weight needs can be different at different ages. For children, weight and growth are tracked over time based on their age.  For older adults, keeping muscle and strength is key, and sudden weight loss should always be checked by your provider.</p>What if I have a sudden gain or loss of weight?<p>Talk to your provider if you're losing or gaining weight without trying. A sudden, unexpected change in weight can be a sign of a medical problem.</p><p>Causes for sudden weight loss can include:</p><ul><li><span class="qt0">Thyroid</span> problems</li><li>Cancer</li><li>Infectious diseases</li><li>Digestive diseases</li><li>Certain medicines</li></ul><p>Causes for sudden weight gain can include:</p><ul><li>Certain medicines</li><li><span class="qt0">Thyroid</span> problems</li><li>Heart failure</li><li>Kidney disease</li><li>Liver disease</li></ul>How can I maintain a healthy body weight?<p>Maintaining a healthy body weight can improve energy, sleep, and overall wellbeing. Talk to your provider about what a healthy weight would be for you. If they recommend that you gain or lose weight, they might refer you to a dietician for support. A dietician is a professional who has special training to help you learn the best way to eat.</p><ul><li>Good nutrition and exercise can help in losing weight. Even a small change in weight can lower your risk of developing certain diseases.</li><li>Eating extra calories within a well-balanced diet and treating any underlying medical problems can help you gain weight.</li></ul><p>Focus on your health, not just the number on the scale. With daily healthy habits and self-compassion, you can support lasting improvements in your overall wellbeing.</p><p>NIH: National Heart, Lung, and Blood Institute</p></content>

    <document rank="6" url="https://medlineplus.gov/primaryovarianinsufficiency.html">
      <content name="title">Primary Ovarian Insufficiency</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Ovarian Insufficiency</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Premature Ovarian Failure</content>
      <content name="altTitle">POF</content>
      <content name="altTitle">POI</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is primary ovarian insufficiency (POI)?<p>Primary ovarian insufficiency (POI) happens when your ovaries stop working normally before you are 40.</p><p>As you get older, fertility naturally decreases. This usually starts around age 40 or later.  Menstrual periods may also become irregular as you transition to menopause. If you have POI, these changes happen earlier, before age 40. Sometimes POI can start as early as the teenage years.</p><p>POI is different from premature menopause. With premature menopause, your periods stop before age 40. You can no longer get pregnant. The cause can be natural or it can be a disease, surgery, chemotherapy, or radiation. With POI, some women still have occasional periods. They may even get pregnant.  In most cases of POI, the cause is unknown.</p>What causes primary ovarian insufficiency (POI)?<p>In about 90% of cases, the exact cause of POI is unknown.</p><p>Research shows that POI is related to problems with the follicles. Follicles are small sacs in your ovaries where eggs grow and mature. One type of follicle problem is that you run out of working follicles earlier than normal. Another is that the follicles are not working properly. In most cases, the cause of the follicle problem is unknown. But sometimes the cause may be:</p><ul><li>Genetic disorders such as Fragile X syndrome and Turner syndrome</li><li>A low number of follicles</li><li>Autoimmune diseases, including thyroiditis and Addison disease</li><li>Chemotherapy or radiation therapy</li><li>Metabolic disorders</li><li>Exposure to toxic substances, such as cigarette smoke, chemicals, and pesticides</li></ul>Who is at risk for primary ovarian insufficiency (POI)?<p>Certain factors can raise your risk of POI:</p><ul><li>Family history. If you have a mother or sister with POI, you are more likely to have it.</li><li>Genes. Some changes to genes and genetic conditions put you at higher risk for POI. For example, women with Fragile X syndrome or Turner syndrome are at higher risk.</li><li>Certain diseases, such as autoimmune diseases and some viral infections.</li><li>Cancer treatments, such as chemotherapy and radiation therapy.</li><li>Age.You can get POI when you're young, but it becomes more common between the ages of 35 and 40.</li></ul>What are the symptoms of primary ovarian insufficiency (POI)?<p>The first sign of POI is usually irregular or missed periods. Later symptoms may be like those of natural menopause, including:</p><ul><li>Hot flashes</li><li>Night sweats</li><li>Irritability</li><li>Poor concentration</li><li>Decreased sex drive</li><li>Pain during sex</li><li>Vaginal dryness</li></ul><p>You may see a health care provider because of difficulty getting pregnant or trouble with infertility.</p>What other problems can primary ovarian insufficiency (POI) cause?<p>Since POI causes you to have lower levels of certain hormones, you are at greater risk for other health conditions, including:</p><ul><li>Anxiety and depression. Hormonal changes caused by POI can contribute to anxiety or lead to depression.</li><li>Dry eye syndrome and eye surface disease. Some women with POI have one of these eye conditions. Both can cause discomfort and may lead to blurred vision. If not treated, these conditions can cause permanent eye damage.</li><li>Heart disease. Lower levels of estrogen can affect the muscles lining the arteries and can increase the buildup of cholesterol. These factors increase your risk of atherosclerosis (hardening of the arteries).</li><li>Infertility.</li><li>Low <span class="qt0">thyroid</span> function. This problem also is called hypothyroidism. The <span class="qt0">thyroid</span> is a gland that makes hormones that control your body's metabolism and energy level. Low levels of <span class="qt0">thyroid</span> hormones can affect your metabolism and can cause very low energy, mental sluggishness, and other symptoms.</li><li>Osteoporosis. The hormone estrogen helps keep bones strong. Without enough estrogen, women with POI often develop osteoporosis. It is a bone disease that causes weak, brittle bones that are more likely to break.</li></ul>How is primary ovarian insufficiency (POI) diagnosed?<p>To diagnose POI, your provider may do:</p><ul><li>A medical history, including asking whether you have relatives with POI</li><li>A pregnancy test, to make sure that you are not pregnant</li><li>A physical exam, to look for signs of other disorders that could be causing your symptoms</li><li>Blood tests, to check for certain hormones. You may also have a blood test to do a chromosome analysis. A chromosome is the part of a cell that contains genetic information.</li><li>A pelvic ultrasound, to see whether the ovaries are enlarged or have multiple follicles.</li></ul>What are the treatments for primary ovarian insufficiency (POI)?<p>Currently, there is no proven treatment to restore normal function to the ovaries. But there are treatments for some of the symptoms of POI. There are also ways to lower your health risks and treat the conditions that POI can cause:</p><ul><li>Hormone therapy for menopause (sometimes called hormone replacement therapy or HRT). This is the most common treatment. It gives your body the estrogen and other hormones that your ovaries are not making. Hormone therapy for menopause improves sexual health and decreases the risks for heart disease and osteoporosis.</li><li>Calcium and vitamin D supplements. Because women with POI are at higher risk for osteoporosis, you should take calcium and vitamin D every day.</li><li>In vitro fertilization (IVF). If you have POI and you wish to become pregnant, you may consider trying IVF.</li><li>Regular physical activity and a healthy body weight.Getting regular exercise and controlling your weight can lower your risk for osteoporosis and heart disease.</li><li>Treatments for associated conditions. If you have a condition that is related to POI, it is important to treat that as well. Treatments may involve medicines and hormones.</li></ul><p>NIH: National Institute of Child Health and Human Development</p></content>

    <document rank="7" url="https://medlineplus.gov/benigntumors.html">
      <content name="title">Benign Tumors</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Adenoma</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Hemangioma</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Tumors, Benign</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Benign neoplasms</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Noncancerous tumors</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>Tumors are abnormal growths in your body. They can be either benign or malignant. Benign tumors aren't cancer. Malignant ones are. Benign tumors grow only in one place. They cannot spread or invade other parts of your body. Even so, they can be dangerous if they press on vital organs, such as your brain.</p><p>Tumors are made up of extra cells. Normally, cells grow and divide to form new cells as your body needs them. When cells grow old, they die, and new cells take their place. Sometimes, this process goes wrong. New cells form when your body does not need them, and old cells do not die when they should. These extra cells can divide without stopping and may form a tumor.</p><p>Treatment often involves surgery. Benign tumors usually don't grow back.</p><p>NIH: National Cancer Institute</p></content>

    <document rank="8" url="https://medlineplus.gov/cancerinchildren.html">
      <content name="title">Cancer in Children</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Childhood Cancer</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>Cancer is a group of related diseases. In all types of cancer, some of the body's cells begin to divide without stopping and spread into surrounding tissues.</p><p>Normally, new cells form as you need them, replacing old cells that die. Sometimes, this process goes wrong. New cells form when you don't need them, and old cells don't die when they should. The extra cells can form a tumor. Tumors can be benign or malignant. Benign tumors aren't cancer. Malignant tumors are cancer, and the cancer cells can spread to nearby tissues. They can also break away and spread to other parts of the body.</p><p>Children can get cancer in the same parts of the body as adults, but there are differences. Childhood cancers may happen suddenly, without early symptoms. Some types can often be cured. The most common children's cancer is leukemia. Some of the other more common types of cancer in children include brain tumors, lymphoma, and soft tissue sarcoma.</p><p>Symptoms and treatment depend on the type of cancer and how advanced it is. Treatments may include:</p><ul><li>Surgery</li><li>Radiation therapy</li><li>Chemotherapy</li><li>Stem cell transplants</li><li>Targeted therapy, which uses drugs or other substances that attack specific cancer cells with less harm to normal cells
</li></ul><p>NIH: National Cancer Institute</p></content>
      <content name="mesh">Neoplasms</content>

      <content name="groupName">Children and Teenagers</content>
      <content name="snippet"> Cancer is a group of related diseases. In all types of cancer, some of the body's cells begin to divide without stopping and spread into surrounding tissues.  ... </content>

    <document rank="9" url="https://medlineplus.gov/healthproblemsinpregnancy.html">
      <content name="title">Health Problems in Pregnancy</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">High Risk Pregnancy</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Pregnancy Complications</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Pregnancy and Health Problems</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Pregnancy, High-Risk</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What are health problems in pregnancy?<p>A health problem in pregnancy is any disease or condition that could affect your health or the health of your fetus. Some health problems may make it more likely that you will have a high-risk pregnancy. A high-risk pregnancy is one in which you, your fetus, or both are at higher risk for health problems than in a typical pregnancy.</p><p>But just because you have health problems, it doesn't mean that you, or your fetus, will have a problem during the pregnancy. Taking care of yourself and getting early and regular prenatal care from a health care provider may help you reduce pregnancy risks from health problems.</p>What raises my risk for health problems during pregnancy?<p>Every pregnancy has some risk of problems, but your lifestyle, as well as factors like certain conditions and health issues, can raise that risk, such as:</p><ul><li>A health condition you had before you got pregnant</li><li>A health condition you develop during pregnancy</li><li>A pregnancy with more than one baby</li><li>A health problem that happened during a previous pregnancy and could happen again</li><li>Substance use during pregnancy</li><li>Being over age 35</li></ul><p>Any of these can affect your health, the health of your fetus, or both.</p>Can chronic health conditions cause problems in pregnancy?<p>Every pregnancy is different. If you have specific risks in one pregnancy, it doesn't mean that you will have them in another. But, if you have a chronic condition, you should talk to your provider about how to minimize your risk before you get pregnant. Once you are pregnant, you may need a health care team to monitor your pregnancy. Some common conditions that can complicate a pregnancy include:</p><ul><li>High blood pressure</li><li>Polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS)</li><li>Kidney problems</li><li>Autoimmune disorders</li><li><span class="qt0">Thyroid</span> disease</li><li>Obesity</li><li>HIV</li><li>Cancer</li><li>Infections</li></ul><p>Other conditions that may make pregnancy risky can develop during pregnancy - for example, gestational diabetes and Rh incompatibility.</p>Can health problems in pregnancy be prevented?<p>You may be able to lower your risk of certain health problems by making healthy lifestyle changes before you get pregnant. These can include reaching a healthy weight, not smoking, and managing any health conditions.</p><p>However, health problems during pregnancy are not always preventable. Some chronic conditions can be treated and controlled, while others aren't treatable and carry a higher risk than usual, even if the health problem is well-managed.</p><p>Share your symptoms with your provider for early detection and treatment of health problems. Sometimes, it's hard to know what's normal. Some symptoms, like nausea, back pain, and fatigue, are common during pregnancy, while other symptoms, like vaginal bleeding or a severe or long-lasting headache, can be signs of a problem. Call your provider to let them know if something is bothering or worrying you.</p></content>

    <document rank="10" url="https://medlineplus.gov/hormones.html">
      <content name="title">Hormones</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>Hormones are your body's chemical messengers. They travel in your bloodstream to tissues or organs. They work slowly, over time, and affect many different processes, including:</p><ul><li>Growth and development</li><li>Metabolism - how your body gets energy from the foods you eat</li><li> Sexual function</li><li> Reproduction</li><li> Mood</li></ul><p>Endocrine glands, which are special groups of cells, make hormones. The major endocrine glands are the pituitary, pineal, thymus, <span class="qt0">thyroid</span>, adrenal glands, and pancreas. In addition, men produce hormones in their testes and women produce them in their ovaries. </p><p>Hormones are powerful. It takes only a tiny amount to cause big changes in cells or even your whole body. That is why too much or too little of a certain hormone can be serious. Laboratory tests can measure the hormone levels in your blood, urine, or saliva.  Your health care provider may perform these tests if you have symptoms of a hormone disorder. Home pregnancy tests are similar - they test for pregnancy hormones in your urine.</p></content>

    <document rank="11" url="https://medlineplus.gov/nuclearscans.html">
      <content name="title">Nuclear Scans</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">PET Scans</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Radionuclide Scans</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Radioisotope scans</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>Nuclear scans use radioactive substances to see structures and functions inside your body. They use a special camera that detects radioactivity.</p><p>Before the test, you receive a small amount of radioactive material. You may get it as an injection. Sometimes you swallow it or inhale it.   Then you lie still on a table while the camera makes images. Most scans take 20 to 45 minutes.</p><p>Nuclear scans can help doctors diagnose many conditions, including cancers, injuries, and infections. They can also show how organs like your heart and lungs are working.</p></content>
      <content name="mesh">Radionuclide Imaging</content>
      <content name="groupName">Diagnostic Tests</content>

    <document rank="12" url="https://medlineplus.gov/parathyroiddisorders.html">
      <content name="title">Parathyroid Disorders</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Hyperparathyroidism</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Hypoparathyroidism</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Multiple Endocrine Neoplasia</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>Most people have four pea-sized glands, called parathyroid glands, on the <span class="qt0">thyroid</span> gland in the neck. Though their names are similar, the <span class="qt0">thyroid</span> and parathyroid glands are completely different. The parathyroid glands make parathyroid hormone (PTH), which helps your body keep the right balance of calcium and phosphorous.</p><p>If your parathyroid glands make too much or too little hormone, it disrupts this balance. If they secrete extra PTH, you have hyperparathyroidism, and your blood calcium rises. In many cases, a benign tumor on a parathyroid gland makes it overactive. Or, the extra hormones can come from enlarged parathyroid glands. Very rarely, the cause is cancer.</p><p>If you do not have enough PTH, you have hypoparathyroidism. Your blood will have too little calcium and too much phosphorous. Causes include injury to the glands, endocrine disorders, or genetic conditions. Treatment is aimed at restoring the balance of calcium and phosphorous.</p><p>NIH: National Institute of Diabetes and Digestive and Kidney Diseases</p></content>

      <content name="groupName">Endocrine System</content>

    <document rank="13" url="https://medlineplus.gov/postpartumdepression.html">
      <content name="title">Postpartum Depression</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Baby Blues</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Post-pregnancy depression</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is postpartum depression?<p>Having a baby can be exciting, but it can also be stressful and bring many strong emotions. During the postpartum period, which is the time after you give birth, you may have mood swings, crying spells, or feel anxious. These feelings are common and are often called the postpartum "baby blues". The baby blues usually go away within a few days to a week.</p><p>If your symptoms are more severe and last longer than two weeks, you may have postpartum depression.</p><p>Postpartum depression is a common but serious mood disorder that can begin anytime within the first year after childbirth. Without treatment, symptoms may make it hard to care for your baby or manage daily tasks.</p>What causes postpartum depression?<p>The exact cause is unknown, but postpartum depression likely results from a combination of factors, including:</p><ul><li>Sudden changes in hormone levels after pregnancy</li><li>Physical changes, such as dropping <span class="qt0">thyroid</span> hormone levels</li><li>Lifestyle factors, such as fatigue, lack of sleep, limited support, or recent stressful events</li></ul>Who is at risk for postpartum depression?<p>Anyone can develop postpartum depression after childbirth. You might be at higher risk if you:</p><ul><li>Have a history of depression, bipolar disorder, or a family health history of these conditions</li><li>Had a multiple birth (twins, triplets, or more)</li><li>Gave birth while in your teens</li><li>Had health problems during pregnancy</li><li>Had preterm labor or other complications during childbirth</li><li>Have a baby with special needs</li><li>Have ever experienced domestic violence</li><li>Are dealing with financial stress</li><li>Had an unplanned pregnancy</li></ul>What are the symptoms of postpartum depression?<p>Postpartum depression symptoms are more intense and last longer than the baby blues.</p><p>Symptoms may include:</p><ul><li>Feeling sad or empty most of the time</li><li>Eating too much or too little</li><li>Sleeping too much or too little</li><li>Crying more than usual or for no clear reason</li><li>Feeling unusually angry</li><li>Pulling away from family and friends</li><li>Feeling worried or anxious often</li><li>Feeling little or no interest in your baby</li><li>Constant doubts about your ability to care for your baby</li><li>Thinking of harming yourself or your baby</li></ul><p>If you have thoughts about suicide, or hurting yourself or your baby get help right away:</p><ul><li>Call 911 or go to your local emergency room</li><li>Contact a crisis hotline. In the United States, you can reach the National Suicide and Crisis Lifeline at any time:
	<ul><li>Call or text 988</li><li>Chat online with Lifeline Chat</li><li>TTY users: Use your preferred relay service or dial 711 then 988</li></ul>
</li><li>Veterans can contact the Veterans Crisis Line:
	<ul><li>Call 988, then press 1</li><li>Text 838255</li><li>Chat online</li></ul>
</li><li>Call your mental health provider or other health care provider</li><li>Reach out to a loved one or close friend</li></ul><p>If these symptoms begin during pregnancy and continue after childbirth, it's called perinatal depression. Without treatment, it can affect bonding with your baby and may contribute to feeding or sleeping problems.</p><p>Very rarely, a new mother may develop postpartum psychosis, a medical emergency. Symptoms may include confusion, hallucinations (seeing or hearing things that aren't real), or dangerous behaviors. Call 911 or go to the nearest emergency room right away if this happens.</p>How is postpartum depression diagnosed?<p>Your provider may diagnose postpartum depression using:</p><ul><li>Screening questions or questionnaires, such as those about your mood, sleep, and thoughts.</li><li>A clinical evaluation based on your symptoms.</li><li>Blood tests, if needed, to check for physical conditions such as <span class="qt0">thyroid</span> disorders, that can cause or worsen depression.</li></ul>What is the treatment for postpartum depression?<p>If you think you have postpartum depression, talk with your provider. Treatments may include:</p><ul><li>Medicines, including antidepressants</li><li>Talk therapy (counseling)</li></ul><p>Tell your provider if you are breastfeeding so they can choose the safest treatment options.</p><p>Along with these treatment options, there are things you can do at home that may help you feel better, such as:</p><ul><li>Asking for help with caring for the baby and household chores</li><li>Taking time for yourself</li><li>Getting physical activity, such as walking</li><li>Resting when the baby rests</li></ul>Can postpartum depression be prevented?<p>You may be able to lower your risk for postpartum depression by:</p><ul><li>Talking with your provider during pregnancy about any history of depression</li><li>Completing recommended screening questionnaires during pregnancy and after birth</li><li>Getting support from family, friends, or support groups</li><li>Attending early Physical changes so symptoms can be found and treated as soon as possible</li></ul><p>Dept. of Health and Human Services Office on Women's Health</p></content>
      <content name="mesh">Depression, Postpartum</content>

    <document rank="14" url="https://medlineplus.gov/anxiety.html">
      <content name="title">Anxiety</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Generalized Anxiety Disorder</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is anxiety?<p>Anxiety is a feeling of fear, dread, and uneasiness. It might cause you to sweat, feel restless and tense, and have a rapid heartbeat.   It can be a normal reaction to stress. For example, you might feel anxious when faced with a difficult problem at work, before taking a test, or before making an important decision. It can help you to cope. The anxiety may give you a boost of energy or help you focus. But for people with anxiety disorders, the fear is not temporary and can be overwhelming.</p>What are anxiety disorders?<p>Anxiety disorders are conditions in which you have anxiety that does not go away and can get worse over time. The symptoms can interfere with daily activities such as job performance, schoolwork, and relationships.</p>What are the types of anxiety disorders?<p>There are several types of anxiety disorders, including: </p><ul><li>Generalized anxiety disorder (GAD). People with GAD worry about ordinary issues such as health, money, work, and family. But their worries are excessive, and they have them almost every day for at least 6 months.</li><li>Panic disorder. People with panic disorder have panic attacks. These are sudden, repeated periods of intense fear when there is no danger. The attacks come on quickly and can last several minutes or more.</li><li>Phobias. People with phobias have an intense fear of something that poses little or no actual danger. Their fear may be about spiders, flying, going to crowded places, or being in social situations (known as social anxiety).</li></ul>What causes anxiety disorders?<p>The cause of anxiety is unknown. Factors such as genetics, brain biology and chemistry, stress, and your environment may play a role.</p>Who is at risk for anxiety disorders?<p>The risk factors for the different types of anxiety disorders can vary. For example, GAD and phobias are more common in women, but social anxiety affects men and women equally. There are some general risk factors for all types of anxiety disorders, including:</p><ul><li>Certain personality traits, such as being shy or withdrawn when you are in new situations or meeting new people</li><li>Traumatic events in early childhood or adulthood</li><li>Family history of anxiety or other mental disorders</li><li>Some physical health conditions, such as <span class="qt0">thyroid</span> problems or arrhythmia</li></ul>What are the symptoms of anxiety disorders?<p>The different types of anxiety disorders can have different symptoms. But they all have a combination of:</p><ul><li>Anxious thoughts or beliefs that are hard to control. They make you feel restless and tense and interfere with your daily life. They do not go away and can get worse over time.</li><li>Physical symptoms, such as a pounding or rapid heartbeat, unexplained aches and pains, dizziness, and shortness of breath</li><li>Changes in behavior, such as avoiding everyday activities you used to do</li></ul><p>Using caffeine, other substances, and certain medicines can make your symptoms worse.</p>How are anxiety disorders diagnosed?<p>To diagnose anxiety disorders, your health care provider will ask about your symptoms and medical history. You may also have a physical exam and lab tests to make sure that a different health problem is not the cause of your symptoms.</p><p>If you don't have another health problem, you will get a psychological evaluation. Your provider may do it, or you may be referred to a mental health professional to get one.</p>What are the treatments for anxiety disorders?<p>The main treatments for anxiety disorders are psychotherapy (talk therapy), medicines, or both:</p><ul><li>Psychotherapy can help you recognize and change troubling emotions, thoughts, and behaviors. To be effective, it should focus on your specific anxieties and needs. There are many types; some of the types used for anxiety disorders include:
  <ul><li>Cognitive behavioral therapy (CBT), which teaches you different ways of thinking and behaving. It can help you change how you react to the things that cause you to feel fear and anxiety. It may include exposure therapy. This therapy focuses on having you confront your fears so that you will be able to do the things that you had been avoiding.</li><li>Acceptance and commitment therapy may help with some anxiety disorders. It uses strategies such as mindfulness and goal setting to reduce discomfort and anxiety.</li></ul> 
</li><li>Medicines to treat anxiety disorders include anti-anxiety medicines and certain antidepressants. Some types of medicines may work better for specific types of anxiety disorders. You should work closely with your provider to identify which medicine is best for you. You may need to try more than one medicine before you can find the right one.</li></ul><p>Support groups and stress management techniques may also be helpful.</p><p>NIH: National Institute of Mental Health</p></content>
