    <document rank="15" url="https://medlineplus.gov/arrhythmia.html">
      <content name="title">Arrhythmia</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Bradycardia</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Irregular Heartbeat</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Multifocal Atrial Tachycardia</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Tachycardia</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is an arrhythmia?<p>An arrhythmia is a problem with the rate or rhythm of your heartbeat. Your heart beats too quickly, too slowly, or with an irregular pattern. Changes in the electrical signals that control your heartbeat cause arrhythmias.</p><ul><li>When your heart beats faster than normal, it's called tachycardia.</li><li>When your heart beats too slowly, it's called bradycardia.</li><li>When the signal to beat comes too early, it's called a premature or extra heartbeat. It may feel like your heart skipped a beat.</li></ul><p>There are many types of arrhythmias. Arrhythmias may affect the upper or lower chamber of your heart. The most common type of arrhythmia is atrial fibrillation, which causes an irregular and fast heartbeat.</p><p>Some arrhythmias are harmless, such as when your heart rate speeds up during exercise and slows down when you sleep. But if you have a frequent irregular rhythm, it may mean your heart isn't pumping enough blood into your body. Getting treatment and following a heart-healthy lifestyle can help control arrhythmias. It may also help prevent heart damage that can trigger some heart arrhythmias.</p>What causes an arrhythmia?<p>Many factors can affect your heart's rhythm, such as having had a heart attack, smoking, congenital heart defects, and stress. Other factors that could increase your risk for some types of arrhythmias could include if you:</p><ul><li>Have a family history of arrhythmias.</li><li>Have certain health conditions, which can include heart and blood vessel diseases, lung diseases, kidney diseases, obesity, and sleep apnea.</li><li>Are an older adult.</li><li>Have had recent surgery for your heart, lungs, or throat.</li><li>Take certain medicines for other health conditions.</li><li>Use illegal drugs.</li></ul><p>If you have risk factors, some situations that make your heart work harder, raise your blood pressure, or cause strong emotional stress may trigger an arrhythmia.</p>What are the symptoms of an arrhythmia?<p>You may not have any noticeable symptoms of an arrhythmia. Your provider may find an arrhythmia during your routine checkup. Symptoms of an arrhythmia can include:</p><ul><li>Fast or slow heartbeat</li><li>Skipping, fluttering, or pounding heartbeats</li><li>Chest pain or discomfort</li><li>Dizziness or fainting</li><li>Shortness of breath</li><li>Sweating</li><li>Tiredness or weakness</li></ul><p>Seek emergency medical care if you have chest pain, shortness of breath, or think you are having a heart attack.</p>How is an arrhythmia diagnosed?<p>To find out if you have an arrhythmia, your health care provider may order an electrocardiogram (EKG).  This test records your heart's electrical activity and is the most common for finding an arrhythmia. Your provider may also:</p><ul><li>Ask about your medical history, including your symptoms and lifestyle habits.</li><li>Do a physical exam, which includes checking your heartbeat and pulse.</li><li>Order blood tests and other heart tests.</li><li>Check your legs or feet for swelling.</li><li>Look for signs of other health conditions that could cause an arrythmia, such as <span class="qt0">thyroid</span> disease.</li></ul>What are the treatments for an arrhythmia?<p>Treatment may include medicines, an implantable cardioverter-defibrillator (ICD) or pacemaker, or sometimes surgery. Your provider may also recommend avoiding activities that may trigger your arrhythmia.</p><p>The goal of treatment is to restore a normal heart rhythm. If not treated, arrhythmias can damage your heart, brain, and other organs and could be life-threatening.</p>Can arrhythmias be prevented?<p>To help prevent an arrhythmia, your provider may suggest that you make heart-healthy lifestyle changes and treat health conditions that may cause arrhythmias.</p><p>NIH: National Heart, Lung, and Blood Institute</p></content>
      <content name="mesh">Arrhythmias, Cardiac</content>
      <content name="groupName">Blood, Heart and Circulation</content>
      <content name="snippet"> ... conditions that could cause an arrythmia, such as <span class="qt0">thyroid</span> disease. What are the treatments for an arrhythmia?  ... </content>
    </document>
    <document rank="16" url="https://medlineplus.gov/autoimmunediseases.html">
      <content name="title">Autoimmune Diseases</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Autoinflammatory Diseases</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What are autoimmune diseases?<p>Your immune system protects you from disease and infection by attacking germs that get into your body, such as viruses and bacteria. Your immune system can tell that the germs aren't part of you, so it destroys them. If you have an autoimmune disease, your immune system attacks the healthy cells of your organs and tissues by mistake.</p><p>There are more than 80 types of autoimmune diseases. They can affect almost any part of your body. For example, alopecia areata is an autoimmune disease of the skin that causes hair loss. Autoimmune hepatitis affects the liver. In type 1 diabetes, the immune system attacks the pancreas.  And in rheumatoid arthritis, the immune system can attack many parts of the body, including the joints, lungs, and eyes.</p>What causes autoimmune diseases?<p>No one is sure why autoimmune diseases happen. But you can't catch them from other people.</p><p>Autoimmune diseases do tend to run in families, which means that certain genes may make some people more likely to develop a problem. Viruses, certain chemicals, and other things in the environment may trigger an autoimmune disease if you already have the genes for it.</p>Who is at risk for autoimmune diseases?<p>Millions of Americans of all ages have autoimmune diseases. Women develop many types of autoimmune diseases much more often than men. And if you have one autoimmune disease, you are more likely to get another.</p>What are the symptoms of autoimmune diseases?<p>The symptoms of an autoimmune disease depend on the part of your body that's affected. Many types of autoimmune diseases cause redness, swelling, heat, and pain, which are the signs and symptoms of inflammation.  But other illnesses can cause the same symptoms.</p><p>The symptoms of autoimmune diseases can come and go. During a flare-up, your symptoms may get severe for a while. Later on, you may have a remission, which means that your symptoms get better or disappear for a period of time.</p>How are autoimmune diseases diagnosed?<p>Doctors often have a hard time diagnosing autoimmune diseases. There's usually not a specific test to show whether you have a certain autoimmune disease. And the symptoms can be confusing. That's because many autoimmune diseases have similar symptoms. And some symptoms, such as muscle aches, are common in many other illnesses. So it can take a long time and some visits to different types of doctors to get a diagnosis.</p><p>To help your doctor find out if an autoimmune disease is causing your symptoms,:</p><ul><li>Learn about the health conditions in your family history.  What health problems did your grandparents, aunts, uncles, and cousins have? Write down what you learn and share it with your doctor.</li><li>Keep track of your symptoms, including how long they last and what makes them better or worse. Share your notes with your doctor.</li><li>See a specialist who deals with the symptoms that bother you most. For example, if you have rash, see a dermatologist (skin doctor).</li></ul>What are the treatments for autoimmune diseases?<p>The treatment depends on the disease. In most cases, the goal of treatment is to suppress (slow down) your immune system, and ease swelling, redness, and pain from inflammation. Your doctor may give you corticosteroids or other medicines to help you feel better. For some diseases, you may need treatment for the rest of your life.</p></content>
      <content name="mesh">Autoimmune Diseases</content>
      <content name="groupName">Immune System</content>
      <content name="snippet">What are autoimmune diseases? Your immune system protects you from disease and infection by attacking germs that get into your body, such as viruses and  ... </content>

    <document rank="17" url="https://medlineplus.gov/biopsy.html">
      <content name="title">Biopsy</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>A biopsy is a procedure that removes cells or tissue from your body.  A doctor called a pathologist looks at the cells or tissue under a microscope to check for damage or disease. The pathologist may also do other tests on it.</p><p>Biopsies can be done on all parts of the body. In most cases, a biopsy is the only test that can tell for sure if a suspicious area is cancer.  But biopsies are performed for many other reasons too.</p><p>There are different types of biopsies. A needle biopsy removes tissue with a needle passed through your skin to the site of the problem. Other kinds of biopsies may require surgery.</p></content>

    <document rank="18" url="https://medlineplus.gov/carpaltunnelsyndrome.html">
      <content name="title">Carpal Tunnel Syndrome</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Median nerve entrapment</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is carpal tunnel syndrome?<p>Carpal tunnel syndrome is the name for a group of problems that includes numbness, tingling, weakness, or pain in your wrist or hand. It's a very common condition that happens when a nerve in your wrist becomes squeezed.</p><p>The carpal tunnel is a narrow opening between the bones in your wrist and the ligament that holds them together.  A nerve, called the median nerve, runs from your lower arm through the carpal tunnel and into your hand. This nerve provides feeling to your thumb and first three fingers.  It also helps you move your thumb.</p><p>Tendons also run from your lower arm, through the carpal tunnel, and into your hand. The tendons connect your finger bones to muscles in your arm, so you can bend your fingers.</p><p>If the tendons become swollen or irritated inside the carpal tunnel, they may press on the nerve. The squeezed nerve may not work well, which can lead to numbness, weakness, and other symptoms of carpal tunnel syndrome.</p>What causes carpal tunnel syndrome?<p>In certain cases, the cause of carpal tunnel syndrome is unknown. But it usually happens from a combination of things that affect the structures in your wrist, such as:</p><ul><li>Injury to your wrist that causes swelling, such as a sprain or broken bone</li><li>Health conditions that can affect your nerves, joints, or other parts of your wrist, for example:
	<ul><li>Diabetes.</li><li>Rheumatoid arthritis.</li><li>A cyst or tumor in your wrist.</li><li>Changes in  certain hormones, which may cause swelling from extra fluid in your body. Examples include an underactive <span class="qt0">thyroid</span> gland (hypothyroidism), pregnancy and menopause.</li><li>Problems with the way your wrist is formed.</li></ul>
</li><li>Doing the same wrist and hand movements over and over, such as: 
	<ul><li>Assembly line work.</li><li>Carpentry or using tools that vibrate.</li><li>Knitting, gardening, or sports, such as golf.</li></ul>
</li></ul><p>Researchers aren't sure whether long-term typing or computer use leads to carpal tunnel syndrome.</p>Who is more likely to develop carpal tunnel syndrome?<p>Carpal tunnel syndrome usually happens only in adults. You're more likely to develop it if you:</p><ul><li>Are a woman. Women are three times more likely to have carpal tunnel syndrome than men.</li><li>Have a health condition that may affect structures in your wrist.</li><li>Do activities with repeated hand motions.</li><li>Have had a wrist injury.</li><li>Have a close relative who has had carpal tunnel syndrome, such as a parent, brother or sister.</li></ul>What are the symptoms of carpal tunnel syndrome?<p>You may feel numbness, tingling or burning in your fingers, especially in your thumb, index, and middle fingers. Symptoms often affect both hands.</p><p>In the early stages, symptoms usually:</p><ul><li>Start slowly in your dominant hand (the one you write with)</li><li>Happen at night if you sleep with your wrists bent</li></ul><p>As symptoms get worse, you may notice:</p><ul><li>Tingling, pain, or weakness with certain activities, such as driving or holding a phone</li><li>Difficulty grasping or holding small things</li><li>Feeling like your fingers are swollen when they're not</li></ul><p>Without treatment, you could lose feeling in some fingers and have permanent weakness in your thumb. But early diagnosis and treatment can help prevent lasting damage.</p><p> 


</p>How is carpal tunnel syndrome diagnosed?<p>To find out if you have carpal tunnel syndrome, your health care provider will:</p><ul><li>Ask about your medical history</li><li>Ask about your family health history</li><li>Do a physical exam</li><li>Order tests if needed, such as:
	<ul><li>Lab tests to look for diseases that may damage nerves</li><li>X-rays to look for broken bones or arthritis</li><li>Tests to measure the electrical activity in your nerves and muscles</li><li>Ultrasound to check the nerve in the carpal tunnel</li></ul></li></ul>What are the treatments for carpal tunnel syndrome?<p>It's easier to treat carpal tunnel syndrome early on, so you should start treatment as soon as possible. Your provider will first treat any health conditions you have that may cause your symptoms. The next step would be treatments for carpal tunnel syndrome. They may include:</p><ul><li>Wrist splints (usually worn at night).</li><li>Rest.</li><li>Medicines to reduce swelling and pain:</li><ul><li>Over-the-counter pain relievers, such as aspirin, ibuprofen, and naproxen sodium.</li><li>Prescriptionsteroids, either pills or an injection (shot) into your wrist.</li></ul><li>Other therapies (complementary and integrative medicine). Research shows that yoga may help with pain and weakness. Some people may find acupuncture and chiropractic care helpful, but research hasn't shown that these therapies can improve carpal tunnel syndrome. If you want to try other therapies, talk with your provider first.</li><li>Surgery to make more space in the carpal tunnel. Surgery may be an option if your symptoms are severe and other treatments don't work. It involves cutting the wrist ligament to take pressure off the nerve.  Recovery may take months. Carpal tunnel syndrome rarely comes back after surgery, but mild symptoms may last.</li></ul>Can carpal tunnel syndrome be prevented?<p>You may help prevent carpal tunnel syndrome if you protect your wrists:</p><ul><li>Use good posture to keep your wrists in a natural position.</li><li>Keep your hands warm to keep your muscles flexible. Fingerless gloves may help.</li><li>Take work breaks and vary your tasks.</li><li>Stretch and exercise your hands and wrists.</li><li>Organize your workspace and tools to reduce strain on your hands and wrists.</li></ul></content>
      <content name="mesh">Carpal Tunnel Syndrome</content>

    <document rank="19" url="https://medlineplus.gov/celiacdisease.html">
      <content name="title">Celiac Disease</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Gluten Intolerance</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Gluten-Sensitive Enteropathy</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Nontropical Sprue</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Celiac sprue</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is celiac disease?<p>Celiac disease is a chronic (long-term) digestive and immune disorder that damages your small intestine. The damage may prevent your body from absorbing vitamins, minerals, and other nutrients from the food you eat. This can lead to malnutrition and other serious health problems</p><p>Celiac disease is triggered by eating foods that contain gluten. Gluten is a protein found in wheat, barley, rye, and other grains. It may also be in other products like vitamins and supplements, hair and skin products, toothpastes, and lip balm.</p><p>Celiac disease is different from gluten sensitivity. Both involve problems with gluten and can cause some of the same symptoms, such as abdominal (belly) pain and fatigue. But gluten sensitivity does not damage the small intestine like celiac disease does.</p><p> Wheat allergy, a type of food allergy, is also different. With both celiac disease and wheat allergy, your immune system is reacting to wheat. But with wheat allergy, you can have some different symptoms, such as itchy eyes or trouble breathing. And a wheat allergy will not cause long-term damage to the small intestine.</p>What causes celiac disease?<p>The exact cause of celiac disease is not known. Research suggests that celiac disease only happens in people who have certain genes and eat food that contains gluten. Researchers are studying other factors that may play a role in causing the disease.</p>Who is more likely to develop celiac disease?<p>Celiac disease is more common if you:</p><ul><li>Have a family member who has the disease</li><li>Have Down syndrome, Turner syndrome, or Williams syndrome</li><li>Are White</li><li>Are female</li></ul>What are the symptoms of celiac disease?<p>The symptoms of celiac disease can be different from person to person. Sometimes the symptoms may come and go. Some people may not notice any symptoms.</p><p>Some of the possible symptoms affect your digestive system. Digestive symptoms are more common in children than in adults. The digestive symptoms include:</p><ul><li>Bloating (feeling fullness or swelling in your belly)</li><li>Chronic (long-term) diarrhea or greasy, bulky, unusually bad-smelling stool (poop)</li><li>Constipation</li><li>Gas</li><li>Lactose intolerance because of damage to the small intestine</li><li>Nausea and vomiting</li><li>Pain in the abdomen (belly)</li><li>Weight loss in adults, or not enough weight gain in children</li></ul><p>Some people with celiac disease have symptoms that affect other parts of the body, such as:</p><ul><li>Fatigue</li><li>Depression and anxiety</li><li>Irritability (in children)</li><li>Dermatitis herpetiformis, an itchy rash with blisters (mainly in adults)</li><li>Bone or joint pain</li><li>Symptoms involving the mouth, such as canker sores or dry mouth</li></ul>What other problems can celiac disease cause?<p>Over time, celiac disease can cause other health problems, especially if it is not treated. These problems can include:</p><ul><li>Malnutrition</li><li>Anemia, especially iron-deficiency anemia</li><li>Bone loss</li><li>Nervous system problems such as headaches, balance problems, or peripheral neuropathy</li><li>Reproductive problems, such as missed menstrual periods and miscarriages in women and infertility in men and women</li></ul>How is celiac disease diagnosed?<p>If you have symptoms of celiac disease, your health care provider will look for signs that you might have celiac disease. To do this, your provider will get your medical and family history and do a physical exam.</p><p>If your provider thinks that you could have celiac disease, you will have some tests. Providers most often use blood tests and biopsies of the small intestine to diagnose celiac disease. The biopsy would be done during an upper gastrointestinal (GI) endoscopy. For this procedure, your provider uses an endoscope (a flexible tube with a camera) to see the lining of your esophagus, stomach, and small intestine. It also allows your provider to take a sample of tissue for a biopsy.</p>What are the treatments for celiac disease?<p>The treatment for celiac disease is following a gluten-free diet for the rest of your life. Sticking with a gluten-free diet will treat or prevent many of the symptoms and other health problems caused by celiac disease. In most cases, it can also heal damage in the small intestine and prevent more damage. </p><p>Your provider may refer you to a registered dietician (a nutrition expert) who can help you learn how to eat a healthy diet without gluten. You will also need to avoid all hidden sources of gluten, such as certain supplements, cosmetics, toothpaste, etc. Reading product labels can sometimes help you avoid gluten. If a label doesn't tell you what is in a product, check with the company that makes the product for an ingredients list. Don't just assume that a product is gluten-free if it doesn't mention it.</p><p>NIH: National Institute of Diabetes and Digestive and Kidney Diseases</p></content>
      <content name="mesh">Celiac Disease</content>
      <content name="groupName">Digestive System</content>

    <document rank="20" url="https://medlineplus.gov/cerebralpalsy.html">
      <content name="title">Cerebral Palsy</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">CP</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is cerebral palsy (CP)?<p>Cerebral palsy (CP) is a group of neurologic disorders that cause problems with movement, balance, and posture. The first part of the name, cerebral, means having to do with the brain. The second part, palsy, means weakness or problems with using the muscles.</p><p>CP can range from mild to severe. The brain damage and the disabilities it causes are permanent. But treatments can improve the lives of people who have the condition. For example, treatments can help to improve their motor skills and ability to communicate.</p>What are the types of cerebral palsy (CP)?<p>There are different types of CP:</p><ul><li>Spastic cerebral palsy, which is the most common type. It causes increased muscle tone, stiff muscles, and awkward movements. Sometimes it only affects one part of the body. In other cases, it can affect both arms and legs, the trunk, and the face.</li><li>Dyskinetic cerebral palsy, which causes problems controlling the movement of the hands, arms, feet, and legs. This can make it hard to sit and walk.</li><li>Ataxic cerebral palsy, which causes problems with balance and coordination.</li><li>Mixed cerebral palsy, which means that you have symptoms of more than one type.</li></ul>What causes cerebral palsy (CP)?<p>CP is caused by abnormal development or damage to the developing brain. When this development or damage happens before birth, it is called congenital CP. Most CP is congenital, and its causes may include:</p><ul><li>Gene changes (variants)</li><li>Brain malformations</li><li>Infections or fevers in the pregnant parent</li><li>An injury to the developing baby</li></ul><p>CP can also happen during or after birth. CP that happens more than 28 days after birth is called acquired CP. Its causes can include:</p><ul><li>Brain damage in the first few months or years of life</li><li>Infections, such as meningitis or encephalitis</li><li>Problems with blood flow to the brain, for example from a stroke or abnormal blood vessels</li><li>Head injuries, for example from a car accident, a fall, or child abuse</li></ul><p>In some cases, the cause of congenital or acquired CP is unknown.</p>Who is more likely to develop cerebral palsy (CP)?<p>Certain medical conditions or events that can happen during pregnancy and delivery may increase a baby's risk of congenital cerebral palsy. These may include:</p><ul><li>Being born too small</li><li>Being born too early</li><li>Being born a twin or other multiple birth</li><li>Being conceived by in vitro fertilization (IVF) or other assisted reproductive technology (ART)</li><li>Infections</li><li>Health problems in the pregnant parent, such as <span class="qt0">thyroid</span> problems and seizures</li><li>Severe newborn jaundice</li><li>Having complications during birth</li><li>Rh incompatibility</li><li>Exposure to toxic chemicals during pregnancy</li></ul>What are the signs of cerebral palsy (CP)?<p>There are many different types and levels of disability with CP. So the signs can be different in each child. These signs usually appear in the early months of life. They may include:</p><ul><li>Developmental delays. Your child may be slow to reach developmental milestones such as learning to roll over, sit, crawl, or walk.</li><li>Abnormal muscle tone. Your child may seem floppy, or they may be stiff or rigid.</li><li>An unusual posture or favoring one side of the body when reaching, crawling, or moving. </li><li>Stiff or tight muscles and exaggerated reflexes (spasticity).
</li><li>Shaking (tremor) of arms, legs, hands, or feet.</li><li>Sudden, uncontrolled movements.</li></ul><p>It's important to know that children without CP can also have these signs. Contact your child's health care provider if your child has any of these signs, so you can get a correct diagnosis.</p>How is cerebral palsy (CP) diagnosed?<p>Diagnosing CP involves several steps:</p><ul><li>Developmental monitoring (or surveillance) means tracking a child's growth and development over time. If there are any concerns about your child's development, they should have a developmental screening test as soon as possible.</li><li>Developmental screening involves giving your child a short test to check for motor, movement, or other developmental delays. If the screenings are not normal, the provider will recommend more evaluations.</li><li>Developmental and medical evaluations are done to diagnose which disorder your child has. To make the diagnosis, your provider:
 <ul><li>Will check your child's motor skills, muscle tone, reflexes, and posture</li><li>Will ask about their medical history</li><li>May order lab tests, genetic tests, and/or imaging tests</li></ul>
</li></ul>What are the treatments for cerebral palsy (CP)?<p>There is no cure for CP, but treatment can improve the lives of those who have it. It is important to begin a treatment program as early as possible.</p><p>A team of health professionals will work with you and your child to develop a treatment plan. Common treatments include:</p><ul><li>Medicines</li><li>Surgery</li><li>Assistive devices such as braces and walkers</li><li>Physical, occupational, recreational, and speech therapy</li></ul>Can cerebral palsy (CP) be prevented?<p>You cannot prevent the genetic problems that can cause CP. But it may be possible to manage or avoid some of the risk factors for CP. For example:</p><ul><li>If you are pregnant, make sure that you are vaccinated against infections that could cause CP in your developing baby.</li><li>Get regular prenatal care, which can reduce the risk of preterm birth or having a low birthweight baby. It can also help you manage any health conditions that could raise the risk of CP.</li><li>Use car seats for your baby to prevent head injuries that could cause CP.</li></ul></content>
      <content name="mesh">Cerebral Palsy</content>
      <content name="groupName">Brain and Nerves</content>

    <document rank="21" url="https://medlineplus.gov/depression.html">
      <content name="title">Depression</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Dysthymic Disorder</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Clinical depression</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Major depressive disorder</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Unipolar depression</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is depression?<p>Depression is more than a feeling of being sad or irritable for a few days. It's a serious mood disorder. As one of the most common mental disorders in the United States, depression can affect how you think, feel, and your everyday life. You may not know why you feel this way, but even basic daily activities such as sleeping, eating, or working may become difficult. Depression often needs long-term treatment but getting help at the earliest sign of a problem can help manage your symptoms.</p>What are the different types of depression?<p>The most common types of depression are major depression and persistent depressive disorder.</p><ul><li>Major depression symptoms include a depressed mood or a loss of interest. Symptoms affect your daily activities and last for at least two weeks.</li><li>Persistent depressive disorder (also called dysthymia or dysthymic disorder) has less severe depressive symptoms, but they last longer, usually for at least two years.</li></ul><p>Other types of depression can include:</p><ul><li>Seasonal affective disorder comes and goes with the seasons. It usually starts in late fall and early winter and goes away during the spring and summer.</li><li>Bipolar disorder is sometimes called manic depression. This mood disorder can cause intense mood swings.</li><li>Depression with symptoms of psychosis is a severe form of depression that also has delusions (false beliefs) or hallucinations (seeing, hearing, or feeling something that isn't there).</li></ul>What causes depression?<p>There are a variety of things that may cause depression, including genetic, biological, environmental, and psychological factors. Depression can happen at any age and can affect anyone, but it's more common in women. This is often due to the physical and hormonal changes that women have during menstruation, pregnancy, the postpartum period, and menopause. Other mental disorders or chronic medical conditions such as diabetes, cancer, or heart disease may occur with depression. This may make the chronic condition or depression worse.</p><p>In some cases, there is no clear cause for depression.</p>Who is at risk of depression?<p>You may be at a higher risk of depression if you have a close relative who has it or another mental health disorder. Going through trauma or stressful life events may raise your risk even more.</p>What are the symptoms of depression?<p>Depression doesn't look the same on everyone. The type and number of symptoms of depression can vary and don't always appear like sadness. Depression may affect you differently based on your:</p><ul><li>Age. The symptoms of depression may be different in a child, who may appear more irritable than sad. Teens may act out or have excessive sleepiness. The symptoms for an older adult may not be as obvious as other age groups.</li><li>Sex. Men often show symptoms and coping behaviors differently than women. Rather than sad, men may appear angry or irritable.</li><li>Medical conditions. Some medicines and medical conditions may cause symptoms that look like depression. Chronic conditions may make depression worse.</li></ul><p>Symptoms of depression are ongoing and affect your day-to-day life. They can include:</p><ul><li>Feeling sad or "empty"</li><li>Losing interest in favorite activities</li><li>Overeating, or not wanting to eat at all</li><li>Sleeping too much, or not enough</li><li>Feeling very tired</li><li>Feeling hopeless, irritable, anxious, or guilty</li><li>Having aches and pains, headaches, cramps, or digestive problems</li><li>Having thoughts of death or suicide</li><li>Changing moods or behaviors</li><li>Increasing use of alcohol or drugs</li><li>Isolating from family and friends</li></ul>How is depression diagnosed?<p>If you think you have depression, talk to your health care provider or a mental health provider. To be diagnosed with depression, your provider may use many tools:</p><ul><li>A medical history will include asking about your symptoms and family history. To be diagnosed with depression, your symptoms must occur most of the day, nearly every day, for at least two weeks. One of the symptoms must be a depressed mood or a loss of interest in most activities.</li><li>Medical tests may be done to rule out other medical conditions. Certain medicines and medical conditions (such as viruses or <span class="qt0">thyroid</span> disorders) may cause symptoms like depression.</li><li>A physical exam to make sure another issue isn't causing your symptoms.</li><li>A mental health evaluation by your provider or a mental health provider.</li></ul>What are the treatments for depression?<p>Depression may need long-term treatment. Most treatments include medicines and/or psychotherapy (talk therapy):</p><ul><li>Medicines such as antidepressants may help control your symptoms. You may need to try several medicines to find which works best for you. Medicine may take time to work and can have side effects. Don't stop taking your medicine without first talking with your provider. Contact your provider if you have any concerns about side effects from the medicine.</li><li>Psychotherapy (talk therapy) under the care of a mental health provider can help you recognize and change troubling emotions, thoughts, and behaviors. This may be done one-on-one or in a group setting. It can give you and your family support, education, skills, and coping strategies.</li><li>Light therapy has been shown to be effective for seasonal affective disorder (SAD).</li><li>Other treatments options may be considered if treatment isn't working or if depression is severe. These may include:
	<ul><li>Electroconvulsive therapy (ECT) is a brain stimulation procedure that uses a mild electric current when you are under general anesthesia. ECT is most often used for severe depression that is not getting better with other treatments.</li><li>Repetitive transcranial magnetic stimulation (rTMS) is a brain stimulation procedure that uses magnetic waves to relieve depression. It's not as powerful as ECT, but with rTMS, you don't need anesthesia. It also has a low risk of negative effects on your memory and thinking.</li></ul>
</li></ul>Can depression be prevented?<p>Most cases of depression can't be prevented, but healthy lifestyle changes can have long-term benefits for your mental health. These can include:</p><ul><li>Getting regular exercise</li><li>Having a consistent sleep schedule</li><li>Controlling stress</li></ul><p>If you, or someone you love is depressed, see your provider or a mental health provider. It's important to get treatment at the earliest sign of a problem.</p><p>If you or someone you know has thoughts of hurting themselves or attempting suicide, get help right away. If it is an emergency, dial 911.</p><p>NIH: National Institute of Mental Health</p></content>
      <content name="mesh">Depression</content>
      <content name="groupName">Mental Health and Behavior</content>
      <content name="snippet"> ... medicines and medical conditions (such as viruses or <span class="qt0">thyroid</span> disorders) may cause symptoms like depression.  A physical  ... </content>

    <document rank="22" url="https://medlineplus.gov/fatigue.html">
      <content name="title">Fatigue</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Tiredness</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Weariness</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is fatigue?<p>Fatigue is a feeling of weariness, tiredness, or lack of energy. It can interfere with your usual daily activities. Fatigue can be a normal response to physical activity, emotional stress, boredom, or lack of sleep. But sometimes it can be a sign of a mental or physical condition. If you have been feeling tired for weeks, contact your health care provider. They can help you find out what's causing your fatigue and recommend ways to relieve it.</p>What causes fatigue?<p>Fatigue itself is not a disease; it's a symptom. It can have many different causes, including pregnancy and various medical problems, treatments, and lifestyle habits such as:</p><ul><li>Medical problems:<ul><li>Chronic (long-lasting) diseases such as diabetes, COPD (chronic obstructive pulmonary disease), and heart, liver, kidney and <span class="qt0">thyroid</span> diseases</li><li>Untreated pain and diseases like fibromyalgia</li><li>Anemia</li><li>Infections</li><li>Parkinson's disease</li><li>Sleep apnea and other sleep disorders</li><li>Recent stroke</li><li>Mental disorders such as depression and anxiety</li></ul></li><li>Treatments:<ul><li>Cancer treatments such as chemotherapy, radiation, and bone marrow transplants</li><li>Recovering from major surgery</li><li>Taking certain medicines, such as antidepressants, antihistamines, and medicines for nausea and pain</li></ul></li><li>Lifestyle habits:<ul><li>Not getting enough sleep</li><li>Staying up too late</li><li>Drinking too much alcohol</li><li>Substance use</li><li>Having too much caffeine (which can keep you from getting a good night's sleep)</li><li>Getting too little or too much exercise</li></ul></li></ul><p>Overwhelming fatigue is part of a disorder called myalgic encephalomyelitis/chronic fatigue syndrome (ME/CFS). The fatigue caused by this disorder is different from a tired feeling that goes away after you rest. Instead, the fatigue in ME/CFS lasts a long time, does not get better with rest, and limits your ability to do ordinary daily activities.</p>What can I do to manage fatigue?<p>If you've had fatigue that does not get better after several weeks, call your provider. They will ask questions about your sleep, daily activities, appetite, and exercise. They will likely do a physical exam and order lab tests. Once they decide on a diagnosis, they can tell you what treatments might help.</p><p>You can also make some lifestyle changes to feel less tired:</p><ul><li>Get regular physical activity</li><li>Improve your sleep habits</li><li>Stop smoking (if you smoke), since smoking is linked to many diseases that can cause fatigue</li><li>Manage stress</li><li>Eat a healthy diet and avoid alcohol</li></ul><p>It also may be helpful for you to keep a fatigue diary. This can help you find patterns throughout the day, such as when you feel more tired and when you have more energy. It can also help you plan for activities that may give you more energy.</p><p>NIH: National Institute on Aging</p></content>

    <document rank="23" url="https://medlineplus.gov/hairloss.html">
      <content name="title">Hair Loss</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Alopecia</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>You lose up to 100 hairs from your scalp every day. That's normal, and in most people, those hairs grow back. But many men -- and some women -- lose hair as they grow older. You can also lose your hair if you have certain diseases, such as <span class="qt0">thyroid</span> problems, diabetes, or lupus. If you take certain medicines or have chemotherapy for cancer, you may also lose your hair. Other causes are stress, a low protein diet, a family history, or poor nutrition. </p><p>Treatment for hair loss depends on the cause. In some cases, treating the underlying cause will correct the problem. Other treatments include medicines and hair restoration. </p></content>
      <content name="mesh">Hypotrichosis</content>
      <content name="groupName">Skin, Hair and Nails</content>

    <document rank="24" url="https://medlineplus.gov/headandneckcancer.html">
      <content name="title">Head and Neck Cancer</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is head and neck cancer? <p>Head and neck cancer is the name for cancers that develop in the mouth, nose and sinuses, salivary glands, and throat and voice box (larynx). Most head and neck cancers are squamous cell cancers. They begin in the moist tissues that line the head and neck. The cancer cells may spread into deeper tissue as the cancer grows.</p><p>There are other cancers that develop in the head and neck, such as brain cancer, eye cancer, and esophageal cancer. But they are usually not considered to be head and neck cancers, because those types of cancer and their treatments are different.</p>Who is more likely to develop head and neck cancer? <p>Anyone can get head and neck cancer, but you are more likely to develop it if you:</p><ul><li>Use tobacco or drink lots of alcohol. Your risk of developing head and neck cancer is even higher if you do both.</li><li>Are male.</li><li>Are over age 50.</li><li>Have a history of head or neck cancer.</li><li>Have HPV (for cancers in the tonsils and base of the tongue).</li><li>Are exposed to wood dusts or certain other toxic substances at work (for upper throat, nose, and sinus cancers).</li><li>Have had radiation exposure to the head and neck (for salivary gland cancer).</li><li>Have Epstein-Barr virus (EBV) infection (for upper throat and salivary gland cancers).</li><li>Are Asian (for upper throat cancer).</li></ul>What are the symptoms of head and neck cancer?<p>The symptoms of head and neck cancer may include:</p><ul><li>A lump in the neck</li><li>A sore in the mouth or the throat that does not heal and may be painful</li><li>A sore throat that does not go away</li><li>Trouble swallowing</li><li>A change or hoarseness in the voice</li></ul><p>Other possible symptoms can depend on the specific type of head and neck cancer.</p>How is head and neck cancer diagnosed?<p>Which exams and tests used to diagnose head and neck cancer can depend on the specific type of cancer. Possible exams and tests may include:</p><ul><li>A physical exam of the area that might have cancer.</li><li>Blood and/or urine tests.</li><li>An endoscopy.</li><li>A biopsy or other procedure to collect cells from the area that might have cancer. The cells are viewed under a microscope to find out if they are abnormal.</li><li>Imaging tests.</li></ul>What are the treatments for head and neck cancer?<p>The treatment for head and neck cancer will depend on the specific type of cancer, the stage (how advanced the cancer is), your overall health, and other factors. The treatment options might include:</p><ul><li>Surgery</li><li>Radiation therapy</li><li>Chemotherapy</li><li>Cancer immunotherapy</li><li>Targeted therapy, which uses drugs or other substances that mainly attack specific cancer cells and cause less harm to normal cells</li></ul><p>In some cases, you may need a combination of treatments. Sometimes surgery can affect how well you can chew, swallow, or talk. Rehabilitation, such as physical therapy, dietary counseling, and speech therapy, may help.</p>Can head and neck cancer be prevented?<p>There are steps you can take to help prevent head and neck cancer:</p><ul><li>Not smoking</li><li>Limiting alcohol use or not drinking at all</li><li>Getting regular dental exams</li><li>Talking to your provider about HPV vaccination</li></ul><p>NIH: National Cancer Institute</p></content>

    <document rank="0" url="https://medlineplus.gov/anemia.html">
      <content name="title"><span class="qt0">Anemia</span></content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Iron Deficiency <span class="qt0">Anemia</span></content>
      <content name="altTitle">Iron poor blood</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>If you have <span class="qt0">anemia</span>, your blood does not carry enough oxygen to the rest of your body.  The most common cause of <span class="qt0">anemia</span> is not having enough iron. Your body needs iron to make hemoglobin. Hemoglobin is an iron-rich protein that gives the red color to blood. It carries oxygen from the lungs to the rest of the body.</p><p><span class="qt0">Anemia</span> has three main causes: blood loss, lack of red blood cell production, and high rates of red blood cell destruction.</p><p>Conditions that may lead to <span class="qt0">anemia</span> include:</p><ul><li>Heavy periods</li><li>Pregnancy</li><li>Ulcers</li><li>Colon polyps or colon cancer</li><li>Inherited disorders</li><li>A diet that does not have enough iron, folic acid or vitamin B12</li><li>Blood disorders such as sickle cell <span class="qt0">anemia</span> and thalassemia, or cancer</li><li>Aplastic <span class="qt0">anemia</span>, a condition that can be inherited or acquired</li><li>G6PD deficiency, a metabolic disorder</li></ul><p><span class="qt0">Anemia</span> can make you feel tired, cold, dizzy, and irritable. You may be short of breath or have a headache.</p><p>Your doctor will diagnose <span class="qt0">anemia</span> with a physical exam and blood tests. Treatment depends on the kind of <span class="qt0">anemia</span> you have.</p><p>NIH: National Heart, Lung, and Blood Institute</p></content>
      <content name="mesh"><span class="qt0">Anemia</span></content>
      <content name="groupName">Blood, Heart and Circulation</content>
      <content name="snippet"> If you have <span class="qt0">anemia</span>, your blood does not carry enough oxygen to the rest of your body.  The most common cause of <span class="qt0">anemia</span> is not having enough iron. Your body  ... </content>

    <document rank="1" url="https://medlineplus.gov/aplasticanemia.html">
      <content name="title">Aplastic <span class="qt0">Anemia</span></content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Fanconi <span class="qt0">Anemia</span></content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is aplastic <span class="qt0">anemia</span>?<p>Aplastic  <span class="qt0">anemia</span> is a rare but serious blood disorder.  If you have it, your bone marrow doesn't make enough new blood cells. It happens when there is damage to stem cells inside your bone marrow. There are different types of aplastic <span class="qt0">anemia</span>, including Fanconi <span class="qt0">anemia</span>.</p>What causes aplastic <span class="qt0">anemia</span>?<p>The causes of aplastic <span class="qt0">anemia</span> can include:</p><ul><li>Autoimmune disorders, which are the most common cause</li><li>Certain inherited gene changes, such as the one that can cause Fanconi <span class="qt0">anemia</span></li><li>Toxic substances, such as pesticides, arsenic, and benzene</li><li>Radiation therapy and chemotherapy for cancer</li><li>Certain medicines</li><li>Viral infections such as hepatitis, Epstein-Barr virus, or HIV</li><li>Pregnancy</li></ul><p>In many people, the cause is unknown. This is called idiopathic aplastic <span class="qt0">anemia</span>.</p>What are the symptoms of aplastic <span class="qt0">anemia</span>?<p>Aplastic <span class="qt0">anemia</span> can develop suddenly or slowly. It can be mild or severe. The symptoms of aplastic <span class="qt0">anemia</span> can include:</p><ul><li>Fatigue</li><li>Weakness</li><li>Dizziness</li><li>Shortness of breath</li><li>Easy bruising or bleeding</li></ul>What other problems can aplastic <span class="qt0">anemia</span> cause?<p>Aplastic <span class="qt0">anemia</span> can cause other problems, including frequent infections and bleeding. It raises your risk of developing a serious blood disorder.</p><p>If not treated, aplastic <span class="qt0">anemia</span> can also lead to heart problems such as an arrhythmia (a problem with the rate or rhythm of your heartbeat), an enlarged heart, or heart failure.</p>How is aplastic <span class="qt0">anemia</span> diagnosed?<p>To find out if you have aplastic <span class="qt0">anemia</span>, your doctor will:</p><p>
</p><ul><li>Take your medical and your family medical histories</li><li>Do a physical exam</li><li>Order tests, such as tests to check if you have low numbers of cells in your bone marrow and blood</li></ul>What are the treatments for aplastic <span class="qt0">anemia</span>?<p>If you have aplastic <span class="qt0">anemia</span>, your doctor will create a treatment plan for you. The plan will be based on how severe the <span class="qt0">anemia</span> is and what is causing it. Treatments can include:</p><ul><li>Blood transfusions</li><li>Blood and marrow stem cell transplants</li><li>Medicines to suppress your immune system</li></ul><p>Because of the risk of blood disorders, your doctor will monitor your condition and screen you for blood disorders regularly.</p><p>NIH: National Heart, Lung, and Blood Institute</p></content>
      <content name="mesh"><span class="qt0">Anemia</span>, Aplastic</content>
      <content name="groupName">Blood, Heart and Circulation</content>

    <document rank="2" url="https://medlineplus.gov/thalassemia.html">
      <content name="title">Thalassemia</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Cooley's <span class="qt0">Anemia</span></content>
      <content name="altTitle">Mediterranean <span class="qt0">Anemia</span></content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>Thalassemias are inherited blood disorders. If you have one, your body makes fewer healthy red blood cells and less hemoglobin. Hemoglobin is a protein that carries oxygen to the body. That leads to <span class="qt0">anemia</span>. Thalassemias occur most often among people of Italian, Greek, Middle Eastern, Southern Asian, and African descent.</p><p>Thalassemias can be mild or severe. Some people have no symptoms or mild <span class="qt0">anemia</span>. The most common severe type in the United States is called Cooley's <span class="qt0">anemia</span>. It usually appears during the first two years of life. People with it may have severe <span class="qt0">anemia</span>, slowed growth and delayed puberty, and problems with the spleen, liver, heart, or bones.</p><p>Doctors diagnose thalassemias using blood tests. Treatments include blood transfusions and treatment to remove excess iron from the body. If you have mild symptoms or no symptoms, you may not need treatment. In some severe cases, you may need a bone marrow transplant.</p><p>NIH: National Heart, Lung, and Blood Institute</p></content>

Some people have no symptoms or mild <span class="qt0">anemia</span>. The most common severe type in the United  ... </content>

    <document rank="3" url="https://medlineplus.gov/sicklecelldisease.html">
      <content name="title">Sickle Cell Disease</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Hemoglobin SS Disease</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Sickle Cell <span class="qt0">Anemia</span></content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is sickle cell disease (SCD)?<p>Sickle cell disease (SCD) is a group of inherited red blood cell disorders. If you have SCD, there is a problem with your hemoglobin. Hemoglobin is a protein in red blood cells that carries oxygen throughout the body. With SCD, the hemoglobin forms into stiff rods within the red blood cells. This changes the shape of the red blood cells. The cells are supposed to be disc-shaped, but instead they are crescent, or sickle, shaped.</p><p>The sickle-shaped cells are not flexible and cannot change shape easily. Many of them burst apart as they move through your blood vessels. The sickle cells usually only last 10 to 20 days, instead of the normal 90 to 120 days. Your body may have trouble making enough new cells to replace the ones that you lost. Because of this, you may not have enough red blood cells. This is a condition called <span class="qt0">anemia</span>, and it can make you feel tired.</p><p>The sickle-shaped cells can also stick to vessel walls, causing a blockage that slows or stops the flow of blood. When this happens, oxygen can't reach nearby tissues. The lack of oxygen can cause attacks of sudden, severe pain, called pain crises. These attacks can occur without warning. If you get one, you might need to go to the hospital for treatment.</p>What causes sickle cell disease (SCD)?<p>SCD is caused by a variant (change) in a gene that has instructions for your body to make one part of the hemoglobin. This changed gene is sometimes called a sickle cell gene. People with SCD are born with two sickle cell genes, one from each parent. </p><p>If you are born with one sickle cell gene, it's called sickle cell trait. People with sickle cell trait are generally healthy, but they can pass the defective gene on to their children.</p>Who is more likely to have sickle cell disease (SCD)?<p>In the United States, most of the people with SCD are African Americans:</p><ul><li>About 1 in 13 Black or African American babies are born with sickle cell trait</li><li>About 1 in every 365 Black or African American babies are born with sickle cell disease</li></ul><p>SCD also affects some people who come from Hispanic, southern European, Middle Eastern, or Asian Indian backgrounds.</p>What are the symptoms of sickle cell disease (SCD)?<p>People with SCD start to have signs of the disease during the first year of life, usually around 5 or 6 months of age. Early symptoms of SCD may include:</p><ul><li>Painful swelling of the hands and feet</li><li>Fatigue or fussiness from <span class="qt0">anemia</span></li><li>A yellowish color of the skin (jaundice) or the whites of the eyes (icterus)</li></ul><p>The effects of SCD vary from person to person and can change over time. Most of the signs and symptoms of SCD are related to complications of the disease. They may include severe pain, <span class="qt0">anemia</span>, organ damage, and infections.</p>How is sickle cell disease (SCD) diagnosed?<p>A blood test can show if you have SCD or sickle cell trait. Genetic tests can tell if you have one or two copies of the sickle cell gene. Genetic tests can help confirm an SCD diagnosis if the results from blood tests are not clear. </p><p>All states now test newborns for SCD (as well as many other treatable conditions) as part of their screening programs. These programs help find the conditions early, so treatment can be started right away.</p><p>Health care providers can also diagnose SCD before a baby is born. That test uses a sample of amniotic fluid (the liquid in the sac surrounding the baby) or tissue taken from the placenta (the organ that brings oxygen and nutrients to the baby).</p><p>People who are thinking about having children can have the test to find out how likely it is that their children will have SCD.</p>What are the treatments for sickle cell disease (SCD)?<p>There are many ways to manage sickle cell disease. Your medical team will probably include a hematologist, a doctor who specializes in blood diseases. You will work with your medical team to set up a treatment plan. Possible treatment options may include:</p><ul><li>Treatments that can help relieve symptoms and lessen complications, including:
  <ul><li>Hydroxyurea, a medicine to reduce sickling of red blood cells. This can help prevent serious symptoms of sickle cell disease. This medicine can be used in adults and in children as young as 9 months old.  But this medicine is not safe during pregnancy.</li><li>Voxelotor, another medicine to prevent the sickling of red blood cells. It can be used in adults and children ages 4 years and older.</li><li>Pain relievers for acute or chronic pain.</li><li>Antibiotics to try to prevent infections in younger children.</li><li>Blood transfusions for severe <span class="qt0">anemia</span>. If you have had some serious complications, such as a stroke, you may have transfusions to prevent more complications.</li><li>Other treatments for specific complications, such as medicines to lower blood pressure  and vitamins to treat a vitamin deficiency.</li></ul>
</li><li>Bone marrow or stem cell transplantation, which can cure SCD. Because these transplants are risky and can have serious side effects, they are usually only used in children with severe SCD. For the transplant to work, the bone marrow must be a close match. Usually, the best donor is a brother or sister.</li><li>Gene therapies to treat SCD in people who are 12 years and older and have had repeated sickle cell crises. These new therapies involve taking some of your blood stem cells and either adding new DNA to them or changing their existing DNA. Then these cells are given back to you, and they can make a type of hemoglobin that is healthy. This can reduce the complications of SCD, including the SCD crises.</li></ul><p>Complementary and alternative medicine (CAM) seems to help some people deal with pain caused by SCD. These types of CAM may lower your pain, especially if it is not well managed with medicines::</p><ul><li>Cognitive behavioral therapy (a type of counseling)</li><li>Acupuncture</li><li>Exercise or movement programs, such as yoga</li><li>Massage</li><li>Meditation and mindfulness practices</li><li>Virtual reality, a computer-generated 3D environment you can see with special goggles</li></ul><p>It's also important to take steps to keep yourself as healthy as possible:</p><ul><li>Get regular medical care</li><li>Get your routine vaccinations</li><li>Live a healthy lifestyle</li><li>Avoid situations that may set off a pain crisis</li></ul><p>NIH: National Heart, Lung, and Blood Institute</p></content>
      <content name="mesh"><span class="qt0">Anemia</span>, Sickle Cell</content>

    <document rank="4" url="https://medlineplus.gov/iron.html">
      <content name="title">Iron</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Ferritin</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is iron?<p>Iron is a mineral that our bodies need for growth and development. Your body uses iron to make hemoglobin, a protein in red blood cells. Hemoglobin carries oxygen from the lungs to all parts of the body. Iron is also important for healthy muscles, bone marrow, and organ function. Your body also needs iron to make some hormones.</p>How do you get iron?<p>Iron is found naturally in many foods and is added to some fortified food products. Foods that are high in iron include:</p><ul><li>Lean meat, seafood, and poultry</li><li>Iron-fortified breakfast cereals and breads</li><li>White beans, lentils, spinach, kidney beans, and peas</li><li>Nuts and some dried fruits, such as raisins</li></ul><p>Iron is available in supplements, either on its own or as part of many multivitamin/mineral supplements.</p>What causes low iron?<p>Most people in the United States get enough iron. The amount that you need each day depends on your age, your sex, and whether you consume a mostly plant-based diet.</p><p>Sometimes people can have trouble getting enough iron. There can be many causes, including blood loss, a poor diet, or a problem absorbing enough iron from foods. Those who are more likely to have low iron include people who:</p><ul><li>Have heavy periods</li><li>Are pregnant or breastfeeding</li><li>Are infants (especially if they were born premature or low birth weight)</li><li>Are frequent blood donors</li><li>Have cancer, certain digestive diseases, or heart failure</li><li>Are on kidney dialysis</li><li>Have trouble absorbing iron because they:
  <ul><li>Have a digestive condition such as celiac disease, ulcerative colitis, Crohn's disease, or Helicobacter pylori infection</li><li>Had weight loss surgery</li></ul>
</li></ul>What happens if you don't get enough iron?<p>If you have too little iron, you may develop iron-deficiency <span class="qt0">anemia</span>. It may not cause symptoms at first, but over time, it can cause fatigue, shortness of breath, and trouble with memory and concentration. Treatment for low iron and iron-deficiency <span class="qt0">anemia</span> is usually with iron supplements.</p>What happens if you get too much iron?<p>Too much iron can damage your body. For example, if you are healthy and take too many iron supplements, you may have symptoms such as constipation, nausea and vomiting, abdominal (belly) pain, and diarrhea. Higher iron levels can cause ulcers. Extremely high levels can lead to organ damage, coma, and death.</p><p>A disease called hemochromatosis can cause too much iron to build up in the body. Hemochromatosis is inherited (passed down through families). It is usually treated by removing blood (and iron) from your body on a regular basis.</p><p>National Institutes of Health, Office of Dietary Supplements</p></content>

      <content name="groupName">Food and Nutrition</content>

    <document rank="5" url="https://medlineplus.gov/bvitamins.html">
      <content name="title">B Vitamins</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Biotin</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Cobalamin</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Niacin</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Pantothenic Acid</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Pyridoxine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Riboflavin</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Thiamine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Vitamin B</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>The B vitamins are:</p><ul><li>B1 (thiamine)</li><li>B2 (riboflavin)</li><li>B3 (niacin)</li><li>B5 (pantothenic acid)</li><li>B6</li><li>	B7 (biotin)</li><li>B12</li><li>Folic acid</li></ul><p>These vitamins help the process your body uses to get or make energy from the food you eat. They also help form red blood cells.  You can get B vitamins from proteins such as fish, poultry, meat, eggs, and dairy products.  Leafy green vegetables, beans, and peas also have B vitamins.  Many cereals and some breads have added B vitamins.</p><p>Not getting enough of certain B vitamins can cause diseases.  A lack of B12 or B6 can cause <span class="qt0">anemia</span>.</p></content>
      <content name="mesh">Vitamin B Complex</content>
      <content name="groupName">Food and Nutrition</content>
      <content name="snippet"> ... A lack of B12 or B6 can cause <span class="qt0">anemia</span>.</content>
    </document>
    <document rank="6" url="https://medlineplus.gov/bonemarrowdiseases.html">
      <content name="title">Bone Marrow Diseases</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Myeloproliferative Disorders</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>Bone marrow is the spongy tissue inside some of your bones, such as your hip and thigh bones. It contains stem cells. The stem cells can develop into the red blood cells that carry oxygen through your body, the white blood cells that fight infections, and the platelets that help with blood clotting.</p><p>With bone marrow disease, there are problems with the stem cells or how they develop:</p><ul><li>In leukemia, a cancer of the blood, the bone marrow makes abnormal white blood cells</li><li>In aplastic <span class="qt0">anemia</span>, the bone marrow doesn't make red blood cells</li><li>In myeloproliferative disorders, the bone marrow makes too many white blood cells</li><li>Other diseases, such as lymphoma, can spread into the bone marrow and affect the production of blood cells</li></ul><p>Causes of bone marrow diseases include genetics and environmental factors. Tests for bone marrow diseases include blood and bone marrow tests. Treatments depend on the disorder and how severe it is. They might involve medicines, blood transfusions or a bone marrow transplant.</p></content>
      <content name="mesh">Bone Marrow Diseases</content>
      <content name="groupName">Immune System</content>

    <document rank="7" url="https://medlineplus.gov/blooddisorders.html">
      <content name="title">Blood Disorders</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Hematologic Disorders</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Hematologic diseases</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>Your blood is living tissue made up of liquid and solids. The liquid part, called plasma, is made of water, salts and protein. Over half of your blood is plasma. The solid part of your blood contains red blood cells, white blood cells and platelets.</p><p>Blood disorders affect one or more parts of the blood and prevent your blood from doing its job.  They can be acute or chronic. Many blood disorders are inherited. Other causes include other diseases, side effects of medicines, and a lack of certain nutrients in your diet.</p><p>Types of blood disorders include:</p><ul><li>Platelet disorders, excessive clotting, and bleeding problems, which affect how your blood clots</li><li><span class="qt0">Anemia</span>, which happens when your blood does not carry enough oxygen to the rest of your body</li><li>Cancers of the blood, such as leukemia and myeloma</li><li>Eosinophilic disorders, which are problems with one type of white blood cell.</li></ul></content>
      <content name="mesh">Hematologic Diseases</content>
      <content name="groupName">Blood, Heart and Circulation</content>

    <document rank="8" url="https://medlineplus.gov/malnutrition.html">
      <content name="title">Malnutrition</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is malnutrition?<p>Malnutrition means that your body isn't getting enough calories or the right balance of nutrients to stay healthy. This can happen if you don't get enough protein, carbohydrates, and fats, eat too much unhealthy food, or don't get the vitamins and minerals your body needs.</p><p>Malnutrition can affect people of all ages, but children, older adults, and people with chronic (long-term) health problems have a higher risk.</p>What are the types of malnutrition?<p>Most people think of undernutrition with malnutrition. But malnutrition can refer to undernutrition, overnutrition, or an imbalance of nutrients.</p><p>Types of malnutrition include:</p><ul><li>Protein-energy undernutrition, also called macronutrient undernutrition, happens when your body doesn't get enough protein or calories from food. Two serious forms of this are kwashiorkor and marasmus.</li><li>An imbalanced nutritional status occurs when you are not getting the right mix of nutrients. You may get enough calories, but not enough vitamins or minerals.</li><li>Vitamin deficiency diseases happen when your body doesn't get enough of certain vitamins. Without the right vitamins, your body cannot stay healthy and may develop specific diseases. For example, scurvy can develop from too little vitamin C, rickets from too little vitamin D, and <span class="qt0">anemia</span> from too little iron or vitamin B12.</li><li>Failure to thrive occurs when children don't gain weight or grow as expected. This may be due to not getting enough food, trouble absorbing nutrients, or other health problems.</li><li>Overnutrition can occur when your body gets too many calories or nutrients. This may lead to obesity or other health issues.</li></ul>What causes malnutrition?<p>Malnutrition may occur for many reasons, including:</p><ul><li>An unbalanced diet that lacks variety or specific nutrients.</li><li>Some medical issues, which could include malabsorption syndromes, dementia, eating disorders, depression, alcohol use disorder (AUD), or cancer.</li><li>Problems with swallowing, digestion, or dental issues.</li><li>Certain medicines that affect how food tastes and smells.</li><li>Not being able to get food or cook due to things such as chronic illness, mobility issues, or low income.</li></ul>Who is at risk for developing malnutrition?<p>Malnutrition can affect anyone, but some people have a higher risk than others. This includes people who:</p><ul><li>Don't have enough knowledge about nutrition</li><li>Don't have access to a variety of food</li><li>Are older adults or children</li><li>Have a low income</li><li>Have a chronic (long-term) illness</li></ul>What are the symptoms of malnutrition?<p>The symptoms of malnutrition can vary. You may have no symptoms, but if you do, they may include:</p><ul><li>Fatigue and weakness</li><li>Dizziness</li><li>Losing weight without trying</li><li>Low body weight</li><li>Slow growth or development in children</li><li>Frequent infections or slow healing</li><li>Dry skin or brittle hair and nails</li><li>Swelling in the legs, feet, or belly</li></ul>How is malnutrition diagnosed?<p>To check for malnutrition, your provider may:</p><ul><li>Do a nutritional screening, which may include questions about weight loss, changes in your appetite, and your ability to eat.</li><li>Do a physical exam and order blood tests to check for signs that you don't have enough nutrients.</li><li>Refer you to other providers that specialize in nutrition, such as a dietician.</li></ul>What are the treatments for malnutrition?<p>Treatment for malnutrition may include improving your diet, replacing missing nutrients, and treating the underlying cause.</p>Can malnutrition be prevented?<p>To help prevent malnutrition, eat a healthy diet with many different foods. Talk with your provider if you are worried about your diet, weight loss, or your child's growth.</p></content>

    <document rank="9" url="https://medlineplus.gov/bonemarrowtransplantation.html">
      <content name="title">Bone Marrow Transplantation</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>Bone marrow is the spongy tissue inside some of your bones, such as your hip and thigh bones. It contains immature cells, called stem cells. The stem cells can develop into red blood cells, which carry oxygen throughout the body, white blood cells, which fight infections, and platelets, which help the blood to clot.</p><p>A bone marrow transplant is a procedure that replaces a person's faulty bone marrow stem cells. Doctors use these transplants to treat people with certain diseases, such as:</p><ul><li>Leukemia</li><li>Severe blood diseases such as thalassemias, aplastic <span class="qt0">anemia</span>, and sickle cell <span class="qt0">anemia</span></li><li>Multiple myeloma</li><li>Certain immune deficiency diseases</li></ul><p>Before you have a transplant, you need to get high doses of chemotherapy and possibly radiation. This destroys the faulty stem cells in your bone marrow. It also suppresses your body's immune system so that it won't attack the new stem cells after the transplant.</p><p>In some cases, you can donate your own bone marrow stem cells in advance. The cells are saved and then used later on. Or you can get cells from a donor. The donor might be a family member or unrelated person.</p><p>Bone marrow transplantation has serious risks. Some complications can be life-threatening. But for some people, it is the best hope for a cure or a longer life.</p><p>NIH: National Heart, Lung, and Blood Institute</p></content>

    <document rank="10" url="https://medlineplus.gov/celiacdisease.html">
      <content name="title">Celiac Disease</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Gluten Intolerance</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Gluten-Sensitive Enteropathy</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Nontropical Sprue</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Celiac sprue</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is celiac disease?<p>Celiac disease is a chronic (long-term) digestive and immune disorder that damages your small intestine. The damage may prevent your body from absorbing vitamins, minerals, and other nutrients from the food you eat. This can lead to malnutrition and other serious health problems</p><p>Celiac disease is triggered by eating foods that contain gluten. Gluten is a protein found in wheat, barley, rye, and other grains. It may also be in other products like vitamins and supplements, hair and skin products, toothpastes, and lip balm.</p><p>Celiac disease is different from gluten sensitivity. Both involve problems with gluten and can cause some of the same symptoms, such as abdominal (belly) pain and fatigue. But gluten sensitivity does not damage the small intestine like celiac disease does.</p><p> Wheat allergy, a type of food allergy, is also different. With both celiac disease and wheat allergy, your immune system is reacting to wheat. But with wheat allergy, you can have some different symptoms, such as itchy eyes or trouble breathing. And a wheat allergy will not cause long-term damage to the small intestine.</p>What causes celiac disease?<p>The exact cause of celiac disease is not known. Research suggests that celiac disease only happens in people who have certain genes and eat food that contains gluten. Researchers are studying other factors that may play a role in causing the disease.</p>Who is more likely to develop celiac disease?<p>Celiac disease is more common if you:</p><ul><li>Have a family member who has the disease</li><li>Have Down syndrome, Turner syndrome, or Williams syndrome</li><li>Are White</li><li>Are female</li></ul>What are the symptoms of celiac disease?<p>The symptoms of celiac disease can be different from person to person. Sometimes the symptoms may come and go. Some people may not notice any symptoms.</p><p>Some of the possible symptoms affect your digestive system. Digestive symptoms are more common in children than in adults. The digestive symptoms include:</p><ul><li>Bloating (feeling fullness or swelling in your belly)</li><li>Chronic (long-term) diarrhea or greasy, bulky, unusually bad-smelling stool (poop)</li><li>Constipation</li><li>Gas</li><li>Lactose intolerance because of damage to the small intestine</li><li>Nausea and vomiting</li><li>Pain in the abdomen (belly)</li><li>Weight loss in adults, or not enough weight gain in children</li></ul><p>Some people with celiac disease have symptoms that affect other parts of the body, such as:</p><ul><li>Fatigue</li><li>Depression and anxiety</li><li>Irritability (in children)</li><li>Dermatitis herpetiformis, an itchy rash with blisters (mainly in adults)</li><li>Bone or joint pain</li><li>Symptoms involving the mouth, such as canker sores or dry mouth</li></ul>What other problems can celiac disease cause?<p>Over time, celiac disease can cause other health problems, especially if it is not treated. These problems can include:</p><ul><li>Malnutrition</li><li><span class="qt0">Anemia</span>, especially iron-deficiency <span class="qt0">anemia</span></li><li>Bone loss</li><li>Nervous system problems such as headaches, balance problems, or peripheral neuropathy</li><li>Reproductive problems, such as missed menstrual periods and miscarriages in women and infertility in men and women</li></ul>How is celiac disease diagnosed?<p>If you have symptoms of celiac disease, your health care provider will look for signs that you might have celiac disease. To do this, your provider will get your medical and family history and do a physical exam.</p><p>If your provider thinks that you could have celiac disease, you will have some tests. Providers most often use blood tests and biopsies of the small intestine to diagnose celiac disease. The biopsy would be done during an upper gastrointestinal (GI) endoscopy. For this procedure, your provider uses an endoscope (a flexible tube with a camera) to see the lining of your esophagus, stomach, and small intestine. It also allows your provider to take a sample of tissue for a biopsy.</p>What are the treatments for celiac disease?<p>The treatment for celiac disease is following a gluten-free diet for the rest of your life. Sticking with a gluten-free diet will treat or prevent many of the symptoms and other health problems caused by celiac disease. In most cases, it can also heal damage in the small intestine and prevent more damage. </p><p>Your provider may refer you to a registered dietician (a nutrition expert) who can help you learn how to eat a healthy diet without gluten. You will also need to avoid all hidden sources of gluten, such as certain supplements, cosmetics, toothpaste, etc. Reading product labels can sometimes help you avoid gluten. If a label doesn't tell you what is in a product, check with the company that makes the product for an ingredients list. Don't just assume that a product is gluten-free if it doesn't mention it.</p><p>NIH: National Institute of Diabetes and Digestive and Kidney Diseases</p></content>

    <document rank="11" url="https://medlineplus.gov/g6pddeficiency.html">
      <content name="title">G6PD Deficiency</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Glucose-6-phosphate Dehydrogenase Deficiency</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>Glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase (G6PD) deficiency is a genetic disorder that is most common in males. About 1 in 10 African American males in the United States has it. G6PD deficiency mainly affects red blood cells, which carry oxygen from the lungs to tissues throughout the body.  The most common medical problem it can cause is hemolytic <span class="qt0">anemia</span>. That happens when red blood cells are destroyed faster than the body can replace them.</p><p>If you have G6PD deficiency, you may not have symptoms. Symptoms happen if your red blood cells are exposed to certain chemicals in food or medicine, certain bacterial or viral infections, or stress. They may include:</p><ul><li>Paleness</li><li>Jaundice</li><li>Dark urine</li><li>Fatigue</li><li>Shortness of breath</li><li>Enlarged spleen</li><li>Rapid heart rate</li></ul><p>A blood test can tell if you have it. Treatments include medicines to treat infection, avoiding substances that cause the problem with red blood cells, and sometimes transfusions.</p><p>NIH: National Library of Medicine</p></content>

      <content name="groupName">Genetics/Birth Defects</content>

    <document rank="12" url="https://medlineplus.gov/hemochromatosis.html">
      <content name="title">Hemochromatosis</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Iron Overload Disease</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>Hemochromatosis is a disease in which too much iron builds up in your body. Your body needs iron but too much of it is toxic. If you have hemochromatosis, you absorb more iron than you need. Your body has no natural way to get rid of the extra iron. It stores it in body tissues, especially the liver, heart, and pancreas. The extra iron can damage your organs. Without treatment, it can cause your organs to fail.</p><p>There are two types of hemochromatosis. Primary hemochromatosis is an inherited disease. Secondary hemochromatosis is usually the result of something else, such as <span class="qt0">anemia</span>, thalassemia, liver disease, or blood transfusions.</p><p>Many symptoms of hemochromatosis are similar to those of other diseases. Not everyone has symptoms. If you do, you may have joint pain, fatigue, general weakness, weight loss, and stomach pain.</p><p>Your doctor will diagnose hemochromatosis based on your medical and family histories, a physical exam, and the results from tests and procedures. Treatments include removing blood (and iron) from your body, medicines, and changes in your diet.</p><p>NIH: National Heart, Lung, and Blood Institute</p></content>
