    <document rank="13" url="https://medlineplus.gov/myelodysplasticsyndromes.html">
      <content name="title">Myelodysplastic Syndromes</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">MDS</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>Your bone marrow is the spongy tissue inside some of your bones, such as your hip and thigh bones. It contains immature cells, called stem cells. The stem cells can develop into the red blood cells that carry oxygen through your body, the white blood cells that fight infections, and the platelets that help with blood clotting. If you have a myelodysplastic syndrome, the stem cells do not mature into healthy blood cells. Many of them die in the bone marrow.  This means that you do not have enough healthy cells, which can lead to infection, <span class="qt0">anemia</span>, or easy bleeding.</p><p>Myelodysplastic syndromes often do not cause early symptoms and are sometimes found during a routine blood test.  If you have symptoms, they may include:</p><ul><li>Shortness of breath</li><li>Weakness or feeling tired</li><li>Skin that is paler than usual</li><li>Easy bruising or bleeding</li><li>Pinpoint spots under the skin caused by bleeding</li><li>Fever or frequent infections</li></ul><p>Myelodysplastic syndromes are rare. People at higher risk are over 60, have had chemotherapy or radiation therapy, or have been exposed to certain chemicals.  Treatment options include transfusions, drug therapy, chemotherapy, and blood or bone marrow stem cell transplants.</p><p>NIH: National Cancer Institute</p></content>
      <content name="mesh">Myelodysplastic Syndromes</content>
      <content name="groupName">Immune System</content>
      <content name="snippet"> ... enough healthy cells, which can lead to infection, <span class="qt0">anemia</span>, or easy bleeding. Myelodysplastic syndromes often do not  ... </content>
    </document>
    <document rank="14" url="https://medlineplus.gov/pulmonaryhypertension.html">
      <content name="title">Pulmonary Hypertension</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Cor Pulmonale</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Hypertension, Pulmonary</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Primary Pulmonary Hypertension</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Pulmonary Arterial Hypertension</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is pulmonary hypertension? <p>Pulmonary hypertension, sometimes called PH, is a serious condition that affects the blood vessels in the lungs. It develops when the blood pressure in your lungs is higher than normal.</p><p>Your heart pumps blood to your lungs so they can add oxygen to the blood. The blood goes back to your heart. From there, it travels to the rest of your body so that your tissues can get the oxygen that they need.</p><p>The blood moves from your heart to your lungs through blood vessels called pulmonary arteries. If the pulmonary arteries become damaged, narrowed, or blocked, the blood does not flow through them as well. This can increase the blood pressure in the arteries and cause pulmonary hypertension.</p><p>There are different types of pulmonary hypertension, including pulmonary arterial hypertension (PAH). The different types are based on what caused the disease.</p>What causes pulmonary hypertension?<p>Pulmonary hypertension can develop on its own or be caused by another condition. Sometimes the cause is unknown or is not clear.</p><p>Some of the possible causes include:</p><ul><li>Heart diseases, including left-sided heart failure and congenital heart disease</li><li>Lung diseases such as COPD (chronic obstructive pulmonary disease), interstitial lung disease, emphysema, and sleep apnea</li><li>Other medical conditions such as:
  <ul><li>Liver diseases</li><li>Sickle cell disease</li><li>Pulmonary embolism (blood clots in the lungs)</li><li>Connective tissue disorders like scleroderma</li></ul>
</li></ul>Who is more likely to develop pulmonary hypertension? <p>Certain factors can make you more likely to develop pulmonary hypertension, such as:</p><ul><li>Your age. The risk increases as you get older. The condition is usually diagnosed between ages 30 and 60.</li><li>Your environment. Being exposed to asbestos or having certain infections caused by parasites can raise your risk.</li><li>Your family history and genetics. Certain genetic disorders, such as Down syndrome, congenital heart disease, and Gaucher disease, can raise your risk of pulmonary hypertension. So can a family history of blood clots.</li><li>Your lifestyle habits.Smoking and illegal drug use can raise your risk of developing pulmonary hypertension.</li><li>Certain medicines. For example, some medicines used to treat cancer and depression can make you more likely to develop pulmonary hypertension.</li><li>Your sex. Pulmonary hypertension is more common in women than in men.</li></ul>What are the symptoms of pulmonary hypertension?<p>The symptoms of pulmonary hypertension are sometimes hard to recognize and are similar to the symptoms of other medical conditions. So sometimes it can take years for someone to get diagnosed with pulmonary hypertension.</p><p>The symptoms of pulmonary hypertension may include:</p><ul><li>Shortness of breath</li><li>Chest pain or pressure</li><li>Dizziness that may lead to fainting</li><li>Fatigue</li><li>Swelling of the abdomen, legs, or feet</li><li>Heart palpitations (the feeling that your heart is pounding or beating too fast)</li></ul>What other problems can pulmonary hypertension cause?<p>Pulmonary hypertension can get worse over time and lead to serious problems, including:</p><ul><li><span class="qt0">Anemia</span>, which can cause your body to not get enough oxygen-rich blood</li><li>Arrhythmias, problems with the rate or rhythm of your heartbeat</li><li>Blood clots in the pulmonary arteries</li><li>Bleeding in the lungs</li><li>Heart failure</li><li>Liver damage</li><li>Pericardial effusion, a collection of fluid around the heart</li><li>Serious pregnancy complications</li></ul>How is pulmonary hypertension diagnosed?<p>To find out if you have pulmonary hypertension, your health care provider:</p><ul><li>Will ask about your medical history and symptoms</li><li>Will do a physical exam, which may include checking your blood oxygen, listening to your heart and lungs, and checking your blood pressure</li><li>Will likely order some tests, such as:
  <ul><li>Blood tests to look for blood clots, stress on the heart, or <span class="qt0">anemia</span></li><li>Heart imaging tests, such as a cardiac MRI</li><li>Lung imaging tests, such as chest x-ray</li><li>An electrocardiogram (ECG or EKG)</li></ul>
</li></ul>What are the treatments for pulmonary hypertension?<p>Often there is no cure for pulmonary hypertension, but treatments can help manage your symptoms. Your provider will work with you to come up with a treatment plan. It will be based on your needs and the cause of the pulmonary hypertension. The plan may include:</p><ul><li>Healthy lifestyle changes, such as:
  <ul><li>Healthy eating, which includes eating less salt</li><li>Regular physical activity, which may be done through pulmonary rehabilitation</li></ul>
</li><li>Medicines, such as:
  <ul><li>Blood thinners</li><li>Medicines to control the rate blood is pumped throughout the body</li><li>Medicines to relax blood vessels and allow the blood to flow better</li><li>Medicine to reduce swelling (diuretics)</li></ul>
</li><li>Oxygen therapy</li><li>Procedures to reduce pressure in the heart or pulmonary artery</li><li>In some severe cases, a lung transplant</li></ul><p>NIH: National Heart, Lung, and Blood Institute</p></content>

      <content name="groupName">Blood, Heart and Circulation</content>
      <content name="groupName">Lungs and Breathing</content>

    <document rank="15" url="https://medlineplus.gov/uncommoninfantandnewbornproblems.html">
      <content name="title">Uncommon Infant and Newborn Problems</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>It can be scary when your baby is sick, especially when it is not an everyday problem like a cold or a fever.  You may not know whether the problem is serious or how to treat it.  If you have concerns about your baby's health, call your health care provider right away.</p><p>Learning information about your baby's condition can help ease your worry.  Do not be afraid to ask questions about your baby's care. By working together with your health care provider, you make sure that your baby gets the best care possible.</p></content>
      <content name="mesh">Infant, Newborn, Diseases</content>
      <content name="groupName">Children and Teenagers</content>
      <content name="snippet"> It can be scary when your baby is sick, especially when it is not an everyday problem like a cold or a fever.  You may not know whether the problem is serious  ... </content>
    </document>
    <document rank="16" url="https://medlineplus.gov/acutelymphocyticleukemia.html">
      <content name="title">Acute Lymphocytic Leukemia</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">ALL</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Acute Lymphoblastic Leukemia</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Leukemia, Acute Lymphoblastic</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Leukemia, Acute Lymphocytic</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is leukemia?<p>Leukemia is a term for cancers of the blood cells. Leukemia starts in blood-forming tissues such as the bone marrow. Your bone marrow makes the cells which will develop into white blood cells, red blood cells, and platelets. Each type of cell has a different job:</p><ul><li>White blood cells help your body fight infection</li><li>Red blood cells deliver oxygen from your lungs to your tissues and organs</li><li>Platelets help form clots to stop bleeding</li></ul><p>When you have leukemia, your bone marrow makes large numbers of abnormal cells. This problem most often happens with white blood cells. These abnormal cells build up in your bone marrow and blood. They crowd out the healthy blood cells and make it hard for your cells and blood to do their work.</p>What is acute lymphocytic leukemia (ALL)?<p>Acute lymphocytic leukemia is a type of acute leukemia. It's also called ALL and acute lymphoblastic leukemia. "Acute" means that it usually gets worse quickly if it's not treated. ALL is the most common type of cancer in children. It can also affect adults.</p><p>In ALL, the bone marrow makes too many lymphocytes, a type of white blood cell. These cells normally help your body fight infection. But in ALL, they are abnormal and cannot fight infection very well. They also crowd out the healthy cells, which can lead to infection, <span class="qt0">anemia</span>, and easy bleeding. These abnormal cells can also spread to other parts of the body, including the brain and spinal cord.</p>What causes acute lymphocytic leukemia (ALL)?<p>ALL happens when there are changes in the genetic material (DNA) in bone marrow cells. The cause of these genetic changes is unknown. However, there are certain factors that raise your risk of ALL.</p>Who is at risk for acute lymphocytic leukemia (ALL)?<p>The factors that raise your risk of ALL include:</p><ul><li>Being male</li><li>Being white</li><li>Being over age 70</li><li>Having had chemotherapy or radiation therapy</li><li>Having been exposed to high levels of radiation</li><li>Having certain genetic disorders, such as Down syndrome</li></ul>What are the symptoms of acute lymphocytic leukemia (ALL)?<p>The signs and symptoms of ALL include:</p><ul><li>Weakness or feeling tired</li><li>Fever or night sweats</li><li>Easy bruising or bleeding</li><li>Petechiae, which are tiny red dots under the skin. They are caused by bleeding.</li><li>Shortness of breath</li><li>Weight loss or loss of appetite</li><li>Pain in the bones or stomach</li><li>Pain or feeling of fullness below the ribs</li><li>Swollen lymph nodes - you may notice them as painless lumps in the neck, underarm, stomach, or groin</li><li>Having had many infections</li></ul>How is acute lymphocytic leukemia (ALL) diagnosed?<p>Your health care provider may use many tools to diagnose ALL and figure out which subtype you have:</p><ul><li>A physical exam</li><li>A medical history</li><li>Blood tests, such as
      <ul><li>Complete blood count (CBC) with differential</li><li>Blood chemistry tests such as a basic metabolic panel (BMP), comprehensive metabolic panel (CMP), kidney function tests, liver function tests, and electrolyte panel</li><li>Blood smear</li></ul>  
</li><li>Bone marrow tests. There are two main types - bone marrow aspiration and bone marrow biopsy. Both tests involve removing a sample of bone marrow and bone. The samples are sent to a lab for testing.</li><li>Genetic tests to look for gene and chromosome changes</li></ul><p>If you are diagnosed with ALL, you may have additional tests to see whether the cancer has spread. These include imaging tests and a lumbar puncture, which is a procedure to collect and test cerebrospinal fluid (CSF).</p>What are the treatments for acute lymphocytic leukemia (ALL)?<p>Treatments for ALL include:</p><ul><li>Chemotherapy</li><li>Radiation therapy</li><li>Chemotherapy with stem cell transplant</li><li>Targeted therapy, which uses drugs or other substances that attack specific cancer cells with less harm to normal cells</li></ul><p>Treatment is usually done in two phases:</p><ul><li>The goal of the first phase is to kill the leukemia cells in the blood and bone marrow. This treatment puts the leukemia into remission. Remission means that the signs and symptoms of cancer are reduced or have disappeared.</li><li>The second phase is known as post-remission therapy. Its goal is to prevent a relapse (return) of the cancer. It involves killing any remaining leukemia cells that may not be active but could begin to regrow.</li></ul><p>Treatment during both phases also usually includes central nervous system (CNS) prophylaxis therapy. This therapy helps prevent the spread of leukemia cells to the brain and spinal cord. It may be high dose chemotherapy or chemotherapy injected into the spinal cord. It also sometimes includes radiation therapy.</p><p>NIH: National Cancer Institute</p></content>

    <document rank="17" url="https://medlineplus.gov/acutemyeloidleukemia.html">
      <content name="title">Acute Myeloid Leukemia</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">AML</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Acute Myeloblastic Leukemia</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Leukemia, Myeloblastic, Acute</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Leukemia, Myelogenous, Acute</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Leukemia, Myeloid, Acute</content>
      <content name="altTitle">ANLL</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Acute myelogenous leukemia</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is leukemia?<p>Leukemia is a term for cancers of the blood cells. Leukemia starts in blood-forming tissues such as the bone marrow. Your bone marrow makes the cells which will develop into white blood cells, red blood cells, and platelets. Each type of cell has a different job:</p><ul><li>White blood cells help your body fight infection</li><li>Red blood cells deliver oxygen from your lungs to your tissues and organs</li><li>Platelets help form clots to stop bleeding</li></ul><p>When you have leukemia, your bone marrow makes large numbers of abnormal cells. This problem most often happens with white blood cells. These abnormal cells build up in your bone marrow and blood. They crowd out the healthy blood cells and make it hard for your cells and blood to do their work.</p>What is acute myeloid leukemia (AML)?<p>Acute myeloid leukemia (AML) is a type of acute leukemia. "Acute" means that the leukemia usually gets worse quickly if it's not treated. In AML, the bone marrow makes abnormal myeloblasts (a type of white blood cell), red blood cells, or platelets. When the abnormal cells crowd out the healthy cells, it can lead to infection, <span class="qt0">anemia</span>, and easy bleeding. The abnormal cells can also spread outside the blood to other parts of the body.</p><p>There are several different subtypes of AML. The subtypes are based on how developed the cancer cells are when you get your diagnosis and how different they are from normal cells.</p>What causes acute myeloid leukemia (AML)?<p>AML happens when there are changes in the genetic material (DNA) in bone marrow cells. The cause of these genetic changes is unknown. However, there are certain factors that raise your risk of AML.</p>Who is at risk for acute myeloid leukemia (AML)?<p>The factors that raise your risk of AML include:</p><ul><li>Being male</li><li>Smoking, especially after age 60</li><li>Having had chemotherapy or radiation therapy</li><li>Treatment for acute lymphoblastic leukemia (ALL) as a child</li><li>Exposure to the chemical benzene</li><li>A history of another blood disorder such as myelodysplastic syndrome</li></ul>What are the symptoms of acute myeloid leukemia (AML)?<p>The signs and symptoms of AML include:</p><ul><li>Fever</li><li>Shortness of breath</li><li>Easy bruising or bleeding</li><li>Petechiae, which are tiny red dots under the skin. They are caused by bleeding.</li><li>Weakness or feeling tired</li><li>Weight loss or loss of appetite</li><li>Bone or joint pain, if the abnormal cells build up near or inside the bones</li></ul>How is acute myeloid leukemia (AML) diagnosed?<p>Your health care provider may use many tools to diagnose AML and figure out which subtype you have:</p><ul><li>A physical exam</li><li>A medical history</li><li>Blood tests, such as a complete blood count (CBC) and blood smear</li><li>Bone marrow tests. There are two main types - bone marrow aspiration and bone marrow biopsy. Both tests involve removing a sample of bone marrow and bone. The samples are sent to a lab for testing.</li><li>Genetic tests to look for gene and chromosome changes</li></ul><p>If you are diagnosed with AML, you may have additional tests to see whether the cancer has spread. These include imaging tests and a lumbar puncture, which is a procedure to collect and test cerebrospinal fluid (CSF).</p>What are the treatments for acute myeloid leukemia (AML)?<p>Treatments for AML include:</p><ul><li>Chemotherapy</li><li>Radiation therapy</li><li>Chemotherapy with stem cell transplant</li><li>Other anticancer medicines</li></ul><p>Which treatment you get often depends on which subtype of AML you have.
Treatment is usually done in two phases:</p><ul><li>The goal of the first phase is to kill the leukemia cells in the blood and bone marrow. This puts the leukemia into remission. Remission means that the signs and symptoms of cancer are reduced or have disappeared.</li><li>The second phase is known as post-remission therapy. Its goal is to prevent a relapse (return) of the cancer. It involves killing any remaining leukemia cells that may not be active but could begin to regrow.</li></ul><p>NIH: National Cancer Institute</p></content>

    <document rank="18" url="https://medlineplus.gov/bleeding.html">
      <content name="title">Bleeding</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Hematoma</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Hemorrhage</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>Bleeding is the loss of blood. It can be external, or outside the body, like when you get a cut or wound. It can also be internal, or inside the body, like when you have an injury to an internal organ.  Some bleeding, such as gastrointestinal bleeding, coughing up blood, or vaginal bleeding, can be a symptom of a disease.</p><p>Normally, when you are injured and start bleeding, a blood clot forms to stop the bleeding quickly. Afterwards, the clot dissolves naturally. To be able to make a clot, your blood needs blood proteins called clotting factors and a type of blood cell called platelets. Some people have a problem with clotting, due to another medical condition or an inherited disease. There are two types of problems:</p><ul><li>Your blood may not form clots normally, known as a bleeding disorder. This happens when your body does not make enough platelets or clotting factors, or they don't work the way they should.</li><li>Your blood may make too many clots, or the clots may not dissolve properly</li></ul><p>Sometimes bleeding can cause other problems. A bruise is bleeding under the skin. Some strokes are caused by bleeding in the brain. Severe bleeding may require first aid or a trip to the emergency room.</p></content>

    <document rank="19" url="https://medlineplus.gov/bloodcounttests.html">
      <content name="title">Blood Count Tests</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Blood Cells</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Blood Tests</content>
      <content name="altTitle">CBC</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Complete Blood Count</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Hematologic Tests</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>Your blood contains red blood cells (RBC), white blood cells (WBC), and platelets. Blood count tests measure the number and types of cells in your blood. This helps doctors check on your overall health. The tests can also help to diagnose diseases and conditions such as <span class="qt0">anemia</span>, infections, clotting problems, blood cancers, and immune system disorders.</p><p>Specific types include tests for:</p><ul><li>RBC - the numbers, size, and types of RBC in the blood</li><li>WBC - the numbers and types of WBC in the blood</li><li>Platelets - the numbers and size of the platelets</li><li>Hemoglobin - an iron-rich protein in red blood cells that carries oxygen</li><li>Hematocrit - how much space red blood cells take up in your blood</li><li>Reticulocyte count - how many young red blood cells are in your blood</li><li>Mean corpuscular volume (MCV) - the average size of your red blood cells</li></ul><p>The complete blood count (CBC) includes most or all of these. The CBC is one of the most common blood tests.</p><p>NIH: National Heart, Lung, and Blood Institute</p></content>
      <content name="mesh">Blood Cell Count</content>
      <content name="groupName">Blood, Heart and Circulation</content>
      <content name="snippet"> ... help to diagnose diseases and conditions such as <span class="qt0">anemia</span>, infections, clotting problems, blood cancers, and immune system  ... </content>
    </document>
    <document rank="20" url="https://medlineplus.gov/cancerchemotherapy.html">
      <content name="title">Cancer Chemotherapy</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Chemotherapy</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is cancer chemotherapy?<p>Cancer chemotherapy is a type of cancer treatment. It uses medicines to destroy cancer cells.</p><p>Normally, your body forms new cells as needed, replacing old cells that die. Cancer cells keep growing without control. New cells grow even when you don't need them, and old cells don't die when they should. These extra cells can form a mass called a tumor. Chemotherapy works by killing the cancer cells, stopping them from spreading, or slowing their growth.</p><p>Chemotherapy is used to:</p><ul><li>Treat cancer by curing the cancer, lessening the chance it will return, or stopping or slowing its growth.</li><li>Ease cancer symptoms by shrinking tumors that are causing pain and other problems.</li></ul>What are the side effects of chemotherapy?<p>Chemotherapy does not just destroy cancer cells. It can also harm some healthy cells, which causes side effects.</p><p>You may have a lot of side effects, some side effects, or none at all. It depends on the type and amount of chemotherapy you get and how your body reacts.</p><p>Some common side effects are:</p><ul><li>Mouth sores</li><li>Fatigue</li><li>Nausea and vomiting</li><li>Pain</li><li>Hair loss</li></ul><p>There are ways to prevent or control some side effects. Talk with your health care provider about how to manage them. Healthy cells usually recover after chemotherapy is over, so most side effects go away over time.</p>What can I expect when getting chemotherapy?<p>You may get chemotherapy in a hospital, at home, at your provider's office, or a medical clinic. You might be given the medicines by mouth, in a shot, as a cream, through a catheter (a thin tube), or intravenously (by IV).</p><p>Your treatment plan will depend on the type of cancer you have, which chemotherapy medicines are used, the treatment goals, and how your body responds to the medicines.</p><p>Chemotherapy may be given alone or with other treatments. You may get treatment every day, every week, or every month. You may have breaks between treatments so that your body has a chance to build new healthy cells.</p><p>NIH: National Cancer Institute</p></content>

    <document rank="21" url="https://medlineplus.gov/childhoodleukemia.html">
      <content name="title">Childhood Leukemia</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Leukemia, Childhood</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is leukemia?<p>Leukemia is a term for cancers of the blood cells. Leukemia starts in blood-forming tissues such as the bone marrow. Your bone marrow makes the cells which will develop into white blood cells, red blood cells, and platelets. Each type of cell has a different job:</p><ul><li>White blood cells help your body fight infection</li><li>Red blood cells deliver oxygen from your lungs to your tissues and organs</li><li>Platelets help form clots to stop bleeding</li></ul><p>When you have leukemia, your bone marrow makes large numbers of abnormal cells. This problem most often happens with white blood cells. These abnormal cells build up in your bone marrow and blood. They crowd out the healthy blood cells and make it hard for your cells and blood to do their work.</p>What are the types of leukemia in children?<p>There are different types of leukemia. Some types are acute (fast growing). They usually get worse quickly if they are not treated. Most childhood leukemias are acute:</p><ul><li>Acute lymphocytic leukemia (ALL), which is the most common type of leukemia in children and the most common cancer in children. In ALL, the bone marrow makes too many lymphocytes, a type of white blood cell.</li><li>Acute myeloid leukemia (AML), which happens when bone marrow makes abnormal myeloblasts (a type of white blood cell), red blood cells, or platelets.</li></ul><p>Other types of leukemia are chronic (slow growing). They usually get worse over a longer period of time. They are rare in children:</p><ul><li>Chronic lymphocytic leukemia (CLL), in which the bone marrow makes abnormal lymphocytes (a type of white blood cell). It is more common in teens than children.</li><li>Chronic myeloid leukemia (CML), in which the bone marrow makes abnormal granulocytes (a type of white blood cell). It is very rare in children.</li></ul><p>There are some other rare types of leukemia in children, including juvenile myelomonocytic leukemia (JMML).</p>What causes leukemia in children?<p>Leukemia happens when there are changes in the genetic material (DNA) in bone marrow cells. The cause of these genetic changes is unknown. However, there are certain factors that raise the risk of childhood leukemia.</p>Who is at risk for leukemia in children?<p>The factors that raise the risk of childhood leukemia include:</p><ul><li>Having a brother or sister, especially a twin, with leukemia</li><li>Past treatment with chemotherapy</li><li>Exposure to radiation, including radiation therapy</li><li>Having certain genetic conditions, such as 
	<ul><li>Ataxia telangiectasia</li><li>Down syndrome</li><li>Fanconi <span class="qt0">anemia</span></li><li>Li-Fraumeni syndrome</li><li>Neurofibromatosis type 1</li></ul>
</li></ul><p>There are other factors that may raise the risk of getting one or more of the specific types of childhood leukemia.</p>What are the symptoms of leukemia in children?<p>Some of the symptoms of leukemia may include:</p><ul><li>Feeling tired</li><li>Fever or night sweats</li><li>Easy bruising or bleeding</li><li>Weight loss or loss of appetite</li><li>Petechiae, which are tiny red dots under the skin. They are caused by bleeding.</li></ul><p>Other leukemia symptoms can be different from type to type. Chronic leukemia may not cause symptoms at first.</p>How is leukemia in children diagnosed?<p>Your health care provider may use many tools to diagnose leukemia:</p><ul><li>A physical exam</li><li>A medical history</li><li>Blood tests, such as a complete blood count (CBC)</li><li>Bone marrow tests. There are two main types - bone marrow aspiration and bone marrow biopsy. Both tests involve removing a sample of bone marrow and bone. The samples are sent to a lab for testing.</li><li>Genetic tests to look for gene and chromosome changes</li></ul><p>Once there is a diagnosis of leukemia, other tests may be done to see whether the cancer has spread. These include imaging tests and a lumbar puncture, which is a procedure to collect and test cerebrospinal fluid (CSF).</p>What are the treatments for leukemia in children?<p>The treatments for leukemia depend on which type it is, how severe the leukemia is, the child's age, and other factors. Possible treatments might include:</p><ul><li>chemotherapy</li><li>radiation therapy</li><li>Chemotherapy with stem cell transplant</li><li>Targeted therapy, which uses drugs or other substances that attack specific cancer cells with less harm to normal cells</li></ul><p>Treatment for childhood leukemia is often successful. But the treatments can cause complications right away or later in life. Children who survived leukemia will need follow-up care the rest of their lives to watch for and treat any complications they may have.</p><p>NIH: National Cancer Institute</p></content>

    <document rank="22" url="https://medlineplus.gov/chronickidneydisease.html">
      <content name="title">Chronic Kidney Disease</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">CKD</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>You have two kidneys, each about the size of your fist.  Their main job is to filter your blood. They remove wastes and extra water, which become urine. They also keep the body's chemicals balanced, help control blood pressure, and make hormones.</p><p>Chronic kidney disease (CKD) means that your kidneys are damaged and can't filter blood as they should. This damage can cause wastes to build up in your body. It can also cause other problems that can harm your health. Diabetes and high blood pressure are the most common causes of CKD.</p><p>The kidney damage occurs slowly over many years. Many people don't have any symptoms until their kidney disease is very advanced. Blood and urine tests are the only way to know if you have kidney disease.</p><p>Treatments cannot cure kidney disease, but they may slow kidney disease. They include medicines to lower blood pressure, control blood sugar, and lower cholesterol. CKD may still get worse over time. Sometimes it can lead to kidney failure. If your kidneys fail, you will need  dialysis or a kidney transplantation.</p><p>You can take steps to keep your kidneys healthier longer:</p><ul><li>Choose foods with less  salt (sodium)</li><li>Control your blood pressure; your health care provider can tell you what your blood pressure should be</li><li>Keep your blood sugar in the target range, if you have diabetes</li><li>Limit the amount of alcohol you drink</li><li>Choose foods that are healthy for your heart: fruits, vegetables, whole grains, and low-fat dairy foods</li><li>Lose weight if you are overweight</li><li>Be physically active</li><li>Don't smoke</li></ul><p>NIH: National Institute of Diabetes and Digestive and Kidney Diseases</p></content>
      <content name="mesh">Renal Insufficiency, Chronic</content>
      <content name="groupName">Kidneys and Urinary System</content>
      <content name="snippet"> You have two kidneys, each about the size of your fist.  Their main job is to filter your blood. They remove wastes and extra water, which become urine. They  ... </content>
    </document>
    <document rank="23" url="https://medlineplus.gov/chroniclymphocyticleukemia.html">
      <content name="title">Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">CLL</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Leukemia, Chronic Lymphocytic</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is leukemia?<p>Leukemia is a term for cancers of the blood cells. Leukemia starts in blood-forming tissues such as the bone marrow. Your bone marrow makes the cells which will develop into white blood cells, red blood cells, and platelets. Each type of cell has a different job:</p><ul><li>White blood cells help your body fight infection</li><li>Red blood cells deliver oxygen from your lungs to your tissues and organs</li><li>Platelets help form clots to stop bleeding</li></ul><p>When you have leukemia, your bone marrow makes large numbers of abnormal cells. This problem most often happens with white blood cells. These abnormal cells build up in your bone marrow and blood. They crowd out the healthy blood cells and make it hard for your cells and blood to do their work.</p>What is chronic lymphocytic leukemia (CLL)?<p>Chronic lymphocytic leukemia (CLL) is a type of chronic leukemia. "Chronic" means that the leukemia usually gets worse slowly. In CLL, the bone marrow makes abnormal lymphocytes (a type of white blood cell). When the abnormal cells crowd out the healthy cells, it can lead to infection, <span class="qt0">anemia</span>, and easy bleeding. The abnormal cells can also spread outside the blood to other parts of the body.
CLL is one of the most common types of leukemia in adults. It often occurs during or after middle age. It is rare in children.</p>What causes chronic lymphocytic leukemia (CLL)?<p>CLL happens when there are changes in the genetic material (DNA) in bone marrow cells. The cause of these genetic changes is unknown, so it's hard to predict who might get CLL. There are a few factors that might raise your risk.</p>Who is at risk for chronic lymphocytic leukemia (CLL)?<p>It is hard to predict who will get CLL. There are a few factors that could raise your risk:</p><ul><li>Age - your risk goes up as you get older. Most people who are diagnosed with CLL are over 50.</li><li>Family history of CLL and other blood and bone marrow diseases</li><li>Racial/ethnic group - CLL is more common in whites than in people from other racial or ethnic groups</li><li>Exposure to certain chemicals, including Agent Orange, a chemical that was used in the Vietnam War</li></ul>What are the symptoms of chronic lymphocytic leukemia (CLL)?<p>In the beginning, CLL does not cause any symptoms. Later, you can have symptoms such as:</p><ul><li>Swollen lymph nodes - you may notice them as painless lumps in the neck, underarm, stomach, or groin</li><li>Weakness or feeling tired</li><li>Pain or a feeling of fullness below the ribs</li><li>Fever and infection</li><li>Easy bruising or bleeding</li><li>Petechiae, which are tiny red dots under the skin. They are caused by bleeding.</li><li>Weight loss for no known reason</li><li>Drenching night sweats</li></ul>How is chronic lymphocytic leukemia (CLL) diagnosed?<p>Your health care provider may use many tools to diagnose CLL:</p><ul><li>A physical exam</li><li>A medical history</li><li>Blood tests, such as a complete blood count (CBC) with differential and blood chemistry tests. Blood chemistry tests measure different substances in the blood, including electrolytes, fats, proteins, glucose (sugar), and enzymes. Specific blood chemistry tests include a basic metabolic panel (BMP), a comprehensive metabolic panel (CMP), kidney function tests, liver function tests, and an electrolyte panel.</li><li>Flow cytometry tests, which check for leukemia cells and identify which type of leukemia it is. The tests can be done on blood, bone marrow, or other tissue.</li><li>Genetic tests to look for gene and chromosome changes</li></ul><p>If you are diagnosed with CLL, you may have additional tests to see whether the cancer has spread. These include imaging tests and bone marrow tests.</p>What are the treatments for chronic lymphocytic leukemia (CLL)?<p>Treatments for CLL include:</p><ul><li>Watchful waiting, which means that you don't get treatment right away. Your health care provider regularly checks to see if your signs or symptoms appear or change.</li><li>Targeted therapy, which uses drugs or other substances that attack specific cancer cells with less harm to normal cells.</li><li>Chemotherapy</li><li>Radiation therapy</li><li>Immunotherapy</li><li>Chemotherapy with bone marrow or stem cell transplant</li></ul><p>The goals of treatment are to slow the growth of the leukemia cells and to give you long periods of remission. Remission means that the signs and symptoms of cancer are reduced or have disappeared. The CLL may come back after remission, and you may need more treatment.</p><p>NIH: National Cancer Institute</p></content>

    <document rank="24" url="https://medlineplus.gov/chronicmyeloidleukemia.html">
      <content name="title">Chronic Myeloid Leukemia</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">CML</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Chronic Granulocytic Leukemia</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Chronic Myelogenous Leukemia</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Leukemia, Myeloid, Chronic</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is leukemia?<p>Leukemia is a term for cancers of the blood cells. Leukemia starts in blood-forming tissues such as the bone marrow. Your bone marrow makes the cells which will develop into white blood cells, red blood cells, and platelets. Each type of cell has a different job:</p><ul><li>White blood cells help your body fight infection</li><li>Red blood cells deliver oxygen from your lungs to your tissues and organs</li><li>Platelets help form clots to stop bleeding</li></ul><p>When you have leukemia, your bone marrow makes large numbers of abnormal cells. This problem most often happens with white blood cells. These abnormal cells build up in your bone marrow and blood. They crowd out the healthy blood cells and make it hard for your cells and blood to do their work.</p>What is chronic myeloid leukemia (CML)? <p>Chronic myeloid leukemia (CML) is a type of chronic leukemia. "Chronic" means that the leukemia usually gets worse slowly. In CML, the bone marrow makes abnormal granulocytes (a type of white blood cell). These abnormal cells are also called blasts. When the abnormal cells crowd out the healthy cells, it can lead to infection, <span class="qt0">anemia</span>, and easy bleeding. The abnormal cells can also spread outside the blood to other parts of the body.</p><p>CML usually occurs in adults during or after middle age. It is rare in children.</p>What causes chronic myeloid leukemia (CML)?<p>Most people with CML have a genetic change called the Philadelphia chromosome. It's called that because researchers in Philadelphia discovered it. People normally have 23 pairs of chromosomes in each cell. These chromosomes contain your DNA (genetic material). In CML, part of the DNA from one chromosome moves to another chromosome. It combines with some DNA there, which creates a new gene called BCR-ABL. This gene causes your bone marrow to make an abnormal protein. This protein allows the leukemia cells to grow out of control.</p><p>The Philadelphia chromosome isn't passed from parent to child. It happens during your lifetime. The cause is unknown.</p>Who is at risk for chronic myeloid leukemia (CML)?<p>It is hard to predict who will get CML. There are a few factors that could raise your risk:</p><ul><li>Age - your risk goes up as you get older</li><li>CML is slightly more common in men</li><li>Exposure to high-dose radiation</li></ul>What are the symptoms of chronic myeloid leukemia (CML)?<p>Sometimes CML does not cause symptoms. If you do have symptoms, they can include:</p><ul><li>Feeling very tired</li><li>Weight loss for no known reason</li><li>Drenching night sweats</li><li>Fever</li><li>Pain or a feeling of fullness below the ribs on the left side</li></ul>How is chronic myeloid leukemia (CML) diagnosed?<p>Your health care provider may use many tools to diagnose CML:</p><ul><li>A physical exam</li><li>A medical history</li><li>Blood tests, such as a complete blood count (CBC) with differential  and blood chemistry tests. Blood chemistry tests measure different substances in the blood, including electrolytes, fats, proteins, glucose (sugar), and enzymes. Specific blood chemistry tests include a basic metabolic panel (BMP), a comprehensive metabolic panel (CMP), kidney function tests, liver function tests, and an electrolyte panel.</li><li>Bone marrow tests. There are two main types - bone marrow aspiration and bone marrow biopsy. Both tests involve removing a sample of bone marrow and bone. The samples are sent to a lab for testing.</li><li>Genetic tests to look for gene and chromosome changes, including tests to look for the Philadelphia chromosome</li></ul><p>If you are diagnosed with CML, you may have additional tests such as imaging tests to see whether the cancer has spread.</p>What are the phases of chronic myeloid leukemia (CML)?<p>CML has three phases. The phases are based on how much the CML has grown or spread:</p><ul><li>Chronic phase, where less than 10% of cells in the blood and bone marrow are blast cells (leukemia cells). Most people are diagnosed in this phase, and many do not have symptoms. Standard treatment usually helps in this phase.</li><li>Accelerated phase, 10% to 19% of the cells in the blood and bone marrow are blast cells. In this phase, people often have symptoms and standard treatment may not be as effective as in the chronic phase.</li><li>Blastic phase, where 20% or more of the cells in the blood or bone marrow are blast cells. The blast cells have spread to other tissues and organs. If you have tiredness, fever, and an enlarged spleen during the blastic phase, it is called a blast crisis. This phase is harder to treat.</li></ul>What are the treatments for chronic myeloid leukemia (CML)?<p>There are several different treatments for CML:</p><ul><li>Targeted therapy, which uses drugs or other substances that attack specific cancer cells with less harm to normal cells. For CML, the drugs are tyrosine kinase inhibitors (TKIs). They block tyrosine kinase, which is an enzyme that causes your bone marrow to make too many blasts.</li><li>Chemotherapy</li><li>Immunotherapy</li><li>High-dose chemotherapy with stem cell transplant</li><li>Donor lymphocyte infusion (DLI). DLI is a treatment that may be used after a stem cell transplant. It involves giving you an infusion (into your bloodstream) of healthy lymphocytes from the stem cell transplant donor. Lymphocytes are a type of white blood cell. These donor lymphocytes may kill the remaining cancer cells.</li><li>Surgery to remove the spleen (splenectomy)</li></ul><p>Which treatments you get will depend on which phase you are in, your age, your overall health, and other factors. When the signs and symptoms of CML are reduced or have disappeared, it is called remission. The CML may come back after remission, and you may need more treatment.</p><p>NIH: National Cancer Institute</p></content>

    <document rank="0" url="https://medlineplus.gov/pneumonia.html">
      <content name="title"><span class="qt0">Pneumonia</span></content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Bronchopneumonia</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Walking <span class="qt0">Pneumonia</span></content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is <span class="qt0">pneumonia</span>?<p><span class="qt0">Pneumonia</span> is an infection in one or both of the lungs. It causes the air sacs of the lungs to fill up with fluid or pus. It can range from mild to severe, depending on the type of germ causing the infection, your age, and your overall health.</p>What causes <span class="qt0">pneumonia</span>?<p>Bacterial, viral, and fungal infections can cause <span class="qt0">pneumonia</span>.</p><p>Bacteria are the most common cause. Bacterial <span class="qt0">pneumonia</span> can occur on its own. It can also develop after you've had certain viral infections such as a cold or the flu. Several different types of bacteria can cause <span class="qt0">pneumonia</span>, including:</p><ul><li>Streptococcus pneumoniae</li><li>Legionella pneumophila; this <span class="qt0">pneumonia</span> is often called Legionnaires' disease</li><li>Mycoplasma pneumoniae</li><li>Chlamydia pneumoniae</li><li>Haemophilus influenzae</li></ul><p>Viruses that infect the respiratory tract may cause <span class="qt0">pneumonia</span>. Viral <span class="qt0">pneumonia</span> is often mild and goes away on its own within a few weeks. But sometimes it is serious enough that you need to get treatment in a hospital. If you have viral <span class="qt0">pneumonia</span>, you are at risk of also getting bacterial <span class="qt0">pneumonia</span>. The different viruses that can cause <span class="qt0">pneumonia</span> include:</p><ul><li>Respiratory syncytial virus (RSV)</li><li>Some common cold and flu viruses</li><li>SARS-CoV-2, the virus that causes COVID-19</li></ul><p>Fungal <span class="qt0">pneumonia</span> is more common in people who have chronic health problems or weakened immune systems. Some of the types include:</p><ul><li>Pneumocystis <span class="qt0">pneumonia</span> (PCP)</li><li>Coccidioidomycosis, which causes valley fever</li><li>Histoplasmosis</li><li>Cryptococcus</li></ul>Who is at risk for <span class="qt0">pneumonia</span>?<p>Anyone can get <span class="qt0">pneumonia</span>, but certain factors can increase your risk:</p><ul><li>Age; the risk is higher for children who are age 2 and under and adults age 65 and older</li><li>Exposure to certain chemicals, pollutants, or toxic fumes</li><li>Lifestyle habits, such as smoking, heavy alcohol use, and malnourishment</li><li>Being in a hospital, especially if you are in the ICU. Being sedated and/or on a ventilator raises the risk even more.</li><li>Having a lung disease</li><li>Having a weakened immune system</li><li>Have trouble coughing or swallowing, from a stroke or other condition</li><li>Recently being sick with a cold or the flu</li></ul>What are the symptoms of <span class="qt0">pneumonia</span>?<p>The symptoms of <span class="qt0">pneumonia</span> can range from mild to severe and include:</p><ul><li>Fever</li><li>Chills</li><li>Cough, usually with phlegm (a slimy substance from deep in your lungs)</li><li>Shortness of breath</li><li>Chest pain when you breathe or cough</li><li>Nausea and/or vomiting</li><li>Diarrhea</li></ul><p>The symptoms can vary for different groups. Newborns and infants may not show any signs of the infection. Others may vomit and have a fever and cough. They might seem sick, with no energy, or be restless.</p><p>Older adults and people who have serious illnesses or weak immune systems may have fewer and milder symptoms. They may even have a lower than normal temperature. Older adults who have <span class="qt0">pneumonia</span> sometimes have sudden changes in mental awareness.</p>What other problems can <span class="qt0">pneumonia</span> cause?<p>Sometimes <span class="qt0">pneumonia</span> can cause serious complications such as:</p><ul><li>Bacteremia, which happens when the bacteria move into the bloodstream. It is serious and can lead to septic shock.</li><li>Lung abscesses, which are collections of pus in cavities of the lungs</li><li>Pleural disorders, which are conditions that affect the pleura. The pleura is the tissue that covers the outside of the lungs and lines the inside of your chest cavity.</li><li>Kidney failure</li><li>Respiratory failure</li></ul>How is <span class="qt0">pneumonia</span> diagnosed?<p>Sometimes <span class="qt0">pneumonia</span> can be hard to diagnose. This is because it can cause some of the same symptoms as a cold or the flu. It may take time for you to realize that you have a more serious condition.</p><p>Your health care provider may use many tools to make a diagnosis:</p><ul><li>A medical history, which includes asking about your symptoms</li><li>A physical exam, including listening to your lungs with a stethoscope</li><li>Various tests, such as
		<ul><li>A chest x-ray</li><li>Blood tests such as a complete blood count (CBC) to see if your immune system is actively fighting an infection</li><li>A Blood culture to find out whether you have a bacterial infection that has spread to your bloodstream</li></ul>
</li></ul><p>If you are in the hospital, have serious symptoms, are older, or have other health problems, you may also have more tests, such as:</p><ul><li>Sputum test, which checks for bacteria in a sample of your sputum (spit) or phlegm (slimy substance from deep in your lungs).</li><li>Chest CT scan to see how much of your lungs is affected. It may also show if you have complications such as lung abscesses or pleural effusions.</li><li>Pleural fluid culture, which checks for bacteria in a fluid sample that was taken from the pleural space</li><li>Pulse oximetry or blood oxygen level test, to check how much oxygen is in your blood</li><li>Bronchoscopy, a procedure used to look inside your lungs' airways</li></ul>What are the treatments for <span class="qt0">pneumonia</span>?<p>Treatment for <span class="qt0">pneumonia</span> depends on the type of <span class="qt0">pneumonia</span>, which germ is causing it, and how severe it is:</p><ul><li>Antibiotics treat bacterial <span class="qt0">pneumonia</span> and some types of fungal <span class="qt0">pneumonia</span>. They do not work for viral <span class="qt0">pneumonia</span>.</li><li>In some cases, your provider may prescribe antiviral medicines for viral <span class="qt0">pneumonia</span></li><li>Antifungal medicines treat other types of fungal <span class="qt0">pneumonia</span></li></ul><p>You may need to be treated in a hospital if your symptoms are severe or if you are at risk for complications. While there, you may get additional treatments. For example, if your blood oxygen level is low, you may receive oxygen therapy.</p><p>It may take time to recover from <span class="qt0">pneumonia</span>. Some people feel better within a week. For other people, it can take a month or more.</p>Can <span class="qt0">pneumonia</span> be prevented?<p>Vaccines can help prevent <span class="qt0">pneumonia</span> caused by pneumococcal bacteria or the flu virus. Having good hygiene, not smoking, and having a healthy lifestyle may also help prevent <span class="qt0">pneumonia</span>.</p><p>NIH: National Heart, Lung, and Blood Institute</p></content>

    <document rank="1" url="https://medlineplus.gov/pneumocystisinfections.html">
      <content name="title">Pneumocystis Infections</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is Pneumocystis jirovecii?<p>Pneumocystis jirovecii is a fungus that is common throughout the world. Many people have been exposed to it as children. Some people have the fungus in their lungs. If you are healthy, it usually will not make you sick. But it can make you sick if you have a weakened immune system, for example if you:</p><ul><li>Have HIV</li><li>Have an autoimmune disease</li><li>Have cancer</li><li>Have a chronic (long-lasting) lung disease</li><li>Had an organ or bone marrow transplant</li><li>Take medicines which weaken your immune system, such as chemotherapy and steroids</li></ul>How does Pneumocystis jirovecii spread? <p>Pneumocystis jirovecii can spread from person to person through the air. You can spread it to others even if it isn't making you sick.</p>What is pneumocystis <span class="qt0">pneumonia</span> (PCP)? <p>The most common type of infection that Pneumocystis jirovecii causes is pneumocystis <span class="qt0">pneumonia</span> (PCP). <span class="qt0">Pneumonia</span> is an infection in one or both of the lungs. It causes the air sacs of the lungs to fill up with fluid or pus.</p>What are the symptoms of pneumocystis <span class="qt0">pneumonia</span> (PCP)? <p>The symptoms of PCP can develop over several days or weeks. They may include:</p><ul><li>Fever</li><li>Cough</li><li>Trouble breathing</li><li>Chest pain</li><li>Chills</li><li>Fatigue</li></ul><p>Contact your healthcare provider if you have symptoms that you think are related to PCP. Since PCP can be life-threatening, it's important to get early treatment.</p>How is pneumocystis <span class="qt0">pneumonia</span> (PCP) diagnosed? <p>To find out if you have PCP, your provider will take a sample of fluid or tissue from your lungs. You may cough up the sample (called sputum), have fluid collected during a bronchoalveolar lavage, or have tissue taken with a biopsy. They will send the sample to the lab to check for Pneumocystis.</p>What are the treatments for pneumocystis <span class="qt0">pneumonia</span> (PCP)? <p>The treatment for PCP is with antibiotics, either by mouth or intravenously (by IV). You need to take them for about 3 weeks. If you have a severe case of PCP, you might need treatment in the hospital.</p>Can pneumocystis <span class="qt0">pneumonia</span> (PCP) be prevented? <p>There is no vaccine to prevent PCP. Some people who are at high risk of getting PCP may need to take antibiotics to prevent it.</p><p>Centers for Disease Control and Prevention</p></content>

    <document rank="2" url="https://medlineplus.gov/legionnairesdisease.html">
      <content name="title">Legionnaires' Disease</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Legionellosis</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is Legionnaires' Disease? <p>Legionnaires' disease is a serious type of <span class="qt0">pneumonia</span> caused by Legionella bacteria. The disease got its name when a group of people at an American Legion convention became ill with this type of <span class="qt0">pneumonia</span>.</p><p>Legionella is found naturally in freshwater, such as lakes and rivers. It can also be found in soil. But people usually only get sick from it when if it grows and spreads in man-made water systems. These systems can include hot tubs, fountains, and the plumbing systems of large buildings, such as hotels or nursing homes.</p><p>Legionella can also cause Pontiac fever. This is a milder, flu-like illness that doesn't infect your lungs.</p><p></p>What causes Legionnaires' disease?<p></p><p>You usually can't get infected by drinking water that contains the bacteria unless you aspirate it (accidentally breathe the water into your lungs). The bacteria don't spread from person to person. Disease outbreaks happen when many people are exposed to the same water source that contains the bacteria.</p>Who is more likely to develop Legionnaires' disease?<p>Most healthy people who come in contact with the bacteria do not become sick. You are more likely to develop an infection if you:</p><ul><li>Are over the age of 50</li><li>Are a current or former smoker</li><li>Have a chronic (long-lasting) disease such as diabetes or kidney failure</li><li>Have a chronic lung disease such as COPD or emphysema</li><li>Have a weakened immune system due to a disease such as HIV or cancer or are taking medicines that suppress your immune system</li><li>Live in a long-term care facility</li><li>Have recently stayed in a hospital or had surgery requiring anesthesia</li></ul>What are the symptoms of Legionnaires' disease? <p>The symptoms usually show up 2 to 14 days after you were exposed to Legionella bacteria. Most of the symptoms are similar to those of other types of <span class="qt0">pneumonia</span>. But Legionnaires' disease may also cause gastrointestinal symptoms and mental changes.</p><p>The symptoms of Legionnaires' disease may include:</p><ul><li>Cough</li><li>High fever</li><li>Chills</li><li>Headache</li><li>Chest pain</li><li>Shortness of breath</li><li>Fatigue</li><li>Nausea and vomiting</li><li>Diarrhea</li><li>Confusion</li></ul>What other problems can Legionnaires' disease cause?<p>Legionnaires' disease can cause life-threatening complications such as respiratory failure, kidney failure, and sepsis.</p><p>About 1 out of every 10 people who get sick with Legionnaires' disease will die from complications of the illness.</p>How is Legionnaires' disease diagnosed?<p>To find out if you have Legionnaires' disease, your health care provider will probably order a chest x-ray to see if you have <span class="qt0">pneumonia</span>. If you do have it, lab tests such as a urine or sputum test can check for the Legionella bacteria. Your provider may also order a blood test to see if you have been exposed to the bacteria.</p>What are the treatments for Legionnaires' disease?<p>If you have Legionnaires' disease, you will likely need care in a hospital. The treatment is with antibiotics. Most people will recover, especially if they were healthy when they got the disease. Getting treatment early can increase the chances of getting better.</p>Can Legionnaires' disease be prevented?<p>You can lower your risk of getting Legionnaires' disease if you:</p><ul><li>Clean all your water-related devices regularly. This could include portable humidifiers, water heaters, air conditioners, and nasal rinses.</li><li>If you haven't used your faucets and showerheads for a week or more, flush them by letting the hot and cold faucets run for at least two minutes before use.</li><li>Avoid exposure to water sources such as decorative foundations or hot tubs if you don't know how well they are maintained.</li><li>Use windshield cleaner in your wiper fluid tank in your car instead of water.</li><li>Ask about how well your buildings' water systems are maintained.</li></ul><p>Centers for Disease Control and Prevention</p></content>

    <document rank="3" url="https://medlineplus.gov/hivandinfections.html">
      <content name="title">HIV and Infections</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">AIDS and Infections</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Opportunistic Infections in AIDS</content>
      <content name="altTitle">AIDS-related opportunistic infections</content>
      <content name="altTitle">OIs</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>Having HIV/AIDS weakens your body's immune system. It destroys the white blood cells that fight infection. This puts you at risk for opportunistic infections (OIs). OIs are serious infections that take advantage of your weak immune system. These infections are less common and less severe in healthy people.</p><p>There are many types of OIs:</p><ul><li>Bacterial infections, including tuberculosis and a serious related disease, Mycobacterium avium complex (MAC)</li><li>Viral infections, such as cytomegalovirus (CMV) and hepatitis C</li><li>Fungal infections, like yeast infections, cryptococcal meningitis, pneumocystis carinii <span class="qt0">pneumonia</span> (PCP) and histoplasmosis</li><li>Parasitic infections, such as crypto (cryptosporidiosis) and toxo (toxoplasmosis)</li></ul><p>Having HIV/AIDS can make infections harder to treat. People with HIV/AIDS are also more likely to have complications from common illnesses such as the flu.</p><p>You can help prevent infections by taking your HIV/AIDS medicines. Other things that can help include practicing safe sex, washing your hands well and often, and cooking your food thoroughly.</p></content>
      <content name="mesh">AIDS-Related Opportunistic Infections</content>
      <content name="groupName">Infections</content>

    <document rank="4" url="https://medlineplus.gov/smoking.html">
      <content name="title">Smoking</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Nicotine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Tobacco Smoking</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Cigar smoking</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Cigarette smoking</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Pipe smoking</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What are the health effects of smoking?<p>There's no way around it; smoking is bad for your health. It harms nearly every organ of the body, even some that you would not expect. Cigarette smoking causes many cancers and other health problems. It is also the cause of nearly one in five deaths in the United States.</p><p>Some of the many health problems that smoking can cause include:</p><ul><li>Cancers. Smoking is the most common cause of lung and oral cancers. But it can also cause cancer in many other parts of your body, such as in your larynx (voice box), esophagus, throat, bladder, kidney, liver, stomach, pancreas, colon and rectum, and cervix. It can also cause acute myeloid leukemia (AML). </li><li>Lung diseases. Smoking is the most common cause of COPD. It can also worsen asthma and raise your risk of <span class="qt0">pneumonia</span>.</li><li>Cardiovascular diseases.  Smoking can damage your blood vessels and make them thicken and grow narrower. This makes your heart  beat faster and raises your blood pressure. Smoking also increases your risk of blood clots and stroke.</li><li>Vision problems. Smoking can raise your risk of  cataracts and cause macular degeneration (AMD).</li></ul><p>You have a greater chance of certain pregnancy problems if you smoke while pregnant.   Your baby is also at higher risk of dying of sudden infant death syndrome (SIDS).</p><p>Smoking also causes addiction to nicotine, a stimulant drug that is in tobacco. Nicotine addiction makes it much harder for people to quit smoking.</p>What are the health risks of secondhand smoke?<p>Your smoke is also bad for other people. If they breathe in your secondhand smoke, they can get many of the same problems as smokers do. These problems can include heart disease and lung cancer. Children exposed to secondhand smoke have a higher risk of ear infections, colds, <span class="qt0">pneumonia</span>, bronchitis, and more severe asthma. If you breathe secondhand smoke while pregnant, you're more likely to have preterm labor and a baby with low birth weight.</p>Are other forms of tobacco also dangerous?<p>Besides cigarettes, there are several other forms of tobacco. Some people smoke tobacco in cigars and water pipes (hookahs). These forms of tobacco also contain harmful chemicals and nicotine. Some cigars contain as much tobacco as an entire pack of cigarettes.</p><p>E-cigarettes often look like cigarettes, but they work differently. They are battery-operated smoking devices. Using an e-cigarette is called vaping. Researchers still have a lot to learn about the health effects of using e-cigarettes. We do know that they contain nicotine, which is highly addictive and a health danger to you and your fetus if you are pregnant, children, and teens. And e-cigarettes also expose non-smokers to secondhand aerosols (rather than secondhand smoke), which contain harmful chemicals.</p><p>Smokeless tobacco, such as chewing tobacco and snuff, is also bad for your health. Smokeless tobacco can cause certain cancers, including oral cancer. It also increases your risk of getting heart disease, gum disease, and oral lesions.</p>Why should I quit?<p>Remember, there is no safe level of tobacco use. Smoking even just one cigarette per day over a lifetime can cause smoking-related cancers and premature death. Quitting smoking can reduce your risk of health problems and add years to your life. The earlier you quit, the greater the benefit. Some immediate benefits of quitting include:</p><ul><li>Lower heart rate and blood pressure</li><li>Less carbon monoxide in the blood (carbon monoxide reduces the blood's ability to carry oxygen)</li><li>Better circulation</li><li>Less coughing and wheezing</li></ul><p>Quitting smoking can be challenging, but it is so important for your health. Contact your health care provider if you need help quitting.</p></content>

      <content name="mesh">Pipe Smoking</content>

    <document rank="5" url="https://medlineplus.gov/staphylococcalinfections.html">
      <content name="title">Staphylococcal Infections</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Vancomycin-Resistant Staphylococcus aureus</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Staph</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What are Staphylococcal (staph) infections?<p>Staphylococcus (staph) is a group of bacteria. There are more than 30 types. A type called Staphylococcus aureus causes most infections.</p><p>Staph bacteria can cause many different types of infections, including:</p><ul><li>Skin infections, which are the most common types of staph infections.</li><li>Bacteremia, an infection of the bloodstream. This can lead to sepsis, a very serious immune response to infection.</li><li>Bone infections.</li><li>Endocarditis, an infection of the inner lining of the heart chambers and valves.</li><li>Food poisoning.</li><li><span class="qt0">Pneumonia</span>.</li><li>Toxic shock syndrome (TSS), a life-threatening condition caused by toxins from certain types of bacteria.</li></ul>What causes staph infections?<p>Some people carry staph bacteria on their skin or in their noses, but they do not get an infection. But if they get a cut or wound, the bacteria can enter the body and cause an infection.</p><p>Staph bacteria can spread from person to person. They can also spread on objects, such as towels, clothing, door handles, athletic equipment, and remotes. If you have staph and do not handle food properly when you are preparing it, you can also spread staph to others.</p>Who is more likely to get a staph infection?<p>Anyone can develop a staph infection, but certain people are more likely to get one, including those who:</p><ul><li>Have a chronic condition such as diabetes, cancer, vascular disease, eczema, and lung disease</li><li>Have a weakened immune system, such as from HIV, medicines to prevent organ rejection, or chemotherapy</li><li>Had surgery</li><li>Are in the hospital</li><li>Use a catheter, breathing tube, or feeding tube</li><li>Have an implanted device, such as a pacemaker or artificial joint or heart valve</li><li>Have burns, especially if they are deep or cover a large area of the body</li><li>Are on dialysis</li><li>Inject illegal drugs</li><li>Do contact sports, since you may have skin-to-skin contact with others or share equipment</li></ul>What are the symptoms of staph infections?<p>The symptoms of a staph infection depend on the type of infection:</p><ul><li>Skin infections can look like pimples or boils. They may be red, swollen, and painful. Sometimes there is pus or other drainage. They can turn into impetigo, which turns into a crust on the skin, or cellulitis, a swollen, red area of skin that feels hot.</li><li>Bone infections can cause pain, swelling, warmth, and redness in the infected area. You may also have chills and a fever.</li><li>Endocarditis causes some flu-like symptoms: fever, chills, and fatigue. It also causes symptoms such as rapid heartbeat, shortness of breath, and fluid buildup in your arms or legs.</li><li>Food poisoning typically causes nausea and vomiting, diarrhea, and fever. If you lose too many fluids, you may also become dehydrated. </li><li><span class="qt0">Pneumonia</span> symptoms include a high fever, chills, and cough that doesn't get better. You may also have chest pain and shortness of breath.</li><li>Toxic shock syndrome (TSS) causes high fever, sudden low blood pressure, vomiting, diarrhea, and confusion. You may have a sunburn-like rash somewhere on your body. TSS can lead to organ failure.</li></ul>How are staph infections diagnosed?<p>Your health care provider will do a physical exam and ask about your symptoms. Often, providers can tell if you have a staph skin infection by looking at it. To check for other types of staph infections, providers may do a culture, with a skin scraping, tissue sample, stool sample, or throat or nasal swabs. There may be other tests, such as imaging tests, depending on the type of infection.</p>What are the treatments for staph infections?<p>Treatment for staph infections is antibiotics. Depending on the type of infection, the antibiotics might be a cream, ointment, medicines (to swallow), or intravenous (IV) medicine. If you have an infected wound, your provider might drain it. Sometimes you may need surgery for bone infections.</p><p>Some staph infections, such as MRSA (methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus), are resistant to many antibiotics. There are still certain antibiotics that can treat these infections.</p>Can staph infections be prevented?<p>Certain steps can help to prevent staph infections:</p><ul><li>Use good hygiene, including washing your hands often.</li><li>Don't share towels, sheets, or clothing with someone who has a staph infection.</li><li>It's best not to share athletic equipment. If you do need to share, make sure that it is properly cleaned and dried before you use it.</li><li>Practice food safety, including not preparing food for others when you have a staph infection.</li><li>If you have a cut or wound, keep it covered.</li></ul></content>

    <document rank="6" url="https://medlineplus.gov/streptococcalinfections.html">
      <content name="title">Streptococcal Infections</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Rheumatic Fever</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Scarlet Fever</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Strep Throat</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Strep</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>Strep is short for Streptococcus, a type of bacteria. There are several types. Two of them cause most of the strep infections in people: group A and group B.</p><p>Group A strep causes:</p><ul><li>Strep throat - a sore, red throat. Your tonsils may be swollen and have white spots on them.</li><li>Scarlet fever - an illness that follows strep throat. It causes a red rash on the body.</li><li>Impetigo - a skin infection</li><li>Toxic shock syndrome</li><li>Cellulitis and necrotizing fasciitis (flesh-eating disease)</li></ul><p>Group B strep can cause blood infections, <span class="qt0">pneumonia</span> and meningitis in newborns. A screening test during pregnancy can tell if you have it. If you do, intravenous (IV) antibiotics during labor can save your baby's life.  Adults can also get group B strep infections, especially if they are 65 or older or already have health problems. Strep B can cause urinary tract infections, blood infections, skin infections and <span class="qt0">pneumonia</span> in adults.</p><p>Antibiotics are used to treat strep infections.</p></content>

A screening test during  ... urinary tract infections, blood infections, skin infections and <span class="qt0">pneumonia</span> in adults. Antibiotics are used to treat strep  ... </content>

    <document rank="7" url="https://medlineplus.gov/birdflu.html">
      <content name="title">Bird Flu</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Avian Influenza</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Avian flu</content>
      <content name="altTitle">H5N1</content>
      <content name="altTitle">H7N9</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is bird flu?<p>Birds, just like people, can get the flu. Another name for bird flu is avian influenza. The viruses that cause bird flu normally only infect birds, including chickens, other poultry, and wild birds such as ducks. But sometimes the viruses can infect other animals and, in rare cases, people.</p><p>A few types of these viruses have caused most of the infections in people. They are the H5N1, H7N9, and H5N6 viruses. These infections in people have mainly been in Asia, Africa, Europe, the Pacific, and the Near East. Although it's very rare, there have also been some infections in people in the United States.</p>How do you get bird flu?<p>The most common ways you can get bird flu are from:</p><ul><li>Touching your eyes, nose, or mouth after handling infected live or dead birds</li><li>Touching surfaces or handling items contaminated by bird flu viruses and then touching your eyes, nose, or mouth</li><li>Breathing in droplets or dust contaminated with the virus</li></ul><p>It's also possible (but very rare) to get bird flu from:</p><ul><li>Another type of animal who has bird flu. Bird flu can also infect many other animals, including some dogs, cats, certain wild and zoo animals, and livestock such as cattle. These animals can then spread the flu to people.</li><li>Another person.</li><li>Eating poultry, eggs, and beef that were not properly handled and cooked.</li><li>Drinking raw milk.</li></ul>Who is more likely to get bird flu?<p>Certain people may be more likely to get bird flu, including:</p><ul><li>Poultry workers</li><li>Animal handlers</li><li>Wildlife biologists</li><li>Disease control workers</li><li>Research laboratory workers</li><li>Veterinarians</li><li>People who travel to countries where bird flu is present</li></ul>What are the symptoms of bird flu in humans?<p>Sometimes bird flu doesn't cause any symptoms. But if you do feel sick, your symptoms can range from mild to severe. Often, the symptoms are similar to the (seasonal) flu, such as:</p><ul><li>Fever (but not everyone has a fever)</li><li>Cough</li><li>Sore throat</li><li>Runny or stuffy nose</li><li>Muscle or body aches</li><li>Fatigue</li><li>Headaches</li><li>Eye redness (conjunctivitis)</li><li>Trouble breathing</li><li>Diarrhea</li></ul><p>People with severe illness from bird flu may have <span class="qt0">pneumonia</span> and might need to be hospitalized.</p>How is bird flu diagnosed?<p>Laboratory testing is used to diagnose bird flu. It's usually done with a nasal or throat swab. This testing is more accurate when the swab is collected during the first few days of illness.</p><p>For people who are severely ill, health care providers may do testing of a different sample, such as fluid taken during a bronchoalveolar lavage or other procedure.</p>What are the treatments for bird flu?<p>Bird flu is treated with antiviral medicines. It's important to get them as soon as possible. The medicines may make your illness less severe.</p><p>You may also be given antiviral medicines if you were exposed to a person or animal who has the virus. This may help prevent you from getting sick.</p>Can bird flu be prevented?<p>There is currently no vaccine available to the public. The government has developed a virus that is similar to some H5N1 viruses. The virus could be used to produce a vaccine for people, if needed.</p><p>It's important to take precautions to prevent bird flu:</p><ul><li>If you have a job or pastime that puts you in contact with birds or other animals, make sure to use proper protective equipment.</li><li>Otherwise try to avoid direct contact with wild birds and other animals.</li><li>Wash your hands with soap and water after touching birds or other animals.</li><li>Since it's possible to get bird flu through some foods, make sure to handle and cook your food safely and avoid raw milk.</li></ul><p>Centers for Disease Control and Prevention</p></content>

    <document rank="8" url="https://medlineplus.gov/breathingproblems.html">
      <content name="title">Breathing Problems</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Dyspnea</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Shortness of Breath</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Tachypnea</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>When you're short of breath, it's hard or uncomfortable for you to take in the oxygen your body needs. You may feel as if you're not getting enough air. Sometimes you can have mild breathing problems because of a stuffy nose or intense exercise. But shortness of breath can also be a sign of a serious disease.</p><p>Many conditions can make you feel short of breath:</p><ul><li>Lung conditions such as asthma, emphysema, or <span class="qt0">pneumonia</span></li><li>Problems with your trachea or bronchi, which are part of your airway system</li><li>Heart disease can make you feel breathless if your heart cannot pump enough blood to supply oxygen to your body</li><li>Anxiety and panic attacks</li><li>Allergies</li></ul><p>If you often have trouble breathing, it is important to find out the cause.</p></content>

    <document rank="9" url="https://medlineplus.gov/chestpain.html">
      <content name="title">Chest Pain</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Pain, Chest</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>Having a pain in your chest can be scary.  It does not always mean that you are having a heart attack.  There can be many other causes, including:</p><ul><li>Other heart problems, such as angina</li><li>Panic attacks</li><li>Digestive problems, such as heartburn or esophagus disorders</li><li>Sore muscles</li><li>Lung diseases, such as <span class="qt0">pneumonia</span>, pleurisy, or pulmonary embolism</li><li>Costochondritis - an inflammation of joints in your chest</li></ul><p>Some of these problems can be serious.  Get immediate medical care if you have chest pain that does not go away, crushing pain or pressure in the chest, or chest pain along with nausea, sweating, dizziness or shortness of breath. Treatment depends on the cause of the pain.</p></content>
