    <document rank="10" url="https://medlineplus.gov/chlamydiainfections.html">
      <content name="title">Chlamydia Infections</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is chlamydia?<p>Chlamydia is a common sexually transmitted infection (STI). It is caused by bacteria called Chlamydia trachomatis. Anyone can get chlamydia. It often doesn't cause symptoms, so people may not know that they have it. Antibiotics can cure it. But if it's not treated, chlamydia can cause serious health problems. </p>How is chlamydia spread?<p>You can get chlamydia during oral, vaginal, or anal sex with someone who has chlamydia. Chlamydia can also be passed to the baby during  childbirth.</p><p>If you've had chlamydia and were treated in the past, you can get re-infected if you have unprotected sex with someone who has it.</p>Who is more likely to get chlamydia?<p>Chlamydia is more common in young people, especially young women. You are more likely to get infected with chlamydia if you don't consistently use a condom or if you have multiple partners.</p>What are the symptoms of chlamydia?<p>Chlamydia doesn't usually cause any symptoms. So you may not realize that you have it. But even if you don't have symptoms, you can still pass the infection to others.</p><p>If you do have symptoms, they may not appear until several weeks after you have sex with someone who has chlamydia.</p><p>Symptoms in women include:</p><ul><li>Abnormal vaginal discharge, which may have a strong smell</li><li>A burning sensation when urinating</li></ul><p>If the infection spreads, you might get lower abdominal (belly) pain, pain during sex, nausea, and fever.</p><p>Symptoms in men include:</p><ul><li>Discharge from your penis</li><li>A burning sensation when urinating (peeing)</li><li>Pain and swelling in one or both testicles (although this is less common)</li></ul><p>If the chlamydia infects the rectum (in men or women), it can cause rectal pain, discharge, and bleeding.</p>How is chlamydia diagnosed?<p>There are lab tests to diagnose chlamydia. Your health care provider may ask you to provide a urine sample. Or your provider may use (or ask you to use) a cotton swab to get a sample from your vagina to test for chlamydia.</p>Who should be tested for chlamydia?<p>You should go to your provider for a test if you have symptoms of chlamydia or if you have a partner who has an STI. If you are pregnant, you should get a test when you go to your first prenatal visit.</p><p>People at higher risk should get checked for chlamydia every year:</p><ul><li>Sexually active women 25 and younger</li><li>Older women who have new or multiple sex partners or a sex partner who has an STI</li><li>Men who have sex with men (MSM)</li></ul>What other problems can chlamydia cause?<p>In women, an untreated infection can spread to your uterus and fallopian tubes, causing pelvic inflammatory disease (PID). PID can cause permanent damage to your reproductive system. This can lead to long-term pelvic pain, infertility, and ectopic pregnancy. Women who have had chlamydia infections more than once are at higher risk of serious reproductive health complications.</p><p>Men often don't have health problems from chlamydia. Sometimes it can infect the epididymis (the tube that carries sperm). This can cause pain, fever, and, rarely, infertility.</p><p>Both men and women can develop reactive arthritis because of a chlamydia infection. Reactive arthritis is a type of arthritis that happens as a "reaction" to an infection in the body.</p><p>Babies born to infected mothers can get eye infections and <span class="qt0">pneumonia</span> from chlamydia. It may also make it more likely for your baby to be born too early.</p><p>Untreated chlamydia may also increase your chances of getting or giving HIV.</p>What are the treatments for chlamydia?<p>Antibiotics will cure the infection. You may get a one-time dose of the antibiotics, or you may need to take medicine every day for 7 days. It is important to take all the medicine that your provider prescribed for you. Antibiotics cannot repair any permanent damage that the disease has caused.</p><p>To prevent spreading the disease to your partner, you should not have sex until the infection has cleared up. If you got a one-time dose of antibiotics, you should wait 7 days after taking the medicine to have sex again. If you have to take medicine every day for 7 days, you should not have sex again until you have finished taking all of the doses of your medicine.</p><p>It is common to get a repeat infection, so you need to get tested again about three months after treatment.</p>Can chlamydia be prevented?<p>The only sure way to prevent chlamydia is to not have vaginal, anal, or oral sex.</p><p>Correct usage of latex condoms greatly reduces, but does not eliminate, the risk of catching or spreading chlamydia. If your or your partner is allergic to latex, you can use polyurethane condoms.</p><p>Centers for Disease Control and Prevention</p></content>

    <document rank="11" url="https://medlineplus.gov/chronicbronchitis.html">
      <content name="title">Chronic Bronchitis</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Bronchitis</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is chronic bronchitis?<p>Chronic bronchitis is a type of COPD (chronic obstructive pulmonary disease). COPD is a group of lung diseases that make it hard to breathe and get worse over time. The other main type of COPD is emphysema. Most people with COPD have both emphysema and chronic bronchitis, but how severe each type is can be different from person to person.</p><p>Chronic bronchitis is inflammation (swelling) and irritation of the bronchial tubes. These tubes are the airways that carry air to and from the air sacs in your lungs. The irritation of the tubes causes mucus to build up. This mucus and the swelling of the tubes make it harder for your lungs to move oxygen in and carbon dioxide out of your body.</p>What causes chronic bronchitis?<p>The cause of chronic bronchitis is usually long-term exposure to irritants that damage your lungs and airways. In the United States, cigarette smoke is the main cause. Pipe, cigar, and other types of tobacco smoke can also cause chronic bronchitis, especially if you inhale them.</p><p>Exposure to other inhaled irritants can contribute to chronic bronchitis. These include secondhand smoke, air pollution, and chemical fumes or dusts from the environment or workplace.</p><p>Rarely, a genetic condition called alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency can play a role in causing chronic bronchitis.</p>Who is at risk for chronic bronchitis?<p>The risk factors for chronic bronchitis include:</p><ul><li>Smoking. This the main risk factor. Up to 75% of people who have chronic bronchitis smoke or used to smoke.</li><li>Long-term exposure to other lung irritants, such as secondhand smoke, air pollution, and chemical fumes and dusts from the environment or workplace.</li><li>Age. Most people who have chronic bronchitis are at least 40 years old when their symptoms begin.</li><li>Genetics. This includes alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency, which is a genetic condition. Also, smokers who get chronic bronchitis are more likely to get it if they have a family history of COPD.</li></ul>What are the symptoms of chronic bronchitis?<p>At first, you may have no symptoms or only mild symptoms. As the disease gets worse, your symptoms usually become more severe. They can include:</p><ul><li>Frequent coughing or a cough that produces a lot mucus</li><li>Wheezing</li><li>A whistling or squeaky sound when you breathe</li><li>Shortness of breath, especially with physical activity</li><li>Tightness in your chest</li></ul><p>Some people with chronic bronchitis get frequent respiratory infections such as colds and the flu. In severe cases, chronic bronchitis can cause weight loss, weakness in your lower muscles, and swelling in your ankles, feet, or legs.</p>How is chronic bronchitis diagnosed?<p>Your health care provider may use many tools to make a diagnosis:</p><ul><li>A medical history, which includes asking about your symptoms</li><li>A family history</li><li>Various tests, such as lung function tests, a chest x-ray or CT scan, and blood tests</li></ul>What are the treatments for chronic bronchitis?<p>There is no cure for chronic bronchitis. However, treatments can help with symptoms, slow the progress of the disease, and improve your ability to stay active. There are also treatments to prevent or treat complications of the disease. Treatments include:</p><ul><li>Lifestyle changes, such as
	<ul><li>Quitting smoking if you are a smoker. This is the most important step you can take to treat chronic bronchitis.</li><li>Avoiding secondhand smoke and places where you might breathe in other lung irritants</li><li>Ask your health care provider for an eating plan that will meet your nutritional needs. Also ask about how much physical activity you can do. Physical activity can strengthen the muscles that help you breathe and improve your overall wellness.</li></ul></li><li>Medicines, such as
	<ul><li>Bronchodilators, which relax the muscles around your airways. This helps open your airways and makes breathing easier. Most bronchodilators are taken through an inhaler. In more severe cases, the inhaler may also contain steroids to reduce inflammation.</li><li>Vaccines for the flu and pneumococcal <span class="qt0">pneumonia</span>, since people with chronic bronchitis are at higher risk for serious problems from these diseases.</li><li>Antibiotics if you get a bacterial or viral lung infection</li></ul></li><li>Oxygen therapy, if you have severe chronic bronchitis and low levels of oxygen in your blood. Oxygen therapy can help you breathe better. You may need extra oxygen all the time or only at certain times.</li><li>Pulmonary rehabilitation, which is a program that helps improve the well-being of people who have chronic breathing problems. It may include</li><ul><li>An exercise program</li><li>Disease management training</li><li>Nutritional counseling</li><li>Psychological counseling</li></ul><li>A lung transplant, as a last resort for people who have severe symptoms that have not gotten better with medicines</li></ul><p>If you have chronic bronchitis, it's important to know when and where to get help for your symptoms. You should get emergency care if you have severe symptoms, such as trouble catching your breath or talking. Call your health care provider if your symptoms are getting worse or if you have signs of an infection, such as a fever.</p>Can chronic bronchitis be prevented?<p>Since smoking causes most cases of chronic bronchitis, the best way to prevent it is to not smoke. It's also important to try to avoid lung irritants such as secondhand smoke, air pollution, chemical fumes, and dusts.</p><p>


</p><p>NIH: National Heart, Lung, and Blood Institute</p></content>

    <document rank="12" url="https://medlineplus.gov/cocaine.html">
      <content name="title">Cocaine</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Crack</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Blow</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Coca</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Coke</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Flake</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Snow</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Soda Cot</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is cocaine?<p>Cocaine is a very addictive drug that is made from leaves of the coca plant found in South America. It is mostly available as an illegal drug that some people use to get high. In rare cases, it is also used as a prescription drug for for anesthesia during certain surgeries.</p><p>As a street (illegal) drug, cocaine is usually a fine, white, crystal powder. Street dealers sometimes mix it with cornstarch, talcum powder, or flour to make it look like they have more cocaine. That way they can make more money. They may also mix it with other illegal drugs. Another form of the drug is crack cocaine. Crack cocaine has been heated to make it into a rock crystal.</p><p></p>How do people use cocaine?<p>People snort cocaine powder through the nose or rub it into their gums. Others dissolve the powder and inject it into the bloodstream. Some people inject a combination of cocaine and heroin, called a "speedball." Crack cocaine is smoked.</p>What are the short-term effects of cocaine?<p>Cocaine is a stimulant that can make people feel like they have more energy and are extra alert. But it can also make people feel restless, irritable, anxious, and paranoid. Large amounts of cocaine can lead to bizarre, unpredictable, and violent behavior.</p><p>Cocaine's effects appear almost immediately and disappear within a few minutes to an hour. How long the effects last and how intense they are will depend on how the person used it.</p><p>In some cases, cocaine can cause very serious health problems such as a heart attack, stroke, or coma.</p>What are the long-term effects of cocaine?<p>People who use cocaine over the long term may develop health problems. Which problems they have will depend on how they used the cocaine:</p><p>
</p><ul><li>Snorting it can lead to a loss of sense of smell, nosebleeds, nasal damage, and trouble swallowing.</li><li>Smoking it can cause a cough, asthma, trouble breathing, and a higher risk of infections like <span class="qt0">pneumonia</span>.</li><li>Injecting it with a needle can lead to skin or soft tissue infections, as well as scarring. It can cause collapsed veins. When a vein collapses, the blood cannot flow through it. Injecting cocaine also puts a person at higher risk of getting diseases such as HIV and hepatitis C.</li></ul><p>Other long-term effects of cocaine use may include malnutrition and movement disorders, including Parkinson's disease.</p><p>Repeated use of cocaine can lead to tolerance. This means users need more and more of the drug to have the same effect. At higher doses over time, the body becomes dependent on cocaine. If someone who is dependent on cocaine stops using it, they will have withdrawal symptoms. These symptoms can include:</p><ul><li>Depression</li><li>Restlessness</li><li>Fatigue</li><li>Increased appetite</li><li>Bad dreams and insomnia</li><li>Slowed thinking</li></ul><p>Repeated use of cocaine can also lead to cocaine use disorder, also called addiction. This is more than physical dependence. It's a chronic (long-lasting) brain disorder. When someone has it, they continue to use cocaine even though it causes problems in their life. Some examples include health problems and not being able to meet responsibilities at work, school, or home. Getting and using cocaine becomes their main purpose in life.</p>Can a person overdose on cocaine?<p>It's possible to overdose on cocaine. This happens when a person uses so much cocaine that it causes a life-threatening reaction or death. Some people use cocaine along with other drugs or alcohol. This can increase the risk of an overdose.</p><p>A cocaine overdose can cause health problems such as:</p><ul><li>Stroke</li><li>Seizures</li><li>irregular heartbeat (arrhythmia)</li><li>Heart attack</li></ul><p>There is no specific medicine to treat an overdose. Health care providers will focus on treating the specific health problems caused by the overdose.</p>What are the treatments for cocaine use disorder?<p>The treatments for cocaine use disorder are different types of behavioral therapies. There are no medicines which can treat it.</p><p>NIH: National Institute on Drug Abuse</p></content>

      <content name="mesh">Cocaine Smoking</content>

    <document rank="13" url="https://medlineplus.gov/collapsedlung.html">
      <content name="title">Collapsed Lung</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Atelectasis</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Pneumothorax</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>A collapsed lung happens when air enters the pleural space, the area between the lung and the chest wall. If it is a total collapse, it is called pneumothorax. If only part of the lung is affected, it is called atelectasis.</p><p>Causes of a collapsed lung include:</p><ul><li>Lung diseases such as <span class="qt0">pneumonia</span> or lung cancer</li><li>Being on a breathing machine</li><li>Surgery on the chest or abdomen</li><li>A blocked airway</li></ul><p>If only a small area of the lung is affected, you may not have symptoms. If a large area is affected, you may feel short of breath and have a rapid heart rate.</p><p>A chest x-ray can tell if you have it. Treatment depends on the underlying cause.</p><p>NIH: National Heart, Lung, and Blood Institute</p></content>
      <content name="mesh">Pulmonary Atelectasis</content>
      <content name="mesh">Pneumothorax</content>
      <content name="groupName">Lungs and Breathing</content>
      <content name="snippet"> ... a collapsed lung include: Lung diseases such as <span class="qt0">pneumonia</span> or lung cancer  Being on a breathing machine   ... </content>

    <document rank="14" url="https://medlineplus.gov/coma.html">
      <content name="title">Coma</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Persistent Vegetative State</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>A coma is a deep state of unconsciousness.   An individual in a coma is alive but unable to move or respond to his or her environment. Coma may occur as a complication of an underlying illness, or as a result of injuries, such as brain injury.</p><p>A coma rarely lasts more than 2 to 4 weeks. The outcome for coma depends on the cause, severity, and site of the damage. People may come out of a coma with physical, intellectual, and psychological problems.  Some people may remain in a coma for years or even decades. For those people, the most common cause of death is infection, such as <span class="qt0">pneumonia</span>.</p><p>NIH: National Institute of Neurological Disorders and Stroke</p></content>
      <content name="mesh">Coma</content>
      <content name="mesh">Persistent Vegetative State</content>
      <content name="groupName">Brain and Nerves</content>

    <document rank="15" url="https://medlineplus.gov/commoncold.html">
      <content name="title">Common Cold</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Cold, Common</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is the common cold?<p>The common cold is a mild infection of your upper respiratory tract (which includes your nose and throat). Colds are probably the most common illness. Adults have an average of 2-3 colds per year, and children have even more. Colds are more common in the winter and spring, but you can get them at any time.</p>What causes the common cold?<p>More than 200 different viruses can cause a cold, but rhinoviruses are the most common type. The viruses that cause colds are very contagious. They can spread from person to person through the air and close personal contact. You can also get infected when you touch something that has the virus on it and then touch your eyes, mouth, or nose. For example, you could get a cold after you shake hands with someone who has a cold or touch a doorknob that has the germs on it, and then touch your face.</p>What are the symptoms of the common cold?<p>The symptoms of a common cold usually include:</p><ul><li>Sneezing</li><li>Stuffy nose (congestion)</li><li>Runny nose</li><li>Sore throat</li><li>Coughing</li><li>Headache</li></ul><p>The symptoms usually start a few days after you become infected with the virus. Some symptoms can last for 10-14 days.</p>What are the treatments for the common cold?<p>There is no cure for the common cold. But there are treatments that can make you feel better while you wait for the cold to go away on its own:</p><ul><li>Getting lots of rest.</li><li>Drinking plenty of fluids.</li><li>Using a clean humidifier or cool mist vaporizer.</li><li>Gargling with warm salt water.</li><li>Using saline nose drops or sprays.</li><li>Taking over-the-counter pain or cold and cough medicines. But you need to be careful with certain medicines:
	<ul><li>Children and teens should not take aspirin.</li><li>Some cold and cough medicines contain ingredients that are not recommended for children. Talk with your child's health care provider before giving your child any cold and cough medicines.</li><li>Some cold and cough medicines contain pain relievers. If you also take a separate pain reliever with these medicines, you could be getting a dangerous amount of the pain reliever. Read the labels on the medicines and follow the instructions carefully. If you have questions, ask your provider or a pharmacist.</li></ul>
</li></ul><p>Antibiotics will not help with a cold. Antibiotics help with bacterial infections, not with viral infections such as colds.</p><p>Most people who have a cold will feel better after a week or two. However, some people who get a cold may develop other illnesses, such as bronchitis or <span class="qt0">pneumonia</span>. This is more common in people with weakened immune systems, asthma, or other respiratory conditions. Contact your provider if you or your child have symptoms that concern you, such as:</p><ul><li>Trouble breathing or fast breathing</li><li>Dehydration</li><li>Fever that lasts longer than 4 days</li><li>Symptoms that last more than 10 days without improvement</li><li>Symptoms, such as fever or cough, that improve but then return or worsen</li><li>Worsening of chronic medical conditions</li></ul>Can the common cold be prevented?<p>There is no vaccine to protect against the common cold. But you may be able to reduce your risk of getting or spreading a cold by:</p><ul><li>Washing your hands often with soap and water for at least 20 seconds.</li><li>Avoiding touching your face, nose, or mouth with unwashed hands.</li><li>Avoiding close contact, such as kissing, shaking hands, and sharing cups and eating utensils, with others if you are sick or they are sick.</li><li>Cleaning and disinfecting surfaces that you frequently touch.</li><li>Covering coughs and sneezes with a tissue. Then throw away the tissue and wash your hands.</li><li>Staying home when sick.</li></ul><p>Centers for Disease Control and Prevention</p></content>

    <document rank="16" url="https://medlineplus.gov/copd.html">
      <content name="title">COPD</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is COPD (chronic obstructive pulmonary disease)?<p>COPD (chronic obstructive pulmonary disease) is a group of lung diseases that make it hard to breathe and get worse over time.</p><p>Normally, the airways and air sacs in your lungs are elastic or stretchy. When you breathe in, the airways bring air to the air sacs. The air sacs fill up with air, like a small balloon. When you breathe out, the air sacs deflate, and the air goes out. If you have COPD, less air flows in and out of your airways because of one or more problems:</p><ul><li>The airways and air sacs in your lungs become less elastic</li><li>The walls between many of the air sacs are destroyed</li><li>The walls of the airways become thick and inflamed</li><li>The airways make more mucus than usual and can become clogged</li></ul>What are the types of COPD (chronic obstructive pulmonary disease)?<p>COPD includes two main types:</p><ul><li>Emphysema affects the air sacs in your lungs, as well as the walls between them. They become damaged and are less elastic.</li><li>Chronic bronchitis, in which the lining of your airways is constantly irritated and inflamed. This causes the lining to swell and make mucus.</li></ul><p>Most people with COPD have both emphysema and chronic bronchitis, but how severe each type is can be different from person to person.</p>What causes COPD (chronic obstructive pulmonary disease)?<p>The cause of COPD is usually long-term exposure to irritants that damage your lungs and airways. In the United States, cigarette smoke is the main cause. Pipe, cigar, and other types of tobacco smoke can also cause COPD, especially if you inhale them.</p><p>Exposure to other inhaled irritants can contribute to COPD. These include secondhand smoke, air pollution, and chemical fumes or dusts from the environment or workplace.</p><p>Rarely, a genetic condition called alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency can play a role in causing COPD.</p>Who is at risk for COPD (chronic obstructive pulmonary disease)?<p>The risk factors for COPD include:</p><ul><li>Smoking. This is the main risk factor. Up to 75% of people who have COPD smoke or used to smoke.</li><li>Long-term exposure to other lung irritants, such as secondhand smoke, air pollution, and chemical fumes and dusts from the environment or workplace</li><li>Age. Most people who have COPD are at least 40 years old when their symptoms begin.</li><li>Genetics. This includes alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency, which is a genetic condition. Also, smokers who get COPD are more likely to get it if they have a family history of COPD.</li><li>Asthma. People who have asthma have more risk of developing COPD than people who don't have asthma. But most people with asthma will not get COPD.</li></ul>What are the symptoms of COPD (chronic obstructive pulmonary disease)?<p>At first, you may have no symptoms or only mild symptoms. As the disease gets worse, your symptoms usually become more severe. They can include:</p><ul><li>Frequent coughing or a cough that produces a lot of mucus</li><li>Wheezing</li><li>A whistling or squeaky sound when you breathe</li><li>Shortness of breath, especially with physical activity</li><li>Tightness in your chest</li></ul><p>Some people with COPD get frequent respiratory infections such as colds and the flu. In severe cases, COPD can cause weight loss, weakness in your lower muscles, and swelling in your ankles, feet, or legs.</p>How is COPD (chronic obstructive pulmonary disease) diagnosed?<p>Your health care provider may use many tools to make a diagnosis:</p><ul><li>A medical history, which includes asking about your symptoms</li><li>A family history</li><li>Various tests, such as lung function tests, a chest x-ray or CT scan, and blood tests</li></ul><p>Your doctor will diagnose COPD based on your signs and symptoms, your medical and family histories, and test results.</p>What are the treatments for COPD (chronic obstructive pulmonary disease)?<p>There is no cure for COPD. However, treatments can help with symptoms, slow the progress of the disease, and improve your ability to stay active. There are also treatments to prevent or treat complications of the disease. Treatments include:</p><ul><li>Lifestyle changes, such as
	<ul><li>Quitting smoking if you are a smoker. This is the most important step you can take to treat COPD.</li><li>Avoiding secondhand smoke and places where you might breathe in other lung irritants</li><li>Ask your health care provider for an eating plan that will meet your nutritional needs. Also ask about how much physical activity you can do. Physical activity can strengthen the muscles that help you breathe and improve your overall wellness.</li></ul></li><li>Medicines, such as
	<ul><li>Bronchodilators, which relax the muscles around your airways. This helps open your airways and makes breathing easier. Most bronchodilators are taken through an inhaler. In more severe cases, the inhaler may also contain steroids to reduce inflammation.</li><li>Vaccines for the flu and pneumococcal <span class="qt0">pneumonia</span>, since people with COPD are at higher risk for serious problems from these diseases</li><li>Antibiotics if you get a bacterial lung infection</li></ul></li><li>Oxygen therapy, if you have severe COPD and low levels of oxygen in your blood. Oxygen therapy can help you breathe better. You may need extra oxygen all the time or only at certain times.</li><li>Pulmonary rehabilitation, which is a program that helps improve the well-being of people who have chronic breathing problems. It may include
	<ul><li>An exercise program</li><li>Disease management training</li><li>Nutritional counseling</li><li>Psychological counseling</li></ul></li><li>Surgery, usually as a last resort for people who have severe symptoms that have not gotten better with medicines:
	<ul><li>For COPD that is mainly related to emphysema, there are surgeries that: 
			<ul><li>Remove damaged lung tissue</li><li>Remove large air spaces (bullae) that can form when air sacs are destroyed. The bullae can interfere with breathing.</li></ul>
		</li><li>For severe COPD, some people may need lung transplant</li></ul></li></ul><p>If you have COPD, it's important to know when and where to get help for your symptoms. You should get emergency care if you have severe symptoms, such as trouble catching your breath or talking. Call your health care provider if your symptoms are getting worse or if you have signs of an infection, such as a fever.</p>Can COPD (chronic obstructive pulmonary disease) be prevented?<p>Since smoking causes most cases of COPD, the best way to prevent it is to not smoke. It's also important to try to avoid lung irritants such as secondhand smoke, air pollution, chemical fumes, and dusts.</p><p>NIH: National Heart, Lung, and Blood Institute</p></content>

    <document rank="17" url="https://medlineplus.gov/cytomegalovirusinfections.html">
      <content name="title">Cytomegalovirus Infections</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">CMV Infections</content>
      <content name="altTitle">CMV</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>Cytomegalovirus (CMV) is a virus found around the world. It is related to the viruses that cause chickenpox and infectious mononucleosis (mono). Between 50% and 80% of adults in the United States have had a CMV infection by age 40. Once CMV is in a person's body, it stays there for life.  </p><p>CMV is spread through close contact with body fluids. Most people with CMV don't get sick and don't know that they've been infected. But infection with the virus can be serious in babies and people with weak immune systems. If a woman gets CMV when she is pregnant, she can pass it on to her baby. Usually the babies do not have health problems. But some babies can develop lifelong disabilities.</p><p>A blood test can tell whether a person has ever been infected with CMV. Most people with CMV don't need treatment. If you have a weakened immune system, your doctor may prescribe antiviral medicine. Good hygiene, including proper hand washing, may help prevent infections.</p><p>Centers for Disease Control and Prevention</p></content>

    <document rank="18" url="https://medlineplus.gov/delirium.html">
      <content name="title">Delirium</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is delirium?<p>Delirium is a mental state in which you are confused, disoriented, and not able to think or remember clearly. It usually starts suddenly. It is often temporary and treatable.</p><p>There are three types of delirium:</p><ul><li>Hypoactive, when you are not active and seem sleepy, tired, or depressed</li><li>Hyperactive, when you are restless or agitated</li><li>Mixed, when you change back and forth between being hypoactive and hyperactive</li></ul>What causes delirium?<p>There are many different problems that can cause delirium. Some of the more common causes include:</p><ul><li>Advanced cancer.</li><li>Alcohol or drugs, either from intoxication or withdrawal. This includes a serious type of alcohol withdrawal syndrome called delirium tremens. It usually happens to people who stop drinking after years of alcohol use disorder (AUD).</li><li>Dehydration and electrolyte imbalances.</li><li>Dementia.</li><li>Hospitalization, especially in intensive care.</li><li>Infections, such as urinary tract infections, <span class="qt0">pneumonia</span>, and the flu.</li><li>Medicines. This could be a side effect of a medicine, such as sedatives or opioids. Or it could be from withdrawal after stopping a medicine.</li><li>Metabolic disorders.</li><li>Organ failure, such as kidney or liver failure.</li><li>Poisoning.</li><li>Serious illnesses.</li><li>Severe pain.</li><li>Sleep deprivation.</li><li>Surgeries, including reactions to anesthesia.</li></ul>Who is more likely to get delirium?<p>Certain factors put you at risk for delirium, including:</p><ul><li>Being in a hospital or nursing home</li><li>Having dementia</li><li>Having a serious illness or more than one illness</li><li>Having an infection</li><li>Older age</li><li>Having surgery</li><li>Taking medicines that affect the mind or behavior</li><li>Taking high doses of pain medicines, such as opioids</li></ul>What are the symptoms of delirium?<p>The symptoms of delirium usually start suddenly, over a few hours or a few days. They often come and go. The most common symptoms include:</p><ul><li>Changes in alertness (usually more alert in the morning, less at night)</li><li>Changing levels of consciousness</li><li>Confusion</li><li>Disorganized thinking, talking in a way that doesn't make sense</li><li>Disrupted sleep patterns, sleepiness</li><li>Emotional changes: anger, agitation, depression, irritability, overexcitement</li><li>Hallucinations and delusions</li><li>Memory problems, especially with short-term memory</li><li>Trouble concentrating</li></ul>How is delirium diagnosed?<p>Your health care provider may use many tools to make a diagnosis:</p><ul><li>A medical history, which includes asking about your symptoms</li><li>Physical and neurological exams</li><li>Mental status testing, which checks for problems with your thinking and alertness </li><li>Lab and  diagnostic imaging tests</li></ul><p>Delirium and dementia have similar symptoms, so it can be hard to tell them apart. You can also have both at the same time. The differences between them are that:</p><ul><li>Delirium starts suddenly and can cause hallucinations. It is mainly a problem with attention and staying alert. The symptoms may get better or worse and can last for hours or weeks.</li><li>Dementia develops slowly and does not cause hallucinations. It usually starts with memory loss. The symptoms don't change often, like they can with delirium. Dementia almost never gets better.</li></ul>What are the treatments for delirium?<p>Treatment of delirium focuses on the causes and symptoms of delirium. The first step is to identify the cause. Often, treating the cause will lead to a full recovery. The recovery may take some time - weeks or sometimes even months. In the meantime, there may be treatments to manage the symptoms, such as:</p><ul><li>Controlling the environment, which includes making sure that the room is quiet and well-lit, having clocks or calendars in view, and having family members around</li><li>Medicines, including those that control aggression or agitation and pain relievers if there is pain</li><li>If needed, making sure that the person has a hearing aid, glasses, or other devices for communication</li></ul>Can delirium be prevented?<p>Treating the conditions that can cause delirium may reduce the risk of getting it. Hospitals can help lower the risk of delirium by avoiding sedatives and making sure that hospital rooms are kept quiet, calm, and well-lit. It can also help to have family members around and to have the same staff members treat the person each day (if possible).</p></content>

    <document rank="19" url="https://medlineplus.gov/emphysema.html">
      <content name="title">Emphysema</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Pulmonary Emphysema</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is emphysema?<p>Emphysema is a type of COPD (chronic obstructive pulmonary disease). COPD is a group of lung diseases that make it hard to breathe and get worse over time. The other main type of COPD is chronic bronchitis. Most people with COPD have both emphysema and chronic bronchitis, but how severe each type is can be different from person to person.</p><p>Emphysema affects the air sacs in your lungs. Normally, these sacs are elastic or stretchy. When you breathe in, each air sac fills up with air, like a small balloon. When you breathe out, the air sacs deflate, and the air goes out.</p><p>In emphysema, the walls between many of the air sacs in the lungs are damaged. This causes the air sacs to lose their shape and become floppy. The damage also can destroy the walls of the air sacs, leading to fewer and larger air sacs instead of many tiny ones. This makes it harder for your lungs to move oxygen in and carbon dioxide out of your body.</p>What causes emphysema?<p>The cause of emphysema is usually long-term exposure to irritants that damage your lungs and the airways. In the United States, cigarette smoke is the main cause. Pipe, cigar, and other types of tobacco smoke can also cause emphysema, especially if you inhale them.</p><p>Exposure to other inhaled irritants can contribute to emphysema. These include secondhand smoke, air pollution, and chemical fumes or dusts from the environment or workplace.</p><p>Rarely, a genetic condition called alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency can play a role in causing emphysema.</p>Who is at risk for emphysema?<p>The risk factors for emphysema include:</p><ul><li>Smoking. This the main risk factor. Up to 75% of people who have emphysema smoke or used to smoke.</li><li>Long-term exposure to other lung irritants, such as secondhand smoke, air pollution, and chemical fumes and dusts from the environment or workplace.</li><li>Age. Most people who have emphysema are at least 40 years old when their symptoms begin.</li><li>Genetics. This includes alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency, which is a genetic condition. Also, smokers who get emphysema are more likely to get it if they have a family history of COPD.</li></ul>What are the symptoms of emphysema?<p>At first, you may have no symptoms or only mild symptoms. As the disease gets worse, your symptoms usually become more severe. They can include:</p><ul><li>Frequent coughing or wheezing</li><li>A cough that produces a lot mucus</li><li>Shortness of breath, especially with physical activity</li><li>A whistling or squeaky sound when you breathe</li><li>Tightness in your chest</li></ul><p>Some people with emphysema get frequent respiratory infections such as colds and the flu. In severe cases, emphysema can cause weight loss, weakness in your lower muscles, and swelling in your ankles, feet, or legs.</p>How is emphysema diagnosed?<p>Your health care provider may use many tools to make a diagnosis:</p><ul><li>A medical history, which includes asking about your symptoms</li><li>A family history</li><li>Other tests, such as lung function tests, a chest x-ray or CT scan, and blood tests</li></ul>What are the treatments for emphysema?<p>There is no cure for emphysema. However, treatments can help with symptoms, slow the progress of the disease, and improve your ability to stay active. There are also treatments to prevent or treat complications of the disease. Treatments include:</p><ul><li>Lifestyle changes, such as
	<ul><li>Quitting smoking if you are a smoker. This is the most important step you can take to treat emphysema.</li><li>Avoiding secondhand smoke and places where you might breathe in other lung irritants</li><li>Ask your health care provider for an eating plan that will meet your nutritional needs. Also ask about how much physical activity you can do. Physical activity can strengthen the muscles that help you breathe and improve your overall wellness.</li></ul></li><li>Medicines, such as
	<ul><li>Bronchodilators, which relax the muscles around your airways. This helps open your airways and makes breathing easier. Most bronchodilators are taken through an inhaler. In more severe cases, the inhaler may also contain steroids to reduce inflammation.</li><li>Vaccines for the flu and pneumococcal <span class="qt0">pneumonia</span>, since people with emphysema are at higher risk for serious problems from these diseases</li><li>Antibiotics if you get a bacterial or viral lung infection</li></ul></li><li>Oxygen therapy, if you have severe emphysema and low levels of oxygen in your blood. Oxygen therapy can help you breathe better. You may need extra oxygen all the time or only at certain times.</li><li>Pulmonary rehabilitation, which is a program that helps improve the well-being of people who have chronic breathing problems. It may include
	<ul><li>An exercise program</li><li>Disease management training</li><li>Nutritional counseling</li><li>Psychological counseling</li></ul></li><li>Surgery, usually as a last resort for people who have severe symptoms that have not gotten better with medicines. There are surgeries to 
	<ul><li>Remove damaged lung tissue</li><li>Remove large air spaces (bullae) that can form when air sacs are destroyed. The bullae can interfere with breathing.</li><li>Do a lung transplant. This is might be an option if you have very severe emphysema.</li></ul></li></ul><p>If you have emphysema, it's important to know when and where to get help for your symptoms. You should get emergency care if you have severe symptoms, such as trouble catching your breath or talking. Call your health care provider if your symptoms are getting worse or if you have signs of an infection, such as a fever.</p>Can emphysema be prevented?<p>Since smoking causes most cases of emphysema, the best way to prevent it is to not smoke. It's also important to try to avoid lung irritants such as secondhand smoke, air pollution, chemical fumes, and dusts.</p><p>NIH: National Heart, Lung, and Blood Institute</p></content>

    <document rank="20" url="https://medlineplus.gov/eosinophilicdisorders.html">
      <content name="title">Eosinophilic Disorders</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Churg-Strauss Syndrome</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Eosinophilia</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Hypereosinophilic Syndrome</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>Eosinophils are a type of white blood cell. They help fight off infections and play a role in your body's immune response.  They can also build up and cause inflammation.</p><p>Normally your blood doesn't have a large number of eosinophils.  Your body may produce more of them in response to:</p><ul><li>Allergic disorders</li><li>Skin conditions</li><li>Parasitic and fungal infections</li><li>Autoimmune diseases</li><li>Some cancers</li><li>Bone marrow disorders</li></ul><p>In some conditions, the eosinophils can move outside the bloodstream and build up in organs and tissues. This can happen in many different parts of the body, including the esophagus, heart, lungs, blood, and intestines. Treatment of eosinophilic disorders can vary, depending on the cause and which part of the body is affected. Steroids are often part of the treatment.</p></content>

      <content name="groupName">Immune System</content>

    <document rank="21" url="https://medlineplus.gov/esophagusdisorders.html">
      <content name="title">Esophagus Disorders</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Achalasia</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Barrett's Esophagus</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Cardiospasm</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What are esophagus disorders?<p>Esophagus disorders are problems with how your esophagus works. The esophagus is the tube that carries food from your mouth to your stomach. You usually don't notice it unless you swallow something too big, hot, or cold. You might also notice it if something is wrong. When that happens, you might feel pain or have trouble swallowing (dysphagia).</p>What causes esophagus disorders?<p>The cause depends on the type of disorder. Some run in families. Others are linked to allergic reactions to food or substances in your environment. Sometimes, the cause is unknown.</p><p>You may be more likely to develop an esophagus disorder if you:</p><ul><li>Are overweight or have obesity</li><li>Are pregnant</li><li>Take certain medicines</li><li>Have had radiation treatmentt to your neck or chest</li><li>Smoke or are around secondhand smoke</li></ul>What are common esophagus disorders?<p>The most common esophagus problem is gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD). This happens when the muscle at the bottom of your esophagus doesn't close properly. Stomach acid can move back up and irritate the esophagus.</p><p>Other esophagus disorders include:</p><ul><li>Heartburn is a burning feeling in your chest or throat. It is often caused by acid reflux.</li><li>Barrett's Esophagus happens when the lining of your esophagus changes. It is often caused by chronic (long-term) GERD.</li><li>Achalasia is a problem where the lower muscle in your esophagus doesn't relax like it should.</li><li>Esophageal Spasms are muscle contractions that cause chest pain or swallowing problems.</li><li>Eosinophilic Esophagitis (EoE) is a rare condition where certain white blood cells (eosinophils) build up in the esophagus, causing swelling, pain, and trouble swallowing.</li><li>Esophageal Cancer is a type of cancer that starts in the tissues of the esophagus.</li></ul>What are symptoms of esophagus disorders?<p>Symptoms can be different for each condition but may include:</p><ul><li>Pain in your chest, abdomen (belly), or back</li><li>A cough or sore throat that doesn't go away</li><li>Trouble swallowing or feeling like food is stuck</li><li>A hoarse voice or wheezing</li><li>Heartburn or a burning feeling in your stomach</li><li>Tasting acid or food in the back of your mouth</li><li>Food or liquid coming back up into your mouth (regurgitation)</li><li>Losing weight without trying</li></ul><p>Get medical help right away if you have chest pain with shortness of breath, or pain in your jaw or arm. These could be signs of a heart problem.</p>How are esophagus disorders diagnosed?<p>Your health care provider will ask about your symptoms and medical history. They may order one or more tests, such as:</p><ul><li>Upper gastrointestinal (GI) endoscopy. This test uses a tiny camera to look inside your esophagus and stomach.</li><li>Biopsy. This test takes a small tissue sample to check.</li><li>Barium swallow. This test uses imaging to see how you swallow.</li><li>Esophageal manometry. This test checks how well your esophagus moves food to your stomach.</li><li>Esophageal pH test. This test measures how often acid backs up into your esophagus and how long the acid stays there.</li></ul>How are esophagus disorders treated?<p>Treatment depends on what's causing the problem and your overall health. Some esophagus disorders get better with over-the-counter medicines, diet changes, or lifestyle changes. Others may need prescription medicine or surgery.</p><p>Without treatment, some disorders may lead to other problems, such as <span class="qt0">pneumonia</span>, from food entering the windpipe. GERD, Barrett's esophagus, and achalasia can also raise your risk for esophageal cancer.</p>Can esophagus disorders be prevented?<p>You can help prevent or reduce symptoms by:</p><ul><li>Not smoking and limiting alcohol</li><li>Eating smaller meals</li><li>Avoiding eating close to bedtime</li><li>Wearing loose-fitting clothes</li><li>Sleeping on your left side or raising the head of your bed</li><li>Maintaining a healthy weight</li></ul><p>NIH: National Institute of Diabetes and Digestive and Kidney Diseases</p></content>

    <document rank="22" url="https://medlineplus.gov/flu.html">
      <content name="title">Flu</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Grippe</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Influenza</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Seasonal flu</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is the flu?<p>The flu, also called influenza, is a respiratory infection caused by viruses. Each year, millions of Americans get sick with the flu. Sometimes it causes mild illness. But it can also be serious or even deadly, especially for people over 65, newborn babies, and people with certain chronic illnesses.</p>What causes the flu?<p>The flu is caused by flu viruses that spread from person to person. When someone with the flu coughs, sneezes, or talks, they spray tiny droplets. These droplets can land in the mouths or noses of people who are nearby. Less often, a person may get flu by touching a surface or object that has flu virus on it and then touching their own mouth, nose, or possibly their eyes.</p>What are the symptoms of the flu?<p>Symptoms of the flu come on suddenly and may include:</p><ul><li>Fever or feeling feverish/chills</li><li>Cough</li><li>Sore throat</li><li>Runny or stuffy nose</li><li>Muscle or body aches</li><li>Headaches</li><li>Fatigue (tiredness)</li></ul><p>Some people may also have vomiting and diarrhea. This is more common in children.</p><p>Sometimes people have trouble figuring out whether they have a cold or the flu. There are differences between them:</p>Signs and SymptomsColdFluStart of symptomsSlowlySuddenlyFeverRarelyUsuallyAchesSometimes (slight)UsuallyFatigue, weaknessSometimesUsuallyHeadacheRarelyCommonStuffy nose, sneezing, or sore throatCommonSometimes<p>Sometimes people say that they have a "flu" when they really have something else. For example, "stomach flu" isn't the flu; it's gastroenteritis.</p>What other problems can the flu cause?<p>Some people who get the flu will develop complications. Some of these complications can be serious or even life-threatening. They include:</p><ul><li>Bronchitis</li><li>Ear infection</li><li>Sinus infection</li><li><span class="qt0">Pneumonia</span></li><li>Inflammation of the heart (myocarditis), brain (encephalitis), or muscle tissues (myositis, rhabdomyolysis)</li></ul><p>The flu also can make chronic health problems worse. For example, people with asthma may have asthma attacks while they have flu.</p><p>Certain people are more likely to have complications from the flu, including:</p><ul><li>Adults 65 and older</li><li>Pregnant women</li><li>Children younger than 5</li><li>People with certain chronic health conditions, such as asthma, diabetes, and heart disease</li></ul>How is the flu diagnosed?<p>To diagnose the flu, health care providers will first do a medical history and ask about your symptoms. There are several tests for the flu. For the tests, your provider will swipe the inside of your nose or the back of your throat with a swab. Then the swab will be tested for the flu virus.</p><p>Some tests are quick and give results in 15-20 minutes. But these tests are not as accurate as other flu tests. These other tests can give you the results in one hour or several hours.</p>What are the treatments for the flu?<p>Most people with the flu recover on their own without medical care. People with mild cases of the flu should stay home and avoid contact with others, except to get medical care.</p><p>But if you have symptoms of flu and are in a high risk group or are very sick or worried about your illness, contact your health care provider. You might need antiviral medicines to treat your flu. Antiviral medicines can make the illness milder and shorten the time you are sick. They also can prevent serious flu complications. They usually work best when you start taking them within 2 days of getting sick.</p>Can the flu be prevented?<p>The best way to prevent the flu is to get a flu vaccine every year. But it's also important to have good health habits like covering your cough and washing your hands often. This can help stop the spread of germs and prevent the flu.</p><p>Centers for Disease Control and Prevention</p></content>

    <document rank="23" url="https://medlineplus.gov/haemophilusinfections.html">
      <content name="title">Haemophilus Infections</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Hib</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>Haemophilus is the name of a group of bacteria. There are several types of Haemophilus. They can cause different types of illnesses involving breathing, bones and joints, and the nervous system.</p><p>One common type, Hib (Haemophilus influenzae type b), causes serious disease. It usually strikes children under 5 years old. Your child can get Hib disease by being around other children or adults who may have the bacteria and not know it. The germs spread from person to person. If the germs stay in the child's nose and throat, the child probably will not get sick. But sometimes the germs spread into the lungs or the bloodstream, and then Hib can cause serious problems such as meningitis and <span class="qt0">pneumonia</span>.</p><p>Treatment is with antibiotics. There is a vaccine to prevent Hib disease. All children younger than 5 years of age should be vaccinated with the Hib vaccine.</p><p>Centers for Disease Control and Prevention</p></content>

    <document rank="24" url="https://medlineplus.gov/homelessnessandhealth.html">
      <content name="title">Homelessness and Health</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Runaways</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What are the causes of homelessness?<p>Each night, hundreds of thousands of people experience homelessness in the United States. Some of these people experience chronic homelessness, while others have temporarily lost their shelter. The reasons why they are homelessness are complex. They can include a combination of factors such as:</p><ul><li>Poverty</li><li>Unemployment</li><li>Lack of affordable housing</li><li>Mental and substance use disorders</li><li>Trauma and violence</li><li>Intimate partner violence</li><li>Justice-system involvement</li><li>Sudden serious illness</li><li>Divorce</li><li>Death of a partner or parent</li><li>Disabilities</li></ul>What is the link between homelessness and health?<p>Poor health can contribute to homelessness. And being homeless can contribute to poor health. Many of the problems that people experiencing homelessness face can make their health worse, including:</p><ul><li>Limited access to health care</li><li>Problems getting enough food</li><li>Trouble staying safe</li><li>Violence</li><li>Stress</li><li>Unsanitary living conditions</li><li>Exposure to severe weather</li></ul>What are some of the common health problems that people experiencing homelessness have?<p>Some of the common health problems that people experiencing homelessness may have include:</p><ul><li>HIV</li><li>Lung diseases, including bronchitis, tuberculosis, and <span class="qt0">pneumonia</span></li><li>Malnutrition</li><li>Mental health problems</li><li>Substance use problems</li><li>Wounds and skin infections</li></ul><p>Many people experiencing homelessness are dealing with trauma. They may have been abused or assaulted. This includes children, who are at risk for emotional and behavioral problems.</p><p>Contact your local homelessness assistance agency to get the help you need, such as access to shelters, health centers, and free meals.</p></content>

    <document rank="0" url="https://medlineplus.gov/acutebronchitis.html">
      <content name="title">Acute <span class="qt0">Bronchitis</span></content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle"><span class="qt0">Bronchitis</span></content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p><span class="qt0">Bronchitis</span> is an inflammation of the bronchial tubes, the airways that carry air to your lungs. It causes a cough that often brings up mucus. It can also cause shortness of breath, wheezing, a low fever, and chest tightness. There are two main types of <span class="qt0">bronchitis</span>: acute and chronic.</p><p>Most cases of acute <span class="qt0">bronchitis</span> get better within several days. But your cough can last for several weeks after the infection is gone.</p><p>The same viruses that cause colds and the flu often cause acute <span class="qt0">bronchitis</span>. These viruses spread through the air when people cough, or though physical contact (for example, on unwashed hands). Being exposed to tobacco smoke, air pollution, dusts, vapors, and fumes can also cause acute <span class="qt0">bronchitis</span>. Less often, bacteria can also cause acute <span class="qt0">bronchitis</span>.</p><p>To diagnose acute <span class="qt0">bronchitis</span>, your health care provider will ask about your symptoms and listen to your breathing. You may also have other tests.</p><p>Treatments include rest, fluids, and aspirin (for adults) or acetaminophen to treat fever. A humidifier or steam can also help. You may need inhaled medicine to open your airways if you are wheezing. Antibiotics won't help if the cause is viral. You may get antibiotics if the cause is bacterial.</p><p>NIH: National Heart, Lung, and Blood Institute</p></content>
      <content name="mesh"><span class="qt0">Bronchitis</span></content>
      <content name="groupName">Infections</content>
      <content name="groupName">Lungs and Breathing</content>
      <content name="snippet"> <span class="qt0">Bronchitis</span> is an inflammation of the bronchial tubes, the airways that carry air to your lungs. It causes a cough that often brings up mucus. It can also cause  ... </content>

    <document rank="1" url="https://medlineplus.gov/chronicbronchitis.html">
      <content name="title">Chronic <span class="qt0">Bronchitis</span></content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle"><span class="qt0">Bronchitis</span></content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is chronic <span class="qt0">bronchitis</span>?<p>Chronic <span class="qt0">bronchitis</span> is a type of COPD (chronic obstructive pulmonary disease). COPD is a group of lung diseases that make it hard to breathe and get worse over time. The other main type of COPD is emphysema. Most people with COPD have both emphysema and chronic <span class="qt0">bronchitis</span>, but how severe each type is can be different from person to person.</p><p>Chronic <span class="qt0">bronchitis</span> is inflammation (swelling) and irritation of the bronchial tubes. These tubes are the airways that carry air to and from the air sacs in your lungs. The irritation of the tubes causes mucus to build up. This mucus and the swelling of the tubes make it harder for your lungs to move oxygen in and carbon dioxide out of your body.</p>What causes chronic <span class="qt0">bronchitis</span>?<p>The cause of chronic <span class="qt0">bronchitis</span> is usually long-term exposure to irritants that damage your lungs and airways. In the United States, cigarette smoke is the main cause. Pipe, cigar, and other types of tobacco smoke can also cause chronic <span class="qt0">bronchitis</span>, especially if you inhale them.</p><p>Exposure to other inhaled irritants can contribute to chronic <span class="qt0">bronchitis</span>. These include secondhand smoke, air pollution, and chemical fumes or dusts from the environment or workplace.</p><p>Rarely, a genetic condition called alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency can play a role in causing chronic <span class="qt0">bronchitis</span>.</p>Who is at risk for chronic <span class="qt0">bronchitis</span>?<p>The risk factors for chronic <span class="qt0">bronchitis</span> include:</p><ul><li>Smoking. This the main risk factor. Up to 75% of people who have chronic <span class="qt0">bronchitis</span> smoke or used to smoke.</li><li>Long-term exposure to other lung irritants, such as secondhand smoke, air pollution, and chemical fumes and dusts from the environment or workplace.</li><li>Age. Most people who have chronic <span class="qt0">bronchitis</span> are at least 40 years old when their symptoms begin.</li><li>Genetics. This includes alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency, which is a genetic condition. Also, smokers who get chronic <span class="qt0">bronchitis</span> are more likely to get it if they have a family history of COPD.</li></ul>What are the symptoms of chronic <span class="qt0">bronchitis</span>?<p>At first, you may have no symptoms or only mild symptoms. As the disease gets worse, your symptoms usually become more severe. They can include:</p><ul><li>Frequent coughing or a cough that produces a lot mucus</li><li>Wheezing</li><li>A whistling or squeaky sound when you breathe</li><li>Shortness of breath, especially with physical activity</li><li>Tightness in your chest</li></ul><p>Some people with chronic <span class="qt0">bronchitis</span> get frequent respiratory infections such as colds and the flu. In severe cases, chronic <span class="qt0">bronchitis</span> can cause weight loss, weakness in your lower muscles, and swelling in your ankles, feet, or legs.</p>How is chronic <span class="qt0">bronchitis</span> diagnosed?<p>Your health care provider may use many tools to make a diagnosis:</p><ul><li>A medical history, which includes asking about your symptoms</li><li>A family history</li><li>Various tests, such as lung function tests, a chest x-ray or CT scan, and blood tests</li></ul>What are the treatments for chronic <span class="qt0">bronchitis</span>?<p>There is no cure for chronic <span class="qt0">bronchitis</span>. However, treatments can help with symptoms, slow the progress of the disease, and improve your ability to stay active. There are also treatments to prevent or treat complications of the disease. Treatments include:</p><ul><li>Lifestyle changes, such as
	<ul><li>Quitting smoking if you are a smoker. This is the most important step you can take to treat chronic <span class="qt0">bronchitis</span>.</li><li>Avoiding secondhand smoke and places where you might breathe in other lung irritants</li><li>Ask your health care provider for an eating plan that will meet your nutritional needs. Also ask about how much physical activity you can do. Physical activity can strengthen the muscles that help you breathe and improve your overall wellness.</li></ul></li><li>Medicines, such as
	<ul><li>Bronchodilators, which relax the muscles around your airways. This helps open your airways and makes breathing easier. Most bronchodilators are taken through an inhaler. In more severe cases, the inhaler may also contain steroids to reduce inflammation.</li><li>Vaccines for the flu and pneumococcal pneumonia, since people with chronic <span class="qt0">bronchitis</span> are at higher risk for serious problems from these diseases.</li><li>Antibiotics if you get a bacterial or viral lung infection</li></ul></li><li>Oxygen therapy, if you have severe chronic <span class="qt0">bronchitis</span> and low levels of oxygen in your blood. Oxygen therapy can help you breathe better. You may need extra oxygen all the time or only at certain times.</li><li>Pulmonary rehabilitation, which is a program that helps improve the well-being of people who have chronic breathing problems. It may include</li><ul><li>An exercise program</li><li>Disease management training</li><li>Nutritional counseling</li><li>Psychological counseling</li></ul><li>A lung transplant, as a last resort for people who have severe symptoms that have not gotten better with medicines</li></ul><p>If you have chronic <span class="qt0">bronchitis</span>, it's important to know when and where to get help for your symptoms. You should get emergency care if you have severe symptoms, such as trouble catching your breath or talking. Call your health care provider if your symptoms are getting worse or if you have signs of an infection, such as a fever.</p>Can chronic <span class="qt0">bronchitis</span> be prevented?<p>Since smoking causes most cases of chronic <span class="qt0">bronchitis</span>, the best way to prevent it is to not smoke. It's also important to try to avoid lung irritants such as secondhand smoke, air pollution, chemical fumes, and dusts.</p><p>


</p><p>NIH: National Heart, Lung, and Blood Institute</p></content>
      <content name="mesh"><span class="qt0">Bronchitis</span>, Chronic</content>
      <content name="groupName">Infections</content>
      <content name="groupName">Lungs and Breathing</content>
      <content name="snippet">What is chronic <span class="qt0">bronchitis</span>? Chronic <span class="qt0">bronchitis</span> is a type of COPD (chronic obstructive pulmonary disease). COPD is a group of lung diseases that make it  ... </content>

    <document rank="2" url="https://medlineplus.gov/copd.html">
      <content name="title">COPD</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is COPD (chronic obstructive pulmonary disease)?<p>COPD (chronic obstructive pulmonary disease) is a group of lung diseases that make it hard to breathe and get worse over time.</p><p>Normally, the airways and air sacs in your lungs are elastic or stretchy. When you breathe in, the airways bring air to the air sacs. The air sacs fill up with air, like a small balloon. When you breathe out, the air sacs deflate, and the air goes out. If you have COPD, less air flows in and out of your airways because of one or more problems:</p><ul><li>The airways and air sacs in your lungs become less elastic</li><li>The walls between many of the air sacs are destroyed</li><li>The walls of the airways become thick and inflamed</li><li>The airways make more mucus than usual and can become clogged</li></ul>What are the types of COPD (chronic obstructive pulmonary disease)?<p>COPD includes two main types:</p><ul><li>Emphysema affects the air sacs in your lungs, as well as the walls between them. They become damaged and are less elastic.</li><li>Chronic <span class="qt0">bronchitis</span>, in which the lining of your airways is constantly irritated and inflamed. This causes the lining to swell and make mucus.</li></ul><p>Most people with COPD have both emphysema and chronic <span class="qt0">bronchitis</span>, but how severe each type is can be different from person to person.</p>What causes COPD (chronic obstructive pulmonary disease)?<p>The cause of COPD is usually long-term exposure to irritants that damage your lungs and airways. In the United States, cigarette smoke is the main cause. Pipe, cigar, and other types of tobacco smoke can also cause COPD, especially if you inhale them.</p><p>Exposure to other inhaled irritants can contribute to COPD. These include secondhand smoke, air pollution, and chemical fumes or dusts from the environment or workplace.</p><p>Rarely, a genetic condition called alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency can play a role in causing COPD.</p>Who is at risk for COPD (chronic obstructive pulmonary disease)?<p>The risk factors for COPD include:</p><ul><li>Smoking. This is the main risk factor. Up to 75% of people who have COPD smoke or used to smoke.</li><li>Long-term exposure to other lung irritants, such as secondhand smoke, air pollution, and chemical fumes and dusts from the environment or workplace</li><li>Age. Most people who have COPD are at least 40 years old when their symptoms begin.</li><li>Genetics. This includes alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency, which is a genetic condition. Also, smokers who get COPD are more likely to get it if they have a family history of COPD.</li><li>Asthma. People who have asthma have more risk of developing COPD than people who don't have asthma. But most people with asthma will not get COPD.</li></ul>What are the symptoms of COPD (chronic obstructive pulmonary disease)?<p>At first, you may have no symptoms or only mild symptoms. As the disease gets worse, your symptoms usually become more severe. They can include:</p><ul><li>Frequent coughing or a cough that produces a lot of mucus</li><li>Wheezing</li><li>A whistling or squeaky sound when you breathe</li><li>Shortness of breath, especially with physical activity</li><li>Tightness in your chest</li></ul><p>Some people with COPD get frequent respiratory infections such as colds and the flu. In severe cases, COPD can cause weight loss, weakness in your lower muscles, and swelling in your ankles, feet, or legs.</p>How is COPD (chronic obstructive pulmonary disease) diagnosed?<p>Your health care provider may use many tools to make a diagnosis:</p><ul><li>A medical history, which includes asking about your symptoms</li><li>A family history</li><li>Various tests, such as lung function tests, a chest x-ray or CT scan, and blood tests</li></ul><p>Your doctor will diagnose COPD based on your signs and symptoms, your medical and family histories, and test results.</p>What are the treatments for COPD (chronic obstructive pulmonary disease)?<p>There is no cure for COPD. However, treatments can help with symptoms, slow the progress of the disease, and improve your ability to stay active. There are also treatments to prevent or treat complications of the disease. Treatments include:</p><ul><li>Lifestyle changes, such as
	<ul><li>Quitting smoking if you are a smoker. This is the most important step you can take to treat COPD.</li><li>Avoiding secondhand smoke and places where you might breathe in other lung irritants</li><li>Ask your health care provider for an eating plan that will meet your nutritional needs. Also ask about how much physical activity you can do. Physical activity can strengthen the muscles that help you breathe and improve your overall wellness.</li></ul></li><li>Medicines, such as
	<ul><li>Bronchodilators, which relax the muscles around your airways. This helps open your airways and makes breathing easier. Most bronchodilators are taken through an inhaler. In more severe cases, the inhaler may also contain steroids to reduce inflammation.</li><li>Vaccines for the flu and pneumococcal pneumonia, since people with COPD are at higher risk for serious problems from these diseases</li><li>Antibiotics if you get a bacterial lung infection</li></ul></li><li>Oxygen therapy, if you have severe COPD and low levels of oxygen in your blood. Oxygen therapy can help you breathe better. You may need extra oxygen all the time or only at certain times.</li><li>Pulmonary rehabilitation, which is a program that helps improve the well-being of people who have chronic breathing problems. It may include
	<ul><li>An exercise program</li><li>Disease management training</li><li>Nutritional counseling</li><li>Psychological counseling</li></ul></li><li>Surgery, usually as a last resort for people who have severe symptoms that have not gotten better with medicines:
	<ul><li>For COPD that is mainly related to emphysema, there are surgeries that: 
			<ul><li>Remove damaged lung tissue</li><li>Remove large air spaces (bullae) that can form when air sacs are destroyed. The bullae can interfere with breathing.</li></ul>
		</li><li>For severe COPD, some people may need lung transplant</li></ul></li></ul><p>If you have COPD, it's important to know when and where to get help for your symptoms. You should get emergency care if you have severe symptoms, such as trouble catching your breath or talking. Call your health care provider if your symptoms are getting worse or if you have signs of an infection, such as a fever.</p>Can COPD (chronic obstructive pulmonary disease) be prevented?<p>Since smoking causes most cases of COPD, the best way to prevent it is to not smoke. It's also important to try to avoid lung irritants such as secondhand smoke, air pollution, chemical fumes, and dusts.</p><p>NIH: National Heart, Lung, and Blood Institute</p></content>

    <document rank="3" url="https://medlineplus.gov/emphysema.html">
      <content name="title">Emphysema</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Pulmonary Emphysema</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is emphysema?<p>Emphysema is a type of COPD (chronic obstructive pulmonary disease). COPD is a group of lung diseases that make it hard to breathe and get worse over time. The other main type of COPD is chronic <span class="qt0">bronchitis</span>. Most people with COPD have both emphysema and chronic <span class="qt0">bronchitis</span>, but how severe each type is can be different from person to person.</p><p>Emphysema affects the air sacs in your lungs. Normally, these sacs are elastic or stretchy. When you breathe in, each air sac fills up with air, like a small balloon. When you breathe out, the air sacs deflate, and the air goes out.</p><p>In emphysema, the walls between many of the air sacs in the lungs are damaged. This causes the air sacs to lose their shape and become floppy. The damage also can destroy the walls of the air sacs, leading to fewer and larger air sacs instead of many tiny ones. This makes it harder for your lungs to move oxygen in and carbon dioxide out of your body.</p>What causes emphysema?<p>The cause of emphysema is usually long-term exposure to irritants that damage your lungs and the airways. In the United States, cigarette smoke is the main cause. Pipe, cigar, and other types of tobacco smoke can also cause emphysema, especially if you inhale them.</p><p>Exposure to other inhaled irritants can contribute to emphysema. These include secondhand smoke, air pollution, and chemical fumes or dusts from the environment or workplace.</p><p>Rarely, a genetic condition called alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency can play a role in causing emphysema.</p>Who is at risk for emphysema?<p>The risk factors for emphysema include:</p><ul><li>Smoking. This the main risk factor. Up to 75% of people who have emphysema smoke or used to smoke.</li><li>Long-term exposure to other lung irritants, such as secondhand smoke, air pollution, and chemical fumes and dusts from the environment or workplace.</li><li>Age. Most people who have emphysema are at least 40 years old when their symptoms begin.</li><li>Genetics. This includes alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency, which is a genetic condition. Also, smokers who get emphysema are more likely to get it if they have a family history of COPD.</li></ul>What are the symptoms of emphysema?<p>At first, you may have no symptoms or only mild symptoms. As the disease gets worse, your symptoms usually become more severe. They can include:</p><ul><li>Frequent coughing or wheezing</li><li>A cough that produces a lot mucus</li><li>Shortness of breath, especially with physical activity</li><li>A whistling or squeaky sound when you breathe</li><li>Tightness in your chest</li></ul><p>Some people with emphysema get frequent respiratory infections such as colds and the flu. In severe cases, emphysema can cause weight loss, weakness in your lower muscles, and swelling in your ankles, feet, or legs.</p>How is emphysema diagnosed?<p>Your health care provider may use many tools to make a diagnosis:</p><ul><li>A medical history, which includes asking about your symptoms</li><li>A family history</li><li>Other tests, such as lung function tests, a chest x-ray or CT scan, and blood tests</li></ul>What are the treatments for emphysema?<p>There is no cure for emphysema. However, treatments can help with symptoms, slow the progress of the disease, and improve your ability to stay active. There are also treatments to prevent or treat complications of the disease. Treatments include:</p><ul><li>Lifestyle changes, such as
	<ul><li>Quitting smoking if you are a smoker. This is the most important step you can take to treat emphysema.</li><li>Avoiding secondhand smoke and places where you might breathe in other lung irritants</li><li>Ask your health care provider for an eating plan that will meet your nutritional needs. Also ask about how much physical activity you can do. Physical activity can strengthen the muscles that help you breathe and improve your overall wellness.</li></ul></li><li>Medicines, such as
	<ul><li>Bronchodilators, which relax the muscles around your airways. This helps open your airways and makes breathing easier. Most bronchodilators are taken through an inhaler. In more severe cases, the inhaler may also contain steroids to reduce inflammation.</li><li>Vaccines for the flu and pneumococcal pneumonia, since people with emphysema are at higher risk for serious problems from these diseases</li><li>Antibiotics if you get a bacterial or viral lung infection</li></ul></li><li>Oxygen therapy, if you have severe emphysema and low levels of oxygen in your blood. Oxygen therapy can help you breathe better. You may need extra oxygen all the time or only at certain times.</li><li>Pulmonary rehabilitation, which is a program that helps improve the well-being of people who have chronic breathing problems. It may include
	<ul><li>An exercise program</li><li>Disease management training</li><li>Nutritional counseling</li><li>Psychological counseling</li></ul></li><li>Surgery, usually as a last resort for people who have severe symptoms that have not gotten better with medicines. There are surgeries to 
	<ul><li>Remove damaged lung tissue</li><li>Remove large air spaces (bullae) that can form when air sacs are destroyed. The bullae can interfere with breathing.</li><li>Do a lung transplant. This is might be an option if you have very severe emphysema.</li></ul></li></ul><p>If you have emphysema, it's important to know when and where to get help for your symptoms. You should get emergency care if you have severe symptoms, such as trouble catching your breath or talking. Call your health care provider if your symptoms are getting worse or if you have signs of an infection, such as a fever.</p>Can emphysema be prevented?<p>Since smoking causes most cases of emphysema, the best way to prevent it is to not smoke. It's also important to try to avoid lung irritants such as secondhand smoke, air pollution, chemical fumes, and dusts.</p><p>NIH: National Heart, Lung, and Blood Institute</p></content>
