    <document rank="4" url="https://medlineplus.gov/antibiotics.html">
      <content name="title">Antibiotics</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What are antibiotics?<p>Antibiotics are medicines that fight bacterial infections in people and animals. They work by killing the bacteria or by making it hard for the bacteria to grow and multiply.</p><p>Antibiotics can be taken in different ways:</p><ul><li>Orally (by mouth). This could be pills, capsules, or liquids.</li><li>Topically. This might be a cream, spray, or ointment that you put on your skin. It could also be eye ointment, eye drops, or ear drops.</li><li>Through an injection or intravenously (IV). This is usually for more serious infections.</li></ul>What do antibiotics treat?<p>Antibiotics only treat certain bacterial infections, such as strep throat, urinary tract infections, and E. coli.</p><p>You may not need to take antibiotics for some bacterial infections. For example, you might not need them for many sinus infections or some ear infections. Taking antibiotics when they're not needed won't help you, and they can have side effects. Your health care provider can decide the best treatment for you when you're sick. Don't ask your provider to prescribe an antibiotic for you.</p>Do antibiotics treat viral infections?<p>Antibiotics do not work on viral infections. For example, you shouldn't take antibiotics for:</p><ul><li>Colds and runny noses, even if the mucus is thick, yellow, or green</li><li>Most sore throats (except strep throat)</li><li>Flu</li><li>Most cases of <span class="qt0">bronchitis</span></li></ul>What are the side effects of antibiotics?<p>The side effects of antibiotics range from minor to very severe. Some of the common side effects include:</p><ul><li>Rash</li><li>Nausea</li><li>Diarrhea</li><li>Yeast infections</li></ul><p>More serious side effects can include:</p><ul><li>C. diff infections, which cause diarrhea that can lead to severe colon damage and sometimes even death</li><li>Severe and life-threatening allergic reactions</li><li>Antibiotic resistance infections</li></ul><p>Call your health care provider if you develop any side effects while taking your antibiotic.</p>Why is it important to take antibiotics only when they're needed?<p>You should only take antibiotics when they are needed because they can cause side effects and can contribute to Antibiotic resistance infections. Antibiotic resistance happens when the bacteria change and become able to resist the effects of an antibiotic. This means that the bacteria continue to grow.</p>How do I use antibiotics correctly?<p>When you take antibiotics, it is important that you take them responsibly:</p><ul><li>Always follow the directions carefully. Finish your medicine even if you feel better. If you stop taking them too soon, some bacteria may survive and re-infect you.</li><li>Don't save your antibiotics for later.</li><li>Don't share your antibiotic with others.</li><li>Don't take antibiotics prescribed for someone else. This may delay the best treatment for you, make you even sicker, or cause side effects.</li></ul><p>Centers for Disease Control and Prevention</p></content>

    <document rank="5" url="https://medlineplus.gov/cough.html">
      <content name="title">Cough</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>Coughing is a reflex that keeps your throat and airways clear.  Although it can be annoying, coughing helps your body heal or protect itself. Coughs can be either acute or chronic.  Acute coughs begin suddenly and usually last no more than 2 to 3 weeks.  Acute coughs are the kind you most often get with a cold, flu, or acute <span class="qt0">bronchitis</span>.  Chronic coughs last longer than 2 to 3 weeks.  Causes of chronic cough include:</p><ul><li>Chronic <span class="qt0">bronchitis</span></li><li>Asthma</li><li>Allergies</li><li>COPD (chronic obstructive pulmonary disease)</li><li>GERD (gastroesophageal reflux disease) </li><li>Smoking</li><li>Throat disorders, such as croup in young children</li><li>Some medicines </li></ul><p>Water can help ease your cough - whether you drink it or add it to the air with a steamy shower or vaporizer. If you have a cold or the flu, antihistamines may work better than non-prescription cough medicines.   Children under four should not have cough medicine.  For children over four, use caution and read labels carefully.</p></content>

Causes of chronic cough include: Chronic <span class="qt0">bronchitis</span>  Asthma  Allergies  COPD (chronic obstructive pulmonary disease)  GERD ( ... </content>

    <document rank="6" url="https://medlineplus.gov/bronchialdisorders.html">
      <content name="title">Bronchial Disorders</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Bronchiectasis</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Bronchiolitis</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>When you breathe in, the air travels down through your trachea (windpipe). It then goes through two tubes to your lungs. These tubes are your bronchi. Bronchial disorders can make it hard for you to breathe.</p><p>The most common problem with the bronchi is <span class="qt0">bronchitis</span>, an inflammation of the tubes. It can be acute or chronic. Other problems include:</p><ul><li>Bronchiectasis - a condition in which damage to the airways causes them to widen and become flabby and scarred</li><li>Exercise-induced bronchospasm - a breathing problem that happens when your airways shrink while you are exercising</li><li>Bronchiolitis - an inflammation of the small airways that branch off from the bronchi</li><li>Bronchopulmonary dysplasia - a chronic lung condition in infants, most often premature infants</li></ul></content>

    <document rank="7" url="https://medlineplus.gov/commoncold.html">
      <content name="title">Common Cold</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Cold, Common</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is the common cold?<p>The common cold is a mild infection of your upper respiratory tract (which includes your nose and throat). Colds are probably the most common illness. Adults have an average of 2-3 colds per year, and children have even more. Colds are more common in the winter and spring, but you can get them at any time.</p>What causes the common cold?<p>More than 200 different viruses can cause a cold, but rhinoviruses are the most common type. The viruses that cause colds are very contagious. They can spread from person to person through the air and close personal contact. You can also get infected when you touch something that has the virus on it and then touch your eyes, mouth, or nose. For example, you could get a cold after you shake hands with someone who has a cold or touch a doorknob that has the germs on it, and then touch your face.</p>What are the symptoms of the common cold?<p>The symptoms of a common cold usually include:</p><ul><li>Sneezing</li><li>Stuffy nose (congestion)</li><li>Runny nose</li><li>Sore throat</li><li>Coughing</li><li>Headache</li></ul><p>The symptoms usually start a few days after you become infected with the virus. Some symptoms can last for 10-14 days.</p>What are the treatments for the common cold?<p>There is no cure for the common cold. But there are treatments that can make you feel better while you wait for the cold to go away on its own:</p><ul><li>Getting lots of rest.</li><li>Drinking plenty of fluids.</li><li>Using a clean humidifier or cool mist vaporizer.</li><li>Gargling with warm salt water.</li><li>Using saline nose drops or sprays.</li><li>Taking over-the-counter pain or cold and cough medicines. But you need to be careful with certain medicines:
	<ul><li>Children and teens should not take aspirin.</li><li>Some cold and cough medicines contain ingredients that are not recommended for children. Talk with your child's health care provider before giving your child any cold and cough medicines.</li><li>Some cold and cough medicines contain pain relievers. If you also take a separate pain reliever with these medicines, you could be getting a dangerous amount of the pain reliever. Read the labels on the medicines and follow the instructions carefully. If you have questions, ask your provider or a pharmacist.</li></ul>
</li></ul><p>Antibiotics will not help with a cold. Antibiotics help with bacterial infections, not with viral infections such as colds.</p><p>Most people who have a cold will feel better after a week or two. However, some people who get a cold may develop other illnesses, such as <span class="qt0">bronchitis</span> or pneumonia. This is more common in people with weakened immune systems, asthma, or other respiratory conditions. Contact your provider if you or your child have symptoms that concern you, such as:</p><ul><li>Trouble breathing or fast breathing</li><li>Dehydration</li><li>Fever that lasts longer than 4 days</li><li>Symptoms that last more than 10 days without improvement</li><li>Symptoms, such as fever or cough, that improve but then return or worsen</li><li>Worsening of chronic medical conditions</li></ul>Can the common cold be prevented?<p>There is no vaccine to protect against the common cold. But you may be able to reduce your risk of getting or spreading a cold by:</p><ul><li>Washing your hands often with soap and water for at least 20 seconds.</li><li>Avoiding touching your face, nose, or mouth with unwashed hands.</li><li>Avoiding close contact, such as kissing, shaking hands, and sharing cups and eating utensils, with others if you are sick or they are sick.</li><li>Cleaning and disinfecting surfaces that you frequently touch.</li><li>Covering coughs and sneezes with a tissue. Then throw away the tissue and wash your hands.</li><li>Staying home when sick.</li></ul><p>Centers for Disease Control and Prevention</p></content>

    <document rank="8" url="https://medlineplus.gov/flu.html">
      <content name="title">Flu</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Grippe</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Influenza</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Seasonal flu</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is the flu?<p>The flu, also called influenza, is a respiratory infection caused by viruses. Each year, millions of Americans get sick with the flu. Sometimes it causes mild illness. But it can also be serious or even deadly, especially for people over 65, newborn babies, and people with certain chronic illnesses.</p>What causes the flu?<p>The flu is caused by flu viruses that spread from person to person. When someone with the flu coughs, sneezes, or talks, they spray tiny droplets. These droplets can land in the mouths or noses of people who are nearby. Less often, a person may get flu by touching a surface or object that has flu virus on it and then touching their own mouth, nose, or possibly their eyes.</p>What are the symptoms of the flu?<p>Symptoms of the flu come on suddenly and may include:</p><ul><li>Fever or feeling feverish/chills</li><li>Cough</li><li>Sore throat</li><li>Runny or stuffy nose</li><li>Muscle or body aches</li><li>Headaches</li><li>Fatigue (tiredness)</li></ul><p>Some people may also have vomiting and diarrhea. This is more common in children.</p><p>Sometimes people have trouble figuring out whether they have a cold or the flu. There are differences between them:</p>Signs and SymptomsColdFluStart of symptomsSlowlySuddenlyFeverRarelyUsuallyAchesSometimes (slight)UsuallyFatigue, weaknessSometimesUsuallyHeadacheRarelyCommonStuffy nose, sneezing, or sore throatCommonSometimes<p>Sometimes people say that they have a "flu" when they really have something else. For example, "stomach flu" isn't the flu; it's gastroenteritis.</p>What other problems can the flu cause?<p>Some people who get the flu will develop complications. Some of these complications can be serious or even life-threatening. They include:</p><ul><li><span class="qt0">Bronchitis</span></li><li>Ear infection</li><li>Sinus infection</li><li>Pneumonia</li><li>Inflammation of the heart (myocarditis), brain (encephalitis), or muscle tissues (myositis, rhabdomyolysis)</li></ul><p>The flu also can make chronic health problems worse. For example, people with asthma may have asthma attacks while they have flu.</p><p>Certain people are more likely to have complications from the flu, including:</p><ul><li>Adults 65 and older</li><li>Pregnant women</li><li>Children younger than 5</li><li>People with certain chronic health conditions, such as asthma, diabetes, and heart disease</li></ul>How is the flu diagnosed?<p>To diagnose the flu, health care providers will first do a medical history and ask about your symptoms. There are several tests for the flu. For the tests, your provider will swipe the inside of your nose or the back of your throat with a swab. Then the swab will be tested for the flu virus.</p><p>Some tests are quick and give results in 15-20 minutes. But these tests are not as accurate as other flu tests. These other tests can give you the results in one hour or several hours.</p>What are the treatments for the flu?<p>Most people with the flu recover on their own without medical care. People with mild cases of the flu should stay home and avoid contact with others, except to get medical care.</p><p>But if you have symptoms of flu and are in a high risk group or are very sick or worried about your illness, contact your health care provider. You might need antiviral medicines to treat your flu. Antiviral medicines can make the illness milder and shorten the time you are sick. They also can prevent serious flu complications. They usually work best when you start taking them within 2 days of getting sick.</p>Can the flu be prevented?<p>The best way to prevent the flu is to get a flu vaccine every year. But it's also important to have good health habits like covering your cough and washing your hands often. This can help stop the spread of germs and prevent the flu.</p><p>Centers for Disease Control and Prevention</p></content>

    <document rank="9" url="https://medlineplus.gov/homelessnessandhealth.html">
      <content name="title">Homelessness and Health</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Runaways</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What are the causes of homelessness?<p>Each night, hundreds of thousands of people experience homelessness in the United States. Some of these people experience chronic homelessness, while others have temporarily lost their shelter. The reasons why they are homelessness are complex. They can include a combination of factors such as:</p><ul><li>Poverty</li><li>Unemployment</li><li>Lack of affordable housing</li><li>Mental and substance use disorders</li><li>Trauma and violence</li><li>Intimate partner violence</li><li>Justice-system involvement</li><li>Sudden serious illness</li><li>Divorce</li><li>Death of a partner or parent</li><li>Disabilities</li></ul>What is the link between homelessness and health?<p>Poor health can contribute to homelessness. And being homeless can contribute to poor health. Many of the problems that people experiencing homelessness face can make their health worse, including:</p><ul><li>Limited access to health care</li><li>Problems getting enough food</li><li>Trouble staying safe</li><li>Violence</li><li>Stress</li><li>Unsanitary living conditions</li><li>Exposure to severe weather</li></ul>What are some of the common health problems that people experiencing homelessness have?<p>Some of the common health problems that people experiencing homelessness may have include:</p><ul><li>HIV</li><li>Lung diseases, including <span class="qt0">bronchitis</span>, tuberculosis, and pneumonia</li><li>Malnutrition</li><li>Mental health problems</li><li>Substance use problems</li><li>Wounds and skin infections</li></ul><p>Many people experiencing homelessness are dealing with trauma. They may have been abused or assaulted. This includes children, who are at risk for emotional and behavioral problems.</p><p>Contact your local homelessness assistance agency to get the help you need, such as access to shelters, health centers, and free meals.</p></content>

    <document rank="10" url="https://medlineplus.gov/interstitiallungdiseases.html">
      <content name="title">Interstitial Lung Diseases</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Coal Miner's Lung</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Farmer's Lung</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Interstitial Pneumonitis</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Pneumoconiosis</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>Interstitial lung disease is the name for a large group of diseases that inflame or scar the lungs. The inflammation and scarring make it hard to get enough oxygen. The scarring is called pulmonary fibrosis.</p><p>Breathing in dust or other particles in the air is responsible for some types of interstitial lung diseases. Specific types include:</p><ul><li>Black lung disease among coal miners, from inhaling coal dust</li><li>Farmer's lung, from inhaling farm dust</li><li>Asbestosis, from inhaling asbestos fibers</li><li>Siderosis, from inhaling iron from mines or welding fumes</li><li>Silicosis, from inhaling silica dust</li></ul><p>Other causes include autoimmune diseases or occupational exposures to molds, gases, or fumes. Some types of interstitial lung disease have no known cause.</p><p>Treatment depends on the type of exposure and the stage of the disease. It may involve medicines, oxygen therapy, or a lung transplant in severe cases.</p></content>

    <document rank="11" url="https://medlineplus.gov/occupationalhealth.html">
      <content name="title">Occupational Health</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Employee Health</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Occupational Injuries</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>Occupational health problems occur at work or because of the kind of work you do. These problems can include:</p><ul><li>Cuts, fractures (broken bones), and sprains and strains</li><li>Loss of limbs</li><li>Repetitive motion disorders</li><li>Hearing problems caused by exposure to noise</li><li>Vision problems</li><li>Illness caused by breathing, touching, or swallowing unsafe substances</li><li>Illness caused by exposure to radiation</li><li>Exposure to germs in health care settings</li></ul><p>Good job safety and prevention practices can reduce your risk of these problems. Try to stay fit, reduce stress, set up your work area properly, and use the right equipment and gear.</p></content>
      <content name="mesh">Accidents, Occupational</content>
      <content name="mesh">Occupational Stress</content>
      <content name="mesh">Occupational Injuries</content>
      <content name="mesh">Occupational Diseases</content>

      <content name="groupName">Safety Issues</content>
      <content name="snippet"> Occupational health problems occur at work or because of the kind of work you do. These problems can include: Cuts, fractures (broken bones), and sprains and strains   ... </content>

    <document rank="12" url="https://medlineplus.gov/ozone.html">
      <content name="title">Ozone</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>Ozone is a gas. It can be good or bad, depending on where it is. "Good" ozone occurs naturally about 10 to 30 miles above the Earth's surface. It shields us from the sun's ultraviolet rays. Part of the good ozone layer is gone. Man-made chemicals have destroyed it. Without enough good ozone, people may get too much ultraviolet radiation.  This may increase the risk of skin cancer, cataracts, and immune system problems.</p><p>"Bad" ozone is at ground level. It forms when pollutants from cars, factories, and other sources react chemically with sunlight. It is the main ingredient in smog. It is usually worst in the summer. Breathing bad ozone can be harmful. It can cause coughing, throat irritation, worsening of asthma, <span class="qt0">bronchitis</span>, and emphysema. It can lead to permanent lung damage, if you are regularly exposed to it.</p><p>Environmental Protection Agency</p></content>
      <content name="mesh">Ozone</content>
      <content name="mesh">Stratospheric Ozone</content>

    <document rank="13" url="https://medlineplus.gov/pulmonaryrehabilitation.html">
      <content name="title">Pulmonary Rehabilitation</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Chest Physical Therapy</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Lung Rehabilitation</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is pulmonary rehabilitation?<p>Pulmonary rehabilitation, also known as pulmonary rehab or PR, is a program for people who have chronic (ongoing) breathing problems. It can help improve your ability to function and quality of life. PR does not replace your medical treatment. Instead, you use them together.</p><p>PR is often an outpatient program that you do in a hospital or clinic. Some people have PR in their homes. You work with a team of health care providers to find ways to lessen your symptoms, increase your ability to exercise, and make it easier to do your daily activities.</p>Who needs pulmonary rehabilitation?<p>Your health care provider may recommend pulmonary rehabilitation (PR) if you have a chronic lung disease or another condition that makes it hard for you to breathe and limits your activities. For example, PR may help you if you:</p><ul><li>Have COPD (chronic obstructive pulmonary disease). The two main types are emphysema and chronic <span class="qt0">bronchitis</span>. In COPD, your airways (tubes that carry air in and out of your lungs) are partially blocked. This makes it hard to get air in and out.</li><li>Have an interstitial lung disease such as sarcoidosis and pulmonary fibrosis. These diseases cause scarring of the lungs over time. This makes it hard to get enough oxygen.</li><li>Have cystic fibrosis (CF). CF is an inherited disease that causes thick, sticky mucus to collect in the lungs and block the airways.</li><li>Need lung surgery. You may have PR before and after lung surgery to help you prepare for and recover from the surgery.</li><li>Have a muscle-wasting disorder that affects the muscles used for breathing. An example is muscular dystrophy.</li></ul><p>PR works best if you start it before your disease is severe. However, even people who have advanced lung disease can benefit from PR.</p>What does pulmonary rehabilitation include?<p>When you first start pulmonary rehabilitation (PR), your team of health care providers will want to learn more about your health. You will have lung function, exercise, and possibly blood tests. Your team will go over your medical history and current treatments. They may check on your mental health and ask about your diet. Then they will work together to create a plan that is right for you. It may include:</p><ul><li>Exercise training. Your team will come up with an exercise plan to improve your endurance and muscle strength. You will likely have exercises for both your arms and legs. You might use a treadmill, stationary bike, or weights. You may need to start slowly and increase your exercise as you get stronger.</li><li>Nutritional counseling. Being either overweight or underweight can affect your breathing. A nutritious eating plan can help you work towards a healthy weight.</li><li>Education about your disease and how to manage it. This includes learning how to avoid situations that make your symptoms worse, how to avoid infections, and how/when to take your medicines.</li><li>Techniques you can use to save your energy. Your team may teach you easier ways to do daily tasks. For example, you may learn ways to avoid reaching, lifting, or bending. Those movements make it harder to breathe, since they use up energy and make you tighten your abdominal muscles. You may also learn how to better deal with stress, since stress can also take up energy and affect your breathing.</li><li>Breathing strategies. You will learn techniques to improve your breathing. These techniques may increase your oxygen levels, decrease how often you take breaths, and keep your airways open longer.</li><li>Psychological counseling and/or group support. It can feel scary to have trouble breathing. If you have a chronic lung disease, you are more likely to have depression, anxiety, or other emotional problems. Many PR programs include counseling and/or support groups. If not, your PR team may be able to refer you to an organization that offers them.</li>
</ul><p>NIH: National Heart, Lung, and Blood Institute</p></content>

    <document rank="14" url="https://medlineplus.gov/smoking.html">
      <content name="title">Smoking</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Nicotine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Tobacco Smoking</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Cigar smoking</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Cigarette smoking</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Pipe smoking</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What are the health effects of smoking?<p>There's no way around it; smoking is bad for your health. It harms nearly every organ of the body, even some that you would not expect. Cigarette smoking causes many cancers and other health problems. It is also the cause of nearly one in five deaths in the United States.</p><p>Some of the many health problems that smoking can cause include:</p><ul><li>Cancers. Smoking is the most common cause of lung and oral cancers. But it can also cause cancer in many other parts of your body, such as in your larynx (voice box), esophagus, throat, bladder, kidney, liver, stomach, pancreas, colon and rectum, and cervix. It can also cause acute myeloid leukemia (AML). </li><li>Lung diseases. Smoking is the most common cause of COPD. It can also worsen asthma and raise your risk of pneumonia.</li><li>Cardiovascular diseases.  Smoking can damage your blood vessels and make them thicken and grow narrower. This makes your heart  beat faster and raises your blood pressure. Smoking also increases your risk of blood clots and stroke.</li><li>Vision problems. Smoking can raise your risk of  cataracts and cause macular degeneration (AMD).</li></ul><p>You have a greater chance of certain pregnancy problems if you smoke while pregnant.   Your baby is also at higher risk of dying of sudden infant death syndrome (SIDS).</p><p>Smoking also causes addiction to nicotine, a stimulant drug that is in tobacco. Nicotine addiction makes it much harder for people to quit smoking.</p>What are the health risks of secondhand smoke?<p>Your smoke is also bad for other people. If they breathe in your secondhand smoke, they can get many of the same problems as smokers do. These problems can include heart disease and lung cancer. Children exposed to secondhand smoke have a higher risk of ear infections, colds, pneumonia, <span class="qt0">bronchitis</span>, and more severe asthma. If you breathe secondhand smoke while pregnant, you're more likely to have preterm labor and a baby with low birth weight.</p>Are other forms of tobacco also dangerous?<p>Besides cigarettes, there are several other forms of tobacco. Some people smoke tobacco in cigars and water pipes (hookahs). These forms of tobacco also contain harmful chemicals and nicotine. Some cigars contain as much tobacco as an entire pack of cigarettes.</p><p>E-cigarettes often look like cigarettes, but they work differently. They are battery-operated smoking devices. Using an e-cigarette is called vaping. Researchers still have a lot to learn about the health effects of using e-cigarettes. We do know that they contain nicotine, which is highly addictive and a health danger to you and your fetus if you are pregnant, children, and teens. And e-cigarettes also expose non-smokers to secondhand aerosols (rather than secondhand smoke), which contain harmful chemicals.</p><p>Smokeless tobacco, such as chewing tobacco and snuff, is also bad for your health. Smokeless tobacco can cause certain cancers, including oral cancer. It also increases your risk of getting heart disease, gum disease, and oral lesions.</p>Why should I quit?<p>Remember, there is no safe level of tobacco use. Smoking even just one cigarette per day over a lifetime can cause smoking-related cancers and premature death. Quitting smoking can reduce your risk of health problems and add years to your life. The earlier you quit, the greater the benefit. Some immediate benefits of quitting include:</p><ul><li>Lower heart rate and blood pressure</li><li>Less carbon monoxide in the blood (carbon monoxide reduces the blood's ability to carry oxygen)</li><li>Better circulation</li><li>Less coughing and wheezing</li></ul><p>Quitting smoking can be challenging, but it is so important for your health. Contact your health care provider if you need help quitting.</p></content>

    <document rank="15" url="https://medlineplus.gov/breathingproblems.html">
      <content name="title">Breathing Problems</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Dyspnea</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Shortness of Breath</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Tachypnea</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>When you're short of breath, it's hard or uncomfortable for you to take in the oxygen your body needs. You may feel as if you're not getting enough air. Sometimes you can have mild breathing problems because of a stuffy nose or intense exercise. But shortness of breath can also be a sign of a serious disease.</p><p>Many conditions can make you feel short of breath:</p><ul><li>Lung conditions such as asthma, emphysema, or pneumonia</li><li>Problems with your trachea or bronchi, which are part of your airway system</li><li>Heart disease can make you feel breathless if your heart cannot pump enough blood to supply oxygen to your body</li><li>Anxiety and panic attacks</li><li>Allergies</li></ul><p>If you often have trouble breathing, it is important to find out the cause.</p></content>

    <document rank="0" url="https://medlineplus.gov/sinusitis.html">
      <content name="title"><span class="qt0">Sinusitis</span></content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Sinus Infection</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p><span class="qt0">Sinusitis</span> means your sinuses are inflamed. The cause can be an infection or another problem. Your sinuses are hollow air spaces within the bones surrounding the nose. They produce mucus, which drains into the nose.  If your nose is swollen, this can block the sinuses and cause pain.</p><p>There are several types of <span class="qt0">sinusitis</span>, including:</p><ul><li>Acute, which lasts up to 4 weeks</li><li>Subacute, which lasts 4 to 12 weeks</li><li>Chronic, which lasts more than 12 weeks and can continue for months or even years</li><li>Recurrent, with several attacks within a year</li></ul><p>Acute <span class="qt0">sinusitis</span> often starts as a cold, which then turns into a bacterial infection.  Allergies, nasal problems, and certain diseases can also cause acute and chronic <span class="qt0">sinusitis</span>.</p><p>Symptoms of <span class="qt0">sinusitis</span> can include fever, weakness, fatigue, cough, and congestion. There may also be mucus drainage in the back of the throat, called postnasal drip. Your health care professional diagnoses <span class="qt0">sinusitis</span> based on your symptoms and an examination of your nose and face. You may also need imaging tests. Treatments include antibiotics, decongestants, and pain relievers. Using heat pads on the inflamed area, saline nasal sprays, and vaporizers can also help.</p></content>
      <content name="mesh"><span class="qt0">Sinusitis</span></content>

    <document rank="1" url="https://medlineplus.gov/allergy.html">
      <content name="title">Allergy</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Allergic Rhinitis</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Hypersensitivity</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>An allergy is a reaction by your immune system to something that does not bother most other people. People who have allergies often are sensitive to more than one thing. Substances that often cause reactions are:</p><ul><li>Pollen</li><li>Dust mites</li><li>Mold spores</li><li>Pet dander</li><li>Food</li><li>Insect stings</li><li>Medicines</li></ul><p>Normally, your immune system fights germs. It is your body's defense system. In most allergic reactions, however, it is responding to a false alarm. Genes and the environment probably both play a role.</p><p>Allergies can cause a variety of symptoms such as a runny nose, sneezing, itching, rashes, swelling, or asthma. Allergies can range from minor to severe.  Anaphylaxis is a severe reaction that can be life-threatening.   Doctors use skin and blood tests to diagnose allergies. Treatments include medicines, allergy shots, and avoiding the substances that cause the reactions.</p></content>

    <document rank="2" url="https://medlineplus.gov/badbreath.html">
      <content name="title">Bad Breath</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Breath Odor</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Halitosis</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>There are many reasons why you might have bad breath.  You can get it if you don't brush and floss regularly. Bacteria that build up in your mouth and between your teeth produce the bad odor. Other problems in your mouth, such as gum disease, dry mouth, or cavities, may also cause it. <span class="qt0">Sinusitis</span> or problems with your nose may be to blame. You can also have bad breath if you eat some foods, like raw onions, garlic, or cabbage. And of course smoking causes its own bad smell. Some diseases and medicines can cause a specific breath odor.</p><p>Having good dental habits, like brushing and flossing regularly, help fight bad breath. Mouthwashes, mints or chewing gum may make your breath fresher. If you have a disease that causes the bad breath, treating the disease may help give you fresher breath.</p></content>

    <document rank="3" url="https://medlineplus.gov/commoncold.html">
      <content name="title">Common Cold</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Cold, Common</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is the common cold?<p>The common cold is a mild infection of your upper respiratory tract (which includes your nose and throat). Colds are probably the most common illness. Adults have an average of 2-3 colds per year, and children have even more. Colds are more common in the winter and spring, but you can get them at any time.</p>What causes the common cold?<p>More than 200 different viruses can cause a cold, but rhinoviruses are the most common type. The viruses that cause colds are very contagious. They can spread from person to person through the air and close personal contact. You can also get infected when you touch something that has the virus on it and then touch your eyes, mouth, or nose. For example, you could get a cold after you shake hands with someone who has a cold or touch a doorknob that has the germs on it, and then touch your face.</p>What are the symptoms of the common cold?<p>The symptoms of a common cold usually include:</p><ul><li>Sneezing</li><li>Stuffy nose (congestion)</li><li>Runny nose</li><li>Sore throat</li><li>Coughing</li><li>Headache</li></ul><p>The symptoms usually start a few days after you become infected with the virus. Some symptoms can last for 10-14 days.</p>What are the treatments for the common cold?<p>There is no cure for the common cold. But there are treatments that can make you feel better while you wait for the cold to go away on its own:</p><ul><li>Getting lots of rest.</li><li>Drinking plenty of fluids.</li><li>Using a clean humidifier or cool mist vaporizer.</li><li>Gargling with warm salt water.</li><li>Using saline nose drops or sprays.</li><li>Taking over-the-counter pain or cold and cough medicines. But you need to be careful with certain medicines:
	<ul><li>Children and teens should not take aspirin.</li><li>Some cold and cough medicines contain ingredients that are not recommended for children. Talk with your child's health care provider before giving your child any cold and cough medicines.</li><li>Some cold and cough medicines contain pain relievers. If you also take a separate pain reliever with these medicines, you could be getting a dangerous amount of the pain reliever. Read the labels on the medicines and follow the instructions carefully. If you have questions, ask your provider or a pharmacist.</li></ul>
</li></ul><p>Antibiotics will not help with a cold. Antibiotics help with bacterial infections, not with viral infections such as colds.</p><p>Most people who have a cold will feel better after a week or two. However, some people who get a cold may develop other illnesses, such as bronchitis or pneumonia. This is more common in people with weakened immune systems, asthma, or other respiratory conditions. Contact your provider if you or your child have symptoms that concern you, such as:</p><ul><li>Trouble breathing or fast breathing</li><li>Dehydration</li><li>Fever that lasts longer than 4 days</li><li>Symptoms that last more than 10 days without improvement</li><li>Symptoms, such as fever or cough, that improve but then return or worsen</li><li>Worsening of chronic medical conditions</li></ul>Can the common cold be prevented?<p>There is no vaccine to protect against the common cold. But you may be able to reduce your risk of getting or spreading a cold by:</p><ul><li>Washing your hands often with soap and water for at least 20 seconds.</li><li>Avoiding touching your face, nose, or mouth with unwashed hands.</li><li>Avoiding close contact, such as kissing, shaking hands, and sharing cups and eating utensils, with others if you are sick or they are sick.</li><li>Cleaning and disinfecting surfaces that you frequently touch.</li><li>Covering coughs and sneezes with a tissue. Then throw away the tissue and wash your hands.</li><li>Staying home when sick.</li></ul><p>Centers for Disease Control and Prevention</p></content>

    <document rank="4" url="https://medlineplus.gov/fungalinfections.html">
      <content name="title">Fungal Infections</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Cryptococcosis</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Infections, Fungal</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Mycoses</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>If you have ever had athlete's foot or a yeast infection, you can blame a fungus. A fungus is a primitive organism. Mushrooms, mold and mildew are examples. Fungi live in air, in soil, on plants and in water. Some live in the human body. Only about half of all types of fungi are harmful.</p><p>Some fungi reproduce through tiny spores in the air. You can inhale the spores or they can land on you. As a result, fungal infections often start in the lungs or on the skin. You are more likely to get a fungal infection if you have a weakened immune system or take antibiotics.</p><p>Fungi can be difficult to kill. For skin and nail infections, you can apply medicine directly to the infected area. Oral antifungal medicines are also available for serious infections.</p></content>
      <content name="mesh">Mycoses</content>
      <content name="groupName">Infections</content>
      <content name="groupName">Skin, Hair and Nails</content>
      <content name="snippet"> If you have ever had athlete's foot or a yeast infection, you can blame a fungus. A fungus is a primitive organism. Mushrooms, mold and mildew are examples.  ... </content>
    </document>
    <document rank="5" url="https://medlineplus.gov/hayfever.html">
      <content name="title">Hay Fever</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Allergic Rhinitis</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Pollen Allergy</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Seasonal Allergies</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>Each spring, summer, and fall, trees, weeds, and grasses release tiny pollen grains into the air. Some of the pollen ends up in your nose and throat. This can trigger a type of allergy called hay fever.</p><p>Symptoms can include:</p><ul><li>Sneezing, often with a runny or clogged nose</li><li>Coughing and postnasal drip</li><li>Itching eyes, nose and throat</li><li>Red and watery eyes</li><li>Dark circles under the eyes</li></ul><p>Your health care provider may diagnose hay fever based on a physical exam and your symptoms. Sometimes skin or blood tests are used. Taking medicines and using nasal sprays can relieve symptoms. You can also rinse out your nose, but be sure to use distilled or sterilized water with saline. Allergy shots can help make you less sensitive to pollen and provide long-term relief.</p></content>
      <content name="mesh">Rhinitis, Allergic, Seasonal</content>
      <content name="groupName">Ear, Nose and Throat</content>
      <content name="groupName">Immune System</content>
      <content name="snippet"> Each spring, summer, and fall, trees, weeds, and grasses release tiny pollen grains into the air. Some of the pollen ends up in your nose and throat. This  ... </content>
    </document>
    <document rank="6" url="https://medlineplus.gov/telehealth.html">
      <content name="title">Telehealth</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Telemedicine</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is telehealth?<p>Telehealth is the use of communications technologies to provide health care from a distance. These technologies may include computers, cameras, videoconferencing, the Internet, and satellite and wireless communications. Some examples of telehealth include:</p><ul><li>A "virtual visit" with a health care provider, through a phone call or video chat</li><li>Remote patient monitoring, which lets your provider check on you while you are at home. For example, you might wear a device that measures your heart rate and sends that information to your provider.</li><li>A surgeon using robotic technology to do surgery from a different location</li><li>Sensors that can alert caregivers if a person with dementia leaves the house</li><li>Sending your provider a message through your electronic health record (EHR)</li><li>Watching an online video that your provider sent you about how to use an inhaler</li><li>Getting an email, phone, or text reminder that it's time for a cancer screening</li></ul>What is the difference between telemedicine and telehealth?<p>Sometimes people use the term telemedicine to mean the same thing as telehealth. Telehealth is a broader term. It includes telemedicine. But it also includes things like training for health care providers, health care administrative meetings, and services provided by pharmacists and social workers.</p>What are the benefits of telehealth?<p>Some of the benefits of telehealth include:</p><ul><li>Getting care at home, especially for people who can't easily get to their providers' offices</li><li>Getting care from a specialist who is not close by</li><li>Getting care after office hours</li><li>More communication with your providers</li><li>Better communication and coordination between health care providers</li><li>More support for people who are managing their health conditions, especially chronic conditions such as diabetes</li><li>Lower cost, since virtual visits may be cheaper than in-person visits</li></ul>What are the problems with telehealth?<p>Some of the problems with telehealth include:</p><ul><li>If your virtual visit is with someone who is not your regular provider, he or she may not have all of your medical history</li><li>After a virtual visit, it may be up to you to coordinate your care with your regular provider</li><li>In some cases, the provider may not be able to make the right diagnosis without examining you in person. Or your provider may need you to come in for a lab test.</li><li>There may be problems with the technology, for example, if you lose the connection, there is a problem with the software, etc.</li><li>Some insurance companies may not cover telehealth visits</li></ul>What types of care can I get using telehealth?<p>The types of care that you can get using telehealth may include:</p><ul><li>General health care, like wellness visits</li><li>Prescriptions for medicine</li><li>Dermatology (skin care)</li><li>Eye exams</li><li>Nutrition counseling</li><li>Mental health counseling</li><li>Urgent care conditions, such as <span class="qt0">sinusitis</span>, urinary tract infections, common rashes, etc.</li></ul><p>For telehealth visits, just like with an in-person visit, it is important to be prepared and have good communication with the provider.</p></content>
      <content name="mesh">Telemedicine</content>
      <content name="groupName">Health System</content>
      <content name="snippet"> ... Mental health counseling  Urgent care conditions, such as <span class="qt0">sinusitis</span>, urinary tract infections, common rashes, etc.  For telehealth  ... </content>

    <document rank="7" url="https://medlineplus.gov/antibioticresistance.html">
      <content name="title">Antibiotic Resistance</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Antimicrobial Resistance</content>
      <content name="altTitle">VRE</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Vancomycin-Resistant Enterococci</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Vancomycin-Resistant Staphylococcus aureus</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What are antibiotics?<p>Antibiotics are medicines that treat bacterial infections in humans and animals. They work by killing the bacteria or making it hard for the bacteria to grow and multiply. When used properly, antibiotics can save lives. But there is a growing problem of antibiotic resistance.</p>What is antibiotic resistance?<p>Antibiotic resistance happens when bacteria change and can resist the effects of an antibiotic. The bacteria are not killed, and they continue to grow. The infections these bacteria cause are called resistant infections. Resistant infections can be difficult, and sometimes impossible, to treat. In some cases, they can even be deadly. </p><p>Antibiotic resistance does not mean that your body is resistant to antibiotics.</p><p>There are many different types of resistant infections. They include MRSA and drug-resistant tuberculosis (TB). If you get a resistant infection, you might need:</p><ul><li>A long hospital stay</li><li>A long recovery</li><li>Follow-up visits with health care providers</li><li>Treatments that are expensive and may have severe side effects</li></ul><p>Antibiotic resistance is one type of antimicrobial resistance. There are other types; viruses, fungi, and parasites can also become resistant to medicines.</p>How do bacteria become resistant to antibiotics?<p>Antibiotic resistance is a natural process that happens over time. To survive, bacteria can develop defense strategies against antibiotics. This happens through genetic changes in the bacteria. These resistant bacteria survive, grow, and spread.</p><p>Each time you take antibiotics, there is a risk that the bacteria will become resistant. So it is important to only take antibiotics when you really need them. They won't work on viral infections such as colds and the flu. And you don't need antibiotics for every bacterial infection. For example, you may not need them for some sinus and ear infections.</p>Who is more likely to develop an antibiotic-resistant infection?<p>Anyone can develop an antibiotic-resistant infection, but certain people may be at higher risk, including those who:</p><ul><li>Have a weakened immune system from diseases such as HIV or from taking medicines that affect the immune system</li><li>Are getting medical care such as surgery and hospital stays</li><li>Take antibiotics for a long time</li><li>Are infants, especially if they were born prematurely</li><li>Are older adults</li></ul>How can antibiotic resistance be prevented?<p>There will always be some antibiotic resistance, since it happens naturally. But you can help fight against antibiotic resistance by taking these steps:</p><ul><li>Don't use antibiotics for viruses.</li><li>Don't pressure your health care provider to give you an antibiotic if they don't think you need one.</li><li>When you take antibiotics, follow the directions carefully.</li><li>Don't share your antibiotics with others.</li><li>Don't save antibiotics for later or use someone else's prescription.</li><li>Help prevent the spread of bacterial infections with good hygiene habits. These include washing your hands often and covering your mouth and nose when coughing or sneezing.</li></ul><p>Centers for Disease Control and Prevention</p></content>
      <content name="mesh">Drug Resistance, Bacterial</content>
      <content name="mesh">Drug Resistance, Microbial</content>

    <document rank="8" url="https://medlineplus.gov/flu.html">
      <content name="title">Flu</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Grippe</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Influenza</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Seasonal flu</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is the flu?<p>The flu, also called influenza, is a respiratory infection caused by viruses. Each year, millions of Americans get sick with the flu. Sometimes it causes mild illness. But it can also be serious or even deadly, especially for people over 65, newborn babies, and people with certain chronic illnesses.</p>What causes the flu?<p>The flu is caused by flu viruses that spread from person to person. When someone with the flu coughs, sneezes, or talks, they spray tiny droplets. These droplets can land in the mouths or noses of people who are nearby. Less often, a person may get flu by touching a surface or object that has flu virus on it and then touching their own mouth, nose, or possibly their eyes.</p>What are the symptoms of the flu?<p>Symptoms of the flu come on suddenly and may include:</p><ul><li>Fever or feeling feverish/chills</li><li>Cough</li><li>Sore throat</li><li>Runny or stuffy nose</li><li>Muscle or body aches</li><li>Headaches</li><li>Fatigue (tiredness)</li></ul><p>Some people may also have vomiting and diarrhea. This is more common in children.</p><p>Sometimes people have trouble figuring out whether they have a cold or the flu. There are differences between them:</p>Signs and SymptomsColdFluStart of symptomsSlowlySuddenlyFeverRarelyUsuallyAchesSometimes (slight)UsuallyFatigue, weaknessSometimesUsuallyHeadacheRarelyCommonStuffy nose, sneezing, or sore throatCommonSometimes<p>Sometimes people say that they have a "flu" when they really have something else. For example, "stomach flu" isn't the flu; it's gastroenteritis.</p>What other problems can the flu cause?<p>Some people who get the flu will develop complications. Some of these complications can be serious or even life-threatening. They include:</p><ul><li>Bronchitis</li><li>Ear infection</li><li>Sinus infection</li><li>Pneumonia</li><li>Inflammation of the heart (myocarditis), brain (encephalitis), or muscle tissues (myositis, rhabdomyolysis)</li></ul><p>The flu also can make chronic health problems worse. For example, people with asthma may have asthma attacks while they have flu.</p><p>Certain people are more likely to have complications from the flu, including:</p><ul><li>Adults 65 and older</li><li>Pregnant women</li><li>Children younger than 5</li><li>People with certain chronic health conditions, such as asthma, diabetes, and heart disease</li></ul>How is the flu diagnosed?<p>To diagnose the flu, health care providers will first do a medical history and ask about your symptoms. There are several tests for the flu. For the tests, your provider will swipe the inside of your nose or the back of your throat with a swab. Then the swab will be tested for the flu virus.</p><p>Some tests are quick and give results in 15-20 minutes. But these tests are not as accurate as other flu tests. These other tests can give you the results in one hour or several hours.</p>What are the treatments for the flu?<p>Most people with the flu recover on their own without medical care. People with mild cases of the flu should stay home and avoid contact with others, except to get medical care.</p><p>But if you have symptoms of flu and are in a high risk group or are very sick or worried about your illness, contact your health care provider. You might need antiviral medicines to treat your flu. Antiviral medicines can make the illness milder and shorten the time you are sick. They also can prevent serious flu complications. They usually work best when you start taking them within 2 days of getting sick.</p>Can the flu be prevented?<p>The best way to prevent the flu is to get a flu vaccine every year. But it's also important to have good health habits like covering your cough and washing your hands often. This can help stop the spread of germs and prevent the flu.</p><p>Centers for Disease Control and Prevention</p></content>

    <document rank="9" url="https://medlineplus.gov/noseinjuriesanddisorders.html">
      <content name="title">Nose Injuries and Disorders</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Nasal Disorders</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Nosebleed</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>Your nose is important to your health. It filters the air you breathe, removing dust, germs, and irritants. It warms and moistens the air to keep your lungs and tubes that lead to them from drying out. Your nose also contains the nerve cells that help your sense of smell. When there is a problem with your nose, your whole body can suffer. For example, the stuffy nose of the common cold can make it hard for you to breathe, sleep, or get comfortable. </p><p>Many problems besides the common cold can affect the nose. They include:</p><ul><li>	Deviated septum - a shifting of the wall that divides the nasal cavity into halves</li><li>	Nasal polyps - soft growths that develop on the lining of your nose or sinuses</li><li>	Nosebleeds </li><li>	Rhinitis - inflammation of the nose and sinuses sometimes caused by allergies. The main symptom is a runny nose.</li><li>Nasal fractures, also known as a broken nose</li></ul></content>

    <document rank="10" url="https://medlineplus.gov/pneumococcalinfections.html">
      <content name="title">Pneumococcal Infections</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Streptococcus Pneumoniae Infections</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>Pneumococci are a type of streptococcus bacteria. The bacteria spread through contact with people who are ill or by healthy people who carry the bacteria in the back of their nose. Pneumococcal infections can be mild or severe. The most common types of infections are:</p><ul><li>Ear infections</li><li>Sinus infections</li><li>Pneumonia</li><li>Sepsis</li><li>Meningitis</li></ul><p>How the diagnosis is made depends upon where the infection is. Your doctor will do a physical exam and ask about your medical history.  Possible tests may include blood, imaging, or lab tests. Treatment is with antibiotics. Vaccines can prevent pneumococcal infections. There are two vaccines. One is for infants and young children.  The other is for people at high risk, including those who are over 65 years old, have chronic illnesses or weak immune systems, smoke, have asthma, or live in long-term care facilities.</p><p>Centers for Disease Control and Prevention</p></content>

    <document rank="0" url="https://medlineplus.gov/migraine.html">
      <content name="title"><span class="qt0">Migraine</span></content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Vascular Headache</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What are <span class="qt0">migraines</span>?<p><span class="qt0">Migraines</span> are a recurring type of headache. They cause moderate to severe pain that is throbbing or pulsing. The pain is often on one side of your head. You may also have other symptoms, such as nausea and weakness. You may be sensitive to light and sound.</p>What causes <span class="qt0">migraines</span>?<p>Researchers believe that <span class="qt0">migraine</span> has a genetic cause. There are also a number of factors that can trigger a <span class="qt0">migraine</span>. These factors vary from person to person, and they include:</p><ul><li>Stress</li><li>Anxiety</li><li>Hormonal changes in women</li><li>Bright or flashing lights</li><li>Loud noises</li><li>Strong smells</li><li>Medicines</li><li>Too much or not enough sleep</li><li>Sudden changes in weather or environment</li><li>Overexertion (too much physical activity)</li><li>Tobacco</li><li>Caffeine or caffeine withdrawal</li><li>Skipped meals</li><li>Medication overuse (taking medicine for <span class="qt0">migraines</span> too often)</li></ul><p>Some people have found that certain foods or ingredients can trigger headaches, especially when they are combined with other triggers. These foods and ingredients include:</p><ul><li>Alcohol</li><li>Chocolate</li><li>Aged cheeses</li><li>Monosodium glutamate (MSG)</li><li>Some fruits and nuts</li><li>Fermented or pickled goods</li><li>Yeast</li><li>Cured or processed meats</li></ul>Who is at risk for <span class="qt0">migraines</span>?<p>About 12% of Americans get <span class="qt0">migraines</span>. They can affect anyone, but you are more likely to have them if you:</p><ul><li>Are a woman. Women are three times more likely than men to get <span class="qt0">migraines</span>.</li><li>Have a family history of <span class="qt0">migraines</span>. Most people with <span class="qt0">migraines</span> have family members who have <span class="qt0">migraines</span>.</li><li>Have other medical conditions, such as depression, anxiety, bipolar disorder, sleep disorders, and epilepsy.</li></ul>What are the symptoms of <span class="qt0">migraines</span>?<p>There are four different phases of <span class="qt0">migraines</span>. You may not always go through every phase each time you have a <span class="qt0">migraine</span>.:</p><ul><li>Prodome. This phase starts up to 24 hours before you get the <span class="qt0">migraine</span>. You have early signs and symptoms, such as food cravings, unexplained mood changes, uncontrollable yawning, fluid retention, and increased urination.</li><li>Aura. If you have this phase, you might see flashing or bright lights or zig-zag lines. You may have muscle weakness or feel like you are being touched or grabbed. An aura can happen just before or during a <span class="qt0">migraine</span>.</li><li>Headache. A <span class="qt0">migraine</span> usually starts gradually and then becomes more severe. It typically causes throbbing or pulsing pain, which is often on one side of your head. But sometimes you can have a <span class="qt0">migraine</span> without a headache. Other <span class="qt0">migraine</span> symptoms may include</li><ul><li>Increased sensitivity to light, noise, and odors</li><li>Nausea and vomiting</li><li>Worsened pain when you move, cough, or sneeze</li></ul><li>Postdrome (following the headache). You may feel exhausted, weak, and confused after a <span class="qt0">migraine</span>. This can last up to a day.</li></ul><p><span class="qt0">Migraines</span> are more common in the morning; people often wake up with them. Some people have <span class="qt0">migraines</span> at predictable times, such as before menstruation or on weekends following a stressful week of work.</p>How are <span class="qt0">migraines</span> diagnosed?<p>To make a diagnosis, your health care provider will:</p><ul><li>Take your medical history</li><li>Ask about your symptoms</li><li>Do a physical and neurological exam</li></ul><p>An important part of diagnosing <span class="qt0">migraines</span> is to rule out other medical conditions which could be causing the symptoms. So you may also have blood tests, an MRI or CT scan, or other tests.</p>How are <span class="qt0">migraines</span> treated?<p>There is no cure for <span class="qt0">migraines</span>. Treatment focuses on relieving symptoms and preventing additional attacks.</p><p>There are different types of medicines to relieve symptoms. They include triptan drugs, ergotamine drugs, and pain relievers. The sooner you take the medicine, the more effective it is.</p><p>There are also other things you can do to feel better:</p><ul><li>Resting with your eyes closed in a quiet, darkened room</li><li>Placing a cool cloth or ice pack on your forehead</li><li>Drinking fluids</li></ul><p>There are some lifestyle changes you can make to prevent <span class="qt0">migraines</span>:</p><ul><li>Stress management strategies, such as exercise, relaxation techniques, and biofeedback, may reduce the number and severity of <span class="qt0">migraines</span>. Biofeedback uses electronic devices to teach you to control certain body functions, such as your heartbeat, blood pressure, and muscle tension.</li><li>Make a log of what seems to trigger your <span class="qt0">migraines</span>. You can learn what you need to avoid, such as certain foods and medicines. It also help you figure out what you should do, such as establishing a consistent sleep schedule and eating regular meals.</li><li>Hormone therapy may help some women whose <span class="qt0">migraines</span> seem to be linked to their menstrual cycle</li><li>If you have obesity, losing weight may also be helpful</li></ul><p>If you have frequent or severe <span class="qt0">migraines</span>, you may need to take medicines to prevent further attacks. Talk with your health care provider about which drug would be right for you.</p><p>Certain natural treatments, such as riboflavin (vitamin B2) and coenzyme Q10, may help prevent <span class="qt0">migraines</span>. If your magnesium level is low, you can try taking magnesium. There is also an herb, butterbur, which some people take to prevent <span class="qt0">migraines</span>. But butterbur may not be safe for long-term use. Always check with your health care provider before taking any supplements.</p><p>NIH: National Institute of Neurological Disorders and Stroke</p></content>
      <content name="mesh"><span class="qt0">Migraine</span> Disorders</content>

    <document rank="1" url="https://medlineplus.gov/headache.html">
      <content name="title">Headache</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Cluster Headache</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Tension Headache</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Vascular Headache</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>Almost everyone has had a headache. Headache is the most common form of pain. It's a major reason people miss days at work or school or visit the doctor.</p><p>The most common type of headache is a tension headache. Tension headaches are due to tight muscles in your shoulders, neck, scalp and jaw. They are often related to stress, depression or anxiety. You are more likely to get tension headaches if you work too much, don't get enough sleep, miss meals, or use alcohol.</p><p>Other common types of headaches include <span class="qt0">migraines</span>, cluster headaches, and sinus headaches. Most people can feel much better by making lifestyle changes, learning ways to relax and taking pain relievers.</p><p>Not all headaches require a doctor's attention. But sometimes headaches warn of a more serious disorder. Let your health care provider know if you have sudden, severe headaches. Get medical help right away if you have a headache after a blow to your head, or if you have a headache along with a stiff neck, fever, confusion, loss of consciousness, or pain in the eye or ear.</p><p>NIH: National Institute of Neurological Disorders and Stroke</p></content>
      <content name="mesh">Headache</content>

    <document rank="2" url="https://medlineplus.gov/caffeine.html">
      <content name="title">Caffeine</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Energy Drinks</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is caffeine?<p>Caffeine is a bitter substance that occurs naturally in more than 60 plants including:</p><ul><li>Coffee beans</li><li>Tea leaves</li><li>Kola nuts, which are used to flavor soft drink colas</li><li>Cacao pods, which are used to make chocolate products</li></ul><p>There is also synthetic (man-made) caffeine, which is added to some medicines, foods, and drinks. For example, some pain relievers, cold medicines, and over-the-counter medicines for alertness contain synthetic caffeine. So do energy drinks and "energy-boosting" gums and snacks.</p><p>Most people consume caffeine from drinks. The amount of caffeine in different drinks can vary a lot, but it is generally:</p><ul><li>An 8-ounce cup of coffee: 95-200 mg</li><li>A 12-ounce can of cola: 35-45 mg</li><li>An 8-ounce energy drink: 70-100 mg</li><li>An 8-ounce cup of tea: 14-60 mg</li></ul>What are caffeine's effects on the body?<p>Caffeine has many effects on your body's metabolism. It:</p><ul><li>Stimulates your central nervous system, which can make you feel more awake and give you a boost of energy</li><li>Is a diuretic, meaning that it helps your body get rid of extra salt and water by urinating more</li><li>Increases the release of acid in your stomach, sometimes leading to an upset stomach or heartburn</li><li>May interfere with the absorption of calcium in the body</li><li>Increases your blood pressure</li></ul><p>Within one hour of eating or drinking caffeine, it reaches its peak level in your blood. You may continue to feel the effects of caffeine for four to six hours.</p>What are the side effects from too much caffeine?<p>For most people, it is not harmful to consume up to 400mg of caffeine a day. If you do eat or drink too much caffeine, it can cause health problems, such as:</p><ul><li>Restlessness and shakiness</li><li>Insomnia</li><li>Headaches</li><li>Dizziness</li><li>Fast heart rate</li><li>Dehydration</li><li>Anxiety</li><li>Dependency, so you need to take more of it to get the same results</li></ul><p>Some people are more sensitive to the effects of caffeine than others.</p>What are energy drinks, and why can they be a problem?<p>Energy drinks are beverages that have added caffeine. The amount of caffeine in energy drinks can vary widely, and sometimes the labels on the drinks do not give you the actual amount of caffeine in them. Energy drinks may also contain sugars, vitamins, herbs, and supplements.</p><p>Companies that make energy drinks claim that the drinks can increase alertness and improve physical and mental performance. This has helped make the drinks popular with American teens and young adults. There's limited data showing that energy drinks might temporarily improve alertness and physical endurance. There is not enough evidence to show that they enhance strength or power. But what we do know is that energy drinks can be dangerous because they have large amounts of caffeine. And since they have lots of sugar, they can contribute to weight gain and worsen diabetes.</p><p>Sometimes young people mix their energy drinks with alcohol. It is dangerous to combine alcohol and caffeine. Caffeine can interfere with your ability to recognize how drunk you are, which can lead you to drink more. This also makes you more likely to make bad decisions.</p>Who should avoid or limit caffeine?<p>You should check with your health care provider about whether you should limit or avoid caffeine if you:</p><ul><li>Are pregnant, since caffeine passes through the placenta to your baby.</li><li>Are breastfeeding, since a small amount of caffeine that you consume is passed along to your baby.</li><li>Have sleep disorders, including insomnia.</li><li>Have <span class="qt0">migraines</span> or other chronic headaches.</li><li>Have anxiety.</li><li>Have GERD or ulcers.</li><li>Have arrhythmia (a problem with the rate or rhythm of your heartbeat).</li><li>Have high blood pressure.</li><li>Take certain medicines or supplements, including stimulants, certain antibiotics, asthma medicines, and heart medicines. Check with your health care provider about whether there might be interactions between caffeine and any medicines and supplements that you take.</li><li>Are a child or teen. Neither should have as much caffeine as adults. Children can be especially sensitive to the effects of caffeine.</li></ul>What is caffeine withdrawal?<p>If you have been consuming caffeine on a regular basis and then suddenly stop, you may have caffeine withdrawal. Symptoms can include:</p><ul><li>Headaches</li><li>Drowsiness</li><li>Irritability</li><li>Nausea</li><li>Trouble concentrating</li></ul><p>These symptoms usually go away after a couple of days.</p></content>
