    <document rank="3" url="https://medlineplus.gov/antidepressants.html">
      <content name="title">Antidepressants</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">SNRI </content>
      <content name="altTitle">SSRIs</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Tricyclic antidepressants</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What are antidepressants?<p>Antidepressants are prescription medicines to treat depression. Depression is more than feeling a little sad or "blue" for a few days. It's a very common, serious medical illness that affects your mood and general mental health It can make you feel tired, hopeless, worried, or fearful. It can change your thinking, sleeping, and eating. Depression may make some people think about ending their lives.</p><p>But antidepressants can help many people who have depression. Researchers think antidepressants may help improve the way your brain uses certain chemicals that control mood or stress.</p>Are antidepressants used for other conditions?<p>A health care provider may prescribe antidepressants for anxiety, chronic pain, or insomnia. Sometimes providers also prescribe antidepressants for other conditions.</p>What are the different types of antidepressants?<p>There are many types of antidepressants. Each one works differently. Providers usually prescribe newer antidepressants first because they don't cause as many side effects as older types. They also seem to help more kinds of depression and anxiety problems.</p><p>Most of the newer antidepressants belong to one of these three groups:</p><ul><li>Selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs)</li><li>Serotonin and norepinephrine reuptake inhibitors (SNRIs)</li><li>Atypical antidepressants, which are newer antidepressants that don't fit into the other groups</li></ul><p>If these antidepressants don't help, your provider might suggest one of the older antidepressants. The older types include tricyclic antidepressants (TCAs), tetracyclics, and monoamine oxidase inhibitors (MAOIs). Even though these antidepressants may cause more serious side effects, the benefits may outweigh the risks for some people.</p>Which type of antidepressant is right for me?<p>Our bodies and brains all work differently. That means one antidepressant won't work for everyone.  You may need to try two or more medicines before you find one that works for you.</p><p>Your provider will work with you to choose the best option to try first. You'll consider questions such as:</p><ul><li>Which symptoms bother you most? Some antidepressants may do a better job helping specific symptoms, such as trouble sleeping.</li><li>What other medicines and supplements do you take? Some antidepressants can cause problems if you take them with certain medicines and herbs.</li><li>Did a certain antidepressant work well for a close relative? An antidepressant that helped a parent, brother, or sister could be a good choice for you, too.</li><li>Do you have other health conditions? Certain antidepressants can make some other conditions better or worse. Any other conditions that you have will be part of choosing your depression treatment.</li><li>Are you pregnant, planning for pregnancy, or breastfeeding?  If so, your provider will help you find a way to treat your depression that's safe for you and your baby.</li></ul>How long do antidepressants take to work?<p>Antidepressants usually take 4 to 8 weeks to work, so you'll need to be patient. You may notice that some problems, such as sleeping and eating, get better before your mood improves. That's a good sign. You may just need to give the medicine a little more time to do its job.</p><p>Sometimes an antidepressant helps at first, but symptoms return while you're still taking it. But there's usually another one you can try. To get more relief from depression, your provider may suggest combining two antidepressants, using another kind of medicine with an antidepressant, or adding talk therapy or other approaches to improve your mental health.</p>How long will I need to take an antidepressant?<p>When an antidepressant starts to work, you and your provider can decide how long you need to stay on it. The typical length of treatment is 6 to 12 months, but some people may stay on antidepressants for much longer.</p>What are the side effects of antidepressants?<p>Not everyone has side effects from antidepressants. But if you do have them, they're usually mild and may get better over time as your body gets used to the new medicine.</p><p>The most common side effects from antidepressants include:</p><ul><li>Nausea and vomiting</li><li>Weight gain</li><li>Diarrhea</li><li>Sleepiness</li><li>Sexual problems, such as a lack of desire or ability to have sex</li></ul><p>When thinking about side effects, it's important to remember that there are also risks from not treating depression. Your provider can help you think through the pros and cons of all your options.</p><p>If you have any side effects from antidepressants, your provider may suggest ways to manage them while you wait to see if the antidepressant will work. If the side effects bother you too much, you may need to change antidepressants. But you should never change your dose or stop taking an antidepressant on your own.</p><p>If you have serious problems or notice any changes that worry you, such as new or worsening symptoms, unusual changes in your mood, or you start acting differently, call your provider right away.</p><p>In some cases, children, teenagers, and young adults under 25 may be more likely to think about hurting or killing themselves when starting antidepressants or when the dose is changed. Get medical help right away if this happens.</p>What can I do to take antidepressants safely?<p>Antidepressants are generally safe when you use them correctly:</p><ul><li>Tell your provider about everything you take. That includes medicines, herbs, supplements, and over-the-counter medicines you take, such as pain relievers and decongestants. Be honest about recreational drugs and alcohol, too.</li><li>Try to get all your medicines from the same pharmacy. That way the pharmacist can warn you and your provider if you take medicines that may cause problems when used together.</li><li>Follow all instructions about how to take your medicine.</li><li>Talk with your provider if side effects bother you.</li><li>Never stop taking antidepressants without your provider's help. Stopping too fast may make depression come back. You could even make your condition worse. To stop antidepressants safely, you need to give your body time to get used to being without the medicine. Your provider can tell you the safest way to go off an antidepressant.</li></ul><p>NIH: National Institute of Mental Health</p></content>
      <content name="mesh">Antidepressive Agents</content>
      <content name="groupName">Mental Health and Behavior</content>
      <content name="groupName">Drug Therapy</content>
      <content name="snippet">What are antidepressants? Antidepressants are prescription medicines to treat depression. Depression is more than feeling a little sad or "blue" for a  ... </content>
    </document>
    <document rank="4" url="https://medlineplus.gov/anxiety.html">
      <content name="title">Anxiety</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Generalized Anxiety Disorder</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is anxiety?<p>Anxiety is a feeling of fear, dread, and uneasiness. It might cause you to sweat, feel restless and tense, and have a rapid heartbeat.   It can be a normal reaction to stress. For example, you might feel anxious when faced with a difficult problem at work, before taking a test, or before making an important decision. It can help you to cope. The anxiety may give you a boost of energy or help you focus. But for people with anxiety disorders, the fear is not temporary and can be overwhelming.</p>What are anxiety disorders?<p>Anxiety disorders are conditions in which you have anxiety that does not go away and can get worse over time. The symptoms can interfere with daily activities such as job performance, schoolwork, and relationships.</p>What are the types of anxiety disorders?<p>There are several types of anxiety disorders, including: </p><ul><li>Generalized anxiety disorder (GAD). People with GAD worry about ordinary issues such as health, money, work, and family. But their worries are excessive, and they have them almost every day for at least 6 months.</li><li>Panic disorder. People with panic disorder have panic attacks. These are sudden, repeated periods of intense fear when there is no danger. The attacks come on quickly and can last several minutes or more.</li><li>Phobias. People with phobias have an intense fear of something that poses little or no actual danger. Their fear may be about spiders, flying, going to crowded places, or being in social situations (known as social anxiety).</li></ul>What causes anxiety disorders?<p>The cause of anxiety is unknown. Factors such as genetics, brain biology and chemistry, stress, and your environment may play a role.</p>Who is at risk for anxiety disorders?<p>The risk factors for the different types of anxiety disorders can vary. For example, GAD and phobias are more common in women, but social anxiety affects men and women equally. There are some general risk factors for all types of anxiety disorders, including:</p><ul><li>Certain personality traits, such as being shy or withdrawn when you are in new situations or meeting new people</li><li>Traumatic events in early childhood or adulthood</li><li>Family history of anxiety or other mental disorders</li><li>Some physical health conditions, such as thyroid problems or arrhythmia</li></ul>What are the symptoms of anxiety disorders?<p>The different types of anxiety disorders can have different symptoms. But they all have a combination of:</p><ul><li>Anxious thoughts or beliefs that are hard to control. They make you feel restless and tense and interfere with your daily life. They do not go away and can get worse over time.</li><li>Physical symptoms, such as a pounding or rapid heartbeat, unexplained aches and pains, dizziness, and shortness of breath</li><li>Changes in behavior, such as avoiding everyday activities you used to do</li></ul><p>Using caffeine, other substances, and certain medicines can make your symptoms worse.</p>How are anxiety disorders diagnosed?<p>To diagnose anxiety disorders, your health care provider will ask about your symptoms and medical history. You may also have a physical exam and lab tests to make sure that a different health problem is not the cause of your symptoms.</p><p>If you don't have another health problem, you will get a psychological evaluation. Your provider may do it, or you may be referred to a mental health professional to get one.</p>What are the treatments for anxiety disorders?<p>The main treatments for anxiety disorders are psychotherapy (talk therapy), medicines, or both:</p><ul><li>Psychotherapy can help you recognize and change troubling emotions, thoughts, and behaviors. To be effective, it should focus on your specific anxieties and needs. There are many types; some of the types used for anxiety disorders include:
  <ul><li>Cognitive behavioral therapy (CBT), which teaches you different ways of thinking and behaving. It can help you change how you react to the things that cause you to feel fear and anxiety. It may include exposure therapy. This therapy focuses on having you confront your fears so that you will be able to do the things that you had been avoiding.</li><li>Acceptance and commitment therapy may help with some anxiety disorders. It uses strategies such as mindfulness and goal setting to reduce discomfort and anxiety.</li></ul> 
</li><li>Medicines to treat anxiety disorders include anti-anxiety medicines and certain antidepressants. Some types of medicines may work better for specific types of anxiety disorders. You should work closely with your provider to identify which medicine is best for you. You may need to try more than one medicine before you can find the right one.</li></ul><p>Support groups and stress management techniques may also be helpful.</p><p>NIH: National Institute of Mental Health</p></content>
      <content name="mesh">Anxiety Disorders</content>
      <content name="mesh">Anxiety</content>

    <document rank="5" url="https://medlineplus.gov/botox.html">
      <content name="title">Botox</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Botulinum Toxin</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Botulinum toxin type A</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>Botox is a drug made from a toxin produced by the bacterium Clostridium botulinum.  It's the same toxin that causes a life-threatening type of food poisoning called botulism. Doctors use it in small doses to treat health problems, including:</p><ul><li>Temporary smoothing of facial wrinkles and improving your appearance</li><li>Severe underarm sweating </li><li>Cervical dystonia - a neurological disorder that causes severe neck and shoulder muscle contractions </li><li>Blepharospasm - uncontrollable blinking  </li><li>Strabismus - misaligned eyes </li><li>Chronic <span class="qt0">migraine</span></li><li>Overactive bladder</li></ul><p>Botox injections work by weakening or paralyzing certain muscles or by blocking certain nerves. The effects last about three to twelve months, depending on what you are treating. The most common side effects are pain, swelling, or bruising at the injection site.  You could also have flu-like symptoms, headache, and upset stomach.  Injections in the face may also cause temporary drooping eyelids. You should not use Botox if you are pregnant or breastfeeding.</p></content>

    <document rank="6" url="https://medlineplus.gov/chronicpain.html">
      <content name="title">Chronic Pain</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Pain, Chronic</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is chronic pain?<p>Pain is a signal from your nervous system that something may be wrong. It is an unpleasant feeling, such as a prick, tingle, sting, burn, or ache. Pain may be sharp or dull. You may feel pain in one area of your body or all over. Each person feels pain differently, even if the reason for the pain is the same.</p><p>Pain might be acute or chronic. The type of pain is based on how long and how often you have pain. Acute pain starts suddenly and goes away when the cause is treated or healed. This pain lets you know that you may be injured or have a problem you need to take care of. Chronic pain lasts longer than three months or the time in which you should have healed.</p><p>If you have chronic pain, it can occur most days or every day and may last for weeks, months, or even years. It can affect all aspects of daily life, including your mood and relationships. Treatment may not get rid of chronic pain, but it can help with your symptoms.</p>What causes chronic pain?<p>Sometimes, acute pain can become chronic pain. The original cause may have been an injury or infection, or you may have an ongoing cause of pain, such as arthritis or cancer. In some cases, there is no clear cause. Environmental factors and psychological factors such as mood and stress can make chronic pain worse.</p>What are the symptoms of chronic pain?<p>Since people feel pain in different ways, two people could have the same issue, but each may have different symptoms. Chronic pain can occur anywhere in your body and may cause other symptoms such as fatigue, mood changes, or difficulty sleeping. If you have depression or stress, it may make chronic pain worse.</p>Who is more likely to get chronic pain?<p>Many older adults have chronic pain. Women also report having more chronic pain than men, and they are at a greater risk for many pain conditions. Some people have two or more chronic pain conditions. You may be more likely to have chronic pain if you have certain medical conditions such as:</p><ul><li>Headaches or <span class="qt0">migraines</span></li><li>Cancer</li><li>Fibromyalgia</li><li>Arthritis</li><li>Nerve damage</li><li>Back problems</li></ul>How is chronic pain diagnosed?<p>Chronic pain lasts three months or longer or when pain continues after your body has healed. If the cause of your pain is unknown, your health care provider may:</p><ul><li>Ask you about your medical history</li><li>Ask you to describe the pain and how it affects your life</li><li>Do a physical exam</li><li>Order blood tests or other medical tests</li></ul>What are the treatments for chronic pain?<p>Chronic pain is not always curable, but treatments can help. Treatments may include medicines, including pain relievers. There are also non-drug treatments, such as acupuncture, physical therapy, and sometimes surgery.</p><p>Depending on the cause of your pain and your symptoms, your provider may also recommend lifestyle changes which may include suggestions for:</p><ul><li>Improving mental health</li><li>Managing stress</li><li>Getting to and staying at a healthy weight</li><li>Adding low-impact exercise</li></ul></content>

    <document rank="7" url="https://medlineplus.gov/depression.html">
      <content name="title">Depression</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Dysthymic Disorder</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Clinical depression</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Major depressive disorder</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Unipolar depression</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is depression?<p>Depression is more than a feeling of being sad or irritable for a few days. It's a serious mood disorder. As one of the most common mental disorders in the United States, depression can affect how you think, feel, and your everyday life. You may not know why you feel this way, but even basic daily activities such as sleeping, eating, or working may become difficult. Depression often needs long-term treatment but getting help at the earliest sign of a problem can help manage your symptoms.</p>What are the different types of depression?<p>The most common types of depression are major depression and persistent depressive disorder.</p><ul><li>Major depression symptoms include a depressed mood or a loss of interest. Symptoms affect your daily activities and last for at least two weeks.</li><li>Persistent depressive disorder (also called dysthymia or dysthymic disorder) has less severe depressive symptoms, but they last longer, usually for at least two years.</li></ul><p>Other types of depression can include:</p><ul><li>Seasonal affective disorder comes and goes with the seasons. It usually starts in late fall and early winter and goes away during the spring and summer.</li><li>Bipolar disorder is sometimes called manic depression. This mood disorder can cause intense mood swings.</li><li>Depression with symptoms of psychosis is a severe form of depression that also has delusions (false beliefs) or hallucinations (seeing, hearing, or feeling something that isn't there).</li></ul>What causes depression?<p>There are a variety of things that may cause depression, including genetic, biological, environmental, and psychological factors. Depression can happen at any age and can affect anyone, but it's more common in women. This is often due to the physical and hormonal changes that women have during menstruation, pregnancy, the postpartum period, and menopause. Other mental disorders or chronic medical conditions such as diabetes, cancer, or heart disease may occur with depression. This may make the chronic condition or depression worse.</p><p>In some cases, there is no clear cause for depression.</p>Who is at risk of depression?<p>You may be at a higher risk of depression if you have a close relative who has it or another mental health disorder. Going through trauma or stressful life events may raise your risk even more.</p>What are the symptoms of depression?<p>Depression doesn't look the same on everyone. The type and number of symptoms of depression can vary and don't always appear like sadness. Depression may affect you differently based on your:</p><ul><li>Age. The symptoms of depression may be different in a child, who may appear more irritable than sad. Teens may act out or have excessive sleepiness. The symptoms for an older adult may not be as obvious as other age groups.</li><li>Sex. Men often show symptoms and coping behaviors differently than women. Rather than sad, men may appear angry or irritable.</li><li>Medical conditions. Some medicines and medical conditions may cause symptoms that look like depression. Chronic conditions may make depression worse.</li></ul><p>Symptoms of depression are ongoing and affect your day-to-day life. They can include:</p><ul><li>Feeling sad or "empty"</li><li>Losing interest in favorite activities</li><li>Overeating, or not wanting to eat at all</li><li>Sleeping too much, or not enough</li><li>Feeling very tired</li><li>Feeling hopeless, irritable, anxious, or guilty</li><li>Having aches and pains, headaches, cramps, or digestive problems</li><li>Having thoughts of death or suicide</li><li>Changing moods or behaviors</li><li>Increasing use of alcohol or drugs</li><li>Isolating from family and friends</li></ul>How is depression diagnosed?<p>If you think you have depression, talk to your health care provider or a mental health provider. To be diagnosed with depression, your provider may use many tools:</p><ul><li>A medical history will include asking about your symptoms and family history. To be diagnosed with depression, your symptoms must occur most of the day, nearly every day, for at least two weeks. One of the symptoms must be a depressed mood or a loss of interest in most activities.</li><li>Medical tests may be done to rule out other medical conditions. Certain medicines and medical conditions (such as viruses or thyroid disorders) may cause symptoms like depression.</li><li>A physical exam to make sure another issue isn't causing your symptoms.</li><li>A mental health evaluation by your provider or a mental health provider.</li></ul>What are the treatments for depression?<p>Depression may need long-term treatment. Most treatments include medicines and/or psychotherapy (talk therapy):</p><ul><li>Medicines such as antidepressants may help control your symptoms. You may need to try several medicines to find which works best for you. Medicine may take time to work and can have side effects. Don't stop taking your medicine without first talking with your provider. Contact your provider if you have any concerns about side effects from the medicine.</li><li>Psychotherapy (talk therapy) under the care of a mental health provider can help you recognize and change troubling emotions, thoughts, and behaviors. This may be done one-on-one or in a group setting. It can give you and your family support, education, skills, and coping strategies.</li><li>Light therapy has been shown to be effective for seasonal affective disorder (SAD).</li><li>Other treatments options may be considered if treatment isn't working or if depression is severe. These may include:
	<ul><li>Electroconvulsive therapy (ECT) is a brain stimulation procedure that uses a mild electric current when you are under general anesthesia. ECT is most often used for severe depression that is not getting better with other treatments.</li><li>Repetitive transcranial magnetic stimulation (rTMS) is a brain stimulation procedure that uses magnetic waves to relieve depression. It's not as powerful as ECT, but with rTMS, you don't need anesthesia. It also has a low risk of negative effects on your memory and thinking.</li></ul>
</li></ul>Can depression be prevented?<p>Most cases of depression can't be prevented, but healthy lifestyle changes can have long-term benefits for your mental health. These can include:</p><ul><li>Getting regular exercise</li><li>Having a consistent sleep schedule</li><li>Controlling stress</li></ul><p>If you, or someone you love is depressed, see your provider or a mental health provider. It's important to get treatment at the earliest sign of a problem.</p><p>If you or someone you know has thoughts of hurting themselves or attempting suicide, get help right away. If it is an emergency, dial 911.</p><p>NIH: National Institute of Mental Health</p></content>

    <document rank="8" url="https://medlineplus.gov/dizzinessandvertigo.html">
      <content name="title">Dizziness and Vertigo</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Vertigo</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Vestibular Diseases</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>When you're dizzy, you may feel lightheaded, woozy, or disoriented. If you feel like you or the room are spinning, you have vertigo. These feelings may make you lose your balance.</p><p>Dizziness can have many different causes. A sudden drop in blood pressure or being dehydrated can make you dizzy. Many people feel lightheaded if they get up too quickly from sitting or lying down. Certain medicines and problems with your inner ear may cause dizziness. So can motion sickness. Sometimes dizziness can be a symptom of other disorders.</p><p>As people get older, they may have more health problems and take more medicines. This makes them more likely to have problems with dizziness and balance.</p><p>Dizziness usually gets better by itself or is easily treated. If you are dizzy often, you should see your health care provider to find the cause.</p><p>NIH: National Institutes of Health</p></content>
      <content name="mesh">Vertigo</content>
      <content name="mesh">Dizziness</content>

    <document rank="9" url="https://medlineplus.gov/eyediseases.html">
      <content name="title">Eye Diseases</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Ophthalmology</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Pinguecula</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What diseases can affect the eye?<p>Even if your eyes feel healthy, you could have a problem and not know it. That's why regular eye exams are so important. Refractive errors are the most common type of vision problem that makes it hard to see clearly. But some eye conditions or diseases don't have any symptoms and can lead to a permanent loss of vision.</p><p>Some common eye diseases and conditions include:</p><ul><li>Cataracts, which happens when the clear lens in the front of your eye becomes cloudy. Cataracts are common as you get older and can lead to vision loss.</li><li>Optic nerve disorders, which includes glaucoma. Glaucoma usually happens when the fluid pressure inside your eyes slowly rises.</li><li>Retinal disorders, which are problems with the nerve layer at the back of your eye.</li><li>Macular degeneration, which is a disease that destroys your sharp, central vision.</li><li>Diabetic eye problems, which can cause vision loss and blindness.</li><li>Conjunctivitis, which is an infection also known as pink eye. It can cause pink or red itchy eyes. Some types of pink eye can spread very easily.</li></ul>What are the symptoms of eye diseases?<p>Some eye conditions and diseases do not always have symptoms. Early detection through a comprehensive dilated eye exam could help your eye care provider detect diseases in the early stages. Proper treatment could help slow or prevent vision loss.</p><p>Get regular eye exams and call your eye care professional right away if you notice any of these symptoms:</p><ul><li>A sudden change in vision</li><li>Everything looks dim</li><li>Flashes of light</li><li>Eye pain</li><li>Double vision</li><li>Fluid coming from your eye</li><li>Inflammation (swelling)</li></ul>Who is more likely to develop eye diseases?<p>Getting older increases your risk of some eye diseases. Other things that may put you at a higher risk of some eye diseases can include if you:</p><ul><li>Are overweight or obese</li><li>Have a family history of eye disease</li><li>Are African American, Hispanic, or Native American</li><li>Have certain health conditions such as Diabetic eye problems, or high blood pressure</li></ul>What are the treatments for eye diseases?<p>Treatment may depend on the eye condition or disease but can include:</p><ul><li>A new prescription for eyeglasses or contacts</li><li>Use of bright lights or magnifying lenses for reading and other activities</li><li>Medicines</li><li>Prescription eye drops or ointments</li><li>Laser treatment</li><li>Surgery</li></ul><p>Depending on your eye condition or disease, treatment may slow vision loss but may not restore vision. Talk to your provider about what treatment is best for you.</p>Can eye diseases be prevented?<p>Your best defense is to have regular checkups. Be sure to tell your provider if any eye diseases run in your family.</p><p>You can also help take care of your vision by making lifestyle changes to lower your risk of health conditions that can cause vision problems. This could include by:</p><ul><li>Protecting your eyes from the sun</li><li>Wearing protective eyewear while using tools or playing certain sports</li><li>Getting regular physical activity</li><li>Staying at a healthy weight</li><li>Quitting smoking (or not starting)</li><li>Eating a healthy diet</li></ul><p>NIH: National Eye Institute</p></content>
      <content name="mesh">Eye Diseases</content>
      <content name="groupName">Eyes and Vision</content>

    <document rank="10" url="https://medlineplus.gov/fibromyalgia.html">
      <content name="title">Fibromyalgia</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">FMS</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Fibro</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>Fibromyalgia is chronic (long-lasting) condition that causes pain all over the body, fatigue, and other symptoms. There is no cure, but treatments can help with the symptoms.</p>What causes fibromyalgia?<p>The exact cause of fibromyalgia is unknown. Studies of the brains of people with fibromyalgia found that they seem to process pain differently than people who don't have it. They may feel pain when others do not, and they may also have a more severe reaction to pain.</p><p>Fibromyalgia can run in families, so genetics may also play a role. Other factors may also be involved, such as having certain diseases that cause pain.</p>Who is at risk for fibromyalgia?<p>Anyone can get fibromyalgia, but it is more common in:</p><ul><li>Women; they are twice as likely to have fibromyalgia</li><li>Middle-aged people; your chance of having it increases as you get older</li><li>People with certain diseases, such as lupus, rheumatoid arthritis, osteoarthritis or ankylosing spondylitis</li><li>People who have a family member with fibromyalgia</li></ul>What are the symptoms of fibromyalgia?<p>The main symptoms of fibromyalgia are:</p><ul><li>Chronic (long-lasting) pain and stiffness all over the body. People often describe it as aching, burning, or throbbing.</li><li>Fatigue and tiredness.</li><li>Trouble sleeping.</li></ul><p>Other symptoms may include:</p><ul><li>Problems with thinking, memory, and concentration (sometimes called "fibro fog")</li><li>Muscle and joint stiffness</li><li>Numbness or tingling in the arms and legs</li><li>Sensitivity to light, noise, odors, and temperature</li></ul><p>People who have fibromyalgia are also more likely to have certain diseases, including:</p><ul><li>Anxiety</li><li>Depression</li><li>Headaches, including <span class="qt0">migraines</span></li><li>Irritable bowel syndrome</li><li>Pain in the face or jaw, including disorders of the jaw know as temporomandibular joint syndrome (TMJ)</li></ul>How is fibromyalgia diagnosed?<p>Fibromyalgia can be hard to diagnose. It sometimes takes visits to several different health care providers to get a diagnosis. One problem is that there isn't a specific test for it. And the main symptoms, pain and fatigue, are common in many other conditions. Providers have to rule out other causes of the symptoms before making a diagnosis of fibromyalgia. This is called making a differential diagnosis.</p><p>To find out if you have fibromyalgia, your provider:</p><ul><li>Will take your medical history, including asking detailed questions about your symptoms</li><li>Will do a physical exam</li><li>May order x-rays and blood tests to rule out other conditions</li></ul>What are the treatments for fibromyalgia?<p>Not all providers are familiar with fibromyalgia and its treatment. You should see a provider or team of providers who specialize in the treatment of fibromyalgia.</p><p>There is no cure for fibromyalgia, so treatment focuses on relieving the symptoms. Providers usually use a combination of treatments, including medicines, lifestyle changes, talk therapy, and complementary therapies:</p><ul><li>Medicines</li><ul><li>Certain antidepressants and anti-seizure medicines, which may help with pain or sleep problems.</li><li>Pain relievers.</li></ul><li>Lifestyle changes</li><ul><li>Getting enough sleep.</li><li>Getting regular physical activity. If you have not already been active, start slowly and gradually increase how much activity you get. You may want to see a physical therapist, who can help you create a plan that is right for you.</li><li>Learning how to manage stress.</li><li>Learning to pace yourself. If you do too much, it can make your symptoms worse. So you need to learn to balance being active with your need for rest.</li></ul><li>Talk therapy, such as cognitive behavioral therapy (CBT), can help you learn strategies to deal with pain, stress, and negative thoughts. If you also have depression along with your fibromyalgia, talk therapy can help with that too.</li><li>Complementary therapies have helped some people with the symptoms of fibromyalgia. But researchers need to do more studies to show which ones are effective. You could consider trying them, but you should check with your provider first. These therapies include:</li><ul><li>Mindfulness meditation.</li><li>Biofeedback, which uses electronic devices to measure body functions such as breathing and heart rate. This teaches you to be more aware of your body functions so you can learn to control them.</li><li>Movement therapies such as yoga and tai chi.</li><li>Massage therapy.</li><li>Acupuncture.</li></ul></ul></content>
      <content name="mesh">Fibromyalgia</content>

    <document rank="11" url="https://medlineplus.gov/intimatepartnerviolence.html">
      <content name="title">Intimate Partner Violence</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Abuse</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Domestic Violence</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Partner Abuse</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Spouse Abuse</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Violence</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Battery</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Spousal abuse</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is intimate partner violence (IPV)?<p>Intimate partner violence (IPV) is abuse that happens in a romantic relationship. The intimate partner could be a current or former spouse or dating partner. IPV is also known as domestic violence.</p><p>IPV can happen one time, or it may be ongoing. It may include different types of abuse, such as:</p><ul><li>Physical violence, which is when a person hurts or tries to hurt a partner by hitting, kicking, or using another type of physical force.</li><li>Sexual violence which involves forcing or attempting to force a partner to take part in sexual activity when the partner does not or cannot consent. The sexual activity could include things like sex acts, sexual touching, or non-physical sexual events (e.g., sexting).</li><li>Emotional abuse, which includes threats, name-calling, put-downs, and humiliation. It can also involve controlling behavior, such as telling a partner how to act or dress and not letting them see family or friends.</li><li>Economic abuse, also called financial abuse, involves controlling access to money.</li><li>Stalking, which is repeated, unwanted contact that causes fear or concern for the safety of the partner. This can include watching or following the partner. The stalker may send repeated, unwanted phone calls or texts.</li></ul>Who is affected by intimate partner violence (IPV)?<p>It is hard to know exactly how common IPV is because it is often not reported.</p><p>But we do know that IPV can happen to anyone. It affects people with all levels of income and education.</p>What are the signs that someone is experiencing intimate partner violence (IPV)?<p>If you think that a loved one might be experiencing IPV, watch for these signs:</p><p>Does your friend or loved one:</p><ul><li>Have unexplained cuts or bruises?</li><li>Avoid friends, family, and favorite activities?</li><li>Make excuses for their partner's behavior?</li><li>Look uncomfortable or fearful around their partner?</li></ul><p>Does your friend or loved one's partner:</p><ul><li>Yell at or make fun of them?</li><li>Try to control them by making all the decisions?</li><li>Check up on them at work or school?</li><li>Force them to do sexual things they don't want to do?</li><li>Threaten to hurt themself if the partner wants to break up?</li></ul>What can I do if I am experiencing intimate partner violence (IPV)?<p>Your safety is the most important concern. If you are in immediate danger, call 911.</p><p>If you are not in immediate danger, you can:</p><ul><li>Get medical care if you have been injured or sexually assaulted.</li><li>Call a helpline for free, anonymous help. You can contact the National Domestic Violence Hotline at 800-799-SAFE (7233) or 800-787-3224 (TTY). You can also chat with them through their website or through text by texting START to 88788.</li><li>Find out where to get help in your community. Contact local organizations that can help you.</li><li>Make a safety plan to leave. Intimate partner violence usually does not get better. Think about a safe place for you to go and all the things you will need when you leave.</li><li>Save the evidence. Keep evidence of abuse, such as pictures of your injuries or threatening emails or texts. Make sure that it is in a safe place the abuser cannot access.</li><li>Talk to someone you trust, such as a family member, a friend, a co-worker, or a spiritual leader.</li><li>Consider getting a restraining order to protect yourself.
</li></ul>How can I help someone who is experiencing intimate partner violence (IPV)?<p>Let your loved one know that being treated this way isn't healthy and that they are not to blame. You should:</p><ul><li>Call 911 if there is immediate danger.</li><li>Watch for the signs of abuse. Learn about the signs and keep track of the ones that you see.</li><li>Find out about local resources. Get the addresses and phone numbers of some local resources in your community. Then, you'll be able to share the information if the person is ready for it.</li><li>Set up a time to talk. Make sure you can have your conversation in a safe, private place. Your loved one's partner may have access to their cell phone or computer, so be careful about sharing information over text or email.</li><li>Be specific about why you are worried. Describe the behaviors that concern you. Be as specific as possible when explaining why you are worried.</li><li>Plan for safety. If your loved one is ready to leave an abusive partner, help them make a plan for getting out of the relationship as safely as possible. An intimate partner violence counselor can help with making a safety plan.</li><li>Be patient and do not judge. You should talk about your concerns with your loved one, but you need to understand that they may not be ready to talk about it. Let them know that you're available to talk at any time, and that you will listen without judging them.</li></ul></content>

    <document rank="12" url="https://medlineplus.gov/mitralvalveprolapse.html">
      <content name="title">Mitral Valve Prolapse</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Barlow's syndrome</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Floppy valve syndrome</content>
      <content name="altTitle">MVP</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is the mitral valve?<p>The mitral valve is one of the four valves in your heart. Heart valves have flaps that open and close. The flaps make sure that blood flows in the right direction through your heart and to the rest of your body. When your heart beats, the flaps open to let blood through. Between heartbeats, they close to stop the blood from flowing backwards.</p><p>The mitral valve opens to let blood flow from your heart's upper left chamber to the lower left chamber. When the lower left chamber contracts (squeezes) to pump blood to your body, the mitral valve closes tightly to keep any blood from flowing backwards.</p>What is mitral valve prolapse (MVP)?<p>Mitral valve prolapse (MVP) happens when the flaps of the mitral valve become floppy and don't close tightly. In some cases, blood may leak backwards through the valve to the chamber it came from. This is called backflow, or regurgitation. When there is a lot of mitral valve backflow, the heart can't push enough blood out to the body.</p><p>But most people who have MVP don't have any backflow. In fact, MVP doesn't cause any health problems for most people who have it.</p>Who is more likely to develop mitral valve prolapse (MVP)?<p>Anyone can have MVP. Most people who have it were born with it. MVP tends to run in families, but researchers don't know the exact cause.</p><p>You may be more likely to develop MVP if you:</p><ul><li>Are older. The risk of MVP increases as aging affects the valve.</li><li>Had rheumatic fever, a disease that can develop after a strep throat infection and cause damage to the heart valves.</li><li>Were born with a connective tissue disorder, such as Marfan syndrome or Ehlers-Danlos syndrome.</li><li>Have Graves' disease, a type of thyroid condition.</li><li>Have scoliosis (a side-to-side curve of the spine) or other problems with the bones of your body.</li><li>Have some types of muscular dystrophy.</li></ul><p>Mitral valve prolapse with backflow is most common in men and people who have high blood pressure.</p>What are the symptoms of mitral valve prolapse (MVP)?<p>Most people who have MVP don't have any symptoms. But if it does cause symptoms, they may include:</p><ul><li>Heart palpitations (feeling that your heart is fluttering, skipping beats, or beating too hard or too fast)</li><li>Shortness of breath (feeling like you can't get enough air)</li><li>A cough</li><li>Fatigue, dizziness, or anxiety</li><li><span class="qt0">Migraines</span></li><li>Chest pain</li></ul>What other problems can mitral valve prolapse (MVP) cause?<p>In rare cases, MVP can cause other problems. They're most often caused by backflow. They can include:</p><ul><li>Arrhythmia, a problem with the rate or rhythm of your heartbeat</li><li>Endocarditis, an infection in the lining of the heart and heart valves</li><li>Heart failure</li></ul>How is mitral valve prolapse (MVP) diagnosed?<p>Health care providers often find MVP during routine health check-ups. If you have MVP, your provider may hear a clicking sound when listening to your heart with a stethoscope. If blood flows backwards through the valve, your heart may also make a whooshing sound called a heart murmur.</p><p>You may also need certain heart tests. The most useful test is an echocardiogram, or echo. This is a type of ultrasound that uses sound waves to make a moving picture of your heart.</p>What are the treatments for mitral valve prolapse (MVP)?<p>Most people don't need any treatment for MVP. If you have symptoms with little or no backflow, you may only need medicine to relieve your discomfort.</p><p>If the amount of backflow is significant, you may need treatment to prevent other heart problems from developing. Treatments may include:</p><ul><li>Medicines to help your heart work better.</li><li>Heart surgery to repair or replace a very abnormal mitral valve with backflow. The goal of surgery is to improve your symptoms and reduce your risk of developing heart failure.</li></ul><p>When possible, valve repair is generally preferred over replacement. That's because repairs are less likely to weaken the heart muscle, and they're less likely to cause heart infection.</p>Can mitral valve prolapse be prevented?<p>You can't prevent mitral valve prolapse. But if you have mitral valve prolapse, you can help prevent the rare but serious problems it can cause by:</p><ul><li>Brushing and flossing your teeth regularly. That helps keep bacteria out of your bloodstream, which further reduces the rare risk of a heart infection.</li><li>Asking your provider if you need to take antibiotics before dental work or surgery to lower your risk of heart infection. This mostly applies to people who have had valve repair or replacement surgery.</li><li>Getting regular check-ups and taking any medicines that your provider may have prescribed.</li><li>Making heart-healthy habits part of your life to prevent heart disease.</li></ul><p>NIH: National Heart, Lung, and Blood Institute</p></content>
      <content name="mesh">Mitral Valve Prolapse</content>
      <content name="groupName">Blood, Heart and Circulation</content>

    <document rank="13" url="https://medlineplus.gov/nauseaandvomiting.html">
      <content name="title">Nausea and Vomiting</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Vomiting</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Emesis</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What are nausea and vomiting?<p>Nausea is when you feel sick to your stomach, as if you are going to throw up. Vomiting is when you throw up.</p>What causes nausea and vomiting?<p>Nausea and vomiting can be symptoms of many different conditions, including:</p><ul><li>Morning sickness during pregnancy</li><li>Gastroenteritis (infection of your intestines) and other infections</li><li><span class="qt0">Migraines</span></li><li>Motion sickness</li><li>Food poisoning</li><li>Medicines, including those for cancer chemotherapy</li><li>GERD (reflux) and ulcers</li><li>Intestinal obstruction</li></ul>When do I need to see a health care provider for nausea and vomiting?<p>Nausea and vomiting are common. They are usually not serious. However, you should contact your health care provider immediately if you have:</p><ul><li>A reason to think that your vomiting is from poisoning</li><li>Vomited for longer than 24 hours</li><li>Blood in the vomit</li><li>Severe abdominal pain</li><li>Severe headache and stiff neck</li><li>Signs of dehydration, such as dry mouth, infrequent urination or dark urine</li></ul>How is the cause of nausea and vomiting diagnosed?<p>Your health care provider will take your medical history, ask about your symptoms and do a physical exam. The provider will look for signs of dehydration. You may have some tests, including blood and urine tests. Women may also have a pregnancy test.</p>What are the treatments for nausea and vomiting?<p>Treatments for nausea and vomiting depend on the cause. You may get treatment for the underlying problem. There are some medicines that can treatment nausea and vomiting. For severe cases of vomiting, you may need extra fluids through an IV (intravenous).</p><p>There are things that you can do to feel better:</p><ul><li>Get enough fluids, to avoid dehydration. If you are having trouble keeping liquids down, drink small amounts of clear liquids often.</li><li>Eat bland foods; stay away from spicy, fatty, or salty foods</li><li>Eat smaller meals more often</li><li>Avoid strong smells, since they can sometimes trigger nausea and vomiting</li><li>If you are pregnant and have morning sickness, eat crackers before you get out of bed in the morning</li></ul></content>

    <document rank="14" url="https://medlineplus.gov/overthecountermedicines.html">
      <content name="title">Over-the-Counter Medicines</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">About Your Medicines</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Drug Information</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Medicines, Over-the-Counter</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Non-Prescription Drugs</content>
      <content name="altTitle">OTC Medicines</content>
      <content name="FullSummary"><p>Over-the-counter (OTC) medicines are drugs you can buy without a prescription. Some OTC medicines relieve aches, pains, and itches. Some prevent or cure diseases, like tooth decay and athlete's foot. Others help manage recurring problems, like <span class="qt0">migraines</span> and allergies.</p><p> In the United States, the Food and Drug Administration decides whether a medicine is safe and effective enough to sell over-the-counter. This allows you to take a more active role in your health care. But you also need to be careful to avoid mistakes. Make sure to follow the instructions on the drug label. If you don't understand the instructions, ask your pharmacist or health care provider.</p><p>Also keep in mind that that there are still risks to taking OTC medicines:</p><ul><li>The medicine you are taking could interact with other medicines, supplements, foods, or drinks</li><li>Some medicines are not right for people with certain medical conditions. For example, people with high blood pressure should not take certain decongestants.</li><li>Some people are allergic to certain medicines</li><li>Many medicines are not safe during pregnancy. If you are pregnant, check with your health care provider before taking any medicine.</li><li>Be careful when giving medicines to children. Make sure that you give your child the correct dose. If you are giving your child a liquid medicine, don't use a kitchen spoon. Instead use a measuring spoon or a dosing cup marked in teaspoons.</li></ul><p>If you have been taking an OTC medicine but your symptoms don't go away, contact your health care provider. You should not take OTC medicines longer or in higher doses than the label recommends. </p><p>Food and Drug Administration</p></content>
      <content name="mesh">Nonprescription Drugs</content>
      <content name="groupName">Drug Therapy</content>

    <document rank="15" url="https://medlineplus.gov/pain.html">
      <content name="title">Pain</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is pain?<p>Pain is a signal in your nervous system that something may be wrong. It is an unpleasant feeling, such as a prick, tingle, sting, burn, or ache. Each person feels pain differently, even if the reason for the pain is the same.  Pain may be sharp or dull. It may be mild or severe. Pain may come and go, or it may be constant. You may feel pain in one area of your body, such as your back, abdomen, chest, or pelvis, or you may feel it all over.</p><p>Pain can help alert you that there is a problem. If you never felt pain, you might seriously hurt yourself without knowing it, or you might not realize you have a medical problem that needs treatment. Finding out what's causing your pain will help your health care provider determine the best way to manage or treat it.</p>What are the types of pain?<p>Patterns and types of pain are named based on how long and how often you have pain. These include:</p><p>
</p><ul><li>Acute pain usually happens suddenly because of a disease, injury, or inflammation (irritation, redness, and/or swelling). This type of pain is often sharp and acts as a warning that something is wrong. It usually goes away when the cause is treated or healed, though sometimes it can turn into chronic pain. Some examples of causes of acute pain include broken bones or dental issues.</li><li>Chronic pain lasts for longer than three months or the time in which you should have healed.  It can affect all aspects of daily life, including your mood and relationships. Some conditions that might cause chronic pain include arthritis or back problems.</li><li>Episodic pain can happen from time to time and may occur with long-term medical conditions. Some conditions that might cause episodic pain include sickle cell disease or chronic <span class="qt0">migraines</span>.</li></ul><p>Pain may also be categorized by what is likely to be the cause of the pain. Pain may be described as nociceptive (caused by tissue damage or inflammation), neuropathic (caused by nerve damage), or nociplastic (caused by changes in how your nervous system processes pain).</p>What causes pain?<p>Understanding what causes pain and why people feel it differently may be difficult. Often, it's easier to find the cause of acute pain due to an injury than the cause of chronic pain, or you may have an ongoing cause of pain, such as cancer.</p><p>In some cases, there is no clear cause. Environmental factors and psychological factors such as stress and beliefs about pain may affect the way you feel pain and respond to treatment.</p>How is pain diagnosed?<p>You are the only one who knows how your pain feels. Your provider can best measure your pain by how you report it. They may ask you:</p><ul><li>Where is the pain in your body?</li><li>How long have you had the pain?</li><li>How does the pain feel, such as stinging, burning, or sharp?</li><li>How often do you have pain?</li><li>When do you feel pain, such as in the morning, evening, or all the time?</li><li>What relieves the pain?</li><li>How does pain affect your daily life?</li><li>Is the pain mild, moderate, or severe?</li><li>To rate the pain on a scale.</li></ul><p>If the cause of your pain is unknown, your provider may also do a physical exam and order blood tests or other medical tests to help find the cause.</p>What are the treatments for pain?<p>Pain is not always curable, but there are many ways to manage and treat it. Treatment depends on the cause and type of pain. Treatments may include medicines, such as pain relievers. There are also non-drug treatments, such as acupuncture, physical therapy, and sometimes surgery.</p><p>Depending on the cause of your pain and your symptoms, your provider may recommend lifestyle changes. These may include suggestions for:</p><ul><li>Eating a healthy diet</li><li>Adding exercise</li><li>Losing weight</li><li>Managing stress</li><li>Improving mental health</li></ul><p>NIH: National Institute of Neurological Disorders and Stroke</p></content>

      <content name="mesh">Pain Perception</content>

    <document rank="16" url="https://medlineplus.gov/posttraumaticstressdisorder.html">
      <content name="title">Post-Traumatic Stress Disorder</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">PTSD</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD)?<p>Post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD) is a mental health disorder that some people develop after they experience or see a traumatic event. The traumatic event may be life-threatening, such as combat, a natural disaster, a car accident, or sexual assault. But sometimes the event is not necessarily a dangerous one. For example, the sudden, unexpected death of a loved one can also cause PTSD.</p><p>It's normal to feel afraid during and after a traumatic situation. The fear triggers a "fight-or-flight" response. This is your body's way of helping to protect itself from possible harm. It causes changes in your body such as the release of certain hormones and increases in alertness, blood pressure, heart rate, and breathing.</p><p>In time, most people recover from this naturally. But people with PTSD don't feel better. They feel stressed and frightened long after the trauma is over. In some cases, the PTSD symptoms may start later on. They might also come and go over time.</p>What causes post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD)?<p>Researchers don't know why some people get PTSD and others don't. Genetics, neurobiology, risk factors, and personal factors may affect whether you get PTSD after a traumatic event.</p>Who is more likely to develop post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD)?<p>You can develop PTSD at any age. Many risk factors play a part in whether you will develop PTSD. They include:</p><ul><li>Your sex; women are more likely to develop PTSD</li><li>Having had trauma in childhood</li><li>Feeling horror, helplessness, or extreme fear</li><li>Going through a traumatic event that lasts a long time</li><li>Having little or no social support after the event</li><li>Dealing with extra stress after the event, such as loss of a loved one, pain and injury, or loss of a job or home</li><li>Having a history of mental illness or substance use</li></ul>What are the symptoms of post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD)?<p>There are four types of PTSD symptoms, but they may not be the same for everyone. Each person experiences symptoms in their own way. The types are:</p><ul><li>Re-experiencing symptoms, when something reminds you of the trauma and you feel that fear again. Examples include
		<ul><li>Flashbacks, which cause you to feel like you are going through the event again</li><li>Nightmares</li><li>Frightening thoughts</li></ul></li><li>Avoidance symptoms, when you try to avoid situations or people that trigger memories of the traumatic event. This may cause you to
		<ul><li>Stay away from places, events, or objects that are reminders of the traumatic experience. For example, if you were in a car accident, you might stop driving.</li><li>Avoiding thoughts or feelings related to the traumatic event. For example, you might try to stay very busy to try to avoid thinking about what happened.</li></ul></li><li>Arousal and reactivity symptoms, which may cause you to be jittery or be on the lookout for danger. They include
		<ul><li>Being easily startled</li><li>Feeling tense or "on edge"</li><li>Having difficulty sleeping</li><li>Having angry outbursts</li></ul></li><li>Cognition and mood symptoms, which are negative changes in beliefs and feelings. They include
		<ul><li>Trouble remembering important things about the traumatic event</li><li>Negative thoughts about yourself or the world</li><li>Feeling blame and guilt</li><li>No longer being interested in things you enjoyed</li><li>Trouble concentrating</li></ul></li></ul><p>The symptoms usually start soon after the traumatic event. But sometimes they may not appear until months or years later. They also may come and go over many years.</p><p>If your symptoms last longer than four weeks, cause you great distress, or interfere with your work or home life, you might have PTSD.</p>How is post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD) diagnosed?<p>A health care provider who has experience helping people with mental illnesses can diagnose PTSD. The provider will do a mental health screening and may also do a physical exam. To get a diagnosis of PTSD, you must have all of these symptoms for at least one month:</p><ul><li>At least one re-experiencing symptom</li><li>At least one avoidance symptom</li><li>At least two arousal and reactivity symptoms</li><li>At least two cognition and mood symptoms</li></ul>What are the treatments for post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD)?<p>The main treatments for PTSD are talk therapy, medicines, or both. PTSD affects people differently, so a treatment that works for one person may not work for another. If you have PTSD, you need to work with a mental health professional to find the best treatment for your symptoms.:</p><ul><li>Talk therapy, or psychotherapy, can teach you about your symptoms. You will learn how to identify what triggers them and how to manage them. There are different types of talk therapy for PTSD.</li><li>Medicines can help with the symptoms of PTSD. Antidepressants may help control symptoms such as sadness, worry, anger, and feeling numb inside. Other medicines can help with sleep problems and nightmares.</li></ul>Can post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD) be prevented?<p>There are certain factors that can help reduce the risk of developing PTSD. These are known as resilience factors, and they include:</p><ul><li>Seeking out support from other people, such as friends, family, or a support group</li><li>Learning to feel good about your actions in the face of danger</li><li>Having a coping strategy or a way of getting through the bad event and learning from it</li><li>Being able to act and respond effectively despite feeling fear</li></ul><p>NIH: National Institute of Mental Health</p></content>
      <content name="mesh">Stress Disorders, Post-Traumatic</content>
      <content name="groupName">Mental Health and Behavior</content>
      <content name="snippet">What is post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD)? Post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD) is a mental health disorder that some people develop after they experience  ... </content>

    <document rank="17" url="https://medlineplus.gov/premenstrualsyndrome.html">
      <content name="title">Premenstrual Syndrome</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">PMS</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is premenstrual syndrome (PMS)?<p>Premenstrual syndrome, or PMS, is a group of physical and emotional symptoms that start one to two weeks before your period. Most women have at least some symptoms of PMS, and the symptoms go away after their periods start. The symptoms may range from mild to severe.</p>What is premenstrual dysphoric disorder (PMDD)?<p>Premenstrual dysphoric disorder (PMDD) is a severe type of PMS. With PMDD, the symptoms are severe enough to interfere with your life.  PMDD much less common than PMS.</p>What causes premenstrual syndrome (PMS)?<p>Researchers don't know exactly what causes PMS. Changes in hormone levels during the menstrual cycle may play a role. These changing hormone levels may affect some women more than others.</p>What are the symptoms of premenstrual syndrome (PMS)?<p>PMS symptoms are different for everyone. You may get physical symptoms, emotional symptoms, or both. Your symptoms may also change throughout your life.</p><p>Physical symptoms may include:</p><ul><li>Breast swelling and tenderness</li><li>Acne</li><li>Bloating and weight gain</li><li>Headache</li><li>Joint pain</li><li>Backache</li><li>Constipation or diarrhea</li><li>Food cravings</li></ul><p>Emotional symptoms may include:</p><ul><li>Irritability</li><li>Mood swings</li><li>Crying spells</li><li>Depression</li><li>Anxiety</li><li>Sleeping too much or too little</li><li>Trouble with concentration and memory</li><li>Less interest in sex</li></ul>How is premenstrual syndrome (PMS) diagnosed?<p>You may wish to see your health care provider if your symptoms bother you or affect your daily life.</p><p>There is no single test for PMS. Your provider will talk with you about your symptoms, including when they happen and how much they affect your life.  To be diagnosed with PMS, your symptoms must:</p><ul><li>Happen in the five days before your period for at least three menstrual cycles in a row</li><li>End within four days after your period starts</li><li>Keep you from enjoying or doing some of your normal activities</li></ul><p>Your provider may wish to do tests to rule out other conditions which may cause similar symptoms.</p>What are the treatments for premenstrual syndrome (PMS)?<p>No single PMS treatment works for everyone. If your symptoms are not severe, you may be able to manage them with: </p><ul><li>Over-the-counter pain relievers such as ibuprofen, aspirin, or naproxen, to help ease cramps, headaches, backaches, and breast tenderness</li><li>Getting regular exercise</li><li>Getting enough sleep</li><li>Eating healthy foods</li><li>Avoiding salt, caffeine, sugar, and alcohol in the two weeks before your period</li></ul><p>Some studies have shown that certain vitamins may help with some symptoms of PMS. They include calcium and vitamin B6.</p><p>Some women take certain herbal supplements for PMS symptoms. But there is not enough evidence to prove that supplements are effective for PMS. Check with your provider before taking any vitamins or supplements.</p><p>If you are not able to manage your PMS symptoms, your provider may suggest prescription medicines. These medicines may also be used to treat PMDD. They include:</p><ul><li>Hormonal birth control, which may help with the physical symptoms of PMS. But sometimes they may make the emotional symptoms worse. You may need to try several different types of birth control before you find the right one.</li><li>Antidepressants, such as selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs), which may help with emotional symptoms.</li><li>Diuretics ("water pills") to reduce symptoms of bloating and breast tenderness.</li><li>Anti-anxiety medicine to ease symptoms of anxiety.</li></ul><p>Dept. of Health and Human Services Office on Women's Health</p></content>
      <content name="mesh">Premenstrual Dysphoric Disorder</content>
      <content name="mesh">Premenstrual Syndrome</content>
      <content name="groupName">Women</content>

    <document rank="18" url="https://medlineplus.gov/raynaudphenomenon.html">
      <content name="title">Raynaud Phenomenon</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Raynaud's Disease</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Raynaud's Syndrome</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is Raynaud phenomenon? <p>Raynaud phenomenon is a condition that affects your blood vessels (the tubes that your blood flows through). It causes your blood vessels to narrow, which decreases blood flow. This is called a Raynaud episode or "attack." The attacks usually affect your fingers and toes, causing them to become cold and numb. They may also change color, usually to white or blue. These attacks happen in response to cold temperatures or stress.</p><p>Raynaud phenomenon may also be called Raynaud disease or Raynaud syndrome.</p>What are the types of Raynaud phenomenon?<p>There are two types of Raynaud phenomenon:</p><ul><li>Primary Raynaud phenomenon is the more common type. Its cause is unknown.</li><li>Secondary Raynaud phenomenon is usually caused by another disease or problem, such as lupus or scleroderma. Other causes may be exposure to cold or certain chemicals. The type can be more serious than the primary type.</li></ul>What causes Raynaud phenomenon?<p>Researchers don't know exactly why some people develop Raynaud phenomenon. But they do understand how the attacks happen. When you are exposed to cold, your body tries to slow the loss of heat and maintain its temperature. To do this, the blood vessels in the top layer of your skin constrict (narrow). This moves blood from those vessels near the surface to vessels deeper in the body. But when you have Raynaud phenomenon, the blood vessels in your hands and feet react to cold or stress by narrowing quickly. They also stay narrowed for a long time.</p>Who is more likely to develop Raynaud phenomenon? <p>Anyone can develop Raynaud phenomenon, but some people are more likely to develop it:</p><ul><li>Primary Raynaud phenomenon has been linked to:
  <ul><li>Your sex. Women get it more often than men.</li><li>Your age. It usually develops in people younger than age 30. It often starts in the teenage years.</li><li>A family history of Raynaud phenomenon. You are more likely to develop Raynaud phenomenon if you have a family member who has it.</li></ul>
</li><li>Secondary Raynaud phenomenon has been linked to:
  <ul><li>Certain diseases. These include lupus, scleroderma, rheumatoid arthritis (RA), carpal tunnel syndrome, and connective tissue disorders.</li><li>Certain medicines. Medicines that treat high blood pressure, <span class="qt0">migraines</span>, and attention deficit hyperactivity disorder (ADHD) may cause similar symptoms to Raynaud phenomenon or make your symptoms worse.</li><li>Work-related exposures, such as repeated use of vibrating machinery (such as a jackhammer), or exposure to cold or certain chemicals.</li></ul>
</li></ul>What are the symptoms of Raynaud phenomenon?<p>Raynaud attacks most often happen when you get cold, for example when you grab something cold from the freezer or go into an air-conditioned building on a warm day. Attacks usually affect your fingers and toes. But sometimes they can affect other parts of your body, such as your ears, nose, lips, or nipples.</p><p>An attack causes the skin to become cold and numb. Your skin may also turn white or blue due to a lack of oxygen. As the blood flow returns, your skin may tingle, throb, or turn red. An attack may last a few minutes or a few hours. If you have darker skin, you may not be able to easily see the skin color changes.</p><p>For many people, especially those with the primary type, the symptoms are mild. People with the secondary type often have more severe symptoms. They may develop skin ulcers (open sores caused by poor blood flow) or skin infections.</p>How is Raynaud phenomenon diagnosed?<p>There is no specific test to diagnose Raynaud phenomenon. To find out if you have it, your health care provider:</p><ul><li>Will take your medical history and ask about your symptoms.</li><li>Will do a physical exam.</li><li>May order blood and other lab tests to check for other conditions which could be causing your symptoms and/or to help decide which type of Raynaud phenomenon you have.</li></ul>What are the treatments for Raynaud phenomenon?<p>Most people with Raynaud phenomenon can keep their symptoms under control by avoiding getting cold. But if this is not enough, medicines and, in some cases, surgical procedures can help.</p><p>Secondary Raynaud phenomenon is more likely to be serious and to need more treatments. It's also important to treat the condition or problem that is causing your Raynaud phenomenon. </p><p>You may need to see a specialist such as a rheumatologist, a doctor who treats diseases of the joints, muscles, and bones.</p>Can Raynaud phenomenon be prevented?<p>Raynaud phenomenon cannot be prevented, but you can help prevent attacks and manage your symptoms by:</p><ul><li>Placing your hands or feet in a warm place when you have an attack. This could mean putting them under warm (not hot) water or under a heating pad.</li><li>Keeping your body, especially your hands and feet, warm in cold weather.</li><li>Avoiding triggers, such as certain medicines and stress.</li><li>Quitting smoking (or not starting smoking).</li><li>Managing stress.</li></ul><p>NIH: National Institute of Arthritis and Musculoskeletal and Skin Diseases</p></content>
      <content name="mesh">Raynaud Disease</content>

      <content name="title">Stress</content>
      <content name="organizationName">National Library of Medicine</content>
      <content name="altTitle">Psychological Stress</content>
      <content name="FullSummary">What is stress?<p>Stress is how your brain and body respond to a challenge or demand. When you are stressed, your body releases certain hormones. Hormones are chemicals that travel in your bloodstream and control how different parts of your body work. The hormones that are released when you are stressed make you alert and ready to act. They can raise your blood pressure, heart rate, and blood glucose (blood sugar) levels. This response is sometimes called a "fight or flight" response.</p><p>Everyone gets stressed from time to time. There are different types of stress. It can be short-term or long-term. It can be caused by something that happens once or something that keeps happening.</p><p>Not all stress is bad. In fact, it can help you survive in a dangerous situation. For example, one kind of stress is the jolt you may feel when a car pulls out in front of you while you are driving. This jolt of hormones helps you quickly hit the brakes to avoid an accident. A little short-term stress can sometimes be helpful. For example, the stress of having a deadline for school or your job may push you to get your work done on time. Once you finish it, that stress goes away.</p><p>But stress that lasts a long time can harm your health.</p>What causes long-term stress?<p>Long-term stress, or chronic stress, lasts for weeks, months, or longer. As you go about your life, your body is acting as if you're being threatened.</p><p>Causes of long-term stress include:</p><ul><li>Routine stress from the demands of work, school, family needs, money problems, and other daily pressures that don't stop.</li><li>Stress from sudden, difficult changes in your life, such as divorce, illness, losing your job, or other unhappy life events that often have a long impact.</li><li>Traumatic stress, which may happen when you're in danger of serious harm or death. Examples include being in a bad accident, a war, a flood, earthquake, or other frightening event. This type of stress can cause a long-lasting problem called post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD).</li></ul>How can long term-stress harm my health?<p>People respond to stress in different ways. If you're stressed for a long time you may notice that you are:</p><ul><li>Getting sick more often than usual because stress weakens your body's ability to fight germs</li><li>Having stomach problems or trouble digesting food</li><li>Having trouble sleeping</li><li>Having headaches</li><li>Feeling sad, angry, or easily upset</li></ul><p>When stress keeps going, your body acts as if you're always in danger. That's a lot of strain that may play a part in developing serious health problems, including:</p><ul><li>Depression</li><li>Anxiety</li><li>Heart disease</li><li>High blood pressure</li><li>Diabetes</li></ul><p>It's possible to get used to the symptoms of stress and not even realize there's a problem. So when there's a lot of stress in your life, it's important to pay attention to how it affects you so you can do something about it.</p>How can I manage long-term stress?<p>Simple things that improve your mental health may be helpful in managing long-term stress, such as:</p><ul><li>Get regular exercise. A 30-minute daily walk can help you feel better and help keep your immune system strong, so you don't get sick.</li><li>Try relaxing activities. You could look for an app or wellness program that uses breathing, meditation, or muscle relaxation exercises.</li><li>Get enough sleep every night.</li><li>Avoid too much caffeine.</li><li>Decide what you need to do now and what can wait. And focus on what you got done each day, not on what you weren't able to do.</li><li>Ask your family or friends for support.</li></ul>When should I ask my health care provider for help with stress?<p>Get help if you're having severe symptoms for 2 weeks or more, including:</p><ul><li>Trouble sleeping</li><li>Changes in your eating that cause unwanted changes in your weight</li><li>Troubles getting out of bed because of your mood</li><li>Difficulty focusing your thoughts</li><li>Losing interest in things you usually enjoy</li><li>Not being able to do your usual daily activities</li></ul><p>Always get help right away if stress is causing you to:</p><ul><li>Have thoughts of suicide</li><li>Feel you can't cope</li><li>Use drugs or alcohol more often than usual</li></ul><p>Your health care provider may refer you to a mental health professional such as a psychologist or social worker.</p><p>NIH: National Institute of Mental Health</p></content>
